head: Clomipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clomipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzazepine class that primarily inhibits serotonin reuptake, with additional norepinephrine reuptake inhibition, via blockade of SERT and NET. It is established for obsessive-compulsive disorder and also used for depression. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic effects and QT prolongation; it is contraindicated with concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Adrenal gland pheochromocytoma is a catecholamine-secreting neuroendocrine tumor of chromaffin cells in the adrenal medulla, affecting the endocrine system. Excess epinephrine and norepinephrine cause episodic or sustained hypertension, headaches, palpitations, sweating, and tremor; it occurs in adults and may be hereditary. Management centers on biochemical confirmation, localization, preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade, and surgical adrenalectomy.

head: Tiludronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tiludronic acid is a non-nitrogen bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, likely via disruption of osteoclast energy metabolism. It is used for Paget disease of bone and, in veterinary medicine, for navicular syndrome in horses. A notable adverse effect is upper gastrointestinal irritation; esophageal abnormalities are a contraindication to oral use.
tail_desc: Osteonecrosis of the jaw is a necrotic bone disorder of the maxillofacial skeleton characterized by exposed or nonhealing jawbone, most often associated with antiresorptive or antiangiogenic therapy, dental extraction, or radiation in susceptible patients. It causes pain, swelling, infection, loosening teeth, and poor oral healing, especially in cancer or osteoporosis patients. Management is usually conservative with oral hygiene, antimicrobial therapy, pain control, and selective surgical debridement.

head: Cidofovir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cidofovir is an acyclic nucleoside phosphonate antiviral, a cytidine analog active against DNA viruses. After intracellular phosphorylation to cidofovir diphosphate, it inhibits viral DNA polymerase and suppresses viral DNA synthesis. It is approved for cytomegalovirus retinitis in patients with AIDS. A notable toxicity is dose-limiting nephrotoxicity, so use requires caution in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Telithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telithromycin is a ketolide antibiotic, structurally related to macrolides, that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosomal subunit, particularly 23S rRNA. It has been used for mild to moderate community-acquired pneumonia caused by susceptible organisms. A notable safety concern is hepatotoxicity; it is also contraindicated in patients with myasthenia gravis because it can precipitate life-threatening respiratory failure.
tail_desc: Diarrheal disease is a gastrointestinal disorder characterized by the passage of loose or watery stools, usually from intestinal infection, toxins, malabsorption, or inflammatory processes that impair fluid absorption and increase secretion. It commonly causes frequent stools, abdominal cramps, nausea, and dehydration, especially in young children and older adults. Management centers on oral or intravenous rehydration, electrolyte replacement, nutritional support, and treating the underlying cause when identified.

head: Hematin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hematin (heme) is an iron-containing porphyrin heme preparation used as a heme replacement product. It suppresses hepatic delta-aminolevulinic acid synthase 1 (ALAS1), reducing accumulation of neurotoxic heme precursors. It is used to treat acute attacks of hepatic porphyrias, particularly acute intermittent porphyria. A notable adverse effect is phlebitis/thrombophlebitis at the infusion site.
tail_desc: Gastroenteritis is an acute inflammatory disorder of the stomach and intestines, usually caused by viral, bacterial, or parasitic infection or their toxins. It typically presents with diarrhea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, nausea, and sometimes fever or dehydration, affecting all ages but especially children and older adults. Management is mainly supportive with oral or intravenous rehydration, with targeted antimicrobials only in selected cases.

head: Sulfanilamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfanilamide is a sulfonamide antibacterial (p-aminobenzenesulfonamide) that acts as a structural analog of para-aminobenzoic acid, competitively inhibiting bacterial dihydropteroate synthase and thereby blocking folate synthesis. It has established topical use for susceptible bacterial infections, historically including vaginal infections. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe sulfonamide skin reactions such as Stevens–Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Hematologic disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the blood, bone marrow, lymphatic system, and hemostatic pathways. It includes malignant, inherited, immune, and acquired conditions that disrupt blood cell production, function, or survival, or coagulation. Common features include anemia, bleeding, thrombosis, infection susceptibility, lymphadenopathy, or splenomegaly, across all ages. Management depends on cause and may involve supportive care, transfusion, immunotherapy, anticoagulation, chemotherapy, or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Labetalol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Labetalol is a mixed adrenergic antagonist of the amino alcohol class that blocks β1-, β2-, and α1-adrenergic receptors, lowering heart rate and peripheral vascular resistance. It is used for hypertension, including severe hypertension and hypertension in pregnancy. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma, overt heart failure, and greater-than-first-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Duloxetine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Duloxetine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is approved for major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, and chronic musculoskeletal pain. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity; concomitant use with monoamine oxidase inhibitors is contraindicated.
tail_desc: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by recurrent episodes of mania or hypomania and depression. Its cause is multifactorial, involving genetic susceptibility and dysregulation of brain neurotransmitter and circadian systems. It typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood and is managed with mood stabilizers, atypical antipsychotics, psychotherapy, and relapse prevention.

head: Octreotide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Octreotide is a synthetic somatostatin analog that activates somatostatin receptors, especially SSTR2 and SSTR5, to suppress secretion of growth hormone, glucagon, insulin, serotonin, and other peptides. It is used for acromegaly, symptomatic control of carcinoid syndrome and vasoactive intestinal peptide-secreting tumors, and to reduce bleeding from esophageal varices. A notable adverse effect is gallstone formation.
tail_desc: Cholelithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, characterized by stone formation in the gallbladder, usually from cholesterol supersaturation or, less often, pigment precipitation. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease typically causes episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cases.

head: Apraclonidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Apraclonidine is a topical ophthalmic imidazoline and relatively selective alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonist that lowers intraocular pressure mainly by reducing aqueous humor production and partly increasing uveoscleral outflow. It is used short-term to prevent or control pressure spikes after anterior segment laser procedures and as adjunctive therapy in glaucoma. A notable adverse effect is allergic blepharoconjunctivitis.
tail_desc: Ascorbic acid deficiency, or scurvy, is a nutritional deficiency disorder affecting connective tissue, skin, bone, and blood vessels due to impaired collagen synthesis from lack of vitamin C. It causes fatigue, gingival swelling or bleeding, perifollicular hemorrhages, petechiae, poor wound healing, and bone pain, especially in malnourished, alcoholic, elderly, or restrictive-diet patients. Management is vitamin C replacement and correction of the underlying dietary deficiency.

head: Heparin, bovine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin, bovine, is an animal-derived unfractionated heparin, a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant obtained from bovine tissues. It potentiates antithrombin III, accelerating inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism and other settings requiring anticoagulation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Diabetic retinopathy is a microvascular complication of diabetes affecting the retina. Chronic hyperglycemia damages retinal capillaries, causing leakage, ischemia, and pathologic neovascularization. It typically occurs in people with type 1 or type 2 diabetes and may present with blurred vision, floaters, macular edema, or progressive vision loss, though early disease can be asymptomatic. Management focuses on glycemic and blood pressure control, regular retinal screening, and ophthalmic treatment such as laser therapy or intravitreal anti-VEGF agents.

head: Nitrofurantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofurantoin is a nitrofuran antibacterial used primarily for treatment and prophylaxis of uncomplicated lower urinary tract infections. In bacteria, nitroreductases reduce it to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, ribosomal proteins, and other macromolecules, so it has no single primary molecular target. A notable risk is pulmonary toxicity; it is contraindicated at term in pregnancy and in significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Vitamin B12 deficiency is a nutritional or malabsorptive disorder affecting hematologic and neurologic systems, caused by inadequate cobalamin intake, impaired absorption (for example pernicious anemia), or gastrointestinal disease. It commonly causes megaloblastic anemia, glossitis, paresthesias, gait disturbance, and cognitive changes, especially in older adults, vegans, or patients with malabsorption. Management involves identifying the cause and replacing vitamin B12, orally or parenterally.

head: Gemifloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Gemifloxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It has been used for treatment of acute bacterial exacerbations of chronic bronchitis and mild-to-moderate community-acquired pneumonia. A notable adverse effect is tendonitis and tendon rupture, and it is generally avoided in patients with known fluoroquinolone hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Busulfan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Busulfan is an alkylating antineoplastic of the alkyl sulfonate family that forms DNA-DNA and DNA-protein crosslinks, causing myeloablation and cell death without a single specific molecular target. It is used in chronic myeloid leukemia and, more commonly, as conditioning before hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. A notable serious toxicity is prolonged bone marrow suppression; seizures and hepatic veno-occlusive disease can also occur.
tail_desc: Postinflammatory pulmonary fibrosis is a chronic interstitial lung disorder of the respiratory system in which prior pulmonary inflammation, such as severe infection or other inflammatory lung injury, heals with fibrotic scarring of the lung parenchyma. Patients may develop persistent dyspnea, dry cough, reduced exercise tolerance, and restrictive impairment on testing. Management focuses on treating the underlying cause, supportive care, and monitoring respiratory function.

head: Ethionamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethionamide is a thioamide antitubercular agent, structurally related to isoniazid, used mainly for multidrug-resistant tuberculosis. It is a prodrug activated by the mycobacterial monooxygenase EthA; the active metabolite inhibits InhA, blocking mycolic acid synthesis and cell-wall formation. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and gastrointestinal intolerance is common.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Aluminium phosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aluminium phosphate is an aluminum salt used as a non-systemic antacid and as a vaccine adjuvant. As an antacid, it neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach, reducing acidity without a specific molecular target. Established uses include symptomatic relief of hyperacidity, heartburn, and peptic ulcer disease. Notable risks include constipation and, particularly in renal impairment, aluminum accumulation.
tail_desc: Hypophosphatemia is an electrolyte/metabolic disorder of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, muscle, and neuromuscular function, caused by inadequate intestinal absorption, intracellular phosphate shifts, or excessive renal losses. Manifestations range from weakness, bone pain, and osteomalacia or rickets to severe respiratory, neurologic, or cardiac dysfunction. Management targets the cause and typically includes oral or intravenous phosphate replacement and correction of associated vitamin D deficiency.

head: Ranolazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ranolazine is a piperazine-derived antianginal agent that inhibits the late inward sodium current (late INa) in cardiac myocytes, reducing intracellular sodium and secondary calcium overload. It is approved for chronic angina, typically as add-on therapy. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, and it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inhibitors or in significant hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Glucosamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glucosamine is an endogenous amino monosaccharide and dietary supplement used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, especially of the knee. Its mechanism is not well established; it is thought to serve as a substrate for glycosaminoglycan and proteoglycan synthesis in cartilage, without a clearly defined primary molecular target. Adverse effects are usually mild gastrointestinal symptoms, and caution is advised in patients with shellfish allergy depending on product source.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Varenicline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Varenicline is a smoking-cessation agent, a synthetic nicotinic receptor partial agonist structurally distinct from nicotine. It acts primarily at the α4β2 neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, reducing nicotine craving and withdrawal while attenuating nicotine’s rewarding effects. It is approved to aid smoking cessation; notable adverse effects include nausea, and it is used cautiously in patients with severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by recurrent episodes of mania or hypomania and depression. Its cause is multifactorial, involving genetic susceptibility and dysregulation of brain neurotransmitter and circadian systems. It typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood and is managed with mood stabilizers, atypical antipsychotics, psychotherapy, and relapse prevention.

head: Ritodrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ritodrine is a beta-adrenergic agonist, relatively selective for β2 receptors, used as a tocolytic. By stimulating β2-adrenoceptors in uterine smooth muscle, it increases cAMP and promotes relaxation, reducing uterine contractions. It has been used to delay preterm labor. Notable adverse effects include maternal tachycardia and palpitations, and it is contraindicated in significant maternal cardiac disease.
tail_desc: Preeclampsia is a hypertensive disorder of pregnancy affecting the maternal vascular, renal, hepatic, and neurologic systems, typically arising after 20 weeks’ gestation. It is linked to abnormal placentation and widespread endothelial dysfunction, causing new-onset hypertension with proteinuria or end-organ injury, often with edema, headache, or visual symptoms. Management centers on maternal-fetal monitoring, blood pressure control, seizure prophylaxis when severe, and timely delivery.

head: Ambrisentan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ambrisentan is an orally active endothelin receptor antagonist of the propanoic acid class that selectively blocks the endothelin type A (ETA) receptor, reducing endothelin-1–mediated vasoconstriction and smooth muscle proliferation. It is approved for pulmonary arterial hypertension to improve exercise capacity and delay clinical worsening. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of embryo-fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Sufentanil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sufentanil is a potent synthetic opioid analgesic of the fentanyl (phenylpiperidine) class that acts primarily as a mu-opioid receptor agonist, producing profound analgesia and sedation. It is used for induction and maintenance of anesthesia and for perioperative analgesia, including epidural use in some settings. A notable adverse effect is dose-dependent respiratory depression.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Cysteamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cysteamine is an aminothiol and cystine-depleting agent used primarily to treat nephropathic cystinosis. Within lysosomes, it reacts with cystine to form cysteine and a cysteine-cysteamine mixed disulfide, enabling export through existing lysine transport pathways and reducing cystine accumulation. It is also used ophthalmically for corneal cystine crystals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and sulfurous body odor.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Methohexital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methohexital is an ultrashort-acting barbiturate intravenous anesthetic, a methylated oxybarbiturate, that enhances inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission primarily by positively modulating GABA_A receptors in the CNS. It is used for induction of general anesthesia and procedural sedation, including for electroconvulsive therapy. A notable risk is respiratory depression; it is contraindicated in patients with acute intermittent porphyria.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Adomiparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Adomiparin is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Intracranial hemorrhage is acute bleeding within the skull affecting the central nervous system, including intracerebral, subarachnoid, subdural, and epidural spaces. It usually results from ruptured vessels due to hypertension, trauma, aneurysm, vascular malformation, or coagulopathy. Patients, often older adults or trauma patients, present with sudden headache, focal neurologic deficits, vomiting, reduced consciousness, or seizures. Management is emergent, focusing on stabilization, blood pressure and coagulation control, and neurosurgical intervention when indicated.

head: Ropivacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ropivacaine is a long-acting amide-type local anesthetic, the pure S-enantiomer of a pipecoloxylidide derivative. It blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, inhibiting impulse conduction to produce local or regional anesthesia and analgesia. It is established for surgical anesthesia and acute pain control, including epidural and peripheral nerve block use. Notable adverse effects include dose-related CNS and cardiovascular toxicity.
tail_desc: Familial visceral myopathy is a rare inherited smooth-muscle disorder of the gastrointestinal and often urinary tract, causing impaired visceral motility. It reflects primary degeneration or dysfunction of the muscularis propria rather than mechanical obstruction. Patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction, abdominal distension, pain, severe constipation or vomiting, and sometimes bladder involvement; management is mainly supportive, with nutritional support, decompression, and selective surgery.

head: Halothane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Halothane is a volatile halogenated hydrocarbon inhalational general anesthetic. Its anesthetic effect is thought to result from nonspecific modulation of multiple CNS ion channels, including enhancement of GABA_A and glycine receptor activity, rather than a single defined target. It has been used for induction and maintenance of general anesthesia. A notable serious adverse effect is rare but potentially fatal halothane-induced hepatitis; it can also trigger malignant hyperthermia in susceptible patients.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Sodium oxybate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium oxybate is the sodium salt of gamma-hydroxybutyrate (GHB), a central nervous system depressant that acts primarily as a GABA_B receptor agonist and also binds GHB-specific sites. It is approved for narcolepsy, particularly cataplexy and excessive daytime sleepiness. A notable risk is profound respiratory and CNS depression, making concomitant alcohol or sedative use contraindicated.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Synephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Synephrine is a naturally occurring protoalkaloid phenylethanolamine, structurally related to ephedrine, found notably in bitter orange (Citrus aurantium). It acts primarily as a sympathomimetic agonist at adrenergic receptors, with reported activity especially at beta-3 and some alpha receptors. It is not an approved prescription drug for established clinical indications in major jurisdictions; use is mainly in supplements. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, so it is contraindicated in significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical system, usually caused by defects in calcium-handling proteins such as RyR2 or CASQ2. It presents in children or young adults with exercise- or emotion-triggered bidirectional or polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest despite a structurally normal heart. Management centers on strict beta-blockade, exercise restriction, flecainide, and selected implantable defibrillators.

head: Butobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butobarbital is a barbiturate sedative-hypnotic, used primarily for the short-term treatment of insomnia and occasionally for preoperative sedation. Like other barbiturates, it enhances GABAergic inhibition by positively modulating the GABA-A receptor, increasing chloride ion influx and depressing central nervous system activity. A notable adverse effect is respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in patients with porphyria.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Alendronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alendronic acid (alendronate) is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive drug. It binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated resorption, primarily by blocking farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway. It is used for osteoporosis and Paget’s disease of bone. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis; it is contraindicated in esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying.
tail_desc: Esophageal ulcer is a mucosal ulcerative lesion of the esophagus, a gastrointestinal disorder most often caused by gastroesophageal reflux, medication-induced injury, infection, or caustic damage. It commonly presents with odynophagia, dysphagia, retrosternal pain, and sometimes bleeding, particularly in patients with severe reflux, immunosuppression, or pill exposure. Management targets the cause, typically including acid suppression, withdrawal of offending agents, and treatment of infectious etiologies when present.

head: Sorafenib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sorafenib is an oral multikinase inhibitor of the biaryl urea class that targets RAF kinases and several receptor tyrosine kinases, including VEGFR, PDGFR, FLT3, RET, and KIT, thereby suppressing tumor-cell proliferation and angiogenesis. It is approved for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma, advanced renal cell carcinoma, and radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is hand-foot skin reaction.
tail_desc: Hypophosphatemia is an electrolyte/metabolic disorder of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, muscle, and neuromuscular function, caused by inadequate intestinal absorption, intracellular phosphate shifts, or excessive renal losses. Manifestations range from weakness, bone pain, and osteomalacia or rickets to severe respiratory, neurologic, or cardiac dysfunction. Management targets the cause and typically includes oral or intravenous phosphate replacement and correction of associated vitamin D deficiency.

head: Quinethazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quinethazone is a quinazoline-sulfonamide thiazide-like diuretic. It inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for edema and hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria and should be used cautiously in sulfonamide-sensitive patients.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Selegiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Selegiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class. By inhibiting MAO-B, it reduces dopamine breakdown in the central nervous system. It is established for Parkinson disease and, in transdermal form, major depressive disorder. Notable risks include insomnia and potentially serious hypertensive or serotonergic interactions with contraindicated sympathomimetic or serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Penbutolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Penbutolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at β1 and β2 receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is generally contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: Cerebral infarction is an ischemic stroke of the central nervous system in which interrupted cerebral blood flow, usually from arterial thrombosis or embolism, causes focal brain tissue necrosis. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or visual loss, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible and secondary stroke prevention.

head: Beta carotene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (a disease)
head_desc: Beta carotene is a carotenoid provitamin A and antioxidant that is enzymatically cleaved to retinal/retinol, supporting vitamin A–dependent visual and epithelial functions; it has no single defined pharmacologic receptor target. It is used as a dietary supplement to prevent or treat vitamin A deficiency. High-dose supplementation is associated with increased lung cancer risk in smokers and asbestos-exposed individuals.
tail_desc: Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin is a benign inherited hemoglobinopathy of the hematologic system caused by genetic variants that sustain gamma-globin expression and continued HbF production after birth. Affected individuals, often identified incidentally, are usually asymptomatic with normal or mild microcytic blood findings; the condition can lessen severity of sickle cell disease or beta-thalassemia. Management is typically reassurance, diagnosis confirmation, and genetic counseling.

head: Zinc gluconate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zinc gluconate is an oral zinc salt supplement and trace element preparation. It supplies zinc ions, an essential cofactor for numerous metalloenzymes and transcription factors, thereby correcting or preventing zinc deficiency rather than acting through a single specific molecular target. It is used for prevention and treatment of zinc deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, especially nausea; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder of copper metabolism affecting mainly the liver and nervous system, caused by ATP7B mutations that impair biliary copper excretion and lead to toxic copper accumulation. It typically presents in children, adolescents, or young adults with hepatitis, cirrhosis, tremor, dystonia, psychiatric symptoms, and Kayser-Fleischer rings. Management centers on lifelong copper chelation or zinc therapy, dietary copper reduction, and liver transplantation for advanced hepatic failure.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the colonic mucosa, typically beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. It reflects immune-mediated intestinal inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals. Common features include bloody diarrhea, urgency, abdominal pain, and relapsing course, often in adolescents and adults. Management focuses on anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapy, with colectomy for refractory or complicated disease.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Apraclonidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Apraclonidine is a topical ophthalmic imidazoline and relatively selective alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonist that lowers intraocular pressure mainly by reducing aqueous humor production and partly increasing uveoscleral outflow. It is used short-term to prevent or control pressure spikes after anterior segment laser procedures and as adjunctive therapy in glaucoma. A notable adverse effect is allergic blepharoconjunctivitis.
tail_desc: Scurvy is a nutritional deficiency disorder affecting connective tissue and blood vessels, caused by prolonged vitamin C deficiency leading to impaired collagen synthesis. It classically presents with fatigue, gingival swelling and bleeding, perifollicular hemorrhages, petechiae, poor wound healing, and bone pain, especially in malnourished adults, infants, or older people. Management is vitamin C replacement and correction of dietary deficiency.

head: Biotin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Biotin (vitamin B7, vitamin H) is a water-soluble B-complex vitamin used as a nutritional supplement and replacement therapy for biotin deficiency, including deficiency associated with inherited biotinidase deficiency or prolonged parenteral nutrition. It functions as an essential cofactor for several carboxylase enzymes involved in fatty acid synthesis, gluconeogenesis, and amino acid metabolism. It is generally well tolerated, but high-dose biotin can interfere with certain laboratory immunoassays, causing misleading test results.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Desipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant, specifically a secondary amine dibenzazepine, that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake via the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with less effect on serotonin uptake. It is used for major depressive disorder and has also been used for neuropathic pain in some settings. A notable risk is cardiotoxicity, including arrhythmias, especially in overdose.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Sertraline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sertraline is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant of the naphthylamine/tetralin class that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, panic disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, social anxiety disorder, and premenstrual dysphoric disorder. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with monoamine oxidase inhibitors, which are contraindicated.
tail_desc: Inappropriate ADH syndrome, or syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH), is a water-balance disorder of the endocrine and renal systems caused by excess ADH release or action, leading to impaired free-water excretion, dilutional hyponatremia, and low serum osmolality. Patients, often with CNS, pulmonary, drug-related, or malignant triggers, may have nausea, confusion, seizures, or coma; management targets the cause and restricts fluids, with careful sodium correction.

head: Beclomethasone dipropionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Beclomethasone dipropionate is a synthetic corticosteroid (glucocorticoid prodrug) that is converted to beclomethasone-17-monopropionate and activates the glucocorticoid receptor, suppressing inflammatory gene expression and mediator release. It is used for maintenance treatment of asthma, allergic rhinitis, and inflammatory bowel disease in certain formulations. A notable adverse effect is oropharyngeal candidiasis with inhaled use.
tail_desc: Herpes simplex virus keratitis is an infectious corneal disease of the eye caused by herpes simplex virus, usually HSV-1, through primary infection or reactivation in the trigeminal ganglion. It typically presents with unilateral eye pain, redness, tearing, photophobia, blurred vision, and characteristic dendritic corneal ulcers, affecting both children and adults. Management centers on topical or oral antiviral therapy, with careful ophthalmologic follow-up to limit scarring and vision loss.

head: Acetaminophen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) is a non-opioid analgesic and antipyretic of the para-aminophenol class. Its mechanism is not fully defined but is thought to involve central inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis, likely via cyclooxygenase pathways in the CNS. It is widely used for mild to moderate pain and fever. A notable adverse effect is dose-dependent hepatotoxicity, especially in overdose or severe liver disease.
tail_desc: Hepatic encephalopathy is a neuropsychiatric complication of acute or chronic liver failure affecting the central nervous system. It results from impaired hepatic detoxification and portosystemic shunting, leading to accumulation of neurotoxins, especially ammonia. Patients, often with cirrhosis, develop confusion, altered consciousness, asterixis, and sometimes coma; management targets precipitating factors and lowers gut-derived ammonia, commonly with lactulose and rifaximin.

head: Calcium glycerophosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium salt of glycerophosphoric acid used as a mineral supplement and urinary/food acid neutralizing agent. It dissociates to provide calcium and phosphate, without a specific primary molecular target. It is used in some formulations to reduce dietary acid effects and as a calcium source. Adverse effects may include hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Ranitidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ranitidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It was used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and other acid-related disorders. A notable adverse effect is headache, and use became widely withdrawn or suspended in many markets because of NDMA contamination concerns.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Alanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alanine is a nonessential, proteinogenic alpha-amino acid used in amino acid formulations for parenteral or enteral nutrition. It has no specific drug-like molecular target; rather, it serves as a metabolic substrate in protein synthesis and glucose-alanine nitrogen transport. Its established clinical use is nutritional supplementation within amino acid mixtures. Excessive administration may contribute to amino acid imbalance or metabolic disturbances, particularly in severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Irbesartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irbesartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl tetrazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and diabetic nephropathy in patients with type 2 diabetes. A notable contraindication is pregnancy because it can cause fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Acquired angioedema is a rare bradykinin-mediated disorder of the vascular/immune system causing recurrent, nonpruritic swelling of the skin and submucosa, often involving the face, extremities, gastrointestinal tract, or airway. It usually occurs in adults due to acquired C1 inhibitor deficiency or autoantibodies, sometimes linked to lymphoproliferative or autoimmune disease. Management focuses on treating acute attacks, airway protection, and addressing the underlying disorder.

head: Sulfadoxine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadoxine is a long-acting sulfonamide antimicrobial and antimalarial antifolate that inhibits parasitic and bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used in fixed combination with pyrimethamine for treatment and intermittent preventive therapy of malaria due to susceptible Plasmodium falciparum. A notable risk is severe hypersensitivity, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome; sulfonamide allergy is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Atenolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker, belonging to the beta-blocker class. It primarily antagonizes beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate, contractility, and blood pressure. It is used for hypertension, angina pectoris, and management after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in sinus bradycardia, heart block, and cardiogenic shock.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Maprotiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Maprotiline is a tetracyclic antidepressant that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake by blocking the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively weak effects on serotonin uptake. It has been used for depressive disorders, including major depression with anxiety features. A notable risk is dose-related seizures, and it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or during the acute recovery phase after myocardial infarction.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Calcium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcium acetate is a calcium salt used as a phosphate binder. In the gastrointestinal tract it dissociates to release calcium, which binds dietary phosphate to form insoluble complexes and lowers phosphate absorption; it has no specific receptor target. It is established for control of hyperphosphatemia in patients with end-stage renal disease, and a notable risk is hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Adenosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Adenosine is an endogenous purine nucleoside antiarrhythmic that activates cardiac A1 adenosine receptors, increasing potassium conductance and suppressing cAMP-dependent calcium currents to transiently slow sinoatrial and atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used acutely to terminate paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia and in pharmacologic cardiac stress testing. Notable adverse effects include brief flushing, chest discomfort, and bronchospasm; it is contraindicated in second- or third-degree AV block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Pericarditis is an inflammatory disease of the pericardium, the sac surrounding the heart, usually affecting the cardiovascular system. It commonly results from viral infection, autoimmune disease, uremia, myocardial injury, or may be idiopathic. Typical features include sharp pleuritic chest pain, a pericardial friction rub, and sometimes pericardial effusion, occurring in adults of any age. Management generally targets the cause and uses anti-inflammatory therapy, with urgent drainage if tamponade develops.

head: Bendamustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bendamustine is a bifunctional alkylating antineoplastic agent of the nitrogen mustard class with a benzimidazole ring. It forms DNA crosslinks, causing DNA damage and apoptosis, without a single specific molecular target. It is used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia and indolent B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma, including rituximab-refractory disease. Notable toxicities include myelosuppression and serious infections.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Alprostadil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alprostadil is a prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) analog and vasodilatory smooth-muscle relaxant that activates prostaglandin E receptors, increasing cAMP. It is used to maintain ductus arteriosus patency in certain congenital heart defects and to treat erectile dysfunction. Notable adverse effects include hypotension; it is contraindicated in neonates with respiratory distress syndrome without cyanotic heart disease.
tail_desc: Balanitis xerotica obliterans is a chronic inflammatory sclerosing dermatosis of the male genital skin, generally regarded as genital lichen sclerosus affecting the glans and foreskin. It causes porcelain-white atrophic plaques, scarring phimosis, painful fissuring, meatal or urethral narrowing, and urinary symptoms, mainly in uncircumcised males. Management centers on potent topical corticosteroids, with circumcision or surgery for refractory disease or stenosis.

head: Arginine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Arginine (L-arginine) is a basic, semi-essential amino acid used as a nutritional/pharmaceutical supplement. It serves as the endogenous substrate for nitric oxide synthase, increasing nitric oxide production, and also participates in the urea cycle. Established uses include treatment of urea cycle disorders and supplementation in arginine deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Docusate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Docusate is an anionic surfactant stool softener, commonly used as the sodium or calcium salt. It acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract by lowering stool surface tension and promoting water and lipid penetration into feces; no specific molecular target is established. It is used for prevention and treatment of constipation, especially when straining should be avoided. Adverse effects include abdominal cramping and diarrhea.
tail_desc: Chronic atrophic gastritis is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the gastric mucosa in the digestive system, characterized by loss of gastric glands with mucosal atrophy, often due to Helicobacter pylori infection or autoimmune destruction of parietal cells. Patients may be asymptomatic or have dyspepsia, anemia, or vitamin B12 deficiency; management targets the cause, corrects deficiencies, and includes surveillance in selected patients.

head: Chlorphentermine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorphentermine is a sympathomimetic anorectic of the amphetamine/phenethylamine family formerly used as an anti-obesity agent. It acts primarily by increasing central monoaminergic signaling, especially promoting norepinephrine release to suppress appetite, though its precise molecular targets are not well defined. Its clinical use has largely been abandoned because of serious pulmonary toxicity, notably phospholipidosis.
tail_desc: Psychotic disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting brain function and behavior, defined by impaired reality testing due to diverse causes, including primary psychotic illnesses, mood disorders, substance use, or medical conditions. Core features include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking or behavior, and reduced insight, usually emerging in adolescents or adults. Management focuses on diagnostic evaluation, treatment of underlying causes, antipsychotic medication, psychotherapy, and safety/supportive care.

head: Silver sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Silver sulfadiazine is a topical sulfonamide antimicrobial combining sulfadiazine with silver ions. It acts mainly by releasing silver, which disrupts bacterial cell membranes and proteins, while sulfadiazine inhibits folate synthesis via dihydropteroate synthase. It is established for prevention and treatment of wound sepsis in second- and third-degree burns. A notable contraindication is use in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: obsolete superimposed infection is a disease or medical condition.

head: Levocarnitine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Levocarnitine (L-carnitine) is a naturally occurring quaternary ammonium compound used as a carnitine replacement agent. It restores deficient carnitine needed for mitochondrial transport of long-chain fatty acids via the carnitine shuttle, rather than acting at a single receptor target. It is indicated for primary systemic carnitine deficiency and secondary carnitine deficiency, including in end-stage renal disease on dialysis; notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Disopyramide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Disopyramide is a class IA antiarrhythmic, structurally related to quinidine, that primarily blocks fast cardiac voltage-gated sodium channels to slow conduction and prolong the action potential and refractory period. It is used for documented ventricular arrhythmias and certain supraventricular arrhythmias, and is also used in obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Notable adverse effects include strong anticholinergic effects and negative inotropy, making it contraindicated in overt heart failure.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Doripenem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Doripenem is a broad-spectrum carbapenem beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, particularly PBP2 and PBP3. It has been used for complicated intra-abdominal and complicated urinary tract infections, including pyelonephritis. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, including serious allergic reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with known severe beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Zolpidem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zolpidem is a nonbenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic of the imidazopyridine class that acts as a positive allosteric modulator at the GABA-A receptor, with relative selectivity for benzodiazepine-1 (omega-1) sites containing the alpha1 subunit. It is approved for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include next-day impairment and complex sleep behaviors; it is contraindicated in patients with prior such episodes.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Tenofovir disoproxil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tenofovir disoproxil is an oral prodrug of tenofovir, an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NtRTI). After conversion to tenofovir diphosphate, it competitively inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase, causing DNA chain termination. It is used to treat HIV-1 infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable adverse effect is renal toxicity, including proximal tubulopathy/Fanconi syndrome.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Methohexital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methohexital is an ultrashort-acting barbiturate intravenous anesthetic, a methylated oxybarbiturate, that enhances inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission primarily by positively modulating GABA_A receptors in the CNS. It is used for induction of general anesthesia and procedural sedation, including for electroconvulsive therapy. A notable risk is respiratory depression; it is contraindicated in patients with acute intermittent porphyria.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Phentermine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phentermine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, structurally related to amphetamine, used as a short-term adjunct to diet and exercise for exogenous obesity. It promotes weight loss primarily by increasing central norepinephrine release, with lesser effects on dopamine and serotonin, reducing appetite via hypothalamic pathways. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and heart rate, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or within 14 days of MAOI use.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Exenatide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Exenatide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, a synthetic incretin mimetic based on exendin-4. It activates the GLP-1 receptor to enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite. It is approved for type 2 diabetes mellitus, including extended-release formulations. Notable adverse effects include nausea and vomiting; it is contraindicated in patients with a history of severe hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Cholelithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, characterized by stone formation in the gallbladder, usually from cholesterol supersaturation or, less often, pigment precipitation. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease typically causes episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cases.

head: Dronabinol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dronabinol is synthetic delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), a cannabinoid receptor agonist that primarily activates CB1 and CB2 receptors in the central and peripheral nervous systems. It is approved to treat chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting refractory to conventional antiemetics and anorexia-associated weight loss in patients with AIDS. A notable adverse effect is dose-related neuropsychiatric toxicity, including dizziness, euphoria, or confusion.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Sulfamethoxazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamethoxazole is a sulfonamide antibacterial that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby DNA precursor production; it is commonly combined with trimethoprim for synergistic activity.
