Established uses include urinary tract infections, acute otitis media, bronchitis exacerbations, shigellosis, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia treatment or prophylaxis. Notable risks include severe hypersensitivity reactions such as Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked inherited red blood cell enzymopathy causing reduced protection against oxidative stress, leading to episodic hemolytic anemia. It commonly affects males, especially in malaria-endemic populations, and may present with neonatal jaundice, pallor, dark urine, fatigue, and jaundice after infections, fava beans, or oxidant drugs. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and supportive care during hemolysis.

head: Diphenoxylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diphenoxylate is a synthetic opioid antidiarrheal, structurally related to meperidine, that acts mainly as a peripheral mu-opioid receptor agonist in the intestine to reduce gastrointestinal motility. It is used, typically with atropine, as adjunctive therapy for diarrhea. Notable risks include central nervous system and respiratory depression in overdose, and it is contraindicated in diarrhea caused by invasive enteric infection.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Cysteamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cysteamine is an aminothiol and cystine-depleting agent used primarily to treat nephropathic cystinosis. Within lysosomes, it reacts with cystine to form cysteine and a cysteine-cysteamine mixed disulfide, enabling export through existing lysine transport pathways and reducing cystine accumulation. It is also used ophthalmically for corneal cystine crystals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and sulfurous body odor.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Resorcinol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Resorcinol is a dihydroxybenzene phenol used topically as a keratolytic and antiseptic. Its effects are nonspecific, involving protein denaturation and disruption of keratinized epidermal tissue rather than a defined molecular target. It has been used in acne, seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, psoriasis, corns, calluses, and warts. Notable adverse effects include local irritation; excessive topical exposure may cause systemic toxicity, including methemoglobinemia.
tail_desc: Peripheral arterial disease is an atherosclerotic vascular disorder of the peripheral arteries, most often affecting the lower limbs. Arterial narrowing reduces blood flow, causing exertional leg pain (intermittent claudication), diminished pulses, and in advanced cases rest pain, ulcers, or tissue loss. It is common in older adults and smokers. Management focuses on risk-factor modification, antiplatelet and statin therapy, supervised exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Acetic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acetic acid is a simple carboxylic acid used topically as an acidifying antimicrobial/antiseptic agent. Its activity is nonspecific, lowering local pH and thereby inhibiting growth of susceptible bacteria and fungi rather than acting through a single defined molecular target. Established uses include treatment of otitis externa and bladder irrigation to help reduce catheter-associated bacteriuria. Notable adverse effects include local irritation, burning, and pain.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Calcium lactate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcium lactate is an oral calcium salt used as a mineral supplement and antacid. It dissociates to provide elemental calcium, supporting neuromuscular function, bone mineralization, and correction of calcium deficiency, without a specific receptor target. Established uses include prevention or treatment of hypocalcemia and calcium supplementation. Notable risks include hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Digitalis poisoning is a toxic cardiovascular condition caused by excessive exposure to cardiac glycosides such as digoxin, producing inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase and resulting electrolyte-mediated conduction abnormalities. It typically affects patients receiving digoxin, especially older adults or those with renal impairment, and presents with nausea, vomiting, confusion, visual disturbances, bradyarrhythmias, or ventricular arrhythmias. Management centers on stopping the drug, correcting electrolytes, monitoring rhythm, and using digoxin-specific antibody fragments in severe cases.

head: Griseofulvin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to CSF3R deficiency (a disease)
head_desc: Griseofulvin is an oral antifungal antibiotic derived from Penicillium, used mainly for dermatophyte infections of the skin, hair, and nails (tinea). It binds fungal tubulin and disrupts microtubule function, inhibiting mitosis, and concentrates in keratin precursor cells to make new tissue resistant to invasion. Notable adverse effects include headache and hepatotoxicity; it is contraindicated in porphyria and severe liver failure.
tail_desc: Autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to CSF3R deficiency is a rare inherited hematologic and immune disorder caused by biallelic loss-of-function variants in CSF3R, the receptor for granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, leading to profound neutropenia from impaired granulopoiesis and poor response to G-CSF. Affected infants or children present with recurrent severe bacterial infections, mucosal inflammation, and fever; management centers on infection prevention and treatment, with hematopoietic stem cell transplantation considered in severe cases.

head: Vancomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vancomycin is a glycopeptide antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding the D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors, blocking polymerization and cross-linking, mainly against Gram-positive organisms. It is used for serious MRSA and other susceptible Gram-positive infections, and orally for Clostridioides difficile colitis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and infusion-related “red man syndrome.”
tail_desc: Hearing disorder is a broad category of auditory-system conditions that impair hearing through conductive, sensorineural, or mixed mechanisms affecting the outer, middle, inner ear, or auditory nerve pathways. Common features include reduced hearing sensitivity, difficulty understanding speech, and sometimes tinnitus, occurring in children or adults from congenital, infectious, traumatic, ototoxic, noise-related, or age-related causes. Management depends on cause and severity, including treating reversible pathology, hearing rehabilitation, and assistive devices such as hearing aids or cochlear implants.

head: Tenofovir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tenofovir is an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor, used clinically as the prodrugs tenofovir disoproxil and tenofovir alafenamide. After intracellular activation to tenofovir diphosphate, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase by competing with natural nucleotides and causing DNA chain termination. It is approved for HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B; notable risks include renal toxicity and reduced bone mineral density.
tail_desc: obsolete pathologic fracture is a disease or medical condition.

head: Sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadiazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections and, with pyrimethamine, for toxoplasmosis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe rash such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome; it is generally contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy and near term pregnancy or in neonates due to kernicterus risk.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Bicalutamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bicalutamide is a nonsteroidal antiandrogen of the anilide class that competitively binds the androgen receptor, blocking androgen-dependent signaling. It is used, typically in combination with a luteinizing hormone-releasing hormone analog or after orchiectomy, for treatment of prostate cancer. A notable adverse effect is gynecomastia; it is also associated with hepatotoxicity, so liver dysfunction is a key caution.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Pregabalin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pregabalin is a gabapentinoid anticonvulsant and analgesic, a structural analogue of gamma-aminobutyric acid that binds the α2δ subunit of presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release. It is used for neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, adjunctive treatment of partial-onset seizures, and generalized anxiety disorder in some regions. Common adverse effects include dizziness and somnolence.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Moxifloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Moxifloxacin is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including community-acquired pneumonia, acute bacterial sinusitis, acute exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and some skin or intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation; fluoroquinolones can also cause tendon rupture.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Teniposide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Teniposide is a semisynthetic podophyllotoxin derivative and epipodophyllotoxin antineoplastic agent. It inhibits DNA topoisomerase II, causing DNA strand breaks and cell-cycle arrest. Teniposide has established use, typically in combination chemotherapy, for refractory childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression, particularly leukopenia, and it should be used cautiously in patients with significant bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Infectious disease is a broad category of disorders caused by pathogenic microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, or prions, affecting virtually any organ system. It results from microbial invasion and host inflammatory response, with manifestations ranging from fever and malaise to organ-specific symptoms. It can occur in any population, especially the very young, elderly, or immunocompromised, and is managed with pathogen-directed therapy, supportive care, and prevention measures such as hygiene, vaccination, and isolation.

head: Arginine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Arginine (L-arginine) is a basic, semi-essential amino acid used as a nutritional/pharmaceutical supplement. It serves as the endogenous substrate for nitric oxide synthase, increasing nitric oxide production, and also participates in the urea cycle. Established uses include treatment of urea cycle disorders and supplementation in arginine deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Chronic kidney disease is a progressive disorder of the renal system defined by persistent kidney damage or reduced glomerular filtration for at least three months, commonly caused by diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. It often affects older adults and may present with albuminuria, edema, anemia, hypertension, and electrolyte abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause, slowing progression, reducing cardiovascular risk, and preparing for dialysis or transplantation in advanced stages.

head: Phylloquinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) is a fat-soluble naphthoquinone vitamin used as a hemostatic antidote to vitamin K deficiency and warfarin-associated hypoprothrombinemia. It acts as a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase in the liver, enabling activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. Notable risks include anaphylactoid reactions with rapid intravenous administration.
tail_desc: Female breast carcinoma is a malignant epithelial tumor of the breast, usually arising from ductal or lobular tissue. It typically affects adult women, especially older women, and may present as a palpable breast mass, skin or nipple changes, or abnormal imaging findings. Management is guided by stage and biomarker status and commonly includes surgery, radiation, endocrine therapy, chemotherapy, and HER2-targeted treatment when indicated.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Atorvastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atorvastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is indicated for hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, and prevention of cardiovascular events. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and during pregnancy.
tail_desc: Congenital hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid axis present at birth, most commonly caused by thyroid dysgenesis or defects in thyroid hormone synthesis, leading to deficient thyroxine. It affects newborns and may cause prolonged jaundice, lethargy, feeding difficulty, constipation, macroglossia, and impaired growth and neurodevelopment if untreated. Management is prompt newborn screening confirmation and early levothyroxine replacement with ongoing monitoring.

head: Romidepsin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Romidepsin is a cyclic peptide histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor prodrug that, after intracellular reduction, inhibits class I HDAC enzymes, increasing histone acetylation and altering gene expression to promote apoptosis. It is approved for relapsed or refractory cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and peripheral T-cell lymphoma. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation, requiring caution in patients with significant cardiac disease or electrolyte abnormalities.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Desirudin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desirudin is a recombinant hirudin analogue and parenteral direct thrombin inhibitor that binds thrombin (factor IIa) with high specificity, blocking conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin and thrombin-mediated coagulation. It is used for prevention of deep vein thrombosis after elective hip replacement surgery. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Diabetic cataract is a lens disorder of the eye occurring in people with diabetes mellitus, caused by chronic hyperglycemia that promotes osmotic and oxidative damage to lens fibers and opacification. It may present earlier than age-related cataract, with progressive blurred vision, glare, and reduced visual acuity. Management centers on good glycemic control and cataract extraction when visual impairment becomes significant.

head: Bismuth subsalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bismuth subsalicylate is a bismuth-containing salicylate antidiarrheal and gastroprotective agent. It acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract, where bismuth has antimicrobial and mucosal protective effects, while salicylate inhibits cyclooxygenase and reduces intestinal prostaglandin-mediated secretion and inflammation. It is used for diarrhea, dyspepsia, heartburn, and nausea. A notable adverse effect is black discoloration of stools and tongue.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Darunavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Darunavir is a second-generation HIV-1 protease inhibitor, a nonpeptidic sulfonamide antiretroviral. It inhibits the viral aspartyl protease, preventing cleavage of Gag-Pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. Boosted with ritonavir or cobicistat, it is indicated for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated with certain CYP3A-dependent drugs due to serious interactions.
tail_desc: Hemophilia is an inherited bleeding disorder of the hematologic/coagulation system, usually caused by deficiency of clotting factor VIII (hemophilia A) or IX (hemophilia B), most often in an X-linked pattern. It causes prolonged bleeding, easy bruising, hemarthroses, and deep muscle or postoperative hemorrhage, typically affecting males. Management centers on replacement of the missing factor, prevention of bleeding, and treatment of complications.

head: Flucytosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flucytosine is a fluorinated pyrimidine antimetabolite antifungal. In susceptible fungi it is taken up by cytosine permease and converted to 5-fluorouracil, which inhibits thymidylate synthase and is incorporated into RNA, disrupting DNA and RNA synthesis. It is used mainly, usually with amphotericin B, for serious Candida and Cryptococcus infections such as cryptococcal meningitis. Bone marrow suppression is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Kostmann syndrome is a severe congenital neutropenia affecting the hematopoietic and immune systems, classically inherited and often linked to HAX1 mutations causing maturation arrest of neutrophil precursors in the bone marrow. It presents from infancy with profound neutropenia, recurrent bacterial infections, oral ulcers, and periodontitis. Management centers on granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, aggressive infection treatment, and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation in refractory cases.

head: Armodafinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Armodafinil is the R-enantiomer of modafinil, a wake-promoting central nervous system agent. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it is known to inhibit the dopamine transporter (DAT), increasing extracellular dopamine. It is approved to improve wakefulness in narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift work disorder. Notable adverse effects include headache and rare serious rash, including Stevens–Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Familial mitral valve prolapse is an inherited cardiovascular disorder affecting the mitral valve, characterized by myxomatous degeneration and superior displacement of one or both leaflets into the left atrium during systole. It often presents in multiple family members with a mid-systolic click, mitral regurgitation, palpitations, chest discomfort, or may be asymptomatic. Management centers on echocardiographic surveillance, symptom control, and valve repair or replacement when regurgitation becomes severe.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Voriconazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Voriconazole is a second-generation triazole antifungal that inhibits fungal cytochrome P450–dependent 14-alpha-sterol demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and disrupting cell membrane formation. It is used for invasive aspergillosis, candidemia and other serious Candida infections, and esophageal candidiasis. A notable adverse effect is visual disturbance; it is also associated with hepatotoxicity and clinically important CYP-mediated drug interactions.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Iopanoic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iopanoic acid is an iodinated radiographic contrast agent in the oral cholecystographic class. It is taken up by hepatocytes and excreted into bile, increasing gallbladder and biliary tract radiopacity for imaging. It was used for oral cholecystography to evaluate gallbladder disease. Because of its high iodine load, it is contraindicated in iodine hypersensitivity and can precipitate thyroid dysfunction.
tail_desc: Sickle cell anemia is an inherited hemoglobinopathy of the blood caused by homozygous HBB mutation producing hemoglobin S, which polymerizes on deoxygenation and leads to red-cell sickling, chronic hemolytic anemia, and vaso-occlusion. It commonly affects people of African ancestry and causes pain crises, jaundice, infection risk, acute chest syndrome, and organ damage. Management includes vaccination, penicillin in childhood, hydroxyurea, transfusions, supportive care, and sometimes stem cell transplantation.

head: Ozanimod (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ozanimod is an oral sphingosine 1-phosphate (S1P) receptor modulator that selectively targets S1P1 and S1P5, reducing lymphocyte egress from lymph nodes and thereby limiting inflammatory cell trafficking. It is approved for relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis and moderately to severely active ulcerative colitis. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia, particularly after treatment initiation.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Orthostatic hypotension is a cardiovascular autonomic disorder marked by an abnormal fall in blood pressure on standing due to impaired baroreflex-mediated vasoconstriction, volume depletion, medications, or autonomic failure. It commonly affects older adults and patients with neurodegenerative or diabetic autonomic neuropathy, causing dizziness, lightheadedness, blurred vision, weakness, or syncope. Management targets underlying causes, medication review, fluid and salt optimization, compression garments, and selected pressor agents.

head: Metyrosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metyrosine is a tyrosine hydroxylase inhibitor and catecholamine synthesis blocker. By inhibiting tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate-limiting enzyme in catecholamine biosynthesis, it lowers production of dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. It is used primarily to manage catecholamine excess in patients with pheochromocytoma, including preoperative preparation. A notable adverse effect is sedation; it is also associated with extrapyramidal symptoms and should be used cautiously in psychiatric illness.
tail_desc: Parkinsonian disorder refers to a group of neurodegenerative movement disorders affecting the central nervous system, characterized by parkinsonism due to dopaminergic pathway dysfunction, often involving degeneration of nigrostriatal neurons. Core features include bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, and postural instability, typically in older adults. Management is mainly symptomatic, using dopaminergic medications, rehabilitation, and treatment of underlying causes when identifiable.

head: Phylloquinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) is a fat-soluble naphthoquinone vitamin used as a hemostatic antidote to vitamin K deficiency and warfarin-associated hypoprothrombinemia. It acts as a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase in the liver, enabling activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. Notable risks include anaphylactoid reactions with rapid intravenous administration.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Zinc oxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zinc oxide is an inorganic zinc compound used topically as a skin protectant and mild astringent. Its effects are primarily physical rather than receptor-mediated, forming a protective barrier on skin and reflecting or scattering ultraviolet radiation in sunscreen formulations, without a defined primary molecular target. Established uses include diaper dermatitis, minor skin irritation, and sun protection; adverse effects are usually limited to local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Primary hyperoxaluria is a rare inherited metabolic disorder of hepatic glyoxylate metabolism that causes oxalate overproduction, primarily affecting the kidneys and urinary tract. It typically presents in childhood or early adulthood with recurrent nephrolithiasis, nephrocalcinosis, progressive kidney impairment, and systemic oxalosis in advanced disease. Management focuses on high fluid intake, crystallization inhibitors, vitamin B6 in responsive cases, and kidney with or without liver transplantation.

head: Desipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant, specifically a secondary amine dibenzazepine, that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake via the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with less effect on serotonin uptake. It is used for major depressive disorder and has also been used for neuropathic pain in some settings. A notable risk is cardiotoxicity, including arrhythmias, especially in overdose.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Nitric Oxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitric oxide is an endogenous gaseous signaling molecule used as an inhaled pulmonary vasodilator. It activates soluble guanylate cyclase in vascular smooth muscle, increasing cyclic GMP and producing selective pulmonary vasodilation. Clinically, inhaled nitric oxide is used for term and near-term neonates with hypoxic respiratory failure and pulmonary hypertension. A notable risk is methemoglobinemia.
tail_desc: Heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment prevents adequate ventricular filling or ejection, reducing cardiac output and/or causing congestion. Common causes include ischemic heart disease, hypertension, and cardiomyopathy. Typical features are dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance, especially in older adults. Management focuses on treating causes, relieving congestion, and using guideline-directed medications and devices when indicated.

head: Azithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Azithromycin is a macrolide (azalide) antibiotic that binds the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin and soft tissue, and certain sexually transmitted infections such as chlamydia. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, so caution is warranted in patients with arrhythmia risk.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Calcitriol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcitriol is the active form of vitamin D (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3), a vitamin D analog that binds the nuclear vitamin D receptor to regulate calcium and phosphate homeostasis and suppress parathyroid hormone. It is used for hypocalcemia and secondary hyperparathyroidism in chronic kidney disease and for hypoparathyroidism. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in hypercalcemia or vitamin D toxicity.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Biotin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Biotin (vitamin B7, vitamin H) is a water-soluble B-complex vitamin used as a nutritional supplement and replacement therapy for biotin deficiency, including deficiency associated with inherited biotinidase deficiency or prolonged parenteral nutrition. It functions as an essential cofactor for several carboxylase enzymes involved in fatty acid synthesis, gluconeogenesis, and amino acid metabolism. It is generally well tolerated, but high-dose biotin can interfere with certain laboratory immunoassays, causing misleading test results.
tail_desc: Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory granulomatous disease that most often affects the lungs and intrathoracic lymph nodes. It is characterized by noncaseating granuloma formation of unclear cause, likely involving immune dysregulation in genetically susceptible people. Common features include cough, dyspnea, fatigue, skin or eye involvement, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, typically in young to middle-aged adults; management ranges from observation to corticosteroids and other immunosuppressive therapy for significant organ disease.

head: Busulfan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Busulfan is an alkylating antineoplastic of the alkyl sulfonate family that forms DNA-DNA and DNA-protein crosslinks, causing myeloablation and cell death without a single specific molecular target. It is used in chronic myeloid leukemia and, more commonly, as conditioning before hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. A notable serious toxicity is prolonged bone marrow suppression; seizures and hepatic veno-occlusive disease can also occur.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Bromocriptine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bromocriptine is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist, primarily targeting dopamine D2 receptors to inhibit pituitary prolactin secretion and suppress growth hormone release. It is used for hyperprolactinemia, prolactin-secreting adenomas, acromegaly, and Parkinson disease; a quick-release form is also approved for type 2 diabetes. Notable adverse effects include nausea and orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension.
tail_desc: Severe pre-eclampsia is a hypertensive disorder of pregnancy affecting the maternal vascular, renal, hepatic, and neurologic systems, caused by abnormal placentation with widespread endothelial dysfunction. It occurs after 20 weeks’ gestation with severe hypertension, proteinuria or end-organ injury, headache, visual symptoms, epigastric pain, thrombocytopenia, and fetal growth restriction. Management requires urgent maternal-fetal assessment, blood pressure control, magnesium sulfate, and timely delivery.

head: Etoposide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Etoposide is a semisynthetic epipodophyllotoxin antineoplastic agent that inhibits topoisomerase II, causing DNA strand breaks and cell-cycle arrest. It is used in established regimens for small cell lung cancer, testicular cancer, and several hematologic or solid malignancies. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially neutropenia; it is generally avoided in severe bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Pulmonary fibrosis is a chronic interstitial lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by progressive scarring of lung parenchyma, which stiffens the lungs and impairs gas exchange. Patients, often older adults, typically develop exertional dyspnea, persistent dry cough, and reduced exercise tolerance. Management generally focuses on treating the cause when identified, slowing progression, supportive oxygen and pulmonary rehabilitation, and lung transplantation in selected cases.

head: Hydroflumethiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroflumethiazide is a thiazide diuretic (benzothiadiazine derivative) that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Choledocholithiasis is a biliary tract disorder in which gallstones are present in the common bile duct, causing partial or complete obstruction of bile flow in the hepatobiliary system. It commonly presents in adults, often with right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, cholestatic liver test abnormalities, and sometimes fever or pancreatitis. Management typically involves stone removal, usually by ERCP, followed by treatment of the underlying gallstone disease when indicated.

head: Metyrosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metyrosine is a tyrosine hydroxylase inhibitor and catecholamine synthesis blocker. By inhibiting tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate-limiting enzyme in catecholamine biosynthesis, it lowers production of dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. It is used primarily to manage catecholamine excess in patients with pheochromocytoma, including preoperative preparation. A notable adverse effect is sedation; it is also associated with extrapyramidal symptoms and should be used cautiously in psychiatric illness.
tail_desc: Basal ganglia disease is a broad neurologic category involving disorders of the deep gray nuclei that regulate movement, cognition, and behavior. It arises from diverse pathologies affecting these structures, including degeneration, vascular injury, infection, inflammation, metabolic disturbance, or toxins. Clinical features commonly include movement abnormalities such as parkinsonism, dystonia, chorea, or tremor, sometimes with cognitive or psychiatric symptoms. Management targets the underlying cause and symptomatic control, often with neurologic rehabilitation.

head: Perindopril (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Perindopril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug converted to the active metabolite perindoprilat. It lowers angiotensin II formation and aldosterone secretion by inhibiting ACE (kininase II), thereby reducing vasoconstriction and increasing bradykinin. It is used for hypertension, heart failure, and stable coronary artery disease; notable risks include dry cough and potentially life-threatening angioedema, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Cartilage disease is a broad musculoskeletal category involving damage, degeneration, inflammation, or developmental abnormalities of cartilage in joints, the spine, airways, or growth plates. Manifestations vary by cause and site but commonly include pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced mobility, or deformity, affecting both children and adults. Management is directed at the underlying disorder and may include rehabilitation, analgesia, anti-inflammatory treatment, or surgery.

head: Amifostine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amifostine is a cytoprotective thiophosphate prodrug dephosphorylated by alkaline phosphatase to the active free thiol WR-1065, which scavenges reactive oxygen species and donates hydrogen ions to protect normal tissues from cisplatin- and radiation-induced DNA damage. It is used to reduce cumulative nephrotoxicity from cisplatin and xerostomia during head and neck radiotherapy. Notable adverse effects include severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Ethosuximide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethosuximide is a succinimide anticonvulsant used primarily for absence (petit mal) seizures. Its antiepileptic effect is attributed mainly to inhibition of low-threshold T-type calcium currents in thalamic neurons, reducing abnormal thalamocortical rhythmic activity. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal upset, and it can also cause serious but rare blood dyscrasias or hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Dimenhydrinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dimenhydrinate is a first-generation H1 antihistamine and antiemetic, a salt of diphenhydramine with 8-chlorotheophylline. It acts primarily by antagonizing histamine H1 receptors, with additional antimuscarinic effects that suppress vestibular stimulation and the vomiting center. It is used for prevention and treatment of motion sickness, nausea, vomiting, and vertigo. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to dimenhydrinate or diphenhydramine.
tail_desc: Pyloric stenosis is a gastrointestinal obstructive disorder, usually infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, caused by hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the pyloric muscle that narrows the gastric outlet. It typically presents in young infants with progressively forceful non-bilious vomiting, dehydration, weight loss, and sometimes a palpable “olive” in the epigastrium. Management is correction of fluid and electrolyte abnormalities followed by pyloromyotomy.

head: Capecitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Capecitabine is an oral fluoropyrimidine antimetabolite and prodrug of 5-fluorouracil that is converted in tumors and tissues to metabolites inhibiting thymidylate synthase and incorporating into RNA and DNA, thereby impairing nucleic acid synthesis. It is used for colorectal and breast cancers, including metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is hand-foot syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with severe dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Mercaptopurine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mercaptopurine is a thiopurine antimetabolite and purine analog used mainly in acute lymphoblastic leukemia and sometimes in inflammatory bowel disease. It is converted intracellularly to active thioguanine nucleotides that inhibit de novo purine synthesis and are incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing cell proliferation. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, with markedly increased toxicity in patients with TPMT or NUDT15 deficiency.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Diiodohydroxyquinoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diiodohydroxyquinoline is a halogenated hydroxyquinoline antiprotozoal, also known as iodoquinol. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it acts luminally in the intestine against Entamoeba histolytica and has metal-chelating properties. It is used for asymptomatic intestinal amebiasis after tissue-active therapy. Notable adverse effects include optic neuritis and peripheral neuropathy, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Familial visceral myopathy is a rare inherited smooth-muscle disorder of the gastrointestinal and often urinary tract, causing impaired visceral motility. It reflects primary degeneration or dysfunction of the muscularis propria rather than mechanical obstruction. Patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction, abdominal distension, pain, severe constipation or vomiting, and sometimes bladder involvement; management is mainly supportive, with nutritional support, decompression, and selective surgery.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Hypoglycemia is a metabolic/endocrine condition of abnormally low blood glucose, affecting systemic energy supply, especially the central nervous system. It commonly results from excess insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy, fasting, alcohol use, or critical illness. Symptoms include sweating, tremor, palpitations, confusion, seizures, or coma, particularly in people with diabetes. Management focuses on prompt glucose administration and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Magnesium hydroxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium hydroxide is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an antacid and osmotic laxative. It neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach and, when taken orally in larger doses, retains water in the intestinal lumen by osmotic action; it has no specific receptor target. Established uses include relief of dyspepsia/heartburn and short-term treatment of constipation. A notable risk is hypermagnesemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hyperparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the parathyroid glands causing excess parathyroid hormone, which raises blood calcium and disrupts bone and kidney homeostasis. It may be primary, secondary to chronic kidney disease or vitamin D deficiency, or tertiary after prolonged stimulation. Common features include hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, bone pain, fractures, weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; management targets the cause, with parathyroidectomy for selected cases.

head: Levetiracetam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Levetiracetam is a pyrrolidone anticonvulsant, structurally related to piracetam, used to treat focal-onset, myoclonic, and primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Its main molecular target is synaptic vesicle protein 2A (SV2A), which modulates neurotransmitter release, though the full mechanism is not completely defined. A notable adverse effect is somnolence; behavioral symptoms such as irritability can also occur.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Clonazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine anticonvulsant and anxiolytic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is used for seizure disorders, including absence and myoclonic seizures, and for panic disorder. A notable adverse effect is sedation; it is contraindicated in significant hepatic impairment and acute narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Substance-related disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting the brain and behavior, characterized by maladaptive use of alcohol, drugs, or medications that leads to intoxication, dependence, withdrawal, and impaired control despite harm. It can occur across all age groups, often with craving, social or occupational dysfunction, and medical complications. Management typically combines screening, psychosocial treatment, relapse prevention, and, for some substances, medication-assisted therapy and supervised withdrawal care.

head: Trichlormethiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trichlormethiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disorders. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Guanethidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanethidine is an adrenergic neuron–blocking antihypertensive of the guanidine class. It is taken up into peripheral sympathetic nerve terminals via the norepinephrine transporter (NET) and depletes or prevents release of norepinephrine, reducing sympathetic tone. It was used for severe hypertension. A notable adverse effect is marked orthostatic hypotension; it is generally ineffective in pheochromocytoma.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Allantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Allantoin is a diureide of glyoxylic acid used topically as a skin protectant and keratolytic in many over-the-counter dermatologic products. Its mechanism is not defined by a specific molecular target; it is thought to promote desquamation and support wound healing by softening keratin and enhancing moisture retention. Established uses include minor wounds, burns, chapped or irritated skin, and oral mucosal irritation. Adverse effects are uncommon but can include local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Cisapride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cisapride is a substituted piperidinyl benzamide gastroprokinetic agent that enhances gastrointestinal motility primarily by acting as a serotonin 5-HT4 receptor agonist, promoting acetylcholine release in the enteric nervous system. It was used for gastroesophageal reflux disease and other motility disorders, but its use became highly restricted because it can prolong the QT interval and cause serious ventricular arrhythmias, especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors.
tail_desc: Long QT syndrome is a cardiac electrical disorder affecting ventricular repolarization, characterized by prolongation of the QT interval on electrocardiography, usually due to inherited ion-channel defects or acquired drug/electrolyte effects. It predisposes children or adults to syncope, seizures, and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia including torsades de pointes, with risk of sudden death; management emphasizes trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, correction of reversible causes, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Melphalan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Melphalan is a bifunctional nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the phenylalanine derivative class that covalently crosslinks DNA, mainly at guanine bases, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and causing cell death. It is used chiefly in multiple myeloma and ovarian carcinoma conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity or marked bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Lipoid nephrosis is the classic older term for minimal change disease, a glomerular kidney disorder and common cause of nephrotic syndrome, especially in children. It is characterized by podocyte foot-process effacement with little or no abnormality on light microscopy, leading to heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia. Management is mainly corticosteroid therapy with supportive control of edema and other nephrotic complications.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis disorder is a vascular disease of the circulatory system characterized by thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls, often related to aging, hypertension, diabetes, or lipid deposition in specific subtypes. It may be asymptomatic or cause ischemic complications such as angina, stroke, or peripheral vascular disease, especially in older adults. Management targets risk-factor control and treatment of cardiovascular complications.

head: Rimantadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rimantadine is an adamantane antiviral active against influenza A viruses. It inhibits the viral M2 proton channel, blocking endosomal uncoating of the virus after entry into host cells. It has been used for prophylaxis and treatment of influenza A infection, although resistance is now common. Notable adverse effects include CNS effects such as insomnia or nervousness; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to adamantanes.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Auranofin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Auranofin is an oral gold(I)-containing disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD). It is thought to act through immunomodulatory effects, notably inhibition of thioredoxin reductase and related sulfhydryl-dependent enzymes, thereby suppressing inflammatory responses. It has been approved for rheumatoid arthritis. Important adverse effects include diarrhea, rash, stomatitis, and potentially serious thrombocytopenia or proteinuria; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Sjögren syndrome is a chronic autoimmune rheumatic disease affecting exocrine glands and other organs, characterized by lymphocytic infiltration and dysfunction of the lacrimal and salivary glands. It commonly occurs in middle-aged women and causes dry eyes, dry mouth, fatigue, and arthralgia, with possible systemic involvement. Management focuses on symptomatic relief, dental and ocular care, and immunomodulatory therapy for significant extraglandular disease.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Small intestine cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the small bowel, most often adenocarcinoma but also including neuroendocrine tumors, lymphomas, and sarcomas, affecting the gastrointestinal system. It arises from uncontrolled proliferation of intestinal cells and may present with abdominal pain, occult bleeding, anemia, weight loss, obstruction, or perforation, typically in adults. Management generally centers on surgical resection, with histology- and stage-directed systemic therapy when indicated.

head: Simvastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Simvastatin is a lipid-lowering statin, a semisynthetic derivative of a fermentation product, that acts as a prodrug inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase (3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase), reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used for hypercholesterolemia and cardiovascular risk reduction. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; active liver disease is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Nodular fasciitis is a benign, rapidly growing reactive myofibroblastic proliferation of the soft tissues, usually arising in subcutaneous fascia of the upper extremities, trunk, or head and neck. It typically affects young to middle-aged adults and presents as a small, sometimes tender solitary mass that can mimic sarcoma clinically and histologically. Management is usually local excision, with low recurrence and occasional spontaneous regression.

head: Azathioprine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Azathioprine is a thiopurine antimetabolite immunosuppressant and prodrug of 6-mercaptopurine that inhibits de novo purine synthesis and incorporation into nucleic acids, suppressing proliferating T and B lymphocytes. It is used to prevent kidney transplant rejection and to treat rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression, particularly in patients with low TPMT or NUDT15 activity.
tail_desc: A fungal infectious disease is an infection caused by pathogenic fungi that can involve the skin, nails, mucosa, lungs, bloodstream, or other organs. It results from fungal invasion and tissue inflammation, ranging from superficial disease to invasive mycosis. Typical features include rash, scaling, itching, oral or vaginal thrush, cough, fever, or sepsis, with higher risk in immunocompromised patients. Management generally relies on antifungal therapy and correction of underlying risk factors.

head: Mitomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mitomycin is an antineoplastic antibiotic of the mitomycin/aziridine alkylating-agent class that is enzymatically activated to form DNA cross-links, inhibiting DNA synthesis and replication without a single specific molecular target. It is used in several cancers, including gastric and pancreatic adenocarcinomas, and intravesically for superficial bladder cancer. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; hemolytic-uremic syndrome can also occur.
tail_desc: Heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment prevents adequate ventricular filling or ejection, reducing cardiac output and/or causing congestion. Common causes include ischemic heart disease, hypertension, and cardiomyopathy. Typical features are dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance, especially in older adults. Management focuses on treating causes, relieving congestion, and using guideline-directed medications and devices when indicated.

head: Anagrelide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Anagrelide is an oral imidazoquinazoline antiplatelet agent that lowers platelet counts primarily by inhibiting megakaryocyte maturation, with phosphodiesterase III inhibition also documented. It is used for thrombocythemia, especially essential thrombocythemia, to reduce elevated platelets and thrombotic risk. A notable adverse effect is cardiovascular toxicity, including tachycardia and palpitations; caution is advised in patients with cardiac disease.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Cetirizine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cetirizine is a second-generation piperazine antihistamine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is widely used for allergic rhinitis and chronic urticaria. Compared with first-generation antihistamines it is less sedating, but somnolence can still occur; caution is advised in severe renal impairment because the drug is substantially renally eliminated.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis disorder is a vascular disease of the circulatory system characterized by thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls, often related to aging, hypertension, diabetes, or lipid deposition in specific subtypes. It may be asymptomatic or cause ischemic complications such as angina, stroke, or peripheral vascular disease, especially in older adults. Management targets risk-factor control and treatment of cardiovascular complications.

head: Sorafenib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sorafenib is an oral multikinase inhibitor of the biaryl urea class that targets RAF kinases and several receptor tyrosine kinases, including VEGFR, PDGFR, FLT3, RET, and KIT, thereby suppressing tumor-cell proliferation and angiogenesis. It is approved for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma, advanced renal cell carcinoma, and radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is hand-foot skin reaction.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Baclofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
