head_desc: Baclofen is a centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant and antispastic agent, structurally a GABA analogue that acts primarily as a GABA_B receptor agonist, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release in the spinal cord. It is used to treat spasticity, including that associated with multiple sclerosis and spinal cord lesions. Notable adverse effects include sedation and dizziness; abrupt withdrawal can cause serious neurologic symptoms.
tail_desc: Cerebrovascular disorder is a broad category of diseases affecting the brain’s blood vessels and cerebral circulation, including ischemic and hemorrhagic conditions. It results from vessel occlusion, rupture, or structural abnormalities that impair blood flow or cause bleeding. Typical features are sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, dizziness, or altered consciousness, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent diagnosis, restoring or controlling cerebral perfusion, treating the cause, and preventing recurrence.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Gemifloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Gemifloxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It has been used for treatment of acute bacterial exacerbations of chronic bronchitis and mild-to-moderate community-acquired pneumonia. A notable adverse effect is tendonitis and tendon rupture, and it is generally avoided in patients with known fluoroquinolone hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Periarthritis is a musculoskeletal disorder involving inflammation and degenerative change in tissues around a joint, such as the capsule, tendons, bursae, and ligaments, rather than the articular surfaces themselves. It typically causes localized pain, tenderness, and restricted movement, often in the shoulder. Management is usually conservative, with rest, physiotherapy, analgesics or NSAIDs, and sometimes corticosteroid injection.

head: Benzoyl peroxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzoyl peroxide is a topical organic peroxide used mainly as an anti-acne agent. It decomposes in the skin to release free-radical oxygen species, producing bactericidal activity against Cutibacterium acnes and mild keratolytic/comedolytic effects. It is established for acne vulgaris treatment, alone or with other agents. Common adverse effects include local skin irritation, dryness, erythema, and peeling.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Cevimeline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cevimeline is a muscarinic cholinergic agonist, a quinuclidine derivative, that primarily stimulates M3 muscarinic receptors to increase exocrine gland secretion, especially salivary flow. It is approved for treatment of dry mouth (xerostomia) in patients with Sjögren’s syndrome. Notable adverse effects include excessive sweating, and it is contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma and in conditions where miosis is undesirable, such as narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Sudden cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which abrupt loss of effective cardiac output, usually from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, causes sudden collapse, unresponsiveness, and absent pulse. It can occur in adults with structural heart disease or acute ischemia, and requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and urgent post-resuscitation care.

head: Nesiritide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nesiritide is a recombinant B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) and vasodilator that activates natriuretic peptide receptor-A, increasing intracellular cGMP. It has been used intravenously for acute decompensated heart failure with dyspnea at rest or with minimal activity. A notable adverse effect is symptomatic hypotension, and it should be avoided in patients with cardiogenic shock or very low blood pressure.
tail_desc: Aortic stenosis is a valvular heart disease of the cardiovascular system in which narrowing of the aortic valve obstructs left ventricular outflow, usually from age-related calcific degeneration, congenital bicuspid valve, or rheumatic scarring. It commonly affects older adults and causes exertional dyspnea, angina, syncope, and eventually heart failure; management ranges from monitoring to aortic valve replacement or transcatheter implantation when severe or symptomatic.

head: Bismuth subsalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bismuth subsalicylate is a bismuth-containing salicylate antidiarrheal and gastroprotective agent. It acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract, where bismuth has antimicrobial and mucosal protective effects, while salicylate inhibits cyclooxygenase and reduces intestinal prostaglandin-mediated secretion and inflammation. It is used for diarrhea, dyspepsia, heartburn, and nausea. A notable adverse effect is black discoloration of stools and tongue.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Dalteparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dalteparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin. It is used for prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including perioperative and certain medical patients. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a rare inherited platelet function disorder of the hemostatic system caused by deficiency or dysfunction of the platelet integrin αIIbβ3 (glycoprotein IIb/IIIa), leading to impaired platelet aggregation. It typically presents from childhood with mucocutaneous bleeding such as epistaxis, easy bruising, gingival bleeding, and menorrhagia. Management focuses on bleeding prevention and control with local measures, antifibrinolytics, platelet transfusion, and sometimes recombinant factor VIIa.

head: Zinc gluconate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zinc gluconate is an oral zinc salt supplement and trace element preparation. It supplies zinc ions, an essential cofactor for numerous metalloenzymes and transcription factors, thereby correcting or preventing zinc deficiency rather than acting through a single specific molecular target. It is used for prevention and treatment of zinc deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, especially nausea; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Iron metabolism disorders are a heterogeneous group of hematologic and systemic conditions affecting iron absorption, transport, storage, or utilization. They arise from genetic defects, chronic disease, blood loss, or iron overload states, causing iron deficiency or excess. Clinical features include anemia, fatigue, organ dysfunction, and tissue iron deposition, occurring in both children and adults. Management targets the cause and iron balance, using supplementation, chelation, phlebotomy, or treatment of underlying disease.

head: Sulfamerazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamerazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial (“sulfa” drug) of the short- to intermediate-acting class that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby suppressing bacterial growth. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including urinary tract and respiratory infections. Important adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions such as rash, and it is generally avoided in patients with sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Folic acid deficiency anemia is a megaloblastic anemia of the hematologic system caused by inadequate folate, impairing DNA synthesis and producing ineffective erythropoiesis with enlarged red cell precursors. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, glossitis, and macrocytosis, often in malnutrition, alcoholism, pregnancy, malabsorption, or increased demand. Management involves folate replacement and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Erythromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis by blocking translocation. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin, and soft-tissue infections, and as an alternative in some penicillin-allergic patients. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, which can increase the risk of serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Hematologic disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the blood, bone marrow, lymphatic system, and hemostatic pathways. It includes malignant, inherited, immune, and acquired conditions that disrupt blood cell production, function, or survival, or coagulation. Common features include anemia, bleeding, thrombosis, infection susceptibility, lymphadenopathy, or splenomegaly, across all ages. Management depends on cause and may involve supportive care, transfusion, immunotherapy, anticoagulation, chemotherapy, or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Lindane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lindane is an organochlorine insecticide and scabicide/pediculicide (the gamma isomer of hexachlorocyclohexane) used topically for scabies and head lice when safer treatments are unsuitable or have failed. It acts on parasite neuronal ion channels, particularly GABA-gated chloride channels, causing neuronal hyperexcitation. A notable risk is neurotoxicity, including seizures, so it is contraindicated in premature infants and patients with seizure disorders.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Ibutilide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ibutilide is a class III antiarrhythmic methanesulfonanilide used intravenously for rapid conversion of recent-onset atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter to sinus rhythm. It prolongs cardiac action potential duration and refractoriness mainly by enhancing a slow inward sodium current and also blocking delayed rectifier potassium currents. A notable risk is QT prolongation with torsades de pointes.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Nabilone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nabilone is a synthetic cannabinoid, structurally related to delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol, that acts mainly as an agonist at cannabinoid CB1 and CB2 receptors in the central nervous system. It is used primarily for chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting refractory to conventional antiemetics. Notable adverse effects include dizziness, drowsiness, and psychotropic reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to cannabinoids or a history of psychosis.
tail_desc: Schizoaffective disorder is a chronic psychiatric disorder affecting the brain, defined by schizophrenia-spectrum psychosis occurring with prominent mood episodes (depressive and/or manic). Its cause is multifactorial and incompletely understood, involving genetic and neurobiologic vulnerability. Patients typically have delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking, plus mood symptoms and functional impairment. Management generally combines antipsychotic medication, mood stabilizers or antidepressants when indicated, and psychotherapy with psychosocial support.

head: Regadenoson (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Regadenoson is a selective adenosine A2A receptor agonist, an adenosine derivative used as a coronary vasodilator. By activating A2A receptors on coronary vascular smooth muscle, it increases coronary blood flow and produces pharmacologic stress for radionuclide myocardial perfusion imaging in patients unable to exercise adequately. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and caution is warranted in patients with reactive airway disease.
tail_desc: Familial sick sinus syndrome is an inherited cardiac conduction disorder affecting the sinoatrial node, the heart’s natural pacemaker. It results from genetic defects that impair impulse generation or conduction, causing sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or atrial arrhythmias, often in affected families across generations. Management is directed by symptoms and rhythm severity, commonly including monitoring and permanent pacemaker implantation when clinically indicated.

head: Tetracaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetracaine is an ester-type local anesthetic of the para-aminobenzoic acid class that reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing impulse conduction. It is used for topical anesthesia of the eye and mucous membranes and in spinal anesthesia. A notable risk is systemic toxicity, including CNS and cardiovascular effects; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to ester local anesthetics.
tail_desc: Conjunctivitis is inflammation of the conjunctiva, an ocular surface disorder affecting the eye. It is most commonly caused by viral or bacterial infection, allergy, or irritant exposure, leading to conjunctival hyperemia, tearing or discharge, discomfort, and sometimes itching. It occurs in both children and adults. Management is cause-directed, with hygiene and supportive care, allergen avoidance, or antimicrobial therapy when indicated.

head: Sulfasalazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfasalazine is a 5-aminosalicylate prodrug linked to sulfapyridine, classified as a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug and anti-inflammatory agent. Colonic bacteria cleave it to mesalamine and sulfapyridine; its anti-inflammatory effects involve inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis and inflammatory cytokine signaling, without a single definitive molecular target. It is used for ulcerative colitis and rheumatoid arthritis. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions, especially in patients with sulfonamide or salicylate allergy.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Penbutolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Penbutolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at β1 and β2 receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is generally contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Raynaud disease is a primary vasospastic disorder of the peripheral circulation affecting small arteries and arterioles, especially in the fingers and toes. Triggered by cold or emotional stress, it causes episodic pallor, cyanosis, and erythema with numbness or pain, often in younger women. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, warming measures, smoking cessation, and vasodilators such as calcium channel blockers when needed.

head: Aminoglutethimide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminoglutethimide is a nonsteroidal aromatase inhibitor and adrenal steroidogenesis inhibitor. It blocks conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone and inhibits aromatase (CYP19), reducing synthesis of glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, estrogens, and androgens. It has been used for Cushing syndrome and advanced breast cancer. Notable adverse effects include sedation and rash; adrenal insufficiency is an important risk.
tail_desc: Hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland characterized by deficient thyroid hormone production, most commonly from autoimmune thyroiditis, thyroid ablation, or iodine deficiency. It causes a generalized slowing of metabolism with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, bradycardia, and depression, and is more common in women and older adults. Management typically involves diagnosing the cause and replacing hormone with levothyroxine.

head: Povidone K30 (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Povidone K30 is a grade of povidone (polyvinylpyrrolidone), a synthetic water-soluble polymer used primarily as a pharmaceutical excipient rather than an active drug. It has no specific molecular target or therapeutic mechanism of action. Its established role is in tablet and capsule formulations as a binder, solubilizer, and film-forming agent. A notable concern is hypersensitivity, with povidone allergy reported rarely.
tail_desc: Respiratory failure is a life-threatening syndrome of the respiratory system in which gas exchange is inadequate, causing hypoxemia with or without hypercapnia. It results from lung, airway, neuromuscular, chest wall, or central ventilatory dysfunction. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachypnea, cyanosis, confusion, or somnolence, especially in critically ill adults or children. Management targets the underlying cause and supports oxygenation and ventilation, including oxygen therapy and noninvasive or mechanical ventilation.

head: Ampicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ampicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, gastrointestinal, and meningitis pathogens such as Listeria monocytogenes and Enterococcus species. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Isoleucine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoleucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid used in amino acid replacement and parenteral nutrition formulations. It acts as a substrate for protein synthesis and energy metabolism rather than through a single pharmacologic receptor target. Clinically, it is included to prevent or treat amino acid deficiency in patients requiring nutritional support. Excessive amino acid administration may contribute to azotemia, especially in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy is a paraneoplastic endocrine-metabolic disorder causing systemic calcium imbalance, most often in patients with solid tumors. It usually results from tumor secretion of parathyroid hormone-related protein, which increases bone resorption and renal calcium retention. Patients develop often severe hypercalcemia with dehydration, weakness, confusion, constipation, and polyuria; management focuses on urgent calcium lowering and treatment of the underlying cancer.

head: Indapamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Indapamide is a thiazide-like sulfonamide diuretic used mainly to treat hypertension and edema. It acts primarily in the distal convoluted tubule by inhibiting the sodium/chloride cotransporter (NCC), increasing urinary sodium and water excretion, with additional antihypertensive vascular effects. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria and should be used cautiously in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Osteitis fibrosa is a metabolic bone disorder of the skeleton, usually caused by hyperparathyroidism with excess parathyroid hormone driving increased osteoclastic bone resorption and replacement by fibrous tissue. It can cause bone pain, fragility fractures, deformity, and “brown tumors,” classically in patients with severe primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism. Management targets the underlying hyperparathyroidism and correction of calcium-phosphate abnormalities.

head: Heparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, thereby inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa and reducing fibrin clot formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndromes, and during procedures such as hemodialysis. A notable adverse effect is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Hematologic disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the blood, bone marrow, lymphatic system, and hemostatic pathways. It includes malignant, inherited, immune, and acquired conditions that disrupt blood cell production, function, or survival, or coagulation. Common features include anemia, bleeding, thrombosis, infection susceptibility, lymphadenopathy, or splenomegaly, across all ages. Management depends on cause and may involve supportive care, transfusion, immunotherapy, anticoagulation, chemotherapy, or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Telmisartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telmisartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl benzimidazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and to reduce cardiovascular risk in selected high-risk patients. Notable risks include hyperkalemia and renal impairment, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Renal artery obstruction is a vascular disorder of the renal circulation in which partial or complete blockage of a renal artery, usually from atherosclerotic stenosis, thromboembolism, or fibromuscular dysplasia, reduces kidney perfusion. It may cause resistant hypertension, worsening renal function, flank pain, or asymmetry in kidney size, affecting mainly older adults or younger women with fibromuscular dysplasia. Management targets blood pressure control and the underlying lesion, with selected cases treated by endovascular or surgical revascularization.

head: Sodium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium acetate is an alkalinizing electrolyte salt and acetate donor used in parenteral nutrition and intravenous fluid therapy. After metabolism, acetate is converted primarily in muscle and other tissues to bicarbonate, helping correct metabolic acidosis; it has no single receptor target. Established uses include correction of hyponatremia and acidosis in compounded IV solutions. Excess administration can cause metabolic alkalosis.
tail_desc: Myotonia congenita is a hereditary skeletal muscle channelopathy caused by pathogenic variants in CLCN1, which impair chloride conductance in muscle fibers and produce delayed relaxation after contraction. It usually presents in childhood with muscle stiffness, especially after rest, improving with repeated movement (warm-up), sometimes with transient weakness or hypertrophy. Management is mainly supportive, with trigger avoidance, physiotherapy, and sodium-channel blockers such as mexiletine for symptomatic relief.

head: Estramustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Estramustine is an antineoplastic estradiol–nitrogen mustard conjugate used mainly for palliative treatment of metastatic or progressive prostate cancer. It binds microtubule-associated proteins and tubulin, disrupting microtubule assembly and mitosis, with additional estrogenic effects. A notable adverse effect is thromboembolism; it is generally contraindicated in patients with active thrombophlebitis or thromboembolic disorders.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Entecavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Entecavir is a guanosine nucleoside analogue antiviral used to treat chronic hepatitis B virus infection. It is phosphorylated intracellularly and inhibits the HBV polymerase/reverse transcriptase, blocking priming, reverse transcription of the negative strand, and synthesis of the positive DNA strand. A notable warning is severe acute exacerbation of hepatitis after discontinuation; lactic acidosis and hepatomegaly with steatosis have also been reported.
tail_desc: Fatty liver disease is a hepatic disorder characterized by excess triglyceride accumulation in hepatocytes, most commonly associated with obesity, insulin resistance, diabetes, dyslipidemia, or alcohol use. It is often asymptomatic but may cause fatigue, hepatomegaly, or abnormal liver enzymes, and can progress to steatohepatitis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and liver failure. Management focuses on treating the cause, especially weight loss, metabolic risk-factor control, alcohol cessation, and monitoring for progression.

head: Salsalate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salsalate is a nonacetylated salicylate NSAID and dimer of salicylic acid that is hydrolyzed to salicylate; it reduces prostaglandin synthesis primarily through cyclooxygenase inhibition, with relatively weak direct platelet inhibition compared with aspirin. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and other painful inflammatory conditions. Notable adverse effects include salicylism and gastrointestinal irritation or ulceration.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Prostate carcinoma is a malignant epithelial cancer of the prostate gland in the male genitourinary system, usually arising as adenocarcinoma driven by genetic alterations and androgen signaling. It mainly affects older men and may be asymptomatic or cause urinary obstruction, hematuria, bone pain, or metastatic disease. Management ranges from active surveillance for low-risk tumors to surgery, radiotherapy, androgen-deprivation, and systemic therapy.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Glucosamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glucosamine is an endogenous amino monosaccharide and dietary supplement used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, especially of the knee. Its mechanism is not well established; it is thought to serve as a substrate for glycosaminoglycan and proteoglycan synthesis in cartilage, without a clearly defined primary molecular target. Adverse effects are usually mild gastrointestinal symptoms, and caution is advised in patients with shellfish allergy depending on product source.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis is a urinary tract disorder characterized by formation of calculi in the kidneys, usually from supersaturation and crystallization of minerals such as calcium, uric acid, or cystine. It commonly causes renal colic, hematuria, nausea, and urinary obstruction, especially in adults. Management focuses on analgesia, hydration, facilitating stone passage, metabolic evaluation, and urologic removal when stones are large, obstructing, or infected.

head: Rifampicin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rifampicin is a rifamycin-class ansamycin antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase by binding the β-subunit encoded by rpoB, thereby blocking RNA synthesis. It is a cornerstone of combination therapy for tuberculosis and is also used for leprosy and prophylaxis of Neisseria meningitidis and Haemophilus influenzae type b exposure. Notable adverse effects include hepatotoxicity and potent cytochrome P450 induction causing major drug interactions.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Bupropion (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bupropion is an aminoketone antidepressant and smoking-cessation agent that inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine, with minimal direct serotonergic activity; nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonism may also contribute. It is approved for major depressive disorder, seasonal affective disorder, and smoking cessation. A notable risk is seizures, so it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or eating disorders.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Sulbactam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulbactam is a beta-lactamase inhibitor of the penicillanic acid sulfone class that irreversibly binds bacterial serine beta-lactamases, protecting susceptible beta-lactam antibiotics from enzymatic degradation; it also has intrinsic activity against Acinetobacter via penicillin-binding proteins. It is used in combination products, such as ampicillin/sulbactam, for susceptible bacterial infections. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Minoxidil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Minoxidil is a direct-acting arteriolar vasodilator of the pyrimidine derivative class, used orally for severe, refractory hypertension and topically for androgenetic alopecia. It is converted to minoxidil sulfate, which opens ATP-sensitive potassium channels in vascular smooth muscle, reducing peripheral resistance; the precise hair-growth mechanism is incompletely defined. Notable adverse effects include hypertrichosis and fluid retention.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Nicardipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nicardipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation with relatively little direct cardiac depression. It is used to treat hypertension, including acute severe hypertension with the intravenous formulation, and chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is reflex tachycardia; it is contraindicated in advanced aortic stenosis.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Metolazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metolazone is a quinazoline sulfonamide-like thiazide-related diuretic that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat hypertension and edema, including edema associated with congestive heart failure or renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Trimipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzazepine class. Unlike many TCAs, it has relatively weak inhibition of norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake; its effects are attributed mainly to antagonism at histamine H1, muscarinic cholinergic, and alpha-adrenergic receptors. It is used for major depressive disorders, particularly when insomnia or anxiety is prominent. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation.
tail_desc: Angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic disease in which the peripheral iris obstructs the anterior chamber angle, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid or intermittent rise in intraocular pressure. It typically affects anatomically predisposed older adults, especially those with shallow anterior chambers. Features include severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos, nausea, and a red eye; management is urgent pressure lowering followed by laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Tetracaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetracaine is an ester-type local anesthetic of the para-aminobenzoic acid class that reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing impulse conduction. It is used for topical anesthesia of the eye and mucous membranes and in spinal anesthesia. A notable risk is systemic toxicity, including CNS and cardiovascular effects; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to ester local anesthetics.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Midodrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Midodrine is an orally administered prodrug and selective alpha-1 adrenergic agonist whose active metabolite, desglymidodrine, causes peripheral vasoconstriction via alpha-1 receptors. It is approved for symptomatic orthostatic hypotension. A notable adverse effect is supine hypertension, and it is generally contraindicated in patients with severe organic heart disease, acute renal disease, urinary retention, pheochromocytoma, or thyrotoxicosis.
tail_desc: Adrenal gland cancer usually refers to malignant tumors of the adrenal cortex or medulla within the endocrine system, most often adrenocortical carcinoma in adults. It arises from uncontrolled adrenal cell growth and may be functional, causing excess cortisol, aldosterone, or androgens, or present as an abdominal mass, pain, or weight loss. Management typically centers on surgical resection, with adjunct systemic therapy and hormonal control when needed.

head: Methadyl acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadyl acetate (levomethadyl acetate, LAAM) is a long-acting diphenylheptane opioid and full agonist at the μ-opioid receptor. It was used for maintenance treatment of opioid dependence as an alternative to methadone. A notable safety concern is QT-interval prolongation with risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, making it contraindicated in patients with known QT prolongation or significant cardiac rhythm disorders.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Thiothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiothixene is a first-generation (typical) antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class. Its antipsychotic effects are primarily attributed to antagonism of central dopamine D2 receptors, with additional activity at other neurotransmitter receptors. It is used for the treatment of schizophrenia. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including drug-induced parkinsonism and akathisia.
tail_desc: Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system, primarily affecting the basal ganglia. It is characterized by degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and accumulation of alpha-synuclein–containing Lewy bodies. Typical features, usually beginning in older adults, include bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and postural instability; management focuses on symptomatic dopaminergic therapy, rehabilitation, and selected device-aided or surgical treatments.

head: Diazoxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diazoxide is a benzothiadiazine derivative and potassium channel opener that activates ATP-sensitive K+ channels, notably SUR1/Kir6.2 in pancreatic beta cells and vascular smooth muscle, suppressing insulin release and causing vasodilation. It is used for hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia and, historically, severe hypertension. Notable adverse effects include fluid retention and hyperglycemia; it is generally avoided in functional hypoglycemia.
tail_desc: obsolete hyperuricemia (disease) is a disease or medical condition.

head: Heparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, thereby inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa and reducing fibrin clot formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndromes, and during procedures such as hemodialysis. A notable adverse effect is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Intracranial hemorrhage is acute bleeding within the skull affecting the central nervous system, including intracerebral, subarachnoid, subdural, and epidural spaces. It usually results from ruptured vessels due to hypertension, trauma, aneurysm, vascular malformation, or coagulopathy. Patients, often older adults or trauma patients, present with sudden headache, focal neurologic deficits, vomiting, reduced consciousness, or seizures. Management is emergent, focusing on stabilization, blood pressure and coagulation control, and neurosurgical intervention when indicated.

head: Bexarotene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bexarotene is a synthetic retinoid of the rexinoid class that selectively activates retinoid X receptors (RXRs), modulating gene transcription involved in cell differentiation and apoptosis. It is approved for treatment of cutaneous manifestations of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, including topical and oral formulations. A notable adverse effect is marked hypertriglyceridemia; it is also teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland characterized by deficient thyroid hormone production, most commonly from autoimmune thyroiditis, thyroid ablation, or iodine deficiency. It causes a generalized slowing of metabolism with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, bradycardia, and depression, and is more common in women and older adults. Management typically involves diagnosing the cause and replacing hormone with levothyroxine.

head: Zinc oxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zinc oxide is an inorganic zinc compound used topically as a skin protectant and mild astringent. Its effects are primarily physical rather than receptor-mediated, forming a protective barrier on skin and reflecting or scattering ultraviolet radiation in sunscreen formulations, without a defined primary molecular target. Established uses include diaper dermatitis, minor skin irritation, and sun protection; adverse effects are usually limited to local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Isometheptene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isometheptene is a sympathomimetic amine with vasoconstrictor activity, used mainly in combination products for acute migraine and tension headache. Its therapeutic effect is attributed to adrenergic stimulation causing cranial vasoconstriction, although a specific primary molecular target is not well established. Because it can raise blood pressure, it is contraindicated in severe hypertension and may cause palpitations or other cardiovascular adverse effects.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Ethyl loflazepate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethyl loflazepate is a long-acting benzodiazepine anxiolytic. Like other benzodiazepines, it acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA\(_A\) receptor at the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders and related psychosomatic symptoms. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated in myasthenia gravis and severe respiratory insufficiency.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Methadyl acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadyl acetate (levomethadyl acetate, LAAM) is a long-acting diphenylheptane opioid and full agonist at the μ-opioid receptor. It was used for maintenance treatment of opioid dependence as an alternative to methadone. A notable safety concern is QT-interval prolongation with risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, making it contraindicated in patients with known QT prolongation or significant cardiac rhythm disorders.
tail_desc: Congenital hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid axis present at birth, most commonly caused by thyroid dysgenesis or defects in thyroid hormone synthesis, leading to deficient thyroxine. It affects newborns and may cause prolonged jaundice, lethargy, feeding difficulty, constipation, macroglossia, and impaired growth and neurodevelopment if untreated. Management is prompt newborn screening confirmation and early levothyroxine replacement with ongoing monitoring.

head: Penbutolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Penbutolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at β1 and β2 receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is generally contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Vemurafenib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vemurafenib is a small-molecule BRAF kinase inhibitor, a diaryl urea derivative, that selectively targets mutant BRAF, especially BRAF V600E, to inhibit MAPK pathway signaling and tumor cell proliferation. It is approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma with BRAF V600 mutation and for Erdheim-Chester disease with BRAF V600 mutations. A notable adverse effect is cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Adomiparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Adomiparin is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Osteoporosis is a metabolic skeletal disease of the musculoskeletal system characterized by low bone mass and deterioration of bone microarchitecture, causing increased bone fragility and fracture risk. It commonly affects older adults, especially postmenopausal women, and may be asymptomatic until vertebral, hip, or wrist fractures occur. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, adequate calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy is a paraneoplastic endocrine-metabolic disorder causing systemic calcium imbalance, most often in patients with solid tumors. It usually results from tumor secretion of parathyroid hormone-related protein, which increases bone resorption and renal calcium retention. Patients develop often severe hypercalcemia with dehydration, weakness, confusion, constipation, and polyuria; management focuses on urgent calcium lowering and treatment of the underlying cancer.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Cystinuria is a hereditary renal tubular transport disorder affecting the urinary system, caused by defective reabsorption of cystine and dibasic amino acids in the proximal tubule. Excess urinary cystine, which is poorly soluble, leads to recurrent cystine kidney stones, often beginning in childhood or young adulthood, with hematuria, flank pain, and obstruction. Management focuses on high fluid intake, urine alkalinization, dietary sodium reduction, and sometimes thiol drugs.

head: Glucosamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glucosamine is an endogenous amino monosaccharide and dietary supplement used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, especially of the knee. Its mechanism is not well established; it is thought to serve as a substrate for glycosaminoglycan and proteoglycan synthesis in cartilage, without a clearly defined primary molecular target. Adverse effects are usually mild gastrointestinal symptoms, and caution is advised in patients with shellfish allergy depending on product source.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Antipyrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Antipyrine (phenazone) is a pyrazolone derivative and non-opioid analgesic/antipyretic. Its effects are attributed to inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis via cyclooxygenase pathways, although it is not used for a highly selective molecular target. It has been used for pain and fever and, in otic combinations, for symptomatic relief of ear pain. Hypersensitivity reactions, including rash, can occur.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Dabigatran etexilate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dabigatran etexilate is an oral direct thrombin inhibitor and the etexilate prodrug of dabigatran. After conversion to dabigatran, it reversibly inhibits thrombin (factor IIa), blocking fibrin formation and thrombin-mediated platelet activation. It is approved for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolism and for stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, including serious gastrointestinal or intracranial hemorrhage.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate is a synthetic progestin, a caproate ester of hydroxyprogesterone, that acts as an agonist at the progesterone receptor. It has been used clinically to reduce risk of recurrent preterm birth in certain pregnant patients with a prior singleton spontaneous preterm delivery. Notable adverse effects include thromboembolic events, and it is contraindicated in patients with hormone-sensitive malignancy or active thrombosis.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Fosamprenavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fosamprenavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor prodrug of amprenavir. After conversion to amprenavir, it inhibits the viral aspartyl protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is rash, and it is contraindicated with drugs highly dependent on CYP3A4 for clearance.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Diiodohydroxyquinoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diiodohydroxyquinoline is a halogenated hydroxyquinoline antiprotozoal, also known as iodoquinol. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it acts luminally in the intestine against Entamoeba histolytica and has metal-chelating properties. It is used for asymptomatic intestinal amebiasis after tissue-active therapy. Notable adverse effects include optic neuritis and peripheral neuropathy, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: capillary disease is a disease or medical condition.

head: Moxifloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Moxifloxacin is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including community-acquired pneumonia, acute bacterial sinusitis, acute exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and some skin or intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation; fluoroquinolones can also cause tendon rupture.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Doxazosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Doxazosin is a quinazoline-derived selective alpha1-adrenergic receptor antagonist that relaxes vascular and prostatic smooth muscle by blocking postsynaptic alpha1 receptors. It is used to treat hypertension and lower urinary tract symptoms associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia. A notable adverse effect is first-dose orthostatic hypotension, which may cause dizziness or syncope.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Naratriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naratriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It treats acute migraine attacks, with or without aura, by causing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. Notable adverse effects include paresthesia, dizziness, and chest or throat tightness; it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease and certain cerebrovascular syndromes.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Pregabalin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pregabalin is a gabapentinoid anticonvulsant and analgesic, a structural analogue of gamma-aminobutyric acid that binds the α2δ subunit of presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release. It is used for neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, adjunctive treatment of partial-onset seizures, and generalized anxiety disorder in some regions. Common adverse effects include dizziness and somnolence.
tail_desc: Angioedema is a vascular permeability disorder of the skin and mucosa causing sudden, localized swelling, often involving the face, lips, tongue, extremities, or airway. It results from mast cell mediator release or bradykinin excess, and may be allergic, medication-induced, or hereditary. Patients present with episodic nonpitting edema, sometimes with urticaria or life-threatening airway compromise; management targets airway protection and treating the underlying mechanism.

head: Vincristine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vincristine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds β-tubulin, inhibits microtubule polymerization, and arrests mitosis. It is used in combination chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia, lymphomas, and several pediatric solid tumors. A notable toxicity is dose-limiting peripheral neuropathy; intrathecal administration is contraindicated because it is uniformly fatal.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: Obesity is a chronic metabolic and nutritional disorder affecting whole-body energy homeostasis, characterized by excessive accumulation of adipose tissue due to sustained positive energy balance interacting with genetic, environmental, and behavioral factors. It typically presents in children or adults with elevated body mass index or central adiposity and related complications. Management centers on lifestyle intervention, treatment of comorbidities, anti-obesity pharmacotherapy, and bariatric surgery when indicated.

head: Cabergoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cabergoline is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist that selectively stimulates dopamine D2 receptors, suppressing pituitary prolactin secretion. It is used primarily for hyperprolactinemic disorders, including prolactinomas, and is also used in some settings for Parkinson disease. A notable safety concern is cardiac valvular fibrosis with long-term or high-dose use; it is contraindicated in patients with uncontrolled hypertension or fibrotic disorders.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Phendimetrazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phendimetrazine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, chemically related to phenethylamines and used short term as an adjunct in exogenous obesity management. It acts mainly as a prodrug of phenmetrazine, increasing central norepinephrine release and suppressing appetite. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or shortly after MAOI therapy.
tail_desc: Atherosclerosis is a chronic arterial disease of the cardiovascular system characterized by lipid-rich plaque formation in the intima, driven by endothelial injury, inflammation, and fibrous cap development. It commonly affects older adults and people with risk factors such as smoking, diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, causing ischemia, angina, myocardial infarction, stroke, or claudication. Management focuses on risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Mechlorethamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mechlorethamine is a nitrogen mustard alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms highly reactive intermediates, causing DNA cross-linking and inhibition of DNA replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used in regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and as topical therapy for mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma). A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is also a potent vesicant if extravasated.
tail_desc: AL amyloidosis is a systemic protein misfolding disorder caused by a clonal plasma-cell dyscrasia producing misfolded immunoglobulin light chains that deposit as amyloid in organs, especially the heart, kidneys, liver, nerves, and gastrointestinal tract. It typically affects adults and presents with nephrotic syndrome, restrictive cardiomyopathy, neuropathy, hepatomegaly, and fatigue. Management targets the plasma-cell clone, with supportive care and selected stem-cell transplantation.

head: Etidocaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Etidocaine is a long-acting amide-type local anesthetic. It blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, inhibiting initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used for infiltration, peripheral nerve block, and epidural anesthesia in surgical and obstetric settings. A notable adverse effect is dose-related cardiotoxicity, so caution is required in patients with significant conduction abnormalities.
tail_desc: Sudden cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which abrupt loss of effective cardiac output, usually from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, causes sudden collapse, unresponsiveness, and absent pulse. It can occur in adults with structural heart disease or acute ischemia, and requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and urgent post-resuscitation care.

head: Nicotinamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nicotinamide, the amide form of vitamin B3 (niacin), is a water-soluble vitamin and precursor of the coenzymes NAD and NADP, supporting cellular redox and energy metabolism rather than acting through a single receptor target. It is used to prevent and treat niacinamide deficiency, including pellagra. Adverse effects are usually mild, but high doses can cause hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Nodular fasciitis is a benign, rapidly growing reactive myofibroblastic proliferation of the soft tissues, usually arising in subcutaneous fascia of the upper extremities, trunk, or head and neck. It typically affects young to middle-aged adults and presents as a small, sometimes tender solitary mass that can mimic sarcoma clinically and histologically. Management is usually local excision, with low recurrence and occasional spontaneous regression.

head: Trichlormethiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trichlormethiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disorders. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Gemcitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Gemcitabine is a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog antimetabolite chemotherapy drug. Intracellular phosphorylation generates active metabolites that inhibit ribonucleotide reductase and are incorporated into DNA, blocking DNA synthesis and inducing apoptosis.
