It is used for pancreatic cancer and also non-small cell lung, bladder, breast, and ovarian cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Diazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine that positively modulates GABA-A receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is used for anxiety disorders, acute alcohol withdrawal, muscle spasm, seizure control including status epilepticus, and procedural sedation. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation with respiratory depression, especially when combined with opioids or other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Prednicarbate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prednicarbate is a nonhalogenated topical corticosteroid of the prednisolone derivative class used primarily for corticosteroid-responsive inflammatory dermatoses such as atopic dermatitis and eczema. It acts by activating intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress proinflammatory cytokines and other mediators. A notable adverse effect is local skin atrophy with prolonged use, especially under occlusion or on thin skin.
tail_desc: Addison disease is primary adrenal insufficiency, an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex causing deficient cortisol and often aldosterone production, most commonly from autoimmune adrenal destruction. Patients may develop chronic fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia, with risk of acute adrenal crisis. Management is lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, often mineralocorticoid replacement, and stress-dose therapy during illness or surgery.

head: Liraglutide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Liraglutide is a long-acting glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, a fatty-acylated incretin mimetic peptide. It activates the GLP-1 receptor to enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, delay gastric emptying, and reduce appetite. It is approved for type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic weight management. Notable risks include gastrointestinal adverse effects and a boxed warning regarding thyroid C-cell tumors.
tail_desc: Thyroid gland disease comprises disorders of the endocrine thyroid, including functional abnormalities and structural lesions such as goiter, thyroiditis, nodules, and cancer. Causes include autoimmune processes, iodine imbalance, inflammation, and neoplasia. Patients may present with hypo- or hyperthyroid symptoms, neck enlargement, or compressive signs. Management depends on cause and may involve hormone replacement, antithyroid therapy, radioactive iodine, surgery, or surveillance.

head: Tenofovir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tenofovir is an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor, used clinically as the prodrugs tenofovir disoproxil and tenofovir alafenamide. After intracellular activation to tenofovir diphosphate, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase by competing with natural nucleotides and causing DNA chain termination. It is approved for HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B; notable risks include renal toxicity and reduced bone mineral density.
tail_desc: Obesity is a chronic metabolic and nutritional disorder affecting whole-body energy homeostasis, characterized by excessive accumulation of adipose tissue due to sustained positive energy balance interacting with genetic, environmental, and behavioral factors. It typically presents in children or adults with elevated body mass index or central adiposity and related complications. Management centers on lifestyle intervention, treatment of comorbidities, anti-obesity pharmacotherapy, and bariatric surgery when indicated.

head: Dasatinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dasatinib is an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the thiazole carboxamide class that inhibits BCR-ABL and Src-family kinases, blocking proliferation signaling. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome–positive chronic myeloid leukemia and Ph+ acute lymphoblastic leukemia. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression; pleural effusion is also a well-recognized toxicity.
tail_desc: Hemopericardium is a cardiovascular condition in which blood accumulates within the pericardial sac, usually from cardiac trauma, myocardial rupture, aortic dissection, invasive procedures, or anticoagulation-related bleeding. It may present with chest pain, dyspnea, hypotension, jugular venous distension, and cardiac tamponade, often in acutely ill or injured patients. Management is urgent and focuses on hemodynamic stabilization, pericardial drainage, and treatment of the bleeding source.

head: Mangafodipir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mangafodipir is a manganese-containing chelate contrast agent, specifically manganese dipyridoxyl diphosphate, formerly used in magnetic resonance imaging. It acts by delivering paramagnetic Mn²⁺ that shortens T1 relaxation time, enhancing liver and pancreatic tissue contrast rather than binding a single receptor target. It was approved for MRI of hepatic lesions. Notable adverse effects include flushing and nausea; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Milrinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Milrinone is a bipyridine phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor and inodilator that increases intracellular cAMP in cardiac myocytes and vascular smooth muscle, enhancing myocardial contractility and promoting vasodilation. It is used for short-term intravenous treatment of acute decompensated heart failure or low-output states. A notable adverse effect is ventricular arrhythmia; significant hypotension can also occur.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Furosemide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Furosemide is a loop diuretic, chemically a sulfamoylanthranilic acid derivative, that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter (NKCC2, SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used for edema associated with heart failure, cirrhosis, or renal disease, and for hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Pancreatitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pancreas in the digestive system, caused by premature activation of pancreatic enzymes leading to autodigestion; common triggers include gallstones, alcohol use, hypertriglyceridemia, and medications. It typically presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting in adults. Management focuses on supportive care, treating the underlying cause, and addressing complications.

head: Rizatriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rizatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, chemically an indole derivative, that acts as a selective agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors to promote cranial vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease due to risk of coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Myocardial ischemia is a cardiovascular condition in which reduced coronary blood flow causes insufficient oxygen delivery to heart muscle, usually due to atherosclerotic coronary artery disease and sometimes vasospasm or thrombosis. It commonly presents in adults, especially those with cardiovascular risk factors, with exertional chest pressure, dyspnea, or silent ischemia. Management focuses on restoring perfusion, relieving symptoms, and reducing risk with antiplatelet therapy, antianginal drugs, risk-factor control, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Maraviroc (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Maraviroc is a small-molecule CCR5 antagonist antiretroviral in the entry inhibitor class. It binds the host chemokine receptor CCR5, blocking attachment and entry of CCR5-tropic HIV-1 into CD4+ cells. It is approved, in combination with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of CCR5-tropic HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, often preceded by rash or systemic allergic features.
tail_desc: Hepatitis C virus infection is a viral infectious disease of the liver caused by hepatitis C virus, leading to hepatic inflammation that may become chronic and progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, or hepatocellular carcinoma. Many patients are initially asymptomatic; others have fatigue, jaundice, or elevated liver enzymes. It affects exposed adults and is managed primarily with direct-acting antiviral therapy and liver disease monitoring.

head: Mexiletine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mexiletine is an oral class IB antiarrhythmic, structurally related to lidocaine, that blocks voltage-gated fast sodium channels, particularly in cardiac tissue, reducing abnormal ventricular automaticity and conduction. It is used for documented life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias and is also established for nondystrophic myotonia. Notable adverse effects include nausea, tremor, and dizziness; it is contraindicated in cardiogenic shock or untreated second- or third-degree AV block.
tail_desc: Familial sick sinus syndrome is an inherited cardiac conduction disorder affecting the sinoatrial node, the heart’s natural pacemaker. It results from genetic defects that impair impulse generation or conduction, causing sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or atrial arrhythmias, often in affected families across generations. Management is directed by symptoms and rhythm severity, commonly including monitoring and permanent pacemaker implantation when clinically indicated.

head: Glutethimide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glutethimide is a piperidine-dione sedative-hypnotic, structurally related to barbiturates, that acts as a central nervous system depressant by enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission, likely via the GABA\(_A\) receptor complex. It was formerly used for insomnia and sedation. Notable risks include respiratory depression and dependence, and it is contraindicated in porphyria.
tail_desc: Hyperbilirubinemia is a metabolic disorder of the hepatobiliary system characterized by elevated bilirubin in blood, caused by increased bilirubin production, impaired hepatic uptake or conjugation, or reduced biliary excretion. It commonly presents with jaundice and scleral icterus, and in neonates may cause kernicterus if severe. Management targets the cause and bilirubin reduction, including phototherapy, exchange transfusion, or treating liver or hemolytic disease.

head: Methadone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadone is a synthetic diphenylheptane opioid analgesic that acts primarily as a full agonist at the mu-opioid receptor; it also has NMDA receptor antagonism and inhibits norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for severe chronic pain and for maintenance or detoxification in opioid use disorder. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation, which can predispose to torsades de pointes.
tail_desc: Pseudotumor cerebri, or idiopathic intracranial hypertension, is a neurologic disorder characterized by elevated intracranial pressure without an intracranial mass or hydrocephalus, likely related to impaired cerebrospinal fluid absorption. It typically affects obese women of childbearing age and causes headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and papilledema. Management focuses on weight loss, acetazolamide, and vision-preserving procedures when severe.

head: Dalteparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dalteparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin. It is used for prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including perioperative and certain medical patients. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Intracerebral hemorrhage is a subtype of stroke in the central nervous system caused by bleeding directly into the brain parenchyma, usually from rupture of small vessels due to chronic hypertension or cerebral amyloid angiopathy. It presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, vomiting, and reduced consciousness, especially in older adults. Management focuses on urgent stabilization, blood pressure control, reversal of anticoagulation, and neurosurgical evaluation in selected cases.

head: Alfentanil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alfentanil is a short-acting synthetic opioid analgesic of the fentanyl (anilidopiperidine) class that acts primarily as a mu-opioid receptor agonist to produce analgesia and profound sedation. It is used intravenously as an analgesic adjunct in anesthesia and for induction or maintenance of general anesthesia. A notable risk is dose-dependent respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in patients with significant respiratory compromise without ventilatory support.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Clofedanol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clofedanol is a centrally acting non-opioid antitussive (cough suppressant), structurally classified as a diphenylmethane derivative. Its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well established, but it suppresses the cough reflex via central action. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of nonproductive cough. Notable adverse effects include drowsiness and dizziness.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Zileuton (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zileuton is an orally active leukotriene synthesis inhibitor, specifically a 5-lipoxygenase inhibitor, that blocks conversion of arachidonic acid to leukotrienes by targeting 5-lipoxygenase (ALOX5). It is approved for prophylaxis and chronic treatment of asthma. A notable safety concern is hepatotoxicity with potential liver enzyme elevations, so it is contraindicated in active liver disease.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Chloral hydrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloral hydrate is a sedative-hypnotic of the aldehyde/trichloroethanol class, historically used for short-term sedation and hypnosis, including pediatric procedural sedation. It is rapidly converted to trichloroethanol, which depresses the central nervous system, likely via potentiation of GABA\(_A\) receptor signaling. Notable adverse effects include respiratory depression; it is contraindicated in significant hepatic or renal impairment and severe cardiac disease.
tail_desc: Peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related gastrointestinal disorder characterized by mucosal ulceration in the stomach or proximal duodenum, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It typically presents with epigastric pain, dyspepsia, nausea, or bleeding, especially in affected adults. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, and avoiding ulcerogenic medications.

head: Flecainide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flecainide is a class Ic antiarrhythmic, a fluorinated benzamide derivative that blocks fast inward sodium current mainly via cardiac voltage-gated sodium channels (primarily Nav1.5/SCN5A), slowing conduction in atrial, ventricular, and His-Purkinje tissue. It is used for paroxysmal supraventricular arrhythmias, including atrial fibrillation/flutter, and certain life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias. A notable contraindication is structural heart disease or prior myocardial infarction due to proarrhythmic risk.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Loperamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Loperamide is a peripherally acting opioid antidiarrheal of the piperidine class that primarily agonizes μ-opioid receptors in the intestinal wall, reducing propulsive motility and intestinal secretion. It is used for symptomatic treatment of acute nonspecific diarrhea, chronic diarrhea, and to reduce ileostomy output. A notable risk is serious cardiac arrhythmias in overdose; it is contraindicated in acute dysentery and diarrhea associated with pseudomembranous colitis.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Almasilate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Almasilate is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Maprotiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Maprotiline is a tetracyclic antidepressant that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake by blocking the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively weak effects on serotonin uptake. It has been used for depressive disorders, including major depression with anxiety features. A notable risk is dose-related seizures, and it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or during the acute recovery phase after myocardial infarction.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Omeprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Omeprazole is a benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric parietal cell H+/K+-ATPase, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, peptic ulcer disease, erosive esophagitis, and Helicobacter pylori eradication regimens, and for hypersecretory states such as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Terfenadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Terfenadine is a second-generation piperidine antihistamine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It was used for symptomatic treatment of seasonal allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable safety concern is dose-related QT interval prolongation and torsades de pointes, especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors or hepatic impairment, leading to its withdrawal in many countries.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Digoxin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Digoxin is a cardiac glycoside that inhibits the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in cardiomyocytes, increasing intracellular calcium and enhancing cardiac contractility while augmenting vagal tone to slow atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used for symptomatic heart failure and ventricular rate control in atrial fibrillation or flutter. A notable adverse effect is potentially life-threatening arrhythmias, especially with toxicity.
tail_desc: Myocarditis is an inflammatory disease of the myocardium in the cardiovascular system, most often triggered by viral infection, immune-mediated injury, or toxins, causing myocyte damage and impaired cardiac function. It may affect children or adults and commonly presents with chest pain, dyspnea, fatigue, palpitations, arrhythmias, or heart failure. Management is mainly supportive, treating the cause when identified and complications such as arrhythmia or ventricular dysfunction.

head: Riluzole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Riluzole is a benzothiazole derivative and glutamate-modulating neuroprotective agent. Its mechanism is not fully defined but includes inhibition of voltage-gated sodium channels and reduction of presynaptic glutamate release. It is approved for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) to modestly prolong survival or time to tracheostomy. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, with liver enzyme elevations requiring monitoring.
tail_desc: Gilbert syndrome is a common, benign inherited liver disorder of bilirubin metabolism caused by reduced UGT1A1 activity, leading to impaired hepatic conjugation of bilirubin and mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. It often presents in adolescents or young adults with intermittent jaundice, especially during fasting, illness, stress, or exertion, while liver function is otherwise normal. Management is typically reassurance and avoidance of known triggers.

head: Apraclonidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Apraclonidine is a topical ophthalmic imidazoline and relatively selective alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonist that lowers intraocular pressure mainly by reducing aqueous humor production and partly increasing uveoscleral outflow. It is used short-term to prevent or control pressure spikes after anterior segment laser procedures and as adjunctive therapy in glaucoma. A notable adverse effect is allergic blepharoconjunctivitis.
tail_desc: Inherited aplastic anemia is a rare inherited bone marrow failure disorder of the hematopoietic system, caused by germline defects that impair marrow stem-cell function and blood-cell production. It typically presents in childhood or adolescence with pancytopenia, causing fatigue, infections, bleeding, and sometimes congenital anomalies depending on the syndrome. Management focuses on supportive transfusions, infection prevention, and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation when appropriate.

head: Isoflurane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoflurane is a halogenated ether volatile inhalational general anesthetic. Its anesthetic action is not fully defined but involves enhancement of inhibitory GABA_A and glycine signaling and effects on two-pore-domain potassium channels, with CNS depression. It is used to induce and maintain general anesthesia during surgery. A notable serious risk is malignant hyperthermia in susceptible patients.
tail_desc: Becker muscular dystrophy is an X-linked inherited neuromuscular disorder affecting skeletal and cardiac muscle, caused by mutations in the DMD gene that reduce or alter dystrophin function. It typically presents in boys or young men with slowly progressive proximal muscle weakness, calf hypertrophy, exercise intolerance, and possible cardiomyopathy. Management is supportive, including physiotherapy, cardiac surveillance, and multidisciplinary care.

head: Nitrofural (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofural (nitrofurazone) is a topical nitrofuran antibacterial. After enzymatic reduction of its nitro group in susceptible microbes, reactive intermediates damage bacterial DNA and other macromolecules, giving broad activity against many Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms. It has been used for topical prevention or treatment of skin infections in burns, grafts, wounds, and ulcers. A notable adverse effect is allergic contact dermatitis.
tail_desc: Vitamin B12 deficiency is a nutritional or malabsorptive disorder affecting hematologic and neurologic systems, caused by inadequate cobalamin intake, impaired absorption (for example pernicious anemia), or gastrointestinal disease. It commonly causes megaloblastic anemia, glossitis, paresthesias, gait disturbance, and cognitive changes, especially in older adults, vegans, or patients with malabsorption. Management involves identifying the cause and replacing vitamin B12, orally or parenterally.

head: Isosorbide dinitrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isosorbide dinitrate is an organic nitrate vasodilator used mainly for prevention and treatment of angina pectoris and in some settings heart failure. It is bioactivated to nitric oxide, which stimulates soluble guanylate cyclase, increases cGMP, and relaxes vascular smooth muscle, predominantly reducing venous preload. A notable adverse effect is headache, and it is contraindicated with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors because of severe hypotension risk.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Cyclopentolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclopentolate is an antimuscarinic mydriatic and cycloplegic agent, chemically a synthetic tertiary amine, that competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle. It is used ophthalmically to induce mydriasis and cycloplegia for eye examination and refraction. A notable adverse effect is transient ocular irritation, and it should be used cautiously in patients with angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Down syndrome is a chromosomal disorder affecting multiple organ systems, caused by full or partial extra chromosome 21 material, most often trisomy 21. It presents from birth with characteristic facial features, hypotonia, developmental delay, and intellectual disability, often with congenital heart defects and other medical comorbidities. Management is supportive and multidisciplinary, including screening for associated conditions, early intervention, educational support, and treatment of specific complications.

head: Enoxaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin (factor IIa). It is used to prevent and treat venous thromboembolism and in acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Vorinostat (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vorinostat is a hydroxamic acid histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor that blocks class I and II HDAC enzymes, increasing histone acetylation and altering gene expression to promote cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis. It is approved for treatment of cutaneous manifestations of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma in patients with persistent, progressive, or recurrent disease. Notable adverse effects include thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Arginine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Arginine (L-arginine) is a basic, semi-essential amino acid used as a nutritional/pharmaceutical supplement. It serves as the endogenous substrate for nitric oxide synthase, increasing nitric oxide production, and also participates in the urea cycle. Established uses include treatment of urea cycle disorders and supplementation in arginine deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Constipation disorder is a gastrointestinal motility and defecation disorder characterized by infrequent bowel movements, hard or difficult-to-pass stools, and a sensation of incomplete evacuation. It commonly results from slow colonic transit, pelvic floor dysfunction, medications, diet, or systemic illness, and affects all ages, especially older adults. Management typically emphasizes hydration, fiber, exercise, treating causes, and use of laxatives or pelvic floor therapy.

head: Glycine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glycine is a nonessential amino acid and inhibitory neurotransmitter used clinically as an irrigating solution in certain urologic procedures and as a component of parenteral nutrition. It acts as an inhibitory agonist at glycine receptors and a co-agonist at NMDA-type glutamate receptors. Excess systemic absorption of glycine irrigation fluid can cause hyperglycinemia, with visual disturbances or encephalopathy.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Dipotassium phosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dipotassium phosphate is an inorganic potassium salt and phosphate replacement/electrolyte supplement. It dissociates to provide phosphate for intracellular metabolic processes and potassium for maintenance of cellular membrane potential, without a single specific molecular target. Clinically, it is used to prevent or treat hypophosphatemia, including in parenteral nutrition formulations. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in patients with severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Desipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant, specifically a secondary amine dibenzazepine, that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake via the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with less effect on serotonin uptake. It is used for major depressive disorder and has also been used for neuropathic pain in some settings. A notable risk is cardiotoxicity, including arrhythmias, especially in overdose.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Pemetrexed (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pemetrexed is a folate antimetabolite chemotherapy agent that inhibits several folate-dependent enzymes, chiefly thymidylate synthase, and also dihydrofolate reductase and GARFT, thereby blocking purine and pyrimidine synthesis. It is approved for malignant pleural mesothelioma and for non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer. A notable toxicity is myelosuppression, and folic acid/vitamin B12 supplementation is routinely used to reduce it.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Diclofenamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diclofenamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor of the sulfonamide class. It reduces aqueous humor formation by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase, primarily in the ciliary processes of the eye. It is used to lower intraocular pressure in glaucoma, including chronic open-angle glaucoma. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Losartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Losartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a nonpeptide biphenyl tetrazole antihypertensive that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone effects. It is used for hypertension, diabetic nephropathy in type 2 diabetes, and to reduce stroke risk in certain patients with left ventricular hypertrophy. A notable contraindication is pregnancy due to fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Fluvoxamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fluvoxamine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, a 2-aminoethyl oxime ether derivative, that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for obsessive-compulsive disorder and, in some regions, major depressive disorder and social anxiety disorder. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with monoamine oxidase inhibitors, which are contraindicated.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Rizatriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rizatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, chemically an indole derivative, that acts as a selective agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors to promote cranial vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease due to risk of coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Vincristine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vincristine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds β-tubulin, inhibits microtubule polymerization, and arrests mitosis. It is used in combination chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia, lymphomas, and several pediatric solid tumors. A notable toxicity is dose-limiting peripheral neuropathy; intrathecal administration is contraindicated because it is uniformly fatal.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Lomustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lomustine is an oral nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that spontaneously forms reactive intermediates causing DNA and RNA alkylation and interstrand cross-linking, without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors, including gliomas, and for relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma in combination regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative myelosuppression; severe bone marrow suppression is a major contraindication.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Cyclosporine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclosporine is a calcineurin-inhibiting immunosuppressant, a cyclic undecapeptide derived from fungi. It binds cyclophilin, and the complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase, reducing NFAT activation and T-cell cytokine transcription, especially interleukin-2. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection and to treat conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and severe dry eye. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Lymphoma is a malignant neoplasm of lymphocytes, usually arising in lymph nodes or other lymphatic tissues and affecting the immune/lymphatic system; major categories are Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin lymphoma. It typically presents with painless lymphadenopathy, B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), or extranodal disease, occurring in both children and adults. Management depends on subtype and stage, commonly using chemotherapy, immunotherapy, radiation, and sometimes stem cell transplantation.

head: Gemfibrozil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Gemfibrozil is a fibric acid derivative lipid-lowering agent (fibrate) that activates peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPAR-α), reducing triglycerides and increasing HDL cholesterol. It is used for hypertriglyceridemia and mixed dyslipidemia, including prevention of pancreatitis in severe triglyceride elevation. Notable risks include gallstones and myopathy, especially when combined with statins; it is contraindicated in severe hepatic or renal dysfunction.
tail_desc: Choledocholithiasis is a biliary tract disorder in which gallstones are present in the common bile duct, causing partial or complete obstruction of bile flow in the hepatobiliary system. It commonly presents in adults, often with right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, cholestatic liver test abnormalities, and sometimes fever or pancreatitis. Management typically involves stone removal, usually by ERCP, followed by treatment of the underlying gallstone disease when indicated.

head: Diclofenamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diclofenamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor of the sulfonamide class. It reduces aqueous humor formation by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase, primarily in the ciliary processes of the eye. It is used to lower intraocular pressure in glaucoma, including chronic open-angle glaucoma. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Renal tubular acidosis is a group of kidney tubular disorders causing normal anion gap metabolic acidosis from impaired renal acid excretion or bicarbonate reabsorption. It may be inherited or acquired. Typical features include hyperchloremia, growth failure in children, nephrolithiasis, hypokalemia or hyperkalemia, and muscle weakness. Management targets the cause and corrects acidosis with alkali therapy, with electrolyte-specific treatment as needed.

head: Benzphetamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzphetamine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic of the amphetamine class, used short term as an adjunct to caloric restriction in the management of exogenous obesity. It acts indirectly by promoting release of norepinephrine, and to a lesser extent dopamine, in the central nervous system to suppress appetite. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and potential for abuse and dependence.
tail_desc: Thyrotoxicosis is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid system caused by excess circulating thyroid hormones, most commonly from hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease, toxic nodular goiter, or thyroiditis. It typically presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, especially in adults. Management targets the cause and symptom control, using beta-blockers and measures such as antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Penbutolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Penbutolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at β1 and β2 receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is generally contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Dimercaprol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dimercaprol is a dithiol heavy-metal chelating agent (2,3-dimercapto-1-propanol) that binds arsenic, mercury, gold, and, with calcium disodium edetate, lead, forming excretable complexes; it also reverses arsenic inhibition of sulfhydryl-dependent enzymes. It is used for acute arsenic, mercury, and gold poisoning and in lead encephalopathy. Notable adverse effects include hypertension and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated in iron or cadmium poisoning.
tail_desc: Glycogen storage disease comprises inherited metabolic disorders, usually affecting liver and/or muscle, caused by enzyme defects in glycogen synthesis or breakdown that lead to abnormal glycogen accumulation or impaired glucose release. Typical features include fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, growth delay, exercise intolerance, and muscle weakness, often presenting in infancy or childhood. Management is mainly dietary and supportive, with subtype-specific monitoring and complication prevention.

head: Histrelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Histrelin is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist, a peptide analog of GnRH. Continuous administration stimulates then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for central precocious puberty and advanced prostate cancer. Notable adverse effects include an initial hormone “flare” and local implant-site reactions.
tail_desc: Obesity is a chronic metabolic and nutritional disorder affecting whole-body energy homeostasis, characterized by excessive accumulation of adipose tissue due to sustained positive energy balance interacting with genetic, environmental, and behavioral factors. It typically presents in children or adults with elevated body mass index or central adiposity and related complications. Management centers on lifestyle intervention, treatment of comorbidities, anti-obesity pharmacotherapy, and bariatric surgery when indicated.

head: Ropinirole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ropinirole is a non-ergoline dopamine agonist of the aminotetralin class that acts primarily at dopamine D2-like receptors, with highest affinity for D3 receptors. It is used to treat signs and symptoms of Parkinson disease and moderate to severe primary restless legs syndrome. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, including sudden sleep onset; it may also cause hallucinations or orthostatic hypotension.
tail_desc: Drug-induced dyskinesia is a medication-related movement disorder of the central nervous system, most often caused by chronic exposure to dopamine receptor–blocking drugs or, in Parkinson disease, levodopa. It features involuntary choreiform, athetoid, or stereotyped movements, commonly affecting the face, tongue, trunk, or limbs, especially in older adults and long-treated patients. Management centers on reducing or changing the offending drug and symptomatic treatment when needed.

head: Sodium fluoride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluoride is an inorganic fluoride salt used as an anticaries agent and fluoride supplement. It acts topically and systemically by delivering fluoride ions that promote fluorapatite formation in tooth enamel and inhibit bacterial acid production. Established uses include prevention of dental caries in toothpaste, rinses, and supplements. Notable adverse effects include dental fluorosis with excessive exposure, especially during tooth development.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Antazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Antazoline is a first-generation ethanolamine antihistamine with anticholinergic properties. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conjunctivitis and rhinitis, often in topical ophthalmic or nasal formulations. A notable adverse effect is sedation; caution is warranted in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention due to anticholinergic effects.
tail_desc: Conjunctivitis is inflammation of the conjunctiva, an ocular surface disorder affecting the eye. It is most commonly caused by viral or bacterial infection, allergy, or irritant exposure, leading to conjunctival hyperemia, tearing or discharge, discomfort, and sometimes itching. It occurs in both children and adults. Management is cause-directed, with hygiene and supportive care, allergen avoidance, or antimicrobial therapy when indicated.

head: Triptorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triptorelin is a synthetic decapeptide gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that binds pituitary GnRH receptors; continuous administration suppresses LH and FSH release, lowering sex steroid production. It is used for prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial hormone “flare” that can transiently worsen symptoms.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Adrenal gland pheochromocytoma is a catecholamine-secreting neuroendocrine tumor of chromaffin cells in the adrenal medulla, affecting the endocrine system. Excess epinephrine and norepinephrine cause episodic or sustained hypertension, headaches, palpitations, sweating, and tremor; it occurs in adults and may be hereditary. Management centers on biochemical confirmation, localization, preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade, and surgical adrenalectomy.

head: Alprostadil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alprostadil is a prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) analog and vasodilatory smooth-muscle relaxant that activates prostaglandin E receptors, increasing cAMP. It is used to maintain ductus arteriosus patency in certain congenital heart defects and to treat erectile dysfunction. Notable adverse effects include hypotension; it is contraindicated in neonates with respiratory distress syndrome without cyanotic heart disease.
tail_desc: Lung disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the respiratory system, especially the lungs and airways. It may result from infection, inflammation, smoking-related injury, environmental exposures, genetic disorders, or vascular and interstitial pathology. Common features include cough, dyspnea, wheeze, chest discomfort, and impaired gas exchange across all ages. Management depends on cause and may include risk-factor reduction, medications, oxygen, pulmonary rehabilitation, or surgery.

head: Frovatriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Frovatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It acts by constricting dilated cranial blood vessels and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is approved for the acute treatment of migraine attacks with or without aura in adults, not for prophylaxis. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease because of coronary vasospasm risk.
tail_desc: Prinzmetal angina is a coronary vasospastic disorder of the cardiovascular system caused by transient spasm of an epicardial coronary artery, producing reversible myocardial ischemia without fixed obstructive disease in many cases. It typically causes recurrent rest angina, often nocturnal or early morning, with transient ST-segment elevation during attacks. Management centers on calcium channel blockers and nitrates, while avoiding triggers such as smoking.

head: Armodafinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Armodafinil is the R-enantiomer of modafinil, a wake-promoting central nervous system agent. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it is known to inhibit the dopamine transporter (DAT), increasing extracellular dopamine. It is approved to improve wakefulness in narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift work disorder. Notable adverse effects include headache and rare serious rash, including Stevens–Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Mitral valve prolapse is a valvular heart disorder in which one or both mitral valve leaflets bulge into the left atrium during systole, commonly due to myxomatous degeneration of the valve apparatus. It is often asymptomatic but may cause midsystolic click, palpitations, chest discomfort, or mitral regurgitation, typically in adults. Management is usually observation, with intervention for significant regurgitation or complications.

head: Sodium fluoride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluoride is an inorganic fluoride salt used as an anticaries agent and fluoride supplement. It acts topically and systemically by delivering fluoride ions that promote fluorapatite formation in tooth enamel and inhibit bacterial acid production. Established uses include prevention of dental caries in toothpaste, rinses, and supplements. Notable adverse effects include dental fluorosis with excessive exposure, especially during tooth development.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Auranofin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Auranofin is an oral gold(I)-containing disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD). It is thought to act through immunomodulatory effects, notably inhibition of thioredoxin reductase and related sulfhydryl-dependent enzymes, thereby suppressing inflammatory responses. It has been approved for rheumatoid arthritis. Important adverse effects include diarrhea, rash, stomatitis, and potentially serious thrombocytopenia or proteinuria; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Dermatitis is a common inflammatory disorder of the skin, often caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, irritant or allergic exposures, or immune dysregulation. It typically presents with pruritus, erythema, scaling, and sometimes vesicles or lichenification, affecting both children and adults depending on subtype. Management generally focuses on trigger avoidance, skin hydration with emollients, and anti-inflammatory topical therapies such as corticosteroids.

head: Cyclosporine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclosporine is a calcineurin-inhibiting immunosuppressant, a cyclic undecapeptide derived from fungi. It binds cyclophilin, and the complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase, reducing NFAT activation and T-cell cytokine transcription, especially interleukin-2. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection and to treat conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and severe dry eye. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Tetracaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetracaine is an ester-type local anesthetic of the para-aminobenzoic acid class that reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing impulse conduction. It is used for topical anesthesia of the eye and mucous membranes and in spinal anesthesia. A notable risk is systemic toxicity, including CNS and cardiovascular effects; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to ester local anesthetics.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Ketoprofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ketoprofen is a propionic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for pain and inflammatory conditions such as osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and dysmenorrhea. A notable risk is gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Metyrosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metyrosine is a tyrosine hydroxylase inhibitor and catecholamine synthesis blocker. By inhibiting tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate-limiting enzyme in catecholamine biosynthesis, it lowers production of dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. It is used primarily to manage catecholamine excess in patients with pheochromocytoma, including preoperative preparation. A notable adverse effect is sedation; it is also associated with extrapyramidal symptoms and should be used cautiously in psychiatric illness.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Povidone-iodine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Povidone-iodine is an iodophor antiseptic, a complex of polyvinylpyrrolidone with iodine, that slowly releases free iodine to oxidize and iodinate proteins, nucleotides, and membrane lipids of bacteria, fungi, viruses, and protozoa. It is used topically for skin disinfection, wound cleansing, and preoperative antisepsis. Notable adverse effects include local irritation and, rarely, iodine-induced thyroid dysfunction.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, often from fibrosis, ischemia, drugs, or congenital conduction disease. It may cause bradycardia, fatigue, dizziness, syncope, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on degree and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for advanced or symptomatic block.

head: Dantrolene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dantrolene is a direct-acting skeletal muscle relaxant of the hydantoin class that inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum by antagonizing the ryanodine receptor RyR1 in skeletal muscle. It is used to treat malignant hyperthermia and chronic spasticity from upper motor neuron disorders. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and it can also cause muscle weakness.
tail_desc: obsolete rare pulmonary disease is a disease or medical condition.

head: Bosentan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bosentan is an oral dual endothelin receptor antagonist, chemically a sulfonamide derivative, that blocks endothelin ETA and ETB receptors to reduce endothelin-1–mediated vasoconstriction and vascular proliferation. It is approved for pulmonary arterial hypertension to improve exercise capacity and delay clinical worsening. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity with elevated liver aminotransferases, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Aminacrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminacrine is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Methimazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methimazole is a thionamide antithyroid agent that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking iodination and coupling of tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin and thereby reducing thyroid hormone synthesis. It is used to treat hyperthyroidism, including Graves disease, and to prepare patients for thyroidectomy or radioiodine therapy. A notable serious adverse effect is agranulocytosis; it is generally avoided in the first trimester of pregnancy.
tail_desc: Myxedema is a severe manifestation of hypothyroidism affecting the endocrine system, caused by profound deficiency of thyroid hormone, with dermal glycosaminoglycan accumulation producing nonpitting edema. It typically occurs in adults with longstanding untreated hypothyroidism and features fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, dry skin, and facial puffiness; management centers on thyroid hormone replacement and treatment of precipitating factors, with myxedema coma requiring urgent intensive care.

head: Alfuzosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alfuzosin is a quinazoline-derived, selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist used primarily to treat lower urinary tract symptoms due to benign prostatic hyperplasia. By blocking postsynaptic alpha-1 receptors in prostatic and bladder neck smooth muscle, it reduces outlet resistance and improves urine flow. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension, and it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors.
tail_desc: Long QT syndrome is a cardiac electrical disorder affecting ventricular repolarization, characterized by prolongation of the QT interval on electrocardiography, usually due to inherited ion-channel defects or acquired drug/electrolyte effects. It predisposes children or adults to syncope, seizures, and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia including torsades de pointes, with risk of sudden death; management emphasizes trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, correction of reversible causes, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Irinotecan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irinotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. Its active metabolite SN-38 stabilizes the topoisomerase I-DNA complex, blocking DNA religation and causing lethal DNA damage during replication. It is used for metastatic colorectal cancer and in some regimens for other solid tumors. Notable toxicities include severe delayed diarrhea and myelosuppression, especially neutropenia.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Regadenoson (a drug)
