relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Regadenoson is a selective adenosine A2A receptor agonist, an adenosine derivative used as a coronary vasodilator. By activating A2A receptors on coronary vascular smooth muscle, it increases coronary blood flow and produces pharmacologic stress for radionuclide myocardial perfusion imaging in patients unable to exercise adequately. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and caution is warranted in patients with reactive airway disease.
tail_desc: Aortic valve stenosis is a valvular heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by narrowing and stiffening of the aortic valve, usually from age-related calcific degeneration or congenital bicuspid valve disease. It can cause exertional dyspnea, angina, syncope, and a systolic ejection murmur, especially in older adults. Management ranges from monitoring mild disease to valve replacement, including surgical or transcatheter approaches, for severe symptomatic cases.

head: Sulfathiazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfathiazole is a short-acting sulfonamide antibacterial of the thiazole-substituted sulfonamide class. It acts as a para-aminobenzoic acid analogue that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. Historically used for susceptible bacterial infections, it is now mainly encountered in topical or veterinary use. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, including severe sulfonamide skin eruptions.
tail_desc: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked inherited red blood cell enzymopathy causing reduced protection against oxidative stress, leading to episodic hemolytic anemia. It commonly affects males, especially in malaria-endemic populations, and may present with neonatal jaundice, pallor, dark urine, fatigue, and jaundice after infections, fava beans, or oxidant drugs. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and supportive care during hemolysis.

head: Benzyl alcohol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzyl alcohol is an aromatic alcohol used primarily as an antimicrobial preservative and, in topical formulations, as a pediculicide. Its pediculicidal effect is physical rather than receptor-mediated, obstructing lice respiratory spiracles and causing asphyxiation; no specific human molecular target is established. Clinically, it is used to preserve multidose injectable products and to treat head lice. A notable contraindication is use in neonates because of potentially fatal “gasping syndrome.”
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis disorder is a vascular disease of the circulatory system characterized by thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls, often related to aging, hypertension, diabetes, or lipid deposition in specific subtypes. It may be asymptomatic or cause ischemic complications such as angina, stroke, or peripheral vascular disease, especially in older adults. Management targets risk-factor control and treatment of cardiovascular complications.

head: Proparacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Proparacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic used topically in ophthalmology. It blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in sensory nerves, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It is indicated for short-term ocular surface anesthesia during diagnostic procedures, tonometry, and minor ophthalmic interventions. Notable adverse effects include transient stinging or burning, and repeated use can cause corneal epithelial toxicity.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Ranitidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ranitidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It was used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and other acid-related disorders. A notable adverse effect is headache, and use became widely withdrawn or suspended in many markets because of NDMA contamination concerns.
tail_desc: Diffuse gastric adenocarcinoma is a histologic subtype of stomach cancer in the gastrointestinal system, characterized by poorly cohesive, often signet-ring, malignant cells that infiltrate the gastric wall diffusely rather than forming a discrete mass. It commonly presents with weight loss, early satiety, abdominal pain, or linitis plastica, often in advanced disease. Management typically involves staging, surgical resection when feasible, and systemic chemotherapy or perioperative therapy.

head: Toremifene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Toremifene is a nonsteroidal triphenylethylene selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that binds estrogen receptors, acting mainly as an antiestrogen in breast tissue. It is approved for treatment of metastatic breast cancer in postmenopausal women with estrogen receptor–positive or unknown tumors. A notable safety concern is QT prolongation, and it is contraindicated in patients with congenital or acquired QT prolongation.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Aortic stenosis is a valvular heart disease of the cardiovascular system in which narrowing of the aortic valve obstructs left ventricular outflow, usually from age-related calcific degeneration, congenital bicuspid valve, or rheumatic scarring. It commonly affects older adults and causes exertional dyspnea, angina, syncope, and eventually heart failure; management ranges from monitoring to aortic valve replacement or transcatheter implantation when severe or symptomatic.

head: Dimenhydrinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dimenhydrinate is a first-generation H1 antihistamine and antiemetic, a salt of diphenhydramine with 8-chlorotheophylline. It acts primarily by antagonizing histamine H1 receptors, with additional antimuscarinic effects that suppress vestibular stimulation and the vomiting center. It is used for prevention and treatment of motion sickness, nausea, vomiting, and vertigo. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to dimenhydrinate or diphenhydramine.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Flumazenil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flumazenil is an imidazobenzodiazepine derivative that acts as a competitive antagonist at the benzodiazepine binding site on the GABA-A receptor. It is used to reverse benzodiazepine sedation and overdose, including postoperative sedation reversal. A notable risk is precipitating seizures, particularly in patients with benzodiazepine dependence or mixed overdose involving proconvulsant drugs.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Dutasteride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dutasteride is a synthetic 4-azasteroid, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor that blocks both type 1 and type 2 5-alpha-reductase (SRD5A1/SRD5A2), reducing conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone. It is approved for benign prostatic hyperplasia, alone or with tamsulosin. Notable adverse effects include decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and reduced ejaculate volume; it is contraindicated in pregnancy exposure.
tail_desc: Intraoperative floppy iris syndrome is an ophthalmic surgical complication affecting the anterior segment during cataract surgery, characterized by a flaccid iris that billows, prolapses toward incisions, and progressive intraoperative miosis. It is strongly associated with systemic alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist use, especially tamsulosin, likely from impaired iris dilator muscle tone. Management emphasizes preoperative recognition and modified surgical technique, often using pharmacologic mydriasis and mechanical pupil expansion devices.

head: Sildenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sildenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a pyrazolopyrimidinone derivative, that blocks PDE5 and increases cGMP-mediated smooth muscle relaxation, especially in the corpus cavernosum and pulmonary vasculature. It is approved for erectile dysfunction and pulmonary arterial hypertension. A notable contraindication is concomitant nitrate therapy, which can cause potentially severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Hearing loss disorder is a sensory disorder of the auditory system characterized by partial or complete reduction in hearing from conductive, sensorineural, or mixed mechanisms involving the outer/middle ear, cochlea, or auditory nerve pathways. It may be congenital or acquired and can present at any age with diminished sound perception, speech understanding difficulty, tinnitus, or delayed language development. Management depends on cause and severity, including medical or surgical treatment, hearing aids, cochlear implants, and rehabilitative support.

head: Rilpivirine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rilpivirine is a diarylpyrimidine non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) that binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, blocking viral DNA synthesis. It is used, in combination with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable safety concern is dose-related QT interval prolongation, and it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inducers that can markedly reduce rilpivirine exposure.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Topiramate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Topiramate is a sulfamate-substituted monosaccharide anticonvulsant that modulates voltage-gated sodium channels, enhances GABA-A–mediated inhibition, antagonizes AMPA/kainate glutamate receptors, and weakly inhibits carbonic anhydrase. It is used for epilepsy and migraine prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include cognitive slowing and metabolic acidosis; it is contraindicated with concomitant alcohol use in the extended-release formulation.
tail_desc: Diarrheal disease is a gastrointestinal disorder characterized by the passage of loose or watery stools, usually from intestinal infection, toxins, malabsorption, or inflammatory processes that impair fluid absorption and increase secretion. It commonly causes frequent stools, abdominal cramps, nausea, and dehydration, especially in young children and older adults. Management centers on oral or intravenous rehydration, electrolyte replacement, nutritional support, and treating the underlying cause when identified.

head: Pentostatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentostatin is a purine analog antineoplastic agent that potently inhibits adenosine deaminase, disrupting purine metabolism and causing accumulation of deoxyadenosine nucleotides with resultant lymphocyte toxicity. It is an established treatment for hairy cell leukemia. A notable adverse effect is severe immunosuppression with risk of serious infections; myelosuppression can also occur.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Lovastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lovastatin is a fungal-derived statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat primary hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to reduce cardiovascular risk. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, and it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Methadone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadone is a synthetic diphenylheptane opioid analgesic that acts primarily as a full agonist at the mu-opioid receptor; it also has NMDA receptor antagonism and inhibits norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for severe chronic pain and for maintenance or detoxification in opioid use disorder. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation, which can predispose to torsades de pointes.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Fosamprenavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fosamprenavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor prodrug of amprenavir. After conversion to amprenavir, it inhibits the viral aspartyl protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is rash, and it is contraindicated with drugs highly dependent on CYP3A4 for clearance.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Latanoprost (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Latanoprost is a prostaglandin F2α analog used as an ophthalmic antiglaucoma agent. It is a prodrug that activates the prostanoid FP receptor in the eye, increasing uveoscleral outflow of aqueous humor and thereby lowering intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is gradual, often permanent, increased brown pigmentation of the iris.
tail_desc: Iritis is inflammation of the iris, typically classified as anterior uveitis of the eye. It may be idiopathic or associated with autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or drug exposure. Patients usually present with eye pain, redness, photophobia, and blurred vision. Management generally involves prompt ophthalmologic evaluation, topical corticosteroids and cycloplegic agents, and treatment of any underlying cause.

head: Magnesium chloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium chloride is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an electrolyte replacement and mineral supplement. It dissociates to provide magnesium, an essential cofactor for many enzymes and a modulator of neuromuscular transmission, membrane stability, and cardiac conduction, without a single specific receptor target. It is used to prevent or treat magnesium deficiency; adverse effects can include diarrhea, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment because of hypermagnesemia risk.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Chromium picolinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chromium picolinate is a nutritional supplement consisting of trivalent chromium chelated to picolinic acid, used to provide chromium in deficiency states. Its mechanism is not fully established; chromium is thought to enhance insulin signaling and glucose metabolism, but no specific primary molecular target is well defined. It is not an FDA-approved drug, and reported adverse effects include renal toxicity.
tail_desc: Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory granulomatous disease that most often affects the lungs and intrathoracic lymph nodes. It is characterized by noncaseating granuloma formation of unclear cause, likely involving immune dysregulation in genetically susceptible people. Common features include cough, dyspnea, fatigue, skin or eye involvement, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, typically in young to middle-aged adults; management ranges from observation to corticosteroids and other immunosuppressive therapy for significant organ disease.

head: Tetrabenazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetrabenazine is a benzoquinolizine derivative and vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) inhibitor that depletes presynaptic dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine stores. It is approved to treat chorea associated with Huntington disease and is also used for hyperkinetic movement disorders such as tardive dyskinesia. Notable risks include depression and suicidality, and it is contraindicated in actively suicidal patients or those with untreated depression.
tail_desc: Lingual-facial-buccal dyskinesia is a hyperkinetic movement disorder of the extrapyramidal nervous system characterized by involuntary, repetitive movements of the tongue, lips, cheeks, and jaw. It most often occurs as a tardive dyskinesia related to chronic dopamine-receptor blocking drugs, especially antipsychotics, and is seen mainly in adults receiving long-term treatment. Management centers on reducing or changing the offending drug and symptomatic treatment.

head: Cyclopentolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclopentolate is an antimuscarinic mydriatic and cycloplegic agent, chemically a synthetic tertiary amine, that competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle. It is used ophthalmically to induce mydriasis and cycloplegia for eye examination and refraction. A notable adverse effect is transient ocular irritation, and it should be used cautiously in patients with angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Calcium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: Calcium acetate is a calcium salt used as a phosphate binder. In the gastrointestinal tract it dissociates to release calcium, which binds dietary phosphate to form insoluble complexes and lowers phosphate absorption; it has no specific receptor target. It is established for control of hyperphosphatemia in patients with end-stage renal disease, and a notable risk is hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Nandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nandrolone is a synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid and testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein synthesis and erythropoiesis. Its ester forms, such as nandrolone decanoate, have been used for anemia associated with chronic kidney disease and some wasting states. Notable adverse effects include virilization and other androgenic effects; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in prostate or male breast carcinoma.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Carmustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carmustine is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms DNA interstrand cross-links and carbamoylates proteins, disrupting DNA replication and RNA/protein synthesis without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors and for multiple myeloma, lymphoma, and other hematologic malignancies, including in conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Infectious meningitis is an acute infection of the meninges within the central nervous system, usually caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi, or, less often, parasites. It produces meningeal inflammation with fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, nausea, and altered mental status; infants, older adults, and immunocompromised patients may present atypically. Management typically requires urgent pathogen-directed antimicrobial therapy, supportive care, and close monitoring for complications.

head: Diflunisal (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diflunisal is a salicylate-derived nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. It is used for mild to moderate pain and for osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with aspirin/NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Active peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related disorder of the upper gastrointestinal tract characterized by an active mucosal ulcer in the stomach or duodenum, most commonly due to Helicobacter pylori infection or NSAID use. Patients typically have epigastric pain or dyspepsia and may develop bleeding or perforation. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, stopping ulcerogenic drugs, and treating complications.

head: Furosemide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Furosemide is a loop diuretic, chemically a sulfamoylanthranilic acid derivative, that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter (NKCC2, SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used for edema associated with heart failure, cirrhosis, or renal disease, and for hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Quinine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quinine is a cinchona alkaloid antimalarial that acts in Plasmodium blood stages by inhibiting heme detoxification, causing toxic heme accumulation within the parasite food vacuole. It is used primarily for treatment of uncomplicated and severe falciparum malaria, often in combination regimens. A notable adverse effect is cinchonism; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to quinine or a history of quinine-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Typical hemolytic-uremic syndrome is a thrombotic microangiopathy affecting the kidneys and microvasculature, usually triggered by Shiga toxin–producing bacterial infection, classically after diarrheal illness. It is characterized by the triad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury, most often in children. Management is mainly supportive, including fluid and electrolyte control, dialysis when needed, and avoidance of unnecessary antibiotics or antimotility agents.

head: Clindamycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clindamycin is a lincosamide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible anaerobic and gram-positive bacterial infections, including skin and soft-tissue, bone and joint, intra-abdominal, and some dental infections, and is also used topically for acne. A notable adverse effect is Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea/pseudomembranous colitis.
tail_desc: Inflammatory bowel disease is a chronic relapsing inflammatory disorder of the gastrointestinal tract, mainly comprising Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis. It is thought to result from dysregulated immune responses to intestinal microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals. Typical features include abdominal pain, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, weight loss, and fatigue, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood; management aims to induce and maintain remission with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, biologic, nutritional, and sometimes surgical therapy.

head: Cysteamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cysteamine is an aminothiol and cystine-depleting agent used primarily to treat nephropathic cystinosis. Within lysosomes, it reacts with cystine to form cysteine and a cysteine-cysteamine mixed disulfide, enabling export through existing lysine transport pathways and reducing cystine accumulation. It is also used ophthalmically for corneal cystine crystals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and sulfurous body odor.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Tolterodine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tolterodine is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a tertiary amine used for overactive bladder with urinary urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence. It acts as a competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, functionally reducing detrusor muscle contractions in the bladder. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Tramadol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tramadol is a centrally acting synthetic opioid analgesic of the aminocyclohexanol class. It provides pain relief through weak agonism at the mu-opioid receptor and inhibition of norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for moderate to moderately severe pain. A notable risk is seizures, and it is contraindicated in significant respiratory depression or acute intoxication with alcohol or other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Cirrhosis of the liver is a chronic end-stage liver disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by diffuse fibrosis and regenerative nodules that distort hepatic architecture, most often from chronic alcohol use, viral hepatitis, or nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. Patients may be asymptomatic initially, then develop jaundice, ascites, portal hypertension, variceal bleeding, and encephalopathy. Management targets the cause, complications, nutrition, and possible liver transplantation.

head: Tamoxifen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM), a triphenylethylene derivative, that primarily antagonizes estrogen receptor signaling in breast tissue. It is used for hormone receptor–positive breast cancer treatment and prevention, including adjuvant and metastatic settings. Important adverse effects include increased risk of venous thromboembolism and endometrial cancer, so it is generally avoided in patients with prior thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Riluzole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Riluzole is a benzothiazole derivative and glutamate-modulating neuroprotective agent. Its mechanism is not fully defined but includes inhibition of voltage-gated sodium channels and reduction of presynaptic glutamate release. It is approved for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) to modestly prolong survival or time to tracheostomy. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, with liver enzyme elevations requiring monitoring.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Ibandronate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ibandronate is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds bone hydroxyapatite and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, reducing bone resorption. It is approved for prevention and treatment of postmenopausal osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis with oral formulations; it is contraindicated in patients with esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying or inability to remain upright.
tail_desc: Dyskinesia of the esophagus is an esophageal motility disorder of the gastrointestinal system characterized by abnormal or uncoordinated smooth-muscle contractions that impair normal peristalsis. It typically presents with dysphagia, noncardiac chest pain, or odynophagia, usually in adults. Management is directed by the specific motility pattern and may include dietary modification, treatment of contributing reflux, smooth-muscle relaxant therapy, and specialist evaluation.

head: Selegiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Selegiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class. By inhibiting MAO-B, it reduces dopamine breakdown in the central nervous system. It is established for Parkinson disease and, in transdermal form, major depressive disorder. Notable risks include insomnia and potentially serious hypertensive or serotonergic interactions with contraindicated sympathomimetic or serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Major affective disorder is not a standard, specific diagnostic term in contemporary medicine; it has been used inconsistently to refer broadly to severe mood disorders such as major depressive disorder or bipolar disorder. Because the exact entity is ambiguous, its pathology, clinical features, affected population, and management cannot be described reliably without further specification.

head: Silodosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Silodosin is a selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist, with high affinity for the alpha-1A subtype in prostatic and bladder neck smooth muscle. By blocking these receptors, it relaxes lower urinary tract smooth muscle and improves urine flow. It is indicated for signs and symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia. A notable adverse effect is abnormal or retrograde ejaculation.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Polythiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Polythiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the sodium-chloride cotransporter (NCC/SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used to treat hypertension and edema, including edema associated with heart failure. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; thiazides are also contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Cholecystolithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, specifically stones within the gallbladder, usually formed by precipitation of cholesterol or pigment in bile. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic patients develop episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and complications can include cholecystitis or biliary obstruction. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic disease.

head: Epirubicin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Epirubicin is an anthracycline antineoplastic antibiotic, structurally related to doxorubicin. It intercalates into DNA, inhibits topoisomerase II, and generates free radicals, impairing DNA replication and transcription. It is used in breast cancer and other solid tumors, including as adjuvant therapy in early breast cancer. A notable toxicity is cumulative dose-related cardiomyopathy/heart failure.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Telbivudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telbivudine is a synthetic thymidine nucleoside analog antiviral active against hepatitis B virus. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HBV DNA polymerase by competing with thymidine substrates and causing DNA chain termination. It is used for chronic hepatitis B infection. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, and peripheral neuropathy risk increases with pegylated interferon alfa coadministration.
tail_desc: Muscular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting skeletal muscle and the neuromuscular system, caused by diverse mechanisms including inherited structural protein defects, inflammation, metabolic abnormalities, or toxic injury. Typical features include progressive weakness, fatigue, cramps, myalgia, and functional impairment across children or adults, depending on etiology. Management is cause-directed and supportive, including rehabilitation, exercise guidance, medications for specific subtypes, and monitoring for respiratory or cardiac complications.

head: Celecoxib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Celecoxib is a selective nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the diaryl-substituted pyrazole class that primarily inhibits cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2, PTGS2), reducing prostaglandin synthesis and thereby inflammation and pain. It is approved for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute pain, and primary dysmenorrhea. A notable risk is serious cardiovascular thrombotic events; it is contraindicated for perioperative pain in coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate is a synthetic progestin, a caproate ester of hydroxyprogesterone, that acts as an agonist at the progesterone receptor. It has been used clinically to reduce risk of recurrent preterm birth in certain pregnant patients with a prior singleton spontaneous preterm delivery. Notable adverse effects include thromboembolic events, and it is contraindicated in patients with hormone-sensitive malignancy or active thrombosis.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Naratriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naratriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It treats acute migraine attacks, with or without aura, by causing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. Notable adverse effects include paresthesia, dizziness, and chest or throat tightness; it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease and certain cerebrovascular syndromes.
tail_desc: Peripheral arterial disease is an atherosclerotic vascular disorder of the peripheral arteries, most often affecting the lower limbs. Arterial narrowing reduces blood flow, causing exertional leg pain (intermittent claudication), diminished pulses, and in advanced cases rest pain, ulcers, or tissue loss. It is common in older adults and smokers. Management focuses on risk-factor modification, antiplatelet and statin therapy, supervised exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Haddad syndrome is a rare congenital neurocristopathy affecting autonomic control of breathing and the enteric nervous system, defined by congenital central hypoventilation syndrome with Hirschsprung disease, often due to PHOX2B mutations. It typically presents in neonates with hypoventilation, especially during sleep, intestinal obstruction from aganglionosis, and autonomic dysfunction. Management centers on lifelong ventilatory support, surgical treatment of Hirschsprung disease, and multidisciplinary monitoring.

head: Sulfamethoxazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamethoxazole is a sulfonamide antibacterial that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby DNA precursor production; it is commonly combined with trimethoprim for synergistic activity. Established uses include urinary tract infections, acute otitis media, bronchitis exacerbations, shigellosis, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia treatment or prophylaxis. Notable risks include severe hypersensitivity reactions such as Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Domperidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Domperidone is a benzimidazole-derived dopamine D2 receptor antagonist with peripheral prokinetic and antiemetic activity. By blocking D2 receptors in the gastrointestinal tract and chemoreceptor trigger zone, it enhances gastric emptying and reduces nausea and vomiting. It is used for symptomatic relief of nausea, vomiting, and gastroparesis-related dyspepsia. A notable risk is QT prolongation with potentially serious ventricular arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Famotidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Famotidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist of the guanidine/thiazole class that competitively blocks H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells, reducing basal and stimulated gastric acid secretion. It is used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypersecretory states such as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Notable adverse effects include headache and dizziness, and dose reduction is recommended in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Inositol nicotinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Inositol nicotinate is a nicotinic acid ester (hexanicotinate of inositol) used as a peripheral vasodilator. It acts as a slow-release source of niacin, although its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well defined beyond promoting vasodilation. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of peripheral vascular disorders such as intermittent claudication and Raynaud-type symptoms. Flushing is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Tetracaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetracaine is an ester-type local anesthetic of the para-aminobenzoic acid class that reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing impulse conduction. It is used for topical anesthesia of the eye and mucous membranes and in spinal anesthesia. A notable risk is systemic toxicity, including CNS and cardiovascular effects; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to ester local anesthetics.
tail_desc: Adult-onset myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system, usually caused by antibodies against acetylcholine receptor or related postsynaptic proteins, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It presents in adults with fluctuating, fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunotherapy, and thymectomy in selected patients.

head: Butobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butobarbital is a barbiturate sedative-hypnotic, used primarily for the short-term treatment of insomnia and occasionally for preoperative sedation. Like other barbiturates, it enhances GABAergic inhibition by positively modulating the GABA-A receptor, increasing chloride ion influx and depressing central nervous system activity. A notable adverse effect is respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in patients with porphyria.
tail_desc: Substance-related disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting the brain and behavior, characterized by maladaptive use of alcohol, drugs, or medications that leads to intoxication, dependence, withdrawal, and impaired control despite harm. It can occur across all age groups, often with craving, social or occupational dysfunction, and medical complications. Management typically combines screening, psychosocial treatment, relapse prevention, and, for some substances, medication-assisted therapy and supervised withdrawal care.

head: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate is a synthetic progestin, a caproate ester of hydroxyprogesterone, that acts as an agonist at the progesterone receptor. It has been used clinically to reduce risk of recurrent preterm birth in certain pregnant patients with a prior singleton spontaneous preterm delivery. Notable adverse effects include thromboembolic events, and it is contraindicated in patients with hormone-sensitive malignancy or active thrombosis.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Tryptophan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tryptophan is an essential aromatic amino acid used as a nutritional supplement rather than a conventional drug. It serves as a biochemical precursor of serotonin, melatonin, and niacin, without a single defined pharmacologic target. It has been used for dietary supplementation and, historically, for sleep or mood symptoms, though not broadly established as an approved therapeutic indication. Notable risks include eosinophilia-myalgia syndrome from contaminated products and possible serotonin syndrome with serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Chlorambucil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorambucil is a nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the bifunctional alkylating antineoplastic class. It forms covalent DNA cross-links, primarily at the N7 position of guanine, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and triggering cell death. It is used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia and certain lymphomas. A notable adverse effect is dose-related myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Postinflammatory pulmonary fibrosis is a chronic interstitial lung disorder of the respiratory system in which prior pulmonary inflammation, such as severe infection or other inflammatory lung injury, heals with fibrotic scarring of the lung parenchyma. Patients may develop persistent dyspnea, dry cough, reduced exercise tolerance, and restrictive impairment on testing. Management focuses on treating the underlying cause, supportive care, and monitoring respiratory function.

head: Lurasidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lurasidone is an atypical antipsychotic of the benzisothiazole class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at 5-HT7 and 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar depression. A notable adverse effect is akathisia; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Synephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Synephrine is a naturally occurring protoalkaloid phenylethanolamine, structurally related to ephedrine, found notably in bitter orange (Citrus aurantium). It acts primarily as a sympathomimetic agonist at adrenergic receptors, with reported activity especially at beta-3 and some alpha receptors. It is not an approved prescription drug for established clinical indications in major jurisdictions; use is mainly in supplements. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, so it is contraindicated in significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Thyroid gland disease comprises disorders of the endocrine thyroid, including functional abnormalities and structural lesions such as goiter, thyroiditis, nodules, and cancer. Causes include autoimmune processes, iodine imbalance, inflammation, and neoplasia. Patients may present with hypo- or hyperthyroid symptoms, neck enlargement, or compressive signs. Management depends on cause and may involve hormone replacement, antithyroid therapy, radioactive iodine, surgery, or surveillance.

head: Nicardipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Nicardipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation with relatively little direct cardiac depression. It is used to treat hypertension, including acute severe hypertension with the intravenous formulation, and chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is reflex tachycardia; it is contraindicated in advanced aortic stenosis.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Carvedilol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carvedilol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic blocker of the carbazole derivative class with additional alpha1-adrenergic antagonism, reducing heart rate, myocardial contractility, and peripheral vascular resistance. It is established for hypertension, heart failure, and left ventricular dysfunction after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma and severe bradycardia or heart block.
tail_desc: Sick sinus syndrome is a cardiac conduction disorder of the sinoatrial node causing abnormal impulse formation, usually from age-related fibrotic degeneration or other atrial disease. It typically affects older adults and presents with sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, tachy-brady episodes, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or palpitations. Management focuses on treating reversible causes and, for symptomatic bradyarrhythmias, permanent pacemaker implantation.

head: Milnacipran (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Milnacipran is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively greater norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. It is approved in some countries for major depressive disorder and in the United States for management of fibromyalgia. Notable adverse effects include nausea and tachycardia; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Tegaserod (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tegaserod is an indole carbazole derivative and selective serotonin 5-HT4 receptor partial agonist that enhances gastrointestinal motility and intestinal secretion via enteric neurons. It is approved for irritable bowel syndrome with constipation in certain adult women and has also been used for chronic idiopathic constipation. A notable concern is ischemic cardiovascular events, making use contraindicated in patients with prior myocardial infarction, stroke, or angina.
tail_desc: Intestinal obstruction is a gastrointestinal disorder in which partial or complete blockage of the small or large bowel prevents normal passage of contents, due to mechanical causes such as adhesions, hernias, tumors, or volvulus, or to impaired motility (ileus). It typically causes crampy abdominal pain, distension, vomiting, and constipation or obstipation, occurs across age groups depending on cause, and is managed with urgent evaluation, fluid resuscitation, bowel decompression, and treatment of the underlying cause, including surgery when indicated.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Thioridazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thioridazine is a phenothiazine antipsychotic of the piperidine subclass that acts mainly by antagonizing dopamine D2 receptors, with additional anticholinergic and alpha-adrenergic blocking effects. It has been used for schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders. A notable risk is dose-related QT prolongation with potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmias, making use contraindicated in patients with significant cardiac arrhythmia.
tail_desc: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is a rare, life-threatening neurologic and systemic reaction to dopamine-blocking antipsychotic drugs or abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic therapy. It is characterized by hyperthermia, severe generalized rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability, typically with elevated creatine kinase. It occurs in patients exposed to these agents and is managed by immediate drug cessation, intensive supportive care, and sometimes dantrolene or bromocriptine.

head: Perindopril (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Perindopril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug converted to the active metabolite perindoprilat. It lowers angiotensin II formation and aldosterone secretion by inhibiting ACE (kininase II), thereby reducing vasoconstriction and increasing bradykinin. It is used for hypertension, heart failure, and stable coronary artery disease; notable risks include dry cough and potentially life-threatening angioedema, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Arginine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (a disease)
head_desc: Arginine (L-arginine) is a basic, semi-essential amino acid used as a nutritional/pharmaceutical supplement. It serves as the endogenous substrate for nitric oxide synthase, increasing nitric oxide production, and also participates in the urea cycle. Established uses include treatment of urea cycle disorders and supplementation in arginine deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin is a benign inherited hemoglobinopathy of the hematologic system caused by genetic variants that sustain gamma-globin expression and continued HbF production after birth. Affected individuals, often identified incidentally, are usually asymptomatic with normal or mild microcytic blood findings; the condition can lessen severity of sickle cell disease or beta-thalassemia. Management is typically reassurance, diagnosis confirmation, and genetic counseling.

head: Bismuth subsalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bismuth subsalicylate is a bismuth-containing salicylate antidiarrheal and gastroprotective agent. It acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract, where bismuth has antimicrobial and mucosal protective effects, while salicylate inhibits cyclooxygenase and reduces intestinal prostaglandin-mediated secretion and inflammation. It is used for diarrhea, dyspepsia, heartburn, and nausea. A notable adverse effect is black discoloration of stools and tongue.
tail_desc: Peripheral neuropathy is a disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused by damage to peripheral nerves, commonly from diabetes, toxins, nutritional deficiencies, infections, or immune-mediated disease. It typically presents with distal numbness, tingling, burning pain, weakness, and reduced reflexes, often in a length-dependent “stocking-glove” pattern, especially in adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the cause, symptom control, and rehabilitation or foot care when needed.

head: Fosamprenavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fosamprenavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor prodrug of amprenavir. After conversion to amprenavir, it inhibits the viral aspartyl protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is rash, and it is contraindicated with drugs highly dependent on CYP3A4 for clearance.
tail_desc: Hypertriglyceridemia is a metabolic lipid disorder affecting the cardiovascular and pancreatic systems, defined by elevated plasma triglycerides from primary genetic defects or secondary causes such as diabetes, obesity, alcohol use, or medications. It is often asymptomatic but can cause eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, and acute pancreatitis, especially at very high levels. Management targets underlying causes, lifestyle change, and triglyceride-lowering therapy.

head: Miglitol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Miglitol is an oral alpha-glucosidase inhibitor, a deoxynojirimycin derivative, that competitively inhibits intestinal brush-border alpha-glucosidases, delaying carbohydrate digestion and reducing postprandial glucose excursions. It is used to improve glycemic control in type 2 diabetes mellitus, typically as adjunctive therapy to diet. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal flatulence and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in inflammatory bowel disease or intestinal obstruction.
tail_desc: Inflammatory bowel disease is a chronic relapsing inflammatory disorder of the gastrointestinal tract, mainly comprising Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis. It is thought to result from dysregulated immune responses to intestinal microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals. Typical features include abdominal pain, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, weight loss, and fatigue, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood; management aims to induce and maintain remission with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, biologic, nutritional, and sometimes surgical therapy.

head: Travoprost (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Travoprost is a prostaglandin F2α analog ophthalmic antiglaucoma agent that acts as an agonist at the prostanoid FP receptor, increasing uveoscleral outflow of aqueous humor and thereby lowering intraocular pressure. It is used for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is conjunctival hyperemia; it can also cause gradual increased brown pigmentation of the iris.
tail_desc: Iritis is inflammation of the iris, typically classified as anterior uveitis of the eye. It may be idiopathic or associated with autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or drug exposure. Patients usually present with eye pain, redness, photophobia, and blurred vision. Management generally involves prompt ophthalmologic evaluation, topical corticosteroids and cycloplegic agents, and treatment of any underlying cause.

head: Efavirenz (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Efavirenz is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) used as part of combination antiretroviral therapy for HIV-1 infection. It binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Established indications include treatment of HIV-1 in adults and children in combination regimens. Notable adverse effects include rash and prominent central nervous system symptoms such as dizziness, insomnia, and vivid dreams.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Dantrolene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dantrolene is a direct-acting skeletal muscle relaxant of the hydantoin class that inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum by antagonizing the ryanodine receptor RyR1 in skeletal muscle. It is used to treat malignant hyperthermia and chronic spasticity from upper motor neuron disorders. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and it can also cause muscle weakness.
tail_desc: Familial periodic paralysis is a group of inherited skeletal muscle channelopathies causing episodic flaccid weakness from abnormal muscle membrane excitability, most often due to ion-channel mutations affecting potassium handling. Attacks typically begin in childhood or adolescence, may be triggered by rest after exercise or diet, and can involve hypo- or hyperkalemia. Management centers on trigger avoidance, correction of potassium abnormalities, and prophylactic therapy such as carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.

head: Dapsone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dapsone is a synthetic sulfone antimicrobial with anti-inflammatory activity. It inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis in susceptible organisms, and is established for treatment of leprosy and for dermatitis herpetiformis; it is also used for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis in selected patients. A notable adverse effect is dose-related hemolytic anemia, especially in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Protriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Protriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzocycloheptadiene family. It acts primarily by inhibiting neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is used for treatment of major depressive disorder. A notable adverse effect is anticholinergic toxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Hiatus hernia is a gastrointestinal disorder in which part of the stomach herniates through the diaphragmatic esophageal hiatus into the thorax, usually from laxity of the phrenoesophageal membrane or increased intra-abdominal pressure. It commonly affects older adults and people with obesity, causing gastroesophageal reflux, heartburn, regurgitation, chest discomfort, or dysphagia. Management is mainly lifestyle measures and acid suppression, with surgery for refractory symptoms or complications.

head: Rivaroxaban (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rivaroxaban is an oral direct factor Xa inhibitor anticoagulant, a small-molecule oxazolidinone derivative.
