It selectively inhibits free and clot-bound factor Xa, reducing thrombin generation and fibrin clot formation. Established indications include prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, and stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it is contraindicated in active pathological bleeding.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Amifostine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amifostine is a cytoprotective thiophosphate prodrug dephosphorylated by alkaline phosphatase to the active free thiol WR-1065, which scavenges reactive oxygen species and donates hydrogen ions to protect normal tissues from cisplatin- and radiation-induced DNA damage. It is used to reduce cumulative nephrotoxicity from cisplatin and xerostomia during head and neck radiotherapy. Notable adverse effects include severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Transient ischemic attack is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by transient focal cerebral, retinal, or spinal cord ischemia without acute infarction, usually from embolic or thrombotic vascular occlusion. It produces sudden, brief focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or vision loss, often in older adults with vascular risk factors, and requires urgent stroke evaluation and secondary prevention.

head: Parnaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Parnaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and to a lesser extent thrombin (factor IIa). It is used for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolic disorders, including postoperative deep vein thrombosis. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major hemorrhage or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Retinitis is inflammation of the retina, a disorder of the eye’s neurosensory visual system. It commonly results from infection, especially viral, fungal, or parasitic causes, but may also occur with autoimmune inflammatory disease. Patients develop blurred vision, floaters, photophobia, and visual field loss; management targets the cause, typically with urgent ophthalmologic evaluation and anti-infective or anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Tazobactam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tazobactam is a penicillanic acid sulfone beta-lactamase inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits many class A beta-lactamases, protecting susceptible beta-lactam antibiotics such as piperacillin from enzymatic degradation. It is used in fixed combinations, notably piperacillin/tazobactam, for treatment of susceptible bacterial infections including intra-abdominal, skin and soft-tissue, and lower respiratory tract infections. Hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Lopinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lopinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, commonly coformulated with ritonavir to boost exposure via CYP3A inhibition. It inhibits the viral HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and producing immature, noninfectious virions. It is approved, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it also has important CYP3A-mediated drug interaction contraindications.
tail_desc: Myocardial disorder refers broadly to diseases of the heart muscle within the cardiovascular system, often involving myocyte injury, inflammation, ischemia, infiltration, or structural remodeling that impairs contraction or relaxation. Clinical manifestations commonly include dyspnea, chest discomfort, palpitations, fatigue, syncope, or heart failure, across varied age groups depending on cause. Management is cause-directed, typically combining treatment of the underlying condition with standard heart-failure or arrhythmia care.

head: Acetazolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acetazolamide is a sulfonamide-derived carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reversibly inhibits carbonic anhydrase isoenzymes, reducing bicarbonate reabsorption and aqueous humor and cerebrospinal fluid production. It is used for glaucoma, prevention and treatment of acute mountain sickness, adjunctive therapy in some epilepsies, and edema. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity and severe hepatic disease.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Trimethadione (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethadione is an oxazolidinedione anticonvulsant used primarily for refractory absence (petit mal) seizures. Its active metabolite, dimethadione, reduces thalamic low-threshold calcium currents by blocking T-type calcium channels, suppressing the cortical rhythm underlying absence seizures. A notable adverse effect is severe teratogenicity, so it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Optic nerve disease comprises disorders of the optic nerve within the visual system, caused by inflammation, ischemia, compression, hereditary degeneration, trauma, or toxic-metabolic injury leading to axonal damage and impaired signal transmission. Typical features include decreased visual acuity, visual field defects, reduced color vision, afferent pupillary defect, and optic disc changes. Management targets the underlying cause, with urgent ophthalmic or neurologic evaluation to preserve vision.

head: Cevimeline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: Cevimeline is a muscarinic cholinergic agonist, a quinuclidine derivative, that primarily stimulates M3 muscarinic receptors to increase exocrine gland secretion, especially salivary flow. It is approved for treatment of dry mouth (xerostomia) in patients with Sjögren’s syndrome. Notable adverse effects include excessive sweating, and it is contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma and in conditions where miosis is undesirable, such as narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Drug-induced liver injury is an adverse hepatic disorder caused by medications, herbal products, or supplements, through intrinsic dose-dependent toxicity or idiosyncratic reactions. It can present with asymptomatic liver enzyme elevations, jaundice, fatigue, nausea, pruritus, or acute liver failure in exposed patients. Management centers on prompt withdrawal of the offending agent, supportive care, and monitoring for resolution or progression.

head: Anagrelide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Anagrelide is an oral imidazoquinazoline antiplatelet agent that lowers platelet counts primarily by inhibiting megakaryocyte maturation, with phosphodiesterase III inhibition also documented. It is used for thrombocythemia, especially essential thrombocythemia, to reduce elevated platelets and thrombotic risk. A notable adverse effect is cardiovascular toxicity, including tachycardia and palpitations; caution is advised in patients with cardiac disease.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Phenylalanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phenylalanine is an essential aromatic amino acid used in medical nutrition and as a component of amino acid supplementation; it has no single drug-like molecular target, serving instead as a substrate for protein synthesis and for conversion to tyrosine. Clinically it is included in parenteral/enteral nutrition formulations. It is contraindicated in phenylketonuria, where accumulation can cause serious neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis is a urinary tract disorder characterized by formation of calculi in the kidneys, usually from supersaturation and crystallization of minerals such as calcium, uric acid, or cystine. It commonly causes renal colic, hematuria, nausea, and urinary obstruction, especially in adults. Management focuses on analgesia, hydration, facilitating stone passage, metabolic evaluation, and urologic removal when stones are large, obstructing, or infected.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Chloroprocaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloroprocaine is a short-acting ester-type local anesthetic of the amino ester family that blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It is used for infiltration, peripheral nerve block, and epidural anesthesia, including obstetric procedures. A notable risk is hypersensitivity to ester anesthetics or para-aminobenzoic acid derivatives.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Bromocriptine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bromocriptine is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist, primarily targeting dopamine D2 receptors to inhibit pituitary prolactin secretion and suppress growth hormone release. It is used for hyperprolactinemia, prolactin-secreting adenomas, acromegaly, and Parkinson disease; a quick-release form is also approved for type 2 diabetes. Notable adverse effects include nausea and orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Procaine benzylpenicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Procaine benzylpenicillin is a depot intramuscular formulation of benzylpenicillin (penicillin G), a beta-lactam penicillin antibiotic combined with procaine. It inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including syphilis in some settings. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins, with risk of anaphylaxis.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Selegiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Selegiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class. By inhibiting MAO-B, it reduces dopamine breakdown in the central nervous system. It is established for Parkinson disease and, in transdermal form, major depressive disorder. Notable risks include insomnia and potentially serious hypertensive or serotonergic interactions with contraindicated sympathomimetic or serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Small intestine cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the small bowel, most often adenocarcinoma but also including neuroendocrine tumors, lymphomas, and sarcomas, affecting the gastrointestinal system. It arises from uncontrolled proliferation of intestinal cells and may present with abdominal pain, occult bleeding, anemia, weight loss, obstruction, or perforation, typically in adults. Management generally centers on surgical resection, with histology- and stage-directed systemic therapy when indicated.

head: Silver sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Silver sulfadiazine is a topical sulfonamide antimicrobial combining sulfadiazine with silver ions. It acts mainly by releasing silver, which disrupts bacterial cell membranes and proteins, while sulfadiazine inhibits folate synthesis via dihydropteroate synthase. It is established for prevention and treatment of wound sepsis in second- and third-degree burns. A notable contraindication is use in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Glycogen storage disease comprises inherited metabolic disorders, usually affecting liver and/or muscle, caused by enzyme defects in glycogen synthesis or breakdown that lead to abnormal glycogen accumulation or impaired glucose release. Typical features include fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, growth delay, exercise intolerance, and muscle weakness, often presenting in infancy or childhood. Management is mainly dietary and supportive, with subtype-specific monitoring and complication prevention.

head: Aurothioglucose (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aurothioglucose is a gold(I) thiolate compound formerly used as a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug for rheumatoid arthritis. Its precise mechanism is incompletely defined, but gold salts modulate immune and inflammatory cell function rather than acting on a single established molecular target. Important adverse effects include dermatitis, stomatitis, proteinuria, and bone marrow suppression; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a chronic multisystem autoimmune connective tissue disease in which loss of immune tolerance leads to autoantibody production and immune-complex–mediated inflammation affecting skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, serosa, and the nervous system. It commonly affects women of childbearing age and causes fatigue, rash, photosensitivity, arthritis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities; management uses sun protection, hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressive or biologic therapies tailored to organ involvement.

head: Tipranavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tipranavir is a nonpeptidic HIV-1 protease inhibitor that blocks the viral aspartyl protease, preventing cleavage of Gag-Pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. Boosted with ritonavir, it is used in combination antiretroviral therapy for treatment-experienced HIV-1 infection, including resistant virus. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity; it is contraindicated in patients with moderate to severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Methapyrilene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methapyrilene is a first-generation antihistamine of the ethylenediamine class that acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist. It was formerly used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions and as an antipruritic, often in topical or combination products. A notable adverse effect is sedation with anticholinergic effects; it is also historically recognized for hepatotoxic and carcinogenic findings in animal studies.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Nilotinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nilotinib is an oral second-generation BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor, structurally an aminopyrimidine derivative related to imatinib. It selectively inhibits BCR-ABL and also KIT and PDGFR kinases, blocking proliferation of Philadelphia chromosome-positive leukemic cells. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome-positive chronic myeloid leukemia. A notable safety risk is QT-interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, or congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Octreotide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Octreotide is a synthetic somatostatin analog that activates somatostatin receptors, especially SSTR2 and SSTR5, to suppress secretion of growth hormone, glucagon, insulin, serotonin, and other peptides. It is used for acromegaly, symptomatic control of carcinoid syndrome and vasoactive intestinal peptide-secreting tumors, and to reduce bleeding from esophageal varices. A notable adverse effect is gallstone formation.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Quercetin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quercetin is a naturally occurring flavonol (flavonoid polyphenol) found in many fruits and vegetables. It has antioxidant activity and modulates multiple signaling enzymes and transporters, but no single primary molecular target or definitive therapeutic mechanism is established. Quercetin is not an approved prescription drug for any specific indication. Reported adverse effects of high-dose supplements include headache and gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder of copper metabolism affecting mainly the liver and nervous system, caused by ATP7B mutations that impair biliary copper excretion and lead to toxic copper accumulation. It typically presents in children, adolescents, or young adults with hepatitis, cirrhosis, tremor, dystonia, psychiatric symptoms, and Kayser-Fleischer rings. Management centers on lifelong copper chelation or zinc therapy, dietary copper reduction, and liver transplantation for advanced hepatic failure.

head: Carmustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carmustine is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms DNA interstrand cross-links and carbamoylates proteins, disrupting DNA replication and RNA/protein synthesis without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors and for multiple myeloma, lymphoma, and other hematologic malignancies, including in conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Obstructive hydrocephalus is a neurologic disorder of the central nervous system caused by blockage of cerebrospinal fluid flow within the ventricular system, leading to ventricular enlargement and raised intracranial pressure. It occurs in infants and adults, with headache, vomiting, gait or visual disturbance, developmental delay, and enlarged head in infants. Management typically requires urgent CSF diversion and treatment of the underlying obstruction.

head: Regadenoson (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Regadenoson is a selective adenosine A2A receptor agonist, an adenosine derivative used as a coronary vasodilator. By activating A2A receptors on coronary vascular smooth muscle, it increases coronary blood flow and produces pharmacologic stress for radionuclide myocardial perfusion imaging in patients unable to exercise adequately. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and caution is warranted in patients with reactive airway disease.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Trimetrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimetrexate is a lipid-soluble antifolate antineoplastic agent that inhibits dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), depleting reduced folates and thereby blocking thymidylate and purine synthesis. It has been used, with leucovorin rescue, for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia in patients intolerant of standard therapy. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; mucositis can also occur.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Duloxetine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrogenic syndrome of inappropriate antidiuresis (a disease)
head_desc: Duloxetine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is approved for major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, and chronic musculoskeletal pain. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity; concomitant use with monoamine oxidase inhibitors is contraindicated.
tail_desc: Nephrogenic syndrome of inappropriate antidiuresis is a rare inherited renal water-balance disorder characterized by inappropriate antidiuresis despite low or undetectable vasopressin, usually due to activating AVPR2 mutations in the collecting duct. Affected patients, often male infants or children, develop euvolemic hyponatremia with low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine, seizures or irritability. Management centers on fluid restriction and measures to prevent recurrent hyponatremia.

head: Thiotepa (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiotepa is an alkylating antineoplastic agent of the ethyleneimine/aziridine family. It forms covalent cross-links with DNA, impairing DNA replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used in conditioning regimens before hematopoietic stem cell transplantation and for certain cancers, including bladder, breast, and ovarian malignancies. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Flunisolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flunisolide is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid (glucocorticoid) used mainly as an intranasal or inhaled anti-inflammatory agent. It acts by binding intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and reduce airway or nasal mucosal inflammation. Established indications include allergic rhinitis and asthma prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include local candidiasis with inhaled use and nasal irritation or epistaxis intranasally.
tail_desc: Nasal cavity disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the upper respiratory tract within the nose, including inflammatory, infectious, allergic, structural, and neoplastic conditions. Common manifestations include nasal obstruction, rhinorrhea, epistaxis, facial pressure, anosmia, and impaired airflow in both children and adults. Management depends on cause and may involve avoidance of triggers, medical therapy, treatment of infection or inflammation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Lovastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lovastatin is a fungal-derived statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat primary hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to reduce cardiovascular risk. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, and it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Fesoterodine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fesoterodine is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a prodrug rapidly converted to 5-hydroxymethyl tolterodine, a competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, particularly M2 and M3 in the bladder detrusor. It is indicated for overactive bladder with urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic disease in which the peripheral iris obstructs the anterior chamber angle, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid or intermittent rise in intraocular pressure. It typically affects anatomically predisposed older adults, especially those with shallow anterior chambers. Features include severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos, nausea, and a red eye; management is urgent pressure lowering followed by laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Netilmicin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Netilmicin is a semisynthetic aminoglycoside antibiotic, derived from sisomicin, that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit and disrupts protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and bactericidal activity against susceptible aerobic Gram-negative bacteria. It is used for serious infections such as urinary tract, respiratory, intra-abdominal, and septicemic infections. Like other aminoglycosides, it can cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Acetic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acetic acid is a simple carboxylic acid used topically as an acidifying antimicrobial/antiseptic agent. Its activity is nonspecific, lowering local pH and thereby inhibiting growth of susceptible bacteria and fungi rather than acting through a single defined molecular target. Established uses include treatment of otitis externa and bladder irrigation to help reduce catheter-associated bacteriuria. Notable adverse effects include local irritation, burning, and pain.
tail_desc: Herpes simplex infectious disease is a viral infection of the skin, mucous membranes, eyes, or nervous system caused by herpes simplex virus type 1 or 2, which establishes latent infection in sensory ganglia with recurrent reactivation. It typically causes grouped painful vesicles or ulcers, and can produce genital herpes, oral lesions, keratitis, or encephalitis; disease may be severe in neonates and immunocompromised patients. Management generally uses antiviral therapy and supportive care.

head: Azatadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Azatadine is a first-generation tricyclic antihistamine of the benzo[c]cycloheptathiophene family that acts primarily as a histamine H1-receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis and urticaria. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation due to central H1 blockade, and it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Atherosclerosis is a chronic arterial disease of the cardiovascular system characterized by lipid-rich plaque formation in the intima, driven by endothelial injury, inflammation, and fibrous cap development. It commonly affects older adults and people with risk factors such as smoking, diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, causing ischemia, angina, myocardial infarction, stroke, or claudication. Management focuses on risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Digoxin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Digoxin is a cardiac glycoside that inhibits the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in cardiomyocytes, increasing intracellular calcium and enhancing cardiac contractility while augmenting vagal tone to slow atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used for symptomatic heart failure and ventricular rate control in atrial fibrillation or flutter. A notable adverse effect is potentially life-threatening arrhythmias, especially with toxicity.
tail_desc: Cor pulmonale is right ventricular enlargement and eventual failure secondary to pulmonary hypertension caused by diseases of the lungs, pulmonary vasculature, or chest wall, rather than left-sided heart disease. It commonly occurs in people with chronic lung disease and presents with dyspnea, fatigue, peripheral edema, elevated jugular venous pressure, and hepatomegaly. Management focuses on treating the underlying pulmonary disorder, improving oxygenation, and reducing right-heart strain.

head: Methadyl acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadyl acetate (levomethadyl acetate, LAAM) is a long-acting diphenylheptane opioid and full agonist at the μ-opioid receptor. It was used for maintenance treatment of opioid dependence as an alternative to methadone. A notable safety concern is QT-interval prolongation with risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, making it contraindicated in patients with known QT prolongation or significant cardiac rhythm disorders.
tail_desc: Pseudotumor cerebri, or idiopathic intracranial hypertension, is a neurologic disorder characterized by elevated intracranial pressure without an intracranial mass or hydrocephalus, likely related to impaired cerebrospinal fluid absorption. It typically affects obese women of childbearing age and causes headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and papilledema. Management focuses on weight loss, acetazolamide, and vision-preserving procedures when severe.

head: Mefenamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mefenamic acid is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug of the anthranilic acid (fenamate) class that inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for mild to moderate pain, including primary dysmenorrhea. Notable risks include gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Diclofenamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diclofenamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor of the sulfonamide class. It reduces aqueous humor formation by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase, primarily in the ciliary processes of the eye. It is used to lower intraocular pressure in glaucoma, including chronic open-angle glaucoma. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Chronic primary adrenal insufficiency is an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex in which intrinsic adrenal destruction or dysfunction causes deficient glucocorticoid and usually mineralocorticoid production. Common causes include autoimmune adrenalitis, with fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia as typical features. It occurs in adults and children and is managed with lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, usually plus mineralocorticoid replacement and stress-dose education.

head: Etidocaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Etidocaine is a long-acting amide-type local anesthetic. It blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, inhibiting initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used for infiltration, peripheral nerve block, and epidural anesthesia in surgical and obstetric settings. A notable adverse effect is dose-related cardiotoxicity, so caution is required in patients with significant conduction abnormalities.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Immune system diseases are a broad category of disorders affecting innate or adaptive immunity, including immunodeficiencies, autoimmune diseases, autoinflammatory syndromes, and hypersensitivity conditions. They result from absent, excessive, misdirected, or dysregulated immune responses, causing recurrent infections, chronic inflammation, tissue or organ damage, allergy, or systemic symptoms in both children and adults. Management depends on subtype and may include infection prevention, immunoglobulin replacement, immunosuppression, biologic therapy, and treatment of complications.

head: Eszopiclone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Eszopiclone is a nonbenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic of the cyclopyrrolone class used for insomnia. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator at the GABA\(_A\) receptor benzodiazepine site, enhancing inhibitory GABA signaling. Established use is for sleep-onset and sleep-maintenance insomnia. A notable adverse effect is dysgeusia (unpleasant taste); it also carries a boxed warning for complex sleep behaviors.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Cetylpyridinium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cetylpyridinium is a quaternary ammonium cationic surfactant and antiseptic, commonly used as cetylpyridinium chloride in oral care products. It disrupts bacterial cell membranes through nonspecific interaction with membrane phospholipids and proteins, reducing oral microbial load. Established uses include antiseptic mouthwashes and lozenges for plaque, gingivitis, and minor sore throat relief. Notable adverse effects include tooth and tongue staining and oral mucosal irritation.
tail_desc: Galactosemia is an inherited metabolic disorder affecting carbohydrate metabolism, usually caused by deficiency of enzymes needed to process galactose, classically galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase. It typically presents in newborns after milk feeding with poor feeding, vomiting, jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, cataracts, and risk of sepsis. Management is prompt lifelong dietary restriction of galactose/lactose and monitoring for complications.

head: Carmustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carmustine is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms DNA interstrand cross-links and carbamoylates proteins, disrupting DNA replication and RNA/protein synthesis without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors and for multiple myeloma, lymphoma, and other hematologic malignancies, including in conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Acute leukemia is a rapidly progressive hematologic malignancy of the bone marrow and blood, caused by clonal proliferation of immature leukocyte precursors that suppress normal hematopoiesis. It presents with fatigue, pallor, infections, fever, bruising or bleeding, and sometimes organ infiltration. It occurs in both children and adults, and management typically involves urgent subtype-specific chemotherapy, supportive care, and in selected patients hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Cevimeline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cevimeline is a muscarinic cholinergic agonist, a quinuclidine derivative, that primarily stimulates M3 muscarinic receptors to increase exocrine gland secretion, especially salivary flow. It is approved for treatment of dry mouth (xerostomia) in patients with Sjögren’s syndrome. Notable adverse effects include excessive sweating, and it is contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma and in conditions where miosis is undesirable, such as narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Bronchitis is inflammation of the bronchi in the lower respiratory tract, most often acute and caused by viral infection, or chronic as part of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, usually related to smoking or irritant exposure. It commonly causes cough, sputum production, wheeze, chest discomfort, and mild dyspnea in children or adults. Management is mainly supportive for acute disease, while chronic bronchitis requires smoking cessation and COPD-directed therapy.

head: Pitavastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pitavastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) used to treat primary hyperlipidemia and mixed dyslipidemia by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and increasing LDL clearance. It lowers LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and may raise HDL cholesterol. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Injury is physical damage to body tissues or organs, typically classified as traumatic and potentially affecting any organ system, including skin, musculoskeletal structures, viscera, or the brain. It results from external mechanical, thermal, chemical, electrical, or radiation forces, causing pain, bleeding, swelling, functional impairment, and sometimes shock. It occurs at all ages; management focuses on stabilization, damage assessment, wound care, pain control, and definitive repair or rehabilitation.

head: Polyvinyl alcohol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Polyvinyl alcohol is a synthetic, water-soluble polymer used as an ophthalmic lubricant and artificial tear excipient. It acts physically rather than via a specific molecular target, stabilizing the tear film and reducing ocular surface friction. Established uses include temporary relief of dry eye irritation and ocular discomfort. Mild transient blurred vision or local eye irritation can occur after instillation.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Chromium picolinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: Chromium picolinate is a nutritional supplement consisting of trivalent chromium chelated to picolinic acid, used to provide chromium in deficiency states. Its mechanism is not fully established; chromium is thought to enhance insulin signaling and glucose metabolism, but no specific primary molecular target is well defined. It is not an FDA-approved drug, and reported adverse effects include renal toxicity.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Choline magnesium trisalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Choline magnesium trisalicylate is a nonacetylated salicylate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for relief of mild to moderate pain, fever, and inflammatory disorders such as arthritis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal irritation or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in salicylate hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Milnacipran (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Milnacipran is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively greater norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. It is approved in some countries for major depressive disorder and in the United States for management of fibromyalgia. Notable adverse effects include nausea and tachycardia; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Serotonin syndrome is an acute drug-induced toxidrome of the central and peripheral nervous systems caused by excess serotonergic activity, typically after serotonergic medication use, overdose, or interactions. It is characterized by altered mental status, autonomic hyperactivity, and neuromuscular abnormalities such as clonus, hyperreflexia, and tremor. Management centers on stopping serotonergic agents, supportive care, benzodiazepines, and severe-case escalation.

head: Cysteamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cysteamine is an aminothiol and cystine-depleting agent used primarily to treat nephropathic cystinosis. Within lysosomes, it reacts with cystine to form cysteine and a cysteine-cysteamine mixed disulfide, enabling export through existing lysine transport pathways and reducing cystine accumulation. It is also used ophthalmically for corneal cystine crystals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and sulfurous body odor.
tail_desc: Seborrheic keratosis is a common benign epidermal skin tumor caused by clonal proliferation of immature keratinocytes. It typically affects middle-aged and older adults, presenting as well-circumscribed, waxy, verrucous, “stuck-on” papules or plaques, often tan to dark brown, on the trunk, face, or extremities. Management is usually reassurance; symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome lesions may be removed with cryotherapy, curettage, or shave excision.

head: Spironolactone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Spironolactone is a steroidal potassium-sparing diuretic and aldosterone antagonist that competitively blocks the mineralocorticoid receptor, reducing sodium and water retention while conserving potassium; it also has antiandrogen activity. Established uses include heart failure, hypertension, primary hyperaldosteronism, edema from cirrhosis or nephrotic syndrome, and prevention of hypokalemia. A notable adverse effect is hyperkalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria or significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Beclomethasone dipropionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Beclomethasone dipropionate is a synthetic corticosteroid (glucocorticoid prodrug) that is converted to beclomethasone-17-monopropionate and activates the glucocorticoid receptor, suppressing inflammatory gene expression and mediator release. It is used for maintenance treatment of asthma, allergic rhinitis, and inflammatory bowel disease in certain formulations. A notable adverse effect is oropharyngeal candidiasis with inhaled use.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Ixabepilone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ixabepilone is a semisynthetic epothilone B analog, a microtubule-stabilizing antineoplastic agent that binds β-tubulin and promotes tubulin polymerization, disrupting mitosis and inducing apoptosis. It is approved for locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer, including disease resistant to anthracyclines, taxanes, or capecitabine, often with capecitabine. A notable toxicity is peripheral neuropathy; it is contraindicated with capecitabine in severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Sodium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium acetate is an alkalinizing electrolyte salt and acetate donor used in parenteral nutrition and intravenous fluid therapy. After metabolism, acetate is converted primarily in muscle and other tissues to bicarbonate, helping correct metabolic acidosis; it has no single receptor target. Established uses include correction of hyponatremia and acidosis in compounded IV solutions. Excess administration can cause metabolic alkalosis.
tail_desc: Disorder of phenylalanine metabolism is an inborn error of amino acid metabolism affecting systemic and especially neurologic development, caused by impaired conversion of phenylalanine, most commonly from phenylalanine hydroxylase or tetrahydrobiopterin defects. It typically presents in infancy or through newborn screening with hyperphenylalaninemia and risk of developmental delay if untreated; management centers on lifelong phenylalanine restriction and selected cofactor-based therapies.

head: Apomorphine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Apomorphine is a non-ergoline dopamine agonist of the aporphine class that primarily stimulates dopamine D1- and D2-like receptors in the central nervous system. It is used for intermittent treatment of “off” episodes in Parkinson disease, and some formulations are used for erectile dysfunction in certain regions. A notable adverse effect is severe nausea and vomiting, and it is contraindicated with 5-HT3 antagonists.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Oxazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxazepam is a short- to intermediate-acting benzodiazepine anxiolytic that positively modulates the GABA\(_A\) receptor at the benzodiazepine site, enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders and symptomatic relief of acute alcohol withdrawal. Notable adverse effects include sedation and psychomotor impairment; benzodiazepines are contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma and should be used cautiously with other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Cysteine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid used pharmaceutically as a nutritional additive, especially in parenteral nutrition solutions. It primarily serves as a metabolic precursor for protein synthesis and glutathione production rather than acting through a discrete drug target or receptor. Clinically, it is indicated to meet amino acid requirements, particularly in neonates and infants. Adverse effects may include hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Disorder of phenylalanine metabolism is an inborn error of amino acid metabolism affecting systemic and especially neurologic development, caused by impaired conversion of phenylalanine, most commonly from phenylalanine hydroxylase or tetrahydrobiopterin defects. It typically presents in infancy or through newborn screening with hyperphenylalaninemia and risk of developmental delay if untreated; management centers on lifelong phenylalanine restriction and selected cofactor-based therapies.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Procarbazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Procarbazine is an oral methylhydrazine alkylating-like antineoplastic agent that is metabolized to reactive intermediates causing DNA strand damage and inhibition of DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis; no single primary molecular target is established. It is used mainly in combination regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and some primary brain tumors. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression; it also has monoamine oxidase–inhibiting activity, creating important drug and tyramine-food interactions.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Felbamate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felbamate is a dicarbamate antiepileptic drug. Its anticonvulsant activity is attributed mainly to inhibition of NMDA receptor–mediated excitatory transmission, particularly at the glycine site, with additional potentiation of GABAergic inhibition. It is approved for focal seizures and for seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. A notable serious toxicity is aplastic anemia, with risk of acute hepatic failure.
tail_desc: Idiopathic aplastic anemia is an acquired bone marrow failure disorder of the hematopoietic system in which immune-mediated destruction or suppression of hematopoietic stem cells leads to hypocellular marrow and pancytopenia without an identifiable cause. Patients of any age present with fatigue, infections, and bleeding or bruising. Management centers on supportive transfusions and infection prevention, with immunosuppressive therapy or allogeneic stem cell transplantation in appropriate candidates.

head: Exenatide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Exenatide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, a synthetic incretin mimetic based on exendin-4. It activates the GLP-1 receptor to enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite. It is approved for type 2 diabetes mellitus, including extended-release formulations. Notable adverse effects include nausea and vomiting; it is contraindicated in patients with a history of severe hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hypoglycemia is a metabolic/endocrine condition of abnormally low blood glucose, affecting systemic energy supply, especially the central nervous system. It commonly results from excess insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy, fasting, alcohol use, or critical illness. Symptoms include sweating, tremor, palpitations, confusion, seizures, or coma, particularly in people with diabetes. Management focuses on prompt glucose administration and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Deserpidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Deserpidine is an antihypertensive indole alkaloid of the Rauwolfia (reserpine-like) family. It irreversibly inhibits vesicular monoamine transporters (primarily VMAT2), depleting norepinephrine, dopamine, and serotonin from sympathetic nerve terminals and the CNS. It has been used for hypertension, historically often in combination products. A notable adverse effect is depression; it is contraindicated in patients with a history of depressive disorders.
tail_desc: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a chronic multisystem autoimmune connective tissue disease in which loss of immune tolerance leads to autoantibody production and immune-complex–mediated inflammation affecting skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, serosa, and the nervous system. It commonly affects women of childbearing age and causes fatigue, rash, photosensitivity, arthritis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities; management uses sun protection, hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressive or biologic therapies tailored to organ involvement.

head: Procarbazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Procarbazine is an oral methylhydrazine alkylating-like antineoplastic agent that is metabolized to reactive intermediates causing DNA strand damage and inhibition of DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis; no single primary molecular target is established. It is used mainly in combination regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and some primary brain tumors. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression; it also has monoamine oxidase–inhibiting activity, creating important drug and tyramine-food interactions.
tail_desc: Dermatophytosis is a superficial fungal infection of keratinized tissues of the integumentary system, caused by dermatophytes such as Trichophyton, Microsporum, and Epidermophyton that invade the stratum corneum, hair, or nails. It typically presents as pruritic, scaly annular plaques, alopecia, or onychodystrophy in children or adults, depending on site. Management usually involves topical antifungals for limited disease and systemic therapy for scalp, nail, or extensive infection.

head: Efavirenz (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Efavirenz is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) used as part of combination antiretroviral therapy for HIV-1 infection. It binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Established indications include treatment of HIV-1 in adults and children in combination regimens. Notable adverse effects include rash and prominent central nervous system symptoms such as dizziness, insomnia, and vivid dreams.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Iopanoic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iopanoic acid is an iodinated radiographic contrast agent in the oral cholecystographic class. It is taken up by hepatocytes and excreted into bile, increasing gallbladder and biliary tract radiopacity for imaging. It was used for oral cholecystography to evaluate gallbladder disease. Because of its high iodine load, it is contraindicated in iodine hypersensitivity and can precipitate thyroid dysfunction.
tail_desc: Heart cancer usually refers to primary cardiac tumors, which are very rare and may be benign or malignant, or more commonly to metastatic spread to the heart from other cancers, affecting the cardiovascular system. Malignant cardiac tumors such as sarcomas can cause obstruction, arrhythmias, heart failure, embolic events, or pericardial effusion. Management depends on tumor type and extent, using surgical resection when feasible, plus oncology-directed systemic therapy or palliation.

head: Mafenide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mafenide is a topical sulfonamide antimicrobial used mainly as mafenide acetate cream or solution for prevention and treatment of infection in severe burn wounds. It inhibits bacterial folate synthesis by competitively targeting dihydropteroate synthase, with broad activity including many Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms. A notable adverse effect is metabolic acidosis due to carbonic anhydrase inhibition; sulfonamide hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: obsolete rare pulmonary disease is a disease or medical condition.

head: Dacarbazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dacarbazine is a triazene alkylating antineoplastic prodrug that is metabolized in the liver to reactive methylating species, causing DNA damage through methylation and inhibition of DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis; it has no single specific molecular target. It is used for metastatic malignant melanoma and Hodgkin lymphoma, and notable toxicities include severe myelosuppression and marked nausea/vomiting.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Stavudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Stavudine is a thymidine analogue nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it competitively inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and causes viral DNA chain termination. It has been used, in combination therapy, for treatment of HIV infection. A notable serious adverse effect is dose-related peripheral neuropathy; pancreatitis and lactic acidosis can also occur.
tail_desc: Peripheral neuropathy is a disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused by damage to peripheral nerves, commonly from diabetes, toxins, nutritional deficiencies, infections, or immune-mediated disease. It typically presents with distal numbness, tingling, burning pain, weakness, and reduced reflexes, often in a length-dependent “stocking-glove” pattern, especially in adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the cause, symptom control, and rehabilitation or foot care when needed.

head: Colistin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Colistin (polymyxin E) is a cationic cyclic polypeptide antibiotic of the polymyxin class that binds lipopolysaccharide and phospholipids in the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria, disrupting membrane integrity and causing cell death. It is used for serious multidrug-resistant Gram-negative infections, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Acinetobacter baumannii, and Klebsiella pneumoniae. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Nilotinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nilotinib is an oral second-generation BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor, structurally an aminopyrimidine derivative related to imatinib. It selectively inhibits BCR-ABL and also KIT and PDGFR kinases, blocking proliferation of Philadelphia chromosome-positive leukemic cells. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome-positive chronic myeloid leukemia. A notable safety risk is QT-interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, or congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Meclofenamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meclofenamic acid is a fenamate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the anthranilic acid class. It nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It has been used to treat mild to moderate pain, primary dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in aspirin-sensitive NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Secretin human (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Secretin human is a synthetic form of the endogenous gastrointestinal peptide hormone secretin. It acts as an agonist at secretin receptors, stimulating pancreatic bicarbonate-rich fluid secretion and modulating gastric acid secretion. Clinically, it is used diagnostically in pancreatic function testing and in provocative testing for gastrinoma/Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. A notable adverse effect is transient nausea, flushing, or abdominal discomfort.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Maprotiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
