head_desc: Maprotiline is a tetracyclic antidepressant that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake by blocking the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively weak effects on serotonin uptake. It has been used for depressive disorders, including major depression with anxiety features. A notable risk is dose-related seizures, and it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or during the acute recovery phase after myocardial infarction.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Misoprostol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog that acts primarily as an agonist at prostaglandin EP receptors, reducing gastric acid secretion and increasing uterine tone and cervical softening. It is used for prevention of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers and in obstetrics/gynecology for medical abortion, cervical ripening, labor induction, and postpartum hemorrhage. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in pregnancy when used for ulcer prevention.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Bendamustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bendamustine is a bifunctional alkylating antineoplastic agent of the nitrogen mustard class with a benzimidazole ring. It forms DNA crosslinks, causing DNA damage and apoptosis, without a single specific molecular target. It is used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia and indolent B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma, including rituximab-refractory disease. Notable toxicities include myelosuppression and serious infections.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Leucine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid used as a nutritional ingredient and in amino acid supplementation. It activates nutrient-sensing pathways involved in protein synthesis, notably mTORC1, but is not typically classified as a conventional targeted drug. Clinically, it is used in medical nutrition to prevent or treat amino acid deficiency; excessive intake may worsen branched-chain ketoacid metabolism disorders such as maple syrup urine disease.
tail_desc: Chronic kidney disease is a progressive disorder of the renal system defined by persistent kidney damage or reduced glomerular filtration for at least three months, commonly caused by diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. It often affects older adults and may present with albuminuria, edema, anemia, hypertension, and electrolyte abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause, slowing progression, reducing cardiovascular risk, and preparing for dialysis or transplantation in advanced stages.

head: Diflunisal (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diflunisal is a salicylate-derived nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. It is used for mild to moderate pain and for osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with aspirin/NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Inulin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Inulin is a plant-derived fructan polysaccharide and soluble dietary fiber used clinically primarily as a marker of glomerular filtration because it is freely filtered at the glomerulus and is not significantly reabsorbed, secreted, or metabolized, rather than acting via a specific molecular target. It is established for measurement of renal function, and gastrointestinal adverse effects such as bloating or flatulence can occur.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis is a urinary tract disorder characterized by formation of calculi in the kidneys, usually from supersaturation and crystallization of minerals such as calcium, uric acid, or cystine. It commonly causes renal colic, hematuria, nausea, and urinary obstruction, especially in adults. Management focuses on analgesia, hydration, facilitating stone passage, metabolic evaluation, and urologic removal when stones are large, obstructing, or infected.

head: Ezetimibe (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ezetimibe is a cholesterol absorption inhibitor of the 2-azetidinone class that selectively blocks the Niemann-Pick C1-Like 1 (NPC1L1) transporter at the intestinal brush border, reducing intestinal uptake of cholesterol. It is indicated for primary hypercholesterolemia, often with a statin, and for homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia or sitosterolemia. Notable adverse effects include elevated hepatic transaminases, especially with statins.
tail_desc: Rhabdomyolysis is an acute skeletal muscle injury syndrome in which damaged myocytes release intracellular contents, including creatine kinase and myoglobin, into the bloodstream. It may follow trauma, extreme exertion, drugs, toxins, infection, or metabolic disorders, and presents with myalgia, weakness, dark urine, and risk of acute kidney injury. Management centers on rapid treatment of the cause, aggressive intravenous fluids, and monitoring/correction of electrolyte and renal complications.

head: Cevimeline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cevimeline is a muscarinic cholinergic agonist, a quinuclidine derivative, that primarily stimulates M3 muscarinic receptors to increase exocrine gland secretion, especially salivary flow. It is approved for treatment of dry mouth (xerostomia) in patients with Sjögren’s syndrome. Notable adverse effects include excessive sweating, and it is contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma and in conditions where miosis is undesirable, such as narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Dexfenfluramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dexfenfluramine is the dextro isomer of fenfluramine, an anorectic amphetamine derivative formerly used as an anti-obesity agent. It acts primarily by increasing serotonergic signaling, mainly by promoting serotonin release and inhibiting its reuptake via the serotonin transporter (SERT). It was withdrawn after being linked to valvular heart disease and pulmonary hypertension.
tail_desc: Pulmonary hypertension is a cardiopulmonary vascular disorder characterized by abnormally elevated pressure in the pulmonary arteries due to vasoconstriction, vascular remodeling, thrombosis, or secondary heart/lung disease. It causes progressive dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, syncope, and right heart failure, affecting adults and children. Management targets the cause, uses supportive therapy, and may include pulmonary vasodilator drugs or transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Zoledronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zoledronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the osteoclast mevalonate pathway, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget disease of bone, hypercalcemia of malignancy, and prevention of skeletal-related events in patients with bone metastases or multiple myeloma. A notable risk is osteonecrosis of the jaw; severe renal impairment is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Hypoparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of calcium-phosphate homeostasis caused by deficient parathyroid hormone secretion or action, usually due to postoperative gland damage, autoimmune disease, or genetic defects. It produces hypocalcemia and hyperphosphatemia, leading to paresthesias, muscle cramps, tetany, seizures, and chronic complications. It can occur in adults or children; management typically uses oral calcium, active vitamin D, and monitoring, with selected cases receiving recombinant PTH.

head: Beclomethasone dipropionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Beclomethasone dipropionate is a synthetic corticosteroid (glucocorticoid prodrug) that is converted to beclomethasone-17-monopropionate and activates the glucocorticoid receptor, suppressing inflammatory gene expression and mediator release. It is used for maintenance treatment of asthma, allergic rhinitis, and inflammatory bowel disease in certain formulations. A notable adverse effect is oropharyngeal candidiasis with inhaled use.
tail_desc: Dermatophytosis is a superficial fungal infection of keratinized tissues of the integumentary system, caused by dermatophytes such as Trichophyton, Microsporum, and Epidermophyton that invade the stratum corneum, hair, or nails. It typically presents as pruritic, scaly annular plaques, alopecia, or onychodystrophy in children or adults, depending on site. Management usually involves topical antifungals for limited disease and systemic therapy for scalp, nail, or extensive infection.

head: Cetirizine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cetirizine is a second-generation piperazine antihistamine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is widely used for allergic rhinitis and chronic urticaria. Compared with first-generation antihistamines it is less sedating, but somnolence can still occur; caution is advised in severe renal impairment because the drug is substantially renally eliminated.
tail_desc: Thyrotoxicosis is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid system caused by excess circulating thyroid hormones, most commonly from hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease, toxic nodular goiter, or thyroiditis. It typically presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, especially in adults. Management targets the cause and symptom control, using beta-blockers and measures such as antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Butobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butobarbital is a barbiturate sedative-hypnotic, used primarily for the short-term treatment of insomnia and occasionally for preoperative sedation. Like other barbiturates, it enhances GABAergic inhibition by positively modulating the GABA-A receptor, increasing chloride ion influx and depressing central nervous system activity. A notable adverse effect is respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in patients with porphyria.
tail_desc: Hepatic coma is the deepest stage of hepatic encephalopathy, a neuropsychiatric complication of severe liver failure or portosystemic shunting affecting the central nervous system. It results from impaired hepatic detoxification, with accumulation of neurotoxic substances such as ammonia. Patients with advanced acute or chronic liver disease develop profound unresponsiveness, often with preceding confusion or asterixis; management targets precipitating causes, lowers ammonia, and provides intensive supportive care.

head: Calcium lactate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcium lactate is an oral calcium salt used as a mineral supplement and antacid. It dissociates to provide elemental calcium, supporting neuromuscular function, bone mineralization, and correction of calcium deficiency, without a specific receptor target. Established uses include prevention or treatment of hypocalcemia and calcium supplementation. Notable risks include hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Alprostadil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alprostadil is a prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) analog and vasodilatory smooth-muscle relaxant that activates prostaglandin E receptors, increasing cAMP. It is used to maintain ductus arteriosus patency in certain congenital heart defects and to treat erectile dysfunction. Notable adverse effects include hypotension; it is contraindicated in neonates with respiratory distress syndrome without cyanotic heart disease.
tail_desc: Peyronie disease is an acquired fibrotic disorder of the penile tunica albuginea in the male genitourinary system, caused by localized plaque formation and scarring, likely related to repetitive microtrauma in susceptible men. It typically affects middle-aged to older men and causes penile curvature, pain, palpable plaques, and sometimes erectile dysfunction. Management ranges from observation and medical or intralesional therapy to surgical correction in stable deformity.

head: Butacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic in the benzoate family. Like other local anesthetics, it acts by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used topically, particularly in ophthalmic anesthesia. As with related ester anesthetics, hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known ester local anesthetic allergy.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Magnesium hydroxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: Magnesium hydroxide is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an antacid and osmotic laxative. It neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach and, when taken orally in larger doses, retains water in the intestinal lumen by osmotic action; it has no specific receptor target. Established uses include relief of dyspepsia/heartburn and short-term treatment of constipation. A notable risk is hypermagnesemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Nifedipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nifedipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels, predominantly in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is used for hypertension and angina, including vasospastic angina. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; immediate-release formulations are generally avoided in acute ischemic heart disease because rapid hypotension may worsen ischemia.
tail_desc: Cirrhosis of the liver is a chronic end-stage liver disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by diffuse fibrosis and regenerative nodules that distort hepatic architecture, most often from chronic alcohol use, viral hepatitis, or nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. Patients may be asymptomatic initially, then develop jaundice, ascites, portal hypertension, variceal bleeding, and encephalopathy. Management targets the cause, complications, nutrition, and possible liver transplantation.

head: Auranofin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Auranofin is an oral gold(I)-containing disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD). It is thought to act through immunomodulatory effects, notably inhibition of thioredoxin reductase and related sulfhydryl-dependent enzymes, thereby suppressing inflammatory responses. It has been approved for rheumatoid arthritis. Important adverse effects include diarrhea, rash, stomatitis, and potentially serious thrombocytopenia or proteinuria; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Racepinephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Racepinephrine is a racemic form of epinephrine, a sympathomimetic catecholamine acting as a nonselective agonist at alpha- and beta-adrenergic receptors. By causing mucosal vasoconstriction and bronchodilation, it is used as an inhaled agent for temporary relief of mild intermittent asthma symptoms and to reduce airway edema, including in croup. Notable adverse effects include tachycardia and other cardiac arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Gastroesophageal reflux disease is a chronic upper gastrointestinal disorder in which reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus, usually from lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction, causes troublesome symptoms or mucosal injury. Typical features are heartburn and acid regurgitation, sometimes chest pain, cough, or dysphagia, affecting adults and children. Management emphasizes weight loss, trigger avoidance, acid suppression with proton pump inhibitors, and selected antireflux procedures.

head: Trimethadione (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethadione is an oxazolidinedione anticonvulsant used primarily for refractory absence (petit mal) seizures. Its active metabolite, dimethadione, reduces thalamic low-threshold calcium currents by blocking T-type calcium channels, suppressing the cortical rhythm underlying absence seizures. A notable adverse effect is severe teratogenicity, so it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Bupropion (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bupropion is an aminoketone antidepressant and smoking-cessation agent that inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine, with minimal direct serotonergic activity; nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonism may also contribute. It is approved for major depressive disorder, seasonal affective disorder, and smoking cessation. A notable risk is seizures, so it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or eating disorders.
tail_desc: Opiate dependence is a substance use disorder affecting the central nervous system, marked by compulsive opioid use with tolerance and a characteristic withdrawal syndrome after reduction or cessation. It arises from neuroadaptation to repeated opioid exposure and occurs in people using heroin or prescription opioids. Features include craving, loss of control, withdrawal symptoms, and social or physical harm; management typically combines medication-assisted treatment, psychosocial support, and harm reduction.

head: Protriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Protriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzocycloheptadiene family. It acts primarily by inhibiting neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is used for treatment of major depressive disorder. A notable adverse effect is anticholinergic toxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Ethotoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethotoin is a hydantoin anticonvulsant used to treat seizure disorders, particularly tonic-clonic and complex partial seizures. Like other hydantoins, it is thought to stabilize neuronal membranes by limiting voltage-gated sodium channel–mediated repetitive firing, though its mechanism is not fully defined. A notable adverse effect is blood dyscrasia, and it is contraindicated in patients with hepatic dysfunction or hematologic abnormalities.
tail_desc: Folic acid deficiency anemia is a megaloblastic anemia of the hematologic system caused by inadequate folate, impairing DNA synthesis and producing ineffective erythropoiesis with enlarged red cell precursors. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, glossitis, and macrocytosis, often in malnutrition, alcoholism, pregnancy, malabsorption, or increased demand. Management involves folate replacement and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Penbutolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Penbutolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at β1 and β2 receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is generally contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment prevents adequate ventricular filling or ejection, reducing cardiac output and/or causing congestion. Common causes include ischemic heart disease, hypertension, and cardiomyopathy. Typical features are dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance, especially in older adults. Management focuses on treating causes, relieving congestion, and using guideline-directed medications and devices when indicated.

head: Sodium fluorophosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluorophosphate is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Lovastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lovastatin is a fungal-derived statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat primary hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to reduce cardiovascular risk. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, and it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Fenoprofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fenoprofen is a propionic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for relief of pain and inflammation in conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and mild to moderate pain. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Losartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Losartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a nonpeptide biphenyl tetrazole antihypertensive that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone effects. It is used for hypertension, diabetic nephropathy in type 2 diabetes, and to reduce stroke risk in certain patients with left ventricular hypertrophy. A notable contraindication is pregnancy due to fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Carmustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carmustine is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms DNA interstrand cross-links and carbamoylates proteins, disrupting DNA replication and RNA/protein synthesis without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors and for multiple myeloma, lymphoma, and other hematologic malignancies, including in conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Intracranial abscess is a serious central nervous system infection in which a localized collection of pus forms within the brain parenchyma or intracranial spaces, usually from bacterial spread by contiguous infection, hematogenous seeding, trauma, or neurosurgery. Typical features include headache, fever, focal neurologic deficits, seizures, and raised intracranial pressure. Management generally involves urgent neuroimaging, prolonged antimicrobial therapy, and often neurosurgical drainage.

head: Nitrofurantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofurantoin is a nitrofuran antibacterial used primarily for treatment and prophylaxis of uncomplicated lower urinary tract infections. In bacteria, nitroreductases reduce it to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, ribosomal proteins, and other macromolecules, so it has no single primary molecular target. A notable risk is pulmonary toxicity; it is contraindicated at term in pregnancy and in significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Pulmonary fibrosis is a chronic interstitial lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by progressive scarring of lung parenchyma, which stiffens the lungs and impairs gas exchange. Patients, often older adults, typically develop exertional dyspnea, persistent dry cough, and reduced exercise tolerance. Management generally focuses on treating the cause when identified, slowing progression, supportive oxygen and pulmonary rehabilitation, and lung transplantation in selected cases.

head: Vigabatrin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vigabatrin is an antiepileptic gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) analog that irreversibly inhibits GABA transaminase (ABAT), increasing central nervous system GABA concentrations. It is used for refractory complex partial seizures and infantile spasms. A notable serious adverse effect is permanent bilateral concentric visual field constriction/retinal toxicity, requiring careful ophthalmic monitoring.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Dihydroergotamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dihydroergotamine is a semisynthetic ergot alkaloid antimigraine agent that acts mainly as an agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors, producing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine attacks and cluster headache. A notable risk is peripheral or coronary vasospasm, so it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease.
tail_desc: Peripheral vascular disease is a circulatory disorder of the peripheral blood vessels, most often involving atherosclerotic narrowing or occlusion of arteries supplying the legs. It commonly affects older adults and people who smoke or have diabetes, causing intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool extremities, poor wound healing, and sometimes critical limb ischemia. Management emphasizes risk-factor reduction, exercise, antiplatelet/statin therapy, and revascularization when severe.

head: Lamivudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lamivudine is a synthetic nucleoside analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI), a cytidine analogue, that is phosphorylated intracellularly and inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus DNA polymerase, causing chain termination. It is used to treat HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable risk is severe lactic acidosis with hepatomegaly and steatosis; abrupt discontinuation in hepatitis B can trigger severe exacerbations.
tail_desc: Pancreatitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pancreas in the digestive system, caused by premature activation of pancreatic enzymes leading to autodigestion; common triggers include gallstones, alcohol use, hypertriglyceridemia, and medications. It typically presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting in adults. Management focuses on supportive care, treating the underlying cause, and addressing complications.

head: Entecavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Entecavir is a guanosine nucleoside analogue antiviral used to treat chronic hepatitis B virus infection. It is phosphorylated intracellularly and inhibits the HBV polymerase/reverse transcriptase, blocking priming, reverse transcription of the negative strand, and synthesis of the positive DNA strand. A notable warning is severe acute exacerbation of hepatitis after discontinuation; lactic acidosis and hepatomegaly with steatosis have also been reported.
tail_desc: Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis is a progressive form of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease affecting the liver, characterized by hepatic fat accumulation with inflammation and hepatocellular injury, often linked to obesity, insulin resistance, and metabolic syndrome. Patients may be asymptomatic or have fatigue and abnormal liver enzymes, with risk of fibrosis and cirrhosis; management centers on weight loss, metabolic risk reduction, and monitoring for liver disease progression.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Abacavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Abacavir is a carbocyclic guanosine analogue nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase by competing with natural nucleotides and causing DNA chain termination. It is used in combination therapy for HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is potentially fatal hypersensitivity reaction, strongly associated with the HLA-B*57:01 allele, making screening essential before use.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Prostate carcinoma is a malignant epithelial cancer of the prostate gland in the male genitourinary system, usually arising as adenocarcinoma driven by genetic alterations and androgen signaling. It mainly affects older men and may be asymptomatic or cause urinary obstruction, hematuria, bone pain, or metastatic disease. Management ranges from active surveillance for low-risk tumors to surgery, radiotherapy, androgen-deprivation, and systemic therapy.

head: Alendronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alendronic acid (alendronate) is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive drug. It binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated resorption, primarily by blocking farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway. It is used for osteoporosis and Paget’s disease of bone. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis; it is contraindicated in esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying.
tail_desc: Osteonecrosis of the jaw is a necrotic bone disorder of the maxillofacial skeleton characterized by exposed or nonhealing jawbone, most often associated with antiresorptive or antiangiogenic therapy, dental extraction, or radiation in susceptible patients. It causes pain, swelling, infection, loosening teeth, and poor oral healing, especially in cancer or osteoporosis patients. Management is usually conservative with oral hygiene, antimicrobial therapy, pain control, and selective surgical debridement.

head: Minoxidil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Minoxidil is a direct-acting arteriolar vasodilator of the pyrimidine derivative class, used orally for severe, refractory hypertension and topically for androgenetic alopecia. It is converted to minoxidil sulfate, which opens ATP-sensitive potassium channels in vascular smooth muscle, reducing peripheral resistance; the precise hair-growth mechanism is incompletely defined. Notable adverse effects include hypertrichosis and fluid retention.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Desvenlafaxine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desvenlafaxine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant and the major active metabolite of venlafaxine. It acts primarily by inhibiting the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic serotonin and norepinephrine. It is approved for major depressive disorder. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and the contraindication of concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is a rare, life-threatening neurologic and systemic reaction to dopamine-blocking antipsychotic drugs or abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic therapy. It is characterized by hyperthermia, severe generalized rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability, typically with elevated creatine kinase. It occurs in patients exposed to these agents and is managed by immediate drug cessation, intensive supportive care, and sometimes dantrolene or bromocriptine.

head: Vorinostat (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vorinostat is a hydroxamic acid histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor that blocks class I and II HDAC enzymes, increasing histone acetylation and altering gene expression to promote cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis. It is approved for treatment of cutaneous manifestations of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma in patients with persistent, progressive, or recurrent disease. Notable adverse effects include thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Rivastigmine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rivastigmine is a carbamate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that also inhibits butyrylcholinesterase, increasing central cholinergic neurotransmission. It is approved for symptomatic treatment of mild to moderate Alzheimer disease and Parkinson disease dementia. Common adverse effects include nausea, vomiting, and weight loss; it should be used cautiously in patients with bradycardia or conduction abnormalities.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Lanreotide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lanreotide is a long-acting synthetic somatostatin analog that activates somatostatin receptors, especially SSTR2 and SSTR5, suppressing secretion of growth hormone and several gastrointestinal peptides. It is used for acromegaly and for symptom control of neuroendocrine tumors, including carcinoid syndrome; some formulations are also approved for gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. A notable adverse effect is cholelithiasis.
tail_desc: Myxedema is a severe manifestation of hypothyroidism affecting the endocrine system, caused by profound deficiency of thyroid hormone, with dermal glycosaminoglycan accumulation producing nonpitting edema. It typically occurs in adults with longstanding untreated hypothyroidism and features fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, dry skin, and facial puffiness; management centers on thyroid hormone replacement and treatment of precipitating factors, with myxedema coma requiring urgent intensive care.

head: Nefazodone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nefazodone is a phenylpiperazine antidepressant classified as a serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor (SARI). It inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake and potently antagonizes 5-HT2A receptors. It has been used for major depressive disorder. A notable safety concern is rare but severe hepatotoxicity, including liver failure, making active liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Tenofovir disoproxil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tenofovir disoproxil is an oral prodrug of tenofovir, an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NtRTI). After conversion to tenofovir diphosphate, it competitively inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase, causing DNA chain termination. It is used to treat HIV-1 infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable adverse effect is renal toxicity, including proximal tubulopathy/Fanconi syndrome.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Oxaprozin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxaprozin is a propionic acid–derived nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity, including aspirin-sensitive asthma.
tail_desc: Active peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related disorder of the upper gastrointestinal tract characterized by an active mucosal ulcer in the stomach or duodenum, most commonly due to Helicobacter pylori infection or NSAID use. Patients typically have epigastric pain or dyspepsia and may develop bleeding or perforation. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, stopping ulcerogenic drugs, and treating complications.

head: Floxuridine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Floxuridine is a fluorinated pyrimidine antimetabolite, an analog of 5-fluorouracil, that is converted intracellularly to fluorodeoxyuridylate, which inhibits thymidylate synthase and impairs DNA synthesis. It has been used mainly by hepatic arterial infusion for metastatic gastrointestinal cancers, especially colorectal liver metastases. A notable toxicity is severe gastrointestinal mucosal injury, including diarrhea and stomatitis.
tail_desc: Gastroesophageal reflux disease is a chronic upper gastrointestinal disorder in which reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus, usually from lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction, causes troublesome symptoms or mucosal injury. Typical features are heartburn and acid regurgitation, sometimes chest pain, cough, or dysphagia, affecting adults and children. Management emphasizes weight loss, trigger avoidance, acid suppression with proton pump inhibitors, and selected antireflux procedures.

head: Ticagrelor (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ticagrelor is an oral antiplatelet agent of the cyclopentyl-triazolo-pyrimidine class that reversibly inhibits the platelet P2Y12 ADP receptor, thereby reducing ADP-mediated platelet activation and aggregation. It is used to prevent atherothrombotic events in acute coronary syndrome and in certain patients with prior myocardial infarction or high-risk coronary artery disease. A notable adverse effect is dyspnea; it is contraindicated in active pathological bleeding and prior intracranial hemorrhage.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Naphazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naphazoline is an imidazoline-derived sympathomimetic decongestant that acts primarily as an alpha-adrenergic receptor agonist, causing vasoconstriction of conjunctival and nasal blood vessels. It is used topically to relieve nasal congestion and ocular redness. A notable adverse effect is rebound congestion or hyperemia with prolonged use; it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Dihydroergotamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dihydroergotamine is a semisynthetic ergot alkaloid antimigraine agent that acts mainly as an agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors, producing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine attacks and cluster headache. A notable risk is peripheral or coronary vasospasm, so it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease.
tail_desc: Prinzmetal angina is a coronary vasospastic disorder of the cardiovascular system caused by transient spasm of an epicardial coronary artery, producing reversible myocardial ischemia without fixed obstructive disease in many cases. It typically causes recurrent rest angina, often nocturnal or early morning, with transient ST-segment elevation during attacks. Management centers on calcium channel blockers and nitrates, while avoiding triggers such as smoking.

head: Azathioprine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Azathioprine is a thiopurine antimetabolite immunosuppressant and prodrug of 6-mercaptopurine that inhibits de novo purine synthesis and incorporation into nucleic acids, suppressing proliferating T and B lymphocytes. It is used to prevent kidney transplant rejection and to treat rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression, particularly in patients with low TPMT or NUDT15 activity.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Ketoprofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ketoprofen is a propionic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for pain and inflammatory conditions such as osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and dysmenorrhea. A notable risk is gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related gastrointestinal disorder characterized by mucosal ulceration in the stomach or proximal duodenum, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It typically presents with epigastric pain, dyspepsia, nausea, or bleeding, especially in affected adults. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, and avoiding ulcerogenic medications.

head: Cisapride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cisapride is a substituted piperidinyl benzamide gastroprokinetic agent that enhances gastrointestinal motility primarily by acting as a serotonin 5-HT4 receptor agonist, promoting acetylcholine release in the enteric nervous system. It was used for gastroesophageal reflux disease and other motility disorders, but its use became highly restricted because it can prolong the QT interval and cause serious ventricular arrhythmias, especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors.
tail_desc: Pulmonary valve insufficiency is a valvular heart disease of the cardiovascular system in which the pulmonary valve fails to close properly, allowing diastolic backflow from the pulmonary artery into the right ventricle. It is often secondary to pulmonary hypertension or congenital/postoperative valve abnormalities, may be asymptomatic or cause exertional dyspnea and right-sided heart failure, and is managed by treating the cause, monitoring right ventricular function, and selected valve repair or replacement.

head: Sulfabenzamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfabenzamide is a sulfonamide antibacterial. Like other sulfonamides, it inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby suppressing susceptible bacterial growth. It has been used mainly as a topical anti-infective, including in some combination rectal or vaginal preparations. Notable risks include sulfonamide hypersensitivity reactions, so it is contraindicated in patients with known sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Cystinuria is a hereditary renal tubular transport disorder affecting the urinary system, caused by defective reabsorption of cystine and dibasic amino acids in the proximal tubule. Excess urinary cystine, which is poorly soluble, leads to recurrent cystine kidney stones, often beginning in childhood or young adulthood, with hematuria, flank pain, and obstruction. Management focuses on high fluid intake, urine alkalinization, dietary sodium reduction, and sometimes thiol drugs.

head: Cetylpyridinium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cetylpyridinium is a quaternary ammonium cationic surfactant and antiseptic, commonly used as cetylpyridinium chloride in oral care products. It disrupts bacterial cell membranes through nonspecific interaction with membrane phospholipids and proteins, reducing oral microbial load. Established uses include antiseptic mouthwashes and lozenges for plaque, gingivitis, and minor sore throat relief. Notable adverse effects include tooth and tongue staining and oral mucosal irritation.
tail_desc: Pyruvate dehydrogenase E3 deficiency is a rare autosomal recessive mitochondrial energy-metabolism disorder affecting the nervous system and other high-demand tissues. It is caused by pathogenic variants in DLD, impairing the E3 subunit shared by pyruvate, alpha-ketoglutarate, and branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase complexes, leading to lactic acidosis. Infants and children commonly present with developmental delay, hypotonia, seizures, episodic decompensation, and liver involvement; management is mainly supportive, avoiding catabolism and treating metabolic crises.

head: Phentermine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phentermine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, structurally related to amphetamine, used as a short-term adjunct to diet and exercise for exogenous obesity. It promotes weight loss primarily by increasing central norepinephrine release, with lesser effects on dopamine and serotonin, reducing appetite via hypothalamic pathways. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and heart rate, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or within 14 days of MAOI use.
tail_desc: Pulmonary hypertension is a cardiopulmonary vascular disorder characterized by abnormally elevated pressure in the pulmonary arteries due to vasoconstriction, vascular remodeling, thrombosis, or secondary heart/lung disease. It causes progressive dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, syncope, and right heart failure, affecting adults and children. Management targets the cause, uses supportive therapy, and may include pulmonary vasodilator drugs or transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Isoleucine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoleucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid used in amino acid replacement and parenteral nutrition formulations. It acts as a substrate for protein synthesis and energy metabolism rather than through a single pharmacologic receptor target. Clinically, it is included to prevent or treat amino acid deficiency in patients requiring nutritional support. Excessive amino acid administration may contribute to azotemia, especially in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Choline magnesium trisalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Choline magnesium trisalicylate is a nonacetylated salicylate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for relief of mild to moderate pain, fever, and inflammatory disorders such as arthritis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal irritation or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in salicylate hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Telithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telithromycin is a ketolide antibiotic, structurally related to macrolides, that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosomal subunit, particularly 23S rRNA. It has been used for mild to moderate community-acquired pneumonia caused by susceptible organisms. A notable safety concern is hepatotoxicity; it is also contraindicated in patients with myasthenia gravis because it can precipitate life-threatening respiratory failure.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Tobramycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tobramycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing mistranslation and bactericidal activity, particularly against aerobic Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections and as inhaled therapy in cystic fibrosis airway infection. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Eye infectious disease is a broad category of ocular disorders affecting the conjunctiva, cornea, eyelids, or intraocular tissues, caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites that invade eye structures and trigger inflammation. Typical features include redness, pain, discharge, photophobia, blurred vision, and sometimes vision loss, occurring in all age groups. Management generally involves prompt pathogen-directed antimicrobial therapy, supportive care, and urgent ophthalmologic evaluation for severe or sight-threatening cases.

head: Quazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine hypnotic of the triazolobenzodiazepine family that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing inhibitory GABA signaling. It is indicated for the treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling asleep and maintaining sleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day sedation; it is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines.
tail_desc: Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the upper airway, characterized by recurrent pharyngeal collapse during sleep causing intermittent airflow obstruction, hypoxemia, and sleep fragmentation. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity, and presents with loud snoring, witnessed apneas, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Management typically includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and selected upper-airway surgery.

head: Penbutolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Penbutolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at β1 and β2 receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is generally contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Meloxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meloxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that preferentially inhibits cyclooxygenase, mainly COX-2 over COX-1, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Phylloquinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) is a fat-soluble naphthoquinone vitamin used as a hemostatic antidote to vitamin K deficiency and warfarin-associated hypoprothrombinemia. It acts as a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase in the liver, enabling activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. Notable risks include anaphylactoid reactions with rapid intravenous administration.
tail_desc: Hypervitaminosis is a toxic condition caused by excessive vitamin intake, most often from supplements, affecting multiple systems; fat-soluble vitamins A and D are classic causes because they accumulate in tissues. Manifestations vary by vitamin but commonly include gastrointestinal symptoms, neurologic complaints, skin or bone changes, and organ dysfunction. Management centers on stopping the vitamin source, supportive care, and treating complications.

head: Febuxostat (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Febuxostat is a non-purine xanthine oxidase inhibitor used to lower uric acid. It selectively inhibits xanthine oxidase, reducing conversion of hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid. It is approved for chronic management of hyperuricemia in patients with gout. A notable safety concern is increased risk of cardiovascular death compared with allopurinol; caution is advised in patients with established cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Cerebrovascular disorder is a broad category of diseases affecting the brain’s blood vessels and cerebral circulation, including ischemic and hemorrhagic conditions. It results from vessel occlusion, rupture, or structural abnormalities that impair blood flow or cause bleeding. Typical features are sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, dizziness, or altered consciousness, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent diagnosis, restoring or controlling cerebral perfusion, treating the cause, and preventing recurrence.

head: Meloxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meloxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that preferentially inhibits cyclooxygenase, mainly COX-2 over COX-1, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Histrelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Histrelin is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist, a peptide analog of GnRH. Continuous administration stimulates then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for central precocious puberty and advanced prostate cancer. Notable adverse effects include an initial hormone “flare” and local implant-site reactions.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Terazosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Terazosin is a quinazoline-derived selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist that relaxes smooth muscle in the prostate, bladder neck, and peripheral vasculature. It is used for hypertension and for symptomatic benign prostatic hyperplasia. A notable adverse effect is first-dose orthostatic hypotension with dizziness or syncope; caution is required in patients prone to hypotension.
tail_desc: Orthostatic hypotension is a cardiovascular autonomic disorder marked by an abnormal fall in blood pressure on standing due to impaired baroreflex-mediated vasoconstriction, volume depletion, medications, or autonomic failure. It commonly affects older adults and patients with neurodegenerative or diabetic autonomic neuropathy, causing dizziness, lightheadedness, blurred vision, weakness, or syncope. Management targets underlying causes, medication review, fluid and salt optimization, compression garments, and selected pressor agents.

head: Vandetanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vandetanib is an oral small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the anilinoquinazoline class that targets RET, VEGFR-2, and EGFR signaling. It is approved for symptomatic or progressive medullary thyroid cancer in patients with unresectable locally advanced or metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is QT-interval prolongation, and it carries a boxed warning for torsades de pointes and sudden death.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Cevimeline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cevimeline is a muscarinic cholinergic agonist, a quinuclidine derivative, that primarily stimulates M3 muscarinic receptors to increase exocrine gland secretion, especially salivary flow. It is approved for treatment of dry mouth (xerostomia) in patients with Sjögren’s syndrome. Notable adverse effects include excessive sweating, and it is contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma and in conditions where miosis is undesirable, such as narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Lovastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lovastatin is a fungal-derived statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat primary hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to reduce cardiovascular risk. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, and it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
