tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Octreotide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Octreotide is a synthetic somatostatin analog that activates somatostatin receptors, especially SSTR2 and SSTR5, to suppress secretion of growth hormone, glucagon, insulin, serotonin, and other peptides. It is used for acromegaly, symptomatic control of carcinoid syndrome and vasoactive intestinal peptide-secreting tumors, and to reduce bleeding from esophageal varices. A notable adverse effect is gallstone formation.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Lindane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lindane is an organochlorine insecticide and scabicide/pediculicide (the gamma isomer of hexachlorocyclohexane) used topically for scabies and head lice when safer treatments are unsuitable or have failed. It acts on parasite neuronal ion channels, particularly GABA-gated chloride channels, causing neuronal hyperexcitation. A notable risk is neurotoxicity, including seizures, so it is contraindicated in premature infants and patients with seizure disorders.
tail_desc: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder driven by epidermal barrier dysfunction and immune dysregulation, often associated with atopy. It causes intense pruritus, xerosis, and eczematous lesions, commonly beginning in infancy or childhood and affecting flexural areas. Management focuses on skin hydration, trigger avoidance, topical anti-inflammatory therapy, and escalation to phototherapy or systemic agents in refractory disease.

head: Trichlormethiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trichlormethiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disorders. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Disulfiram (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Disulfiram is a thiuram disulfide aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor used in alcohol aversion therapy for alcohol use disorder. It irreversibly inhibits hepatic aldehyde dehydrogenase, causing acetaldehyde accumulation after ethanol ingestion and an unpleasant reaction. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated with concurrent alcohol exposure because it can provoke severe disulfiram-ethanol reactions.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Paricalcitol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Paricalcitol is a synthetic vitamin D analog (19-nor-1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D2) that selectively activates the vitamin D receptor (VDR), suppressing parathyroid hormone synthesis and secretion. It is used to prevent and treat secondary hyperparathyroidism in patients with chronic kidney disease, including those on dialysis. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or vitamin D toxicity.
tail_desc: Hyperphosphatemia is an electrolyte and mineral metabolism disorder affecting systemic homeostasis, most often due to impaired renal phosphate excretion in chronic kidney disease. It may be asymptomatic or contribute to hypocalcemia, pruritus, soft-tissue and vascular calcification, and secondary hyperparathyroidism, especially in patients with advanced kidney failure. Management focuses on treating the cause, restricting dietary phosphate, and using phosphate binders or dialysis when needed.

head: Amiloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amiloride is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pyrazinecarbonylguanidine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal nephron, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for hypertension and edema, often with other diuretics, and in Liddle syndrome. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in renal impairment or with potassium supplementation.
tail_desc: obsolete hyperuricemia (disease) is a disease or medical condition.

head: Rivaroxaban (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rivaroxaban is an oral direct factor Xa inhibitor anticoagulant, a small-molecule oxazolidinone derivative. It selectively inhibits free and clot-bound factor Xa, reducing thrombin generation and fibrin clot formation. Established indications include prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, and stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it is contraindicated in active pathological bleeding.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Acetazolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acetazolamide is a sulfonamide-derived carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reversibly inhibits carbonic anhydrase isoenzymes, reducing bicarbonate reabsorption and aqueous humor and cerebrospinal fluid production. It is used for glaucoma, prevention and treatment of acute mountain sickness, adjunctive therapy in some epilepsies, and edema. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity and severe hepatic disease.
tail_desc: Addison disease is primary adrenal insufficiency, an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex causing deficient cortisol and often aldosterone production, most commonly from autoimmune adrenal destruction. Patients may develop chronic fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia, with risk of acute adrenal crisis. Management is lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, often mineralocorticoid replacement, and stress-dose therapy during illness or surgery.

head: Colchicine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Colchicine is a tricyclic alkaloid anti-inflammatory agent that binds tubulin and inhibits microtubule polymerization, suppressing neutrophil chemotaxis, inflammasome activity, and inflammatory mediator release. It is established for acute gout flares, gout prophylaxis, and familial Mediterranean fever, and is also approved in some settings for pericarditis. Notable toxicity includes dose-related gastrointestinal upset, especially diarrhea; avoid use with strong CYP3A4 or P-glycoprotein inhibitors due to toxicity risk.
tail_desc: Constitutional neutropenia is a benign, usually lifelong form of chronic neutropenia affecting the hematologic/immune system, characterized by persistently low absolute neutrophil counts without an acquired cause or major marrow pathology. It is often seen in otherwise healthy individuals, including some ethnic groups, and typically causes no or few infections. Management is mainly reassurance, exclusion of secondary causes, and clinical monitoring rather than routine treatment.

head: Alfentanil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alfentanil is a short-acting synthetic opioid analgesic of the fentanyl (anilidopiperidine) class that acts primarily as a mu-opioid receptor agonist to produce analgesia and profound sedation. It is used intravenously as an analgesic adjunct in anesthesia and for induction or maintenance of general anesthesia. A notable risk is dose-dependent respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in patients with significant respiratory compromise without ventilatory support.
tail_desc: Pseudotumor cerebri, or idiopathic intracranial hypertension, is a neurologic disorder characterized by elevated intracranial pressure without an intracranial mass or hydrocephalus, likely related to impaired cerebrospinal fluid absorption. It typically affects obese women of childbearing age and causes headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and papilledema. Management focuses on weight loss, acetazolamide, and vision-preserving procedures when severe.

head: L-Lysine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: L-Lysine is an essential basic amino acid used as a nutritional supplement rather than a receptor-targeted drug. Its primary role is to replace dietary lysine deficiency and support normal protein synthesis; no specific pharmacologic molecular target is established for routine supplementation. Established use is prevention or treatment of lysine deficiency. It is generally well tolerated, but gastrointestinal upset such as abdominal pain or diarrhea can occur.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Doxycycline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Doxycycline is a semisynthetic tetracycline antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat a wide range of bacterial infections, including respiratory, skin, sexually transmitted, and tick-borne infections, and for malaria prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include photosensitivity, and it is generally avoided during pregnancy and in young children due to tooth discoloration.
tail_desc: Dyskinesia of the esophagus is an esophageal motility disorder of the gastrointestinal system characterized by abnormal or uncoordinated smooth-muscle contractions that impair normal peristalsis. It typically presents with dysphagia, noncardiac chest pain, or odynophagia, usually in adults. Management is directed by the specific motility pattern and may include dietary modification, treatment of contributing reflux, smooth-muscle relaxant therapy, and specialist evaluation.

head: Azatadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Azatadine is a first-generation tricyclic antihistamine of the benzo[c]cycloheptathiophene family that acts primarily as a histamine H1-receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis and urticaria. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation due to central H1 blockade, and it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Nilotinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nilotinib is an oral second-generation BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor, structurally an aminopyrimidine derivative related to imatinib. It selectively inhibits BCR-ABL and also KIT and PDGFR kinases, blocking proliferation of Philadelphia chromosome-positive leukemic cells. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome-positive chronic myeloid leukemia. A notable safety risk is QT-interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, or congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Methadyl acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadyl acetate (levomethadyl acetate, LAAM) is a long-acting diphenylheptane opioid and full agonist at the μ-opioid receptor. It was used for maintenance treatment of opioid dependence as an alternative to methadone. A notable safety concern is QT-interval prolongation with risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, making it contraindicated in patients with known QT prolongation or significant cardiac rhythm disorders.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Cyclobenzaprine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclobenzaprine is a centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant, structurally related to tricyclic antidepressants. Its exact mechanism is not fully defined, but it acts within the brainstem to reduce tonic somatic motor activity rather than directly at the neuromuscular junction. It is used for short-term relief of acute painful musculoskeletal spasm; notable adverse effects include marked drowsiness and anticholinergic effects, and it is contraindicated with MAO inhibitors.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular dissociation is a cardiac conduction/rhythm disorder in which atrial and ventricular activation occur independently because the sinus node no longer controls ventricular depolarization, often due to slowing of the sinus rate, accelerated junctional or ventricular rhythms, or complete AV block. Patients may be asymptomatic or have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or bradycardia-related symptoms; management targets the underlying cause, with urgent treatment or pacing when hemodynamic compromise or high-grade block is present.

head: Silver sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Silver sulfadiazine is a topical sulfonamide antimicrobial combining sulfadiazine with silver ions. It acts mainly by releasing silver, which disrupts bacterial cell membranes and proteins, while sulfadiazine inhibits folate synthesis via dihydropteroate synthase. It is established for prevention and treatment of wound sepsis in second- and third-degree burns. A notable contraindication is use in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Trimethadione (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethadione is an oxazolidinedione anticonvulsant used primarily for refractory absence (petit mal) seizures. Its active metabolite, dimethadione, reduces thalamic low-threshold calcium currents by blocking T-type calcium channels, suppressing the cortical rhythm underlying absence seizures. A notable adverse effect is severe teratogenicity, so it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Rowell syndrome is a rare autoimmune dermatologic variant of cutaneous lupus erythematosus characterized by lupus occurring with erythema multiforme-like lesions and a distinctive serologic profile, often including ANA positivity and anti-Ro/SSA or anti-La/SSB antibodies. It mainly affects middle-aged women. Patients develop lupus-associated skin lesions, sometimes with photosensitivity. Management follows cutaneous lupus care, using trigger avoidance, topical or systemic corticosteroids, and antimalarials.

head: Glucosamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glucosamine is an endogenous amino monosaccharide and dietary supplement used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, especially of the knee. Its mechanism is not well established; it is thought to serve as a substrate for glycosaminoglycan and proteoglycan synthesis in cartilage, without a clearly defined primary molecular target. Adverse effects are usually mild gastrointestinal symptoms, and caution is advised in patients with shellfish allergy depending on product source.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Sulfamethoxazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamethoxazole is a sulfonamide antibacterial that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby DNA precursor production; it is commonly combined with trimethoprim for synergistic activity. Established uses include urinary tract infections, acute otitis media, bronchitis exacerbations, shigellosis, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia treatment or prophylaxis. Notable risks include severe hypersensitivity reactions such as Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Pantoprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pantoprazole is a substituted benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing basal and stimulated acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, erosive esophagitis, and other acid-related disorders, including pathological hypersecretory states such as Zollinger–Ellison syndrome. A notable adverse effect is hypomagnesemia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune megaloblastic anemia of the hematologic and gastrointestinal systems caused by intrinsic factor deficiency, usually from autoimmune gastritis with parietal cell loss, leading to vitamin B12 malabsorption. It typically affects older adults and causes fatigue, pallor, glossitis, and sometimes neurologic deficits such as paresthesias. Management centers on lifelong vitamin B12 replacement and monitoring for associated autoimmune disease.

head: Enoxaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin (factor IIa). It is used to prevent and treat venous thromboembolism and in acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Intracranial hemorrhage is acute bleeding within the skull affecting the central nervous system, including intracerebral, subarachnoid, subdural, and epidural spaces. It usually results from ruptured vessels due to hypertension, trauma, aneurysm, vascular malformation, or coagulopathy. Patients, often older adults or trauma patients, present with sudden headache, focal neurologic deficits, vomiting, reduced consciousness, or seizures. Management is emergent, focusing on stabilization, blood pressure and coagulation control, and neurosurgical intervention when indicated.

head: Doxazosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Doxazosin is a quinazoline-derived selective alpha1-adrenergic receptor antagonist that relaxes vascular and prostatic smooth muscle by blocking postsynaptic alpha1 receptors. It is used to treat hypertension and lower urinary tract symptoms associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia. A notable adverse effect is first-dose orthostatic hypotension, which may cause dizziness or syncope.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Metipranolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metipranolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class used primarily as an ophthalmic agent. By antagonizing β1- and β2-adrenergic receptors in the ciliary epithelium, it reduces aqueous humor production and lowers intraocular pressure. It is indicated for ocular hypertension and open-angle glaucoma; notable risks include bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in asthma and severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Pregabalin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pregabalin is a gabapentinoid anticonvulsant and analgesic, a structural analogue of gamma-aminobutyric acid that binds the α2δ subunit of presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release. It is used for neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, adjunctive treatment of partial-onset seizures, and generalized anxiety disorder in some regions. Common adverse effects include dizziness and somnolence.
tail_desc: Muscular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting skeletal muscle and the neuromuscular system, caused by diverse mechanisms including inherited structural protein defects, inflammation, metabolic abnormalities, or toxic injury. Typical features include progressive weakness, fatigue, cramps, myalgia, and functional impairment across children or adults, depending on etiology. Management is cause-directed and supportive, including rehabilitation, exercise guidance, medications for specific subtypes, and monitoring for respiratory or cardiac complications.

head: Doxepin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Doxepin is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzoxepin class that inhibits neuronal reuptake of serotonin and norepinephrine and is also a potent histamine H1 receptor antagonist. It is used for major depressive disorder and anxiety disorders, and at low doses for insomnia. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation; it is contraindicated in untreated narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Manic bipolar affective disorder is a mood disorder within the bipolar spectrum affecting the central nervous system, characterized by manic episodes with abnormally elevated or irritable mood, increased energy, reduced need for sleep, pressured speech, impulsivity, and possible psychosis, often alternating with depression. It typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood, and management centers on mood stabilizers, antipsychotics, and ongoing psychiatric care.

head: Celecoxib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Celecoxib is a selective nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the diaryl-substituted pyrazole class that primarily inhibits cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2, PTGS2), reducing prostaglandin synthesis and thereby inflammation and pain. It is approved for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute pain, and primary dysmenorrhea. A notable risk is serious cardiovascular thrombotic events; it is contraindicated for perioperative pain in coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
tail_desc: Cerebrovascular disorder is a broad category of diseases affecting the brain’s blood vessels and cerebral circulation, including ischemic and hemorrhagic conditions. It results from vessel occlusion, rupture, or structural abnormalities that impair blood flow or cause bleeding. Typical features are sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, dizziness, or altered consciousness, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent diagnosis, restoring or controlling cerebral perfusion, treating the cause, and preventing recurrence.

head: Phylloquinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) is a fat-soluble naphthoquinone vitamin used as a hemostatic antidote to vitamin K deficiency and warfarin-associated hypoprothrombinemia. It acts as a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase in the liver, enabling activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. Notable risks include anaphylactoid reactions with rapid intravenous administration.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Antipyrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Antipyrine (phenazone) is a pyrazolone derivative and non-opioid analgesic/antipyretic. Its effects are attributed to inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis via cyclooxygenase pathways, although it is not used for a highly selective molecular target. It has been used for pain and fever and, in otic combinations, for symptomatic relief of ear pain. Hypersensitivity reactions, including rash, can occur.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Clemastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clemastine is a first-generation antihistamine of the ethanolamine class that acts primarily as a reversible H1 histamine receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is used for allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and pruritic allergic conditions. A notable adverse effect is sedation due to central nervous system penetration; it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Zanamivir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zanamivir is a neuraminidase inhibitor antiviral, a sialic acid analogue active against influenza A and B viruses. It inhibits the viral neuraminidase surface glycoprotein, blocking release of newly formed virions from infected cells. It is approved for treatment and prophylaxis of influenza. Inhaled zanamivir can cause bronchospasm and is generally avoided in patients with underlying airway disease such as asthma or COPD.
tail_desc: Severe early-onset COPD is a chronic obstructive airway disease of the respiratory system characterized by persistent airflow limitation from emphysema and/or chronic bronchitis, arising unusually early in adult life. It commonly reflects heavy smoking, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, or other susceptibility factors, and presents with progressive dyspnea, cough, wheeze, and frequent exacerbations. Management centers on smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccination, oxygen when indicated, and selected advanced interventions.

head: Amoxicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amoxicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibacterial agent. It binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to cell lysis. It is widely used for susceptible infections such as otitis media, sinusitis, streptococcal pharyngitis, lower respiratory, urinary tract, skin infections, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins.
tail_desc: Infectious mononucleosis is an acute viral infection of the lymphoreticular system, most commonly caused by Epstein–Barr virus. It typically affects adolescents and young adults and presents with fever, pharyngitis, cervical lymphadenopathy, fatigue, and sometimes hepatosplenomegaly. Management is mainly supportive, including rest, hydration, and analgesics, with avoidance of contact sports when splenomegaly is present.

head: Sulindac (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulindac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the indene acetic acid derivative class, used as a prodrug whose active sulfide metabolite reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is established for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and acute gouty arthritis. Notable risks include serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated after coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Labetalol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Labetalol is a mixed adrenergic antagonist of the amino alcohol class that blocks β1-, β2-, and α1-adrenergic receptors, lowering heart rate and peripheral vascular resistance. It is used for hypertension, including severe hypertension and hypertension in pregnancy. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma, overt heart failure, and greater-than-first-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Cefuroxime (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefuroxime is a second-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including respiratory tract, skin and soft tissue, urinary tract infections, and gonorrhea; perioperative prophylaxis is also established for injectable forms. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, with contraindication in patients with serious cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Sunitinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sunitinib is an oral small-molecule multi-target tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the indolinone class that blocks VEGFR, PDGFR, KIT, FLT3, CSF1R, and RET signaling, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and proliferation. It is approved for renal cell carcinoma, imatinib-resistant or intolerant gastrointestinal stromal tumor, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. A notable toxicity is cardiotoxicity, including decreased left ventricular ejection fraction.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Carvedilol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carvedilol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic blocker of the carbazole derivative class with additional alpha1-adrenergic antagonism, reducing heart rate, myocardial contractility, and peripheral vascular resistance. It is established for hypertension, heart failure, and left ventricular dysfunction after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma and severe bradycardia or heart block.
tail_desc: Peripheral arterial disease is an atherosclerotic vascular disorder of the peripheral arteries, most often affecting the lower limbs. Arterial narrowing reduces blood flow, causing exertional leg pain (intermittent claudication), diminished pulses, and in advanced cases rest pain, ulcers, or tissue loss. It is common in older adults and smokers. Management focuses on risk-factor modification, antiplatelet and statin therapy, supervised exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Sulfamethazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamethazine is a long-acting sulfonamide antibacterial (sulfonamide antifolate) that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It has been used mainly in veterinary medicine for susceptible bacterial infections and historically in some human infections. A notable risk is severe sulfonamide hypersensitivity, including Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Cyproheptadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyproheptadine is a first-generation antihistamine of the piperidine class with additional antiserotonergic and anticholinergic activity. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist and also blocks serotonin 5-HT2 receptors. It is used for allergic conditions such as allergic rhinitis and urticaria, and commonly causes marked sedation; it is contraindicated in newborns and during breastfeeding.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: vitamin K deficiency hemorrhagic disease (a disease)
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Vitamin K deficiency hemorrhagic disease is a neonatal bleeding disorder of the hematologic/coagulation system caused by insufficient vitamin K–dependent activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. It typically affects newborns, especially those without vitamin K prophylaxis, and presents with bruising, gastrointestinal, umbilical, mucosal, or intracranial bleeding. Management consists of prompt vitamin K administration, supportive treatment of hemorrhage, and routine neonatal prophylaxis.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Triamterene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triamterene is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pteridine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal renal tubule and collecting duct, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for edema and hypertension, often with a thiazide diuretic. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria, severe renal impairment, or preexisting hyperkalemia.
tail_desc: Hyperparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the parathyroid glands causing excess parathyroid hormone, which raises blood calcium and disrupts bone and kidney homeostasis. It may be primary, secondary to chronic kidney disease or vitamin D deficiency, or tertiary after prolonged stimulation. Common features include hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, bone pain, fractures, weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; management targets the cause, with parathyroidectomy for selected cases.

head: Physostigmine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Physostigmine is a reversible carbamate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (an indirect cholinergic agonist) that increases acetylcholine at muscarinic and nicotinic synapses, including in the CNS. It is used to reverse anticholinergic toxicity and has also been used for glaucoma. Notable risks include bradycardia and seizures, and it is contraindicated in tricyclic antidepressant overdose due to potential cardiac toxicity.
tail_desc: Iritis is inflammation of the iris, typically classified as anterior uveitis of the eye. It may be idiopathic or associated with autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or drug exposure. Patients usually present with eye pain, redness, photophobia, and blurred vision. Management generally involves prompt ophthalmologic evaluation, topical corticosteroids and cycloplegic agents, and treatment of any underlying cause.

head: Chlorphentermine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorphentermine is a sympathomimetic anorectic of the amphetamine/phenethylamine family formerly used as an anti-obesity agent. It acts primarily by increasing central monoaminergic signaling, especially promoting norepinephrine release to suppress appetite, though its precise molecular targets are not well defined. Its clinical use has largely been abandoned because of serious pulmonary toxicity, notably phospholipidosis.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Arginine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Arginine (L-arginine) is a basic, semi-essential amino acid used as a nutritional/pharmaceutical supplement. It serves as the endogenous substrate for nitric oxide synthase, increasing nitric oxide production, and also participates in the urea cycle. Established uses include treatment of urea cycle disorders and supplementation in arginine deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Renal tubular acidosis is a group of kidney tubular disorders causing normal anion gap metabolic acidosis from impaired renal acid excretion or bicarbonate reabsorption. It may be inherited or acquired. Typical features include hyperchloremia, growth failure in children, nephrolithiasis, hypokalemia or hyperkalemia, and muscle weakness. Management targets the cause and corrects acidosis with alkali therapy, with electrolyte-specific treatment as needed.

head: Prednicarbate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prednicarbate is a nonhalogenated topical corticosteroid of the prednisolone derivative class used primarily for corticosteroid-responsive inflammatory dermatoses such as atopic dermatitis and eczema. It acts by activating intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress proinflammatory cytokines and other mediators. A notable adverse effect is local skin atrophy with prolonged use, especially under occlusion or on thin skin.
tail_desc: Skin atrophy is a dermatologic condition characterized by thinning and loss of normal epidermal, dermal, or subcutaneous tissue, often due to aging, chronic corticosteroid exposure, sun damage, inflammation, or inherited disorders. It presents with fragile, shiny, wrinkled, sometimes translucent skin and easy tearing or bruising, especially in older adults or treated areas. Management focuses on addressing the cause, protecting skin, and minimizing further injury.

head: Guanabenz (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanabenz is a centrally acting antihypertensive of the imidazoline/guanidine class that functions primarily as an alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonist in the brainstem, reducing sympathetic outflow and lowering blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable adverse effect is sedation or drowsiness; abrupt withdrawal may also cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Piroxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Piroxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and other musculoskeletal pain and inflammation. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Aluminium phosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aluminium phosphate is an aluminum salt used as a non-systemic antacid and as a vaccine adjuvant. As an antacid, it neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach, reducing acidity without a specific molecular target. Established uses include symptomatic relief of hyperacidity, heartburn, and peptic ulcer disease. Notable risks include constipation and, particularly in renal impairment, aluminum accumulation.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Sodium tetradecyl sulfate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium tetradecyl sulfate is an anionic surfactant detergent and sclerosing agent used for injection. It damages venous endothelium, triggering inflammation, thrombosis, and subsequent fibrosis/obliteration of the vessel; it does not have a specific molecular target. It is used to treat small uncomplicated varicose veins, including lower-extremity varicosities and venules. Notable risks include anaphylaxis and tissue necrosis after extravasation.
tail_desc: Atherosclerosis is a chronic arterial disease of the cardiovascular system characterized by lipid-rich plaque formation in the intima, driven by endothelial injury, inflammation, and fibrous cap development. It commonly affects older adults and people with risk factors such as smoking, diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, causing ischemia, angina, myocardial infarction, stroke, or claudication. Management focuses on risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Iloprost (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iloprost is a synthetic prostacyclin (PGI2) analogue that activates prostacyclin IP receptors, increasing cAMP to promote potent vasodilation and inhibition of platelet aggregation. It is used for pulmonary arterial hypertension and, in some settings, severe peripheral vascular disease such as critical limb ischemia or Raynaud-related digital ulcers. Common adverse effects include headache, flushing, and hypotension.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Amiodarone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amiodarone is a class III antiarrhythmic, an iodinated benzofuran derivative that primarily prolongs cardiac repolarization by blocking potassium channels, while also inhibiting sodium and calcium channels and exerting noncompetitive beta-adrenergic effects. It is used for life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias and supraventricular arrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation. A notable adverse effect is pulmonary toxicity, including interstitial pneumonitis and fibrosis.
tail_desc: Eosinophilic pneumonia is an inflammatory lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by eosinophil-rich infiltration of the pulmonary interstitium and alveoli, occurring in acute or chronic forms and sometimes triggered by drugs, smoking, infections, or systemic eosinophilic disorders. Patients typically develop cough, dyspnea, fever, and pulmonary infiltrates; management centers on identifying causes and treatment with systemic corticosteroids, often with rapid improvement.

head: Ammonia (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ammonia is a small endogenous/basic nitrogenous compound rather than a conventional therapeutic drug. Medicinally, ammonia solution is used mainly as an inhalant respiratory stimulant in managing vasovagal syncope or near-fainting, acting by irritating nasal and respiratory mucosa to trigger reflex inhalation; it has no specific molecular drug target. It can cause mucosal irritation and should not be applied directly to eyes or damaged skin.
tail_desc: Common wart (verruca vulgaris) is a benign viral infection of the skin caused by human papillomavirus, usually affecting the epidermis of the hands, fingers, knees, or periungual areas. It presents as firm, rough, hyperkeratotic papules with a verrucous surface, especially in children and young adults. Many resolve spontaneously; management includes topical salicylic acid, cryotherapy, and other destructive or immunomodulatory treatments when bothersome or persistent.

head: Cholestyramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant, an anion-exchange resin that binds bile acids in the intestinal lumen, preventing their reabsorption via the enterohepatic circulation and thereby lowering LDL cholesterol. It is used for hypercholesterolemia and pruritus due to partial biliary obstruction. Notable adverse effects include constipation; it is contraindicated in complete biliary obstruction.
tail_desc: Cholelithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, characterized by stone formation in the gallbladder, usually from cholesterol supersaturation or, less often, pigment precipitation. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease typically causes episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cases.

head: Carvedilol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carvedilol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic blocker of the carbazole derivative class with additional alpha1-adrenergic antagonism, reducing heart rate, myocardial contractility, and peripheral vascular resistance. It is established for hypertension, heart failure, and left ventricular dysfunction after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma and severe bradycardia or heart block.
tail_desc: Peripheral vascular disease is a circulatory disorder of the peripheral blood vessels, most often involving atherosclerotic narrowing or occlusion of arteries supplying the legs. It commonly affects older adults and people who smoke or have diabetes, causing intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool extremities, poor wound healing, and sometimes critical limb ischemia. Management emphasizes risk-factor reduction, exercise, antiplatelet/statin therapy, and revascularization when severe.

head: Indapamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Indapamide is a thiazide-like sulfonamide diuretic used mainly to treat hypertension and edema. It acts primarily in the distal convoluted tubule by inhibiting the sodium/chloride cotransporter (NCC), increasing urinary sodium and water excretion, with additional antihypertensive vascular effects. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria and should be used cautiously in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Flurbiprofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flurbiprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the propionic acid derivative class that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for pain and inflammation, including rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis, and as an ophthalmic agent to inhibit intraoperative miosis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Iron (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iron is an essential mineral and hematinic used in oral and parenteral preparations for iron replacement. It replenishes body iron stores needed for hemoglobin and myoglobin synthesis rather than acting on a single receptor target. It is established for prevention and treatment of iron deficiency and iron-deficiency anemia. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation and constipation; it is contraindicated in iron overload states such as hemochromatosis.
tail_desc: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder of copper metabolism affecting mainly the liver and nervous system, caused by ATP7B mutations that impair biliary copper excretion and lead to toxic copper accumulation. It typically presents in children, adolescents, or young adults with hepatitis, cirrhosis, tremor, dystonia, psychiatric symptoms, and Kayser-Fleischer rings. Management centers on lifelong copper chelation or zinc therapy, dietary copper reduction, and liver transplantation for advanced hepatic failure.

head: Irinotecan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irinotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. Its active metabolite SN-38 stabilizes the topoisomerase I-DNA complex, blocking DNA religation and causing lethal DNA damage during replication. It is used for metastatic colorectal cancer and in some regimens for other solid tumors. Notable toxicities include severe delayed diarrhea and myelosuppression, especially neutropenia.
tail_desc: Interstitial lung disease is a heterogeneous group of parenchymal lung disorders affecting the pulmonary interstitium and alveoli, characterized by inflammation and/or progressive fibrosis that impair gas exchange. Patients, often adults, commonly present with exertional dyspnea, dry cough, and restrictive physiology with diffuse infiltrates. Management targets the underlying cause, using antigen avoidance or immunosuppression in selected cases, antifibrotic therapy for fibrotic forms, and supportive oxygen or transplantation when advanced.

head: Clopidogrel (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clopidogrel is an oral thienopyridine antiplatelet prodrug that irreversibly inhibits the platelet ADP receptor P2Y12, thereby reducing ADP-mediated platelet activation and aggregation. It is used to prevent atherothrombotic events in acute coronary syndrome, recent myocardial infarction, recent ischemic stroke, and established peripheral arterial disease, including after percutaneous coronary intervention. A notable adverse effect is bleeding.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenic purpura is a hematologic bleeding disorder characterized by thrombocytopenia, often due to immune-mediated platelet destruction (as in immune thrombocytopenic purpura) or, less commonly, platelet consumption in microangiopathic disease. It causes petechiae, purpura, easy bruising, epistaxis, and mucosal bleeding, affecting both children and adults. Management targets the cause and bleeding risk, using observation, corticosteroids, IVIG, or other cause-specific therapy.

head: Lopinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lopinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, commonly coformulated with ritonavir to boost exposure via CYP3A inhibition. It inhibits the viral HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and producing immature, noninfectious virions. It is approved, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it also has important CYP3A-mediated drug interaction contraindications.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Lopinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lopinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, commonly coformulated with ritonavir to boost exposure via CYP3A inhibition. It inhibits the viral HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and producing immature, noninfectious virions. It is approved, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it also has important CYP3A-mediated drug interaction contraindications.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Clomipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clomipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzazepine class that primarily inhibits serotonin reuptake, with additional norepinephrine reuptake inhibition, via blockade of SERT and NET. It is established for obsessive-compulsive disorder and also used for depression. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic effects and QT prolongation; it is contraindicated with concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Methazolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: familial adrenal hypoplasia with absent pituitary luteinizing hormone (a disease)
head_desc: Methazolamide is a systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, a sulfonamide derivative, that reduces aqueous humor formation by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase, primarily in the ciliary processes of the eye. It is used to lower elevated intraocular pressure in glaucoma, including chronic open-angle glaucoma and acute angle-closure situations. A notable risk is sulfonamide hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in significant hepatic or renal impairment.
tail_desc: familial adrenal hypoplasia with absent pituitary luteinizing hormone is a disease or medical condition.

head: Trimethoprim (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethoprim is a synthetic diaminopyrimidine antibacterial that selectively inhibits bacterial dihydrofolate reductase, blocking tetrahydrofolate synthesis and thereby impairing nucleic acid production. It is established for treatment and prophylaxis of susceptible urinary tract infections and, in combination with sulfamethoxazole, for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and other bacterial infections. Notable adverse effects include hyperkalemia and megaloblastic anemia, especially in folate deficiency.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Pergolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pergolide is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist used historically in Parkinson disease, acting primarily at dopamine D2 and also D1 receptors to improve motor symptoms. It has also been used for hyperprolactinemic disorders. A notable serious adverse effect is cardiac valvulopathy linked to ergot-associated fibrotic reactions, making it contraindicated in patients with valvular heart disease.
tail_desc: Hemopericardium is a cardiovascular condition in which blood accumulates within the pericardial sac, usually from cardiac trauma, myocardial rupture, aortic dissection, invasive procedures, or anticoagulation-related bleeding. It may present with chest pain, dyspnea, hypotension, jugular venous distension, and cardiac tamponade, often in acutely ill or injured patients. Management is urgent and focuses on hemodynamic stabilization, pericardial drainage, and treatment of the bleeding source.

head: Naratriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naratriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It treats acute migraine attacks, with or without aura, by causing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. Notable adverse effects include paresthesia, dizziness, and chest or throat tightness; it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease and certain cerebrovascular syndromes.
tail_desc: Peripheral vascular disease is a circulatory disorder of the peripheral blood vessels, most often involving atherosclerotic narrowing or occlusion of arteries supplying the legs. It commonly affects older adults and people who smoke or have diabetes, causing intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool extremities, poor wound healing, and sometimes critical limb ischemia. Management emphasizes risk-factor reduction, exercise, antiplatelet/statin therapy, and revascularization when severe.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: Porphyrin metabolism disease refers to disorders of heme biosynthesis or porphyrin handling, affecting the hematologic and hepatic systems and often termed porphyrias. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and age. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, supportive care, and subtype-specific measures such as hemin for acute hepatic attacks.

head: Dasatinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dasatinib is an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the thiazole carboxamide class that inhibits BCR-ABL and Src-family kinases, blocking proliferation signaling. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome–positive chronic myeloid leukemia and Ph+ acute lymphoblastic leukemia. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression; pleural effusion is also a well-recognized toxicity.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Amphotericin B (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amphotericin B is a polyene macrolide antifungal that binds ergosterol in fungal cell membranes, forming pores that increase membrane permeability and cause cell death. It is used for severe systemic fungal infections, including invasive candidiasis, aspergillosis, cryptococcosis, and mucormycosis. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity, especially with conventional formulations.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Amikacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amikacin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that irreversibly binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing bactericidal activity. It is used for serious infections due to susceptible Gram-negative bacteria, including complicated urinary tract, intra-abdominal, respiratory, and bloodstream infections, and has activity against some mycobacteria. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Furosemide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Furosemide is a loop diuretic, chemically a sulfamoylanthranilic acid derivative, that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter (NKCC2, SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used for edema associated with heart failure, cirrhosis, or renal disease, and for hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Heparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, thereby inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa and reducing fibrin clot formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndromes, and during procedures such as hemodialysis. A notable adverse effect is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Osteoporosis is a metabolic skeletal disease of the musculoskeletal system characterized by low bone mass and deterioration of bone microarchitecture, causing increased bone fragility and fracture risk. It commonly affects older adults, especially postmenopausal women, and may be asymptomatic until vertebral, hip, or wrist fractures occur. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, adequate calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Lomustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
