head_desc: Lomustine is an oral nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that spontaneously forms reactive intermediates causing DNA and RNA alkylation and interstrand cross-linking, without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors, including gliomas, and for relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma in combination regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative myelosuppression; severe bone marrow suppression is a major contraindication.
tail_desc: Infectious disease is a broad category of disorders caused by pathogenic microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, or prions, affecting virtually any organ system. It results from microbial invasion and host inflammatory response, with manifestations ranging from fever and malaise to organ-specific symptoms. It can occur in any population, especially the very young, elderly, or immunocompromised, and is managed with pathogen-directed therapy, supportive care, and prevention measures such as hygiene, vaccination, and isolation.

head: Cyclosporine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclosporine is a calcineurin-inhibiting immunosuppressant, a cyclic undecapeptide derived from fungi. It binds cyclophilin, and the complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase, reducing NFAT activation and T-cell cytokine transcription, especially interleukin-2. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection and to treat conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and severe dry eye. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Lymphosarcoma is an older term historically used for malignant lymphoma, a cancer of lymphoid tissue affecting the lymphatic and hematopoietic systems. It arises from neoplastic proliferation of lymphocytes in lymph nodes or extranodal sites. Typical features include painless lymphadenopathy, constitutional “B” symptoms, and organ-specific effects, occurring mainly in adults. Management generally follows lymphoma subtype and stage, using chemotherapy, immunotherapy, radiotherapy, and sometimes stem cell transplantation.

head: Isoflurane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoflurane is a halogenated ether volatile inhalational general anesthetic. Its anesthetic action is not fully defined but involves enhancement of inhibitory GABA_A and glycine signaling and effects on two-pore-domain potassium channels, with CNS depression. It is used to induce and maintain general anesthesia during surgery. A notable serious risk is malignant hyperthermia in susceptible patients.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Esmolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting cardioselective beta-blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class that primarily antagonizes beta-1 adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, contractility, and AV nodal conduction. It is used intravenously for rapid control of supraventricular tachycardia and perioperative or emergency hypertension/tachycardia. A notable risk is hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe bradycardia or advanced heart block.
tail_desc: Pulmonary emphysema is a chronic obstructive lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by permanent enlargement of distal air spaces and destruction of alveolar walls, causing loss of elastic recoil and airflow limitation. It occurs most often in smokers, or with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, and presents with progressive dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance, and hyperinflation; management emphasizes smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen when indicated, and selected advanced interventions.

head: Tretamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tretamine is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Naratriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naratriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It treats acute migraine attacks, with or without aura, by causing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. Notable adverse effects include paresthesia, dizziness, and chest or throat tightness; it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease and certain cerebrovascular syndromes.
tail_desc: Cerebral infarction is an ischemic stroke of the central nervous system in which interrupted cerebral blood flow, usually from arterial thrombosis or embolism, causes focal brain tissue necrosis. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or visual loss, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible and secondary stroke prevention.

head: Dichlorophen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dichlorophen is a halogenated bisphenol anthelmintic and taeniacide formerly used mainly in veterinary medicine. It acts locally in the intestinal lumen against cestodes, though its precise molecular target is not well established. Established use has included treatment of tapeworm infections in animals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation such as vomiting and diarrhea, especially with overdosing.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Lopinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lopinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, commonly coformulated with ritonavir to boost exposure via CYP3A inhibition. It inhibits the viral HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and producing immature, noninfectious virions. It is approved, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it also has important CYP3A-mediated drug interaction contraindications.
tail_desc: Cardiomyopathy is a group of primary diseases of the heart muscle causing structural and functional myocardial abnormalities not explained solely by coronary, valvular, or hypertensive disease. It may be genetic or acquired and can present at any age with dyspnea, fatigue, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, heart failure, or arrhythmias. Management focuses on treating heart failure and rhythm complications, reducing sudden-death risk, and addressing the underlying subtype.

head: Nicardipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nicardipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation with relatively little direct cardiac depression. It is used to treat hypertension, including acute severe hypertension with the intravenous formulation, and chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is reflex tachycardia; it is contraindicated in advanced aortic stenosis.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Vorinostat (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vorinostat is a hydroxamic acid histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor that blocks class I and II HDAC enzymes, increasing histone acetylation and altering gene expression to promote cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis. It is approved for treatment of cutaneous manifestations of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma in patients with persistent, progressive, or recurrent disease. Notable adverse effects include thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Long QT syndrome is a cardiac electrical disorder affecting ventricular repolarization, characterized by prolongation of the QT interval on electrocardiography, usually due to inherited ion-channel defects or acquired drug/electrolyte effects. It predisposes children or adults to syncope, seizures, and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia including torsades de pointes, with risk of sudden death; management emphasizes trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, correction of reversible causes, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Tegaserod (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tegaserod is an indole carbazole derivative and selective serotonin 5-HT4 receptor partial agonist that enhances gastrointestinal motility and intestinal secretion via enteric neurons. It is approved for irritable bowel syndrome with constipation in certain adult women and has also been used for chronic idiopathic constipation. A notable concern is ischemic cardiovascular events, making use contraindicated in patients with prior myocardial infarction, stroke, or angina.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Ibandronate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ibandronate is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds bone hydroxyapatite and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, reducing bone resorption. It is approved for prevention and treatment of postmenopausal osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis with oral formulations; it is contraindicated in patients with esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying or inability to remain upright.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Tobramycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tobramycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing mistranslation and bactericidal activity, particularly against aerobic Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections and as inhaled therapy in cystic fibrosis airway infection. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Corneal pigmentation is an ocular corneal disorder characterized by abnormal deposition of pigment within the corneal epithelium, stroma, or endothelium, arising from iron, melanin, blood breakdown products, drugs, or secondary migration from adjacent ocular tissues. It may appear as visible brown, black, or golden corneal discoloration, sometimes with reduced vision or photophobia, and management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, with observation or specialist ophthalmic care as needed.

head: Irbesartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irbesartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl tetrazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and diabetic nephropathy in patients with type 2 diabetes. A notable contraindication is pregnancy because it can cause fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Renal artery obstruction is a vascular disorder of the renal circulation in which partial or complete blockage of a renal artery, usually from atherosclerotic stenosis, thromboembolism, or fibromuscular dysplasia, reduces kidney perfusion. It may cause resistant hypertension, worsening renal function, flank pain, or asymmetry in kidney size, affecting mainly older adults or younger women with fibromuscular dysplasia. Management targets blood pressure control and the underlying lesion, with selected cases treated by endovascular or surgical revascularization.

head: Sodium tetradecyl sulfate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium tetradecyl sulfate is an anionic surfactant detergent and sclerosing agent used for injection. It damages venous endothelium, triggering inflammation, thrombosis, and subsequent fibrosis/obliteration of the vessel; it does not have a specific molecular target. It is used to treat small uncomplicated varicose veins, including lower-extremity varicosities and venules. Notable risks include anaphylaxis and tissue necrosis after extravasation.
tail_desc: Infectious disease is a broad category of disorders caused by pathogenic microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, or prions, affecting virtually any organ system. It results from microbial invasion and host inflammatory response, with manifestations ranging from fever and malaise to organ-specific symptoms. It can occur in any population, especially the very young, elderly, or immunocompromised, and is managed with pathogen-directed therapy, supportive care, and prevention measures such as hygiene, vaccination, and isolation.

head: Quinupristin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quinupristin is a streptogramin B antibiotic, used in fixed combination with dalfopristin as quinupristin/dalfopristin. It binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis synergistically with dalfopristin. The combination is approved for serious vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium infections and complicated skin and skin-structure infections due to susceptible Gram-positive bacteria. Notable adverse effects include arthralgia and myalgia.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Entecavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Entecavir is a guanosine nucleoside analogue antiviral used to treat chronic hepatitis B virus infection. It is phosphorylated intracellularly and inhibits the HBV polymerase/reverse transcriptase, blocking priming, reverse transcription of the negative strand, and synthesis of the positive DNA strand. A notable warning is severe acute exacerbation of hepatitis after discontinuation; lactic acidosis and hepatomegaly with steatosis have also been reported.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Temozolomide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating antineoplastic, a triazene derivative, that spontaneously converts to MTIC and methylates DNA, primarily at O6- and N7-guanine residues, causing cytotoxic damage. It is approved for glioblastoma multiforme and refractory anaplastic astrocytoma. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia and neutropenia.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Ondansetron (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ondansetron is a selective serotonin 5-HT3 receptor antagonist, chemically a carbazole derivative, used as an antiemetic. It blocks 5-HT3 receptors on vagal afferents and in the chemoreceptor trigger zone to prevent nausea and vomiting, particularly those induced by chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and surgery. A notable adverse effect is QT interval prolongation, so caution is required in patients with congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Dicloxacillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dicloxacillin is an isoxazolyl, penicillinase-resistant beta-lactam penicillin antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing cell lysis. It is used for infections caused by susceptible penicillinase-producing staphylococci, including skin and soft tissue infections. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Phentolamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phentolamine is a nonselective, competitive alpha-adrenergic antagonist of the imidazoline class that blocks alpha1 and alpha2 receptors, producing peripheral vasodilation. It is used to prevent or control catecholamine-induced hypertensive episodes in pheochromocytoma and for treatment of dermal necrosis or ischemia after norepinephrine extravasation. Notable adverse effects include orthostatic hypotension and reflex tachycardia.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Trimethadione (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethadione is an oxazolidinedione anticonvulsant used primarily for refractory absence (petit mal) seizures. Its active metabolite, dimethadione, reduces thalamic low-threshold calcium currents by blocking T-type calcium channels, suppressing the cortical rhythm underlying absence seizures. A notable adverse effect is severe teratogenicity, so it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Piroxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Piroxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and other musculoskeletal pain and inflammation. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Lopinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lopinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, commonly coformulated with ritonavir to boost exposure via CYP3A inhibition. It inhibits the viral HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and producing immature, noninfectious virions. It is approved, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it also has important CYP3A-mediated drug interaction contraindications.
tail_desc: Hemophilia is an inherited bleeding disorder of the hematologic/coagulation system, usually caused by deficiency of clotting factor VIII (hemophilia A) or IX (hemophilia B), most often in an X-linked pattern. It causes prolonged bleeding, easy bruising, hemarthroses, and deep muscle or postoperative hemorrhage, typically affecting males. Management centers on replacement of the missing factor, prevention of bleeding, and treatment of complications.

head: Pyrazinamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pyrazinamide is a nicotinamide analog antitubercular drug used in combination therapy for active tuberculosis. It is a prodrug converted by mycobacterial pyrazinamidase (pncA) to pyrazinoic acid, which is active against Mycobacterium tuberculosis, though its precise molecular target is not fully defined. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity; it can also cause hyperuricemia and precipitate gout.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Porphyrin metabolism disease refers to disorders of heme biosynthesis or porphyrin handling, affecting the hematologic and hepatic systems and often termed porphyrias. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and age. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, supportive care, and subtype-specific measures such as hemin for acute hepatic attacks.

head: Fluvastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fluvastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that competitively inhibits hepatic 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to lower cardiovascular risk. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Tropicamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tropicamide is a short-acting antimuscarinic mydriatic and cycloplegic ophthalmic agent, structurally a synthetic parasympatholytic. It competitively antagonizes muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, primarily M4 and other ocular muscarinic receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle, producing pupil dilation and cycloplegia. It is used for diagnostic mydriasis and cycloplegic refraction; notable risks include increased intraocular pressure and precipitation of angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Cianidanol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cianidanol (also called catechin) is a flavan-3-ol polyphenol that was investigated as a hepatoprotective agent. Its precise primary molecular target is not well established; reported effects have been attributed broadly to antioxidant and membrane-stabilizing actions rather than a defined receptor or enzyme target. It was used in some countries for chronic liver disorders, but its clinical use was curtailed because of severe immune-mediated hemolytic anemia.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Milrinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Milrinone is a bipyridine phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor and inodilator that increases intracellular cAMP in cardiac myocytes and vascular smooth muscle, enhancing myocardial contractility and promoting vasodilation. It is used for short-term intravenous treatment of acute decompensated heart failure or low-output states. A notable adverse effect is ventricular arrhythmia; significant hypotension can also occur.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Meloxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meloxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that preferentially inhibits cyclooxygenase, mainly COX-2 over COX-1, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hyperlipidemia is a metabolic disorder of the cardiovascular system characterized by abnormally elevated blood lipids, especially cholesterol and/or triglycerides, due to genetic factors, diet, obesity, diabetes, hypothyroidism, or other secondary causes. It is often asymptomatic but increases risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and pancreatitis with severe hypertriglyceridemia. Management focuses on lifestyle modification, treating secondary causes, and lipid-lowering therapy such as statins when indicated.

head: Diiodohydroxyquinoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diiodohydroxyquinoline is a halogenated hydroxyquinoline antiprotozoal, also known as iodoquinol. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it acts luminally in the intestine against Entamoeba histolytica and has metal-chelating properties. It is used for asymptomatic intestinal amebiasis after tissue-active therapy. Notable adverse effects include optic neuritis and peripheral neuropathy, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Adrenocortical insufficiency is an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex characterized by deficient glucocorticoid production, often with mineralocorticoid deficiency in primary disease, due to autoimmune destruction, infection, hemorrhage, or impaired pituitary ACTH stimulation. Patients may develop fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hyperpigmentation in primary forms. Management centers on lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, mineralocorticoid replacement when needed, and stress-dose therapy during illness.

head: Bupropion (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bupropion is an aminoketone antidepressant and smoking-cessation agent that inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine, with minimal direct serotonergic activity; nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonism may also contribute. It is approved for major depressive disorder, seasonal affective disorder, and smoking cessation. A notable risk is seizures, so it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or eating disorders.
tail_desc: Bulimia nervosa is an eating disorder affecting mental health and multiple body systems, characterized by recurrent binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors such as self-induced vomiting, laxative misuse, fasting, or excessive exercise. It typically arises from multifactorial psychological, genetic, and sociocultural influences, often in adolescents and young adults, especially females. Management centers on nutritional rehabilitation, cognitive-behavioral therapy, and treatment of medical and psychiatric complications.

head: Efavirenz (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Efavirenz is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) used as part of combination antiretroviral therapy for HIV-1 infection. It binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Established indications include treatment of HIV-1 in adults and children in combination regimens. Notable adverse effects include rash and prominent central nervous system symptoms such as dizziness, insomnia, and vivid dreams.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Dalteparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dalteparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin. It is used for prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including perioperative and certain medical patients. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system caused primarily by estrogen deficiency after menopause, which accelerates bone resorption and reduces bone mass and microarchitectural strength. It mainly affects older postmenopausal women and is often silent until fragility fractures occur, especially in the vertebrae, hip, or wrist. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Iopanoic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iopanoic acid is an iodinated radiographic contrast agent in the oral cholecystographic class. It is taken up by hepatocytes and excreted into bile, increasing gallbladder and biliary tract radiopacity for imaging. It was used for oral cholecystography to evaluate gallbladder disease. Because of its high iodine load, it is contraindicated in iodine hypersensitivity and can precipitate thyroid dysfunction.
tail_desc: Allergic asthma is a common phenotype of asthma, a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways in the respiratory system, driven by allergen-triggered, IgE-associated immune responses with airway hyperresponsiveness and variable airflow obstruction. It often affects children and atopic individuals, causing episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often after allergen exposure. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and standard asthma therapy, especially inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.

head: Mivacurium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mivacurium is a short-acting, nondepolarizing benzylisoquinolinium neuromuscular blocking agent that competitively antagonizes nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the skeletal muscle motor end plate, producing paralysis. It is used as an adjunct to general anesthesia to facilitate endotracheal intubation and provide skeletal muscle relaxation during surgery or mechanical ventilation. Histamine release with hypotension or bronchospasm is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Adult-onset myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system, usually caused by antibodies against acetylcholine receptor or related postsynaptic proteins, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It presents in adults with fluctuating, fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunotherapy, and thymectomy in selected patients.

head: Mangafodipir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mangafodipir is a manganese-containing chelate contrast agent, specifically manganese dipyridoxyl diphosphate, formerly used in magnetic resonance imaging. It acts by delivering paramagnetic Mn²⁺ that shortens T1 relaxation time, enhancing liver and pancreatic tissue contrast rather than binding a single receptor target. It was approved for MRI of hepatic lesions. Notable adverse effects include flushing and nausea; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Peptic esophagitis is an inflammatory disorder of the esophagus, usually caused by reflux of acidic gastric contents in gastroesophageal reflux disease. It commonly presents in adults with heartburn, retrosternal pain, acid regurgitation, and sometimes dysphagia or odynophagia. Management focuses on acid suppression, especially proton pump inhibitors, along with lifestyle measures and treatment of the underlying reflux.

head: Cinchocaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cinchocaine (dibucaine) is a potent amide-type local anesthetic of the quinoline/carboxamide class. It acts mainly by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It is used topically and rectally for local anesthesia, including relief of pain and pruritus from hemorrhoids and minor anorectal conditions. Systemic absorption or overdose can cause serious CNS and cardiac toxicity.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Mesoridazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mesoridazine is a piperidine phenothiazine antipsychotic, historically used for schizophrenia. Like other typical antipsychotics, it acts primarily through central dopamine D2 receptor antagonism. Its clinical use became severely restricted because of dose-related QT interval prolongation and risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, including torsades de pointes, making it contraindicated in patients with known cardiac conduction abnormalities or significant QT prolongation.
tail_desc: X-linked severe congenital neutropenia is a rare inherited primary immunodeficiency affecting myeloid development, caused by pathogenic variants in WAS that impair actin cytoskeleton regulation and arrest neutrophil maturation, producing profound neutropenia from infancy. Affected boys develop recurrent severe bacterial infections, oral ulcers, and skin or respiratory infections. Management centers on granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, prompt antimicrobial treatment, and consideration of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Propofol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Propofol is an intravenous general anesthetic of the alkylphenol class that primarily potentiates GABA-A receptor–mediated inhibitory neurotransmission in the CNS. It is widely used for induction and maintenance of general anesthesia and for sedation of mechanically ventilated or monitored patients. A notable adverse effect is dose-dependent hypotension; it is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to propofol or formulation components.
tail_desc: Respiratory failure is a life-threatening syndrome of the respiratory system in which gas exchange is inadequate, causing hypoxemia with or without hypercapnia. It results from lung, airway, neuromuscular, chest wall, or central ventilatory dysfunction. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachypnea, cyanosis, confusion, or somnolence, especially in critically ill adults or children. Management targets the underlying cause and supports oxygenation and ventilation, including oxygen therapy and noninvasive or mechanical ventilation.

head: Temozolomide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating antineoplastic, a triazene derivative, that spontaneously converts to MTIC and methylates DNA, primarily at O6- and N7-guanine residues, causing cytotoxic damage. It is approved for glioblastoma multiforme and refractory anaplastic astrocytoma. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia and neutropenia.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Vardenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vardenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzenesulfonamide derivative, that selectively blocks PDE5 to increase cyclic GMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation in the corpus cavernosum. It is approved for erectile dysfunction. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nitrates, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Levobunolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Levobunolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class used primarily as an ophthalmic agent. By blocking beta-1 and beta-2 receptors in the ciliary body, it reduces aqueous humor production and lowers intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. Notable adverse effects include bronchospasm, so it is contraindicated in patients with asthma or severe COPD.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Parnaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Parnaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and to a lesser extent thrombin (factor IIa). It is used for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolic disorders, including postoperative deep vein thrombosis. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major hemorrhage or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system caused primarily by estrogen deficiency after menopause, which accelerates bone resorption and reduces bone mass and microarchitectural strength. It mainly affects older postmenopausal women and is often silent until fragility fractures occur, especially in the vertebrae, hip, or wrist. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Prazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic, structurally a 1,4-benzodiazepine derivative, used mainly for the short-term treatment of anxiety disorders. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing GABA-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated in myasthenia gravis and severe respiratory insufficiency.
tail_desc: Psychotic disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting brain function and behavior, defined by impaired reality testing due to diverse causes, including primary psychotic illnesses, mood disorders, substance use, or medical conditions. Core features include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking or behavior, and reduced insight, usually emerging in adolescents or adults. Management focuses on diagnostic evaluation, treatment of underlying causes, antipsychotic medication, psychotherapy, and safety/supportive care.

head: Chlorprothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorprothixene is a typical antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class that primarily acts by antagonizing dopamine D2 receptors, with additional antihistaminic and anticholinergic effects. It has been used to treat schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, and sometimes severe agitation. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation; extrapyramidal symptoms can also occur.
tail_desc: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is a rare, life-threatening neurologic and systemic reaction to dopamine-blocking antipsychotic drugs or abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic therapy. It is characterized by hyperthermia, severe generalized rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability, typically with elevated creatine kinase. It occurs in patients exposed to these agents and is managed by immediate drug cessation, intensive supportive care, and sometimes dantrolene or bromocriptine.

head: Biotin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Biotin (vitamin B7, vitamin H) is a water-soluble B-complex vitamin used as a nutritional supplement and replacement therapy for biotin deficiency, including deficiency associated with inherited biotinidase deficiency or prolonged parenteral nutrition. It functions as an essential cofactor for several carboxylase enzymes involved in fatty acid synthesis, gluconeogenesis, and amino acid metabolism. It is generally well tolerated, but high-dose biotin can interfere with certain laboratory immunoassays, causing misleading test results.
tail_desc: A lytic metastatic bone lesion is a secondary malignant lesion of the skeletal system caused by spread of a primary cancer to bone, with osteolytic destruction predominating. It commonly affects adults with advanced solid tumors and may cause localized bone pain, pathologic fractures, spinal cord compression, and hypercalcemia. Management targets the primary malignancy and skeletal complications using systemic anticancer therapy, radiotherapy, surgery, and bone-modifying agents.

head: Clofedanol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clofedanol is a centrally acting non-opioid antitussive (cough suppressant), structurally classified as a diphenylmethane derivative. Its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well established, but it suppresses the cough reflex via central action. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of nonproductive cough. Notable adverse effects include drowsiness and dizziness.
tail_desc: Bladder neck obstruction is a lower urinary tract outflow disorder of the urinary system in which the bladder neck fails to open adequately during voiding, causing functional or anatomic resistance to urine flow. It typically presents with obstructive and irritative voiding symptoms, elevated post-void residual, or urinary retention, often in men. Management generally includes alpha-blockers, treatment of contributing causes, and selected surgical bladder neck incision or resection.

head: Selegiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Selegiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class. By inhibiting MAO-B, it reduces dopamine breakdown in the central nervous system. It is established for Parkinson disease and, in transdermal form, major depressive disorder. Notable risks include insomnia and potentially serious hypertensive or serotonergic interactions with contraindicated sympathomimetic or serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Melanoma is a malignant neoplasm of melanocytes, usually arising in the skin but also occurring in the eye or mucosal surfaces. It is driven by UV-related DNA damage and oncogenic mutations, and often presents as a new or changing pigmented lesion with asymmetry, irregular borders, color variation, or nodularity, mainly in adults. Management centers on surgical excision, staging, and, for advanced disease, immunotherapy or targeted therapy.

head: Cevimeline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cevimeline is a muscarinic cholinergic agonist, a quinuclidine derivative, that primarily stimulates M3 muscarinic receptors to increase exocrine gland secretion, especially salivary flow. It is approved for treatment of dry mouth (xerostomia) in patients with Sjögren’s syndrome. Notable adverse effects include excessive sweating, and it is contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma and in conditions where miosis is undesirable, such as narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Cholelithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, characterized by stone formation in the gallbladder, usually from cholesterol supersaturation or, less often, pigment precipitation. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease typically causes episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cases.

head: Tiagabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tiagabine is a nipecotic acid derivative anticonvulsant that selectively inhibits the GABA transporter type 1 (GAT-1, SLC6A1), reducing neuronal and glial reuptake of GABA and increasing synaptic GABA levels. It is approved as adjunctive therapy for partial seizures in epilepsy. A notable adverse effect is dizziness or somnolence, and it can precipitate seizures in non-epileptic patients.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Benzoyl peroxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzoyl peroxide is a topical organic peroxide used mainly as an anti-acne agent. It decomposes in the skin to release free-radical oxygen species, producing bactericidal activity against Cutibacterium acnes and mild keratolytic/comedolytic effects. It is established for acne vulgaris treatment, alone or with other agents. Common adverse effects include local skin irritation, dryness, erythema, and peeling.
tail_desc: Photosensitivity disease is not a standard, specific diagnostic entity but a broad term for disorders in which skin reacts abnormally to ultraviolet or visible light. Mechanisms vary and include phototoxic, photoallergic, genetic, autoimmune, or metabolic causes. Patients typically develop sun-exposed erythema, rash, burning, or blistering. Management focuses on identifying the cause, strict photoprotection, and treating the underlying disorder.

head: Tripelennamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tripelennamine is a first-generation antihistamine of the ethylenediamine class that acts primarily as a histamine H1 receptor antagonist. It has been used to relieve allergic conditions such as allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and pruritic skin reactions. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and caution is warranted because its anticholinergic effects can worsen angle-closure glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Clavulanic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clavulanic acid is a beta-lactam beta-lactamase inhibitor, structurally a clavem, used with beta-lactam antibiotics such as amoxicillin to overcome bacterial resistance. It irreversibly inhibits many serine beta-lactamases, protecting the partner antibiotic rather than exerting strong antibacterial activity alone. Established uses include susceptible respiratory, skin, urinary, and other mixed infections; cholestatic hepatitis is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Triptorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triptorelin is a synthetic decapeptide gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that binds pituitary GnRH receptors; continuous administration suppresses LH and FSH release, lowering sex steroid production. It is used for prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial hormone “flare” that can transiently worsen symptoms.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Temozolomide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating antineoplastic, a triazene derivative, that spontaneously converts to MTIC and methylates DNA, primarily at O6- and N7-guanine residues, causing cytotoxic damage. It is approved for glioblastoma multiforme and refractory anaplastic astrocytoma. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia and neutropenia.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Silicon dioxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Silicon dioxide is an inorganic silicon oxide used in pharmaceutical products mainly as an excipient, including glidant, anti-caking agent, and tablet/capsule formulation aid, rather than as an active therapeutic drug. It has no defined pharmacologic mechanism or primary molecular target in routine medicinal use and no established therapeutic indication as an active agent. Inhalational exposure to crystalline silica is associated with pulmonary toxicity, including silicosis.
tail_desc: Hemorrhoid is a common anorectal vascular condition of the lower gastrointestinal system caused by enlargement and distal displacement of normal anal vascular cushions, often associated with increased venous pressure and straining. Typical features include painless bright red rectal bleeding, prolapse, pruritus, discomfort, or pain if thrombosed, especially in adults. Management ranges from fiber, fluids, and topical or office procedures to surgical hemorrhoidectomy for severe disease.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Connective tissue disease is a broad category of disorders affecting collagen- and elastin-rich tissues, often involving skin, joints, muscles, blood vessels, and internal organs. Many are autoimmune inflammatory conditions, such as lupus, scleroderma, and Sjögren syndrome. Common features include arthralgia, Raynaud phenomenon, rash, sicca symptoms, and fatigue, often in women; management depends on subtype and organ involvement, using immunomodulation and supportive care.

head: Resorcinol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Resorcinol is a dihydroxybenzene phenol used topically as a keratolytic and antiseptic. Its effects are nonspecific, involving protein denaturation and disruption of keratinized epidermal tissue rather than a defined molecular target. It has been used in acne, seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, psoriasis, corns, calluses, and warts. Notable adverse effects include local irritation; excessive topical exposure may cause systemic toxicity, including methemoglobinemia.
tail_desc: Respiratory failure is a life-threatening syndrome of the respiratory system in which gas exchange is inadequate, causing hypoxemia with or without hypercapnia. It results from lung, airway, neuromuscular, chest wall, or central ventilatory dysfunction. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachypnea, cyanosis, confusion, or somnolence, especially in critically ill adults or children. Management targets the underlying cause and supports oxygenation and ventilation, including oxygen therapy and noninvasive or mechanical ventilation.

head: Felbamate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felbamate is a dicarbamate antiepileptic drug. Its anticonvulsant activity is attributed mainly to inhibition of NMDA receptor–mediated excitatory transmission, particularly at the glycine site, with additional potentiation of GABAergic inhibition. It is approved for focal seizures and for seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. A notable serious toxicity is aplastic anemia, with risk of acute hepatic failure.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Lamivudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lamivudine is a synthetic nucleoside analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI), a cytidine analogue, that is phosphorylated intracellularly and inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus DNA polymerase, causing chain termination. It is used to treat HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable risk is severe lactic acidosis with hepatomegaly and steatosis; abrupt discontinuation in hepatitis B can trigger severe exacerbations.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Butacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic in the benzoate family. Like other local anesthetics, it acts by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used topically, particularly in ophthalmic anesthesia. As with related ester anesthetics, hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known ester local anesthetic allergy.
tail_desc: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia is a congenital malformation of the diaphragm and thoracoabdominal system in which incomplete diaphragmatic development allows abdominal organs to herniate into the chest, impairing lung growth and causing pulmonary hypoplasia and pulmonary hypertension. It usually presents in neonates with severe respiratory distress and a scaphoid abdomen; management includes respiratory stabilization, treatment of pulmonary hypertension, and surgical repair.

head: Capecitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Capecitabine is an oral fluoropyrimidine antimetabolite and prodrug of 5-fluorouracil that is converted in tumors and tissues to metabolites inhibiting thymidylate synthase and incorporating into RNA and DNA, thereby impairing nucleic acid synthesis. It is used for colorectal and breast cancers, including metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is hand-foot syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with severe dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Glucosamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glucosamine is an endogenous amino monosaccharide and dietary supplement used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, especially of the knee. Its mechanism is not well established; it is thought to serve as a substrate for glycosaminoglycan and proteoglycan synthesis in cartilage, without a clearly defined primary molecular target. Adverse effects are usually mild gastrointestinal symptoms, and caution is advised in patients with shellfish allergy depending on product source.
tail_desc: Hyperlipidemia is a metabolic disorder of the cardiovascular system characterized by abnormally elevated blood lipids, especially cholesterol and/or triglycerides, due to genetic factors, diet, obesity, diabetes, hypothyroidism, or other secondary causes. It is often asymptomatic but increases risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and pancreatitis with severe hypertriglyceridemia. Management focuses on lifestyle modification, treating secondary causes, and lipid-lowering therapy such as statins when indicated.

head: Cefixime (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefixime is an oral third-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibiotic, that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including uncomplicated urinary tract infections, otitis media, pharyngitis/tonsillitis, acute bronchitis exacerbations, and uncomplicated gonorrhea. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Lurasidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lurasidone is an atypical antipsychotic of the benzisothiazole class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at 5-HT7 and 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar depression. A notable adverse effect is akathisia; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Cefoperazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefoperazone is a third-generation cephalosporin β-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, intra-abdominal, skin/soft-tissue, and biliary tract infections. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia with bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Misoprostol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog that acts primarily as an agonist at prostaglandin EP receptors, reducing gastric acid secretion and increasing uterine tone and cervical softening. It is used for prevention of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers and in obstetrics/gynecology for medical abortion, cervical ripening, labor induction, and postpartum hemorrhage. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in pregnancy when used for ulcer prevention.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Fluocinonide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fluocinonide is a high-potency synthetic fluorinated topical corticosteroid. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory cytokines and reduce vasodilation, edema, and pruritus. It is used for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses, including eczema, psoriasis, and allergic or inflammatory skin rashes. Notable adverse effects include skin atrophy; prolonged use can also cause hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression.
tail_desc: Acquired pituitary hormone deficiency is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary system caused by loss of one or more anterior or posterior pituitary hormones after previously normal function, commonly due to pituitary tumors, surgery, radiation, trauma, inflammation, or infarction. Manifestations reflect the deficient hormone(s), including growth failure, delayed puberty, amenorrhea, infertility, fatigue, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, or diabetes insipidus; management targets the cause and lifelong hormone replacement.

head: Ceftibuten (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ceftibuten is an oral third-generation cephalosporin antibiotic (a beta-lactam) that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis. It is used for susceptible infections including otitis media, pharyngitis/tonsillitis, acute bacterial exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and uncomplicated urinary tract infections. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in patients with known serious hypersensitivity to ceftibuten or other cephalosporins.
tail_desc: Colitis is inflammation of the colon, a gastrointestinal disorder that may result from inflammatory bowel disease, infection, ischemia, medication effects, or radiation. It commonly causes abdominal pain, diarrhea, urgency, rectal bleeding, and sometimes fever or weight loss, affecting both children and adults depending on cause. Management targets the etiology and severity, including hydration, dietary support, anti-inflammatory or antimicrobial therapy, and sometimes surgery for complications.

head: Dirithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dirithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic and an ester prodrug of erythromycylamine. After conversion to the active compound, it binds the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It has been used for susceptible mild-to-moderate respiratory tract infections and uncomplicated skin and soft-tissue infections. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, and it is contraindicated in patients with macrolide hypersensitivity.
