tail_desc: Sudden cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which abrupt loss of effective cardiac output, usually from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, causes sudden collapse, unresponsiveness, and absent pulse. It can occur in adults with structural heart disease or acute ischemia, and requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and urgent post-resuscitation care.

head: Isoleucine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoleucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid used in amino acid replacement and parenteral nutrition formulations. It acts as a substrate for protein synthesis and energy metabolism rather than through a single pharmacologic receptor target. Clinically, it is included to prevent or treat amino acid deficiency in patients requiring nutritional support. Excessive amino acid administration may contribute to azotemia, especially in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Parnaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Parnaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and to a lesser extent thrombin (factor IIa). It is used for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolic disorders, including postoperative deep vein thrombosis. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major hemorrhage or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis obliterans is a chronic occlusive peripheral arterial disease, usually affecting lower-extremity arteries, caused by progressive atherosclerotic plaque formation with luminal narrowing and impaired blood flow. It typically occurs in older adults, especially smokers and patients with diabetes, presenting with intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool limbs, rest pain, or ischemic ulcers. Management focuses on risk-factor control, antiplatelet therapy, exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Dihydrotachysterol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dihydrotachysterol is a synthetic vitamin D analog (secosteroid) used as a calcium-regulating agent. After hepatic activation, it acts via the vitamin D receptor to increase intestinal calcium absorption and help restore serum calcium. It has been used for hypocalcemia, particularly in hypoparathyroidism. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia, so it is contraindicated in patients with pre-existing hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Polyvinyl alcohol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Polyvinyl alcohol is a synthetic, water-soluble polymer used as an ophthalmic lubricant and artificial tear excipient. It acts physically rather than via a specific molecular target, stabilizing the tear film and reducing ocular surface friction. Established uses include temporary relief of dry eye irritation and ocular discomfort. Mild transient blurred vision or local eye irritation can occur after instillation.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Triptorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triptorelin is a synthetic decapeptide gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that binds pituitary GnRH receptors; continuous administration suppresses LH and FSH release, lowering sex steroid production. It is used for prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial hormone “flare” that can transiently worsen symptoms.
tail_desc: Urinary tract obstruction is a genitourinary disorder in which partial or complete blockage of urine flow occurs anywhere from the renal pelvis to the urethra, causing increased upstream pressure, hydronephrosis, infection risk, and potential renal impairment. Symptoms may include flank or abdominal pain, lower urinary tract symptoms, anuria, hematuria, or recurrent urinary infection. Management focuses on urgent decompression when indicated and treatment of the underlying cause.

head: Yohimbine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Yohimbine is an indole alkaloid and selective alpha-2 adrenergic receptor antagonist that increases central sympathetic outflow and norepinephrine release. It has been used clinically, notably as yohimbine hydrochloride, for erectile dysfunction, though its approval status varies by country and it is not widely used now. Notable adverse effects include hypertension, tachycardia, anxiety, and insomnia.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Vardenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vardenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzenesulfonamide derivative, that selectively blocks PDE5 to increase cyclic GMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation in the corpus cavernosum. It is approved for erectile dysfunction. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nitrates, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Balanitis xerotica obliterans is a chronic inflammatory sclerosing dermatosis of the male genital skin, generally regarded as genital lichen sclerosus affecting the glans and foreskin. It causes porcelain-white atrophic plaques, scarring phimosis, painful fissuring, meatal or urethral narrowing, and urinary symptoms, mainly in uncircumcised males. Management centers on potent topical corticosteroids, with circumcision or surgery for refractory disease or stenosis.

head: Clemastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clemastine is a first-generation antihistamine of the ethanolamine class that acts primarily as a reversible H1 histamine receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is used for allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and pruritic allergic conditions. A notable adverse effect is sedation due to central nervous system penetration; it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Bladder neck obstruction is a lower urinary tract outflow disorder of the urinary system in which the bladder neck fails to open adequately during voiding, causing functional or anatomic resistance to urine flow. It typically presents with obstructive and irritative voiding symptoms, elevated post-void residual, or urinary retention, often in men. Management generally includes alpha-blockers, treatment of contributing causes, and selected surgical bladder neck incision or resection.

head: Letrozole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Letrozole is a nonsteroidal triazole aromatase inhibitor that selectively inhibits CYP19A1, blocking conversion of androgens to estrogens and thereby lowering estrogen levels. It is established for hormone receptor-positive breast cancer in postmenopausal women, including adjuvant and advanced settings. A notable adverse effect is bone mineral density loss with increased fracture risk.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system caused primarily by estrogen deficiency after menopause, which accelerates bone resorption and reduces bone mass and microarchitectural strength. It mainly affects older postmenopausal women and is often silent until fragility fractures occur, especially in the vertebrae, hip, or wrist. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Cycloserine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cycloserine is an oral second-line antitubercular antibiotic, a cyclic analog of D-alanine. It inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by targeting alanine racemase and D-alanine:D-alanine ligase. It is used in combination regimens for active tuberculosis, especially drug-resistant disease. A notable adverse effect is dose-related central nervous system toxicity, including seizures and psychosis.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Isosorbide mononitrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isosorbide mononitrate is an organic nitrate antianginal and active metabolite of isosorbide dinitrate. It acts as a nitric oxide donor, activating soluble guanylate cyclase in vascular smooth muscle to increase cGMP and promote predominantly venous vasodilation, reducing preload and myocardial oxygen demand. It is used for prevention of angina pectoris. A notable contraindication is concomitant phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor use, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Intracerebral hemorrhage is a subtype of stroke in the central nervous system caused by bleeding directly into the brain parenchyma, usually from rupture of small vessels due to chronic hypertension or cerebral amyloid angiopathy. It presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, vomiting, and reduced consciousness, especially in older adults. Management focuses on urgent stabilization, blood pressure control, reversal of anticoagulation, and neurosurgical evaluation in selected cases.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Sympathetic paraganglioma is a neuroendocrine tumor arising from extra-adrenal paraganglionic tissue of the sympathetic nervous system, usually in the abdomen or thorax. It often secretes catecholamines, causing episodic or sustained hypertension, headaches, palpitations, and sweating; some cases are hereditary. Management typically involves biochemical testing, imaging for localization, preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade, and surgical resection when feasible.

head: Nandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nandrolone is a synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid and testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein synthesis and erythropoiesis. Its ester forms, such as nandrolone decanoate, have been used for anemia associated with chronic kidney disease and some wasting states. Notable adverse effects include virilization and other androgenic effects; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in prostate or male breast carcinoma.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Echothiophate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Echothiophate is an organophosphate, irreversible cholinesterase inhibitor used topically in ophthalmology. By covalently inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, it increases acetylcholine at cholinergic synapses, producing prolonged miosis and enhanced aqueous humor outflow. It has been used for glaucoma and accommodative esotropia. A notable adverse effect is cataract formation; it is generally avoided in angle-closure glaucoma unless definitive treatment is provided.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Alvimopan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alvimopan is a peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor antagonist, structurally a selective opioid receptor blocker with limited central nervous system penetration. It counteracts opioid-induced inhibition of gastrointestinal motility by blocking peripheral MORs in the gut. It is approved to accelerate gastrointestinal recovery after partial large or small bowel resection with primary anastomosis. A notable contraindication is use in patients receiving therapeutic opioids for more than seven consecutive days before treatment.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Telmisartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telmisartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl benzimidazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and to reduce cardiovascular risk in selected high-risk patients. Notable risks include hyperkalemia and renal impairment, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Cyclopentolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclopentolate is an antimuscarinic mydriatic and cycloplegic agent, chemically a synthetic tertiary amine, that competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle. It is used ophthalmically to induce mydriasis and cycloplegia for eye examination and refraction. A notable adverse effect is transient ocular irritation, and it should be used cautiously in patients with angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Pirbuterol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pirbuterol is a short-acting sympathomimetic bronchodilator of the beta2-adrenergic agonist class. It activates beta2-adrenergic receptors on bronchial smooth muscle, increasing cyclic AMP and producing bronchodilation. It is used for the treatment or prevention of bronchospasm in patients with reversible airway disease, including asthma. Notable adverse effects include tremor, nervousness, and tachycardia.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Oxazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxazepam is a short- to intermediate-acting benzodiazepine anxiolytic that positively modulates the GABA\(_A\) receptor at the benzodiazepine site, enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders and symptomatic relief of acute alcohol withdrawal. Notable adverse effects include sedation and psychomotor impairment; benzodiazepines are contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma and should be used cautiously with other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Dorzolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dorzolamide is a topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, a sulfonamide derivative, used primarily in ophthalmology. It inhibits carbonic anhydrase II in the ciliary processes, reducing aqueous humor formation and lowering intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is local ocular burning or stinging, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to sulfonamides.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Felodipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is established for the treatment of hypertension and, in some settings, chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; it is generally contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug.
tail_desc: Connective tissue disease is a broad category of disorders affecting collagen- and elastin-rich tissues, often involving skin, joints, muscles, blood vessels, and internal organs. Many are autoimmune inflammatory conditions, such as lupus, scleroderma, and Sjögren syndrome. Common features include arthralgia, Raynaud phenomenon, rash, sicca symptoms, and fatigue, often in women; management depends on subtype and organ involvement, using immunomodulation and supportive care.

head: Magnesium hydroxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium hydroxide is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an antacid and osmotic laxative. It neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach and, when taken orally in larger doses, retains water in the intestinal lumen by osmotic action; it has no specific receptor target. Established uses include relief of dyspepsia/heartburn and short-term treatment of constipation. A notable risk is hypermagnesemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Fenfluramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fenfluramine is an amphetamine-derived serotonergic agent and antiseizure medication that increases extracellular serotonin, with activity at serotonin transporters and receptors including sigma-1 receptor modulation. It is approved for seizures associated with Dravet syndrome and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. A notable risk is valvular heart disease and pulmonary arterial hypertension, requiring cardiac monitoring.
tail_desc: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder of glucose regulation in the endocrine system, characterized by insulin resistance and progressive pancreatic beta-cell dysfunction causing hyperglycemia. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity or sedentary lifestyle, and may present with polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and long-term vascular complications. Management focuses on weight loss, diet, exercise, glucose-lowering medications, and cardiovascular risk reduction.

head: Chlorprothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorprothixene is a typical antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class that primarily acts by antagonizing dopamine D2 receptors, with additional antihistaminic and anticholinergic effects. It has been used to treat schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, and sometimes severe agitation. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation; extrapyramidal symptoms can also occur.
tail_desc: Alzheimer disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system and the most common cause of dementia in older adults. It is characterized by accumulation of beta-amyloid plaques and tau neurofibrillary tangles with neuronal loss, leading to gradual memory impairment, executive and language dysfunction, and behavioral changes. Management is supportive, with cognitive symptom treatments, safety planning, and caregiver support.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Hemophilia is an inherited bleeding disorder of the hematologic/coagulation system, usually caused by deficiency of clotting factor VIII (hemophilia A) or IX (hemophilia B), most often in an X-linked pattern. It causes prolonged bleeding, easy bruising, hemarthroses, and deep muscle or postoperative hemorrhage, typically affecting males. Management centers on replacement of the missing factor, prevention of bleeding, and treatment of complications.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Prednisolone acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prednisolone acetate is an esterified glucocorticoid corticosteroid that acts as a prodrug of prednisolone, binding the glucocorticoid receptor to regulate gene transcription and suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It is established mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory for steroid-responsive ocular inflammation, including postoperative inflammation. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, and it is contraindicated in untreated ocular viral, fungal, or mycobacterial infections.
tail_desc: Viral eye infection is an infectious ocular disorder, most often involving the conjunctiva or cornea, caused by viruses such as adenovirus or herpes simplex. It typically presents with eye redness, tearing, irritation, photophobia, discharge, and sometimes blurred vision, affecting both children and adults. Management is usually supportive with hygiene measures, while confirmed herpetic disease may require antiviral therapy and ophthalmologic follow-up.

head: Sodium fluoride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluoride is an inorganic fluoride salt used as an anticaries agent and fluoride supplement. It acts topically and systemically by delivering fluoride ions that promote fluorapatite formation in tooth enamel and inhibit bacterial acid production. Established uses include prevention of dental caries in toothpaste, rinses, and supplements. Notable adverse effects include dental fluorosis with excessive exposure, especially during tooth development.
tail_desc: Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory granulomatous disease that most often affects the lungs and intrathoracic lymph nodes. It is characterized by noncaseating granuloma formation of unclear cause, likely involving immune dysregulation in genetically susceptible people. Common features include cough, dyspnea, fatigue, skin or eye involvement, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, typically in young to middle-aged adults; management ranges from observation to corticosteroids and other immunosuppressive therapy for significant organ disease.

head: Coenzyme M (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Coenzyme M is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Darifenacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Darifenacin is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a selective muscarinic M3 receptor antagonist. By blocking M3 receptors on bladder detrusor smooth muscle, it reduces involuntary contractions and increases bladder capacity. It is approved for treatment of overactive bladder with urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency. A notable adverse effect is constipation; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Inositol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Inositol is a naturally occurring cyclitol (six-carbon polyol), often used as a nutritional supplement rather than a conventional drug. It serves as a precursor in phosphatidylinositol and inositol phosphate signaling pathways, so it does not have a single primary molecular target. Clinically, it is established mainly for treatment of inborn errors such as acute intermittent porphyria; gastrointestinal upset is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Chlorprothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorprothixene is a typical antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class that primarily acts by antagonizing dopamine D2 receptors, with additional antihistaminic and anticholinergic effects. It has been used to treat schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, and sometimes severe agitation. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation; extrapyramidal symptoms can also occur.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Heart cancer usually refers to primary cardiac tumors, which are very rare and may be benign or malignant, or more commonly to metastatic spread to the heart from other cancers, affecting the cardiovascular system. Malignant cardiac tumors such as sarcomas can cause obstruction, arrhythmias, heart failure, embolic events, or pericardial effusion. Management depends on tumor type and extent, using surgical resection when feasible, plus oncology-directed systemic therapy or palliation.

head: Dipotassium phosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dipotassium phosphate is an inorganic potassium salt and phosphate replacement/electrolyte supplement. It dissociates to provide phosphate for intracellular metabolic processes and potassium for maintenance of cellular membrane potential, without a single specific molecular target. Clinically, it is used to prevent or treat hypophosphatemia, including in parenteral nutrition formulations. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in patients with severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypoaldosteronism is an endocrine disorder of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis causing deficient aldosterone action in the adrenal/mineralocorticoid system. It may result from reduced adrenal production or impaired renin release, and classically causes hyperkalemia, mild non-anion gap metabolic acidosis, and sometimes hypotension or salt wasting, especially in adrenal insufficiency or diabetic kidney disease. Management targets the cause, potassium control, and mineralocorticoid replacement when indicated.

head: Protriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Protriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzocycloheptadiene family. It acts primarily by inhibiting neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is used for treatment of major depressive disorder. A notable adverse effect is anticholinergic toxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Elagolix (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Elagolix is an orally active, nonpeptide gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) receptor antagonist that suppresses pituitary LH and FSH secretion, lowering ovarian estradiol production. It is approved for management of moderate to severe pain associated with endometriosis and, in combination regimens, heavy menstrual bleeding associated with uterine fibroids. A notable adverse effect is decreased bone mineral density; it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Loxapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Loxapine is a dibenzoxazepine tricyclic antipsychotic, generally classified as a typical antipsychotic, that acts mainly by antagonizing dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2 receptors. It is used to treat schizophrenia, and an inhaled formulation is approved for acute agitation in schizophrenia or bipolar I disorder. A notable risk is bronchospasm with inhaled loxapine, making it contraindicated in asthma or COPD.
tail_desc: Alzheimer disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system and the most common cause of dementia in older adults. It is characterized by accumulation of beta-amyloid plaques and tau neurofibrillary tangles with neuronal loss, leading to gradual memory impairment, executive and language dysfunction, and behavioral changes. Management is supportive, with cognitive symptom treatments, safety planning, and caregiver support.

head: Disopyramide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Disopyramide is a class IA antiarrhythmic, structurally related to quinidine, that primarily blocks fast cardiac voltage-gated sodium channels to slow conduction and prolong the action potential and refractory period. It is used for documented ventricular arrhythmias and certain supraventricular arrhythmias, and is also used in obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Notable adverse effects include strong anticholinergic effects and negative inotropy, making it contraindicated in overt heart failure.
tail_desc: Hypoglycemia is a metabolic/endocrine condition of abnormally low blood glucose, affecting systemic energy supply, especially the central nervous system. It commonly results from excess insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy, fasting, alcohol use, or critical illness. Symptoms include sweating, tremor, palpitations, confusion, seizures, or coma, particularly in people with diabetes. Management focuses on prompt glucose administration and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Teniposide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Teniposide is a semisynthetic podophyllotoxin derivative and epipodophyllotoxin antineoplastic agent. It inhibits DNA topoisomerase II, causing DNA strand breaks and cell-cycle arrest. Teniposide has established use, typically in combination chemotherapy, for refractory childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression, particularly leukopenia, and it should be used cautiously in patients with significant bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Lurasidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lurasidone is an atypical antipsychotic of the benzisothiazole class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at 5-HT7 and 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar depression. A notable adverse effect is akathisia; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Tropicamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tropicamide is a short-acting antimuscarinic mydriatic and cycloplegic ophthalmic agent, structurally a synthetic parasympatholytic. It competitively antagonizes muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, primarily M4 and other ocular muscarinic receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle, producing pupil dilation and cycloplegia. It is used for diagnostic mydriasis and cycloplegic refraction; notable risks include increased intraocular pressure and precipitation of angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Thyrotoxicosis is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid system caused by excess circulating thyroid hormones, most commonly from hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease, toxic nodular goiter, or thyroiditis. It typically presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, especially in adults. Management targets the cause and symptom control, using beta-blockers and measures such as antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Prednisolone acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prednisolone acetate is an esterified glucocorticoid corticosteroid that acts as a prodrug of prednisolone, binding the glucocorticoid receptor to regulate gene transcription and suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It is established mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory for steroid-responsive ocular inflammation, including postoperative inflammation. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, and it is contraindicated in untreated ocular viral, fungal, or mycobacterial infections.
tail_desc: Ocular tuberculosis is an extrapulmonary Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection or immune-mediated ocular inflammatory disorder affecting structures such as the uvea, retina, choroid, sclera, or optic nerve. It often presents with chronic or recurrent uveitis, choroiditis, retinal vasculitis, or granulomatous inflammation, especially in patients from TB-endemic settings or with systemic tuberculosis. Management typically involves standard multidrug antituberculous therapy, often with adjunctive corticosteroids for sight-threatening inflammation.

head: Ceftriaxone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections including pneumonia, meningitis, gonorrhea, sepsis, urinary tract and intra-abdominal infections. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions in beta-lactam–allergic patients and biliary sludging/pseudolithiasis.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Trimethadione (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethadione is an oxazolidinedione anticonvulsant used primarily for refractory absence (petit mal) seizures. Its active metabolite, dimethadione, reduces thalamic low-threshold calcium currents by blocking T-type calcium channels, suppressing the cortical rhythm underlying absence seizures. A notable adverse effect is severe teratogenicity, so it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Proteinuria is a renal/urinary abnormality characterized by excess protein, mainly albumin, in the urine due to increased glomerular permeability, reduced tubular reabsorption, or overflow from excess circulating proteins. It may be asymptomatic or associated with foamy urine, edema, and kidney disease, occurring in adults and children. Management targets the underlying cause and often includes blood pressure control and reduction of protein excretion.

head: Meticillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meticillin (methicillin) is a penicillinase-resistant beta-lactam antibiotic of the penicillin class. It inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It was used for infections caused by penicillinase-producing staphylococci, especially methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including serious allergic reactions.
tail_desc: Infectious mononucleosis is an acute viral infection of the lymphoreticular system, most commonly caused by Epstein–Barr virus. It typically affects adolescents and young adults and presents with fever, pharyngitis, cervical lymphadenopathy, fatigue, and sometimes hepatosplenomegaly. Management is mainly supportive, including rest, hydration, and analgesics, with avoidance of contact sports when splenomegaly is present.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Succinic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (a disease)
head_desc: Succinic acid is a naturally occurring dicarboxylic acid and intermediate of the tricarboxylic acid cycle rather than a standard therapeutic drug class. It participates in cellular energy metabolism, but no specific approved pharmacologic molecular target or mechanism is established for succinic acid as a medicinal agent. It has no well-established approved clinical indication as a standalone drug, and concentrated exposure may cause gastrointestinal irritation.
tail_desc: Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin is a benign inherited hemoglobinopathy of the hematologic system caused by genetic variants that sustain gamma-globin expression and continued HbF production after birth. Affected individuals, often identified incidentally, are usually asymptomatic with normal or mild microcytic blood findings; the condition can lessen severity of sickle cell disease or beta-thalassemia. Management is typically reassurance, diagnosis confirmation, and genetic counseling.

head: Capecitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Capecitabine is an oral fluoropyrimidine antimetabolite and prodrug of 5-fluorouracil that is converted in tumors and tissues to metabolites inhibiting thymidylate synthase and incorporating into RNA and DNA, thereby impairing nucleic acid synthesis. It is used for colorectal and breast cancers, including metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is hand-foot syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with severe dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Vardenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vardenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzenesulfonamide derivative, that selectively blocks PDE5 to increase cyclic GMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation in the corpus cavernosum. It is approved for erectile dysfunction. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nitrates, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Armodafinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Armodafinil is the R-enantiomer of modafinil, a wake-promoting central nervous system agent. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it is known to inhibit the dopamine transporter (DAT), increasing extracellular dopamine. It is approved to improve wakefulness in narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift work disorder. Notable adverse effects include headache and rare serious rash, including Stevens–Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Omeprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Omeprazole is a benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric parietal cell H+/K+-ATPase, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, peptic ulcer disease, erosive esophagitis, and Helicobacter pylori eradication regimens, and for hypersecretory states such as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea.
tail_desc: Osteoporosis is a metabolic skeletal disease of the musculoskeletal system characterized by low bone mass and deterioration of bone microarchitecture, causing increased bone fragility and fracture risk. It commonly affects older adults, especially postmenopausal women, and may be asymptomatic until vertebral, hip, or wrist fractures occur. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, adequate calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Duloxetine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Duloxetine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is approved for major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, and chronic musculoskeletal pain. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity; concomitant use with monoamine oxidase inhibitors is contraindicated.
tail_desc: Angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic disease in which the peripheral iris obstructs the anterior chamber angle, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid or intermittent rise in intraocular pressure. It typically affects anatomically predisposed older adults, especially those with shallow anterior chambers. Features include severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos, nausea, and a red eye; management is urgent pressure lowering followed by laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Desloratadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desloratadine is a second-generation tricyclic antihistamine and the active metabolite of loratadine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing allergic symptoms. It is used for seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, although it is generally less sedating than first-generation antihistamines; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to desloratadine or loratadine.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Alprazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alprazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine anxiolytic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is approved for the treatment of anxiety disorders and panic disorder. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; concomitant use with opioids can cause profound respiratory depression.
tail_desc: Substance-related disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting the brain and behavior, characterized by maladaptive use of alcohol, drugs, or medications that leads to intoxication, dependence, withdrawal, and impaired control despite harm. It can occur across all age groups, often with craving, social or occupational dysfunction, and medical complications. Management typically combines screening, psychosocial treatment, relapse prevention, and, for some substances, medication-assisted therapy and supervised withdrawal care.

head: Estazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Estazolam is a triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling or staying asleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day sedation, and it is contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Ibandronate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ibandronate is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds bone hydroxyapatite and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, reducing bone resorption. It is approved for prevention and treatment of postmenopausal osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis with oral formulations; it is contraindicated in patients with esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying or inability to remain upright.
tail_desc: Gastroesophageal reflux disease is a chronic upper gastrointestinal disorder in which reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus, usually from lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction, causes troublesome symptoms or mucosal injury. Typical features are heartburn and acid regurgitation, sometimes chest pain, cough, or dysphagia, affecting adults and children. Management emphasizes weight loss, trigger avoidance, acid suppression with proton pump inhibitors, and selected antireflux procedures.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Thiopental (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiopental is an ultra-short-acting barbiturate intravenous anesthetic. It enhances inhibitory neurotransmission primarily by positively modulating GABA-A receptors, increasing chloride channel opening and causing rapid CNS depression. It is used for induction of general anesthesia and has established use in seizure control and reduction of intracranial pressure. A notable risk is profound respiratory and cardiovascular depression; it is contraindicated in acute porphyria.
tail_desc: Addison disease is primary adrenal insufficiency, an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex causing deficient cortisol and often aldosterone production, most commonly from autoimmune adrenal destruction. Patients may develop chronic fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia, with risk of acute adrenal crisis. Management is lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, often mineralocorticoid replacement, and stress-dose therapy during illness or surgery.

head: Magnesium chloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium chloride is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an electrolyte replacement and mineral supplement. It dissociates to provide magnesium, an essential cofactor for many enzymes and a modulator of neuromuscular transmission, membrane stability, and cardiac conduction, without a single specific receptor target. It is used to prevent or treat magnesium deficiency; adverse effects can include diarrhea, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment because of hypermagnesemia risk.
tail_desc: Phenylketonuria is an autosomal recessive inborn error of amino acid metabolism affecting the nervous system, caused most often by phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency, leading to toxic phenylalanine accumulation. It typically presents in infancy or is detected by newborn screening; untreated patients develop intellectual disability, seizures, developmental delay, eczema, and hypopigmentation. Management is early lifelong dietary phenylalanine restriction, tyrosine supplementation, and selected use of sapropterin.

head: Ibandronate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ibandronate is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds bone hydroxyapatite and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, reducing bone resorption. It is approved for prevention and treatment of postmenopausal osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis with oral formulations; it is contraindicated in patients with esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying or inability to remain upright.
tail_desc: Achalasia is a primary esophageal motility disorder of the gastrointestinal system caused by degeneration of inhibitory neurons in the myenteric plexus, leading to impaired lower esophageal sphincter relaxation and absent peristalsis. It typically presents in adults with progressive dysphagia to solids and liquids, regurgitation, chest pain, and weight loss. Management aims to reduce sphincter outflow obstruction with pneumatic dilation, myotomy, or botulinum toxin in selected patients.

head: Chlorprothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorprothixene is a typical antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class that primarily acts by antagonizing dopamine D2 receptors, with additional antihistaminic and anticholinergic effects. It has been used to treat schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, and sometimes severe agitation. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation; extrapyramidal symptoms can also occur.
tail_desc: Cyclic hematopoiesis is a rare disorder of the hematopoietic system characterized by periodic oscillations in blood cell production, classically causing recurrent neutropenia. It is often linked to inherited defects affecting granulopoiesis regulation, especially ELANE mutations in cyclic neutropenia. Patients, often presenting in infancy or childhood, have recurrent fevers, oral ulcers, and infections; management is mainly supportive, with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor commonly used.

head: Cobalamin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cobalamin (vitamin B12) is a water-soluble cobalt-containing corrinoid vitamin used as replacement therapy. It acts as a cofactor for methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, restoring DNA synthesis and neurologic function in deficiency states. It is indicated for vitamin B12 deficiency, including pernicious anemia. Hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known cobalt or cobalamin hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Chronic atrophic gastritis is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the gastric mucosa in the digestive system, characterized by loss of gastric glands with mucosal atrophy, often due to Helicobacter pylori infection or autoimmune destruction of parietal cells. Patients may be asymptomatic or have dyspepsia, anemia, or vitamin B12 deficiency; management targets the cause, corrects deficiencies, and includes surveillance in selected patients.

head: Dorzolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dorzolamide is a topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, a sulfonamide derivative, used primarily in ophthalmology. It inhibits carbonic anhydrase II in the ciliary processes, reducing aqueous humor formation and lowering intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is local ocular burning or stinging, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to sulfonamides.
tail_desc: Anaphylaxis is an acute, life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction affecting multiple organ systems, especially respiratory and cardiovascular. It is usually triggered by IgE-mediated mast-cell and basophil degranulation after exposure to foods, drugs, insect stings, or other allergens. Typical features include urticaria, angioedema, wheeze, hypotension, and gastrointestinal symptoms in sensitized individuals. Management requires immediate intramuscular epinephrine, airway and circulatory support, trigger avoidance, and specialist follow-up.

head: Leucine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid used as a nutritional ingredient and in amino acid supplementation. It activates nutrient-sensing pathways involved in protein synthesis, notably mTORC1, but is not typically classified as a conventional targeted drug. Clinically, it is used in medical nutrition to prevent or treat amino acid deficiency; excessive intake may worsen branched-chain ketoacid metabolism disorders such as maple syrup urine disease.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Nortriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nortriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the secondary amine dibenzocycloheptene class that primarily inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and, to a lesser extent, serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters. It is used for major depressive disorder and is also established for certain neuropathic pain conditions. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic effects such as dry mouth, constipation, and sedation.
tail_desc: Cerebral infarction is an ischemic stroke of the central nervous system in which interrupted cerebral blood flow, usually from arterial thrombosis or embolism, causes focal brain tissue necrosis. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or visual loss, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible and secondary stroke prevention.

head: Oxyphencyclimine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxyphencyclimine is a synthetic antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent, structurally a tertiary amine used as an antispasmodic. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal smooth-muscle tone, motility, and secretions. It has been used in peptic ulcer disease and other gastrointestinal hypermotility or spastic disorders. Typical adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, and urinary retention.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Fluvoxamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrogenic syndrome of inappropriate antidiuresis (a disease)
head_desc: Fluvoxamine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, a 2-aminoethyl oxime ether derivative, that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for obsessive-compulsive disorder and, in some regions, major depressive disorder and social anxiety disorder. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with monoamine oxidase inhibitors, which are contraindicated.
tail_desc: Nephrogenic syndrome of inappropriate antidiuresis is a rare inherited renal water-balance disorder characterized by inappropriate antidiuresis despite low or undetectable vasopressin, usually due to activating AVPR2 mutations in the collecting duct. Affected patients, often male infants or children, develop euvolemic hyponatremia with low serum osmolality, inappropriately concentrated urine, seizures or irritability. Management centers on fluid restriction and measures to prevent recurrent hyponatremia.

head: Felodipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is established for the treatment of hypertension and, in some settings, chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; it is generally contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug.
tail_desc: Coronary atherosclerosis is a cardiovascular disease in which atherosclerotic plaques of lipids, inflammatory cells, and fibrous tissue narrow or obstruct the coronary arteries, reducing myocardial blood flow. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults and may cause angina, dyspnea, acute coronary syndromes, or remain asymptomatic. Management focuses on risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Telmisartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telmisartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl benzimidazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and to reduce cardiovascular risk in selected high-risk patients. Notable risks include hyperkalemia and renal impairment, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hepatic encephalopathy is a neuropsychiatric complication of acute or chronic liver failure affecting the central nervous system. It results from impaired hepatic detoxification and portosystemic shunting, leading to accumulation of neurotoxins, especially ammonia. Patients, often with cirrhosis, develop confusion, altered consciousness, asterixis, and sometimes coma; management targets precipitating factors and lowers gut-derived ammonia, commonly with lactulose and rifaximin.

head: Pralatrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pralatrexate is a folate analogue antimetabolite antifolate chemotherapy agent that selectively enters cells via the reduced folate carrier and inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, disrupting DNA synthesis. It is approved for relapsed or refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma. A notable toxicity is severe mucositis; folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation are recommended to reduce hematologic and mucosal adverse effects.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Cholic Acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cholic acid is a primary bile acid used as a bile acid replacement therapy. It acts by restoring the endogenous bile acid pool and activating the farnesoid X receptor (FXR), which suppresses bile acid synthesis. It is indicated for bile acid synthesis disorders due to single-enzyme defects and as adjunctive treatment of peroxisomal disorders including Zellweger spectrum disorders. Diarrhea is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Benign recurrent intrahepatic cholestasis is a rare inherited hepatobiliary disorder causing intermittent episodes of intrahepatic cholestasis, usually from childhood or adolescence, without progressive liver damage between attacks. It is linked to defects in hepatocellular bile secretion, classically ATP8B1 or ABCB11 variants, and presents with recurrent jaundice, intense pruritus, and elevated bile acids. Management is mainly supportive and symptom directed, including antipruritic therapy and nutritional monitoring.

head: Misoprostol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog that acts primarily as an agonist at prostaglandin EP receptors, reducing gastric acid secretion and increasing uterine tone and cervical softening. It is used for prevention of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers and in obstetrics/gynecology for medical abortion, cervical ripening, labor induction, and postpartum hemorrhage. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in pregnancy when used for ulcer prevention.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Tiludronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
