head_desc: Tiludronic acid is a non-nitrogen bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, likely via disruption of osteoclast energy metabolism. It is used for Paget disease of bone and, in veterinary medicine, for navicular syndrome in horses. A notable adverse effect is upper gastrointestinal irritation; esophageal abnormalities are a contraindication to oral use.
tail_desc: Osteomalacia is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system characterized by defective mineralization of mature bone, most often due to vitamin D deficiency, phosphate depletion, or impaired vitamin D metabolism. It causes diffuse bone pain, muscle weakness, fragility fractures, and difficulty walking, especially in adults with malnutrition, malabsorption, kidney disease, or limited sun exposure. Management targets the cause, typically vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate replacement.

head: Polyvinyl alcohol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Polyvinyl alcohol is a synthetic, water-soluble polymer used as an ophthalmic lubricant and artificial tear excipient. It acts physically rather than via a specific molecular target, stabilizing the tear film and reducing ocular surface friction. Established uses include temporary relief of dry eye irritation and ocular discomfort. Mild transient blurred vision or local eye irritation can occur after instillation.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Mitomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mitomycin is an antineoplastic antibiotic of the mitomycin/aziridine alkylating-agent class that is enzymatically activated to form DNA cross-links, inhibiting DNA synthesis and replication without a single specific molecular target. It is used in several cancers, including gastric and pancreatic adenocarcinomas, and intravesically for superficial bladder cancer. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; hemolytic-uremic syndrome can also occur.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Tetrabenazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetrabenazine is a benzoquinolizine derivative and vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) inhibitor that depletes presynaptic dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine stores. It is approved to treat chorea associated with Huntington disease and is also used for hyperkinetic movement disorders such as tardive dyskinesia. Notable risks include depression and suicidality, and it is contraindicated in actively suicidal patients or those with untreated depression.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Lorazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic and sedative-hypnotic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA\(_A\) receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders, short-term relief of anxiety symptoms, status epilepticus, preanesthetic sedation, and insomnia in selected settings. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation and respiratory depression, especially with other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Flumethasone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flumethasone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts by binding the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It has been used mainly in topical dermatologic and otic/ophthalmic preparations for inflammatory conditions. Like other corticosteroids, prolonged local use can cause skin atrophy, and it is generally contraindicated in untreated infections at the application site.
tail_desc: Pituitary deficiency is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis in which reduced secretion of one or more pituitary hormones causes secondary failure of target glands. It may result from congenital defects, tumors, surgery, radiation, trauma, or infarction. Features vary by hormone loss and age, including growth failure, delayed puberty, infertility, fatigue, and adrenal or thyroid insufficiency. Management focuses on treating the cause and replacing deficient hormones.

head: Ticarcillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Ticarcillin is a semisynthetic carboxypenicillin, a beta-lactam antipseudomonal antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing cell lysis. It has been used for susceptible gram-negative infections, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, often in combination with clavulanate. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Molindone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Molindone is a typical antipsychotic of the dihydroindolone class used for the treatment of schizophrenia. Its antipsychotic effect is primarily attributed to central dopamine D2 receptor antagonism. Like other first-generation antipsychotics, it can cause extrapyramidal symptoms; tardive dyskinesia is a notable serious adverse effect with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Acquired hyperprolactinemia is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis characterized by elevated serum prolactin arising after birth, most often from pituitary adenomas, dopamine-antagonist drugs, hypothyroidism, or stalk disruption. It commonly causes galactorrhea, menstrual disturbance, infertility, and hypogonadism in women, and decreased libido or erectile dysfunction in men. Management targets the cause, with dopamine agonists or treatment of underlying disease.

head: Aminoglutethimide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminoglutethimide is a nonsteroidal aromatase inhibitor and adrenal steroidogenesis inhibitor. It blocks conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone and inhibits aromatase (CYP19), reducing synthesis of glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, estrogens, and androgens. It has been used for Cushing syndrome and advanced breast cancer. Notable adverse effects include sedation and rash; adrenal insufficiency is an important risk.
tail_desc: Infectious disease is a broad category of disorders caused by pathogenic microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, or prions, affecting virtually any organ system. It results from microbial invasion and host inflammatory response, with manifestations ranging from fever and malaise to organ-specific symptoms. It can occur in any population, especially the very young, elderly, or immunocompromised, and is managed with pathogen-directed therapy, supportive care, and prevention measures such as hygiene, vaccination, and isolation.

head: Dobutamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dobutamine is a synthetic catecholamine inotropic agent that primarily stimulates cardiac beta-1 adrenergic receptors, increasing myocardial contractility and stroke volume with less effect on heart rate than isoproterenol. It is used for short-term treatment of cardiac decompensation due to heart failure or cardiac surgery and in pharmacologic stress testing. A notable adverse effect is tachyarrhythmia.
tail_desc: Familial hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is an inherited cardiac muscle disorder characterized by unexplained left ventricular hypertrophy, usually caused by autosomal dominant mutations in sarcomere protein genes. It may present in adolescents or adults with dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or sudden cardiac death. Management focuses on risk stratification, symptom control with beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers, lifestyle guidance, and selected use of septal reduction or implantable defibrillators.

head: Propylthiouracil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Propylthiouracil is a thiourea antithyroid drug (thionamide) that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking iodination and coupling steps in thyroid hormone synthesis, and also inhibits peripheral conversion of thyroxine (T4) to triiodothyronine (T3). It is used to treat hyperthyroidism, including Graves disease and thyroid storm. A notable serious adverse effect is severe hepatotoxicity; agranulocytosis can also occur.
tail_desc: Constitutional neutropenia is a benign, usually lifelong form of chronic neutropenia affecting the hematologic/immune system, characterized by persistently low absolute neutrophil counts without an acquired cause or major marrow pathology. It is often seen in otherwise healthy individuals, including some ethnic groups, and typically causes no or few infections. Management is mainly reassurance, exclusion of secondary causes, and clinical monitoring rather than routine treatment.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Pimecrolimus (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pimecrolimus is a topical calcineurin inhibitor of the ascomycin macrolactam class. It binds FKBP-12, inhibiting calcineurin phosphatase and thereby reducing T-cell activation and inflammatory cytokine production. It is approved for mild to moderate atopic dermatitis (eczema), particularly in non-immunocompromised patients aged 2 years and older. A notable adverse effect is local burning or irritation at the application site.
tail_desc: Herpes zoster is a viral infection of the skin and peripheral nervous system caused by reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus in sensory ganglia. It typically affects older adults and immunocompromised patients, producing a painful, unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash and sometimes postherpetic neuralgia. Management centers on prompt antiviral therapy, analgesia, and prevention through zoster vaccination.

head: Foscarnet (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Foscarnet is a pyrophosphate analog antiviral that directly inhibits viral DNA polymerase and HIV reverse transcriptase by blocking the pyrophosphate-binding site, without requiring activation by viral kinases. It is used for cytomegalovirus retinitis and for acyclovir-resistant mucocutaneous herpes simplex infections, especially in immunocompromised patients. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity, often with electrolyte disturbances such as hypocalcemia.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Polythiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Polythiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the sodium-chloride cotransporter (NCC/SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used to treat hypertension and edema, including edema associated with heart failure. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; thiazides are also contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Eptifibatide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Eptifibatide is an intravenous cyclic heptapeptide glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor that reversibly blocks the platelet integrin αIIbβ3 receptor, preventing fibrinogen-mediated platelet aggregation. It is used in acute coronary syndromes and during percutaneous coronary intervention to reduce ischemic complications. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it is contraindicated in active internal bleeding or severe thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Estramustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Estramustine is an antineoplastic estradiol–nitrogen mustard conjugate used mainly for palliative treatment of metastatic or progressive prostate cancer. It binds microtubule-associated proteins and tubulin, disrupting microtubule assembly and mitosis, with additional estrogenic effects. A notable adverse effect is thromboembolism; it is generally contraindicated in patients with active thrombophlebitis or thromboembolic disorders.
tail_desc: Congestive heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment reduces the heart’s ability to pump or fill effectively, causing pulmonary and/or systemic fluid congestion. It commonly affects older adults and patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy, producing dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance. Management focuses on treating the cause, diuresis, guideline-directed heart-failure medications, sodium/fluid control, and selected device or advanced therapies.

head: Hydroxychloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxychloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in lysosomes, raises endosomal pH, and inhibits toll-like receptor signaling. It is approved for malaria and widely used for systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis. A notable adverse effect is retinal toxicity with prolonged use, requiring ophthalmologic monitoring.
tail_desc: Retinal disease refers to a broad group of ophthalmic disorders affecting the retina, the light-sensitive neural tissue of the eye. Causes include vascular, degenerative, inflammatory, inherited, or traumatic damage, leading to photoreceptor or retinal pigment epithelium dysfunction. Common features include blurred vision, scotomas, metamorphopsia, night blindness, or vision loss. Management depends on cause and may involve risk-factor control, laser therapy, intravitreal drugs, or surgery.

head: Vitamin A (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vitamin A comprises fat-soluble retinoids, including retinol and retinyl esters. It is converted to active retinoic acid, which binds nuclear retinoic acid receptors (RARs) and retinoid X receptors (RXRs) to regulate gene transcription involved in vision, epithelial differentiation, growth, and immune function. It is used to prevent and treat vitamin A deficiency; excessive doses can cause hepatotoxicity and teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Hemosiderosis is an iron-overload condition affecting reticuloendothelial and parenchymal tissues, especially the lungs, liver, and spleen, characterized by excessive deposition of hemosiderin without the tissue injury typical of hemochromatosis. It may result from recurrent hemorrhage, chronic transfusions, or hemolysis. Clinical features depend on the involved organ, and management focuses on treating the cause and reducing iron burden when appropriate.

head: Methohexital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methohexital is an ultrashort-acting barbiturate intravenous anesthetic, a methylated oxybarbiturate, that enhances inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission primarily by positively modulating GABA_A receptors in the CNS. It is used for induction of general anesthesia and procedural sedation, including for electroconvulsive therapy. A notable risk is respiratory depression; it is contraindicated in patients with acute intermittent porphyria.
tail_desc: Morbid obesity is a severe form of obesity, a chronic metabolic and endocrine-related disorder affecting the whole body, defined by excessive adipose tissue accumulation, commonly with body mass index at least 40 kg/m² or at least 35 with serious comorbidity. It causes marked functional limitation and increases risks of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, sleep apnea, and osteoarthritis; management centers on intensive lifestyle therapy, obesity pharmacotherapy, and often bariatric surgery.

head: Clofedanol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clofedanol is a centrally acting non-opioid antitussive (cough suppressant), structurally classified as a diphenylmethane derivative. Its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well established, but it suppresses the cough reflex via central action. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of nonproductive cough. Notable adverse effects include drowsiness and dizziness.
tail_desc: Acidosis disorder is a metabolic or respiratory acid-base disturbance affecting systemic physiology, characterized by excess hydrogen ion accumulation or reduced bicarbonate, leading to abnormally low blood pH. Causes include impaired ventilation, renal dysfunction, ketoacidosis, lactic acidosis, or toxin exposure. Patients may develop rapid breathing, confusion, fatigue, and cardiovascular instability. Management focuses on urgent identification and treatment of the underlying cause, with supportive correction of acid-base imbalance.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenic purpura is a hematologic bleeding disorder characterized by thrombocytopenia, often due to immune-mediated platelet destruction (as in immune thrombocytopenic purpura) or, less commonly, platelet consumption in microangiopathic disease. It causes petechiae, purpura, easy bruising, epistaxis, and mucosal bleeding, affecting both children and adults. Management targets the cause and bleeding risk, using observation, corticosteroids, IVIG, or other cause-specific therapy.

head: Flucytosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flucytosine is a fluorinated pyrimidine antimetabolite antifungal. In susceptible fungi it is taken up by cytosine permease and converted to 5-fluorouracil, which inhibits thymidylate synthase and is incorporated into RNA, disrupting DNA and RNA synthesis. It is used mainly, usually with amphotericin B, for serious Candida and Cryptococcus infections such as cryptococcal meningitis. Bone marrow suppression is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: congenital neutropenia-myelofibrosis-nephromegaly syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Lurasidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lurasidone is an atypical antipsychotic of the benzisothiazole class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at 5-HT7 and 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar depression. A notable adverse effect is akathisia; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Inositol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Inositol is a naturally occurring cyclitol (six-carbon polyol), often used as a nutritional supplement rather than a conventional drug. It serves as a precursor in phosphatidylinositol and inositol phosphate signaling pathways, so it does not have a single primary molecular target. Clinically, it is established mainly for treatment of inborn errors such as acute intermittent porphyria; gastrointestinal upset is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Enoxaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin (factor IIa). It is used to prevent and treat venous thromboembolism and in acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Biliary tract disease comprises disorders of the hepatobiliary system affecting the bile ducts and gallbladder, commonly caused by gallstones, inflammation, infection, obstruction, or malignancy that impair bile flow. Typical features include right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, fever, pruritus, and abnormal liver tests, often in adults. Management depends on cause and may include supportive care, antibiotics, endoscopic biliary drainage, or surgery.

head: Prazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic, structurally a 1,4-benzodiazepine derivative, used mainly for the short-term treatment of anxiety disorders. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing GABA-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated in myasthenia gravis and severe respiratory insufficiency.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Capecitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Capecitabine is an oral fluoropyrimidine antimetabolite and prodrug of 5-fluorouracil that is converted in tumors and tissues to metabolites inhibiting thymidylate synthase and incorporating into RNA and DNA, thereby impairing nucleic acid synthesis. It is used for colorectal and breast cancers, including metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is hand-foot syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with severe dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Labetalol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Labetalol is a mixed adrenergic antagonist of the amino alcohol class that blocks β1-, β2-, and α1-adrenergic receptors, lowering heart rate and peripheral vascular resistance. It is used for hypertension, including severe hypertension and hypertension in pregnancy. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma, overt heart failure, and greater-than-first-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Osteitis fibrosa is a metabolic bone disorder of the skeleton, usually caused by hyperparathyroidism with excess parathyroid hormone driving increased osteoclastic bone resorption and replacement by fibrous tissue. It can cause bone pain, fragility fractures, deformity, and “brown tumors,” classically in patients with severe primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism. Management targets the underlying hyperparathyroidism and correction of calcium-phosphate abnormalities.

head: Chenodeoxycholic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chenodeoxycholic acid is a primary bile acid used as a bile acid replacement and litholytic agent. It reduces hepatic cholesterol secretion and cholesterol saturation of bile, promoting dissolution of radiolucent cholesterol gallstones, and also suppresses bile acid synthesis via FXR-mediated feedback. Established uses include selected cholesterol gallstones and cerebrotendinous xanthomatosis. Notable adverse effects include diarrhea and hepatotoxicity; it is contraindicated in complete biliary obstruction.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Neostigmine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Neostigmine is a reversible carbamate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (indirect cholinergic agonist) that inhibits acetylcholinesterase, increasing acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions and parasympathetic synapses. It is used to treat myasthenia gravis and postoperative urinary retention or ileus, and to reverse nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and other muscarinic cholinergic effects; it is contraindicated in mechanical gastrointestinal or urinary obstruction.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Granisetron (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Granisetron is a selective 5-hydroxytryptamine type 3 (5-HT3) receptor antagonist antiemetic, chemically an indazole derivative. It blocks serotonin at peripheral vagal afferents and central 5-HT3 receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone, preventing nausea and vomiting. It is used for chemotherapy-, radiotherapy-, and postoperative-induced nausea and vomiting. A notable adverse effect is QT interval prolongation.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Desmopressin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desmopressin is a synthetic vasopressin analogue with selective agonist activity at V2 vasopressin receptors, increasing renal collecting-duct water reabsorption and promoting endothelial release of factor VIII and von Willebrand factor. It is used for central diabetes insipidus, primary nocturnal enuresis/nocturia, and certain bleeding disorders including mild hemophilia A and type 1 von Willebrand disease. A notable risk is hyponatremia due to water intoxication.
tail_desc: The common cold is an acute, self-limited viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, primarily affecting the nose and throat. It is caused by numerous respiratory viruses, especially rhinoviruses, producing mucosal inflammation. Typical features include nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, sore throat, cough, and malaise in children and adults. Management is supportive, with rest, fluids, and symptom relief rather than antibiotics.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Bimatoprost (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bimatoprost is a synthetic prostaglandin F2α analog (prostamide) used primarily as an ophthalmic antiglaucoma agent. It lowers intraocular pressure by increasing aqueous humor outflow, mainly through the uveoscleral pathway, via prostamide/prostaglandin receptor-mediated effects. It is approved for open-angle glaucoma, ocular hypertension, and eyelash hypotrichosis. A notable adverse effect is conjunctival hyperemia, and it may also cause increased iris pigmentation.
tail_desc: Ciliary body disease refers to disorders affecting the ciliary body of the eye’s uveal tract, within the visual system. It may result from inflammation, neoplastic involvement, cysts, trauma, or degenerative change, disrupting aqueous humor production and accommodation. Clinical features can include ocular pain, redness, blurred vision, altered intraocular pressure, and visual disturbance. Management depends on cause and typically involves ophthalmologic evaluation, targeted medical therapy, and sometimes surgery.

head: Risedronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Risedronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate (pyridinyl bisphosphonate) that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget’s disease of bone, and prevention or treatment of glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophageal irritation or ulceration, and it is contraindicated in hypocalcemia and significant esophageal motility disorders.
tail_desc: Achalasia is a primary esophageal motility disorder of the gastrointestinal system caused by degeneration of inhibitory neurons in the myenteric plexus, leading to impaired lower esophageal sphincter relaxation and absent peristalsis. It typically presents in adults with progressive dysphagia to solids and liquids, regurgitation, chest pain, and weight loss. Management aims to reduce sphincter outflow obstruction with pneumatic dilation, myotomy, or botulinum toxin in selected patients.

head: Dutasteride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dutasteride is a synthetic 4-azasteroid, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor that blocks both type 1 and type 2 5-alpha-reductase (SRD5A1/SRD5A2), reducing conversion of testosterone to dihydrotestosterone. It is approved for benign prostatic hyperplasia, alone or with tamsulosin. Notable adverse effects include decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and reduced ejaculate volume; it is contraindicated in pregnancy exposure.
tail_desc: Orthostatic hypotension is a cardiovascular autonomic disorder marked by an abnormal fall in blood pressure on standing due to impaired baroreflex-mediated vasoconstriction, volume depletion, medications, or autonomic failure. It commonly affects older adults and patients with neurodegenerative or diabetic autonomic neuropathy, causing dizziness, lightheadedness, blurred vision, weakness, or syncope. Management targets underlying causes, medication review, fluid and salt optimization, compression garments, and selected pressor agents.

head: Adenosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Adenosine is an endogenous purine nucleoside antiarrhythmic that activates cardiac A1 adenosine receptors, increasing potassium conductance and suppressing cAMP-dependent calcium currents to transiently slow sinoatrial and atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used acutely to terminate paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia and in pharmacologic cardiac stress testing. Notable adverse effects include brief flushing, chest discomfort, and bronchospasm; it is contraindicated in second- or third-degree AV block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Aortic valve disease is a cardiac valvular disorder affecting the aortic valve, most commonly causing stenosis or regurgitation from age-related calcific degeneration, congenital bicuspid valve, or rheumatic damage. Patients, often older adults, may develop exertional dyspnea, angina, syncope, or heart failure. Management includes echocardiographic surveillance, medical treatment of complications, and valve repair or replacement when disease becomes severe or symptomatic.

head: Zolmitriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zolmitriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, an indole/tryptamine derivative that acts as a selective 5-HT1B/5-HT1D serotonin receptor agonist, producing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for the acute treatment of migraine attacks, with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease because triptans can cause chest tightness and coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Peripheral arterial disease is an atherosclerotic vascular disorder of the peripheral arteries, most often affecting the lower limbs. Arterial narrowing reduces blood flow, causing exertional leg pain (intermittent claudication), diminished pulses, and in advanced cases rest pain, ulcers, or tissue loss. It is common in older adults and smokers. Management focuses on risk-factor modification, antiplatelet and statin therapy, supervised exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Temazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Temazepam is a benzodiazepine hypnotic used for the short-term treatment of insomnia. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride influx and neuronal inhibition. Common adverse effects include sedation, dizziness, and impaired psychomotor performance; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Adefovir dipivoxil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Adefovir dipivoxil is an oral prodrug of adefovir, an acyclic nucleotide analogue of adenosine monophosphate and antiviral agent. After conversion to adefovir diphosphate, it inhibits hepatitis B virus DNA polymerase (reverse transcriptase) and terminates viral DNA chain elongation. It is approved for chronic hepatitis B, and a notable risk is dose-related nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Citalopram (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Citalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, a bicyclic phthalane derivative, that inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder and is also used for panic disorder in some jurisdictions. A notable safety concern is dose-dependent QT interval prolongation, making it contraindicated with pimozide and requiring caution in patients with congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Glutamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glutamic acid is a nonessential amino acid and acidic amino acid used mainly as a nutritional or electrolyte replacement component rather than a receptor-targeted drug. It serves as a metabolic substrate and precursor in amino acid and neurotransmitter pathways, without a well-defined primary pharmacologic target in approved therapy. Clinical use is limited to nutritional supplementation; gastrointestinal irritation may occur, and caution is warranted in severe renal or hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Digitalis poisoning is a toxic cardiovascular condition caused by excessive exposure to cardiac glycosides such as digoxin, producing inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase and resulting electrolyte-mediated conduction abnormalities. It typically affects patients receiving digoxin, especially older adults or those with renal impairment, and presents with nausea, vomiting, confusion, visual disturbances, bradyarrhythmias, or ventricular arrhythmias. Management centers on stopping the drug, correcting electrolytes, monitoring rhythm, and using digoxin-specific antibody fragments in severe cases.

head: Methocarbamol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methocarbamol is a centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant, chemically a carbamate derivative of guaifenesin. Its precise molecular target is not established; it is thought to produce muscle relaxation via generalized CNS depression rather than direct action on skeletal muscle. It is used to relieve acute painful musculoskeletal spasm and as adjunctive treatment for tetanus. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Amcinonide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amcinonide is a high-potency synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid used topically for corticosteroid-responsive inflammatory dermatoses, such as eczema and psoriasis. It acts as a glucocorticoid receptor agonist, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators, vasodilation, and pruritus. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy with prolonged use; it is generally avoided on untreated cutaneous infections due to local immunosuppressive effects.
tail_desc: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is an autoimmune endocrine disorder of the pancreas in which immune-mediated destruction of insulin-producing beta cells causes absolute insulin deficiency and chronic hyperglycemia. It often begins in childhood or adolescence, though it can occur at any age, and commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and sometimes diabetic ketoacidosis. Management requires lifelong insulin replacement with glucose monitoring, nutrition guidance, and complication prevention.

head: Tesamorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tesamorelin is a synthetic growth hormone–releasing hormone analog that binds pituitary GHRH receptors to stimulate endogenous growth hormone secretion and increase IGF-1. It is approved to reduce excess abdominal visceral adipose tissue in adults with HIV-associated lipodystrophy. Notable risks include glucose intolerance or hyperglycemia, and it is contraindicated in active malignancy and disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
tail_desc: Sheehan syndrome is postpartum hypopituitarism caused by ischemic necrosis of the pituitary gland after severe obstetric hemorrhage, affecting the endocrine system. It occurs in women after childbirth and commonly presents with failure to lactate, amenorrhea, fatigue, hypotension, and features of adrenal and thyroid hormone deficiency. Management centers on prompt diagnosis and lifelong replacement of deficient pituitary-dependent hormones.

head: Amiodarone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amiodarone is a class III antiarrhythmic, an iodinated benzofuran derivative that primarily prolongs cardiac repolarization by blocking potassium channels, while also inhibiting sodium and calcium channels and exerting noncompetitive beta-adrenergic effects. It is used for life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias and supraventricular arrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation. A notable adverse effect is pulmonary toxicity, including interstitial pneumonitis and fibrosis.
tail_desc: Myxedema is a severe manifestation of hypothyroidism affecting the endocrine system, caused by profound deficiency of thyroid hormone, with dermal glycosaminoglycan accumulation producing nonpitting edema. It typically occurs in adults with longstanding untreated hypothyroidism and features fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, dry skin, and facial puffiness; management centers on thyroid hormone replacement and treatment of precipitating factors, with myxedema coma requiring urgent intensive care.

head: Sufentanil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sufentanil is a potent synthetic opioid analgesic of the fentanyl (phenylpiperidine) class that acts primarily as a mu-opioid receptor agonist, producing profound analgesia and sedation. It is used for induction and maintenance of anesthesia and for perioperative analgesia, including epidural use in some settings. A notable adverse effect is dose-dependent respiratory depression.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Mivacurium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mivacurium is a short-acting, nondepolarizing benzylisoquinolinium neuromuscular blocking agent that competitively antagonizes nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the skeletal muscle motor end plate, producing paralysis. It is used as an adjunct to general anesthesia to facilitate endotracheal intubation and provide skeletal muscle relaxation during surgery or mechanical ventilation. Histamine release with hypotension or bronchospasm is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Tetracaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetracaine is an ester-type local anesthetic of the para-aminobenzoic acid class that reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing impulse conduction. It is used for topical anesthesia of the eye and mucous membranes and in spinal anesthesia. A notable risk is systemic toxicity, including CNS and cardiovascular effects; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to ester local anesthetics.
tail_desc: Eye infectious disease is a broad category of ocular disorders affecting the conjunctiva, cornea, eyelids, or intraocular tissues, caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites that invade eye structures and trigger inflammation. Typical features include redness, pain, discharge, photophobia, blurred vision, and sometimes vision loss, occurring in all age groups. Management generally involves prompt pathogen-directed antimicrobial therapy, supportive care, and urgent ophthalmologic evaluation for severe or sight-threatening cases.

head: Glutamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glutamic acid is a nonessential amino acid and acidic amino acid used mainly as a nutritional or electrolyte replacement component rather than a receptor-targeted drug. It serves as a metabolic substrate and precursor in amino acid and neurotransmitter pathways, without a well-defined primary pharmacologic target in approved therapy. Clinical use is limited to nutritional supplementation; gastrointestinal irritation may occur, and caution is warranted in severe renal or hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Ventricular fibrillation is a life-threatening cardiac arrhythmia of the cardiovascular system in which disorganized ventricular electrical activity prevents effective contraction and cardiac output, often arising in ischemic heart disease or structural heart disease. It causes sudden collapse, pulselessness, and cardiac arrest, especially in adults with underlying heart disease. Management requires immediate defibrillation and cardiopulmonary resuscitation, followed by treatment of the precipitating cause and secondary prevention.

head: Aminobenzoic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminobenzoic acid, usually referring to para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), is a benzoic acid derivative and B-complex–related compound rather than a standard targeted drug. It has no well-defined therapeutic molecular target; historically, potassium para-aminobenzoate has been used clinically for Peyronie’s disease and certain fibrotic dermatoses. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal intolerance, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to PABA-related compounds.
tail_desc: Anorexia nervosa is a psychiatric eating disorder affecting nutritional, endocrine, and cardiovascular systems, characterized by self-induced energy restriction driven by intense fear of weight gain and distorted body image. It commonly begins in adolescents or young adults, especially females, and causes low body weight, amenorrhea, bradycardia, and medical instability. Management centers on nutritional rehabilitation, psychotherapy, family-based treatment, and monitoring for complications.

head: Noscapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Noscapine is a phthalideisoquinoline alkaloid derived from opium poppy and used as a centrally acting antitussive. Unlike opioid analgesics, it has little narcotic activity; its cough-suppressant effect is attributed to action on the medullary cough center, and it has also been shown to bind tubulin and alter microtubule dynamics. Adverse effects can include drowsiness, and concomitant use with warfarin is a notable precaution due to bleeding risk.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Kanamycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Kanamycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing mistranslation. It is used for treatment of susceptible serious bacterial infections and has also been used as a second-line agent for multidrug-resistant tuberculosis. A notable adverse effect is ototoxicity, with nephrotoxicity also an important risk.
tail_desc: Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system, primarily affecting the basal ganglia. It is characterized by degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and accumulation of alpha-synuclein–containing Lewy bodies. Typical features, usually beginning in older adults, include bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and postural instability; management focuses on symptomatic dopaminergic therapy, rehabilitation, and selected device-aided or surgical treatments.

head: Leucovorin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leucovorin (folinic acid) is a reduced folate rescue agent and folate analog that bypasses dihydrofolate reductase, replenishing tetrahydrofolate pools; it also stabilizes the 5-fluorouracil–thymidylate synthase complex, enhancing 5-FU cytotoxicity. It is used for methotrexate rescue, megaloblastic anemia from folate deficiency, and with 5-FU in colorectal cancer. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it should not be used alone for pernicious anemia.
tail_desc: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune megaloblastic anemia of the hematologic and gastrointestinal systems caused by intrinsic factor deficiency, usually from autoimmune gastritis with parietal cell loss, leading to vitamin B12 malabsorption. It typically affects older adults and causes fatigue, pallor, glossitis, and sometimes neurologic deficits such as paresthesias. Management centers on lifelong vitamin B12 replacement and monitoring for associated autoimmune disease.

head: Duloxetine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Duloxetine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is approved for major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, and chronic musculoskeletal pain. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity; concomitant use with monoamine oxidase inhibitors is contraindicated.
tail_desc: Fatty liver disease is a hepatic disorder characterized by excess triglyceride accumulation in hepatocytes, most commonly associated with obesity, insulin resistance, diabetes, dyslipidemia, or alcohol use. It is often asymptomatic but may cause fatigue, hepatomegaly, or abnormal liver enzymes, and can progress to steatohepatitis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and liver failure. Management focuses on treating the cause, especially weight loss, metabolic risk-factor control, alcohol cessation, and monitoring for progression.

head: Rizatriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rizatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, chemically an indole derivative, that acts as a selective agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors to promote cranial vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease due to risk of coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Intermittent vascular claudication is a manifestation of peripheral arterial disease affecting the lower-extremity vascular system, usually caused by atherosclerotic arterial narrowing that limits blood flow during exertion. It classically produces reproducible calf, thigh, or buttock cramping pain with walking that resolves with rest, most often in older adults and smokers. Management centers on risk-factor modification, exercise therapy, antiplatelet/statin treatment, and revascularization when symptoms are severe.

head: Rotigotine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rotigotine is a non-ergoline dopamine agonist of the aminotetralin class that activates dopamine receptors, particularly D3, D2, and D1. Delivered mainly as a transdermal patch, it is approved for Parkinson disease and moderate-to-severe restless legs syndrome. A notable adverse effect is application-site reaction; it may also cause nausea, somnolence, or hallucinations.
tail_desc: Orthostatic hypotension is a cardiovascular autonomic disorder marked by an abnormal fall in blood pressure on standing due to impaired baroreflex-mediated vasoconstriction, volume depletion, medications, or autonomic failure. It commonly affects older adults and patients with neurodegenerative or diabetic autonomic neuropathy, causing dizziness, lightheadedness, blurred vision, weakness, or syncope. Management targets underlying causes, medication review, fluid and salt optimization, compression garments, and selected pressor agents.

head: Duloxetine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Duloxetine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is approved for major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, and chronic musculoskeletal pain. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity; concomitant use with monoamine oxidase inhibitors is contraindicated.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Piroxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Piroxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and other musculoskeletal pain and inflammation. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Vision disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the visual system, including the eyes, optic nerve, and central visual pathways, that impair visual acuity, visual fields, ocular alignment, or visual processing. Causes include refractive error, congenital or acquired ocular disease, neurologic injury, and systemic illness. Patients of any age may present with blurred vision, diplopia, field loss, or reduced visual function; management targets the underlying cause with optical correction, medical or surgical treatment, rehabilitation, and urgent evaluation when acute or progressive.

head: Mesoridazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to CSF3R deficiency (a disease)
head_desc: Mesoridazine is a piperidine phenothiazine antipsychotic, historically used for schizophrenia. Like other typical antipsychotics, it acts primarily through central dopamine D2 receptor antagonism. Its clinical use became severely restricted because of dose-related QT interval prolongation and risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, including torsades de pointes, making it contraindicated in patients with known cardiac conduction abnormalities or significant QT prolongation.
tail_desc: Autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to CSF3R deficiency is a rare inherited hematologic and immune disorder caused by biallelic loss-of-function variants in CSF3R, the receptor for granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, leading to profound neutropenia from impaired granulopoiesis and poor response to G-CSF. Affected infants or children present with recurrent severe bacterial infections, mucosal inflammation, and fever; management centers on infection prevention and treatment, with hematopoietic stem cell transplantation considered in severe cases.

head: Meclizine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meclizine is a first-generation piperazine antihistamine with antiemetic and antivertigo activity. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist, with additional anticholinergic effects that suppress vestibular stimulation and the chemoreceptor trigger pathway. It is used for motion sickness and vertigo associated with vestibular disorders. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; caution is warranted in glaucoma and urinary retention.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Colchicine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Colchicine is a tricyclic alkaloid anti-inflammatory agent that binds tubulin and inhibits microtubule polymerization, suppressing neutrophil chemotaxis, inflammasome activity, and inflammatory mediator release. It is established for acute gout flares, gout prophylaxis, and familial Mediterranean fever, and is also approved in some settings for pericarditis. Notable toxicity includes dose-related gastrointestinal upset, especially diarrhea; avoid use with strong CYP3A4 or P-glycoprotein inhibitors due to toxicity risk.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Proline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Proline is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Medetomidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Medetomidine is an imidazole-derived alpha2-adrenergic receptor agonist sedative and analgesic used mainly in veterinary medicine. By stimulating central presynaptic and postsynaptic alpha2 receptors, it decreases norepinephrine release and sympathetic tone, producing sedation, anxiolysis, and analgesia. It is established for sedation and premedication before anesthesia in animals. A notable adverse effect is marked bradycardia, and it is contraindicated in significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Amcinonide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amcinonide is a high-potency synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid used topically for corticosteroid-responsive inflammatory dermatoses, such as eczema and psoriasis. It acts as a glucocorticoid receptor agonist, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators, vasodilation, and pruritus. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy with prolonged use; it is generally avoided on untreated cutaneous infections due to local immunosuppressive effects.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Atracurium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atracurium is a nondepolarizing benzylisoquinolinium neuromuscular blocking agent that competitively antagonizes nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the skeletal muscle neuromuscular junction, producing paralysis. It is used to facilitate endotracheal intubation and provide skeletal muscle relaxation during surgery or mechanical ventilation. A notable adverse effect is histamine release, which can cause hypotension, flushing, or bronchospasm.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Cetirizine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cetirizine is a second-generation piperazine antihistamine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is widely used for allergic rhinitis and chronic urticaria. Compared with first-generation antihistamines it is less sedating, but somnolence can still occur; caution is advised in severe renal impairment because the drug is substantially renally eliminated.
tail_desc: Atherosclerosis is a chronic arterial disease of the cardiovascular system characterized by lipid-rich plaque formation in the intima, driven by endothelial injury, inflammation, and fibrous cap development. It commonly affects older adults and people with risk factors such as smoking, diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, causing ischemia, angina, myocardial infarction, stroke, or claudication. Management focuses on risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Esomeprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Esomeprazole is the S-isomer proton pump inhibitor of omeprazole, a substituted benzimidazole that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, erosive esophagitis, peptic ulcer disease including NSAID-associated ulcers, and Helicobacter pylori eradication regimens. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea.
tail_desc: Bone fracture is a traumatic or pathologic injury of the musculoskeletal system in which bone continuity is disrupted, usually from acute force, repetitive stress, or weakened bone such as osteoporosis. Typical features include localized pain, swelling, deformity, bruising, and impaired function. It occurs at any age; management focuses on immobilization, pain control, reduction and fixation when needed, and rehabilitation.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Risedronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Risedronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate (pyridinyl bisphosphonate) that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget’s disease of bone, and prevention or treatment of glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophageal irritation or ulceration, and it is contraindicated in hypocalcemia and significant esophageal motility disorders.
tail_desc: Osteitis fibrosa is a metabolic bone disorder of the skeleton, usually caused by hyperparathyroidism with excess parathyroid hormone driving increased osteoclastic bone resorption and replacement by fibrous tissue. It can cause bone pain, fragility fractures, deformity, and “brown tumors,” classically in patients with severe primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism. Management targets the underlying hyperparathyroidism and correction of calcium-phosphate abnormalities.

head: Paromomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Paromomycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic, poorly absorbed orally, that binds the 30S bacterial ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis; it also has luminal antiparasitic activity. It is used for intestinal amebiasis and as a nonabsorbable agent to reduce gut flora, including adjunctive use in hepatic encephalopathy. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal intolerance and, with systemic absorption, ototoxicity or nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Penbutolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Penbutolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at β1 and β2 receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is generally contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis.
