Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Nitisinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitisinone is a triketone, small-molecule inhibitor of 4-hydroxyphenylpyruvate dioxygenase (HPPD), blocking tyrosine catabolism upstream of homogentisic acid formation. It is approved for hereditary tyrosinemia type 1 and used to reduce homogentisic acid in alkaptonuria. A notable adverse effect is elevated plasma tyrosine causing corneal keratopathy and other ocular toxicity, so dietary tyrosine/phenylalanine restriction is required.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Calcium lactate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcium lactate is an oral calcium salt used as a mineral supplement and antacid. It dissociates to provide elemental calcium, supporting neuromuscular function, bone mineralization, and correction of calcium deficiency, without a specific receptor target. Established uses include prevention or treatment of hypocalcemia and calcium supplementation. Notable risks include hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Eltrombopag (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Eltrombopag is an oral, small-molecule thrombopoietin receptor agonist that activates the c-Mpl receptor to stimulate megakaryocyte proliferation and platelet production. It is used for thrombocytopenia in chronic immune thrombocytopenia, severe aplastic anemia, and thrombocytopenia associated with chronic hepatitis C treatment. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Idarubicin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Idarubicin is an anthracycline antineoplastic antibiotic, a semisynthetic daunorubicin derivative. It intercalates into DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, blocking nucleic acid synthesis and promoting DNA strand breaks. It is used mainly for induction treatment of acute myeloid leukemia, often in combination regimens. A notable toxicity is dose-related cardiomyopathy/heart failure, limiting cumulative exposure.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Tioguanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tioguanine (thioguanine, 6-thioguanine) is a thiopurine antimetabolite, a guanine analog that is converted intracellularly to thioguanine nucleotides, which inhibit de novo purine synthesis and become incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing replication and cell proliferation. It is used mainly in acute myeloid leukemia. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression, and hepatotoxicity including hepatic veno-occlusive disease can occur.
tail_desc: obsolete hyperuricemia (disease) is a disease or medical condition.

head: Resorcinol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Resorcinol is a dihydroxybenzene phenol used topically as a keratolytic and antiseptic. Its effects are nonspecific, involving protein denaturation and disruption of keratinized epidermal tissue rather than a defined molecular target. It has been used in acne, seborrheic dermatitis, eczema, psoriasis, corns, calluses, and warts. Notable adverse effects include local irritation; excessive topical exposure may cause systemic toxicity, including methemoglobinemia.
tail_desc: Exanthem is an acute generalized eruptive skin condition, usually reflecting a systemic infection or drug hypersensitivity affecting the integumentary system. It presents as a widespread rash, often with fever and other constitutional symptoms, especially in children with viral illnesses. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, supportive care, and stopping any offending medication.

head: Nefazodone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nefazodone is a phenylpiperazine antidepressant classified as a serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor (SARI). It inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake and potently antagonizes 5-HT2A receptors. It has been used for major depressive disorder. A notable safety concern is rare but severe hepatotoxicity, including liver failure, making active liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Cefepime (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefepime is a fourth-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used to treat susceptible bacterial infections including pneumonia, urinary tract infections, skin and soft tissue infections, intra-abdominal infections, and febrile neutropenia. A notable adverse effect is neurotoxicity, including encephalopathy or seizures, especially in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Idarubicin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Idarubicin is an anthracycline antineoplastic antibiotic, a semisynthetic daunorubicin derivative. It intercalates into DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, blocking nucleic acid synthesis and promoting DNA strand breaks. It is used mainly for induction treatment of acute myeloid leukemia, often in combination regimens. A notable toxicity is dose-related cardiomyopathy/heart failure, limiting cumulative exposure.
tail_desc: Endomyocardial fibrosis is a restrictive cardiomyopathy of the heart characterized by fibrous thickening of the ventricular endocardium, often involving the apices and inflow tracts, causing impaired ventricular filling and atrioventricular valve regurgitation. It occurs mainly in children and young adults in tropical regions and presents with progressive heart failure, ascites, and edema; management is supportive heart-failure therapy, anticoagulation when indicated, and sometimes surgical endocardial resection with valve repair.

head: Apomorphine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Apomorphine is a non-ergoline dopamine agonist of the aporphine class that primarily stimulates dopamine D1- and D2-like receptors in the central nervous system. It is used for intermittent treatment of “off” episodes in Parkinson disease, and some formulations are used for erectile dysfunction in certain regions. A notable adverse effect is severe nausea and vomiting, and it is contraindicated with 5-HT3 antagonists.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Efavirenz (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Efavirenz is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) used as part of combination antiretroviral therapy for HIV-1 infection. It binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Established indications include treatment of HIV-1 in adults and children in combination regimens. Notable adverse effects include rash and prominent central nervous system symptoms such as dizziness, insomnia, and vivid dreams.
tail_desc: Fanconi renotubular syndrome is a proximal renal tubular disorder causing generalized failure of solute reabsorption in the kidney. It may be inherited or acquired, leading to urinary wasting of bicarbonate, phosphate, glucose, amino acids, and other electrolytes. Typical features, especially in children, include polyuria, dehydration, growth failure, rickets/osteomalacia, and metabolic acidosis; management targets the cause and replaces fluid, bicarbonate, phosphate, and vitamin D.

head: Tamsulosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tamsulosin is a selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist, chemically a sulfonamide derivative, that preferentially blocks alpha-1A and alpha-1D receptors in prostatic and bladder neck smooth muscle to improve urinary flow. It is established for treatment of signs and symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension, and it can also cause abnormal ejaculation.
tail_desc: Priapism is a urologic emergency of the male genital system characterized by a persistent penile erection unrelated to sexual stimulation, usually caused by impaired venous outflow (ischemic/low-flow) or, less often, unregulated arterial inflow (nonischemic/high-flow). It presents with prolonged erection, often painful in ischemic cases, in males of any age, including those with sickle cell disease or drug exposure, and requires urgent treatment to preserve erectile tissue and function.

head: Lepirudin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lepirudin is a recombinant hirudin derivative and direct thrombin inhibitor that binds both free and clot-bound thrombin (factor IIa), preventing fibrin formation and thrombin-mediated coagulation. It was used as an anticoagulant for patients with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, including those requiring anticoagulation. A notable risk is major bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to hirudins.
tail_desc: Cerebral infarction is an ischemic stroke of the central nervous system in which interrupted cerebral blood flow, usually from arterial thrombosis or embolism, causes focal brain tissue necrosis. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or visual loss, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible and secondary stroke prevention.

head: Simvastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Simvastatin is a lipid-lowering statin, a semisynthetic derivative of a fermentation product, that acts as a prodrug inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase (3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase), reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used for hypercholesterolemia and cardiovascular risk reduction. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; active liver disease is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Iocetamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iocetamic acid is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Heart cancer usually refers to primary cardiac tumors, which are very rare and may be benign or malignant, or more commonly to metastatic spread to the heart from other cancers, affecting the cardiovascular system. Malignant cardiac tumors such as sarcomas can cause obstruction, arrhythmias, heart failure, embolic events, or pericardial effusion. Management depends on tumor type and extent, using surgical resection when feasible, plus oncology-directed systemic therapy or palliation.

head: Physostigmine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Physostigmine is a reversible carbamate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (an indirect cholinergic agonist) that increases acetylcholine at muscarinic and nicotinic synapses, including in the CNS. It is used to reverse anticholinergic toxicity and has also been used for glaucoma. Notable risks include bradycardia and seizures, and it is contraindicated in tricyclic antidepressant overdose due to potential cardiac toxicity.
tail_desc: Allergic asthma is a common phenotype of asthma, a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways in the respiratory system, driven by allergen-triggered, IgE-associated immune responses with airway hyperresponsiveness and variable airflow obstruction. It often affects children and atopic individuals, causing episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often after allergen exposure. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and standard asthma therapy, especially inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.

head: Pramipexole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pramipexole is a non-ergot dopamine agonist of the aminobenzothiazole class that primarily stimulates dopamine D2-family receptors, with preference for D3 receptors. It is used for Parkinson disease and moderate-to-severe primary restless legs syndrome. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, including sudden sleep onset; caution is warranted in patients with psychotic disorders.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Synephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Synephrine is a naturally occurring protoalkaloid phenylethanolamine, structurally related to ephedrine, found notably in bitter orange (Citrus aurantium). It acts primarily as a sympathomimetic agonist at adrenergic receptors, with reported activity especially at beta-3 and some alpha receptors. It is not an approved prescription drug for established clinical indications in major jurisdictions; use is mainly in supplements. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, so it is contraindicated in significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadiazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections and, with pyrimethamine, for toxoplasmosis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe rash such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome; it is generally contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy and near term pregnancy or in neonates due to kernicterus risk.
tail_desc: obsolete superimposed infection is a disease or medical condition.

head: Metolazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metolazone is a quinazoline sulfonamide-like thiazide-related diuretic that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat hypertension and edema, including edema associated with congestive heart failure or renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: HELLP syndrome is a severe pregnancy-associated hypertensive disorder, usually considered a variant of preeclampsia, affecting the maternal hematologic and hepatic systems. It is defined by hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets, reflecting microangiopathic endothelial injury. It typically occurs in late pregnancy or postpartum with hypertension, right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and malaise; management centers on maternal stabilization and prompt delivery.

head: Quazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine hypnotic of the triazolobenzodiazepine family that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing inhibitory GABA signaling. It is indicated for the treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling asleep and maintaining sleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day sedation; it is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines.
tail_desc: Lung disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the respiratory system, especially the lungs and airways. It may result from infection, inflammation, smoking-related injury, environmental exposures, genetic disorders, or vascular and interstitial pathology. Common features include cough, dyspnea, wheeze, chest discomfort, and impaired gas exchange across all ages. Management depends on cause and may include risk-factor reduction, medications, oxygen, pulmonary rehabilitation, or surgery.

head: Vemurafenib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vemurafenib is a small-molecule BRAF kinase inhibitor, a diaryl urea derivative, that selectively targets mutant BRAF, especially BRAF V600E, to inhibit MAPK pathway signaling and tumor cell proliferation. It is approved for unresectable or metastatic melanoma with BRAF V600 mutation and for Erdheim-Chester disease with BRAF V600 mutations. A notable adverse effect is cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma.
tail_desc: Hyperbilirubinemia is a metabolic disorder of the hepatobiliary system characterized by elevated bilirubin in blood, caused by increased bilirubin production, impaired hepatic uptake or conjugation, or reduced biliary excretion. It commonly presents with jaundice and scleral icterus, and in neonates may cause kernicterus if severe. Management targets the cause and bilirubin reduction, including phototherapy, exchange transfusion, or treating liver or hemolytic disease.

head: Isometheptene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isometheptene is a sympathomimetic amine with vasoconstrictor activity, used mainly in combination products for acute migraine and tension headache. Its therapeutic effect is attributed to adrenergic stimulation causing cranial vasoconstriction, although a specific primary molecular target is not well established. Because it can raise blood pressure, it is contraindicated in severe hypertension and may cause palpitations or other cardiovascular adverse effects.
tail_desc: Peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related gastrointestinal disorder characterized by mucosal ulceration in the stomach or proximal duodenum, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It typically presents with epigastric pain, dyspepsia, nausea, or bleeding, especially in affected adults. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, and avoiding ulcerogenic medications.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Peliosis hepatis is a rare vascular disorder of the liver characterized by multiple blood-filled cystic spaces within the hepatic parenchyma, likely related to sinusoidal dilatation and hepatocellular/reticulin disruption. It may be asymptomatic or cause hepatomegaly, abdominal pain, jaundice, liver dysfunction, or life-threatening intra-abdominal hemorrhage, often in association with certain drugs, chronic illness, or immunosuppression. Management focuses on removing the underlying cause and treating complications.

head: Isradipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isradipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels, mainly in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation. It is an established antihypertensive agent used for treatment of hypertension. A notable adverse effect is dose-related peripheral edema; like other dihydropyridines, it may also cause dizziness, flushing, and reflex tachycardia.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Ethotoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethotoin is a hydantoin anticonvulsant used to treat seizure disorders, particularly tonic-clonic and complex partial seizures. Like other hydantoins, it is thought to stabilize neuronal membranes by limiting voltage-gated sodium channel–mediated repetitive firing, though its mechanism is not fully defined. A notable adverse effect is blood dyscrasia, and it is contraindicated in patients with hepatic dysfunction or hematologic abnormalities.
tail_desc: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a chronic multisystem autoimmune connective tissue disease in which loss of immune tolerance leads to autoantibody production and immune-complex–mediated inflammation affecting skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, serosa, and the nervous system. It commonly affects women of childbearing age and causes fatigue, rash, photosensitivity, arthritis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities; management uses sun protection, hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressive or biologic therapies tailored to organ involvement.

head: Butobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butobarbital is a barbiturate sedative-hypnotic, used primarily for the short-term treatment of insomnia and occasionally for preoperative sedation. Like other barbiturates, it enhances GABAergic inhibition by positively modulating the GABA-A receptor, increasing chloride ion influx and depressing central nervous system activity. A notable adverse effect is respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in patients with porphyria.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Oxybutynin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) tertiary amine used to treat overactive bladder symptoms, including urinary urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence, and some cases of neurogenic detrusor overactivity. It acts mainly by antagonizing muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, particularly M3 receptors in bladder smooth muscle, reducing detrusor contractions. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Triptorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triptorelin is a synthetic decapeptide gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that binds pituitary GnRH receptors; continuous administration suppresses LH and FSH release, lowering sex steroid production. It is used for prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial hormone “flare” that can transiently worsen symptoms.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Salsalate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salsalate is a nonacetylated salicylate NSAID and dimer of salicylic acid that is hydrolyzed to salicylate; it reduces prostaglandin synthesis primarily through cyclooxygenase inhibition, with relatively weak direct platelet inhibition compared with aspirin. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and other painful inflammatory conditions. Notable adverse effects include salicylism and gastrointestinal irritation or ulceration.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Cisapride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cisapride is a substituted piperidinyl benzamide gastroprokinetic agent that enhances gastrointestinal motility primarily by acting as a serotonin 5-HT4 receptor agonist, promoting acetylcholine release in the enteric nervous system. It was used for gastroesophageal reflux disease and other motility disorders, but its use became highly restricted because it can prolong the QT interval and cause serious ventricular arrhythmias, especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Flumethasone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flumethasone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts by binding the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It has been used mainly in topical dermatologic and otic/ophthalmic preparations for inflammatory conditions. Like other corticosteroids, prolonged local use can cause skin atrophy, and it is generally contraindicated in untreated infections at the application site.
tail_desc: Congenital hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is a rare genetic endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis caused by deficient gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion or action, leading to low LH/FSH and sex steroid deficiency. It typically presents in infancy or adolescence with micropenis or cryptorchidism in males, absent or delayed puberty, infertility, and sometimes anosmia in Kallmann syndrome. Management focuses on sex steroid replacement and fertility induction with gonadotropins or pulsatile GnRH.

head: Metolazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metolazone is a quinazoline sulfonamide-like thiazide-related diuretic that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat hypertension and edema, including edema associated with congestive heart failure or renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Lacosamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lacosamide is a functionalized amino acid antiepileptic drug that selectively enhances slow inactivation of voltage-gated sodium channels, reducing neuronal hyperexcitability. It is approved for focal-onset seizures and, in some settings, adjunctive treatment of primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. A notable adverse effect is dose-related PR-interval prolongation, so caution is warranted in patients with cardiac conduction abnormalities.
tail_desc: Atrial fibrillation is a common supraventricular cardiac arrhythmia of the cardiovascular system caused by disorganized atrial electrical activity, leading to ineffective atrial contraction and an irregularly irregular ventricular response. It often affects older adults and patients with hypertension, valvular, ischemic, or structural heart disease, causing palpitations, dyspnea, fatigue, or stroke. Management typically focuses on rate or rhythm control and anticoagulation to reduce thromboembolic risk.

head: Dexrabeprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dexrabeprazole is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Osteoporosis is a metabolic skeletal disease of the musculoskeletal system characterized by low bone mass and deterioration of bone microarchitecture, causing increased bone fragility and fracture risk. It commonly affects older adults, especially postmenopausal women, and may be asymptomatic until vertebral, hip, or wrist fractures occur. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, adequate calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Methadone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadone is a synthetic diphenylheptane opioid analgesic that acts primarily as a full agonist at the mu-opioid receptor; it also has NMDA receptor antagonism and inhibits norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for severe chronic pain and for maintenance or detoxification in opioid use disorder. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation, which can predispose to torsades de pointes.
tail_desc: Urethral stricture is a narrowing of the urethra in the genitourinary system, usually caused by fibrosis and scar formation after inflammation, trauma, instrumentation, or prior infection. It most often affects males and presents with weak urinary stream, straining, incomplete bladder emptying, urinary retention, or recurrent urinary tract infection. Management typically involves endoscopic dilation or urethrotomy, with urethroplasty for recurrent or complex strictures.

head: Amoxicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Amoxicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibacterial agent. It binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to cell lysis. It is widely used for susceptible infections such as otitis media, sinusitis, streptococcal pharyngitis, lower respiratory, urinary tract, skin infections, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins.
tail_desc: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Lurasidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lurasidone is an atypical antipsychotic of the benzisothiazole class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at 5-HT7 and 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar depression. A notable adverse effect is akathisia; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers.
tail_desc: Vertebrobasilar insufficiency is a cerebrovascular disorder of the posterior circulation in which reduced blood flow through the vertebral and basilar arteries, usually from atherosclerotic stenosis or embolic disease, causes transient or persistent brainstem, cerebellar, or occipital ischemia. It typically affects older adults and presents with vertigo, diplopia, dysarthria, ataxia, visual symptoms, or drop attacks. Management focuses on urgent stroke evaluation, vascular risk reduction, antithrombotic therapy, and selected revascularization.

head: Methadone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadone is a synthetic diphenylheptane opioid analgesic that acts primarily as a full agonist at the mu-opioid receptor; it also has NMDA receptor antagonism and inhibits norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for severe chronic pain and for maintenance or detoxification in opioid use disorder. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation, which can predispose to torsades de pointes.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Tioguanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tioguanine (thioguanine, 6-thioguanine) is a thiopurine antimetabolite, a guanine analog that is converted intracellularly to thioguanine nucleotides, which inhibit de novo purine synthesis and become incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing replication and cell proliferation. It is used mainly in acute myeloid leukemia. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression, and hepatotoxicity including hepatic veno-occlusive disease can occur.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Atherosclerosis is a chronic arterial disease of the cardiovascular system characterized by lipid-rich plaque formation in the intima, driven by endothelial injury, inflammation, and fibrous cap development. It commonly affects older adults and people with risk factors such as smoking, diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia, causing ischemia, angina, myocardial infarction, stroke, or claudication. Management focuses on risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Amoxicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amoxicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibacterial agent. It binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to cell lysis. It is widely used for susceptible infections such as otitis media, sinusitis, streptococcal pharyngitis, lower respiratory, urinary tract, skin infections, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins.
tail_desc: Adult-onset myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system, usually caused by antibodies against acetylcholine receptor or related postsynaptic proteins, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It presents in adults with fluctuating, fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunotherapy, and thymectomy in selected patients.

head: Mepivacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepivacaine is an amide-type local anesthetic of the pipecoloxylidide family that reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It is used for local or regional anesthesia, including infiltration, peripheral nerve block, epidural, and spinal procedures. A notable risk is dose-related central nervous system and cardiovascular toxicity, especially with inadvertent intravascular injection.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Psoriasis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory disease primarily affecting the skin, driven by dysregulated T-cell and cytokine signaling that accelerates keratinocyte proliferation. It causes well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale, commonly on the scalp, elbows, and knees, and may involve nails or joints. It occurs in children and adults; management includes topical therapy, phototherapy, and systemic or biologic agents for more severe disease.

head: Lanreotide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lanreotide is a long-acting synthetic somatostatin analog that activates somatostatin receptors, especially SSTR2 and SSTR5, suppressing secretion of growth hormone and several gastrointestinal peptides. It is used for acromegaly and for symptom control of neuroendocrine tumors, including carcinoid syndrome; some formulations are also approved for gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. A notable adverse effect is cholelithiasis.
tail_desc: Cholelithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, characterized by stone formation in the gallbladder, usually from cholesterol supersaturation or, less often, pigment precipitation. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease typically causes episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cases.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Noscapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Noscapine is a phthalideisoquinoline alkaloid derived from opium poppy and used as a centrally acting antitussive. Unlike opioid analgesics, it has little narcotic activity; its cough-suppressant effect is attributed to action on the medullary cough center, and it has also been shown to bind tubulin and alter microtubule dynamics. Adverse effects can include drowsiness, and concomitant use with warfarin is a notable precaution due to bleeding risk.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Protriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Protriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzocycloheptadiene family. It acts primarily by inhibiting neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is used for treatment of major depressive disorder. A notable adverse effect is anticholinergic toxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Peptic esophagitis is an inflammatory disorder of the esophagus, usually caused by reflux of acidic gastric contents in gastroesophageal reflux disease. It commonly presents in adults with heartburn, retrosternal pain, acid regurgitation, and sometimes dysphagia or odynophagia. Management focuses on acid suppression, especially proton pump inhibitors, along with lifestyle measures and treatment of the underlying reflux.

head: Bosentan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bosentan is an oral dual endothelin receptor antagonist, chemically a sulfonamide derivative, that blocks endothelin ETA and ETB receptors to reduce endothelin-1–mediated vasoconstriction and vascular proliferation. It is approved for pulmonary arterial hypertension to improve exercise capacity and delay clinical worsening. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity with elevated liver aminotransferases, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Gabapentin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Gabapentin is a gabapentinoid and structural analogue of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) that binds the alpha-2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release. It is approved for partial seizures and postherpetic neuralgia, and is also established for certain neuropathic pain conditions. A notable adverse effect is somnolence or dizziness.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Tolmetin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tolmetin is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), a heteroaryl acetic acid derivative, that reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with aspirin-sensitive asthma.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Mechlorethamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mechlorethamine is a nitrogen mustard alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms highly reactive intermediates, causing DNA cross-linking and inhibition of DNA replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used in regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and as topical therapy for mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma). A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is also a potent vesicant if extravasated.
tail_desc: Cyclic hematopoiesis is a rare disorder of the hematopoietic system characterized by periodic oscillations in blood cell production, classically causing recurrent neutropenia. It is often linked to inherited defects affecting granulopoiesis regulation, especially ELANE mutations in cyclic neutropenia. Patients, often presenting in infancy or childhood, have recurrent fevers, oral ulcers, and infections; management is mainly supportive, with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor commonly used.

head: Ramelteon (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ramelteon is a melatonin receptor agonist of the tricyclic indan derivative class that selectively activates MT1 and MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus to regulate circadian sleep-wake signaling. It is approved for insomnia characterized by difficulty with sleep onset. A notable adverse effect is somnolence; it is contraindicated in patients taking fluvoxamine because of markedly increased ramelteon exposure.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Cysteamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cysteamine is an aminothiol and cystine-depleting agent used primarily to treat nephropathic cystinosis. Within lysosomes, it reacts with cystine to form cysteine and a cysteine-cysteamine mixed disulfide, enabling export through existing lysine transport pathways and reducing cystine accumulation. It is also used ophthalmically for corneal cystine crystals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and sulfurous body odor.
tail_desc: Actinic keratosis is a premalignant keratinocytic lesion of sun-exposed skin caused by cumulative ultraviolet radiation–induced atypia in the epidermis. It typically affects fair-skinned, older adults and presents as rough, scaly, erythematous papules or plaques, especially on the face, scalp, ears, and hands. Management focuses on lesion destruction or field therapy and photoprotection to reduce progression to cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma.

head: Sorafenib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sorafenib is an oral multikinase inhibitor of the biaryl urea class that targets RAF kinases and several receptor tyrosine kinases, including VEGFR, PDGFR, FLT3, RET, and KIT, thereby suppressing tumor-cell proliferation and angiogenesis. It is approved for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma, advanced renal cell carcinoma, and radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is hand-foot skin reaction.
tail_desc: Pancreatitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pancreas in the digestive system, caused by premature activation of pancreatic enzymes leading to autodigestion; common triggers include gallstones, alcohol use, hypertriglyceridemia, and medications. It typically presents with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting in adults. Management focuses on supportive care, treating the underlying cause, and addressing complications.

head: Netilmicin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Netilmicin is a semisynthetic aminoglycoside antibiotic, derived from sisomicin, that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit and disrupts protein synthesis, causing mistranslation and bactericidal activity against susceptible aerobic Gram-negative bacteria. It is used for serious infections such as urinary tract, respiratory, intra-abdominal, and septicemic infections. Like other aminoglycosides, it can cause nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Lithium citrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lithium citrate is a lithium salt used as a mood stabilizer in bipolar disorder, particularly for acute mania and maintenance therapy. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but lithium modulates intracellular signaling, including inhibition of inositol monophosphatase and glycogen synthase kinase-3. A notable toxicity is nephrogenic diabetes insipidus; it is contraindicated or used with great caution in significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Diabetes insipidus is an endocrine disorder of water balance involving the hypothalamic-pituitary-renal axis, caused by deficient antidiuretic hormone secretion (central) or renal resistance to it (nephrogenic). It presents with polyuria, polydipsia, dilute urine, nocturia, and risk of dehydration and hypernatremia in children or adults. Management targets the cause, with desmopressin for central disease and hydration plus measures for nephrogenic forms.

head: Sulfadoxine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadoxine is a long-acting sulfonamide antimicrobial and antimalarial antifolate that inhibits parasitic and bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used in fixed combination with pyrimethamine for treatment and intermittent preventive therapy of malaria due to susceptible Plasmodium falciparum. A notable risk is severe hypersensitivity, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome; sulfonamide allergy is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Glycogen storage disease comprises inherited metabolic disorders, usually affecting liver and/or muscle, caused by enzyme defects in glycogen synthesis or breakdown that lead to abnormal glycogen accumulation or impaired glucose release. Typical features include fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, growth delay, exercise intolerance, and muscle weakness, often presenting in infancy or childhood. Management is mainly dietary and supportive, with subtype-specific monitoring and complication prevention.

head: Sulindac (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulindac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the indene acetic acid derivative class, used as a prodrug whose active sulfide metabolite reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is established for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and acute gouty arthritis. Notable risks include serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated after coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Bromotheophylline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bromotheophylline is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Ardeparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ardeparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant derived from porcine mucosal heparin. It potentiates antithrombin III, producing greater inhibition of factor Xa than thrombin (factor IIa). It has been used for prophylaxis of postoperative deep vein thrombosis, particularly after knee surgery. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it can also cause heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Diabetic retinopathy is a microvascular complication of diabetes affecting the retina. Chronic hyperglycemia damages retinal capillaries, causing leakage, ischemia, and pathologic neovascularization. It typically occurs in people with type 1 or type 2 diabetes and may present with blurred vision, floaters, macular edema, or progressive vision loss, though early disease can be asymptomatic. Management focuses on glycemic and blood pressure control, regular retinal screening, and ophthalmic treatment such as laser therapy or intravitreal anti-VEGF agents.

head: Meclocycline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meclocycline is a tetracycline-class antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by reversibly binding the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment. It has been used mainly as a topical anti-infective for superficial bacterial skin infections. A notable adverse effect is photosensitivity; like other tetracyclines, it is generally contraindicated during pregnancy and in young children because of tooth discoloration and effects on bone growth.
tail_desc: Central diabetes insipidus is an endocrine disorder of water balance caused by deficient secretion of arginine vasopressin from the hypothalamus or posterior pituitary, affecting the neuroendocrine-renal axis. It leads to inability to concentrate urine, producing polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, and risk of hypernatremia; it occurs in children or adults from idiopathic, genetic, traumatic, neoplastic, or postoperative causes. Management centers on desmopressin and careful fluid and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Lithium carbonate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lithium carbonate is an oral lithium salt and mood stabilizer used primarily for acute mania and maintenance treatment in bipolar disorder. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it modulates intracellular signaling, including inhibition of inositol monophosphatase and glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK-3). A notable risk is nephrotoxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients with significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment prevents adequate ventricular filling or ejection, reducing cardiac output and/or causing congestion. Common causes include ischemic heart disease, hypertension, and cardiomyopathy. Typical features are dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance, especially in older adults. Management focuses on treating causes, relieving congestion, and using guideline-directed medications and devices when indicated.

head: Clofedanol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clofedanol is a centrally acting non-opioid antitussive (cough suppressant), structurally classified as a diphenylmethane derivative. Its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well established, but it suppresses the cough reflex via central action. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of nonproductive cough. Notable adverse effects include drowsiness and dizziness.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Tacrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tacrine is a centrally acting aminoacridine cholinesterase inhibitor that reversibly inhibits acetylcholinesterase, and to a lesser extent butyrylcholinesterase, increasing synaptic acetylcholine. It was approved for symptomatic treatment of mild to moderate Alzheimer disease dementia. A notable limitation is hepatotoxicity, with clinically significant elevations of liver transaminases requiring monitoring and restricting its use.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Zolmitriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zolmitriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, an indole/tryptamine derivative that acts as a selective 5-HT1B/5-HT1D serotonin receptor agonist, producing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for the acute treatment of migraine attacks, with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease because triptans can cause chest tightness and coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Cefamandole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefamandole is a second-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It has been used parenterally for susceptible respiratory, urinary tract, skin/soft-tissue, bone and joint, and gynecologic infections, and perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia with bleeding risk due to its N-methylthiotetrazole side chain.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Auranofin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Auranofin is an oral gold(I)-containing disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD). It is thought to act through immunomodulatory effects, notably inhibition of thioredoxin reductase and related sulfhydryl-dependent enzymes, thereby suppressing inflammatory responses. It has been approved for rheumatoid arthritis. Important adverse effects include diarrhea, rash, stomatitis, and potentially serious thrombocytopenia or proteinuria; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Alanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alanine is a nonessential, proteinogenic alpha-amino acid used in amino acid formulations for parenteral or enteral nutrition. It has no specific drug-like molecular target; rather, it serves as a metabolic substrate in protein synthesis and glucose-alanine nitrogen transport. Its established clinical use is nutritional supplementation within amino acid mixtures. Excessive administration may contribute to amino acid imbalance or metabolic disturbances, particularly in severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Digitalis poisoning is a toxic cardiovascular condition caused by excessive exposure to cardiac glycosides such as digoxin, producing inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase and resulting electrolyte-mediated conduction abnormalities. It typically affects patients receiving digoxin, especially older adults or those with renal impairment, and presents with nausea, vomiting, confusion, visual disturbances, bradyarrhythmias, or ventricular arrhythmias. Management centers on stopping the drug, correcting electrolytes, monitoring rhythm, and using digoxin-specific antibody fragments in severe cases.

head: Pramipexole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pramipexole is a non-ergot dopamine agonist of the aminobenzothiazole class that primarily stimulates dopamine D2-family receptors, with preference for D3 receptors. It is used for Parkinson disease and moderate-to-severe primary restless legs syndrome. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, including sudden sleep onset; caution is warranted in patients with psychotic disorders.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Diazoxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diazoxide is a benzothiadiazine derivative and potassium channel opener that activates ATP-sensitive K+ channels, notably SUR1/Kir6.2 in pancreatic beta cells and vascular smooth muscle, suppressing insulin release and causing vasodilation. It is used for hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia and, historically, severe hypertension. Notable adverse effects include fluid retention and hyperglycemia; it is generally avoided in functional hypoglycemia.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Apraclonidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Apraclonidine is a topical ophthalmic imidazoline and relatively selective alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonist that lowers intraocular pressure mainly by reducing aqueous humor production and partly increasing uveoscleral outflow. It is used short-term to prevent or control pressure spikes after anterior segment laser procedures and as adjunctive therapy in glaucoma. A notable adverse effect is allergic blepharoconjunctivitis.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Proflavine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Proflavine is an acridine dye derivative and topical antiseptic/disinfectant. It acts mainly by intercalating into DNA and disrupting nucleic acid replication and cellular function in microorganisms, rather than through a single selective molecular target. It has been used topically for wound and skin antisepsis. A notable concern is local irritation, and its mutagenic potential limits clinical use.
tail_desc: A canker sore is a common, benign aphthous ulcer of the oral mucosa, affecting the mouth. It is a shallow, painful ulcer with a yellow-white base and erythematous halo; the exact cause is unclear but likely multifactorial, with local trauma, stress, and nutritional deficiency as triggers. It occurs in otherwise healthy children and adults and is usually managed symptomatically with topical corticosteroids, analgesia, and avoidance of irritants.

head: Carmustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carmustine is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms DNA interstrand cross-links and carbamoylates proteins, disrupting DNA replication and RNA/protein synthesis without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors and for multiple myeloma, lymphoma, and other hematologic malignancies, including in conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Infectious disease is a broad category of disorders caused by pathogenic microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, or prions, affecting virtually any organ system. It results from microbial invasion and host inflammatory response, with manifestations ranging from fever and malaise to organ-specific symptoms. It can occur in any population, especially the very young, elderly, or immunocompromised, and is managed with pathogen-directed therapy, supportive care, and prevention measures such as hygiene, vaccination, and isolation.

head: Trimipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzazepine class.
