Unlike many TCAs, it has relatively weak inhibition of norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake; its effects are attributed mainly to antagonism at histamine H1, muscarinic cholinergic, and alpha-adrenergic receptors. It is used for major depressive disorders, particularly when insomnia or anxiety is prominent. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation.
tail_desc: Ocular hypertension is an ophthalmic condition characterized by persistently elevated intraocular pressure without detectable glaucomatous optic nerve damage or visual field loss. It results from impaired aqueous humor outflow, increasing risk of primary open-angle glaucoma. Patients are often asymptomatic and identified on routine eye examination, especially adults with risk factors. Management centers on pressure-lowering eye drops and regular monitoring of optic nerves, visual fields, and intraocular pressure.

head: Tramadol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tramadol is a centrally acting synthetic opioid analgesic of the aminocyclohexanol class. It provides pain relief through weak agonism at the mu-opioid receptor and inhibition of norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for moderate to moderately severe pain. A notable risk is seizures, and it is contraindicated in significant respiratory depression or acute intoxication with alcohol or other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Respiratory failure is a life-threatening syndrome of the respiratory system in which gas exchange is inadequate, causing hypoxemia with or without hypercapnia. It results from lung, airway, neuromuscular, chest wall, or central ventilatory dysfunction. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachypnea, cyanosis, confusion, or somnolence, especially in critically ill adults or children. Management targets the underlying cause and supports oxygenation and ventilation, including oxygen therapy and noninvasive or mechanical ventilation.

head: Colchicine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Colchicine is a tricyclic alkaloid anti-inflammatory agent that binds tubulin and inhibits microtubule polymerization, suppressing neutrophil chemotaxis, inflammasome activity, and inflammatory mediator release. It is established for acute gout flares, gout prophylaxis, and familial Mediterranean fever, and is also approved in some settings for pericarditis. Notable toxicity includes dose-related gastrointestinal upset, especially diarrhea; avoid use with strong CYP3A4 or P-glycoprotein inhibitors due to toxicity risk.
tail_desc: Peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related gastrointestinal disorder characterized by mucosal ulceration in the stomach or proximal duodenum, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It typically presents with epigastric pain, dyspepsia, nausea, or bleeding, especially in affected adults. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, and avoiding ulcerogenic medications.

head: Cefazolin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefazolin is a first-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It is widely used for susceptible gram-positive and some gram-negative infections and for perioperative surgical prophylaxis. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, including anaphylaxis, particularly in patients with cephalosporin or serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Synephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Synephrine is a naturally occurring protoalkaloid phenylethanolamine, structurally related to ephedrine, found notably in bitter orange (Citrus aurantium). It acts primarily as a sympathomimetic agonist at adrenergic receptors, with reported activity especially at beta-3 and some alpha receptors. It is not an approved prescription drug for established clinical indications in major jurisdictions; use is mainly in supplements. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, so it is contraindicated in significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Efavirenz (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Efavirenz is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) used as part of combination antiretroviral therapy for HIV-1 infection. It binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Established indications include treatment of HIV-1 in adults and children in combination regimens. Notable adverse effects include rash and prominent central nervous system symptoms such as dizziness, insomnia, and vivid dreams.
tail_desc: Hepatitis C virus infection is a viral infectious disease of the liver caused by hepatitis C virus, leading to hepatic inflammation that may become chronic and progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, or hepatocellular carcinoma. Many patients are initially asymptomatic; others have fatigue, jaundice, or elevated liver enzymes. It affects exposed adults and is managed primarily with direct-acting antiviral therapy and liver disease monitoring.

head: Physostigmine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Physostigmine is a reversible carbamate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (an indirect cholinergic agonist) that increases acetylcholine at muscarinic and nicotinic synapses, including in the CNS. It is used to reverse anticholinergic toxicity and has also been used for glaucoma. Notable risks include bradycardia and seizures, and it is contraindicated in tricyclic antidepressant overdose due to potential cardiac toxicity.
tail_desc: Keratitis is inflammation of the cornea, an ocular surface disorder of the eye that may be infectious (bacterial, viral, fungal, or protozoal) or noninfectious from trauma, dry eye, or contact lens wear. It typically causes eye pain, redness, photophobia, tearing, and blurred vision, and can threaten vision. Management depends on cause, with urgent ophthalmic evaluation and targeted antimicrobial or anti-inflammatory treatment.

head: Ranitidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ranitidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It was used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and other acid-related disorders. A notable adverse effect is headache, and use became widely withdrawn or suspended in many markets because of NDMA contamination concerns.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Ropivacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ropivacaine is a long-acting amide-type local anesthetic, the pure S-enantiomer of a pipecoloxylidide derivative. It blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, inhibiting impulse conduction to produce local or regional anesthesia and analgesia. It is established for surgical anesthesia and acute pain control, including epidural and peripheral nerve block use. Notable adverse effects include dose-related CNS and cardiovascular toxicity.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Oxazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxazepam is a short- to intermediate-acting benzodiazepine anxiolytic that positively modulates the GABA\(_A\) receptor at the benzodiazepine site, enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders and symptomatic relief of acute alcohol withdrawal. Notable adverse effects include sedation and psychomotor impairment; benzodiazepines are contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma and should be used cautiously with other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Lung disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the respiratory system, especially the lungs and airways. It may result from infection, inflammation, smoking-related injury, environmental exposures, genetic disorders, or vascular and interstitial pathology. Common features include cough, dyspnea, wheeze, chest discomfort, and impaired gas exchange across all ages. Management depends on cause and may include risk-factor reduction, medications, oxygen, pulmonary rehabilitation, or surgery.

head: Heparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, thereby inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa and reducing fibrin clot formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndromes, and during procedures such as hemodialysis. A notable adverse effect is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Retinal disease refers to a broad group of ophthalmic disorders affecting the retina, the light-sensitive neural tissue of the eye. Causes include vascular, degenerative, inflammatory, inherited, or traumatic damage, leading to photoreceptor or retinal pigment epithelium dysfunction. Common features include blurred vision, scotomas, metamorphopsia, night blindness, or vision loss. Management depends on cause and may involve risk-factor control, laser therapy, intravitreal drugs, or surgery.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, often from fibrosis, ischemia, drugs, or congenital conduction disease. It may cause bradycardia, fatigue, dizziness, syncope, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on degree and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for advanced or symptomatic block.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Sudden cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which abrupt loss of effective cardiac output, usually from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, causes sudden collapse, unresponsiveness, and absent pulse. It can occur in adults with structural heart disease or acute ischemia, and requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and urgent post-resuscitation care.

head: Benzphetamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzphetamine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic of the amphetamine class, used short term as an adjunct to caloric restriction in the management of exogenous obesity. It acts indirectly by promoting release of norepinephrine, and to a lesser extent dopamine, in the central nervous system to suppress appetite. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and potential for abuse and dependence.
tail_desc: Schizoaffective disorder is a chronic psychiatric disorder affecting the brain, defined by schizophrenia-spectrum psychosis occurring with prominent mood episodes (depressive and/or manic). Its cause is multifactorial and incompletely understood, involving genetic and neurobiologic vulnerability. Patients typically have delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking, plus mood symptoms and functional impairment. Management generally combines antipsychotic medication, mood stabilizers or antidepressants when indicated, and psychotherapy with psychosocial support.

head: Miglitol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Miglitol is an oral alpha-glucosidase inhibitor, a deoxynojirimycin derivative, that competitively inhibits intestinal brush-border alpha-glucosidases, delaying carbohydrate digestion and reducing postprandial glucose excursions. It is used to improve glycemic control in type 2 diabetes mellitus, typically as adjunctive therapy to diet. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal flatulence and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in inflammatory bowel disease or intestinal obstruction.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Aspartic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aspartic acid is a nonessential acidic amino acid (dicarboxylic amino acid) present in human metabolism and used mainly as a nutrient or component of electrolyte/combination supplements rather than a standard standalone drug. It has no well-established specific pharmacologic molecular target in routine therapeutics. Clinically, it is used in nutritional supplementation. Adverse effects are generally mild gastrointestinal upset; it should be used cautiously in significant renal impairment depending on formulation.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Amantadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amantadine is an adamantane derivative antiviral and antiparkinsonian agent. It blocks the influenza A M2 proton channel and, in the CNS, increases dopaminergic signaling and has NMDA receptor antagonist activity. It is used for Parkinson disease and drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms; antiviral use is limited by resistance. Notable adverse effects include confusion, hallucinations, and livedo reticularis.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Allergic asthma is a common phenotype of asthma, a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways in the respiratory system, driven by allergen-triggered, IgE-associated immune responses with airway hyperresponsiveness and variable airflow obstruction. It often affects children and atopic individuals, causing episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often after allergen exposure. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and standard asthma therapy, especially inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.

head: Zidovudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zidovudine (azidothymidine, AZT) is a thymidine analogue nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and causes viral DNA chain termination. It is used, in combination regimens, for treatment of HIV infection and to reduce maternal-fetal transmission. A notable adverse effect is bone marrow suppression, especially anemia and neutropenia.
tail_desc: Non-alcoholic steatohepatitis is a progressive form of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease affecting the liver, characterized by hepatic fat accumulation with inflammation and hepatocellular injury, often linked to obesity, insulin resistance, and metabolic syndrome. Patients may be asymptomatic or have fatigue and abnormal liver enzymes, with risk of fibrosis and cirrhosis; management centers on weight loss, metabolic risk reduction, and monitoring for liver disease progression.

head: Paroxetine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Paroxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant of the phenylpiperidine class that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder and several anxiety-related disorders, including panic disorder, social anxiety disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, OCD, and PTSD. A notable adverse effect is sexual dysfunction; use is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Sodium fluorophosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluorophosphate is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Arginine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Arginine (L-arginine) is a basic, semi-essential amino acid used as a nutritional/pharmaceutical supplement. It serves as the endogenous substrate for nitric oxide synthase, increasing nitric oxide production, and also participates in the urea cycle. Established uses include treatment of urea cycle disorders and supplementation in arginine deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Hyperparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the parathyroid glands causing excess parathyroid hormone, which raises blood calcium and disrupts bone and kidney homeostasis. It may be primary, secondary to chronic kidney disease or vitamin D deficiency, or tertiary after prolonged stimulation. Common features include hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, bone pain, fractures, weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; management targets the cause, with parathyroidectomy for selected cases.

head: Papaverine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Papaverine is an opium alkaloid–derived benzylisoquinoline antispasmodic and vasodilator. It acts mainly by nonselective phosphodiesterase inhibition, increasing intracellular cAMP and cGMP in smooth muscle and causing relaxation, without opioid analgesic activity. Clinically it is used for visceral spasm and vasospasm, and by intracavernosal injection for erectile dysfunction. A notable adverse effect is hypotension.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Lovastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lovastatin is a fungal-derived statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat primary hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to reduce cardiovascular risk. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, and it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Acute coronary syndrome is a cardiovascular emergency encompassing unstable angina and myocardial infarction caused by abrupt reduction in coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, or radiation to the arm or jaw. Management centers on urgent risk stratification, antiplatelet/antithrombotic therapy, anti-ischemic treatment, and timely coronary reperfusion when indicated.

head: Dalfopristin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dalfopristin is a streptogramin A antibacterial, used clinically in fixed combination with quinupristin as quinupristin/dalfopristin. It binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis and acting synergistically with quinupristin. The combination is approved for serious infections caused by susceptible gram-positive bacteria, including vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium and complicated skin infections. Notable adverse effects include arthralgia/myalgia and infusion-site pain.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Fondaparinux (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fondaparinux is a synthetic pentasaccharide anticoagulant that selectively binds antithrombin and indirectly inhibits factor Xa, reducing thrombin generation. It is approved for prophylaxis and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including use in acute coronary syndromes in some settings. A notable risk is major bleeding, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment and in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Diabetic cataract is a lens disorder of the eye occurring in people with diabetes mellitus, caused by chronic hyperglycemia that promotes osmotic and oxidative damage to lens fibers and opacification. It may present earlier than age-related cataract, with progressive blurred vision, glare, and reduced visual acuity. Management centers on good glycemic control and cataract extraction when visual impairment becomes significant.

head: Thiotepa (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiotepa is an alkylating antineoplastic agent of the ethyleneimine/aziridine family. It forms covalent cross-links with DNA, impairing DNA replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used in conditioning regimens before hematopoietic stem cell transplantation and for certain cancers, including bladder, breast, and ovarian malignancies. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Acetazolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acetazolamide is a sulfonamide-derived carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reversibly inhibits carbonic anhydrase isoenzymes, reducing bicarbonate reabsorption and aqueous humor and cerebrospinal fluid production. It is used for glaucoma, prevention and treatment of acute mountain sickness, adjunctive therapy in some epilepsies, and edema. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity and severe hepatic disease.
tail_desc: IDDM 1 is type 1 insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, an autoimmune endocrine disorder of the pancreas caused by T-cell-mediated destruction of insulin-producing beta cells. It typically begins in childhood or adolescence, causing hyperglycemia, polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and risk of diabetic ketoacidosis. Management centers on lifelong insulin replacement, glucose monitoring, diet and education, and prevention of acute and chronic complications.

head: Floxuridine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Floxuridine is a fluorinated pyrimidine antimetabolite, an analog of 5-fluorouracil, that is converted intracellularly to fluorodeoxyuridylate, which inhibits thymidylate synthase and impairs DNA synthesis. It has been used mainly by hepatic arterial infusion for metastatic gastrointestinal cancers, especially colorectal liver metastases. A notable toxicity is severe gastrointestinal mucosal injury, including diarrhea and stomatitis.
tail_desc: Myocardial ischemia is a cardiovascular condition in which reduced coronary blood flow causes insufficient oxygen delivery to heart muscle, usually due to atherosclerotic coronary artery disease and sometimes vasospasm or thrombosis. It commonly presents in adults, especially those with cardiovascular risk factors, with exertional chest pressure, dyspnea, or silent ischemia. Management focuses on restoring perfusion, relieving symptoms, and reducing risk with antiplatelet therapy, antianginal drugs, risk-factor control, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Povidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Povidone (polyvinylpyrrolidone, PVP) is a synthetic water-soluble polymer used primarily as an excipient and tablet binder, film former, and solubilizing/suspending agent in pharmaceutical formulations rather than as an active drug. It has no specific pharmacologic molecular target or therapeutic mechanism of action. Clinically, it is established in oral, topical, and parenteral products as a formulation aid; rare hypersensitivity reactions have been reported.
tail_desc: obsolete heart block is a disease or medical condition.

head: Tioguanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tioguanine (thioguanine, 6-thioguanine) is a thiopurine antimetabolite, a guanine analog that is converted intracellularly to thioguanine nucleotides, which inhibit de novo purine synthesis and become incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing replication and cell proliferation. It is used mainly in acute myeloid leukemia. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression, and hepatotoxicity including hepatic veno-occlusive disease can occur.
tail_desc: Benign recurrent intrahepatic cholestasis is a rare inherited hepatobiliary disorder causing intermittent episodes of intrahepatic cholestasis, usually from childhood or adolescence, without progressive liver damage between attacks. It is linked to defects in hepatocellular bile secretion, classically ATP8B1 or ABCB11 variants, and presents with recurrent jaundice, intense pruritus, and elevated bile acids. Management is mainly supportive and symptom directed, including antipruritic therapy and nutritional monitoring.

head: Butacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic in the benzoate family. Like other local anesthetics, it acts by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used topically, particularly in ophthalmic anesthesia. As with related ester anesthetics, hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known ester local anesthetic allergy.
tail_desc: Bladder neck obstruction is a lower urinary tract outflow disorder of the urinary system in which the bladder neck fails to open adequately during voiding, causing functional or anatomic resistance to urine flow. It typically presents with obstructive and irritative voiding symptoms, elevated post-void residual, or urinary retention, often in men. Management generally includes alpha-blockers, treatment of contributing causes, and selected surgical bladder neck incision or resection.

head: Vorinostat (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vorinostat is a hydroxamic acid histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor that blocks class I and II HDAC enzymes, increasing histone acetylation and altering gene expression to promote cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis. It is approved for treatment of cutaneous manifestations of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma in patients with persistent, progressive, or recurrent disease. Notable adverse effects include thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Oxaliplatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxaliplatin is a third-generation platinum-based antineoplastic (diaminocyclohexane platinum) that forms DNA intra- and interstrand crosslinks, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and triggering apoptosis. It is used mainly in colorectal cancer, including adjuvant treatment and metastatic disease, commonly in FOLFOX regimens. A notable adverse effect is cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy, often precipitated or worsened by cold exposure.
tail_desc: Peripheral nervous system disease comprises disorders affecting peripheral nerves, nerve roots, neuromuscular junctions, or peripheral ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord. Causes include inherited abnormalities, metabolic or immune-mediated injury, infection, trauma, and toxins. Typical features are distal weakness, sensory loss, pain, paresthesias, and reduced reflexes in children or adults. Management targets the cause, symptom control, rehabilitation, and prevention of complications.

head: Salbutamol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salbutamol (albuterol) is a short-acting β2-adrenergic receptor agonist bronchodilator, chemically a phenethylamine derivative. By selectively stimulating β2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, it increases cyclic AMP and produces rapid bronchodilation. It is used for relief and prevention of bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, including exercise-induced bronchospasm. A notable adverse effect is tremor.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Calcium glucoheptonate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: Calcium glucoheptonate is a soluble organic calcium salt used as a mineral supplement to prevent or treat calcium deficiency states. It acts by supplying elemental calcium, increasing extracellular calcium availability rather than acting at a specific molecular target. Established uses include hypocalcemia and supplementation during periods of increased calcium requirement. Adverse effects may include constipation or, with excessive intake, hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Synephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Synephrine is a naturally occurring protoalkaloid phenylethanolamine, structurally related to ephedrine, found notably in bitter orange (Citrus aurantium). It acts primarily as a sympathomimetic agonist at adrenergic receptors, with reported activity especially at beta-3 and some alpha receptors. It is not an approved prescription drug for established clinical indications in major jurisdictions; use is mainly in supplements. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, so it is contraindicated in significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Ardeparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ardeparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant derived from porcine mucosal heparin. It potentiates antithrombin III, producing greater inhibition of factor Xa than thrombin (factor IIa). It has been used for prophylaxis of postoperative deep vein thrombosis, particularly after knee surgery. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it can also cause heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Antithrombin III deficiency is an inherited or acquired thrombophilia of the coagulation system caused by reduced antithrombin quantity or function, leading to inadequate inhibition of thrombin and factor Xa. It predisposes mainly adolescents and adults to recurrent venous thromboembolism, including deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism. Management focuses on anticoagulation, avoidance of provoking risk factors, and perioperative or pregnancy-related thromboprophylaxis.

head: Chloramphenicol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloramphenicol is a broad-spectrum amphenicol antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocking peptidyl transferase activity. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including meningitis, rickettsial infections, and topical treatment of bacterial conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is dose-independent aplastic anemia; use is also limited by gray baby syndrome in neonates.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Nizatidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nizatidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It is used for duodenal ulcer, benign gastric ulcer, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and erosive esophagitis. Notable adverse effects include headache and diarrhea, and dose adjustment is recommended in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Irbesartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irbesartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl tetrazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and diabetic nephropathy in patients with type 2 diabetes. A notable contraindication is pregnancy because it can cause fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Lithium citrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lithium citrate is a lithium salt used as a mood stabilizer in bipolar disorder, particularly for acute mania and maintenance therapy. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but lithium modulates intracellular signaling, including inhibition of inositol monophosphatase and glycogen synthase kinase-3. A notable toxicity is nephrogenic diabetes insipidus; it is contraindicated or used with great caution in significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Cognitive disorder is a broad neuropsychiatric category affecting brain function, defined by acquired impairment in one or more cognitive domains such as memory, attention, language, or executive function. Causes include neurodegenerative disease, cerebrovascular injury, trauma, metabolic or toxic states, and psychiatric or medical illness. Patients, often older adults, present with decline in thinking and daily functioning; management targets the underlying cause, safety, rehabilitation, and supportive care.

head: Methadone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadone is a synthetic diphenylheptane opioid analgesic that acts primarily as a full agonist at the mu-opioid receptor; it also has NMDA receptor antagonism and inhibits norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for severe chronic pain and for maintenance or detoxification in opioid use disorder. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation, which can predispose to torsades de pointes.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Peripheral nervous system disease comprises disorders affecting peripheral nerves, nerve roots, neuromuscular junctions, or peripheral ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord. Causes include inherited abnormalities, metabolic or immune-mediated injury, infection, trauma, and toxins. Typical features are distal weakness, sensory loss, pain, paresthesias, and reduced reflexes in children or adults. Management targets the cause, symptom control, rehabilitation, and prevention of complications.

head: Cyclobenzaprine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclobenzaprine is a centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant, structurally related to tricyclic antidepressants. Its exact mechanism is not fully defined, but it acts within the brainstem to reduce tonic somatic motor activity rather than directly at the neuromuscular junction. It is used for short-term relief of acute painful musculoskeletal spasm; notable adverse effects include marked drowsiness and anticholinergic effects, and it is contraindicated with MAO inhibitors.
tail_desc: Open-angle glaucoma is a chronic optic neuropathy of the eye, usually associated with impaired aqueous humor outflow through an anatomically open anterior chamber angle, often causing elevated intraocular pressure. It typically affects older adults and is characterized by progressive peripheral visual field loss and optic nerve cupping, often without early symptoms. Management focuses on lowering intraocular pressure with topical medications, laser trabeculoplasty, or surgery.

head: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate is a synthetic progestin, a caproate ester of hydroxyprogesterone, that acts as an agonist at the progesterone receptor. It has been used clinically to reduce risk of recurrent preterm birth in certain pregnant patients with a prior singleton spontaneous preterm delivery. Notable adverse effects include thromboembolic events, and it is contraindicated in patients with hormone-sensitive malignancy or active thrombosis.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Desloratadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desloratadine is a second-generation tricyclic antihistamine and the active metabolite of loratadine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing allergic symptoms. It is used for seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, although it is generally less sedating than first-generation antihistamines; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to desloratadine or loratadine.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Selegiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Selegiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class. By inhibiting MAO-B, it reduces dopamine breakdown in the central nervous system. It is established for Parkinson disease and, in transdermal form, major depressive disorder. Notable risks include insomnia and potentially serious hypertensive or serotonergic interactions with contraindicated sympathomimetic or serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Adrenal gland cancer usually refers to malignant tumors of the adrenal cortex or medulla within the endocrine system, most often adrenocortical carcinoma in adults. It arises from uncontrolled adrenal cell growth and may be functional, causing excess cortisol, aldosterone, or androgens, or present as an abdominal mass, pain, or weight loss. Management typically centers on surgical resection, with adjunct systemic therapy and hormonal control when needed.

head: Ethambutol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethambutol is a synthetic antimycobacterial agent used as a first-line component of combination therapy for tuberculosis, including active Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection. It inhibits mycobacterial cell-wall synthesis by targeting arabinosyl transferases encoded by emb genes, disrupting arabinogalactan formation. A notable adverse effect is dose-related optic neuritis, with decreased visual acuity and red-green color discrimination impairment.
tail_desc: Optic neuritis is an inflammatory demyelinating disorder of the optic nerve in the central nervous system, often idiopathic or associated with multiple sclerosis or related autoimmune conditions. It typically affects young adults and presents with acute unilateral vision loss, periocular pain worsened by eye movement, and impaired color vision. Management usually includes ophthalmologic/neurologic evaluation and, when indicated, corticosteroids and investigation for underlying demyelinating disease.

head: Atenolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker, belonging to the beta-blocker class. It primarily antagonizes beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate, contractility, and blood pressure. It is used for hypertension, angina pectoris, and management after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in sinus bradycardia, heart block, and cardiogenic shock.
tail_desc: IDDM 1 is type 1 insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, an autoimmune endocrine disorder of the pancreas caused by T-cell-mediated destruction of insulin-producing beta cells. It typically begins in childhood or adolescence, causing hyperglycemia, polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and risk of diabetic ketoacidosis. Management centers on lifelong insulin replacement, glucose monitoring, diet and education, and prevention of acute and chronic complications.

head: Nandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nandrolone is a synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid and testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein synthesis and erythropoiesis. Its ester forms, such as nandrolone decanoate, have been used for anemia associated with chronic kidney disease and some wasting states. Notable adverse effects include virilization and other androgenic effects; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in prostate or male breast carcinoma.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Amiloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amiloride is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pyrazinecarbonylguanidine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal nephron, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for hypertension and edema, often with other diuretics, and in Liddle syndrome. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in renal impairment or with potassium supplementation.
tail_desc: Anuria is a severe urinary system disorder defined by absent or near-absent urine output, usually reflecting acute kidney failure, urinary tract obstruction, or profound renal hypoperfusion. It presents with markedly reduced urination, fluid overload, electrolyte disturbances, and uremic features, often in critically ill patients or those with obstructive uropathy. Management focuses on urgent identification and reversal of the cause, hemodynamic stabilization, relief of obstruction, and renal replacement therapy when indicated.

head: Tegaserod (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tegaserod is an indole carbazole derivative and selective serotonin 5-HT4 receptor partial agonist that enhances gastrointestinal motility and intestinal secretion via enteric neurons. It is approved for irritable bowel syndrome with constipation in certain adult women and has also been used for chronic idiopathic constipation. A notable concern is ischemic cardiovascular events, making use contraindicated in patients with prior myocardial infarction, stroke, or angina.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Treprostinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Treprostinil is a prostacyclin (PGI2) analog vasodilator that primarily activates the prostacyclin IP receptor, increasing cAMP and inhibiting vascular smooth-muscle proliferation and platelet aggregation. It is established for pulmonary arterial hypertension and, in inhaled form, pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease to improve exercise capacity. Common adverse effects include infusion-site pain, headache, flushing, and hypotension.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Papaverine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Papaverine is an opium alkaloid–derived benzylisoquinoline antispasmodic and vasodilator. It acts mainly by nonselective phosphodiesterase inhibition, increasing intracellular cAMP and cGMP in smooth muscle and causing relaxation, without opioid analgesic activity. Clinically it is used for visceral spasm and vasospasm, and by intracavernosal injection for erectile dysfunction. A notable adverse effect is hypotension.
tail_desc: Sudden cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which abrupt loss of effective cardiac output, usually from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, causes sudden collapse, unresponsiveness, and absent pulse. It can occur in adults with structural heart disease or acute ischemia, and requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and urgent post-resuscitation care.

head: Prazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic, structurally a 1,4-benzodiazepine derivative, used mainly for the short-term treatment of anxiety disorders. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing GABA-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated in myasthenia gravis and severe respiratory insufficiency.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Topiramate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Topiramate is a sulfamate-substituted monosaccharide anticonvulsant that modulates voltage-gated sodium channels, enhances GABA-A–mediated inhibition, antagonizes AMPA/kainate glutamate receptors, and weakly inhibits carbonic anhydrase. It is used for epilepsy and migraine prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include cognitive slowing and metabolic acidosis; it is contraindicated with concomitant alcohol use in the extended-release formulation.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis is a urinary tract disorder characterized by formation of calculi in the kidneys, usually from supersaturation and crystallization of minerals such as calcium, uric acid, or cystine. It commonly causes renal colic, hematuria, nausea, and urinary obstruction, especially in adults. Management focuses on analgesia, hydration, facilitating stone passage, metabolic evaluation, and urologic removal when stones are large, obstructing, or infected.

head: Lopinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lopinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, commonly coformulated with ritonavir to boost exposure via CYP3A inhibition. It inhibits the viral HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and producing immature, noninfectious virions. It is approved, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it also has important CYP3A-mediated drug interaction contraindications.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Tolcapone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tolcapone is a nitrocatechol catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) inhibitor that blocks peripheral and central COMT, reducing levodopa metabolism and prolonging its effect. It is used as an adjunct to levodopa/carbidopa for Parkinson disease with end-of-dose motor fluctuations. A notable risk is severe, potentially fatal hepatotoxicity, making liver disease a major contraindication and requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Erythromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis by blocking translocation. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin, and soft-tissue infections, and as an alternative in some penicillin-allergic patients. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, which can increase the risk of serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Sorafenib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sorafenib is an oral multikinase inhibitor of the biaryl urea class that targets RAF kinases and several receptor tyrosine kinases, including VEGFR, PDGFR, FLT3, RET, and KIT, thereby suppressing tumor-cell proliferation and angiogenesis. It is approved for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma, advanced renal cell carcinoma, and radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is hand-foot skin reaction.
tail_desc: Acute coronary syndrome is a cardiovascular emergency encompassing unstable angina and myocardial infarction caused by abrupt reduction in coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, or radiation to the arm or jaw. Management centers on urgent risk stratification, antiplatelet/antithrombotic therapy, anti-ischemic treatment, and timely coronary reperfusion when indicated.

head: Dasatinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dasatinib is an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the thiazole carboxamide class that inhibits BCR-ABL and Src-family kinases, blocking proliferation signaling. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome–positive chronic myeloid leukemia and Ph+ acute lymphoblastic leukemia. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression; pleural effusion is also a well-recognized toxicity.
tail_desc: Heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment prevents adequate ventricular filling or ejection, reducing cardiac output and/or causing congestion. Common causes include ischemic heart disease, hypertension, and cardiomyopathy. Typical features are dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance, especially in older adults. Management focuses on treating causes, relieving congestion, and using guideline-directed medications and devices when indicated.

head: Quinine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quinine is a cinchona alkaloid antimalarial that acts in Plasmodium blood stages by inhibiting heme detoxification, causing toxic heme accumulation within the parasite food vacuole. It is used primarily for treatment of uncomplicated and severe falciparum malaria, often in combination regimens. A notable adverse effect is cinchonism; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to quinine or a history of quinine-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Optic neuritis is an inflammatory demyelinating disorder of the optic nerve in the central nervous system, often idiopathic or associated with multiple sclerosis or related autoimmune conditions. It typically affects young adults and presents with acute unilateral vision loss, periocular pain worsened by eye movement, and impaired color vision. Management usually includes ophthalmologic/neurologic evaluation and, when indicated, corticosteroids and investigation for underlying demyelinating disease.

head: Succinic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Succinic acid is a naturally occurring dicarboxylic acid and intermediate of the tricarboxylic acid cycle rather than a standard therapeutic drug class. It participates in cellular energy metabolism, but no specific approved pharmacologic molecular target or mechanism is established for succinic acid as a medicinal agent. It has no well-established approved clinical indication as a standalone drug, and concentrated exposure may cause gastrointestinal irritation.
tail_desc: Inflammatory bowel disease is a chronic relapsing inflammatory disorder of the gastrointestinal tract, mainly comprising Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis. It is thought to result from dysregulated immune responses to intestinal microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals. Typical features include abdominal pain, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, weight loss, and fatigue, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood; management aims to induce and maintain remission with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, biologic, nutritional, and sometimes surgical therapy.

head: Trimethoprim (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethoprim is a synthetic diaminopyrimidine antibacterial that selectively inhibits bacterial dihydrofolate reductase, blocking tetrahydrofolate synthesis and thereby impairing nucleic acid production. It is established for treatment and prophylaxis of susceptible urinary tract infections and, in combination with sulfamethoxazole, for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and other bacterial infections. Notable adverse effects include hyperkalemia and megaloblastic anemia, especially in folate deficiency.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Clobetasol propionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clobetasol propionate is a super-high-potency topical corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune mediators and reduce vasodilation and pruritus. It is used short term for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses such as plaque psoriasis and eczema. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy, and prolonged use may cause systemic hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression.
tail_desc: Addison disease is primary adrenal insufficiency, an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex causing deficient cortisol and often aldosterone production, most commonly from autoimmune adrenal destruction. Patients may develop chronic fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia, with risk of acute adrenal crisis. Management is lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, often mineralocorticoid replacement, and stress-dose therapy during illness or surgery.

head: Dipotassium phosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dipotassium phosphate is an inorganic potassium salt and phosphate replacement/electrolyte supplement. It dissociates to provide phosphate for intracellular metabolic processes and potassium for maintenance of cellular membrane potential, without a single specific molecular target. Clinically, it is used to prevent or treat hypophosphatemia, including in parenteral nutrition formulations. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in patients with severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Prednicarbate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prednicarbate is a nonhalogenated topical corticosteroid of the prednisolone derivative class used primarily for corticosteroid-responsive inflammatory dermatoses such as atopic dermatitis and eczema. It acts by activating intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress proinflammatory cytokines and other mediators. A notable adverse effect is local skin atrophy with prolonged use, especially under occlusion or on thin skin.
tail_desc: Telangiectasis is a vascular abnormality of the skin and mucous membranes characterized by permanent dilation of superficial small blood vessels, especially capillaries and venules. It appears as fine red or purple threadlike lesions or networks, occurring in adults or in inherited disorders such as hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia. Management targets the cause and symptoms, including skin-directed laser therapy and treatment of associated bleeding or systemic disease.

head: Vitamin A (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vitamin A comprises fat-soluble retinoids, including retinol and retinyl esters. It is converted to active retinoic acid, which binds nuclear retinoic acid receptors (RARs) and retinoid X receptors (RXRs) to regulate gene transcription involved in vision, epithelial differentiation, growth, and immune function. It is used to prevent and treat vitamin A deficiency; excessive doses can cause hepatotoxicity and teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Iron metabolism disorders are a heterogeneous group of hematologic and systemic conditions affecting iron absorption, transport, storage, or utilization. They arise from genetic defects, chronic disease, blood loss, or iron overload states, causing iron deficiency or excess. Clinical features include anemia, fatigue, organ dysfunction, and tissue iron deposition, occurring in both children and adults. Management targets the cause and iron balance, using supplementation, chelation, phlebotomy, or treatment of underlying disease.

head: Mifepristone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mifepristone is a synthetic steroidal antiprogestin that primarily antagonizes the progesterone receptor and also has glucocorticoid receptor antagonist activity. It is approved for medical termination of intrauterine pregnancy, management of early pregnancy loss in combination regimens, and control of hyperglycemia in endogenous Cushing syndrome. A notable contraindication is suspected ectopic pregnancy; heavy uterine bleeding can occur.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Guanethidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanethidine is an adrenergic neuron–blocking antihypertensive of the guanidine class. It is taken up into peripheral sympathetic nerve terminals via the norepinephrine transporter (NET) and depletes or prevents release of norepinephrine, reducing sympathetic tone. It was used for severe hypertension. A notable adverse effect is marked orthostatic hypotension; it is generally ineffective in pheochromocytoma.
tail_desc: Hepatic encephalopathy is a neuropsychiatric complication of acute or chronic liver failure affecting the central nervous system. It results from impaired hepatic detoxification and portosystemic shunting, leading to accumulation of neurotoxins, especially ammonia. Patients, often with cirrhosis, develop confusion, altered consciousness, asterixis, and sometimes coma; management targets precipitating factors and lowers gut-derived ammonia, commonly with lactulose and rifaximin.

head: Aminocaproic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminocaproic acid is a synthetic lysine analogue antifibrinolytic that inhibits plasminogen activation and plasmin binding to fibrin, thereby reducing fibrinolysis. It is used to treat or prevent bleeding due to hyperfibrinolysis, including perioperative hemorrhage in selected settings. Important risks include thrombosis, and it is generally contraindicated in active intravascular clotting or disseminated intravascular coagulation unless carefully managed.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Tropicamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tropicamide is a short-acting antimuscarinic mydriatic and cycloplegic ophthalmic agent, structurally a synthetic parasympatholytic. It competitively antagonizes muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, primarily M4 and other ocular muscarinic receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle, producing pupil dilation and cycloplegia. It is used for diagnostic mydriasis and cycloplegic refraction; notable risks include increased intraocular pressure and precipitation of angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Sudden cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which abrupt loss of effective cardiac output, usually from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, causes sudden collapse, unresponsiveness, and absent pulse.
