It can occur in adults with structural heart disease or acute ischemia, and requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and urgent post-resuscitation care.

head: Oxaprozin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxaprozin is a propionic acid–derived nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity, including aspirin-sensitive asthma.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Ergometrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ergometrine (ergonovine) is an ergot alkaloid uterotonic that stimulates uterine smooth muscle chiefly via serotonin 5-HT2 and alpha-adrenergic receptors, producing sustained contractions. It is used to prevent or treat postpartum and postabortion uterine atony and hemorrhage. A notable risk is severe hypertension or vasospasm, so it is contraindicated in patients with hypertension or preeclampsia.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Zoledronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zoledronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the osteoclast mevalonate pathway, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget disease of bone, hypercalcemia of malignancy, and prevention of skeletal-related events in patients with bone metastases or multiple myeloma. A notable risk is osteonecrosis of the jaw; severe renal impairment is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Thiothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiothixene is a first-generation (typical) antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class. Its antipsychotic effects are primarily attributed to antagonism of central dopamine D2 receptors, with additional activity at other neurotransmitter receptors. It is used for the treatment of schizophrenia. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including drug-induced parkinsonism and akathisia.
tail_desc: X-linked severe congenital neutropenia is a rare inherited primary immunodeficiency affecting myeloid development, caused by pathogenic variants in WAS that impair actin cytoskeleton regulation and arrest neutrophil maturation, producing profound neutropenia from infancy. Affected boys develop recurrent severe bacterial infections, oral ulcers, and skin or respiratory infections. Management centers on granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, prompt antimicrobial treatment, and consideration of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Mercaptopurine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mercaptopurine is a thiopurine antimetabolite and purine analog used mainly in acute lymphoblastic leukemia and sometimes in inflammatory bowel disease. It is converted intracellularly to active thioguanine nucleotides that inhibit de novo purine synthesis and are incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing cell proliferation. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, with markedly increased toxicity in patients with TPMT or NUDT15 deficiency.
tail_desc: Cholestasis is a hepatobiliary disorder in which bile formation or flow is impaired within the liver or biliary tree, causing retention of bile acids, bilirubin, and cholesterol. It may result from intrahepatic disease, drugs, pregnancy, genetic disorders, or extrahepatic obstruction such as gallstones or tumors. Typical features include jaundice, pruritus, dark urine, pale stools, and elevated cholestatic liver enzymes; management targets the underlying cause and symptom relief.

head: Triamterene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triamterene is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pteridine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal renal tubule and collecting duct, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for edema and hypertension, often with a thiazide diuretic. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria, severe renal impairment, or preexisting hyperkalemia.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Temazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Temazepam is a benzodiazepine hypnotic used for the short-term treatment of insomnia. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride influx and neuronal inhibition. Common adverse effects include sedation, dizziness, and impaired psychomotor performance; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Desvenlafaxine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desvenlafaxine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant and the major active metabolite of venlafaxine. It acts primarily by inhibiting the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic serotonin and norepinephrine. It is approved for major depressive disorder. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and the contraindication of concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Mitoxantrone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mitoxantrone is an anthracenedione antineoplastic agent that intercalates into DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, disrupting DNA replication and repair. It is used to treat certain leukemias, metastatic breast cancer, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, and worsening relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis. A notable risk is cumulative dose-related cardiotoxicity, including reduced left ventricular ejection fraction and congestive heart failure.
tail_desc: Infectious disease is a broad category of disorders caused by pathogenic microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, or prions, affecting virtually any organ system. It results from microbial invasion and host inflammatory response, with manifestations ranging from fever and malaise to organ-specific symptoms. It can occur in any population, especially the very young, elderly, or immunocompromised, and is managed with pathogen-directed therapy, supportive care, and prevention measures such as hygiene, vaccination, and isolation.

head: Darifenacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Darifenacin is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a selective muscarinic M3 receptor antagonist. By blocking M3 receptors on bladder detrusor smooth muscle, it reduces involuntary contractions and increases bladder capacity. It is approved for treatment of overactive bladder with urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency. A notable adverse effect is constipation; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Acquired angioedema is a rare bradykinin-mediated disorder of the vascular/immune system causing recurrent, nonpruritic swelling of the skin and submucosa, often involving the face, extremities, gastrointestinal tract, or airway. It usually occurs in adults due to acquired C1 inhibitor deficiency or autoantibodies, sometimes linked to lymphoproliferative or autoimmune disease. Management focuses on treating acute attacks, airway protection, and addressing the underlying disorder.

head: Valproic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Valproic acid is a branched-chain fatty acid anticonvulsant and mood-stabilizing agent. Its mechanisms are multifactorial, including increased central GABA availability, blockade of voltage-gated sodium channels, and modulation of T-type calcium currents. It is used for epilepsy, bipolar disorder, and migraine prophylaxis. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of major teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Hematologic disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the blood, bone marrow, lymphatic system, and hemostatic pathways. It includes malignant, inherited, immune, and acquired conditions that disrupt blood cell production, function, or survival, or coagulation. Common features include anemia, bleeding, thrombosis, infection susceptibility, lymphadenopathy, or splenomegaly, across all ages. Management depends on cause and may involve supportive care, transfusion, immunotherapy, anticoagulation, chemotherapy, or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Emtricitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Emtricitabine is a synthetic nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI), a fluorinated cytidine analogue used as an antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase by competing with deoxycytidine triphosphate and causing viral DNA chain termination. It is approved, in combination regimens, for HIV-1 infection and is also used in HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis combinations. A notable warning is severe acute exacerbation of hepatitis B after discontinuation in coinfected patients.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Daptomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Daptomycin is a cyclic lipopeptide antibiotic that binds bacterial cell membranes in a calcium-dependent manner, causing rapid membrane depolarization and inhibition of protein, DNA, and RNA synthesis, primarily against Gram-positive organisms. It is used for complicated skin and soft tissue infections, Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia, and right-sided infective endocarditis. A notable adverse effect is myopathy with creatine phosphokinase elevation.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Desloratadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desloratadine is a second-generation tricyclic antihistamine and the active metabolite of loratadine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing allergic symptoms. It is used for seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, although it is generally less sedating than first-generation antihistamines; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to desloratadine or loratadine.
tail_desc: Coronary atherosclerosis is a cardiovascular disease in which atherosclerotic plaques of lipids, inflammatory cells, and fibrous tissue narrow or obstruct the coronary arteries, reducing myocardial blood flow. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults and may cause angina, dyspnea, acute coronary syndromes, or remain asymptomatic. Management focuses on risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Phenylalanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phenylalanine is an essential aromatic amino acid used in medical nutrition and as a component of amino acid supplementation; it has no single drug-like molecular target, serving instead as a substrate for protein synthesis and for conversion to tyrosine. Clinically it is included in parenteral/enteral nutrition formulations. It is contraindicated in phenylketonuria, where accumulation can cause serious neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Ventricular fibrillation is a life-threatening cardiac arrhythmia of the cardiovascular system in which disorganized ventricular electrical activity prevents effective contraction and cardiac output, often arising in ischemic heart disease or structural heart disease. It causes sudden collapse, pulselessness, and cardiac arrest, especially in adults with underlying heart disease. Management requires immediate defibrillation and cardiopulmonary resuscitation, followed by treatment of the precipitating cause and secondary prevention.

head: Mirtazapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mirtazapine is a tetracyclic antidepressant of the piperazino-azepine class used primarily for major depressive disorder. It acts mainly by antagonizing central presynaptic alpha-2 adrenergic autoreceptors and heteroreceptors, increasing norepinephrine and serotonin release, and also blocks 5-HT2, 5-HT3, and histamine H1 receptors. Notable adverse effects include sedation and weight gain; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Nefazodone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nefazodone is a phenylpiperazine antidepressant classified as a serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor (SARI). It inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake and potently antagonizes 5-HT2A receptors. It has been used for major depressive disorder. A notable safety concern is rare but severe hepatotoxicity, including liver failure, making active liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Cerebrovascular disorder is a broad category of diseases affecting the brain’s blood vessels and cerebral circulation, including ischemic and hemorrhagic conditions. It results from vessel occlusion, rupture, or structural abnormalities that impair blood flow or cause bleeding. Typical features are sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, dizziness, or altered consciousness, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent diagnosis, restoring or controlling cerebral perfusion, treating the cause, and preventing recurrence.

head: Tizanidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tizanidine is a centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant, chemically an imidazoline derivative, that functions primarily as an alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonist in the spinal cord to reduce excitatory neurotransmitter release and muscle spasticity. It is used for management of spasticity, including in multiple sclerosis or spinal cord injury. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and concomitant use with strong CYP1A2 inhibitors is contraindicated.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Nortriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Nortriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the secondary amine dibenzocycloheptene class that primarily inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and, to a lesser extent, serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters. It is used for major depressive disorder and is also established for certain neuropathic pain conditions. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic effects such as dry mouth, constipation, and sedation.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Cromoglicic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cromoglicic acid (cromolyn sodium) is a mast-cell stabilizer, chemically a chromone derivative, used mainly for prophylaxis of allergic asthma, allergic rhinitis, allergic conjunctivitis, and mastocytosis-related symptoms. Its mechanism is not fully defined but inhibits mast-cell degranulation and release of histamine and leukotrienes, rather than acting on a single established molecular target. Common adverse effects include throat irritation or cough with inhalation.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Pilocarpine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pilocarpine is a direct-acting cholinergic agonist, an alkaloid muscarinic receptor agonist that primarily stimulates M3 receptors on exocrine glands and smooth muscle. It is used to treat xerostomia, including after head and neck radiotherapy and in Sjögren syndrome, and ophthalmically for glaucoma or miosis. A notable adverse effect is sweating; it is generally contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Cholelithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, characterized by stone formation in the gallbladder, usually from cholesterol supersaturation or, less often, pigment precipitation. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease typically causes episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cases.

head: Sorafenib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sorafenib is an oral multikinase inhibitor of the biaryl urea class that targets RAF kinases and several receptor tyrosine kinases, including VEGFR, PDGFR, FLT3, RET, and KIT, thereby suppressing tumor-cell proliferation and angiogenesis. It is approved for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma, advanced renal cell carcinoma, and radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is hand-foot skin reaction.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Ketamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ketamine is a phencyclidine-derivative dissociative anesthetic that primarily acts as a noncompetitive antagonist of the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor. It is used for induction and maintenance of anesthesia and analgesia; the S-enantiomer esketamine is approved for treatment-resistant depression. A notable adverse effect is emergence reactions with hallucinations and dissociation.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Aluminium phosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aluminium phosphate is an aluminum salt used as a non-systemic antacid and as a vaccine adjuvant. As an antacid, it neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach, reducing acidity without a specific molecular target. Established uses include symptomatic relief of hyperacidity, heartburn, and peptic ulcer disease. Notable risks include constipation and, particularly in renal impairment, aluminum accumulation.
tail_desc: Intestinal impaction is a gastrointestinal obstructive condition in which inspissated intestinal contents, commonly fecal material or indigestible matter, accumulate and block the bowel lumen. It can cause abdominal pain, distension, nausea, vomiting, and constipation or obstipation, and may occur in older adults, debilitated patients, or those with impaired motility. Management typically involves relieving the obstruction, correcting fluids and electrolytes, and treating contributing causes, sometimes with endoscopic or surgical intervention.

head: Benzphetamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzphetamine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic of the amphetamine class, used short term as an adjunct to caloric restriction in the management of exogenous obesity. It acts indirectly by promoting release of norepinephrine, and to a lesser extent dopamine, in the central nervous system to suppress appetite. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and potential for abuse and dependence.
tail_desc: Pulmonary hypertension is a cardiopulmonary vascular disorder characterized by abnormally elevated pressure in the pulmonary arteries due to vasoconstriction, vascular remodeling, thrombosis, or secondary heart/lung disease. It causes progressive dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, syncope, and right heart failure, affecting adults and children. Management targets the cause, uses supportive therapy, and may include pulmonary vasodilator drugs or transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Cyproheptadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyproheptadine is a first-generation antihistamine of the piperidine class with additional antiserotonergic and anticholinergic activity. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist and also blocks serotonin 5-HT2 receptors. It is used for allergic conditions such as allergic rhinitis and urticaria, and commonly causes marked sedation; it is contraindicated in newborns and during breastfeeding.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Magnesium chloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium chloride is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an electrolyte replacement and mineral supplement. It dissociates to provide magnesium, an essential cofactor for many enzymes and a modulator of neuromuscular transmission, membrane stability, and cardiac conduction, without a single specific receptor target. It is used to prevent or treat magnesium deficiency; adverse effects can include diarrhea, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment because of hypermagnesemia risk.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Nelfinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nelfinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, chemically a peptidomimetic protease inhibitor. It blocks the viral HIV protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and thereby producing immature, noninfectious virions. It has been used, in combination antiretroviral therapy, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea, and it is contraindicated with certain CYP3A-dependent drugs because of serious interaction risk.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Rizatriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rizatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, chemically an indole derivative, that acts as a selective agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors to promote cranial vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease due to risk of coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Celecoxib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Celecoxib is a selective nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the diaryl-substituted pyrazole class that primarily inhibits cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2, PTGS2), reducing prostaglandin synthesis and thereby inflammation and pain. It is approved for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute pain, and primary dysmenorrhea. A notable risk is serious cardiovascular thrombotic events; it is contraindicated for perioperative pain in coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Antazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Antazoline is a first-generation ethanolamine antihistamine with anticholinergic properties. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conjunctivitis and rhinitis, often in topical ophthalmic or nasal formulations. A notable adverse effect is sedation; caution is warranted in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention due to anticholinergic effects.
tail_desc: Ocular hypertension is an ophthalmic condition characterized by persistently elevated intraocular pressure without detectable glaucomatous optic nerve damage or visual field loss. It results from impaired aqueous humor outflow, increasing risk of primary open-angle glaucoma. Patients are often asymptomatic and identified on routine eye examination, especially adults with risk factors. Management centers on pressure-lowering eye drops and regular monitoring of optic nerves, visual fields, and intraocular pressure.

head: Bupropion (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bupropion is an aminoketone antidepressant and smoking-cessation agent that inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine, with minimal direct serotonergic activity; nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonism may also contribute. It is approved for major depressive disorder, seasonal affective disorder, and smoking cessation. A notable risk is seizures, so it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or eating disorders.
tail_desc: Psychotic disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting brain function and behavior, defined by impaired reality testing due to diverse causes, including primary psychotic illnesses, mood disorders, substance use, or medical conditions. Core features include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking or behavior, and reduced insight, usually emerging in adolescents or adults. Management focuses on diagnostic evaluation, treatment of underlying causes, antipsychotic medication, psychotherapy, and safety/supportive care.

head: Dichlorophen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dichlorophen is a halogenated bisphenol anthelmintic and taeniacide formerly used mainly in veterinary medicine. It acts locally in the intestinal lumen against cestodes, though its precise molecular target is not well established. Established use has included treatment of tapeworm infections in animals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation such as vomiting and diarrhea, especially with overdosing.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Telavancin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telavancin is a semisynthetic lipoglycopeptide antibiotic, a vancomycin derivative, that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding the D-Ala-D-Ala termini of peptidoglycan precursors and also disrupts bacterial membrane potential and permeability. It is approved for complicated skin and skin structure infections and hospital-acquired/ventilator-associated bacterial pneumonia due to susceptible Gram-positive pathogens. Notable risks include nephrotoxicity and fetal harm; avoid use in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Meloxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meloxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that preferentially inhibits cyclooxygenase, mainly COX-2 over COX-1, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Aurothioglucose (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aurothioglucose is a gold(I) thiolate compound formerly used as a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug for rheumatoid arthritis. Its precise mechanism is incompletely defined, but gold salts modulate immune and inflammatory cell function rather than acting on a single established molecular target. Important adverse effects include dermatitis, stomatitis, proteinuria, and bone marrow suppression; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Ceftizoxime (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ceftizoxime is a third-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibacterial, that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, skin and soft-tissue, bone and joint, and intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Tiludronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tiludronic acid is a non-nitrogen bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, likely via disruption of osteoclast energy metabolism. It is used for Paget disease of bone and, in veterinary medicine, for navicular syndrome in horses. A notable adverse effect is upper gastrointestinal irritation; esophageal abnormalities are a contraindication to oral use.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Bumetanide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bumetanide is a sulfonamide loop diuretic that inhibits the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter NKCC2 (SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, increasing renal sodium, chloride, and water excretion. It is used to treat edema associated with heart failure, hepatic cirrhosis, and renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Auditory system disease comprises disorders of the ear and central auditory pathways that impair sound conduction, sensory transduction, or neural processing. Causes include congenital anomalies, infection, inflammation, trauma, noise exposure, ototoxic drugs, degenerative change, and tumors. Typical manifestations are hearing loss, tinnitus, ear pain, vertigo, and speech-language delay in children. Management targets the cause and functional deficit with medical, surgical, rehabilitative, and hearing-support interventions.

head: Benzphetamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzphetamine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic of the amphetamine class, used short term as an adjunct to caloric restriction in the management of exogenous obesity. It acts indirectly by promoting release of norepinephrine, and to a lesser extent dopamine, in the central nervous system to suppress appetite. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and potential for abuse and dependence.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis disorder is a vascular disease of the circulatory system characterized by thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls, often related to aging, hypertension, diabetes, or lipid deposition in specific subtypes. It may be asymptomatic or cause ischemic complications such as angina, stroke, or peripheral vascular disease, especially in older adults. Management targets risk-factor control and treatment of cardiovascular complications.

head: Phylloquinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) is a fat-soluble naphthoquinone vitamin used as a hemostatic antidote to vitamin K deficiency and warfarin-associated hypoprothrombinemia. It acts as a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase in the liver, enabling activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. Notable risks include anaphylactoid reactions with rapid intravenous administration.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Pindolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pindolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at beta-1 and beta-2 receptors to reduce sympathetic cardiovascular effects. It is used as an antihypertensive agent. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Aminoglutethimide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminoglutethimide is a nonsteroidal aromatase inhibitor and adrenal steroidogenesis inhibitor. It blocks conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone and inhibits aromatase (CYP19), reducing synthesis of glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, estrogens, and androgens. It has been used for Cushing syndrome and advanced breast cancer. Notable adverse effects include sedation and rash; adrenal insufficiency is an important risk.
tail_desc: Myxedema is a severe manifestation of hypothyroidism affecting the endocrine system, caused by profound deficiency of thyroid hormone, with dermal glycosaminoglycan accumulation producing nonpitting edema. It typically occurs in adults with longstanding untreated hypothyroidism and features fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, dry skin, and facial puffiness; management centers on thyroid hormone replacement and treatment of precipitating factors, with myxedema coma requiring urgent intensive care.

head: Clomipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clomipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzazepine class that primarily inhibits serotonin reuptake, with additional norepinephrine reuptake inhibition, via blockade of SERT and NET. It is established for obsessive-compulsive disorder and also used for depression. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic effects and QT prolongation; it is contraindicated with concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Varenicline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Varenicline is a smoking-cessation agent, a synthetic nicotinic receptor partial agonist structurally distinct from nicotine. It acts primarily at the α4β2 neuronal nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, reducing nicotine craving and withdrawal while attenuating nicotine’s rewarding effects. It is approved to aid smoking cessation; notable adverse effects include nausea, and it is used cautiously in patients with severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Intermittent vascular claudication is a manifestation of peripheral arterial disease affecting the lower-extremity vascular system, usually caused by atherosclerotic arterial narrowing that limits blood flow during exertion. It classically produces reproducible calf, thigh, or buttock cramping pain with walking that resolves with rest, most often in older adults and smokers. Management centers on risk-factor modification, exercise therapy, antiplatelet/statin treatment, and revascularization when symptoms are severe.

head: Galantamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Galantamine is a tertiary alkaloid acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, also reported to allosterically modulate nicotinic acetylcholine receptors. By inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, it increases synaptic acetylcholine. It is approved for the symptomatic treatment of mild to moderate Alzheimer disease dementia. Notable adverse effects include nausea, vomiting, and bradycardia; it should be used cautiously in patients with cardiac conduction disorders.
tail_desc: Intrinsic asthma is a nonallergic subtype of asthma affecting the lower respiratory tract, characterized by chronic airway inflammation, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and reversible airflow obstruction without an identifiable external allergen trigger. It often begins in adulthood and presents with episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, commonly provoked by infections, irritants, exercise, or stress. Management follows standard asthma control with trigger avoidance, inhaled corticosteroids, and bronchodilators.

head: Ethadione (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethadione is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Docusate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Docusate is an anionic surfactant stool softener, commonly used as the sodium or calcium salt. It acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract by lowering stool surface tension and promoting water and lipid penetration into feces; no specific molecular target is established. It is used for prevention and treatment of constipation, especially when straining should be avoided. Adverse effects include abdominal cramping and diarrhea.
tail_desc: Primary hyperoxaluria is a rare inherited metabolic disorder of hepatic glyoxylate metabolism that causes oxalate overproduction, primarily affecting the kidneys and urinary tract. It typically presents in childhood or early adulthood with recurrent nephrolithiasis, nephrocalcinosis, progressive kidney impairment, and systemic oxalosis in advanced disease. Management focuses on high fluid intake, crystallization inhibitors, vitamin B6 in responsive cases, and kidney with or without liver transplantation.

head: Flurbiprofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flurbiprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the propionic acid derivative class that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for pain and inflammation, including rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis, and as an ophthalmic agent to inhibit intraoperative miosis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Active peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related disorder of the upper gastrointestinal tract characterized by an active mucosal ulcer in the stomach or duodenum, most commonly due to Helicobacter pylori infection or NSAID use. Patients typically have epigastric pain or dyspepsia and may develop bleeding or perforation. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, stopping ulcerogenic drugs, and treating complications.

head: Zoledronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zoledronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the osteoclast mevalonate pathway, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget disease of bone, hypercalcemia of malignancy, and prevention of skeletal-related events in patients with bone metastases or multiple myeloma. A notable risk is osteonecrosis of the jaw; severe renal impairment is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Cyclosporine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclosporine is a calcineurin-inhibiting immunosuppressant, a cyclic undecapeptide derived from fungi. It binds cyclophilin, and the complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase, reducing NFAT activation and T-cell cytokine transcription, especially interleukin-2. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection and to treat conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and severe dry eye. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Intracranial hypertension is a neurologic disorder characterized by abnormally elevated pressure within the skull due to increased brain tissue, cerebrospinal fluid, blood volume, or impaired CSF absorption/outflow. It commonly causes headache, nausea, vomiting, papilledema, visual disturbance, and sometimes altered consciousness. Management targets the cause and pressure reduction, using urgent monitoring, medical therapy, CSF diversion, or decompressive procedures when severe.

head: Dorzolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dorzolamide is a topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, a sulfonamide derivative, used primarily in ophthalmology. It inhibits carbonic anhydrase II in the ciliary processes, reducing aqueous humor formation and lowering intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is local ocular burning or stinging, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to sulfonamides.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Goserelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Goserelin is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH/LHRH) agonist analogue that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing testosterone or estradiol production. It is used for hormone-sensitive prostate cancer, premenopausal breast cancer, endometriosis, and uterine fibroids. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor flare in prostate cancer.
tail_desc: Hyperglycemia is a metabolic abnormality of the endocrine system characterized by elevated blood glucose, usually due to insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, most commonly in diabetes mellitus. It may cause polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision, and weight loss, and occurs in people with diabetes or acute illness. Management focuses on correcting the cause, hydration, glucose monitoring, and insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy.

head: Bisacodyl (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative of the diphenylmethane derivative class. It acts locally in the colon as a prodrug converted to an active metabolite that stimulates enteric nerves and promotes colonic fluid and electrolyte secretion; no single defined molecular target is established. It is used for constipation and bowel evacuation before procedures. A notable adverse effect is abdominal cramping.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Alprostadil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alprostadil is a prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) analog and vasodilatory smooth-muscle relaxant that activates prostaglandin E receptors, increasing cAMP. It is used to maintain ductus arteriosus patency in certain congenital heart defects and to treat erectile dysfunction. Notable adverse effects include hypotension; it is contraindicated in neonates with respiratory distress syndrome without cyanotic heart disease.
tail_desc: Penile fibromatosis is a benign fibrous disorder of the penis, typically corresponding to Peyronie disease, involving localized fibroproliferative plaque formation in the tunica albuginea. It most often affects middle-aged men and commonly causes penile curvature, pain with erection, palpable plaques, and sometimes erectile dysfunction. Management ranges from observation and conservative measures to intralesional therapy or corrective surgery for persistent, functionally significant deformity.

head: Diphenoxylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diphenoxylate is a synthetic opioid antidiarrheal, structurally related to meperidine, that acts mainly as a peripheral mu-opioid receptor agonist in the intestine to reduce gastrointestinal motility. It is used, typically with atropine, as adjunctive therapy for diarrhea. Notable risks include central nervous system and respiratory depression in overdose, and it is contraindicated in diarrhea caused by invasive enteric infection.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: L-Lysine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: L-Lysine is an essential basic amino acid used as a nutritional supplement rather than a receptor-targeted drug. Its primary role is to replace dietary lysine deficiency and support normal protein synthesis; no specific pharmacologic molecular target is established for routine supplementation. Established use is prevention or treatment of lysine deficiency. It is generally well tolerated, but gastrointestinal upset such as abdominal pain or diarrhea can occur.
tail_desc: Chronic kidney disease is a progressive disorder of the renal system defined by persistent kidney damage or reduced glomerular filtration for at least three months, commonly caused by diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. It often affects older adults and may present with albuminuria, edema, anemia, hypertension, and electrolyte abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause, slowing progression, reducing cardiovascular risk, and preparing for dialysis or transplantation in advanced stages.

head: Lovastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lovastatin is a fungal-derived statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat primary hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to reduce cardiovascular risk. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, and it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Nodular fasciitis is a benign, rapidly growing reactive myofibroblastic proliferation of the soft tissues, usually arising in subcutaneous fascia of the upper extremities, trunk, or head and neck. It typically affects young to middle-aged adults and presents as a small, sometimes tender solitary mass that can mimic sarcoma clinically and histologically. Management is usually local excision, with low recurrence and occasional spontaneous regression.

head: Tramazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tramazoline is an imidazoline-derived sympathomimetic nasal decongestant. It acts primarily as an alpha-adrenergic receptor agonist on nasal mucosal blood vessels, producing vasoconstriction and reducing mucosal edema. It is used for symptomatic relief of nasal congestion, such as in rhinitis or sinusitis. A notable adverse effect is rebound nasal congestion with prolonged use; it is generally avoided in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Oxacillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxacillin is a penicillinase-resistant, semisynthetic beta-lactam penicillin used mainly for infections caused by methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus and other susceptible gram-positive bacteria. It binds bacterial penicillin-binding proteins, inhibiting peptidoglycan cross-linking and cell wall synthesis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with a serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Infectious mononucleosis is an acute viral infection of the lymphoreticular system, most commonly caused by Epstein–Barr virus. It typically affects adolescents and young adults and presents with fever, pharyngitis, cervical lymphadenopathy, fatigue, and sometimes hepatosplenomegaly. Management is mainly supportive, including rest, hydration, and analgesics, with avoidance of contact sports when splenomegaly is present.

head: Allopurinol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Allopurinol is a purine analog xanthine oxidase inhibitor that lowers uric acid by blocking conversion of hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid. It is used for chronic gout/hyperuricemia, prevention of uric acid nephropathy during cancer chemotherapy, and recurrent calcium oxalate stones with hyperuricosuria. A notable risk is severe allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis.
tail_desc: Anti-glomerular basement membrane disease is a rare autoimmune small-vessel disorder affecting the kidneys and often the lungs, caused by autoantibodies against the glomerular basement membrane (classically the α3 chain of type IV collagen). It typically presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, hematuria, renal failure, and sometimes pulmonary hemorrhage, usually in adults. Management is urgent immunosuppression with corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide plus plasmapheresis.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Oxyphencyclimine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxyphencyclimine is a synthetic antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent, structurally a tertiary amine used as an antispasmodic. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal smooth-muscle tone, motility, and secretions. It has been used in peptic ulcer disease and other gastrointestinal hypermotility or spastic disorders. Typical adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, and urinary retention.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Topotecan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Topotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin antineoplastic agent and topoisomerase I inhibitor. It stabilizes the topoisomerase I-DNA cleavage complex, preventing DNA religation and causing DNA strand breaks during replication. It is used for ovarian cancer, small cell lung cancer, and cervical cancer. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression, particularly neutropenia.
tail_desc: Diarrheal disease is a gastrointestinal disorder characterized by the passage of loose or watery stools, usually from intestinal infection, toxins, malabsorption, or inflammatory processes that impair fluid absorption and increase secretion. It commonly causes frequent stools, abdominal cramps, nausea, and dehydration, especially in young children and older adults. Management centers on oral or intravenous rehydration, electrolyte replacement, nutritional support, and treating the underlying cause when identified.

head: Quazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine hypnotic of the triazolobenzodiazepine family that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing inhibitory GABA signaling. It is indicated for the treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling asleep and maintaining sleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day sedation; it is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines.
tail_desc: Respiratory failure is a life-threatening syndrome of the respiratory system in which gas exchange is inadequate, causing hypoxemia with or without hypercapnia. It results from lung, airway, neuromuscular, chest wall, or central ventilatory dysfunction. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachypnea, cyanosis, confusion, or somnolence, especially in critically ill adults or children. Management targets the underlying cause and supports oxygenation and ventilation, including oxygen therapy and noninvasive or mechanical ventilation.

head: Zolmitriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Zolmitriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, an indole/tryptamine derivative that acts as a selective 5-HT1B/5-HT1D serotonin receptor agonist, producing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for the acute treatment of migraine attacks, with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease because triptans can cause chest tightness and coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Vardenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vardenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzenesulfonamide derivative, that selectively blocks PDE5 to increase cyclic GMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation in the corpus cavernosum. It is approved for erectile dysfunction. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nitrates, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Penile fibromatosis is a benign fibrous disorder of the penis, typically corresponding to Peyronie disease, involving localized fibroproliferative plaque formation in the tunica albuginea. It most often affects middle-aged men and commonly causes penile curvature, pain with erection, palpable plaques, and sometimes erectile dysfunction. Management ranges from observation and conservative measures to intralesional therapy or corrective surgery for persistent, functionally significant deformity.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Phendimetrazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phendimetrazine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, chemically related to phenethylamines and used short term as an adjunct in exogenous obesity management. It acts mainly as a prodrug of phenmetrazine, increasing central norepinephrine release and suppressing appetite. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or shortly after MAOI therapy.
tail_desc: Preeclampsia is a hypertensive disorder of pregnancy affecting the maternal vascular, renal, hepatic, and neurologic systems, typically arising after 20 weeks’ gestation. It is linked to abnormal placentation and widespread endothelial dysfunction, causing new-onset hypertension with proteinuria or end-organ injury, often with edema, headache, or visual symptoms. Management centers on maternal-fetal monitoring, blood pressure control, seizure prophylaxis when severe, and timely delivery.

head: Neostigmine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Neostigmine is a reversible carbamate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (indirect cholinergic agonist) that inhibits acetylcholinesterase, increasing acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions and parasympathetic synapses. It is used to treat myasthenia gravis and postoperative urinary retention or ileus, and to reverse nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and other muscarinic cholinergic effects; it is contraindicated in mechanical gastrointestinal or urinary obstruction.
tail_desc: Thyrotoxicosis is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid system caused by excess circulating thyroid hormones, most commonly from hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease, toxic nodular goiter, or thyroiditis. It typically presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, especially in adults. Management targets the cause and symptom control, using beta-blockers and measures such as antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Sulfamerazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamerazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial (“sulfa” drug) of the short- to intermediate-acting class that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby suppressing bacterial growth. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including urinary tract and respiratory infections. Important adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions such as rash, and it is generally avoided in patients with sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Lacosamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
