head_desc: Lacosamide is a functionalized amino acid antiepileptic drug that selectively enhances slow inactivation of voltage-gated sodium channels, reducing neuronal hyperexcitability. It is approved for focal-onset seizures and, in some settings, adjunctive treatment of primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. A notable adverse effect is dose-related PR-interval prolongation, so caution is warranted in patients with cardiac conduction abnormalities.
tail_desc: Orthostatic hypotension is a cardiovascular autonomic disorder marked by an abnormal fall in blood pressure on standing due to impaired baroreflex-mediated vasoconstriction, volume depletion, medications, or autonomic failure. It commonly affects older adults and patients with neurodegenerative or diabetic autonomic neuropathy, causing dizziness, lightheadedness, blurred vision, weakness, or syncope. Management targets underlying causes, medication review, fluid and salt optimization, compression garments, and selected pressor agents.

head: Trichlormethiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trichlormethiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disorders. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Selegiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Selegiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class. By inhibiting MAO-B, it reduces dopamine breakdown in the central nervous system. It is established for Parkinson disease and, in transdermal form, major depressive disorder. Notable risks include insomnia and potentially serious hypertensive or serotonergic interactions with contraindicated sympathomimetic or serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Nifedipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nifedipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels, predominantly in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is used for hypertension and angina, including vasospastic angina. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; immediate-release formulations are generally avoided in acute ischemic heart disease because rapid hypotension may worsen ischemia.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Cefotetan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefotetan is a parenteral second-generation cephalosporin (cephamycin) beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, skin/soft tissue, urinary tract, and perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia/bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Sulfathiazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfathiazole is a short-acting sulfonamide antibacterial of the thiazole-substituted sulfonamide class. It acts as a para-aminobenzoic acid analogue that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. Historically used for susceptible bacterial infections, it is now mainly encountered in topical or veterinary use. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, including severe sulfonamide skin eruptions.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Dexlansoprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dexlansoprazole is a proton pump inhibitor, the R-enantiomer of lansoprazole, that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is approved for healing and maintenance of healed erosive esophagitis and for treatment of symptomatic nonerosive gastroesophageal reflux disease. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea, including risk of Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Clomifene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clomifene is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (triphenylethylene derivative) that acts mainly as an estrogen receptor antagonist in the hypothalamus, blocking estrogen feedback and increasing GnRH, LH, and FSH release to induce ovulation. It is established for treatment of anovulatory infertility, particularly in polycystic ovary syndrome. A notable adverse effect is ovarian hyperstimulation, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Active peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related disorder of the upper gastrointestinal tract characterized by an active mucosal ulcer in the stomach or duodenum, most commonly due to Helicobacter pylori infection or NSAID use. Patients typically have epigastric pain or dyspepsia and may develop bleeding or perforation. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, stopping ulcerogenic drugs, and treating complications.

head: Isosorbide dinitrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Isosorbide dinitrate is an organic nitrate vasodilator used mainly for prevention and treatment of angina pectoris and in some settings heart failure. It is bioactivated to nitric oxide, which stimulates soluble guanylate cyclase, increases cGMP, and relaxes vascular smooth muscle, predominantly reducing venous preload. A notable adverse effect is headache, and it is contraindicated with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors because of severe hypotension risk.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Acrivastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acrivastine is a second-generation antihistamine of the alkylamine/propylamine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used for symptomatic relief of seasonal allergic rhinitis and urticaria, including sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itching. Compared with older antihistamines it is less sedating, but drowsiness can still occur; caution is advised in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Doxepin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Doxepin is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzoxepin class that inhibits neuronal reuptake of serotonin and norepinephrine and is also a potent histamine H1 receptor antagonist. It is used for major depressive disorder and anxiety disorders, and at low doses for insomnia. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation; it is contraindicated in untreated narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Prazosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prazosin is a quinazoline-derived selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist that blocks postsynaptic alpha-1 receptors, reducing peripheral vascular resistance and blood pressure. It is an established antihypertensive and is also used for symptomatic benign prostatic hyperplasia by relaxing smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck. A notable adverse effect is first-dose orthostatic hypotension, which may cause syncope.
tail_desc: X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy is an inherited peroxisomal disorder affecting the nervous system and adrenal cortex, caused by ABCD1 mutations that impair very-long-chain fatty acid transport and degradation. It typically affects boys and men, causing progressive cerebral demyelination or adrenomyeloneuropathy with spastic paraparesis, cognitive decline, and adrenal insufficiency. Management includes adrenal hormone replacement, supportive neurologic care, and early hematopoietic stem cell transplantation for cerebral disease.

head: Labetalol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Labetalol is a mixed adrenergic antagonist of the amino alcohol class that blocks β1-, β2-, and α1-adrenergic receptors, lowering heart rate and peripheral vascular resistance. It is used for hypertension, including severe hypertension and hypertension in pregnancy. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma, overt heart failure, and greater-than-first-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Trazodone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trazodone is a triazolopyridine antidepressant classified as a serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor (SARI). It inhibits the serotonin transporter and antagonizes 5-HT2A/5-HT2C receptors, with additional H1-histamine and alpha1-adrenergic blocking activity. It is approved for major depressive disorder and is commonly used for insomnia. Notable adverse effects include sedation, orthostatic hypotension, and rare priapism.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Nesiritide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nesiritide is a recombinant B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) and vasodilator that activates natriuretic peptide receptor-A, increasing intracellular cGMP. It has been used intravenously for acute decompensated heart failure with dyspnea at rest or with minimal activity. A notable adverse effect is symptomatic hypotension, and it should be avoided in patients with cardiogenic shock or very low blood pressure.
tail_desc: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is a genetic heart muscle disorder characterized by unexplained left ventricular hypertrophy, usually from sarcomere protein gene mutations, causing diastolic dysfunction and sometimes left ventricular outflow obstruction. Patients, often adolescents or adults, may have dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or sudden cardiac death. Management includes activity guidance, beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers, and selected septal reduction or implantable defibrillators.

head: Dorzolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dorzolamide is a topical carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, a sulfonamide derivative, used primarily in ophthalmology. It inhibits carbonic anhydrase II in the ciliary processes, reducing aqueous humor formation and lowering intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is local ocular burning or stinging, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to sulfonamides.
tail_desc: Pulmonary emphysema is a chronic obstructive lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by permanent enlargement of distal air spaces and destruction of alveolar walls, causing loss of elastic recoil and airflow limitation. It occurs most often in smokers, or with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, and presents with progressive dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance, and hyperinflation; management emphasizes smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen when indicated, and selected advanced interventions.

head: Zidovudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zidovudine (azidothymidine, AZT) is a thymidine analogue nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and causes viral DNA chain termination. It is used, in combination regimens, for treatment of HIV infection and to reduce maternal-fetal transmission. A notable adverse effect is bone marrow suppression, especially anemia and neutropenia.
tail_desc: Hypertriglyceridemia is a metabolic lipid disorder affecting the cardiovascular and pancreatic systems, defined by elevated plasma triglycerides from primary genetic defects or secondary causes such as diabetes, obesity, alcohol use, or medications. It is often asymptomatic but can cause eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, and acute pancreatitis, especially at very high levels. Management targets underlying causes, lifestyle change, and triglyceride-lowering therapy.

head: Dalteparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dalteparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin. It is used for prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including perioperative and certain medical patients. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Obstructive jaundice is a hepatobiliary disorder caused by impaired bile flow from blockage of the extrahepatic or intrahepatic biliary tract, leading to conjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Typical features include jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, pruritus, and sometimes abdominal pain or cholangitis, in adults or children depending on cause. Management focuses on relieving obstruction and treating the underlying lesion, often with endoscopic, radiologic, or surgical intervention.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Cyclosporine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclosporine is a calcineurin-inhibiting immunosuppressant, a cyclic undecapeptide derived from fungi. It binds cyclophilin, and the complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase, reducing NFAT activation and T-cell cytokine transcription, especially interleukin-2. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection and to treat conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and severe dry eye. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Hypertriglyceridemia is a metabolic lipid disorder affecting the cardiovascular and pancreatic systems, defined by elevated plasma triglycerides from primary genetic defects or secondary causes such as diabetes, obesity, alcohol use, or medications. It is often asymptomatic but can cause eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, and acute pancreatitis, especially at very high levels. Management targets underlying causes, lifestyle change, and triglyceride-lowering therapy.

head: Propylthiouracil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Propylthiouracil is a thiourea antithyroid drug (thionamide) that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking iodination and coupling steps in thyroid hormone synthesis, and also inhibits peripheral conversion of thyroxine (T4) to triiodothyronine (T3). It is used to treat hyperthyroidism, including Graves disease and thyroid storm. A notable serious adverse effect is severe hepatotoxicity; agranulocytosis can also occur.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Selegiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Selegiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class. By inhibiting MAO-B, it reduces dopamine breakdown in the central nervous system. It is established for Parkinson disease and, in transdermal form, major depressive disorder. Notable risks include insomnia and potentially serious hypertensive or serotonergic interactions with contraindicated sympathomimetic or serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Tardive dyskinesia is a hyperkinetic movement disorder of the nervous system, usually caused by chronic exposure to dopamine receptor–blocking drugs, especially antipsychotics and some antiemetics, likely involving dopamine receptor supersensitivity in basal ganglia circuits. It causes persistent involuntary, repetitive movements, especially of the face, mouth, tongue, and sometimes trunk or limbs, mainly in patients receiving long-term neuroleptic therapy; management focuses on reducing offending drugs when feasible and using VMAT2 inhibitors.

head: Methionine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methionine is an essential sulfur-containing amino acid used as a nutritional supplement and urinary acidifier. It acts by serving as a metabolic precursor of S-adenosylmethionine and by acidifying urine through sulfate-generating metabolism rather than via a specific receptor target. Established uses include dietary supplementation in deficiency and urinary acidification. Excessive use may contribute to metabolic acidosis; caution is advised in severe liver disease.
tail_desc: Disorder of phenylalanine metabolism is an inborn error of amino acid metabolism affecting systemic and especially neurologic development, caused by impaired conversion of phenylalanine, most commonly from phenylalanine hydroxylase or tetrahydrobiopterin defects. It typically presents in infancy or through newborn screening with hyperphenylalaninemia and risk of developmental delay if untreated; management centers on lifelong phenylalanine restriction and selected cofactor-based therapies.

head: Saquinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Saquinavir is a peptidomimetic HIV protease inhibitor antiretroviral that selectively inhibits the HIV-1 and HIV-2 protease enzymes, preventing cleavage of viral polyprotein precursors and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, typically with ritonavir boosting, in combination therapy for HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is PR interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in patients with significant cardiac conduction disorders.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Felodipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is established for the treatment of hypertension and, in some settings, chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; it is generally contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug.
tail_desc: Hereditary angioedema is a rare inherited disorder of the complement/bradykinin pathway causing recurrent, nonpruritic swelling of the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and upper airway. It most often results from SERPING1 mutations causing C1 inhibitor deficiency or dysfunction. Symptoms usually begin in childhood or adolescence; management focuses on on-demand treatment of attacks, especially laryngeal edema, and prophylaxis to prevent recurrences.

head: Cloxacillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cloxacillin is an isoxazolyl, penicillinase-resistant beta-lactam penicillin antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for infections caused by susceptible penicillinase-producing staphylococci and other sensitive Gram-positive bacteria, including skin and soft-tissue infections. A notable adverse effect is cholestatic hepatitis, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to penicillins.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Cyclosporine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclosporine is a calcineurin-inhibiting immunosuppressant, a cyclic undecapeptide derived from fungi. It binds cyclophilin, and the complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase, reducing NFAT activation and T-cell cytokine transcription, especially interleukin-2. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection and to treat conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and severe dry eye. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Gemifloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Gemifloxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It has been used for treatment of acute bacterial exacerbations of chronic bronchitis and mild-to-moderate community-acquired pneumonia. A notable adverse effect is tendonitis and tendon rupture, and it is generally avoided in patients with known fluoroquinolone hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Amoxapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amoxapine is a dibenzoxazepine tricyclic antidepressant. It inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin and also antagonizes dopamine D2 receptors. It has been used for the treatment of depressive disorders, including anxiety-associated depression. A notable adverse effect is tardive dyskinesia or other extrapyramidal symptoms, and it is contraindicated during the acute recovery phase after myocardial infarction.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Isosorbide dinitrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isosorbide dinitrate is an organic nitrate vasodilator used mainly for prevention and treatment of angina pectoris and in some settings heart failure. It is bioactivated to nitric oxide, which stimulates soluble guanylate cyclase, increases cGMP, and relaxes vascular smooth muscle, predominantly reducing venous preload. A notable adverse effect is headache, and it is contraindicated with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors because of severe hypotension risk.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Aminosalicylic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminosalicylic acid, usually referring to para-aminosalicylic acid (PAS), is an aminosalicylate antibacterial and second-line antitubercular agent. It acts as a structural analog of para-aminobenzoic acid, inhibiting folate synthesis in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily through dihydropteroate synthase. It is used in multidrug-resistant tuberculosis regimens. Notable adverse effects include significant gastrointestinal intolerance and hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Vitamin B12 deficiency is a nutritional or malabsorptive disorder affecting hematologic and neurologic systems, caused by inadequate cobalamin intake, impaired absorption (for example pernicious anemia), or gastrointestinal disease. It commonly causes megaloblastic anemia, glossitis, paresthesias, gait disturbance, and cognitive changes, especially in older adults, vegans, or patients with malabsorption. Management involves identifying the cause and replacing vitamin B12, orally or parenterally.

head: Sulfamerazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamerazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial (“sulfa” drug) of the short- to intermediate-acting class that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby suppressing bacterial growth. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including urinary tract and respiratory infections. Important adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions such as rash, and it is generally avoided in patients with sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Acetohydroxamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acetohydroxamic acid is a hydroxamic acid urease inhibitor that targets bacterial urease, reducing hydrolysis of urea to ammonia and thereby lowering urinary alkalinization. It is used as adjunctive therapy in chronic urea-splitting urinary tract infections, often to help manage struvite stones. A notable adverse effect is hemolytic anemia; it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hemolytic anemia is a blood disorder of the hematologic system caused by premature destruction of red blood cells, leading to reduced oxygen-carrying capacity. It may be inherited or acquired, including immune-mediated forms. Typical features include fatigue, pallor, jaundice, dark urine, and splenomegaly, affecting both children and adults. Management targets the cause and may include transfusion, folate, corticosteroids, or splenectomy.

head: Vincristine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vincristine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds β-tubulin, inhibits microtubule polymerization, and arrests mitosis. It is used in combination chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia, lymphomas, and several pediatric solid tumors. A notable toxicity is dose-limiting peripheral neuropathy; intrathecal administration is contraindicated because it is uniformly fatal.
tail_desc: Benign recurrent intrahepatic cholestasis is a rare inherited hepatobiliary disorder causing intermittent episodes of intrahepatic cholestasis, usually from childhood or adolescence, without progressive liver damage between attacks. It is linked to defects in hepatocellular bile secretion, classically ATP8B1 or ABCB11 variants, and presents with recurrent jaundice, intense pruritus, and elevated bile acids. Management is mainly supportive and symptom directed, including antipruritic therapy and nutritional monitoring.

head: Diphenoxylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diphenoxylate is a synthetic opioid antidiarrheal, structurally related to meperidine, that acts mainly as a peripheral mu-opioid receptor agonist in the intestine to reduce gastrointestinal motility. It is used, typically with atropine, as adjunctive therapy for diarrhea. Notable risks include central nervous system and respiratory depression in overdose, and it is contraindicated in diarrhea caused by invasive enteric infection.
tail_desc: Dysentery is an acute infectious inflammatory disorder of the intestines, especially the colon, most often caused by Shigella bacteria or Entamoeba histolytica. It is characterized by frequent small-volume diarrhea containing blood and mucus, abdominal cramps, tenesmus, and fever, affecting both children and adults, particularly in poor-sanitation settings. Management centers on rehydration, electrolyte replacement, and targeted antimicrobial therapy when indicated.

head: Flucytosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flucytosine is a fluorinated pyrimidine antimetabolite antifungal. In susceptible fungi it is taken up by cytosine permease and converted to 5-fluorouracil, which inhibits thymidylate synthase and is incorporated into RNA, disrupting DNA and RNA synthesis. It is used mainly, usually with amphotericin B, for serious Candida and Cryptococcus infections such as cryptococcal meningitis. Bone marrow suppression is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Alvimopan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alvimopan is a peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor antagonist, structurally a selective opioid receptor blocker with limited central nervous system penetration. It counteracts opioid-induced inhibition of gastrointestinal motility by blocking peripheral MORs in the gut. It is approved to accelerate gastrointestinal recovery after partial large or small bowel resection with primary anastomosis. A notable contraindication is use in patients receiving therapeutic opioids for more than seven consecutive days before treatment.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Propylthiouracil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Propylthiouracil is a thiourea antithyroid drug (thionamide) that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking iodination and coupling steps in thyroid hormone synthesis, and also inhibits peripheral conversion of thyroxine (T4) to triiodothyronine (T3). It is used to treat hyperthyroidism, including Graves disease and thyroid storm. A notable serious adverse effect is severe hepatotoxicity; agranulocytosis can also occur.
tail_desc: congenital neutropenia-myelofibrosis-nephromegaly syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Tamsulosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tamsulosin is a selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist, chemically a sulfonamide derivative, that preferentially blocks alpha-1A and alpha-1D receptors in prostatic and bladder neck smooth muscle to improve urinary flow. It is established for treatment of signs and symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension, and it can also cause abnormal ejaculation.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Dalteparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dalteparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin. It is used for prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including perioperative and certain medical patients. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Infective endocarditis is a microbial infection of the endocardial surface, usually involving heart valves within the cardiovascular system. It is typically caused by bacteremia seeding damaged or prosthetic valves, forming infected vegetations. Patients present with fever, new or changing murmur, and embolic or immunologic phenomena, especially those with valve disease, prosthetic valves, or injection drug use; management requires prolonged targeted intravenous antimicrobials and often valve surgery.

head: Duloxetine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Duloxetine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is approved for major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, diabetic peripheral neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, and chronic musculoskeletal pain. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity; concomitant use with monoamine oxidase inhibitors is contraindicated.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Hydroflumethiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroflumethiazide is a thiazide diuretic (benzothiadiazine derivative) that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Cholecystolithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, specifically stones within the gallbladder, usually formed by precipitation of cholesterol or pigment in bile. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic patients develop episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and complications can include cholecystitis or biliary obstruction. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic disease.

head: Etacrynic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Etacrynic acid is a non-sulfonamide loop diuretic of the aryloxyacetic acid class that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter (NKCC2, SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of Henle, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat edema associated with heart failure, renal disease, or hepatic cirrhosis. A notable adverse effect is ototoxicity, especially with high doses or intravenous use.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Povidone-iodine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Povidone-iodine is an iodophor antiseptic, a complex of polyvinylpyrrolidone with iodine, that slowly releases free iodine to oxidize and iodinate proteins, nucleotides, and membrane lipids of bacteria, fungi, viruses, and protozoa. It is used topically for skin disinfection, wound cleansing, and preoperative antisepsis. Notable adverse effects include local irritation and, rarely, iodine-induced thyroid dysfunction.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Tyramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tyramine is a naturally occurring trace biogenic amine, derived from tyrosine, that acts mainly as an indirect sympathomimetic by entering adrenergic nerve terminals via the norepinephrine transporter and promoting norepinephrine release. It is not an approved therapeutic drug or established indication. Excess tyramine can precipitate severe hypertension, particularly in patients taking monoamine oxidase inhibitors, making MAOI use a key contraindication to high tyramine exposure.
tail_desc: Disorder of phenylalanine metabolism is an inborn error of amino acid metabolism affecting systemic and especially neurologic development, caused by impaired conversion of phenylalanine, most commonly from phenylalanine hydroxylase or tetrahydrobiopterin defects. It typically presents in infancy or through newborn screening with hyperphenylalaninemia and risk of developmental delay if untreated; management centers on lifelong phenylalanine restriction and selected cofactor-based therapies.

head: Baclofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Baclofen is a centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant and antispastic agent, structurally a GABA analogue that acts primarily as a GABA_B receptor agonist, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release in the spinal cord. It is used to treat spasticity, including that associated with multiple sclerosis and spinal cord lesions. Notable adverse effects include sedation and dizziness; abrupt withdrawal can cause serious neurologic symptoms.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Degarelix (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Degarelix is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) receptor antagonist that competitively blocks pituitary GnRH receptors, rapidly suppressing luteinizing hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, and testosterone without an initial flare. It is approved for advanced hormone-sensitive prostate cancer. A notable adverse effect is injection-site reactions; it can also prolong the QT interval.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Thiothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiothixene is a first-generation (typical) antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class. Its antipsychotic effects are primarily attributed to antagonism of central dopamine D2 receptors, with additional activity at other neurotransmitter receptors. It is used for the treatment of schizophrenia. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including drug-induced parkinsonism and akathisia.
tail_desc: Constitutional neutropenia is a benign, usually lifelong form of chronic neutropenia affecting the hematologic/immune system, characterized by persistently low absolute neutrophil counts without an acquired cause or major marrow pathology. It is often seen in otherwise healthy individuals, including some ethnic groups, and typically causes no or few infections. Management is mainly reassurance, exclusion of secondary causes, and clinical monitoring rather than routine treatment.

head: Acrivastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acrivastine is a second-generation antihistamine of the alkylamine/propylamine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used for symptomatic relief of seasonal allergic rhinitis and urticaria, including sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itching. Compared with older antihistamines it is less sedating, but drowsiness can still occur; caution is advised in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic disease in which the peripheral iris obstructs the anterior chamber angle, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid or intermittent rise in intraocular pressure. It typically affects anatomically predisposed older adults, especially those with shallow anterior chambers. Features include severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos, nausea, and a red eye; management is urgent pressure lowering followed by laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Naratriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naratriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It treats acute migraine attacks, with or without aura, by causing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. Notable adverse effects include paresthesia, dizziness, and chest or throat tightness; it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease and certain cerebrovascular syndromes.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Heparin, bovine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin, bovine, is an animal-derived unfractionated heparin, a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant obtained from bovine tissues. It potentiates antithrombin III, accelerating inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism and other settings requiring anticoagulation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a rare inherited platelet function disorder of the hemostatic system caused by deficiency or dysfunction of the platelet integrin αIIbβ3 (glycoprotein IIb/IIIa), leading to impaired platelet aggregation. It typically presents from childhood with mucocutaneous bleeding such as epistaxis, easy bruising, gingival bleeding, and menorrhagia. Management focuses on bleeding prevention and control with local measures, antifibrinolytics, platelet transfusion, and sometimes recombinant factor VIIa.

head: Chloramphenicol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloramphenicol is a broad-spectrum amphenicol antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocking peptidyl transferase activity. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including meningitis, rickettsial infections, and topical treatment of bacterial conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is dose-independent aplastic anemia; use is also limited by gray baby syndrome in neonates.
tail_desc: Idiopathic aplastic anemia is an acquired bone marrow failure disorder of the hematopoietic system in which immune-mediated destruction or suppression of hematopoietic stem cells leads to hypocellular marrow and pancytopenia without an identifiable cause. Patients of any age present with fatigue, infections, and bleeding or bruising. Management centers on supportive transfusions and infection prevention, with immunosuppressive therapy or allogeneic stem cell transplantation in appropriate candidates.

head: Potassium gluconate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Potassium gluconate is an oral potassium salt and electrolyte replacement used to prevent or treat hypokalemia. It dissociates to supply potassium, the major intracellular cation, helping restore normal membrane potential and neuromuscular and cardiac conduction; it has no specific receptor target. Important adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation, and it is contraindicated in hyperkalemia or severe renal impairment because of arrhythmia risk.
tail_desc: Hyperkalemic periodic paralysis is a rare inherited skeletal muscle channelopathy causing episodic weakness or paralysis. It is usually autosomal dominant and most often results from SCN4A sodium-channel mutations that impair muscle membrane excitability. Attacks often begin in childhood, are triggered by rest after exercise, fasting, or potassium intake, and may include myotonia. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, acute carbohydrate or mild exercise, and preventive agents such as thiazides or acetazolamide.

head: Isosorbide dinitrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isosorbide dinitrate is an organic nitrate vasodilator used mainly for prevention and treatment of angina pectoris and in some settings heart failure. It is bioactivated to nitric oxide, which stimulates soluble guanylate cyclase, increases cGMP, and relaxes vascular smooth muscle, predominantly reducing venous preload. A notable adverse effect is headache, and it is contraindicated with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors because of severe hypotension risk.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Hydroxyamphetamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxyamphetamine is a sympathomimetic amine and indirect-acting adrenergic agonist, structurally related to amphetamine, that promotes release of norepinephrine from postganglionic sympathetic nerve terminals. Ophthalmically, it is used diagnostically to aid localization of Horner syndrome and can produce mydriasis. A notable adverse effect is precipitation of angle-closure glaucoma, so it is contraindicated in patients with narrow anterior chamber angles.
tail_desc: Sudden cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which abrupt loss of effective cardiac output, usually from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, causes sudden collapse, unresponsiveness, and absent pulse. It can occur in adults with structural heart disease or acute ischemia, and requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and urgent post-resuscitation care.

head: Nitazoxanide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitazoxanide is a synthetic thiazolide antiparasitic and broad-spectrum anti-infective prodrug, rapidly converted to tizoxanide. It disrupts the pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase (PFOR) enzyme–dependent electron transfer pathway essential for anaerobic energy metabolism. It is approved for treatment of diarrhea caused by Giardia lamblia and Cryptosporidium parvum. Common adverse effects include abdominal pain, nausea, and headache.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Bendamustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bendamustine is a bifunctional alkylating antineoplastic agent of the nitrogen mustard class with a benzimidazole ring. It forms DNA crosslinks, causing DNA damage and apoptosis, without a single specific molecular target. It is used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia and indolent B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma, including rituximab-refractory disease. Notable toxicities include myelosuppression and serious infections.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Ciprofloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including urinary tract infections, gastrointestinal infections, skin infections, and certain respiratory infections, and for post-exposure anthrax. A notable adverse effect is tendinitis and tendon rupture; it is generally avoided in patients with prior fluoroquinolone-associated tendon disorders.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Flavoxate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flavoxate is a synthetic antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, chemically a flavone derivative, used to relieve symptoms of overactive or irritable bladder such as urgency, frequency, dysuria, and suprapubic pain. Its effect is attributed mainly to smooth-muscle relaxation with weak muscarinic receptor antagonism; the precise primary molecular target is not well defined. Notable adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, and it is contraindicated in urinary retention.
tail_desc: Urinary tract obstruction is a genitourinary disorder in which partial or complete blockage of urine flow occurs anywhere from the renal pelvis to the urethra, causing increased upstream pressure, hydronephrosis, infection risk, and potential renal impairment. Symptoms may include flank or abdominal pain, lower urinary tract symptoms, anuria, hematuria, or recurrent urinary infection. Management focuses on urgent decompression when indicated and treatment of the underlying cause.

head: Disulfiram (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Disulfiram is a thiuram disulfide aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor used in alcohol aversion therapy for alcohol use disorder. It irreversibly inhibits hepatic aldehyde dehydrogenase, causing acetaldehyde accumulation after ethanol ingestion and an unpleasant reaction. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated with concurrent alcohol exposure because it can provoke severe disulfiram-ethanol reactions.
tail_desc: Schizoaffective disorder is a chronic psychiatric disorder affecting the brain, defined by schizophrenia-spectrum psychosis occurring with prominent mood episodes (depressive and/or manic). Its cause is multifactorial and incompletely understood, involving genetic and neurobiologic vulnerability. Patients typically have delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking, plus mood symptoms and functional impairment. Management generally combines antipsychotic medication, mood stabilizers or antidepressants when indicated, and psychotherapy with psychosocial support.

head: Ticlopidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ticlopidine is a thienopyridine antiplatelet agent that irreversibly inhibits ADP-mediated platelet aggregation by blocking the platelet P2Y12 receptor. It has been used to reduce thrombotic stroke risk and to prevent subacute stent thrombosis, particularly when aspirin is unsuitable. A notable serious adverse effect is neutropenia, and it can also cause thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura.
tail_desc: Familial intrahepatic cholestasis is a rare inherited hepatobiliary disorder causing impaired bile secretion within the liver, usually due to genetic defects in hepatocellular bile transport. It typically presents in infancy or childhood with recurrent or persistent cholestasis, jaundice, severe pruritus, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, and growth impairment. Management is supportive and subtype-specific, including nutritional supplementation, antipruritic/choleretic therapy, and sometimes liver transplantation.

head: Romidepsin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Romidepsin is a cyclic peptide histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor prodrug that, after intracellular reduction, inhibits class I HDAC enzymes, increasing histone acetylation and altering gene expression to promote apoptosis. It is approved for relapsed or refractory cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and peripheral T-cell lymphoma. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation, requiring caution in patients with significant cardiac disease or electrolyte abnormalities.
tail_desc: Chronic kidney disease is a progressive disorder of the renal system defined by persistent kidney damage or reduced glomerular filtration for at least three months, commonly caused by diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. It often affects older adults and may present with albuminuria, edema, anemia, hypertension, and electrolyte abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause, slowing progression, reducing cardiovascular risk, and preparing for dialysis or transplantation in advanced stages.

head: Methadyl acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadyl acetate (levomethadyl acetate, LAAM) is a long-acting diphenylheptane opioid and full agonist at the μ-opioid receptor. It was used for maintenance treatment of opioid dependence as an alternative to methadone. A notable safety concern is QT-interval prolongation with risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, making it contraindicated in patients with known QT prolongation or significant cardiac rhythm disorders.
tail_desc: Cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder in the biliary system, most often caused by gallstone obstruction of the cystic duct; less commonly it is acalculous in critically ill patients. It typically presents with right upper quadrant pain, fever, nausea, and Murphy sign, sometimes with leukocytosis. Management usually includes supportive care, antibiotics when infection is suspected, and cholecystectomy.

head: Escitalopram (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Escitalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, the S-enantiomer of citalopram, that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT) to increase synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety disorder. A notable adverse effect is QT interval prolongation, and it should be used cautiously with other serotonergic or QT-prolonging drugs.
tail_desc: Serotonin syndrome is an acute drug-induced toxidrome of the central and peripheral nervous systems caused by excess serotonergic activity, typically after serotonergic medication use, overdose, or interactions. It is characterized by altered mental status, autonomic hyperactivity, and neuromuscular abnormalities such as clonus, hyperreflexia, and tremor. Management centers on stopping serotonergic agents, supportive care, benzodiazepines, and severe-case escalation.

head: Dichlorophen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dichlorophen is a halogenated bisphenol anthelmintic and taeniacide formerly used mainly in veterinary medicine. It acts locally in the intestinal lumen against cestodes, though its precise molecular target is not well established. Established use has included treatment of tapeworm infections in animals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation such as vomiting and diarrhea, especially with overdosing.
tail_desc: X-linked adrenal hypoplasia congenita is a rare inherited primary adrenal insufficiency caused by pathogenic variants in NR0B1 (DAX1), leading to underdevelopment of the adrenal cortex and often associated hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. It typically affects boys, presenting in infancy or childhood with vomiting, dehydration, hypoglycemia, salt wasting, and failure to thrive; management centers on lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement and later endocrine care for pubertal and reproductive issues.

head: Choline magnesium trisalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Choline magnesium trisalicylate is a nonacetylated salicylate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for relief of mild to moderate pain, fever, and inflammatory disorders such as arthritis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal irritation or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in salicylate hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Prazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic, structurally a 1,4-benzodiazepine derivative, used mainly for the short-term treatment of anxiety disorders. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing GABA-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated in myasthenia gravis and severe respiratory insufficiency.
tail_desc: Personality disorder is a group of mental disorders affecting psychological and behavioral functioning, characterized by enduring, inflexible patterns of cognition, emotion regulation, interpersonal behavior, and impulse control that deviate from cultural norms. It typically begins by adolescence or early adulthood and causes distress or impairment. Management usually centers on structured psychotherapy, with medications used mainly for comorbid symptoms or specific behavioral targets.

head: Dobutamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dobutamine is a synthetic catecholamine inotropic agent that primarily stimulates cardiac beta-1 adrenergic receptors, increasing myocardial contractility and stroke volume with less effect on heart rate than isoproterenol. It is used for short-term treatment of cardiac decompensation due to heart failure or cardiac surgery and in pharmacologic stress testing. A notable adverse effect is tachyarrhythmia.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Diclofenamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diclofenamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor of the sulfonamide class. It reduces aqueous humor formation by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase, primarily in the ciliary processes of the eye. It is used to lower intraocular pressure in glaucoma, including chronic open-angle glaucoma. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Adrenal insufficiency is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis characterized by deficient cortisol production, with or without aldosterone deficiency, due to primary adrenal failure or secondary/tertiary ACTH suppression. It causes fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, gastrointestinal symptoms, and sometimes hyperpigmentation; adrenal crisis may occur. Management centers on glucocorticoid replacement, mineralocorticoid replacement in primary disease, and treatment of the underlying cause.

head: Lamivudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lamivudine is a synthetic nucleoside analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI), a cytidine analogue, that is phosphorylated intracellularly and inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus DNA polymerase, causing chain termination. It is used to treat HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable risk is severe lactic acidosis with hepatomegaly and steatosis; abrupt discontinuation in hepatitis B can trigger severe exacerbations.
tail_desc: Lactic acidosis is a metabolic acidosis affecting systemic acid-base balance, caused by accumulation of lactate from tissue hypoxia or impaired lactate metabolism, including sepsis, shock, liver failure, or certain drugs. Patients may develop tachypnea, nausea, weakness, confusion, and hypotension, often in critically ill adults. Management focuses on urgent treatment of the underlying cause, restoration of oxygen delivery and perfusion, and supportive care.

head: Sildenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sildenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a pyrazolopyrimidinone derivative, that blocks PDE5 and increases cGMP-mediated smooth muscle relaxation, especially in the corpus cavernosum and pulmonary vasculature. It is approved for erectile dysfunction and pulmonary arterial hypertension. A notable contraindication is concomitant nitrate therapy, which can cause potentially severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Plasma cell myeloma, or multiple myeloma, is a malignant plasma-cell neoplasm of the bone marrow and hematologic system characterized by clonal immunoglobulin-producing cells and monoclonal protein. It typically affects older adults and causes bone pain or lytic lesions, anemia, renal impairment, hypercalcemia, and recurrent infections. Management usually combines systemic anti-myeloma therapy, supportive care, and, in eligible patients, autologous stem-cell transplantation.

head: Phendimetrazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phendimetrazine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, chemically related to phenethylamines and used short term as an adjunct in exogenous obesity management. It acts mainly as a prodrug of phenmetrazine, increasing central norepinephrine release and suppressing appetite. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or shortly after MAOI therapy.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Sertraline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sertraline is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant of the naphthylamine/tetralin class that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, panic disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, social anxiety disorder, and premenstrual dysphoric disorder. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with monoamine oxidase inhibitors, which are contraindicated.
tail_desc: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by recurrent episodes of mania or hypomania and depression. Its cause is multifactorial, involving genetic susceptibility and dysregulation of brain neurotransmitter and circadian systems.
