It typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood and is managed with mood stabilizers, atypical antipsychotics, psychotherapy, and relapse prevention.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Abacavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Abacavir is a carbocyclic guanosine analogue nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase by competing with natural nucleotides and causing DNA chain termination. It is used in combination therapy for HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is potentially fatal hypersensitivity reaction, strongly associated with the HLA-B*57:01 allele, making screening essential before use.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Triamterene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triamterene is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pteridine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal renal tubule and collecting duct, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for edema and hypertension, often with a thiazide diuretic. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria, severe renal impairment, or preexisting hyperkalemia.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Lepirudin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lepirudin is a recombinant hirudin derivative and direct thrombin inhibitor that binds both free and clot-bound thrombin (factor IIa), preventing fibrin formation and thrombin-mediated coagulation. It was used as an anticoagulant for patients with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, including those requiring anticoagulation. A notable risk is major bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to hirudins.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Sodium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium acetate is an alkalinizing electrolyte salt and acetate donor used in parenteral nutrition and intravenous fluid therapy. After metabolism, acetate is converted primarily in muscle and other tissues to bicarbonate, helping correct metabolic acidosis; it has no single receptor target. Established uses include correction of hyponatremia and acidosis in compounded IV solutions. Excess administration can cause metabolic alkalosis.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis is a urinary tract disorder characterized by formation of calculi in the kidneys, usually from supersaturation and crystallization of minerals such as calcium, uric acid, or cystine. It commonly causes renal colic, hematuria, nausea, and urinary obstruction, especially in adults. Management focuses on analgesia, hydration, facilitating stone passage, metabolic evaluation, and urologic removal when stones are large, obstructing, or infected.

head: Rasagiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rasagiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class that increases central dopamine by inhibiting MAO-B. It is approved for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease, as monotherapy or adjunct to levodopa. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome when combined with serotonergic drugs; it is also contraindicated with other MAO inhibitors.
tail_desc: Drug-induced dyskinesia is a medication-related movement disorder of the central nervous system, most often caused by chronic exposure to dopamine receptor–blocking drugs or, in Parkinson disease, levodopa. It features involuntary choreiform, athetoid, or stereotyped movements, commonly affecting the face, tongue, trunk, or limbs, especially in older adults and long-treated patients. Management centers on reducing or changing the offending drug and symptomatic treatment when needed.

head: Tenofovir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tenofovir is an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor, used clinically as the prodrugs tenofovir disoproxil and tenofovir alafenamide. After intracellular activation to tenofovir diphosphate, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase by competing with natural nucleotides and causing DNA chain termination. It is approved for HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B; notable risks include renal toxicity and reduced bone mineral density.
tail_desc: Lactic acidosis is a metabolic acidosis affecting systemic acid-base balance, caused by accumulation of lactate from tissue hypoxia or impaired lactate metabolism, including sepsis, shock, liver failure, or certain drugs. Patients may develop tachypnea, nausea, weakness, confusion, and hypotension, often in critically ill adults. Management focuses on urgent treatment of the underlying cause, restoration of oxygen delivery and perfusion, and supportive care.

head: Maprotiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Maprotiline is a tetracyclic antidepressant that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake by blocking the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively weak effects on serotonin uptake. It has been used for depressive disorders, including major depression with anxiety features. A notable risk is dose-related seizures, and it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or during the acute recovery phase after myocardial infarction.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Bismuth subsalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bismuth subsalicylate is a bismuth-containing salicylate antidiarrheal and gastroprotective agent. It acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract, where bismuth has antimicrobial and mucosal protective effects, while salicylate inhibits cyclooxygenase and reduces intestinal prostaglandin-mediated secretion and inflammation. It is used for diarrhea, dyspepsia, heartburn, and nausea. A notable adverse effect is black discoloration of stools and tongue.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Inositol nicotinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Inositol nicotinate is a nicotinic acid ester (hexanicotinate of inositol) used as a peripheral vasodilator. It acts as a slow-release source of niacin, although its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well defined beyond promoting vasodilation. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of peripheral vascular disorders such as intermittent claudication and Raynaud-type symptoms. Flushing is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Hyperglycemia is a metabolic abnormality of the endocrine system characterized by elevated blood glucose, usually due to insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, most commonly in diabetes mellitus. It may cause polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision, and weight loss, and occurs in people with diabetes or acute illness. Management focuses on correcting the cause, hydration, glucose monitoring, and insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy.

head: Romidepsin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Romidepsin is a cyclic peptide histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor prodrug that, after intracellular reduction, inhibits class I HDAC enzymes, increasing histone acetylation and altering gene expression to promote apoptosis. It is approved for relapsed or refractory cutaneous T-cell lymphoma and peripheral T-cell lymphoma. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation, requiring caution in patients with significant cardiac disease or electrolyte abnormalities.
tail_desc: Long QT syndrome is a cardiac electrical disorder affecting ventricular repolarization, characterized by prolongation of the QT interval on electrocardiography, usually due to inherited ion-channel defects or acquired drug/electrolyte effects. It predisposes children or adults to syncope, seizures, and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia including torsades de pointes, with risk of sudden death; management emphasizes trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, correction of reversible causes, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Lamivudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lamivudine is a synthetic nucleoside analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI), a cytidine analogue, that is phosphorylated intracellularly and inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus DNA polymerase, causing chain termination. It is used to treat HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable risk is severe lactic acidosis with hepatomegaly and steatosis; abrupt discontinuation in hepatitis B can trigger severe exacerbations.
tail_desc: Myopathy is a heterogeneous group of disorders of skeletal muscle, affecting the neuromuscular system. It results from inherited or acquired structural, metabolic, inflammatory, toxic, or endocrine abnormalities that impair muscle fiber function. Typical features include proximal muscle weakness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, myalgias, and sometimes elevated creatine kinase, occurring in children or adults. Management targets the cause and may include immunotherapy, stopping offending drugs, treating endocrine disease, rehabilitation, and supportive care.

head: Zolmitriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zolmitriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, an indole/tryptamine derivative that acts as a selective 5-HT1B/5-HT1D serotonin receptor agonist, producing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for the acute treatment of migraine attacks, with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease because triptans can cause chest tightness and coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Carcinoid syndrome is a neuroendocrine paraneoplastic syndrome, usually affecting the gastrointestinal and cardiopulmonary systems, caused by serotonin and other vasoactive substances released from metastatic carcinoid tumors, classically midgut neuroendocrine tumors with liver metastases. It typically causes episodic flushing, secretory diarrhea, bronchospasm, and right-sided valvular heart disease. Management focuses on tumor control and symptom reduction, especially with somatostatin analogs and treatment of metastatic disease.

head: Candesartan cilexetil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Candesartan cilexetil is an orally administered prodrug of the angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) candesartan, a benzimidazole derivative that selectively antagonizes the AT1 receptor. It is used for hypertension and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. A notable risk is hyperkalemia; like other renin-angiotensin system blockers, it is contraindicated during pregnancy due to fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Osteitis fibrosa is a metabolic bone disorder of the skeleton, usually caused by hyperparathyroidism with excess parathyroid hormone driving increased osteoclastic bone resorption and replacement by fibrous tissue. It can cause bone pain, fragility fractures, deformity, and “brown tumors,” classically in patients with severe primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism. Management targets the underlying hyperparathyroidism and correction of calcium-phosphate abnormalities.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Amifostine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amifostine is a cytoprotective thiophosphate prodrug dephosphorylated by alkaline phosphatase to the active free thiol WR-1065, which scavenges reactive oxygen species and donates hydrogen ions to protect normal tissues from cisplatin- and radiation-induced DNA damage. It is used to reduce cumulative nephrotoxicity from cisplatin and xerostomia during head and neck radiotherapy. Notable adverse effects include severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Cerebrovascular disorder is a broad category of diseases affecting the brain’s blood vessels and cerebral circulation, including ischemic and hemorrhagic conditions. It results from vessel occlusion, rupture, or structural abnormalities that impair blood flow or cause bleeding. Typical features are sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, dizziness, or altered consciousness, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent diagnosis, restoring or controlling cerebral perfusion, treating the cause, and preventing recurrence.

head: Desvenlafaxine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desvenlafaxine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant and the major active metabolite of venlafaxine. It acts primarily by inhibiting the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic serotonin and norepinephrine. It is approved for major depressive disorder. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and the contraindication of concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Telavancin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telavancin is a semisynthetic lipoglycopeptide antibiotic, a vancomycin derivative, that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding the D-Ala-D-Ala termini of peptidoglycan precursors and also disrupts bacterial membrane potential and permeability. It is approved for complicated skin and skin structure infections and hospital-acquired/ventilator-associated bacterial pneumonia due to susceptible Gram-positive pathogens. Notable risks include nephrotoxicity and fetal harm; avoid use in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadiazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections and, with pyrimethamine, for toxoplasmosis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe rash such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome; it is generally contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy and near term pregnancy or in neonates due to kernicterus risk.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Meclofenamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meclofenamic acid is a fenamate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the anthranilic acid class. It nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It has been used to treat mild to moderate pain, primary dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in aspirin-sensitive NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Urea (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Urea is a small endogenous diamide and topical keratolytic/humectant. In dermatologic preparations it acts non-specifically by disrupting hydrogen bonding in keratin and increasing water retention in the stratum corneum, without a defined primary molecular target. It is used topically to soften and debride hyperkeratotic skin and nails, including calluses, corns, ichthyosis, xerosis, and some nail dystrophies. A notable adverse effect is local skin irritation or burning.
tail_desc: Otitis media is an inflammatory and usually infectious disease of the middle ear, within the auditory system, commonly following eustachian tube dysfunction during upper respiratory infection. It is especially frequent in young children and typically causes ear pain, fever, irritability, and reduced hearing, sometimes with middle-ear effusion. Management includes analgesia, observation or antibiotics when indicated, and addressing recurrent or persistent effusions.

head: Sevelamer (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sevelamer is a non-absorbed, polymeric phosphate binder (available as hydrochloride or carbonate) that binds dietary phosphate in the gastrointestinal tract via ionic interactions, reducing intestinal phosphate absorption without a specific systemic molecular target. It is used to control hyperphosphatemia in patients with chronic kidney disease, especially those on dialysis. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, including constipation and possible bowel obstruction.
tail_desc: Gastroparesis is a chronic gastrointestinal motility disorder in which delayed gastric emptying occurs without mechanical obstruction, usually due to impaired gastric neuromuscular function, often related to diabetes, postsurgical vagal injury, or idiopathic causes. Typical features include early satiety, postprandial fullness, nausea, vomiting, bloating, and weight loss. Management emphasizes dietary modification, treating underlying causes, antiemetics, and prokinetic therapy, with nutritional support or procedures for refractory cases.

head: Chlorphentermine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorphentermine is a sympathomimetic anorectic of the amphetamine/phenethylamine family formerly used as an anti-obesity agent. It acts primarily by increasing central monoaminergic signaling, especially promoting norepinephrine release to suppress appetite, though its precise molecular targets are not well defined. Its clinical use has largely been abandoned because of serious pulmonary toxicity, notably phospholipidosis.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Cholic Acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cholic acid is a primary bile acid used as a bile acid replacement therapy. It acts by restoring the endogenous bile acid pool and activating the farnesoid X receptor (FXR), which suppresses bile acid synthesis. It is indicated for bile acid synthesis disorders due to single-enzyme defects and as adjunctive treatment of peroxisomal disorders including Zellweger spectrum disorders. Diarrhea is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Familial intrahepatic cholestasis is a rare inherited hepatobiliary disorder causing impaired bile secretion within the liver, usually due to genetic defects in hepatocellular bile transport. It typically presents in infancy or childhood with recurrent or persistent cholestasis, jaundice, severe pruritus, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, and growth impairment. Management is supportive and subtype-specific, including nutritional supplementation, antipruritic/choleretic therapy, and sometimes liver transplantation.

head: Racepinephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Racepinephrine is a racemic form of epinephrine, a sympathomimetic catecholamine acting as a nonselective agonist at alpha- and beta-adrenergic receptors. By causing mucosal vasoconstriction and bronchodilation, it is used as an inhaled agent for temporary relief of mild intermittent asthma symptoms and to reduce airway edema, including in croup. Notable adverse effects include tachycardia and other cardiac arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Ifosfamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ifosfamide is an oxazaphosphorine alkylating agent, structurally related to cyclophosphamide. It is a prodrug activated by hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes to metabolites that form DNA interstrand and intrastrand cross-links, impairing replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used for sarcomas, testicular cancer, and some lymphomas; notable toxicities include hemorrhagic cystitis and encephalopathy.
tail_desc: Hyperbilirubinemia is a metabolic disorder of the hepatobiliary system characterized by elevated bilirubin in blood, caused by increased bilirubin production, impaired hepatic uptake or conjugation, or reduced biliary excretion. It commonly presents with jaundice and scleral icterus, and in neonates may cause kernicterus if severe. Management targets the cause and bilirubin reduction, including phototherapy, exchange transfusion, or treating liver or hemolytic disease.

head: Felbamate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felbamate is a dicarbamate antiepileptic drug. Its anticonvulsant activity is attributed mainly to inhibition of NMDA receptor–mediated excitatory transmission, particularly at the glycine site, with additional potentiation of GABAergic inhibition. It is approved for focal seizures and for seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. A notable serious toxicity is aplastic anemia, with risk of acute hepatic failure.
tail_desc: Porphyrin metabolism disease refers to disorders of heme biosynthesis or porphyrin handling, affecting the hematologic and hepatic systems and often termed porphyrias. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and age. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, supportive care, and subtype-specific measures such as hemin for acute hepatic attacks.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: Incessant infant ventricular tachycardia is a rare pediatric cardiac arrhythmia in which sustained or frequently recurring ventricular tachycardia arises during infancy, affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system. It typically presents with persistent tachycardia, poor feeding, irritability, pallor, heart failure, or tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. Management is directed at urgent rhythm control, antiarrhythmic therapy, evaluation for structural or metabolic causes, and supportive cardiac care.

head: Aminosalicylic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminosalicylic acid, usually referring to para-aminosalicylic acid (PAS), is an aminosalicylate antibacterial and second-line antitubercular agent. It acts as a structural analog of para-aminobenzoic acid, inhibiting folate synthesis in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily through dihydropteroate synthase. It is used in multidrug-resistant tuberculosis regimens. Notable adverse effects include significant gastrointestinal intolerance and hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Galactosemia is an inherited metabolic disorder affecting carbohydrate metabolism, usually caused by deficiency of enzymes needed to process galactose, classically galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase. It typically presents in newborns after milk feeding with poor feeding, vomiting, jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, cataracts, and risk of sepsis. Management is prompt lifelong dietary restriction of galactose/lactose and monitoring for complications.

head: Felbamate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felbamate is a dicarbamate antiepileptic drug. Its anticonvulsant activity is attributed mainly to inhibition of NMDA receptor–mediated excitatory transmission, particularly at the glycine site, with additional potentiation of GABAergic inhibition. It is approved for focal seizures and for seizures associated with Lennox-Gastaut syndrome. A notable serious toxicity is aplastic anemia, with risk of acute hepatic failure.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Saquinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Saquinavir is a peptidomimetic HIV protease inhibitor antiretroviral that selectively inhibits the HIV-1 and HIV-2 protease enzymes, preventing cleavage of viral polyprotein precursors and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, typically with ritonavir boosting, in combination therapy for HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is PR interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in patients with significant cardiac conduction disorders.
tail_desc: Cirrhosis of the liver is a chronic end-stage liver disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by diffuse fibrosis and regenerative nodules that distort hepatic architecture, most often from chronic alcohol use, viral hepatitis, or nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. Patients may be asymptomatic initially, then develop jaundice, ascites, portal hypertension, variceal bleeding, and encephalopathy. Management targets the cause, complications, nutrition, and possible liver transplantation.

head: Tobramycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tobramycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing mistranslation and bactericidal activity, particularly against aerobic Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections and as inhaled therapy in cystic fibrosis airway infection. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Adult-onset myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system, usually caused by antibodies against acetylcholine receptor or related postsynaptic proteins, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It presents in adults with fluctuating, fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunotherapy, and thymectomy in selected patients.

head: Triazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic that positively modulates GABA\(_A\) receptors by binding the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include anterograde amnesia and next-day psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inhibitors.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Salbutamol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salbutamol (albuterol) is a short-acting β2-adrenergic receptor agonist bronchodilator, chemically a phenethylamine derivative. By selectively stimulating β2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, it increases cyclic AMP and produces rapid bronchodilation. It is used for relief and prevention of bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, including exercise-induced bronchospasm. A notable adverse effect is tremor.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Degarelix (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Degarelix is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) receptor antagonist that competitively blocks pituitary GnRH receptors, rapidly suppressing luteinizing hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, and testosterone without an initial flare. It is approved for advanced hormone-sensitive prostate cancer. A notable adverse effect is injection-site reactions; it can also prolong the QT interval.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Cycloserine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cycloserine is an oral second-line antitubercular antibiotic, a cyclic analog of D-alanine. It inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by targeting alanine racemase and D-alanine:D-alanine ligase. It is used in combination regimens for active tuberculosis, especially drug-resistant disease. A notable adverse effect is dose-related central nervous system toxicity, including seizures and psychosis.
tail_desc: Schizoaffective disorder is a chronic psychiatric disorder affecting the brain, defined by schizophrenia-spectrum psychosis occurring with prominent mood episodes (depressive and/or manic). Its cause is multifactorial and incompletely understood, involving genetic and neurobiologic vulnerability. Patients typically have delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking, plus mood symptoms and functional impairment. Management generally combines antipsychotic medication, mood stabilizers or antidepressants when indicated, and psychotherapy with psychosocial support.

head: Antipyrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Antipyrine (phenazone) is a pyrazolone derivative and non-opioid analgesic/antipyretic. Its effects are attributed to inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis via cyclooxygenase pathways, although it is not used for a highly selective molecular target. It has been used for pain and fever and, in otic combinations, for symptomatic relief of ear pain. Hypersensitivity reactions, including rash, can occur.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Halofantrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Halofantrine is a phenanthrene-methanol antimalarial related to quinoline compounds, used for treatment of acute, uncomplicated malaria, especially due to Plasmodium falciparum. Its precise molecular target is not fully defined, but it is thought to interfere with parasite heme detoxification in the food vacuole. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious ventricular arrhythmias, making it contraindicated in patients with cardiac conduction abnormalities.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Rabeprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rabeprazole is a substituted benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, healing and maintenance of erosive esophagitis, duodenal ulcer, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori eradication. Notable adverse effects include headache and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to rabeprazole or other proton pump inhibitors.
tail_desc: Osteoporosis is a metabolic skeletal disease of the musculoskeletal system characterized by low bone mass and deterioration of bone microarchitecture, causing increased bone fragility and fracture risk. It commonly affects older adults, especially postmenopausal women, and may be asymptomatic until vertebral, hip, or wrist fractures occur. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, adequate calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Nortriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nortriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the secondary amine dibenzocycloheptene class that primarily inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and, to a lesser extent, serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters. It is used for major depressive disorder and is also established for certain neuropathic pain conditions. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic effects such as dry mouth, constipation, and sedation.
tail_desc: Open-angle glaucoma is a chronic optic neuropathy of the eye, usually associated with impaired aqueous humor outflow through an anatomically open anterior chamber angle, often causing elevated intraocular pressure. It typically affects older adults and is characterized by progressive peripheral visual field loss and optic nerve cupping, often without early symptoms. Management focuses on lowering intraocular pressure with topical medications, laser trabeculoplasty, or surgery.

head: Hydroxyamphetamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxyamphetamine is a sympathomimetic amine and indirect-acting adrenergic agonist, structurally related to amphetamine, that promotes release of norepinephrine from postganglionic sympathetic nerve terminals. Ophthalmically, it is used diagnostically to aid localization of Horner syndrome and can produce mydriasis. A notable adverse effect is precipitation of angle-closure glaucoma, so it is contraindicated in patients with narrow anterior chamber angles.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Maraviroc (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Maraviroc is a small-molecule CCR5 antagonist antiretroviral in the entry inhibitor class. It binds the host chemokine receptor CCR5, blocking attachment and entry of CCR5-tropic HIV-1 into CD4+ cells. It is approved, in combination with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of CCR5-tropic HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, often preceded by rash or systemic allergic features.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Tetrabenazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetrabenazine is a benzoquinolizine derivative and vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) inhibitor that depletes presynaptic dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine stores. It is approved to treat chorea associated with Huntington disease and is also used for hyperkinetic movement disorders such as tardive dyskinesia. Notable risks include depression and suicidality, and it is contraindicated in actively suicidal patients or those with untreated depression.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Topotecan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Topotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin antineoplastic agent and topoisomerase I inhibitor. It stabilizes the topoisomerase I-DNA cleavage complex, preventing DNA religation and causing DNA strand breaks during replication. It is used for ovarian cancer, small cell lung cancer, and cervical cancer. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression, particularly neutropenia.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Atenolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker, belonging to the beta-blocker class. It primarily antagonizes beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate, contractility, and blood pressure. It is used for hypertension, angina pectoris, and management after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in sinus bradycardia, heart block, and cardiogenic shock.
tail_desc: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is an autoimmune endocrine disorder of the pancreas in which immune-mediated destruction of insulin-producing beta cells causes absolute insulin deficiency and chronic hyperglycemia. It often begins in childhood or adolescence, though it can occur at any age, and commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and sometimes diabetic ketoacidosis. Management requires lifelong insulin replacement with glucose monitoring, nutrition guidance, and complication prevention.

head: Dalfopristin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dalfopristin is a streptogramin A antibacterial, used clinically in fixed combination with quinupristin as quinupristin/dalfopristin. It binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis and acting synergistically with quinupristin. The combination is approved for serious infections caused by susceptible gram-positive bacteria, including vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecium and complicated skin infections. Notable adverse effects include arthralgia/myalgia and infusion-site pain.
tail_desc: Hyperbilirubinemia is a metabolic disorder of the hepatobiliary system characterized by elevated bilirubin in blood, caused by increased bilirubin production, impaired hepatic uptake or conjugation, or reduced biliary excretion. It commonly presents with jaundice and scleral icterus, and in neonates may cause kernicterus if severe. Management targets the cause and bilirubin reduction, including phototherapy, exchange transfusion, or treating liver or hemolytic disease.

head: Tigecycline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tigecycline is an intravenous glycylcycline antibiotic, a tetracycline derivative that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit to inhibit protein synthesis. It is approved for complicated skin and skin-structure infections, complicated intra-abdominal infections, and community-acquired bacterial pneumonia. A notable safety concern is increased all-cause mortality observed versus comparator antibiotics; nausea and vomiting are also common adverse effects.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Flumethasone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flumethasone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts by binding the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It has been used mainly in topical dermatologic and otic/ophthalmic preparations for inflammatory conditions. Like other corticosteroids, prolonged local use can cause skin atrophy, and it is generally contraindicated in untreated infections at the application site.
tail_desc: Skin atrophy is a dermatologic condition characterized by thinning and loss of normal epidermal, dermal, or subcutaneous tissue, often due to aging, chronic corticosteroid exposure, sun damage, inflammation, or inherited disorders. It presents with fragile, shiny, wrinkled, sometimes translucent skin and easy tearing or bruising, especially in older adults or treated areas. Management focuses on addressing the cause, protecting skin, and minimizing further injury.

head: Calcifediol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcifediol (25-hydroxyvitamin D3, calcidiol) is a vitamin D metabolite and prohormone used to raise vitamin D status. It is converted in the kidney to calcitriol, which activates the vitamin D receptor (VDR) to increase intestinal calcium and phosphate absorption and suppress parathyroid hormone. It is used for vitamin D deficiency/insufficiency and secondary hyperparathyroidism in chronic kidney disease; notable risks include hypercalcemia and hyperphosphatemia.
tail_desc: Hyperphosphatemia is an electrolyte and mineral metabolism disorder affecting systemic homeostasis, most often due to impaired renal phosphate excretion in chronic kidney disease. It may be asymptomatic or contribute to hypocalcemia, pruritus, soft-tissue and vascular calcification, and secondary hyperparathyroidism, especially in patients with advanced kidney failure. Management focuses on treating the cause, restricting dietary phosphate, and using phosphate binders or dialysis when needed.

head: Cisapride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cisapride is a substituted piperidinyl benzamide gastroprokinetic agent that enhances gastrointestinal motility primarily by acting as a serotonin 5-HT4 receptor agonist, promoting acetylcholine release in the enteric nervous system. It was used for gastroesophageal reflux disease and other motility disorders, but its use became highly restricted because it can prolong the QT interval and cause serious ventricular arrhythmias, especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Tolterodine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tolterodine is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a tertiary amine used for overactive bladder with urinary urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence. It acts as a competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, functionally reducing detrusor muscle contractions in the bladder. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Pyloric stenosis is a gastrointestinal obstructive disorder, usually infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, caused by hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the pyloric muscle that narrows the gastric outlet. It typically presents in young infants with progressively forceful non-bilious vomiting, dehydration, weight loss, and sometimes a palpable “olive” in the epigastrium. Management is correction of fluid and electrolyte abnormalities followed by pyloromyotomy.

head: Desipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant, specifically a secondary amine dibenzazepine, that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake via the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with less effect on serotonin uptake. It is used for major depressive disorder and has also been used for neuropathic pain in some settings. A notable risk is cardiotoxicity, including arrhythmias, especially in overdose.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Dabigatran etexilate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dabigatran etexilate is an oral direct thrombin inhibitor and the etexilate prodrug of dabigatran. After conversion to dabigatran, it reversibly inhibits thrombin (factor IIa), blocking fibrin formation and thrombin-mediated platelet activation. It is approved for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolism and for stroke prevention in nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, including serious gastrointestinal or intracranial hemorrhage.
tail_desc: Intracerebral hemorrhage is a subtype of stroke in the central nervous system caused by bleeding directly into the brain parenchyma, usually from rupture of small vessels due to chronic hypertension or cerebral amyloid angiopathy. It presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, vomiting, and reduced consciousness, especially in older adults. Management focuses on urgent stabilization, blood pressure control, reversal of anticoagulation, and neurosurgical evaluation in selected cases.

head: Pentoxifylline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentoxifylline is a methylxanthine hemorheologic agent and nonselective phosphodiesterase inhibitor that increases erythrocyte flexibility, reduces blood viscosity, and may improve microcirculatory flow, partly via increased intracellular cAMP. It is approved for intermittent claudication due to chronic occlusive peripheral arterial disease. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, and it is contraindicated in patients with recent cerebral or retinal hemorrhage.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Alendronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alendronic acid (alendronate) is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive drug. It binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated resorption, primarily by blocking farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway. It is used for osteoporosis and Paget’s disease of bone. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis; it is contraindicated in esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying.
tail_desc: Hypervitaminosis is a toxic condition caused by excessive vitamin intake, most often from supplements, affecting multiple systems; fat-soluble vitamins A and D are classic causes because they accumulate in tissues. Manifestations vary by vitamin but commonly include gastrointestinal symptoms, neurologic complaints, skin or bone changes, and organ dysfunction. Management centers on stopping the vitamin source, supportive care, and treating complications.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Alanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: Alanine is a nonessential, proteinogenic alpha-amino acid used in amino acid formulations for parenteral or enteral nutrition. It has no specific drug-like molecular target; rather, it serves as a metabolic substrate in protein synthesis and glucose-alanine nitrogen transport. Its established clinical use is nutritional supplementation within amino acid mixtures. Excessive administration may contribute to amino acid imbalance or metabolic disturbances, particularly in severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Cefoperazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefoperazone is a third-generation cephalosporin β-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, intra-abdominal, skin/soft-tissue, and biliary tract infections. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia with bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Magnesium hydroxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium hydroxide is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an antacid and osmotic laxative. It neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach and, when taken orally in larger doses, retains water in the intestinal lumen by osmotic action; it has no specific receptor target. Established uses include relief of dyspepsia/heartburn and short-term treatment of constipation. A notable risk is hypermagnesemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Osteitis fibrosa is a metabolic bone disorder of the skeleton, usually caused by hyperparathyroidism with excess parathyroid hormone driving increased osteoclastic bone resorption and replacement by fibrous tissue. It can cause bone pain, fragility fractures, deformity, and “brown tumors,” classically in patients with severe primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism. Management targets the underlying hyperparathyroidism and correction of calcium-phosphate abnormalities.

head: Misoprostol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog that acts primarily as an agonist at prostaglandin EP receptors, reducing gastric acid secretion and increasing uterine tone and cervical softening. It is used for prevention of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers and in obstetrics/gynecology for medical abortion, cervical ripening, labor induction, and postpartum hemorrhage. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in pregnancy when used for ulcer prevention.
tail_desc: Non-infectious meningitis is an inflammatory disorder of the meninges in the central nervous system not caused by pathogens. It may result from autoimmune disease, malignancy, medications, or chemical irritation and typically presents with headache, fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, and cerebrospinal fluid pleocytosis. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, withdrawing offending agents, and providing supportive care, sometimes with corticosteroids.

head: Inulin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Inulin is a plant-derived fructan polysaccharide and soluble dietary fiber used clinically primarily as a marker of glomerular filtration because it is freely filtered at the glomerulus and is not significantly reabsorbed, secreted, or metabolized, rather than acting via a specific molecular target. It is established for measurement of renal function, and gastrointestinal adverse effects such as bloating or flatulence can occur.
tail_desc: Hyperparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the parathyroid glands causing excess parathyroid hormone, which raises blood calcium and disrupts bone and kidney homeostasis. It may be primary, secondary to chronic kidney disease or vitamin D deficiency, or tertiary after prolonged stimulation. Common features include hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, bone pain, fractures, weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; management targets the cause, with parathyroidectomy for selected cases.

head: Triamterene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triamterene is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pteridine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal renal tubule and collecting duct, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for edema and hypertension, often with a thiazide diuretic. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria, severe renal impairment, or preexisting hyperkalemia.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Tramazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tramazoline is an imidazoline-derived sympathomimetic nasal decongestant. It acts primarily as an alpha-adrenergic receptor agonist on nasal mucosal blood vessels, producing vasoconstriction and reducing mucosal edema. It is used for symptomatic relief of nasal congestion, such as in rhinitis or sinusitis. A notable adverse effect is rebound nasal congestion with prolonged use; it is generally avoided in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Bladder neck obstruction is a lower urinary tract outflow disorder of the urinary system in which the bladder neck fails to open adequately during voiding, causing functional or anatomic resistance to urine flow. It typically presents with obstructive and irritative voiding symptoms, elevated post-void residual, or urinary retention, often in men. Management generally includes alpha-blockers, treatment of contributing causes, and selected surgical bladder neck incision or resection.

head: Cetirizine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cetirizine is a second-generation piperazine antihistamine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is widely used for allergic rhinitis and chronic urticaria. Compared with first-generation antihistamines it is less sedating, but somnolence can still occur; caution is advised in severe renal impairment because the drug is substantially renally eliminated.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Thalidomide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thalidomide is an immunomodulatory glutamic acid derivative (IMiD) that binds cereblon, the substrate receptor of the CRL4 E3 ubiquitin ligase, altering degradation of transcription factors and suppressing TNF-α. It is used for multiple myeloma and erythema nodosum leprosum. A notable toxicity is profound teratogenicity, making pregnancy a strict contraindication; peripheral neuropathy can also occur.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Cefotetan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefotetan is a parenteral second-generation cephalosporin (cephamycin) beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, skin/soft tissue, urinary tract, and perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia/bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Minoxidil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Minoxidil is a direct-acting arteriolar vasodilator of the pyrimidine derivative class, used orally for severe, refractory hypertension and topically for androgenetic alopecia. It is converted to minoxidil sulfate, which opens ATP-sensitive potassium channels in vascular smooth muscle, reducing peripheral resistance; the precise hair-growth mechanism is incompletely defined. Notable adverse effects include hypertrichosis and fluid retention.
tail_desc: Hemopericardium is a cardiovascular condition in which blood accumulates within the pericardial sac, usually from cardiac trauma, myocardial rupture, aortic dissection, invasive procedures, or anticoagulation-related bleeding. It may present with chest pain, dyspnea, hypotension, jugular venous distension, and cardiac tamponade, often in acutely ill or injured patients. Management is urgent and focuses on hemodynamic stabilization, pericardial drainage, and treatment of the bleeding source.

head: Lemborexant (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lemborexant is an orexin receptor antagonist, specifically a dual antagonist of orexin OX1R and OX2R (hypocretin receptors), that promotes sleep by inhibiting wakefulness signaling. It is approved for the treatment of insomnia, including difficulties with sleep onset and sleep maintenance. A notable adverse effect is next-day somnolence; it is contraindicated in patients with narcolepsy.
tail_desc: Central nervous system disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the brain and spinal cord, arising from diverse mechanisms including congenital, vascular, infectious, inflammatory, degenerative, neoplastic, traumatic, or toxic injury. Clinical features vary but may include headache, seizures, weakness, sensory loss, cognitive or behavioral change, gait disturbance, or altered consciousness, in children or adults. Management depends on cause and typically combines etiologic treatment, symptom control, and rehabilitation.

head: Chloramphenicol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloramphenicol is a broad-spectrum amphenicol antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocking peptidyl transferase activity. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including meningitis, rickettsial infections, and topical treatment of bacterial conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is dose-independent aplastic anemia; use is also limited by gray baby syndrome in neonates.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Sunitinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sunitinib is an oral small-molecule multi-target tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the indolinone class that blocks VEGFR, PDGFR, KIT, FLT3, CSF1R, and RET signaling, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and proliferation. It is approved for renal cell carcinoma, imatinib-resistant or intolerant gastrointestinal stromal tumor, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. A notable toxicity is cardiotoxicity, including decreased left ventricular ejection fraction.
tail_desc: Gilbert syndrome is a common, benign inherited liver disorder of bilirubin metabolism caused by reduced UGT1A1 activity, leading to impaired hepatic conjugation of bilirubin and mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
