It often presents in adolescents or young adults with intermittent jaundice, especially during fasting, illness, stress, or exertion, while liver function is otherwise normal. Management is typically reassurance and avoidance of known triggers.

head: Metronidazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole antimicrobial and antiprotozoal prodrug that is reduced in anaerobic bacteria and certain protozoa to reactive radicals that damage DNA. It is used for anaerobic bacterial infections, bacterial vaginosis, Clostridioides difficile infection, and protozoal diseases such as trichomoniasis, giardiasis, and amebiasis. A notable adverse effect is a disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Zinc gluconate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zinc gluconate is an oral zinc salt supplement and trace element preparation. It supplies zinc ions, an essential cofactor for numerous metalloenzymes and transcription factors, thereby correcting or preventing zinc deficiency rather than acting through a single specific molecular target. It is used for prevention and treatment of zinc deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, especially nausea; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Multiple sclerosis is a chronic autoimmune inflammatory demyelinating disease of the central nervous system, affecting brain, spinal cord, and optic nerves. It involves immune-mediated myelin and axonal injury causing relapsing or progressive neurologic deficits such as visual loss, weakness, sensory symptoms, ataxia, and fatigue, often beginning in young adults. Management focuses on relapse treatment, disease-modifying therapy, symptom control, and rehabilitation.

head: Diphenoxylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diphenoxylate is a synthetic opioid antidiarrheal, structurally related to meperidine, that acts mainly as a peripheral mu-opioid receptor agonist in the intestine to reduce gastrointestinal motility. It is used, typically with atropine, as adjunctive therapy for diarrhea. Notable risks include central nervous system and respiratory depression in overdose, and it is contraindicated in diarrhea caused by invasive enteric infection.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Antipyrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Antipyrine (phenazone) is a pyrazolone derivative and non-opioid analgesic/antipyretic. Its effects are attributed to inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis via cyclooxygenase pathways, although it is not used for a highly selective molecular target. It has been used for pain and fever and, in otic combinations, for symptomatic relief of ear pain. Hypersensitivity reactions, including rash, can occur.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Albendazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Albendazole is a benzimidazole anthelmintic that binds parasite β-tubulin, inhibiting microtubule polymerization and impairing glucose uptake and energy metabolism. It is used to treat a range of helminth infections, including neurocysticercosis and hydatid disease, as well as intestinal nematode infections. Notable adverse effects include hepatotoxicity; it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy, especially during the first trimester.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Irinotecan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irinotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. Its active metabolite SN-38 stabilizes the topoisomerase I-DNA complex, blocking DNA religation and causing lethal DNA damage during replication. It is used for metastatic colorectal cancer and in some regimens for other solid tumors. Notable toxicities include severe delayed diarrhea and myelosuppression, especially neutropenia.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Nabilone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nabilone is a synthetic cannabinoid, structurally related to delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol, that acts mainly as an agonist at cannabinoid CB1 and CB2 receptors in the central nervous system. It is used primarily for chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting refractory to conventional antiemetics. Notable adverse effects include dizziness, drowsiness, and psychotropic reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to cannabinoids or a history of psychosis.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Alprazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alprazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine anxiolytic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is approved for the treatment of anxiety disorders and panic disorder. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; concomitant use with opioids can cause profound respiratory depression.
tail_desc: Monogenic obesity is a rare genetic disorder of energy-balance regulation affecting the neuroendocrine/hypothalamic pathways that control appetite and body weight. It usually results from single-gene defects, often in the leptin-melanocortin pathway, causing severe early-onset obesity with marked hyperphagia, sometimes with endocrine or developmental abnormalities. Management centers on specialist genetic evaluation, intensive weight-management support, and, in selected molecularly defined cases, targeted therapy.

head: Sodium fluorophosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluorophosphate is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis is a urinary tract disorder characterized by formation of calculi in the kidneys, usually from supersaturation and crystallization of minerals such as calcium, uric acid, or cystine. It commonly causes renal colic, hematuria, nausea, and urinary obstruction, especially in adults. Management focuses on analgesia, hydration, facilitating stone passage, metabolic evaluation, and urologic removal when stones are large, obstructing, or infected.

head: Azatadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Azatadine is a first-generation tricyclic antihistamine of the benzo[c]cycloheptathiophene family that acts primarily as a histamine H1-receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis and urticaria. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation due to central H1 blockade, and it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Misoprostol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog that acts primarily as an agonist at prostaglandin EP receptors, reducing gastric acid secretion and increasing uterine tone and cervical softening. It is used for prevention of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers and in obstetrics/gynecology for medical abortion, cervical ripening, labor induction, and postpartum hemorrhage. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in pregnancy when used for ulcer prevention.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Celecoxib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Celecoxib is a selective nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the diaryl-substituted pyrazole class that primarily inhibits cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2, PTGS2), reducing prostaglandin synthesis and thereby inflammation and pain. It is approved for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute pain, and primary dysmenorrhea. A notable risk is serious cardiovascular thrombotic events; it is contraindicated for perioperative pain in coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Salsalate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salsalate is a nonacetylated salicylate NSAID and dimer of salicylic acid that is hydrolyzed to salicylate; it reduces prostaglandin synthesis primarily through cyclooxygenase inhibition, with relatively weak direct platelet inhibition compared with aspirin. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and other painful inflammatory conditions. Notable adverse effects include salicylism and gastrointestinal irritation or ulceration.
tail_desc: Active peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related disorder of the upper gastrointestinal tract characterized by an active mucosal ulcer in the stomach or duodenum, most commonly due to Helicobacter pylori infection or NSAID use. Patients typically have epigastric pain or dyspepsia and may develop bleeding or perforation. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, stopping ulcerogenic drugs, and treating complications.

head: Loxapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: prolactin producing pituitary gland tumor (a disease)
head_desc: Loxapine is a dibenzoxazepine tricyclic antipsychotic, generally classified as a typical antipsychotic, that acts mainly by antagonizing dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2 receptors. It is used to treat schizophrenia, and an inhaled formulation is approved for acute agitation in schizophrenia or bipolar I disorder. A notable risk is bronchospasm with inhaled loxapine, making it contraindicated in asthma or COPD.
tail_desc: A prolactin-producing pituitary gland tumor (prolactinoma) is a usually benign functioning pituitary adenoma of the endocrine system that autonomously secretes prolactin. It commonly causes galactorrhea, menstrual disturbance, infertility, and hypogonadism in women, and decreased libido or erectile dysfunction in men; larger tumors may cause headache or visual field defects. Management typically centers on dopamine agonists, with surgery reserved for selected cases.

head: Melphalan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Melphalan is a bifunctional nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the phenylalanine derivative class that covalently crosslinks DNA, mainly at guanine bases, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and causing cell death. It is used chiefly in multiple myeloma and ovarian carcinoma conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity or marked bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Severe congenital neutropenia is a rare inherited hematologic and immune disorder of granulopoiesis, usually caused by pathogenic variants such as in ELANE or HAX1 that arrest neutrophil maturation, producing profound neutropenia from infancy. Affected children develop recurrent severe bacterial infections, oral ulcers, gingivitis, and skin or deep-tissue infections. Management centers on granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, aggressive infection treatment, and hematopoietic stem cell transplantation in refractory or high-risk cases.

head: Methionine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methionine is an essential sulfur-containing amino acid used as a nutritional supplement and urinary acidifier. It acts by serving as a metabolic precursor of S-adenosylmethionine and by acidifying urine through sulfate-generating metabolism rather than via a specific receptor target. Established uses include dietary supplementation in deficiency and urinary acidification. Excessive use may contribute to metabolic acidosis; caution is advised in severe liver disease.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Rutin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (a disease)
head_desc: Rutin is a naturally occurring flavonol glycoside (quercetin-3-O-rutinoside) found in various plants and used in some countries as a venoactive/vasoprotective agent. Its pharmacology is not linked to a single validated primary molecular target; reported actions include antioxidant effects and reduced capillary permeability. It has been used for chronic venous insufficiency and hemorrhoidal symptoms. Notable adverse effects are usually mild gastrointestinal upset or headache; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin is a benign inherited hemoglobinopathy of the hematologic system caused by genetic variants that sustain gamma-globin expression and continued HbF production after birth. Affected individuals, often identified incidentally, are usually asymptomatic with normal or mild microcytic blood findings; the condition can lessen severity of sickle cell disease or beta-thalassemia. Management is typically reassurance, diagnosis confirmation, and genetic counseling.

head: Nitrogen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrogen is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Sulfhemoglobinemia is a rare acquired hematologic disorder of the blood in which sulfur is irreversibly incorporated into hemoglobin, impairing oxygen transport. It usually follows exposure to sulfur-containing drugs or chemicals and causes persistent cyanosis, often with low pulse oximetry despite relatively normal oxygen tension; symptoms are commonly mild unless levels are high. Management centers on stopping the offending agent and supportive care, with transfusion reserved for severe cases.

head: Potassium bicarbonate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Potassium bicarbonate is an oral potassium salt and systemic alkalinizing agent. It dissociates to provide potassium and bicarbonate; bicarbonate buffers hydrogen ions and raises urinary and systemic pH, while potassium replenishes body potassium stores. It is used for prevention or treatment of hypokalemia and, in some formulations, urinary alkalinization. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Albendazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Albendazole is a benzimidazole anthelmintic that binds parasite β-tubulin, inhibiting microtubule polymerization and impairing glucose uptake and energy metabolism. It is used to treat a range of helminth infections, including neurocysticercosis and hydatid disease, as well as intestinal nematode infections. Notable adverse effects include hepatotoxicity; it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy, especially during the first trimester.
tail_desc: Aplastic anemia is a bone marrow failure disorder of the hematologic system characterized by hypocellular marrow and reduced production of red cells, white cells, and platelets, often due to immune-mediated destruction of hematopoietic stem cells; it may be idiopathic or secondary to drugs, toxins, radiation, infections, or inherited syndromes. Patients present with fatigue, infections, and bleeding; treatment includes removing causes, supportive transfusions, immunosuppression, or hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Sodium fluoride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluoride is an inorganic fluoride salt used as an anticaries agent and fluoride supplement. It acts topically and systemically by delivering fluoride ions that promote fluorapatite formation in tooth enamel and inhibit bacterial acid production. Established uses include prevention of dental caries in toothpaste, rinses, and supplements. Notable adverse effects include dental fluorosis with excessive exposure, especially during tooth development.
tail_desc: Constipation disorder is a gastrointestinal motility and defecation disorder characterized by infrequent bowel movements, hard or difficult-to-pass stools, and a sensation of incomplete evacuation. It commonly results from slow colonic transit, pelvic floor dysfunction, medications, diet, or systemic illness, and affects all ages, especially older adults. Management typically emphasizes hydration, fiber, exercise, treating causes, and use of laxatives or pelvic floor therapy.

head: Thiopental (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiopental is an ultra-short-acting barbiturate intravenous anesthetic. It enhances inhibitory neurotransmission primarily by positively modulating GABA-A receptors, increasing chloride channel opening and causing rapid CNS depression. It is used for induction of general anesthesia and has established use in seizure control and reduction of intracranial pressure. A notable risk is profound respiratory and cardiovascular depression; it is contraindicated in acute porphyria.
tail_desc: X-linked adrenal hypoplasia congenita is a rare inherited primary adrenal insufficiency caused by pathogenic variants in NR0B1 (DAX1), leading to underdevelopment of the adrenal cortex and often associated hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. It typically affects boys, presenting in infancy or childhood with vomiting, dehydration, hypoglycemia, salt wasting, and failure to thrive; management centers on lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement and later endocrine care for pubertal and reproductive issues.

head: Trimethoprim (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethoprim is a synthetic diaminopyrimidine antibacterial that selectively inhibits bacterial dihydrofolate reductase, blocking tetrahydrofolate synthesis and thereby impairing nucleic acid production. It is established for treatment and prophylaxis of susceptible urinary tract infections and, in combination with sulfamethoxazole, for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and other bacterial infections. Notable adverse effects include hyperkalemia and megaloblastic anemia, especially in folate deficiency.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Triazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic that positively modulates GABA\(_A\) receptors by binding the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include anterograde amnesia and next-day psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inhibitors.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Dactinomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dactinomycin (actinomycin D) is an antineoplastic polypeptide antibiotic of the actinomycin family that intercalates into DNA, primarily at guanine-cytosine pairs, thereby inhibiting DNA-dependent RNA synthesis. It is used in cancers including Wilms tumor, rhabdomyosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and gestational trophoblastic neoplasia. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Prednisolone acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prednisolone acetate is an esterified glucocorticoid corticosteroid that acts as a prodrug of prednisolone, binding the glucocorticoid receptor to regulate gene transcription and suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It is established mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory for steroid-responsive ocular inflammation, including postoperative inflammation. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, and it is contraindicated in untreated ocular viral, fungal, or mycobacterial infections.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Nabilone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nabilone is a synthetic cannabinoid, structurally related to delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol, that acts mainly as an agonist at cannabinoid CB1 and CB2 receptors in the central nervous system. It is used primarily for chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting refractory to conventional antiemetics. Notable adverse effects include dizziness, drowsiness, and psychotropic reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to cannabinoids or a history of psychosis.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Prasugrel (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prasugrel is an oral thienopyridine antiplatelet prodrug that irreversibly inhibits the platelet P2Y12 ADP receptor, reducing ADP-mediated platelet activation and aggregation. It is used to prevent thrombotic cardiovascular events in patients with acute coronary syndrome managed with percutaneous coronary intervention. Major bleeding is a notable adverse effect, and it is contraindicated in patients with prior stroke or transient ischemic attack.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Candesartan cilexetil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Candesartan cilexetil is an orally administered prodrug of the angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) candesartan, a benzimidazole derivative that selectively antagonizes the AT1 receptor. It is used for hypertension and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. A notable risk is hyperkalemia; like other renin-angiotensin system blockers, it is contraindicated during pregnancy due to fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Pediatric systemic lupus erythematosus is a childhood-onset, chronic multisystem autoimmune connective tissue disease in which loss of self-tolerance and immune-complex–mediated inflammation can affect skin, joints, kidneys, blood, and the central nervous system. It typically presents in children and adolescents with rash, arthritis, fever, cytopenias, and nephritis, and is managed with sun protection, hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressive therapy guided by organ involvement.

head: Colistin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Colistin (polymyxin E) is a cationic cyclic polypeptide antibiotic of the polymyxin class that binds lipopolysaccharide and phospholipids in the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria, disrupting membrane integrity and causing cell death. It is used for serious multidrug-resistant Gram-negative infections, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Acinetobacter baumannii, and Klebsiella pneumoniae. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Adult-onset myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system, usually caused by antibodies against acetylcholine receptor or related postsynaptic proteins, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It presents in adults with fluctuating, fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunotherapy, and thymectomy in selected patients.

head: Beclomethasone dipropionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Beclomethasone dipropionate is a synthetic corticosteroid (glucocorticoid prodrug) that is converted to beclomethasone-17-monopropionate and activates the glucocorticoid receptor, suppressing inflammatory gene expression and mediator release. It is used for maintenance treatment of asthma, allergic rhinitis, and inflammatory bowel disease in certain formulations. A notable adverse effect is oropharyngeal candidiasis with inhaled use.
tail_desc: Ocular hypertension is an ophthalmic condition characterized by persistently elevated intraocular pressure without detectable glaucomatous optic nerve damage or visual field loss. It results from impaired aqueous humor outflow, increasing risk of primary open-angle glaucoma. Patients are often asymptomatic and identified on routine eye examination, especially adults with risk factors. Management centers on pressure-lowering eye drops and regular monitoring of optic nerves, visual fields, and intraocular pressure.

head: Demeclocycline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Demeclocycline is a tetracycline-class antibiotic that binds the 30S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections and, notably, to treat chronic hyponatremia due to syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) by reducing renal responsiveness to ADH. A notable adverse effect is photosensitivity; it is also contraindicated in pregnancy and young children because of tooth discoloration and effects on bone growth.
tail_desc: Central diabetes insipidus is an endocrine disorder of water balance caused by deficient secretion of arginine vasopressin from the hypothalamus or posterior pituitary, affecting the neuroendocrine-renal axis. It leads to inability to concentrate urine, producing polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, and risk of hypernatremia; it occurs in children or adults from idiopathic, genetic, traumatic, neoplastic, or postoperative causes. Management centers on desmopressin and careful fluid and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Fexofenadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fexofenadine is a second-generation piperidine antihistamine and the active carboxylated metabolite of terfenadine. It selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated symptoms. It is established for seasonal allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is headache, and it is generally minimally sedating compared with first-generation antihistamines.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: Neuromuscular junction disease is a disorder of peripheral nerve–muscle transmission, usually caused by autoimmune attack on presynaptic or postsynaptic components of the junction, as in myasthenia gravis or Lambert-Eaton syndrome. It typically produces fluctuating fatigable weakness, often affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, or respiratory muscles in adults. Management focuses on treating the cause, improving transmission, and providing immunotherapy and supportive respiratory care when needed.

head: Sulfamethoxazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamethoxazole is a sulfonamide antibacterial that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby DNA precursor production; it is commonly combined with trimethoprim for synergistic activity. Established uses include urinary tract infections, acute otitis media, bronchitis exacerbations, shigellosis, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia treatment or prophylaxis. Notable risks include severe hypersensitivity reactions such as Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Alprostadil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alprostadil is a prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) analog and vasodilatory smooth-muscle relaxant that activates prostaglandin E receptors, increasing cAMP. It is used to maintain ductus arteriosus patency in certain congenital heart defects and to treat erectile dysfunction. Notable adverse effects include hypotension; it is contraindicated in neonates with respiratory distress syndrome without cyanotic heart disease.
tail_desc: Acute respiratory distress syndrome is a severe inflammatory lung disorder of the respiratory system, usually triggered by sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, or major trauma, causing diffuse alveolar-capillary injury, noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, and profound hypoxemia. Patients, often critically ill adults, develop rapid-onset dyspnea, tachypnea, and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Management is supportive and directed at the cause, especially lung-protective mechanical ventilation and intensive care.

head: Meropenem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meropenem is a broad-spectrum carbapenem beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins, inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis and causing bactericidal activity. It is established for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including complicated intra-abdominal and skin infections, meningitis, and hospital-acquired pneumonia. A notable adverse effect is seizure risk, particularly in patients with CNS disorders or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Alvimopan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alvimopan is a peripherally acting mu-opioid receptor antagonist, structurally a selective opioid receptor blocker with limited central nervous system penetration. It counteracts opioid-induced inhibition of gastrointestinal motility by blocking peripheral MORs in the gut. It is approved to accelerate gastrointestinal recovery after partial large or small bowel resection with primary anastomosis. A notable contraindication is use in patients receiving therapeutic opioids for more than seven consecutive days before treatment.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Mifepristone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mifepristone is a synthetic steroidal antiprogestin that primarily antagonizes the progesterone receptor and also has glucocorticoid receptor antagonist activity. It is approved for medical termination of intrauterine pregnancy, management of early pregnancy loss in combination regimens, and control of hyperglycemia in endogenous Cushing syndrome. A notable contraindication is suspected ectopic pregnancy; heavy uterine bleeding can occur.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Ethosuximide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethosuximide is a succinimide anticonvulsant used primarily for absence (petit mal) seizures. Its antiepileptic effect is attributed mainly to inhibition of low-threshold T-type calcium currents in thalamic neurons, reducing abnormal thalamocortical rhythmic activity. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal upset, and it can also cause serious but rare blood dyscrasias or hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Sodium oxybate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium oxybate is the sodium salt of gamma-hydroxybutyrate (GHB), a central nervous system depressant that acts primarily as a GABA_B receptor agonist and also binds GHB-specific sites. It is approved for narcolepsy, particularly cataplexy and excessive daytime sleepiness. A notable risk is profound respiratory and CNS depression, making concomitant alcohol or sedative use contraindicated.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Oxybutynin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) tertiary amine used to treat overactive bladder symptoms, including urinary urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence, and some cases of neurogenic detrusor overactivity. It acts mainly by antagonizing muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, particularly M3 receptors in bladder smooth muscle, reducing detrusor contractions. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic disease in which the peripheral iris obstructs the anterior chamber angle, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid or intermittent rise in intraocular pressure. It typically affects anatomically predisposed older adults, especially those with shallow anterior chambers. Features include severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos, nausea, and a red eye; management is urgent pressure lowering followed by laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Fingolimod (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fingolimod is a sphingosine 1-phosphate receptor modulator, structurally a sphingosine analog, that is phosphorylated in vivo and functionally antagonizes S1P receptors, especially S1P1, to sequester lymphocytes in lymph nodes. It is approved for relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis. A notable risk is first-dose bradycardia and atrioventricular conduction block; it is contraindicated in certain recent serious cardiovascular conditions.
tail_desc: Familial sick sinus syndrome is an inherited cardiac conduction disorder affecting the sinoatrial node, the heart’s natural pacemaker. It results from genetic defects that impair impulse generation or conduction, causing sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or atrial arrhythmias, often in affected families across generations. Management is directed by symptoms and rhythm severity, commonly including monitoring and permanent pacemaker implantation when clinically indicated.

head: Phylloquinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) is a fat-soluble naphthoquinone vitamin used as a hemostatic antidote to vitamin K deficiency and warfarin-associated hypoprothrombinemia. It acts as a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase in the liver, enabling activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. Notable risks include anaphylactoid reactions with rapid intravenous administration.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Protriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Protriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzocycloheptadiene family. It acts primarily by inhibiting neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is used for treatment of major depressive disorder. A notable adverse effect is anticholinergic toxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Intrinsic asthma is a nonallergic subtype of asthma affecting the lower respiratory tract, characterized by chronic airway inflammation, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and reversible airflow obstruction without an identifiable external allergen trigger. It often begins in adulthood and presents with episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, commonly provoked by infections, irritants, exercise, or stress. Management follows standard asthma control with trigger avoidance, inhaled corticosteroids, and bronchodilators.

head: Bacampicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Bacampicillin is an oral ester prodrug of ampicillin, a beta-lactam aminopenicillin antibiotic. After hydrolysis to ampicillin, it binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, skin/soft tissue, and some gastrointestinal infections. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Tetracaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetracaine is an ester-type local anesthetic of the para-aminobenzoic acid class that reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing impulse conduction. It is used for topical anesthesia of the eye and mucous membranes and in spinal anesthesia. A notable risk is systemic toxicity, including CNS and cardiovascular effects; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to ester local anesthetics.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Milrinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Milrinone is a bipyridine phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor and inodilator that increases intracellular cAMP in cardiac myocytes and vascular smooth muscle, enhancing myocardial contractility and promoting vasodilation. It is used for short-term intravenous treatment of acute decompensated heart failure or low-output states. A notable adverse effect is ventricular arrhythmia; significant hypotension can also occur.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Vitamin A (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vitamin A comprises fat-soluble retinoids, including retinol and retinyl esters. It is converted to active retinoic acid, which binds nuclear retinoic acid receptors (RARs) and retinoid X receptors (RXRs) to regulate gene transcription involved in vision, epithelial differentiation, growth, and immune function. It is used to prevent and treat vitamin A deficiency; excessive doses can cause hepatotoxicity and teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory granulomatous disease that most often affects the lungs and intrathoracic lymph nodes. It is characterized by noncaseating granuloma formation of unclear cause, likely involving immune dysregulation in genetically susceptible people. Common features include cough, dyspnea, fatigue, skin or eye involvement, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, typically in young to middle-aged adults; management ranges from observation to corticosteroids and other immunosuppressive therapy for significant organ disease.

head: Salbutamol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salbutamol (albuterol) is a short-acting β2-adrenergic receptor agonist bronchodilator, chemically a phenethylamine derivative. By selectively stimulating β2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, it increases cyclic AMP and produces rapid bronchodilation. It is used for relief and prevention of bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, including exercise-induced bronchospasm. A notable adverse effect is tremor.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Dacarbazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dacarbazine is a triazene alkylating antineoplastic prodrug that is metabolized in the liver to reactive methylating species, causing DNA damage through methylation and inhibition of DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis; it has no single specific molecular target. It is used for metastatic malignant melanoma and Hodgkin lymphoma, and notable toxicities include severe myelosuppression and marked nausea/vomiting.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Auranofin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Auranofin is an oral gold(I)-containing disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD). It is thought to act through immunomodulatory effects, notably inhibition of thioredoxin reductase and related sulfhydryl-dependent enzymes, thereby suppressing inflammatory responses. It has been approved for rheumatoid arthritis. Important adverse effects include diarrhea, rash, stomatitis, and potentially serious thrombocytopenia or proteinuria; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Dry eye syndrome is a common ocular surface disorder of the lacrimal functional unit in which reduced tear production or excessive tear evaporation causes tear-film instability and hyperosmolarity, leading to surface inflammation and epithelial damage. Typical symptoms are dryness, burning, foreign-body sensation, fluctuating vision, and reflex tearing, especially in older adults. Management focuses on lubricating tears, environmental measures, eyelid/meibomian care, and treating underlying inflammation or contributing causes.

head: Quazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine hypnotic of the triazolobenzodiazepine family that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing inhibitory GABA signaling. It is indicated for the treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling asleep and maintaining sleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day sedation; it is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Clomifene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clomifene is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (triphenylethylene derivative) that acts mainly as an estrogen receptor antagonist in the hypothalamus, blocking estrogen feedback and increasing GnRH, LH, and FSH release to induce ovulation. It is established for treatment of anovulatory infertility, particularly in polycystic ovary syndrome. A notable adverse effect is ovarian hyperstimulation, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Endometrial carcinoma is a malignant epithelial cancer of the uterine endometrium in the female reproductive system, usually arising from endometrial glandular cells and often linked to prolonged estrogen exposure. It typically affects postmenopausal women and commonly presents with abnormal uterine bleeding. Management is primarily surgical, with hysterectomy and staging, with radiotherapy, chemotherapy, or hormonal therapy used according to stage and tumor risk features.

head: Tipranavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tipranavir is a nonpeptidic HIV-1 protease inhibitor that blocks the viral aspartyl protease, preventing cleavage of Gag-Pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. Boosted with ritonavir, it is used in combination antiretroviral therapy for treatment-experienced HIV-1 infection, including resistant virus. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity; it is contraindicated in patients with moderate to severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Hypertriglyceridemia is a metabolic lipid disorder affecting the cardiovascular and pancreatic systems, defined by elevated plasma triglycerides from primary genetic defects or secondary causes such as diabetes, obesity, alcohol use, or medications. It is often asymptomatic but can cause eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, and acute pancreatitis, especially at very high levels. Management targets underlying causes, lifestyle change, and triglyceride-lowering therapy.

head: Nilotinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nilotinib is an oral second-generation BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor, structurally an aminopyrimidine derivative related to imatinib. It selectively inhibits BCR-ABL and also KIT and PDGFR kinases, blocking proliferation of Philadelphia chromosome-positive leukemic cells. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome-positive chronic myeloid leukemia. A notable safety risk is QT-interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, or congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Isoflurane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoflurane is a halogenated ether volatile inhalational general anesthetic. Its anesthetic action is not fully defined but involves enhancement of inhibitory GABA_A and glycine signaling and effects on two-pore-domain potassium channels, with CNS depression. It is used to induce and maintain general anesthesia during surgery. A notable serious risk is malignant hyperthermia in susceptible patients.
tail_desc: Hypotensive disorder is a cardiovascular hemodynamic condition defined by abnormally low arterial blood pressure, causing inadequate tissue perfusion. It may result from reduced blood volume, impaired cardiac output, vasodilation, autonomic dysfunction, medications, or endocrine disease. Common features include dizziness, weakness, syncope, blurred vision, and fatigue, especially in older adults or acutely ill patients. Management targets the underlying cause, with fluids, medication adjustment, and pressor support when needed.

head: Simvastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Simvastatin is a lipid-lowering statin, a semisynthetic derivative of a fermentation product, that acts as a prodrug inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase (3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase), reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used for hypercholesterolemia and cardiovascular risk reduction. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; active liver disease is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Trimipramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzazepine class. Unlike many TCAs, it has relatively weak inhibition of norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake; its effects are attributed mainly to antagonism at histamine H1, muscarinic cholinergic, and alpha-adrenergic receptors. It is used for major depressive disorders, particularly when insomnia or anxiety is prominent. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Frovatriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Frovatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It acts by constricting dilated cranial blood vessels and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is approved for the acute treatment of migraine attacks with or without aura in adults, not for prophylaxis. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease because of coronary vasospasm risk.
tail_desc: Monogenic obesity is a rare genetic disorder of energy-balance regulation affecting the neuroendocrine/hypothalamic pathways that control appetite and body weight. It usually results from single-gene defects, often in the leptin-melanocortin pathway, causing severe early-onset obesity with marked hyperphagia, sometimes with endocrine or developmental abnormalities. Management centers on specialist genetic evaluation, intensive weight-management support, and, in selected molecularly defined cases, targeted therapy.

head: Ammonia (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ammonia is a small endogenous/basic nitrogenous compound rather than a conventional therapeutic drug. Medicinally, ammonia solution is used mainly as an inhalant respiratory stimulant in managing vasovagal syncope or near-fainting, acting by irritating nasal and respiratory mucosa to trigger reflex inhalation; it has no specific molecular drug target. It can cause mucosal irritation and should not be applied directly to eyes or damaged skin.
tail_desc: Intermittent vascular claudication is a manifestation of peripheral arterial disease affecting the lower-extremity vascular system, usually caused by atherosclerotic arterial narrowing that limits blood flow during exertion. It classically produces reproducible calf, thigh, or buttock cramping pain with walking that resolves with rest, most often in older adults and smokers. Management centers on risk-factor modification, exercise therapy, antiplatelet/statin treatment, and revascularization when symptoms are severe.

head: Mitotane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mitotane is an adrenolytic antineoplastic agent, chemically related to the insecticide DDT (o,p'-DDD). It selectively damages the adrenal cortex and suppresses steroidogenesis, though its precise molecular targets are not fully defined. It is used for unresectable, recurrent, or metastatic adrenocortical carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is adrenal insufficiency, often requiring glucocorticoid replacement.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Nandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nandrolone is a synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid and testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein synthesis and erythropoiesis. Its ester forms, such as nandrolone decanoate, have been used for anemia associated with chronic kidney disease and some wasting states. Notable adverse effects include virilization and other androgenic effects; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in prostate or male breast carcinoma.
tail_desc: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy is a paraneoplastic endocrine-metabolic disorder causing systemic calcium imbalance, most often in patients with solid tumors. It usually results from tumor secretion of parathyroid hormone-related protein, which increases bone resorption and renal calcium retention. Patients develop often severe hypercalcemia with dehydration, weakness, confusion, constipation, and polyuria; management focuses on urgent calcium lowering and treatment of the underlying cancer.

head: Quercetin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quercetin is a naturally occurring flavonol (flavonoid polyphenol) found in many fruits and vegetables. It has antioxidant activity and modulates multiple signaling enzymes and transporters, but no single primary molecular target or definitive therapeutic mechanism is established. Quercetin is not an approved prescription drug for any specific indication. Reported adverse effects of high-dose supplements include headache and gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Amoxicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amoxicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibacterial agent. It binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to cell lysis. It is widely used for susceptible infections such as otitis media, sinusitis, streptococcal pharyngitis, lower respiratory, urinary tract, skin infections, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Gastritis is an inflammatory condition of the gastric mucosa in the digestive system, commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection, NSAID use, alcohol, bile reflux, or autoimmune injury. It may be acute or chronic and can cause epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, bloating, or gastrointestinal bleeding, though some patients are asymptomatic. Management targets the cause, often with acid suppression and eradication of H. pylori when present.

head: Methadyl acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadyl acetate (levomethadyl acetate, LAAM) is a long-acting diphenylheptane opioid and full agonist at the μ-opioid receptor. It was used for maintenance treatment of opioid dependence as an alternative to methadone. A notable safety concern is QT-interval prolongation with risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, making it contraindicated in patients with known QT prolongation or significant cardiac rhythm disorders.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Monopotassium phosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Monopotassium phosphate is an inorganic potassium salt and phosphate replacement agent used to prevent or treat hypophosphatemia, including in parenteral nutrition. It dissociates to provide phosphate for cellular metabolic processes and potassium as an electrolyte, without a specific receptor target. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, so it is contraindicated or used with great caution in severe renal impairment or preexisting elevated serum potassium.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Nortriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nortriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the secondary amine dibenzocycloheptene class that primarily inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and, to a lesser extent, serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters. It is used for major depressive disorder and is also established for certain neuropathic pain conditions. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic effects such as dry mouth, constipation, and sedation.
tail_desc: Thyrotoxicosis is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid system caused by excess circulating thyroid hormones, most commonly from hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease, toxic nodular goiter, or thyroiditis. It typically presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, especially in adults. Management targets the cause and symptom control, using beta-blockers and measures such as antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Voglibose (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Voglibose is an oral alpha-glucosidase inhibitor, a valiolamine-derived antidiabetic agent, that competitively inhibits intestinal brush-border alpha-glucosidases, delaying carbohydrate digestion and reducing postprandial blood glucose excursions. It is used for type 2 diabetes mellitus, particularly for postprandial hyperglycemia. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal flatulence and abdominal discomfort, and it is contraindicated in diabetic ketoacidosis or severe intestinal obstruction.
tail_desc: Peripheral nervous system disease comprises disorders affecting peripheral nerves, nerve roots, neuromuscular junctions, or peripheral ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord. Causes include inherited abnormalities, metabolic or immune-mediated injury, infection, trauma, and toxins. Typical features are distal weakness, sensory loss, pain, paresthesias, and reduced reflexes in children or adults. Management targets the cause, symptom control, rehabilitation, and prevention of complications.

head: Cholestyramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant, an anion-exchange resin that binds bile acids in the intestinal lumen, preventing their reabsorption via the enterohepatic circulation and thereby lowering LDL cholesterol. It is used for hypercholesterolemia and pruritus due to partial biliary obstruction. Notable adverse effects include constipation; it is contraindicated in complete biliary obstruction.
tail_desc: Hemorrhoid is a common anorectal vascular condition of the lower gastrointestinal system caused by enlargement and distal displacement of normal anal vascular cushions, often associated with increased venous pressure and straining. Typical features include painless bright red rectal bleeding, prolapse, pruritus, discomfort, or pain if thrombosed, especially in adults. Management ranges from fiber, fluids, and topical or office procedures to surgical hemorrhoidectomy for severe disease.

head: Voriconazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Voriconazole is a second-generation triazole antifungal that inhibits fungal cytochrome P450–dependent 14-alpha-sterol demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and disrupting cell membrane formation. It is used for invasive aspergillosis, candidemia and other serious Candida infections, and esophageal candidiasis. A notable adverse effect is visual disturbance; it is also associated with hepatotoxicity and clinically important CYP-mediated drug interactions.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Ticlopidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ticlopidine is a thienopyridine antiplatelet agent that irreversibly inhibits ADP-mediated platelet aggregation by blocking the platelet P2Y12 receptor. It has been used to reduce thrombotic stroke risk and to prevent subacute stent thrombosis, particularly when aspirin is unsuitable.
