A notable serious adverse effect is neutropenia, and it can also cause thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura.
tail_desc: Familial hyperlipidemia is an inherited metabolic disorder of lipid handling affecting the cardiovascular system, characterized by persistently elevated plasma cholesterol and/or triglycerides due to genetic defects in lipoprotein metabolism. It often presents in families, sometimes from childhood or early adulthood, with xanthomas and premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk. Management focuses on diet, exercise, and lipid-lowering therapy, with specialist monitoring.

head: Tesamorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tesamorelin is a synthetic growth hormone–releasing hormone analog that binds pituitary GHRH receptors to stimulate endogenous growth hormone secretion and increase IGF-1. It is approved to reduce excess abdominal visceral adipose tissue in adults with HIV-associated lipodystrophy. Notable risks include glucose intolerance or hyperglycemia, and it is contraindicated in active malignancy and disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
tail_desc: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is an endocrine reproductive disorder caused by deficient hypothalamic or pituitary secretion of gonadotropin-releasing hormone, luteinizing hormone, or follicle-stimulating hormone, resulting in low sex steroid production. It typically presents with delayed or absent puberty, infertility, amenorrhea in females, and low libido or erectile dysfunction in males. Management focuses on treating the cause and using sex hormone replacement or fertility-inducing gonadotropin/GnRH therapy.

head: Levobunolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Levobunolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class used primarily as an ophthalmic agent. By blocking beta-1 and beta-2 receptors in the ciliary body, it reduces aqueous humor production and lowers intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. Notable adverse effects include bronchospasm, so it is contraindicated in patients with asthma or severe COPD.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Levetiracetam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Levetiracetam is a pyrrolidone anticonvulsant, structurally related to piracetam, used to treat focal-onset, myoclonic, and primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Its main molecular target is synaptic vesicle protein 2A (SV2A), which modulates neurotransmitter release, though the full mechanism is not completely defined. A notable adverse effect is somnolence; behavioral symptoms such as irritability can also occur.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Diclofenamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diclofenamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor of the sulfonamide class. It reduces aqueous humor formation by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase, primarily in the ciliary processes of the eye. It is used to lower intraocular pressure in glaucoma, including chronic open-angle glaucoma. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: X-linked adrenal hypoplasia congenita is a rare inherited primary adrenal insufficiency caused by pathogenic variants in NR0B1 (DAX1), leading to underdevelopment of the adrenal cortex and often associated hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. It typically affects boys, presenting in infancy or childhood with vomiting, dehydration, hypoglycemia, salt wasting, and failure to thrive; management centers on lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement and later endocrine care for pubertal and reproductive issues.

head: Nepafenac (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nepafenac is a topical ophthalmic nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), a prodrug converted in ocular tissues to amfenac, which inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes (COX-1 and COX-2) and reduces prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to treat ocular pain and inflammation after cataract surgery and, in some formulations, to reduce the risk of postoperative macular edema in diabetic patients. A notable adverse effect is delayed corneal healing.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Oxybutynin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) tertiary amine used to treat overactive bladder symptoms, including urinary urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence, and some cases of neurogenic detrusor overactivity. It acts mainly by antagonizing muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, particularly M3 receptors in bladder smooth muscle, reducing detrusor contractions. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Amrinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amrinone (inamrinone) is a bipyridine phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor and positive inotrope/vasodilator. By inhibiting PDE3 in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, it increases intracellular cAMP, enhancing myocardial contractility and reducing preload and afterload. It has been used intravenously for short-term management of acute decompensated congestive heart failure. A notable adverse effect is thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Carmustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carmustine is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms DNA interstrand cross-links and carbamoylates proteins, disrupting DNA replication and RNA/protein synthesis without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors and for multiple myeloma, lymphoma, and other hematologic malignancies, including in conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Brain edema is a neurologic condition of the central nervous system characterized by excess fluid accumulation within brain tissue, causing increased intracranial pressure. It commonly follows trauma, stroke, tumor, infection, or toxic-metabolic injury and may present with headache, vomiting, altered consciousness, seizures, and focal deficits in affected children or adults. Management targets the cause and reduction of intracranial pressure, using intensive monitoring and measures such as osmotherapy or surgery when needed.

head: Ethionamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethionamide is a thioamide antitubercular agent, structurally related to isoniazid, used mainly for multidrug-resistant tuberculosis. It is a prodrug activated by the mycobacterial monooxygenase EthA; the active metabolite inhibits InhA, blocking mycolic acid synthesis and cell-wall formation. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and gastrointestinal intolerance is common.
tail_desc: Congenital hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid axis present at birth, most commonly caused by thyroid dysgenesis or defects in thyroid hormone synthesis, leading to deficient thyroxine. It affects newborns and may cause prolonged jaundice, lethargy, feeding difficulty, constipation, macroglossia, and impaired growth and neurodevelopment if untreated. Management is prompt newborn screening confirmation and early levothyroxine replacement with ongoing monitoring.

head: Echothiophate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Echothiophate is an organophosphate, irreversible cholinesterase inhibitor used topically in ophthalmology. By covalently inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, it increases acetylcholine at cholinergic synapses, producing prolonged miosis and enhanced aqueous humor outflow. It has been used for glaucoma and accommodative esotropia. A notable adverse effect is cataract formation; it is generally avoided in angle-closure glaucoma unless definitive treatment is provided.
tail_desc: Neurocirculatory asthenia is a functional autonomic disorder affecting the cardiovascular system, historically overlapping with anxiety-related syndromes and “Da Costa syndrome.” It lacks a defined structural cardiac lesion and is thought to reflect dysregulation of autonomic and psychophysiologic responses. Typical features include palpitations, chest discomfort, fatigue, dyspnea, dizziness, and exercise intolerance, often in younger adults; management is mainly reassurance, conditioning, and treatment of associated anxiety or stress.

head: Ceftibuten (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ceftibuten is an oral third-generation cephalosporin antibiotic (a beta-lactam) that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis. It is used for susceptible infections including otitis media, pharyngitis/tonsillitis, acute bacterial exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and uncomplicated urinary tract infections. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in patients with known serious hypersensitivity to ceftibuten or other cephalosporins.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Diethylpropion (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diethylpropion is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, structurally related to amphetamines, used as a short-term adjunct to caloric restriction in the management of exogenous obesity. It acts primarily by increasing central norepinephrine signaling, likely through promotion of catecholamine release in hypothalamic appetite pathways. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated in advanced cardiovascular disease or hyperthyroidism.
tail_desc: Glaucoma is a group of chronic optic neuropathies affecting the eye, characterized by progressive retinal ganglion cell and optic nerve damage, often associated with elevated intraocular pressure from impaired aqueous humor outflow. It may be asymptomatic early, then cause peripheral visual field loss and eventual blindness, especially in older adults. Management aims to lower intraocular pressure with medications, laser therapy, or surgery.

head: Amifostine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Amifostine is a cytoprotective thiophosphate prodrug dephosphorylated by alkaline phosphatase to the active free thiol WR-1065, which scavenges reactive oxygen species and donates hydrogen ions to protect normal tissues from cisplatin- and radiation-induced DNA damage. It is used to reduce cumulative nephrotoxicity from cisplatin and xerostomia during head and neck radiotherapy. Notable adverse effects include severe hypotension.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Rosuvastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rosuvastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) used to lower LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and to reduce cardiovascular risk. It competitively inhibits hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, decreasing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors to enhance LDL clearance. Established indications include hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, and prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular events. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Congenital hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid axis present at birth, most commonly caused by thyroid dysgenesis or defects in thyroid hormone synthesis, leading to deficient thyroxine. It affects newborns and may cause prolonged jaundice, lethargy, feeding difficulty, constipation, macroglossia, and impaired growth and neurodevelopment if untreated. Management is prompt newborn screening confirmation and early levothyroxine replacement with ongoing monitoring.

head: Diazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine that positively modulates GABA-A receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is used for anxiety disorders, acute alcohol withdrawal, muscle spasm, seizure control including status epilepticus, and procedural sedation. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation with respiratory depression, especially when combined with opioids or other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Substance-related disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting the brain and behavior, characterized by maladaptive use of alcohol, drugs, or medications that leads to intoxication, dependence, withdrawal, and impaired control despite harm. It can occur across all age groups, often with craving, social or occupational dysfunction, and medical complications. Management typically combines screening, psychosocial treatment, relapse prevention, and, for some substances, medication-assisted therapy and supervised withdrawal care.

head: Cefepime (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefepime is a fourth-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used to treat susceptible bacterial infections including pneumonia, urinary tract infections, skin and soft tissue infections, intra-abdominal infections, and febrile neutropenia. A notable adverse effect is neurotoxicity, including encephalopathy or seizures, especially in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Alfentanil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alfentanil is a short-acting synthetic opioid analgesic of the fentanyl (anilidopiperidine) class that acts primarily as a mu-opioid receptor agonist to produce analgesia and profound sedation. It is used intravenously as an analgesic adjunct in anesthesia and for induction or maintenance of general anesthesia. A notable risk is dose-dependent respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in patients with significant respiratory compromise without ventilatory support.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Mitomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mitomycin is an antineoplastic antibiotic of the mitomycin/aziridine alkylating-agent class that is enzymatically activated to form DNA cross-links, inhibiting DNA synthesis and replication without a single specific molecular target. It is used in several cancers, including gastric and pancreatic adenocarcinomas, and intravesically for superficial bladder cancer. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; hemolytic-uremic syndrome can also occur.
tail_desc: Hemolytic-uremic syndrome is a thrombotic microangiopathy affecting mainly the renal and hematologic systems, characterized by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury. It commonly follows Shiga toxin-producing Escherichia coli infection in children, though atypical complement-mediated forms occur. Clinical features include pallor, fatigue, decreased urine output, and hypertension; management is primarily supportive, with dialysis as needed and targeted complement inhibition in atypical cases.

head: Nilotinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nilotinib is an oral second-generation BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor, structurally an aminopyrimidine derivative related to imatinib. It selectively inhibits BCR-ABL and also KIT and PDGFR kinases, blocking proliferation of Philadelphia chromosome-positive leukemic cells. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome-positive chronic myeloid leukemia. A notable safety risk is QT-interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, or congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Nitrofurantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofurantoin is a nitrofuran antibacterial used primarily for treatment and prophylaxis of uncomplicated lower urinary tract infections. In bacteria, nitroreductases reduce it to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, ribosomal proteins, and other macromolecules, so it has no single primary molecular target. A notable risk is pulmonary toxicity; it is contraindicated at term in pregnancy and in significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked inherited red blood cell enzymopathy causing reduced protection against oxidative stress, leading to episodic hemolytic anemia. It commonly affects males, especially in malaria-endemic populations, and may present with neonatal jaundice, pallor, dark urine, fatigue, and jaundice after infections, fava beans, or oxidant drugs. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and supportive care during hemolysis.

head: Midodrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Midodrine is an orally administered prodrug and selective alpha-1 adrenergic agonist whose active metabolite, desglymidodrine, causes peripheral vasoconstriction via alpha-1 receptors. It is approved for symptomatic orthostatic hypotension. A notable adverse effect is supine hypertension, and it is generally contraindicated in patients with severe organic heart disease, acute renal disease, urinary retention, pheochromocytoma, or thyrotoxicosis.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Misoprostol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Misoprostol is a synthetic prostaglandin E1 analog that acts primarily as an agonist at prostaglandin EP receptors, reducing gastric acid secretion and increasing uterine tone and cervical softening. It is used for prevention of NSAID-induced gastric ulcers and in obstetrics/gynecology for medical abortion, cervical ripening, labor induction, and postpartum hemorrhage. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in pregnancy when used for ulcer prevention.
tail_desc: Allergic asthma is a common phenotype of asthma, a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways in the respiratory system, driven by allergen-triggered, IgE-associated immune responses with airway hyperresponsiveness and variable airflow obstruction. It often affects children and atopic individuals, causing episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often after allergen exposure. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and standard asthma therapy, especially inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.

head: Gabapentin enacarbil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Gabapentin enacarbil is an orally administered prodrug of gabapentin, an anticonvulsant/analgesic related to the GABA analog class. After conversion to gabapentin, it binds the α2δ subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release. It is approved for moderate-to-severe primary restless legs syndrome and postherpetic neuralgia. Somnolence is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Solifenacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Solifenacin is a competitive antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, chemically a tertiary amine, that primarily antagonizes muscarinic M3 receptors in bladder smooth muscle to reduce detrusor overactivity. It is indicated for overactive bladder with urgency, frequency, and urge urinary incontinence, and for neurogenic detrusor overactivity in some pediatric patients. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention.
tail_desc: Angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic disease in which the peripheral iris obstructs the anterior chamber angle, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid or intermittent rise in intraocular pressure. It typically affects anatomically predisposed older adults, especially those with shallow anterior chambers. Features include severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos, nausea, and a red eye; management is urgent pressure lowering followed by laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Cysteine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid used pharmaceutically as a nutritional additive, especially in parenteral nutrition solutions. It primarily serves as a metabolic precursor for protein synthesis and glutathione production rather than acting through a discrete drug target or receptor. Clinically, it is indicated to meet amino acid requirements, particularly in neonates and infants. Adverse effects may include hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Phenylketonuria is an autosomal recessive inborn error of amino acid metabolism affecting the nervous system, caused most often by phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency, leading to toxic phenylalanine accumulation. It typically presents in infancy or is detected by newborn screening; untreated patients develop intellectual disability, seizures, developmental delay, eczema, and hypopigmentation. Management is early lifelong dietary phenylalanine restriction, tyrosine supplementation, and selected use of sapropterin.

head: Isosorbide dinitrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isosorbide dinitrate is an organic nitrate vasodilator used mainly for prevention and treatment of angina pectoris and in some settings heart failure. It is bioactivated to nitric oxide, which stimulates soluble guanylate cyclase, increases cGMP, and relaxes vascular smooth muscle, predominantly reducing venous preload. A notable adverse effect is headache, and it is contraindicated with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors because of severe hypotension risk.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Dolasetron (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dolasetron is a serotonin 5-HT3 receptor antagonist antiemetic, administered as a prodrug rapidly converted to hydrodolasetron. By blocking 5-HT3 receptors on vagal afferents and centrally in the chemoreceptor trigger zone, it prevents nausea and vomiting, particularly chemotherapy-induced and postoperative emesis. A notable safety concern is dose-related QT interval prolongation, which can precipitate serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Nephrotic syndrome is a glomerular disorder of the kidneys characterized by increased glomerular permeability causing heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and often hyperlipidemia. It can occur in children and adults, commonly from primary glomerular diseases or secondary systemic conditions such as diabetes. Management targets the underlying cause and includes edema control, reduction of proteinuria, and prevention of complications.

head: Isoprenaline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoprenaline (isoproterenol) is a synthetic catecholamine and nonselective beta-adrenergic agonist that stimulates beta1 and beta2 adrenoceptors, increasing heart rate and contractility while causing bronchodilation. It has established use for symptomatic bradycardia and atrioventricular block, and historically for bronchospasm. Notable adverse effects include tachyarrhythmias; it is generally contraindicated in tachycardia or arrhythmia-prone ischemic heart disease.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Echothiophate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Echothiophate is an organophosphate, irreversible cholinesterase inhibitor used topically in ophthalmology. By covalently inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, it increases acetylcholine at cholinergic synapses, producing prolonged miosis and enhanced aqueous humor outflow. It has been used for glaucoma and accommodative esotropia. A notable adverse effect is cataract formation; it is generally avoided in angle-closure glaucoma unless definitive treatment is provided.
tail_desc: Iritis is inflammation of the iris, typically classified as anterior uveitis of the eye. It may be idiopathic or associated with autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or drug exposure. Patients usually present with eye pain, redness, photophobia, and blurred vision. Management generally involves prompt ophthalmologic evaluation, topical corticosteroids and cycloplegic agents, and treatment of any underlying cause.

head: Armodafinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Armodafinil is the R-enantiomer of modafinil, a wake-promoting central nervous system agent. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it is known to inhibit the dopamine transporter (DAT), increasing extracellular dopamine. It is approved to improve wakefulness in narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift work disorder. Notable adverse effects include headache and rare serious rash, including Stevens–Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Schizophreniform disorder is a psychotic disorder affecting the central nervous system, defined by schizophrenia-like symptoms lasting at least 1 month but less than 6 months. Its cause is not fully understood, likely involving multifactorial neurobiologic and genetic factors. Patients, often adolescents or young adults, present with delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech or behavior, and sometimes negative symptoms; management typically includes antipsychotic treatment, safety assessment, and psychiatric follow-up.

head: Chlorprothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorprothixene is a typical antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class that primarily acts by antagonizing dopamine D2 receptors, with additional antihistaminic and anticholinergic effects. It has been used to treat schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, and sometimes severe agitation. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation; extrapyramidal symptoms can also occur.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Allantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Allantoin is a diureide of glyoxylic acid used topically as a skin protectant and keratolytic in many over-the-counter dermatologic products. Its mechanism is not defined by a specific molecular target; it is thought to promote desquamation and support wound healing by softening keratin and enhancing moisture retention. Established uses include minor wounds, burns, chapped or irritated skin, and oral mucosal irritation. Adverse effects are uncommon but can include local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Dermatitis is a common inflammatory disorder of the skin, often caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, irritant or allergic exposures, or immune dysregulation. It typically presents with pruritus, erythema, scaling, and sometimes vesicles or lichenification, affecting both children and adults depending on subtype. Management generally focuses on trigger avoidance, skin hydration with emollients, and anti-inflammatory topical therapies such as corticosteroids.

head: Ferrous gluconate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ferrous gluconate is an oral iron salt, a ferrous (Fe2+) gluconate preparation used as a hematinic. It replenishes body iron stores and supplies elemental iron for hemoglobin and other iron-containing proteins; it does not act via a single specific receptor target. It is used to prevent and treat iron deficiency anemia. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation, especially constipation, nausea, and dark stools.
tail_desc: Inflammatory bowel disease is a chronic relapsing inflammatory disorder of the gastrointestinal tract, mainly comprising Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis. It is thought to result from dysregulated immune responses to intestinal microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals. Typical features include abdominal pain, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, weight loss, and fatigue, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood; management aims to induce and maintain remission with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, biologic, nutritional, and sometimes surgical therapy.

head: Eprosartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Eprosartan is a non-biphenyl, non-tetrazole angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) that selectively antagonizes the AT1 receptor, reducing angiotensin II–mediated vasoconstriction and aldosterone effects. It is used for the treatment of hypertension. Like other ARBs, it can cause hyperkalemia, and it is contraindicated during pregnancy because of fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Hepatic encephalopathy is a neuropsychiatric complication of acute or chronic liver failure affecting the central nervous system. It results from impaired hepatic detoxification and portosystemic shunting, leading to accumulation of neurotoxins, especially ammonia. Patients, often with cirrhosis, develop confusion, altered consciousness, asterixis, and sometimes coma; management targets precipitating factors and lowers gut-derived ammonia, commonly with lactulose and rifaximin.

head: Irbesartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irbesartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl tetrazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and diabetic nephropathy in patients with type 2 diabetes. A notable contraindication is pregnancy because it can cause fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Hereditary angioedema is a rare inherited disorder of the complement/bradykinin pathway causing recurrent, nonpruritic swelling of the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and upper airway. It most often results from SERPING1 mutations causing C1 inhibitor deficiency or dysfunction. Symptoms usually begin in childhood or adolescence; management focuses on on-demand treatment of attacks, especially laryngeal edema, and prophylaxis to prevent recurrences.

head: Antazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Antazoline is a first-generation ethanolamine antihistamine with anticholinergic properties. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conjunctivitis and rhinitis, often in topical ophthalmic or nasal formulations. A notable adverse effect is sedation; caution is warranted in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention due to anticholinergic effects.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Treprostinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Treprostinil is a prostacyclin (PGI2) analog vasodilator that primarily activates the prostacyclin IP receptor, increasing cAMP and inhibiting vascular smooth-muscle proliferation and platelet aggregation. It is established for pulmonary arterial hypertension and, in inhaled form, pulmonary hypertension associated with interstitial lung disease to improve exercise capacity. Common adverse effects include infusion-site pain, headache, flushing, and hypotension.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Floxuridine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Floxuridine is a fluorinated pyrimidine antimetabolite, an analog of 5-fluorouracil, that is converted intracellularly to fluorodeoxyuridylate, which inhibits thymidylate synthase and impairs DNA synthesis. It has been used mainly by hepatic arterial infusion for metastatic gastrointestinal cancers, especially colorectal liver metastases. A notable toxicity is severe gastrointestinal mucosal injury, including diarrhea and stomatitis.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Fluocinolone acetonide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fluocinolone acetonide is a synthetic fluorinated glucocorticoid corticosteroid. It binds the intracellular glucocorticoid receptor (NR3C1), modulating gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune mediators. It is used topically or intravitreally for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses and certain ocular inflammatory conditions, including diabetic macular edema. Notable adverse effects include skin atrophy with topical use and increased intraocular pressure or cataract with ocular administration.
tail_desc: Glaucoma is a group of chronic optic neuropathies affecting the eye, characterized by progressive retinal ganglion cell and optic nerve damage, often associated with elevated intraocular pressure from impaired aqueous humor outflow. It may be asymptomatic early, then cause peripheral visual field loss and eventual blindness, especially in older adults. Management aims to lower intraocular pressure with medications, laser therapy, or surgery.

head: Iodine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iodine is an essential halogen and topical anti-infective/thyroid-active agent. As elemental iodine or iodophors, it rapidly iodinates and oxidizes microbial proteins and nucleotides, producing broad-spectrum antiseptic activity; as iodide, it serves as substrate for thyroid hormone synthesis in the thyroid gland. Established uses include skin antisepsis and prevention or treatment of iodine deficiency. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions and iodine-induced thyroid dysfunction.
tail_desc: Congenital hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid axis present at birth, most commonly caused by thyroid dysgenesis or defects in thyroid hormone synthesis, leading to deficient thyroxine. It affects newborns and may cause prolonged jaundice, lethargy, feeding difficulty, constipation, macroglossia, and impaired growth and neurodevelopment if untreated. Management is prompt newborn screening confirmation and early levothyroxine replacement with ongoing monitoring.

head: Salsalate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salsalate is a nonacetylated salicylate NSAID and dimer of salicylic acid that is hydrolyzed to salicylate; it reduces prostaglandin synthesis primarily through cyclooxygenase inhibition, with relatively weak direct platelet inhibition compared with aspirin. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and other painful inflammatory conditions. Notable adverse effects include salicylism and gastrointestinal irritation or ulceration.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Zidovudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zidovudine (azidothymidine, AZT) is a thymidine analogue nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and causes viral DNA chain termination. It is used, in combination regimens, for treatment of HIV infection and to reduce maternal-fetal transmission. A notable adverse effect is bone marrow suppression, especially anemia and neutropenia.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Domperidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Domperidone is a benzimidazole-derived dopamine D2 receptor antagonist with peripheral prokinetic and antiemetic activity. By blocking D2 receptors in the gastrointestinal tract and chemoreceptor trigger zone, it enhances gastric emptying and reduces nausea and vomiting. It is used for symptomatic relief of nausea, vomiting, and gastroparesis-related dyspepsia. A notable risk is QT prolongation with potentially serious ventricular arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Felodipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is established for the treatment of hypertension and, in some settings, chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; it is generally contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Icosapent (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Icosapent is an ethyl ester prodrug of eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), an omega-3 fatty acid. Its lipid-lowering effects are not fully defined but include reduced hepatic very-low-density lipoprotein triglyceride synthesis and enhanced triglyceride clearance, without a single established molecular target. It is approved as icosapent ethyl to reduce severe hypertriglyceridemia and, in selected high-risk adults, cardiovascular risk; notable adverse effects include atrial fibrillation/flutter.
tail_desc: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by recurrent episodes of mania or hypomania and depression. Its cause is multifactorial, involving genetic susceptibility and dysregulation of brain neurotransmitter and circadian systems. It typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood and is managed with mood stabilizers, atypical antipsychotics, psychotherapy, and relapse prevention.

head: Raltegravir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Raltegravir is an HIV-1 integrase strand transfer inhibitor (INSTI) of the hydroxypyrimidinone antiretroviral class that blocks viral DNA integration by inhibiting the HIV integrase enzyme. It is approved, in combination with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. Notable adverse effects include rash and creatine kinase elevation, with rare severe skin hypersensitivity reactions reported.
tail_desc: Myopathy is a heterogeneous group of disorders of skeletal muscle, affecting the neuromuscular system. It results from inherited or acquired structural, metabolic, inflammatory, toxic, or endocrine abnormalities that impair muscle fiber function. Typical features include proximal muscle weakness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, myalgias, and sometimes elevated creatine kinase, occurring in children or adults. Management targets the cause and may include immunotherapy, stopping offending drugs, treating endocrine disease, rehabilitation, and supportive care.

head: Mesalazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mesalazine (mesalamine; 5-aminosalicylic acid) is an aminosalicylate anti-inflammatory drug used mainly for mild to moderate ulcerative colitis, including induction and maintenance of remission. It acts topically in the intestinal mucosa, inhibiting cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase pathways and reducing pro-inflammatory prostaglandins and leukotrienes. A notable risk is nephrotoxicity, including interstitial nephritis; hypersensitivity to salicylates is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Darunavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Darunavir is a second-generation HIV-1 protease inhibitor, a nonpeptidic sulfonamide antiretroviral. It inhibits the viral aspartyl protease, preventing cleavage of Gag-Pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. Boosted with ritonavir or cobicistat, it is indicated for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated with certain CYP3A-dependent drugs due to serious interactions.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Telmisartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telmisartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl benzimidazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and to reduce cardiovascular risk in selected high-risk patients. Notable risks include hyperkalemia and renal impairment, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Neonatal jaundice is a common condition of newborns involving yellow discoloration of the skin and sclera from elevated unconjugated bilirubin due to immature hepatic bilirubin conjugation and increased red cell turnover. It typically appears in the first days of life; management includes bilirubin monitoring, ensuring adequate feeding, treating underlying causes, and phototherapy or exchange transfusion when severe.

head: Penbutolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Penbutolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity, acting primarily at β1 and β2 receptors to reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It has been used for the treatment of hypertension. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is generally contraindicated in patients with bronchial asthma.
tail_desc: Congestive heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment reduces the heart’s ability to pump or fill effectively, causing pulmonary and/or systemic fluid congestion. It commonly affects older adults and patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy, producing dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance. Management focuses on treating the cause, diuresis, guideline-directed heart-failure medications, sodium/fluid control, and selected device or advanced therapies.

head: Sodium fluoride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluoride is an inorganic fluoride salt used as an anticaries agent and fluoride supplement. It acts topically and systemically by delivering fluoride ions that promote fluorapatite formation in tooth enamel and inhibit bacterial acid production. Established uses include prevention of dental caries in toothpaste, rinses, and supplements. Notable adverse effects include dental fluorosis with excessive exposure, especially during tooth development.
tail_desc: Hyperparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the parathyroid glands causing excess parathyroid hormone, which raises blood calcium and disrupts bone and kidney homeostasis. It may be primary, secondary to chronic kidney disease or vitamin D deficiency, or tertiary after prolonged stimulation. Common features include hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, bone pain, fractures, weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; management targets the cause, with parathyroidectomy for selected cases.

head: Efavirenz (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic hepatitis B virus infection (a disease)
head_desc: Efavirenz is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) used as part of combination antiretroviral therapy for HIV-1 infection. It binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, inhibiting viral DNA synthesis. Established indications include treatment of HIV-1 in adults and children in combination regimens. Notable adverse effects include rash and prominent central nervous system symptoms such as dizziness, insomnia, and vivid dreams.
tail_desc: Chronic hepatitis B virus infection is a chronic viral liver disease caused by persistent hepatitis B virus replication in hepatocytes, leading to ongoing hepatic inflammation and variable fibrosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with fatigue, abnormal liver enzymes, cirrhosis, or hepatocellular carcinoma risk, often affecting perinatally infected individuals or exposed adults. Management centers on monitoring and antiviral suppression to reduce liver damage and transmission.

head: Tazobactam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Tazobactam is a penicillanic acid sulfone beta-lactamase inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits many class A beta-lactamases, protecting susceptible beta-lactam antibiotics such as piperacillin from enzymatic degradation. It is used in fixed combinations, notably piperacillin/tazobactam, for treatment of susceptible bacterial infections including intra-abdominal, skin and soft-tissue, and lower respiratory tract infections. Hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Bezafibrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bezafibrate is a fibric acid derivative (fibrate) lipid-lowering agent that activates peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors, mainly PPAR-α, to reduce triglycerides and increase HDL cholesterol. It is used for hypertriglyceridemia and mixed hyperlipidemia. Notable adverse effects include myopathy, with increased risk when combined with statins, and it is generally contraindicated in severe hepatic or renal impairment.
tail_desc: End-stage renal failure is the terminal stage of chronic kidney disease in the renal system, marked by irreversible loss of kidney function and severely reduced glomerular filtration. It usually results from progressive disorders such as diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. Patients develop uremic symptoms, fluid and electrolyte disturbances, and anemia; management centers on dialysis, kidney transplantation, and treatment of complications.

head: Isoleucine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoleucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid used in amino acid replacement and parenteral nutrition formulations. It acts as a substrate for protein synthesis and energy metabolism rather than through a single pharmacologic receptor target. Clinically, it is included to prevent or treat amino acid deficiency in patients requiring nutritional support. Excessive amino acid administration may contribute to azotemia, especially in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Chronic kidney disease is a progressive disorder of the renal system defined by persistent kidney damage or reduced glomerular filtration for at least three months, commonly caused by diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. It often affects older adults and may present with albuminuria, edema, anemia, hypertension, and electrolyte abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause, slowing progression, reducing cardiovascular risk, and preparing for dialysis or transplantation in advanced stages.

head: Tenofovir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tenofovir is an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor, used clinically as the prodrugs tenofovir disoproxil and tenofovir alafenamide. After intracellular activation to tenofovir diphosphate, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase by competing with natural nucleotides and causing DNA chain termination. It is approved for HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B; notable risks include renal toxicity and reduced bone mineral density.
tail_desc: Monogenic obesity is a rare genetic disorder of energy-balance regulation affecting the neuroendocrine/hypothalamic pathways that control appetite and body weight. It usually results from single-gene defects, often in the leptin-melanocortin pathway, causing severe early-onset obesity with marked hyperphagia, sometimes with endocrine or developmental abnormalities. Management centers on specialist genetic evaluation, intensive weight-management support, and, in selected molecularly defined cases, targeted therapy.

head: Triazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic that positively modulates GABA\(_A\) receptors by binding the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include anterograde amnesia and next-day psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inhibitors.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Nitrofural (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofural (nitrofurazone) is a topical nitrofuran antibacterial. After enzymatic reduction of its nitro group in susceptible microbes, reactive intermediates damage bacterial DNA and other macromolecules, giving broad activity against many Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms. It has been used for topical prevention or treatment of skin infections in burns, grafts, wounds, and ulcers. A notable adverse effect is allergic contact dermatitis.
tail_desc: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked inherited red blood cell enzymopathy causing reduced protection against oxidative stress, leading to episodic hemolytic anemia. It commonly affects males, especially in malaria-endemic populations, and may present with neonatal jaundice, pallor, dark urine, fatigue, and jaundice after infections, fava beans, or oxidant drugs. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and supportive care during hemolysis.

head: Lithium carbonate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lithium carbonate is an oral lithium salt and mood stabilizer used primarily for acute mania and maintenance treatment in bipolar disorder. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it modulates intracellular signaling, including inhibition of inositol monophosphatase and glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK-3). A notable risk is nephrotoxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients with significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Pralatrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pralatrexate is a folate analogue antimetabolite antifolate chemotherapy agent that selectively enters cells via the reduced folate carrier and inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, disrupting DNA synthesis. It is approved for relapsed or refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma. A notable toxicity is severe mucositis; folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation are recommended to reduce hematologic and mucosal adverse effects.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Zaleplon (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zaleplon is a nonbenzodiazepine hypnotic of the pyrazolopyrimidine class that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA\(_A\) receptor benzodiazepine site, with relative selectivity for α1-containing receptors. It is approved for short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling asleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day drowsiness or dizziness, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug.
tail_desc: Congenital central hypoventilation syndrome is a rare genetic disorder of autonomic control of breathing, primarily affecting the respiratory and nervous systems, usually caused by PHOX2B mutations. It presents in neonates or infants with inadequate ventilatory response to hypercapnia and hypoxemia, especially during sleep, causing hypoventilation and apnea. Management centers on lifelong ventilatory support, monitoring, and care for associated autonomic dysfunction.

head: Furazolidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Furazolidone is a nitrofuran antimicrobial and antiprotozoal agent. After enzymatic reduction in susceptible organisms, it generates reactive intermediates that damage DNA and other macromolecules, though no single primary molecular target is established. It has been used for bacterial and protozoal gastrointestinal infections, including diarrhea, giardiasis, and cholera. A notable concern is monoamine oxidase inhibition, creating a contraindication with tyramine-rich foods or interacting sympathomimetic drugs.
tail_desc: Galactosemia is an inherited metabolic disorder affecting carbohydrate metabolism, usually caused by deficiency of enzymes needed to process galactose, classically galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase. It typically presents in newborns after milk feeding with poor feeding, vomiting, jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, cataracts, and risk of sepsis. Management is prompt lifelong dietary restriction of galactose/lactose and monitoring for complications.

head: Ambenonium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ambenonium is a quaternary ammonium anticholinesterase (reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) that increases acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase. It has been used in the symptomatic treatment of myasthenia gravis. A notable adverse effect is cholinergic toxicity, including abdominal cramps, diarrhea, bradycardia, and excessive salivation; it is generally contraindicated in mechanical intestinal or urinary obstruction.
tail_desc: Urinary tract obstruction is a genitourinary disorder in which partial or complete blockage of urine flow occurs anywhere from the renal pelvis to the urethra, causing increased upstream pressure, hydronephrosis, infection risk, and potential renal impairment. Symptoms may include flank or abdominal pain, lower urinary tract symptoms, anuria, hematuria, or recurrent urinary infection. Management focuses on urgent decompression when indicated and treatment of the underlying cause.

head: Tazobactam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tazobactam is a penicillanic acid sulfone beta-lactamase inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits many class A beta-lactamases, protecting susceptible beta-lactam antibiotics such as piperacillin from enzymatic degradation. It is used in fixed combinations, notably piperacillin/tazobactam, for treatment of susceptible bacterial infections including intra-abdominal, skin and soft-tissue, and lower respiratory tract infections. Hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Tamoxifen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM), a triphenylethylene derivative, that primarily antagonizes estrogen receptor signaling in breast tissue. It is used for hormone receptor–positive breast cancer treatment and prevention, including adjuvant and metastatic settings. Important adverse effects include increased risk of venous thromboembolism and endometrial cancer, so it is generally avoided in patients with prior thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Megestrol acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Megestrol acetate is a synthetic progestin derivative of progesterone that acts primarily as an agonist of the progesterone receptor, with additional glucocorticoid activity. It is used as an appetite stimulant for anorexia, cachexia, or significant weight loss, including in AIDS, and as hormonal therapy for advanced endometrial carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of thromboembolic events.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Isradipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isradipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels, mainly in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation. It is an established antihypertensive agent used for treatment of hypertension. A notable adverse effect is dose-related peripheral edema; like other dihydropyridines, it may also cause dizziness, flushing, and reflex tachycardia.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Isoniazid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoniazid is a first-line antitubercular hydrazide (isonicotinic acid hydrazide) prodrug activated by the mycobacterial catalase-peroxidase KatG; it inhibits mycolic acid synthesis primarily by targeting InhA, impairing mycobacterial cell-wall formation. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of tuberculosis, including latent TB infection. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity; peripheral neuropathy can also occur.
tail_desc: Peripheral neuropathy is a disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused by damage to peripheral nerves, commonly from diabetes, toxins, nutritional deficiencies, infections, or immune-mediated disease. It typically presents with distal numbness, tingling, burning pain, weakness, and reduced reflexes, often in a length-dependent “stocking-glove” pattern, especially in adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the cause, symptom control, and rehabilitation or foot care when needed.

head: Amiodarone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amiodarone is a class III antiarrhythmic, an iodinated benzofuran derivative that primarily prolongs cardiac repolarization by blocking potassium channels, while also inhibiting sodium and calcium channels and exerting noncompetitive beta-adrenergic effects. It is used for life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias and supraventricular arrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation. A notable adverse effect is pulmonary toxicity, including interstitial pneumonitis and fibrosis.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Nystatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nystatin is a polyene macrolide antifungal that binds ergosterol in fungal cell membranes, creating pores that increase permeability and cause leakage of intracellular contents. It is used topically or orally for mucocutaneous Candida infections, including oral candidiasis and cutaneous candidiasis. Notable adverse effects include local irritation and, with oral use, gastrointestinal upset; it is not used for systemic fungal infections.
tail_desc: Addison disease is primary adrenal insufficiency, an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex causing deficient cortisol and often aldosterone production, most commonly from autoimmune adrenal destruction. Patients may develop chronic fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia, with risk of acute adrenal crisis. Management is lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, often mineralocorticoid replacement, and stress-dose therapy during illness or surgery.

head: Digoxin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Digoxin is a cardiac glycoside that inhibits the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in cardiomyocytes, increasing intracellular calcium and enhancing cardiac contractility while augmenting vagal tone to slow atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used for symptomatic heart failure and ventricular rate control in atrial fibrillation or flutter.
