A notable adverse effect is potentially life-threatening arrhythmias, especially with toxicity.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Calcium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcium acetate is a calcium salt used as a phosphate binder. In the gastrointestinal tract it dissociates to release calcium, which binds dietary phosphate to form insoluble complexes and lowers phosphate absorption; it has no specific receptor target. It is established for control of hyperphosphatemia in patients with end-stage renal disease, and a notable risk is hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Digitalis poisoning is a toxic cardiovascular condition caused by excessive exposure to cardiac glycosides such as digoxin, producing inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase and resulting electrolyte-mediated conduction abnormalities. It typically affects patients receiving digoxin, especially older adults or those with renal impairment, and presents with nausea, vomiting, confusion, visual disturbances, bradyarrhythmias, or ventricular arrhythmias. Management centers on stopping the drug, correcting electrolytes, monitoring rhythm, and using digoxin-specific antibody fragments in severe cases.

head: Moxifloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Moxifloxacin is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including community-acquired pneumonia, acute bacterial sinusitis, acute exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and some skin or intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation; fluoroquinolones can also cause tendon rupture.
tail_desc: Peripheral nervous system disease comprises disorders affecting peripheral nerves, nerve roots, neuromuscular junctions, or peripheral ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord. Causes include inherited abnormalities, metabolic or immune-mediated injury, infection, trauma, and toxins. Typical features are distal weakness, sensory loss, pain, paresthesias, and reduced reflexes in children or adults. Management targets the cause, symptom control, rehabilitation, and prevention of complications.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Horner syndrome is a neuro-ophthalmic disorder of the sympathetic pathway supplying the eye and face, caused by disruption anywhere from the hypothalamus to the cervical sympathetic chain. It classically presents with ipsilateral ptosis, miosis, and facial anhidrosis, sometimes with apparent enophthalmos; congenital cases may show iris heterochromia. Management focuses on urgently identifying and treating the underlying lesion or cause.

head: Nafarelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nafarelin is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that binds pituitary GnRH receptors; after initial stimulation, continuous use downregulates gonadotropin secretion, suppressing ovarian steroid production. It is used for endometriosis and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is hypoestrogenic bone mineral loss; it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Coenzyme M (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Coenzyme M is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Vandetanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vandetanib is an oral small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the anilinoquinazoline class that targets RET, VEGFR-2, and EGFR signaling. It is approved for symptomatic or progressive medullary thyroid cancer in patients with unresectable locally advanced or metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is QT-interval prolongation, and it carries a boxed warning for torsades de pointes and sudden death.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Difluprednate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Difluprednate is a difluorinated prednisolone-derived corticosteroid used mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts as a glucocorticoid receptor agonist, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and leukocyte activity. It is approved for postoperative ocular inflammation and pain and for endogenous anterior uveitis. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, and it is contraindicated in most active ocular viral, mycobacterial, or fungal infections.
tail_desc: Ocular hypertension is an ophthalmic condition characterized by persistently elevated intraocular pressure without detectable glaucomatous optic nerve damage or visual field loss. It results from impaired aqueous humor outflow, increasing risk of primary open-angle glaucoma. Patients are often asymptomatic and identified on routine eye examination, especially adults with risk factors. Management centers on pressure-lowering eye drops and regular monitoring of optic nerves, visual fields, and intraocular pressure.

head: Lurasidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lurasidone is an atypical antipsychotic of the benzisothiazole class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at 5-HT7 and 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar depression. A notable adverse effect is akathisia; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Acrivastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acrivastine is a second-generation antihistamine of the alkylamine/propylamine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used for symptomatic relief of seasonal allergic rhinitis and urticaria, including sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itching. Compared with older antihistamines it is less sedating, but drowsiness can still occur; caution is advised in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Non-histaminic angioedema is a bradykinin-mediated or otherwise non–mast cell form of angioedema affecting the skin and mucosal/submucosal tissues. It causes recurrent, nonpruritic swelling of the face, extremities, gastrointestinal tract, or airway, often without urticaria and with poor response to antihistamines, corticosteroids, or epinephrine. Management focuses on airway protection, identifying the cause, and using targeted therapies such as C1-inhibitor replacement or bradykinin-pathway agents when indicated.

head: Lithium carbonate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lithium carbonate is an oral lithium salt and mood stabilizer used primarily for acute mania and maintenance treatment in bipolar disorder. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it modulates intracellular signaling, including inhibition of inositol monophosphatase and glycogen synthase kinase-3 (GSK-3). A notable risk is nephrotoxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients with significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Psoriasis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory disease primarily affecting the skin, driven by dysregulated T-cell and cytokine signaling that accelerates keratinocyte proliferation. It causes well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale, commonly on the scalp, elbows, and knees, and may involve nails or joints. It occurs in children and adults; management includes topical therapy, phototherapy, and systemic or biologic agents for more severe disease.

head: Protriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Protriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzocycloheptadiene family. It acts primarily by inhibiting neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is used for treatment of major depressive disorder. A notable adverse effect is anticholinergic toxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Gastroesophageal reflux disease is a chronic upper gastrointestinal disorder in which reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus, usually from lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction, causes troublesome symptoms or mucosal injury. Typical features are heartburn and acid regurgitation, sometimes chest pain, cough, or dysphagia, affecting adults and children. Management emphasizes weight loss, trigger avoidance, acid suppression with proton pump inhibitors, and selected antireflux procedures.

head: Enalapril (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Enalapril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug converted to enalaprilat that inhibits ACE, reducing formation of angiotensin II and aldosterone. It is established for treating hypertension, heart failure, and asymptomatic left ventricular dysfunction. A notable adverse effect is persistent dry cough; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in patients with a history of ACE inhibitor–associated angioedema.
tail_desc: Angioedema is a vascular permeability disorder of the skin and mucosa causing sudden, localized swelling, often involving the face, lips, tongue, extremities, or airway. It results from mast cell mediator release or bradykinin excess, and may be allergic, medication-induced, or hereditary. Patients present with episodic nonpitting edema, sometimes with urticaria or life-threatening airway compromise; management targets airway protection and treating the underlying mechanism.

head: Galantamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Galantamine is a tertiary alkaloid acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, also reported to allosterically modulate nicotinic acetylcholine receptors. By inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, it increases synaptic acetylcholine. It is approved for the symptomatic treatment of mild to moderate Alzheimer disease dementia. Notable adverse effects include nausea, vomiting, and bradycardia; it should be used cautiously in patients with cardiac conduction disorders.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Cilastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cilastatin is a renal dehydropeptidase I inhibitor, a carbapenem adjunct rather than an antibacterial itself. It blocks brush-border DHP-I in the kidney, preventing renal metabolism of imipenem and reducing nephrotoxic metabolites, thereby increasing urinary recovery of active imipenem. It is approved in fixed combination with imipenem/cilastatin for susceptible bacterial infections; adverse effects can include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Ferrous sulfate anhydrous (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ferrous sulfate anhydrous is an oral iron salt and hematinic used to prevent and treat iron deficiency and iron-deficiency anemia. It supplies elemental ferrous iron (Fe2+), which is absorbed mainly in the duodenum and incorporated into hemoglobin and other iron-containing proteins; it does not act via a specific receptor target. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation, especially nausea or constipation.
tail_desc: Iron metabolism disorders are a heterogeneous group of hematologic and systemic conditions affecting iron absorption, transport, storage, or utilization. They arise from genetic defects, chronic disease, blood loss, or iron overload states, causing iron deficiency or excess. Clinical features include anemia, fatigue, organ dysfunction, and tissue iron deposition, occurring in both children and adults. Management targets the cause and iron balance, using supplementation, chelation, phlebotomy, or treatment of underlying disease.

head: Dexrabeprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dexrabeprazole is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Chronic atrophic gastritis is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the gastric mucosa in the digestive system, characterized by loss of gastric glands with mucosal atrophy, often due to Helicobacter pylori infection or autoimmune destruction of parietal cells. Patients may be asymptomatic or have dyspepsia, anemia, or vitamin B12 deficiency; management targets the cause, corrects deficiencies, and includes surveillance in selected patients.

head: Dasatinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dasatinib is an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the thiazole carboxamide class that inhibits BCR-ABL and Src-family kinases, blocking proliferation signaling. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome–positive chronic myeloid leukemia and Ph+ acute lymphoblastic leukemia. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression; pleural effusion is also a well-recognized toxicity.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Docetaxel (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Docetaxel is a semisynthetic taxane antineoplastic agent that binds the β-subunit of tubulin, promoting microtubule assembly and inhibiting depolymerization, thereby blocking mitosis. It is used for breast cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, prostate cancer, gastric adenocarcinoma, and head and neck cancer. A notable toxicity is severe neutropenia; use is contraindicated in patients with baseline neutrophil counts below 1,500 cells/mm³.
tail_desc: Pulmonary edema is a respiratory condition in which fluid accumulates in the lung interstitium and alveoli, most often from elevated pulmonary capillary pressure due to left-sided heart failure, or less often from increased capillary permeability. It causes acute dyspnea, orthopnea, hypoxemia, and crackles, especially in patients with cardiac disease or critical illness. Management targets oxygenation and the underlying cause, often with diuretics and cardiovascular support.

head: Cevimeline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cevimeline is a muscarinic cholinergic agonist, a quinuclidine derivative, that primarily stimulates M3 muscarinic receptors to increase exocrine gland secretion, especially salivary flow. It is approved for treatment of dry mouth (xerostomia) in patients with Sjögren’s syndrome. Notable adverse effects include excessive sweating, and it is contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma and in conditions where miosis is undesirable, such as narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder in the biliary system, most often caused by gallstone obstruction of the cystic duct; less commonly it is acalculous in critically ill patients. It typically presents with right upper quadrant pain, fever, nausea, and Murphy sign, sometimes with leukocytosis. Management usually includes supportive care, antibiotics when infection is suspected, and cholecystectomy.

head: Hydroflumethiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroflumethiazide is a thiazide diuretic (benzothiadiazine derivative) that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Fosamprenavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fosamprenavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor prodrug of amprenavir. After conversion to amprenavir, it inhibits the viral aspartyl protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is rash, and it is contraindicated with drugs highly dependent on CYP3A4 for clearance.
tail_desc: Hepatitis C virus infection is a viral infectious disease of the liver caused by hepatitis C virus, leading to hepatic inflammation that may become chronic and progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, or hepatocellular carcinoma. Many patients are initially asymptomatic; others have fatigue, jaundice, or elevated liver enzymes. It affects exposed adults and is managed primarily with direct-acting antiviral therapy and liver disease monitoring.

head: Ipratropium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ipratropium is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic bronchodilator, structurally related to atropine. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle, reducing vagally mediated bronchoconstriction and secretions. It is used as an inhaled treatment for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and also for bronchospasm in asthma. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Tioguanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tioguanine (thioguanine, 6-thioguanine) is a thiopurine antimetabolite, a guanine analog that is converted intracellularly to thioguanine nucleotides, which inhibit de novo purine synthesis and become incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing replication and cell proliferation. It is used mainly in acute myeloid leukemia. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression, and hepatotoxicity including hepatic veno-occlusive disease can occur.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Paralytic ileus is a functional, nonmechanical obstruction of the gastrointestinal tract caused by impaired intestinal motility, commonly affecting the small and large bowel. It often occurs after abdominal surgery, severe illness, electrolyte disturbances, medications such as opioids, or peritonitis, and presents with abdominal distension, pain, nausea, vomiting, and absent or reduced bowel sounds. Management is mainly supportive, treating the underlying cause, bowel rest, fluid and electrolyte correction, and minimizing precipitating drugs.

head: Flurandrenolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flurandrenolide is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid (glucocorticoid) used topically for steroid-responsive inflammatory and pruritic dermatoses. It acts by binding the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and immune responses. Established indications include topical treatment of eczema and dermatitis. A notable adverse effect is local skin atrophy, and it is contraindicated in untreated cutaneous bacterial, fungal, or viral infections.
tail_desc: Adrenocortical insufficiency is an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex characterized by deficient glucocorticoid production, often with mineralocorticoid deficiency in primary disease, due to autoimmune destruction, infection, hemorrhage, or impaired pituitary ACTH stimulation. Patients may develop fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hyperpigmentation in primary forms. Management centers on lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, mineralocorticoid replacement when needed, and stress-dose therapy during illness.

head: Guanethidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanethidine is an adrenergic neuron–blocking antihypertensive of the guanidine class. It is taken up into peripheral sympathetic nerve terminals via the norepinephrine transporter (NET) and depletes or prevents release of norepinephrine, reducing sympathetic tone. It was used for severe hypertension. A notable adverse effect is marked orthostatic hypotension; it is generally ineffective in pheochromocytoma.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Vorinostat (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vorinostat is a hydroxamic acid histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor that blocks class I and II HDAC enzymes, increasing histone acetylation and altering gene expression to promote cell-cycle arrest and apoptosis. It is approved for treatment of cutaneous manifestations of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma in patients with persistent, progressive, or recurrent disease. Notable adverse effects include thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Vilazodone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vilazodone is a serotonergic antidepressant classified as a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor and 5-HT1A receptor partial agonist. Its primary target is the serotonin transporter (SERT), with additional partial agonism at 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for treatment of major depressive disorder in adults. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, particularly when combined with MAO inhibitors, which are contraindicated.
tail_desc: Serotonin syndrome is an acute drug-induced toxidrome of the central and peripheral nervous systems caused by excess serotonergic activity, typically after serotonergic medication use, overdose, or interactions. It is characterized by altered mental status, autonomic hyperactivity, and neuromuscular abnormalities such as clonus, hyperreflexia, and tremor. Management centers on stopping serotonergic agents, supportive care, benzodiazepines, and severe-case escalation.

head: Quinethazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quinethazone is a quinazoline-sulfonamide thiazide-like diuretic. It inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for edema and hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria and should be used cautiously in sulfonamide-sensitive patients.
tail_desc: Cholelithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, characterized by stone formation in the gallbladder, usually from cholesterol supersaturation or, less often, pigment precipitation. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease typically causes episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cases.

head: Bacampicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bacampicillin is an oral ester prodrug of ampicillin, a beta-lactam aminopenicillin antibiotic. After hydrolysis to ampicillin, it binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, skin/soft tissue, and some gastrointestinal infections. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Ammonia (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ammonia is a small endogenous/basic nitrogenous compound rather than a conventional therapeutic drug. Medicinally, ammonia solution is used mainly as an inhalant respiratory stimulant in managing vasovagal syncope or near-fainting, acting by irritating nasal and respiratory mucosa to trigger reflex inhalation; it has no specific molecular drug target. It can cause mucosal irritation and should not be applied directly to eyes or damaged skin.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Beclomethasone dipropionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Beclomethasone dipropionate is a synthetic corticosteroid (glucocorticoid prodrug) that is converted to beclomethasone-17-monopropionate and activates the glucocorticoid receptor, suppressing inflammatory gene expression and mediator release. It is used for maintenance treatment of asthma, allergic rhinitis, and inflammatory bowel disease in certain formulations. A notable adverse effect is oropharyngeal candidiasis with inhaled use.
tail_desc: Addison disease is primary adrenal insufficiency, an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex causing deficient cortisol and often aldosterone production, most commonly from autoimmune adrenal destruction. Patients may develop chronic fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia, with risk of acute adrenal crisis. Management is lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, often mineralocorticoid replacement, and stress-dose therapy during illness or surgery.

head: Testosterone propionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Testosterone propionate is a short-acting ester of testosterone, an androgen/anabolic steroid. After hydrolysis to testosterone, it binds and activates the androgen receptor, regulating transcription of androgen-responsive genes. It has been used as testosterone replacement for male hypogonadism and delayed puberty in selected males. Notable adverse effects include virilization, and it is contraindicated in prostate or male breast cancer.
tail_desc: Obesity is a chronic metabolic and nutritional disorder affecting whole-body energy homeostasis, characterized by excessive accumulation of adipose tissue due to sustained positive energy balance interacting with genetic, environmental, and behavioral factors. It typically presents in children or adults with elevated body mass index or central adiposity and related complications. Management centers on lifestyle intervention, treatment of comorbidities, anti-obesity pharmacotherapy, and bariatric surgery when indicated.

head: Darifenacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Darifenacin is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a selective muscarinic M3 receptor antagonist. By blocking M3 receptors on bladder detrusor smooth muscle, it reduces involuntary contractions and increases bladder capacity. It is approved for treatment of overactive bladder with urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency. A notable adverse effect is constipation; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Angioedema is a vascular permeability disorder of the skin and mucosa causing sudden, localized swelling, often involving the face, lips, tongue, extremities, or airway. It results from mast cell mediator release or bradykinin excess, and may be allergic, medication-induced, or hereditary. Patients present with episodic nonpitting edema, sometimes with urticaria or life-threatening airway compromise; management targets airway protection and treating the underlying mechanism.

head: Sodium fluorophosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluorophosphate is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Hyperparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the parathyroid glands causing excess parathyroid hormone, which raises blood calcium and disrupts bone and kidney homeostasis. It may be primary, secondary to chronic kidney disease or vitamin D deficiency, or tertiary after prolonged stimulation. Common features include hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, bone pain, fractures, weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; management targets the cause, with parathyroidectomy for selected cases.

head: Cysteamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cysteamine is an aminothiol and cystine-depleting agent used primarily to treat nephropathic cystinosis. Within lysosomes, it reacts with cystine to form cysteine and a cysteine-cysteamine mixed disulfide, enabling export through existing lysine transport pathways and reducing cystine accumulation. It is also used ophthalmically for corneal cystine crystals. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and sulfurous body odor.
tail_desc: Pseudotumor cerebri, or idiopathic intracranial hypertension, is a neurologic disorder characterized by elevated intracranial pressure without an intracranial mass or hydrocephalus, likely related to impaired cerebrospinal fluid absorption. It typically affects obese women of childbearing age and causes headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and papilledema. Management focuses on weight loss, acetazolamide, and vision-preserving procedures when severe.

head: Pilocarpine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pilocarpine is a direct-acting cholinergic agonist, an alkaloid muscarinic receptor agonist that primarily stimulates M3 receptors on exocrine glands and smooth muscle. It is used to treat xerostomia, including after head and neck radiotherapy and in Sjögren syndrome, and ophthalmically for glaucoma or miosis. A notable adverse effect is sweating; it is generally contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Iritis is inflammation of the iris, typically classified as anterior uveitis of the eye. It may be idiopathic or associated with autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or drug exposure. Patients usually present with eye pain, redness, photophobia, and blurred vision. Management generally involves prompt ophthalmologic evaluation, topical corticosteroids and cycloplegic agents, and treatment of any underlying cause.

head: Ambrisentan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ambrisentan is an orally active endothelin receptor antagonist of the propanoic acid class that selectively blocks the endothelin type A (ETA) receptor, reducing endothelin-1–mediated vasoconstriction and smooth muscle proliferation. It is approved for pulmonary arterial hypertension to improve exercise capacity and delay clinical worsening. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of embryo-fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Bortezomib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bortezomib is a boronic acid dipeptide proteasome inhibitor that reversibly targets the 26S proteasome, disrupting ubiquitin-mediated protein degradation and promoting apoptosis in malignant plasma cells. It is approved for multiple myeloma and mantle cell lymphoma. A notable adverse effect is peripheral neuropathy; it is contraindicated with intrathecal administration because of fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Pulmonary hypertension is a cardiopulmonary vascular disorder characterized by abnormally elevated pressure in the pulmonary arteries due to vasoconstriction, vascular remodeling, thrombosis, or secondary heart/lung disease. It causes progressive dyspnea, fatigue, chest discomfort, syncope, and right heart failure, affecting adults and children. Management targets the cause, uses supportive therapy, and may include pulmonary vasodilator drugs or transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Heparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, thereby inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa and reducing fibrin clot formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndromes, and during procedures such as hemodialysis. A notable adverse effect is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the colonic mucosa, typically beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. It reflects immune-mediated intestinal inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals. Common features include bloody diarrhea, urgency, abdominal pain, and relapsing course, often in adolescents and adults. Management focuses on anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapy, with colectomy for refractory or complicated disease.

head: Tramadol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tramadol is a centrally acting synthetic opioid analgesic of the aminocyclohexanol class. It provides pain relief through weak agonism at the mu-opioid receptor and inhibition of norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for moderate to moderately severe pain. A notable risk is seizures, and it is contraindicated in significant respiratory depression or acute intoxication with alcohol or other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Pseudotumor cerebri, or idiopathic intracranial hypertension, is a neurologic disorder characterized by elevated intracranial pressure without an intracranial mass or hydrocephalus, likely related to impaired cerebrospinal fluid absorption. It typically affects obese women of childbearing age and causes headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and papilledema. Management focuses on weight loss, acetazolamide, and vision-preserving procedures when severe.

head: Protriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Protriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzocycloheptadiene family. It acts primarily by inhibiting neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is used for treatment of major depressive disorder. A notable adverse effect is anticholinergic toxicity, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Methapyrilene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methapyrilene is a first-generation antihistamine of the ethylenediamine class that acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist. It was formerly used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions and as an antipruritic, often in topical or combination products. A notable adverse effect is sedation with anticholinergic effects; it is also historically recognized for hepatotoxic and carcinogenic findings in animal studies.
tail_desc: Thyrotoxicosis is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid system caused by excess circulating thyroid hormones, most commonly from hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease, toxic nodular goiter, or thyroiditis. It typically presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, especially in adults. Management targets the cause and symptom control, using beta-blockers and measures such as antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Tamoxifen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM), a triphenylethylene derivative, that primarily antagonizes estrogen receptor signaling in breast tissue. It is used for hormone receptor–positive breast cancer treatment and prevention, including adjuvant and metastatic settings. Important adverse effects include increased risk of venous thromboembolism and endometrial cancer, so it is generally avoided in patients with prior thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Cholic Acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cholic acid is a primary bile acid used as a bile acid replacement therapy. It acts by restoring the endogenous bile acid pool and activating the farnesoid X receptor (FXR), which suppresses bile acid synthesis. It is indicated for bile acid synthesis disorders due to single-enzyme defects and as adjunctive treatment of peroxisomal disorders including Zellweger spectrum disorders. Diarrhea is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Sclerosing cholangitis is a chronic cholestatic disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by inflammation, fibrosis, and multifocal stricturing of intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic bile ducts, leading to impaired bile flow and progressive liver injury. Patients may have fatigue, pruritus, jaundice, or recurrent cholangitis; many with primary disease have inflammatory bowel disease. Management focuses on symptom control, treating complications, endoscopic relief of dominant strictures, and liver transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Trifluoperazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trifluoperazine is a first-generation phenothiazine antipsychotic that primarily antagonizes dopamine D2 receptors in the central nervous system. It is used for schizophrenia and, at lower doses, short-term treatment of nonpsychotic anxiety. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including parkinsonism and tardive dyskinesia; it is generally contraindicated in coma or severe central nervous system depression.
tail_desc: Retinal disease refers to a broad group of ophthalmic disorders affecting the retina, the light-sensitive neural tissue of the eye. Causes include vascular, degenerative, inflammatory, inherited, or traumatic damage, leading to photoreceptor or retinal pigment epithelium dysfunction. Common features include blurred vision, scotomas, metamorphopsia, night blindness, or vision loss. Management depends on cause and may involve risk-factor control, laser therapy, intravitreal drugs, or surgery.

head: Dexlansoprazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dexlansoprazole is a proton pump inhibitor, the R-enantiomer of lansoprazole, that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is approved for healing and maintenance of healed erosive esophagitis and for treatment of symptomatic nonerosive gastroesophageal reflux disease. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea, including risk of Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system caused primarily by estrogen deficiency after menopause, which accelerates bone resorption and reduces bone mass and microarchitectural strength. It mainly affects older postmenopausal women and is often silent until fragility fractures occur, especially in the vertebrae, hip, or wrist. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Allopurinol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Allopurinol is a purine analog xanthine oxidase inhibitor that lowers uric acid by blocking conversion of hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid. It is used for chronic gout/hyperuricemia, prevention of uric acid nephropathy during cancer chemotherapy, and recurrent calcium oxalate stones with hyperuricosuria. A notable risk is severe allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis.
tail_desc: Cutaneous leukocytoclastic angiitis is a small-vessel vasculitis limited to the skin, usually caused by immune complex–mediated inflammation of postcapillary venules with neutrophilic vessel wall damage and nuclear debris. It typically presents in adults with palpable purpura, especially on the lower legs, sometimes with urticarial or necrotic lesions. Management focuses on removing triggers, skin-limited supportive care, and corticosteroids or other immunosuppression for persistent or severe disease.

head: Pyrantel (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pyrantel is a tetrahydropyrimidine anthelmintic, commonly used as pyrantel pamoate, that acts as a depolarizing neuromuscular blocker by activating nicotinic acetylcholine receptors on susceptible helminths, causing spastic paralysis and expulsion. It is used to treat intestinal nematode infections, especially enterobiasis (pinworm), ascariasis, and hookworm. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, and it is generally avoided in severe hepatic dysfunction.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Mebendazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mebendazole is a benzimidazole anthelmintic that binds parasite β-tubulin, inhibiting microtubule polymerization and glucose uptake, leading to helminth death. It is established for treatment of intestinal nematode infections including pinworm, roundworm, and hookworm. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal upset; it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy, particularly during the first trimester.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Ferrous gluconate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ferrous gluconate is an oral iron salt, a ferrous (Fe2+) gluconate preparation used as a hematinic. It replenishes body iron stores and supplies elemental iron for hemoglobin and other iron-containing proteins; it does not act via a single specific receptor target. It is used to prevent and treat iron deficiency anemia. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation, especially constipation, nausea, and dark stools.
tail_desc: Hereditary hemochromatosis is an inherited iron-overload disorder affecting multiple organs, especially the liver, pancreas, heart, joints, and skin. It most often results from HFE-related increased intestinal iron absorption, causing progressive tissue iron deposition. Adults may develop fatigue, arthralgia, hepatomegaly, diabetes, cirrhosis, cardiomyopathy, and skin bronzing. Management centers on regular phlebotomy, with iron studies monitoring and treatment of complications.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Constipation disorder is a gastrointestinal motility and defecation disorder characterized by infrequent bowel movements, hard or difficult-to-pass stools, and a sensation of incomplete evacuation. It commonly results from slow colonic transit, pelvic floor dysfunction, medications, diet, or systemic illness, and affects all ages, especially older adults. Management typically emphasizes hydration, fiber, exercise, treating causes, and use of laxatives or pelvic floor therapy.

head: Tetryzoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetryzoline is an imidazoline derivative sympathomimetic decongestant that acts primarily as an alpha-adrenergic receptor agonist, producing vasoconstriction of conjunctival blood vessels. It is used topically in ophthalmic preparations to relieve eye redness from minor irritation. A notable risk is rebound hyperemia with prolonged use, and it is generally contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Esmolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting cardioselective beta-blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class that primarily antagonizes beta-1 adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, contractility, and AV nodal conduction. It is used intravenously for rapid control of supraventricular tachycardia and perioperative or emergency hypertension/tachycardia. A notable risk is hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe bradycardia or advanced heart block.
tail_desc: Anaphylaxis is an acute, life-threatening systemic hypersensitivity reaction affecting multiple organ systems, especially respiratory and cardiovascular. It is usually triggered by IgE-mediated mast-cell and basophil degranulation after exposure to foods, drugs, insect stings, or other allergens. Typical features include urticaria, angioedema, wheeze, hypotension, and gastrointestinal symptoms in sensitized individuals. Management requires immediate intramuscular epinephrine, airway and circulatory support, trigger avoidance, and specialist follow-up.

head: Tesamorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with or without anosmia (a disease)
head_desc: Tesamorelin is a synthetic growth hormone–releasing hormone analog that binds pituitary GHRH receptors to stimulate endogenous growth hormone secretion and increase IGF-1. It is approved to reduce excess abdominal visceral adipose tissue in adults with HIV-associated lipodystrophy. Notable risks include glucose intolerance or hyperglycemia, and it is contraindicated in active malignancy and disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
tail_desc: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with or without anosmia is a rare genetic endocrine disorder of hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis development or signaling, causing deficient gonadotropin-releasing hormone activity and low sex steroid production. It typically presents in adolescents or adults with delayed or absent puberty, infertility, and sometimes anosmia (Kallmann syndrome). Management centers on sex steroid replacement and fertility induction with gonadotropins or pulsatile GnRH.

head: Decitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Decitabine is a hypomethylating antineoplastic agent, a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog. After intracellular activation, it incorporates into DNA and inhibits DNA methyltransferases, causing DNA hypomethylation and altered gene expression. It is approved for myelodysplastic syndromes and used in acute myeloid leukemia. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression, including neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Thiothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiothixene is a first-generation (typical) antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class. Its antipsychotic effects are primarily attributed to antagonism of central dopamine D2 receptors, with additional activity at other neurotransmitter receptors. It is used for the treatment of schizophrenia. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including drug-induced parkinsonism and akathisia.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Trifluoperazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trifluoperazine is a first-generation phenothiazine antipsychotic that primarily antagonizes dopamine D2 receptors in the central nervous system. It is used for schizophrenia and, at lower doses, short-term treatment of nonpsychotic anxiety. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including parkinsonism and tardive dyskinesia; it is generally contraindicated in coma or severe central nervous system depression.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadiazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections and, with pyrimethamine, for toxoplasmosis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe rash such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome; it is generally contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy and near term pregnancy or in neonates due to kernicterus risk.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Hydroquinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroquinone is a topical depigmenting agent and phenolic compound used to treat hyperpigmentation disorders such as melasma and solar lentigines. It reduces melanogenesis primarily by inhibiting tyrosinase in melanocytes and may affect melanosome formation. A notable adverse effect is local skin irritation; prolonged or inappropriate use can cause exogenous ochronosis, and it is generally avoided on damaged skin.
tail_desc: Dermatitis is a common inflammatory disorder of the skin, often caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, irritant or allergic exposures, or immune dysregulation. It typically presents with pruritus, erythema, scaling, and sometimes vesicles or lichenification, affecting both children and adults depending on subtype. Management generally focuses on trigger avoidance, skin hydration with emollients, and anti-inflammatory topical therapies such as corticosteroids.

head: Metyrapone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: familial adrenal hypoplasia with absent pituitary luteinizing hormone (a disease)
head_desc: Metyrapone is an adrenal steroidogenesis inhibitor, a pyridine derivative that selectively inhibits 11beta-hydroxylase (CYP11B1), reducing cortisol synthesis and increasing 11-deoxycortisol. It is used to diagnose hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis function and to treat endogenous Cushing syndrome, particularly hypercortisolemia. Notable adverse effects include hypertension and hirsutism from accumulation of mineralocorticoid and androgen precursors.
tail_desc: familial adrenal hypoplasia with absent pituitary luteinizing hormone is a disease or medical condition.

head: Adomiparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Adomiparin is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system caused primarily by estrogen deficiency after menopause, which accelerates bone resorption and reduces bone mass and microarchitectural strength. It mainly affects older postmenopausal women and is often silent until fragility fractures occur, especially in the vertebrae, hip, or wrist. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Megestrol acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Megestrol acetate is a synthetic progestin derivative of progesterone that acts primarily as an agonist of the progesterone receptor, with additional glucocorticoid activity. It is used as an appetite stimulant for anorexia, cachexia, or significant weight loss, including in AIDS, and as hormonal therapy for advanced endometrial carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of thromboembolic events.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Risedronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Risedronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate (pyridinyl bisphosphonate) that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget’s disease of bone, and prevention or treatment of glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophageal irritation or ulceration, and it is contraindicated in hypocalcemia and significant esophageal motility disorders.
tail_desc: Acute urate nephropathy is an acute kidney injury caused by intratubular precipitation of uric acid crystals in the kidneys, typically after rapid cell breakdown such as tumor lysis syndrome in patients with leukemia or lymphoma. It presents with abrupt azotemia, oliguria, hyperuricemia, and sometimes flank pain or hematuria. Management focuses on vigorous hydration, urate-lowering therapy, correction of metabolic abnormalities, and dialysis if severe.

head: Tolcapone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tolcapone is a nitrocatechol catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) inhibitor that blocks peripheral and central COMT, reducing levodopa metabolism and prolonging its effect. It is used as an adjunct to levodopa/carbidopa for Parkinson disease with end-of-dose motor fluctuations. A notable risk is severe, potentially fatal hepatotoxicity, making liver disease a major contraindication and requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Tardive dyskinesia is a hyperkinetic movement disorder of the nervous system, usually caused by chronic exposure to dopamine receptor–blocking drugs, especially antipsychotics and some antiemetics, likely involving dopamine receptor supersensitivity in basal ganglia circuits. It causes persistent involuntary, repetitive movements, especially of the face, mouth, tongue, and sometimes trunk or limbs, mainly in patients receiving long-term neuroleptic therapy; management focuses on reducing offending drugs when feasible and using VMAT2 inhibitors.

head: Bismuth subsalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bismuth subsalicylate is a bismuth-containing salicylate antidiarrheal and gastroprotective agent. It acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract, where bismuth has antimicrobial and mucosal protective effects, while salicylate inhibits cyclooxygenase and reduces intestinal prostaglandin-mediated secretion and inflammation. It is used for diarrhea, dyspepsia, heartburn, and nausea. A notable adverse effect is black discoloration of stools and tongue.
tail_desc: Brain disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the central nervous system, specifically the brain, rather than a single diagnosis. Causes include degenerative, vascular, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, neoplastic, congenital, or metabolic pathology. Clinical features vary widely, including cognitive, behavioral, motor, sensory, language, seizure, or consciousness disturbances, across all ages. Management depends on the specific cause and typically combines diagnosis-directed treatment, symptom control, and rehabilitation.

head: Benzoyl peroxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzoyl peroxide is a topical organic peroxide used mainly as an anti-acne agent. It decomposes in the skin to release free-radical oxygen species, producing bactericidal activity against Cutibacterium acnes and mild keratolytic/comedolytic effects. It is established for acne vulgaris treatment, alone or with other agents. Common adverse effects include local skin irritation, dryness, erythema, and peeling.
tail_desc: Peripheral arterial disease is an atherosclerotic vascular disorder of the peripheral arteries, most often affecting the lower limbs. Arterial narrowing reduces blood flow, causing exertional leg pain (intermittent claudication), diminished pulses, and in advanced cases rest pain, ulcers, or tissue loss. It is common in older adults and smokers. Management focuses on risk-factor modification, antiplatelet and statin therapy, supervised exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Dimenhydrinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dimenhydrinate is a first-generation H1 antihistamine and antiemetic, a salt of diphenhydramine with 8-chlorotheophylline. It acts primarily by antagonizing histamine H1 receptors, with additional antimuscarinic effects that suppress vestibular stimulation and the vomiting center. It is used for prevention and treatment of motion sickness, nausea, vomiting, and vertigo. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to dimenhydrinate or diphenhydramine.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Pitavastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pitavastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) used to treat primary hyperlipidemia and mixed dyslipidemia by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and increasing LDL clearance. It lowers LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and may raise HDL cholesterol. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Phylloquinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phylloquinone (vitamin K1) is a fat-soluble naphthoquinone vitamin used as a hemostatic antidote to vitamin K deficiency and warfarin-associated hypoprothrombinemia. It acts as a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase in the liver, enabling activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. Notable risks include anaphylactoid reactions with rapid intravenous administration.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis obliterans is a chronic occlusive peripheral arterial disease, usually affecting lower-extremity arteries, caused by progressive atherosclerotic plaque formation with luminal narrowing and impaired blood flow. It typically occurs in older adults, especially smokers and patients with diabetes, presenting with intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool limbs, rest pain, or ischemic ulcers. Management focuses on risk-factor control, antiplatelet therapy, exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Nitrofural (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofural (nitrofurazone) is a topical nitrofuran antibacterial. After enzymatic reduction of its nitro group in susceptible microbes, reactive intermediates damage bacterial DNA and other macromolecules, giving broad activity against many Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms. It has been used for topical prevention or treatment of skin infections in burns, grafts, wounds, and ulcers. A notable adverse effect is allergic contact dermatitis.
tail_desc: Drug-induced liver injury is an adverse hepatic disorder caused by medications, herbal products, or supplements, through intrinsic dose-dependent toxicity or idiosyncratic reactions. It can present with asymptomatic liver enzyme elevations, jaundice, fatigue, nausea, pruritus, or acute liver failure in exposed patients. Management centers on prompt withdrawal of the offending agent, supportive care, and monitoring for resolution or progression.

head: Cefazolin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefazolin is a first-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It is widely used for susceptible gram-positive and some gram-negative infections and for perioperative surgical prophylaxis. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, including anaphylaxis, particularly in patients with cephalosporin or serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Quinine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quinine is a cinchona alkaloid antimalarial that acts in Plasmodium blood stages by inhibiting heme detoxification, causing toxic heme accumulation within the parasite food vacuole. It is used primarily for treatment of uncomplicated and severe falciparum malaria, often in combination regimens. A notable adverse effect is cinchonism; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to quinine or a history of quinine-induced thrombocytopenia.
