tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Histidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Histidine is an essential amino acid used as a nutritional/pharmaceutical ingredient, including in amino acid formulations and specialty foods for patients with histidinemia. It serves as a substrate in protein synthesis and as a precursor of histamine rather than acting through a single drug receptor target. Adverse effects with supplementation may include gastrointestinal intolerance, and caution is warranted in disorders of amino acid metabolism.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Physostigmine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Physostigmine is a reversible carbamate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (an indirect cholinergic agonist) that increases acetylcholine at muscarinic and nicotinic synapses, including in the CNS. It is used to reverse anticholinergic toxicity and has also been used for glaucoma. Notable risks include bradycardia and seizures, and it is contraindicated in tricyclic antidepressant overdose due to potential cardiac toxicity.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Cobalamin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cobalamin (vitamin B12) is a water-soluble cobalt-containing corrinoid vitamin used as replacement therapy. It acts as a cofactor for methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, restoring DNA synthesis and neurologic function in deficiency states. It is indicated for vitamin B12 deficiency, including pernicious anemia. Hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known cobalt or cobalamin hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Oxymetholone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetholone is a synthetic 17α-alkylated anabolic-androgenic steroid, an orally active testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor to promote erythropoiesis and protein anabolism. It has been used for anemia due to deficient red cell production, including aplastic anemia. A notable risk is serious hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice and hepatic tumors, making liver disease a contraindication.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Ampicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ampicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, gastrointestinal, and meningitis pathogens such as Listeria monocytogenes and Enterococcus species. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Galantamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Galantamine is a tertiary alkaloid acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, also reported to allosterically modulate nicotinic acetylcholine receptors. By inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, it increases synaptic acetylcholine. It is approved for the symptomatic treatment of mild to moderate Alzheimer disease dementia. Notable adverse effects include nausea, vomiting, and bradycardia; it should be used cautiously in patients with cardiac conduction disorders.
tail_desc: Allergic asthma is a common phenotype of asthma, a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways in the respiratory system, driven by allergen-triggered, IgE-associated immune responses with airway hyperresponsiveness and variable airflow obstruction. It often affects children and atopic individuals, causing episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often after allergen exposure. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and standard asthma therapy, especially inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.

head: Febuxostat (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Febuxostat is a non-purine xanthine oxidase inhibitor used to lower uric acid. It selectively inhibits xanthine oxidase, reducing conversion of hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid. It is approved for chronic management of hyperuricemia in patients with gout. A notable safety concern is increased risk of cardiovascular death compared with allopurinol; caution is advised in patients with established cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Desoximetasone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desoximetasone is a high-potency topical corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class, used to relieve inflammatory and pruritic manifestations of corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses such as psoriasis and eczema. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and immune responses. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy; it is generally contraindicated in untreated cutaneous infections.
tail_desc: Sheehan syndrome is postpartum hypopituitarism caused by ischemic necrosis of the pituitary gland after severe obstetric hemorrhage, affecting the endocrine system. It occurs in women after childbirth and commonly presents with failure to lactate, amenorrhea, fatigue, hypotension, and features of adrenal and thyroid hormone deficiency. Management centers on prompt diagnosis and lifelong replacement of deficient pituitary-dependent hormones.

head: Glutethimide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glutethimide is a piperidine-dione sedative-hypnotic, structurally related to barbiturates, that acts as a central nervous system depressant by enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission, likely via the GABA\(_A\) receptor complex. It was formerly used for insomnia and sedation. Notable risks include respiratory depression and dependence, and it is contraindicated in porphyria.
tail_desc: Hypoglycemia is a metabolic/endocrine condition of abnormally low blood glucose, affecting systemic energy supply, especially the central nervous system. It commonly results from excess insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy, fasting, alcohol use, or critical illness. Symptoms include sweating, tremor, palpitations, confusion, seizures, or coma, particularly in people with diabetes. Management focuses on prompt glucose administration and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Naratriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naratriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It treats acute migraine attacks, with or without aura, by causing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. Notable adverse effects include paresthesia, dizziness, and chest or throat tightness; it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease and certain cerebrovascular syndromes.
tail_desc: Obesity is a chronic metabolic and nutritional disorder affecting whole-body energy homeostasis, characterized by excessive accumulation of adipose tissue due to sustained positive energy balance interacting with genetic, environmental, and behavioral factors. It typically presents in children or adults with elevated body mass index or central adiposity and related complications. Management centers on lifestyle intervention, treatment of comorbidities, anti-obesity pharmacotherapy, and bariatric surgery when indicated.

head: Silver sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Silver sulfadiazine is a topical sulfonamide antimicrobial combining sulfadiazine with silver ions. It acts mainly by releasing silver, which disrupts bacterial cell membranes and proteins, while sulfadiazine inhibits folate synthesis via dihydropteroate synthase. It is established for prevention and treatment of wound sepsis in second- and third-degree burns. A notable contraindication is use in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Lenalidomide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lenalidomide is an immunomodulatory thalidomide analogue (IMiD) that binds cereblon, altering the CRL4 E3 ubiquitin ligase to promote degradation of Ikaros proteins IKZF1/IKZF3 and enhance antitumor immune effects. It is approved for multiple myeloma, myelodysplastic syndrome with del(5q), mantle cell lymphoma, and follicular lymphoma. A notable risk is severe teratogenicity, making pregnancy a contraindication.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Lopinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lopinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, commonly coformulated with ritonavir to boost exposure via CYP3A inhibition. It inhibits the viral HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and producing immature, noninfectious virions. It is approved, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it also has important CYP3A-mediated drug interaction contraindications.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Glycine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glycine is a nonessential amino acid and inhibitory neurotransmitter used clinically as an irrigating solution in certain urologic procedures and as a component of parenteral nutrition. It acts as an inhibitory agonist at glycine receptors and a co-agonist at NMDA-type glutamate receptors. Excess systemic absorption of glycine irrigation fluid can cause hyperglycinemia, with visual disturbances or encephalopathy.
tail_desc: Phenylketonuria is an autosomal recessive inborn error of amino acid metabolism affecting the nervous system, caused most often by phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency, leading to toxic phenylalanine accumulation. It typically presents in infancy or is detected by newborn screening; untreated patients develop intellectual disability, seizures, developmental delay, eczema, and hypopigmentation. Management is early lifelong dietary phenylalanine restriction, tyrosine supplementation, and selected use of sapropterin.

head: Nitrofurantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofurantoin is a nitrofuran antibacterial used primarily for treatment and prophylaxis of uncomplicated lower urinary tract infections. In bacteria, nitroreductases reduce it to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, ribosomal proteins, and other macromolecules, so it has no single primary molecular target. A notable risk is pulmonary toxicity; it is contraindicated at term in pregnancy and in significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Cefoperazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefoperazone is a third-generation cephalosporin β-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, intra-abdominal, skin/soft-tissue, and biliary tract infections. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia with bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Sunitinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sunitinib is an oral small-molecule multi-target tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the indolinone class that blocks VEGFR, PDGFR, KIT, FLT3, CSF1R, and RET signaling, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and proliferation. It is approved for renal cell carcinoma, imatinib-resistant or intolerant gastrointestinal stromal tumor, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. A notable toxicity is cardiotoxicity, including decreased left ventricular ejection fraction.
tail_desc: Posterior leukoencephalopathy syndrome, more commonly called posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome, is an acute neurologic disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by vasogenic edema predominantly in the parieto-occipital white matter, often triggered by severe hypertension, eclampsia, renal failure, sepsis, or immunosuppressive drugs. It typically presents with headache, seizures, visual disturbance, and encephalopathy in adults or children, and management focuses on correcting the precipitating cause and controlling blood pressure and seizures.

head: Dactinomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dactinomycin (actinomycin D) is an antineoplastic polypeptide antibiotic of the actinomycin family that intercalates into DNA, primarily at guanine-cytosine pairs, thereby inhibiting DNA-dependent RNA synthesis. It is used in cancers including Wilms tumor, rhabdomyosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and gestational trophoblastic neoplasia. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Orciprenaline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Orciprenaline (metaproterenol) is a sympathomimetic phenylethanolamine and selective beta-2 adrenergic receptor agonist that relaxes bronchial smooth muscle by stimulating adenylate cyclase and increasing cyclic AMP. It has been used as a bronchodilator for reversible bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive airway disease. A notable adverse effect is tachycardia, and it is used cautiously in patients with significant cardiac arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Dipotassium phosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dipotassium phosphate is an inorganic potassium salt and phosphate replacement/electrolyte supplement. It dissociates to provide phosphate for intracellular metabolic processes and potassium for maintenance of cellular membrane potential, without a single specific molecular target. Clinically, it is used to prevent or treat hypophosphatemia, including in parenteral nutrition formulations. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in patients with severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Mitomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mitomycin is an antineoplastic antibiotic of the mitomycin/aziridine alkylating-agent class that is enzymatically activated to form DNA cross-links, inhibiting DNA synthesis and replication without a single specific molecular target. It is used in several cancers, including gastric and pancreatic adenocarcinomas, and intravesically for superficial bladder cancer. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; hemolytic-uremic syndrome can also occur.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Atovaquone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atovaquone is a hydroxynaphthoquinone antiparasitic/antiprotozoal agent that inhibits the mitochondrial electron transport chain by selectively targeting the cytochrome bc1 complex in susceptible organisms. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and, combined with proguanil, for malaria; it is also used with azithromycin for babesiosis. Notable adverse effects include rash, gastrointestinal upset, and hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Quinethazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quinethazone is a quinazoline-sulfonamide thiazide-like diuretic. It inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for edema and hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria and should be used cautiously in sulfonamide-sensitive patients.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Oxaprozin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxaprozin is a propionic acid–derived nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity, including aspirin-sensitive asthma.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Heparin, bovine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin, bovine, is an animal-derived unfractionated heparin, a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant obtained from bovine tissues. It potentiates antithrombin III, accelerating inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism and other settings requiring anticoagulation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Intracerebral hemorrhage is a subtype of stroke in the central nervous system caused by bleeding directly into the brain parenchyma, usually from rupture of small vessels due to chronic hypertension or cerebral amyloid angiopathy. It presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits, headache, vomiting, and reduced consciousness, especially in older adults. Management focuses on urgent stabilization, blood pressure control, reversal of anticoagulation, and neurosurgical evaluation in selected cases.

head: Rasagiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rasagiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class that increases central dopamine by inhibiting MAO-B. It is approved for the treatment of Parkinson’s disease, as monotherapy or adjunct to levodopa. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome when combined with serotonergic drugs; it is also contraindicated with other MAO inhibitors.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Cerivastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cerivastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It was approved for hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia but was withdrawn worldwide because of a high risk of severe myopathy and potentially fatal rhabdomyolysis, especially with gemfibrozil coadministration.
tail_desc: Nodular fasciitis is a benign, rapidly growing reactive myofibroblastic proliferation of the soft tissues, usually arising in subcutaneous fascia of the upper extremities, trunk, or head and neck. It typically affects young to middle-aged adults and presents as a small, sometimes tender solitary mass that can mimic sarcoma clinically and histologically. Management is usually local excision, with low recurrence and occasional spontaneous regression.

head: Nelarabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nelarabine is a purine nucleoside antimetabolite and prodrug of arabinosylguanine (ara-G). It is converted intracellularly to ara-GTP, which is incorporated into DNA and inhibits DNA synthesis, causing cytotoxicity particularly in T lymphoblasts. It is approved for relapsed or refractory T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia and T-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma. A notable toxicity is severe neurotoxicity, including peripheral and central neurologic effects.
tail_desc: Peripheral neuropathy is a disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused by damage to peripheral nerves, commonly from diabetes, toxins, nutritional deficiencies, infections, or immune-mediated disease. It typically presents with distal numbness, tingling, burning pain, weakness, and reduced reflexes, often in a length-dependent “stocking-glove” pattern, especially in adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the cause, symptom control, and rehabilitation or foot care when needed.

head: Cefotetan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefotetan is a parenteral second-generation cephalosporin (cephamycin) beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, skin/soft tissue, urinary tract, and perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia/bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Hemolytic anemia is a blood disorder of the hematologic system caused by premature destruction of red blood cells, leading to reduced oxygen-carrying capacity. It may be inherited or acquired, including immune-mediated forms. Typical features include fatigue, pallor, jaundice, dark urine, and splenomegaly, affecting both children and adults. Management targets the cause and may include transfusion, folate, corticosteroids, or splenectomy.

head: Prazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic, structurally a 1,4-benzodiazepine derivative, used mainly for the short-term treatment of anxiety disorders. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing GABA-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated in myasthenia gravis and severe respiratory insufficiency.
tail_desc: Schizophreniform disorder is a psychotic disorder affecting the central nervous system, defined by schizophrenia-like symptoms lasting at least 1 month but less than 6 months. Its cause is not fully understood, likely involving multifactorial neurobiologic and genetic factors. Patients, often adolescents or young adults, present with delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech or behavior, and sometimes negative symptoms; management typically includes antipsychotic treatment, safety assessment, and psychiatric follow-up.

head: Carbenicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carbenicillin is a semisynthetic carboxypenicillin, a beta-lactam antibiotic active particularly against gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits the transpeptidation step of bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan synthesis, leading to cell lysis. It has been used for susceptible urinary tract and systemic infections. Notable adverse effects include bleeding due to impaired platelet function, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Furosemide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Furosemide is a loop diuretic, chemically a sulfamoylanthranilic acid derivative, that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter (NKCC2, SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used for edema associated with heart failure, cirrhosis, or renal disease, and for hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Bladder neck obstruction is a lower urinary tract outflow disorder of the urinary system in which the bladder neck fails to open adequately during voiding, causing functional or anatomic resistance to urine flow. It typically presents with obstructive and irritative voiding symptoms, elevated post-void residual, or urinary retention, often in men. Management generally includes alpha-blockers, treatment of contributing causes, and selected surgical bladder neck incision or resection.

head: Quinethazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quinethazone is a quinazoline-sulfonamide thiazide-like diuretic. It inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for edema and hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria and should be used cautiously in sulfonamide-sensitive patients.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Enoxaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin (factor IIa). It is used to prevent and treat venous thromboembolism and in acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Hypertensive retinopathy is a retinal microvascular disorder of the eye caused by chronic or severe systemic hypertension, leading to arteriolar narrowing, vascular leakage, and ischemic injury. It may be asymptomatic or cause blurred vision; fundus findings include arteriovenous nicking, hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, hard exudates, and sometimes papilledema in malignant hypertension. Management centers on prompt, sustained blood pressure control and treatment of associated cardiovascular risk factors.

head: Vardenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vardenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzenesulfonamide derivative, that selectively blocks PDE5 to increase cyclic GMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation in the corpus cavernosum. It is approved for erectile dysfunction. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nitrates, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Plasma cell myeloma, or multiple myeloma, is a malignant plasma-cell neoplasm of the bone marrow and hematologic system characterized by clonal immunoglobulin-producing cells and monoclonal protein. It typically affects older adults and causes bone pain or lytic lesions, anemia, renal impairment, hypercalcemia, and recurrent infections. Management usually combines systemic anti-myeloma therapy, supportive care, and, in eligible patients, autologous stem-cell transplantation.

head: Lorazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic and sedative-hypnotic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA\(_A\) receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders, short-term relief of anxiety symptoms, status epilepticus, preanesthetic sedation, and insomnia in selected settings. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation and respiratory depression, especially with other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Nevirapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nevirapine is a dipyridodiazepinone non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) that binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, blocking viral DNA synthesis. It is used, in combination antiretroviral therapy, for treatment of HIV-1 infection and has also been used to reduce mother-to-child transmission. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, including potentially fatal hepatitis, and serious rash such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Drug-induced liver injury is an adverse hepatic disorder caused by medications, herbal products, or supplements, through intrinsic dose-dependent toxicity or idiosyncratic reactions. It can present with asymptomatic liver enzyme elevations, jaundice, fatigue, nausea, pruritus, or acute liver failure in exposed patients. Management centers on prompt withdrawal of the offending agent, supportive care, and monitoring for resolution or progression.

head: Tinidazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tinidazole is a 5-nitroimidazole antimicrobial and antiprotozoal drug. In susceptible anaerobic bacteria and protozoa, its nitro group is reduced by low-redox electron transport proteins, generating reactive intermediates that damage DNA and inhibit nucleic acid synthesis. It is used for trichomoniasis, giardiasis, amebiasis, and bacterial vaginosis. A notable contraindication is concomitant alcohol use due to a disulfiram-like reaction.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Griseofulvin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Griseofulvin is an oral antifungal antibiotic derived from Penicillium, used mainly for dermatophyte infections of the skin, hair, and nails (tinea). It binds fungal tubulin and disrupts microtubule function, inhibiting mitosis, and concentrates in keratin precursor cells to make new tissue resistant to invasion. Notable adverse effects include headache and hepatotoxicity; it is contraindicated in porphyria and severe liver failure.
tail_desc: Constitutional neutropenia is a benign, usually lifelong form of chronic neutropenia affecting the hematologic/immune system, characterized by persistently low absolute neutrophil counts without an acquired cause or major marrow pathology. It is often seen in otherwise healthy individuals, including some ethnic groups, and typically causes no or few infections. Management is mainly reassurance, exclusion of secondary causes, and clinical monitoring rather than routine treatment.

head: Leucine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid used as a nutritional ingredient and in amino acid supplementation. It activates nutrient-sensing pathways involved in protein synthesis, notably mTORC1, but is not typically classified as a conventional targeted drug. Clinically, it is used in medical nutrition to prevent or treat amino acid deficiency; excessive intake may worsen branched-chain ketoacid metabolism disorders such as maple syrup urine disease.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Desvenlafaxine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desvenlafaxine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant and the major active metabolite of venlafaxine. It acts primarily by inhibiting the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic serotonin and norepinephrine. It is approved for major depressive disorder. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and the contraindication of concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Lincomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lincomycin is a lincosamide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used for susceptible serious Gram-positive infections, especially when penicillin cannot be used, including certain staphylococcal, streptococcal, and pneumococcal infections. A notable adverse effect is antibiotic-associated colitis, including severe Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea.
tail_desc: Perinatal necrotizing enterocolitis is a severe inflammatory-necrotic gastrointestinal emergency of the neonatal intestine, classically affecting premature or very low birth weight infants. It is thought to result from immature gut barrier function, dysregulated microbial colonization, and intestinal ischemic injury, leading to feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management centers on bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, intensive supportive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Nalidixic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nalidixic acid is a first-generation quinolone antibacterial, a naphthyridone derivative, that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and thereby blocks DNA replication. It has been used primarily for treatment of uncomplicated urinary tract infections caused by susceptible Gram-negative organisms. Notable risks include central nervous system effects and it is generally contraindicated in infants younger than 3 months.
tail_desc: Glycogen storage disease comprises inherited metabolic disorders, usually affecting liver and/or muscle, caused by enzyme defects in glycogen synthesis or breakdown that lead to abnormal glycogen accumulation or impaired glucose release. Typical features include fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, growth delay, exercise intolerance, and muscle weakness, often presenting in infancy or childhood. Management is mainly dietary and supportive, with subtype-specific monitoring and complication prevention.

head: Irbesartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irbesartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl tetrazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and diabetic nephropathy in patients with type 2 diabetes. A notable contraindication is pregnancy because it can cause fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Streptozocin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Streptozocin is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent, structurally related to glucosamine, that causes DNA alkylation and damage after uptake into pancreatic islet cells via GLUT2. It is approved for metastatic or unresectable pancreatic islet-cell carcinoma, including neuroendocrine tumors. A notable toxicity is nephrotoxicity; severe renal impairment is therefore an important contraindication or reason for caution.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Bisacodyl (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative of the diphenylmethane derivative class. It acts locally in the colon as a prodrug converted to an active metabolite that stimulates enteric nerves and promotes colonic fluid and electrolyte secretion; no single defined molecular target is established. It is used for constipation and bowel evacuation before procedures. A notable adverse effect is abdominal cramping.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Tenofovir disoproxil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic hepatitis C virus infection (a disease)
head_desc: Tenofovir disoproxil is an oral prodrug of tenofovir, an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NtRTI). After conversion to tenofovir diphosphate, it competitively inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase, causing DNA chain termination. It is used to treat HIV-1 infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable adverse effect is renal toxicity, including proximal tubulopathy/Fanconi syndrome.
tail_desc: Chronic hepatitis C virus infection is a chronic viral liver disease caused by persistent infection with hepatitis C virus, producing ongoing hepatic inflammation that can progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. Many patients are asymptomatic or have fatigue and abnormal liver enzymes; it occurs in infected adults and children. Management centers on antiviral therapy with direct-acting agents, plus fibrosis assessment and liver-related monitoring.

head: Digoxin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Digoxin is a cardiac glycoside that inhibits the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in cardiomyocytes, increasing intracellular calcium and enhancing cardiac contractility while augmenting vagal tone to slow atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used for symptomatic heart failure and ventricular rate control in atrial fibrillation or flutter. A notable adverse effect is potentially life-threatening arrhythmias, especially with toxicity.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Mirtazapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mirtazapine is a tetracyclic antidepressant of the piperazino-azepine class used primarily for major depressive disorder. It acts mainly by antagonizing central presynaptic alpha-2 adrenergic autoreceptors and heteroreceptors, increasing norepinephrine and serotonin release, and also blocks 5-HT2, 5-HT3, and histamine H1 receptors. Notable adverse effects include sedation and weight gain; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Impaired renal function is not a specific disease but a clinical state of reduced kidney function in the renal/urinary system, arising from acute or chronic kidney injury due to causes such as diabetes, hypertension, glomerular disease, ischemia, toxins, or obstruction. It may present with elevated creatinine, reduced urine output, fluid overload, electrolyte abnormalities, and uremic symptoms, especially in older adults or patients with comorbidities; management targets the underlying cause, supportive renal protection, and dialysis when severe.

head: Adenosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Adenosine is an endogenous purine nucleoside antiarrhythmic that activates cardiac A1 adenosine receptors, increasing potassium conductance and suppressing cAMP-dependent calcium currents to transiently slow sinoatrial and atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used acutely to terminate paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia and in pharmacologic cardiac stress testing. Notable adverse effects include brief flushing, chest discomfort, and bronchospasm; it is contraindicated in second- or third-degree AV block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Pulmonary emphysema is a chronic obstructive lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by permanent enlargement of distal air spaces and destruction of alveolar walls, causing loss of elastic recoil and airflow limitation. It occurs most often in smokers, or with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, and presents with progressive dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance, and hyperinflation; management emphasizes smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, oxygen when indicated, and selected advanced interventions.

head: Amoxapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amoxapine is a dibenzoxazepine tricyclic antidepressant. It inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin and also antagonizes dopamine D2 receptors. It has been used for the treatment of depressive disorders, including anxiety-associated depression. A notable adverse effect is tardive dyskinesia or other extrapyramidal symptoms, and it is contraindicated during the acute recovery phase after myocardial infarction.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Desoximetasone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Desoximetasone is a high-potency topical corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class, used to relieve inflammatory and pruritic manifestations of corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses such as psoriasis and eczema. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and immune responses. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy; it is generally contraindicated in untreated cutaneous infections.
tail_desc: Peripheral vascular disease is a circulatory disorder of the peripheral blood vessels, most often involving atherosclerotic narrowing or occlusion of arteries supplying the legs. It commonly affects older adults and people who smoke or have diabetes, causing intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool extremities, poor wound healing, and sometimes critical limb ischemia. Management emphasizes risk-factor reduction, exercise, antiplatelet/statin therapy, and revascularization when severe.

head: Bortezomib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bortezomib is a boronic acid dipeptide proteasome inhibitor that reversibly targets the 26S proteasome, disrupting ubiquitin-mediated protein degradation and promoting apoptosis in malignant plasma cells. It is approved for multiple myeloma and mantle cell lymphoma. A notable adverse effect is peripheral neuropathy; it is contraindicated with intrathecal administration because of fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Intestinal obstruction is a gastrointestinal disorder in which partial or complete blockage of the small or large bowel prevents normal passage of contents, due to mechanical causes such as adhesions, hernias, tumors, or volvulus, or to impaired motility (ileus). It typically causes crampy abdominal pain, distension, vomiting, and constipation or obstipation, occurs across age groups depending on cause, and is managed with urgent evaluation, fluid resuscitation, bowel decompression, and treatment of the underlying cause, including surgery when indicated.

head: Tirofiban (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tirofiban is a nonpeptide small-molecule antiplatelet agent of the glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor class. It reversibly blocks the platelet integrin αIIbβ3 (GP IIb/IIIa) receptor, preventing fibrinogen-mediated platelet aggregation. It is used in acute coronary syndromes, including unstable angina or non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction, and during percutaneous coronary intervention. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; active internal bleeding is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Intracranial hemorrhage is acute bleeding within the skull affecting the central nervous system, including intracerebral, subarachnoid, subdural, and epidural spaces. It usually results from ruptured vessels due to hypertension, trauma, aneurysm, vascular malformation, or coagulopathy. Patients, often older adults or trauma patients, present with sudden headache, focal neurologic deficits, vomiting, reduced consciousness, or seizures. Management is emergent, focusing on stabilization, blood pressure and coagulation control, and neurosurgical intervention when indicated.

head: Digoxin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Digoxin is a cardiac glycoside that inhibits the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in cardiomyocytes, increasing intracellular calcium and enhancing cardiac contractility while augmenting vagal tone to slow atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used for symptomatic heart failure and ventricular rate control in atrial fibrillation or flutter. A notable adverse effect is potentially life-threatening arrhythmias, especially with toxicity.
tail_desc: Familial hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is an inherited cardiac muscle disorder characterized by unexplained left ventricular hypertrophy, usually caused by autosomal dominant mutations in sarcomere protein genes. It may present in adolescents or adults with dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or sudden cardiac death. Management focuses on risk stratification, symptom control with beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers, lifestyle guidance, and selected use of septal reduction or implantable defibrillators.

head: Apomorphine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Apomorphine is a non-ergoline dopamine agonist of the aporphine class that primarily stimulates dopamine D1- and D2-like receptors in the central nervous system. It is used for intermittent treatment of “off” episodes in Parkinson disease, and some formulations are used for erectile dysfunction in certain regions. A notable adverse effect is severe nausea and vomiting, and it is contraindicated with 5-HT3 antagonists.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Dimenhydrinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dimenhydrinate is a first-generation H1 antihistamine and antiemetic, a salt of diphenhydramine with 8-chlorotheophylline. It acts primarily by antagonizing histamine H1 receptors, with additional antimuscarinic effects that suppress vestibular stimulation and the vomiting center. It is used for prevention and treatment of motion sickness, nausea, vomiting, and vertigo. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to dimenhydrinate or diphenhydramine.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Triazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic that positively modulates GABA\(_A\) receptors by binding the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include anterograde amnesia and next-day psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inhibitors.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Tropicamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tropicamide is a short-acting antimuscarinic mydriatic and cycloplegic ophthalmic agent, structurally a synthetic parasympatholytic. It competitively antagonizes muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, primarily M4 and other ocular muscarinic receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle, producing pupil dilation and cycloplegia. It is used for diagnostic mydriasis and cycloplegic refraction; notable risks include increased intraocular pressure and precipitation of angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Galantamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Galantamine is a tertiary alkaloid acetylcholinesterase inhibitor, also reported to allosterically modulate nicotinic acetylcholine receptors. By inhibiting acetylcholinesterase, it increases synaptic acetylcholine. It is approved for the symptomatic treatment of mild to moderate Alzheimer disease dementia. Notable adverse effects include nausea, vomiting, and bradycardia; it should be used cautiously in patients with cardiac conduction disorders.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Nandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nandrolone is a synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid and testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein synthesis and erythropoiesis. Its ester forms, such as nandrolone decanoate, have been used for anemia associated with chronic kidney disease and some wasting states. Notable adverse effects include virilization and other androgenic effects; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in prostate or male breast carcinoma.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Eltrombopag (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Eltrombopag is an oral, small-molecule thrombopoietin receptor agonist that activates the c-Mpl receptor to stimulate megakaryocyte proliferation and platelet production. It is used for thrombocytopenia in chronic immune thrombocytopenia, severe aplastic anemia, and thrombocytopenia associated with chronic hepatitis C treatment. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Rifampicin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rifampicin is a rifamycin-class ansamycin antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase by binding the β-subunit encoded by rpoB, thereby blocking RNA synthesis. It is a cornerstone of combination therapy for tuberculosis and is also used for leprosy and prophylaxis of Neisseria meningitidis and Haemophilus influenzae type b exposure. Notable adverse effects include hepatotoxicity and potent cytochrome P450 induction causing major drug interactions.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Streptomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Streptomycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic produced by Streptomyces griseus that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting initiation and causing mRNA misreading to inhibit protein synthesis. It is used mainly for tuberculosis in combination regimens and for plague and tularemia. A notable adverse effect is ototoxicity, and it can also cause nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Sulfadoxine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadoxine is a long-acting sulfonamide antimicrobial and antimalarial antifolate that inhibits parasitic and bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used in fixed combination with pyrimethamine for treatment and intermittent preventive therapy of malaria due to susceptible Plasmodium falciparum. A notable risk is severe hypersensitivity, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome; sulfonamide allergy is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Lovastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lovastatin is a fungal-derived statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat primary hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to reduce cardiovascular risk. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, and it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related gastrointestinal disorder characterized by mucosal ulceration in the stomach or proximal duodenum, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It typically presents with epigastric pain, dyspepsia, nausea, or bleeding, especially in affected adults. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, and avoiding ulcerogenic medications.

head: Isoflurane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoflurane is a halogenated ether volatile inhalational general anesthetic. Its anesthetic action is not fully defined but involves enhancement of inhibitory GABA_A and glycine signaling and effects on two-pore-domain potassium channels, with CNS depression. It is used to induce and maintain general anesthesia during surgery. A notable serious risk is malignant hyperthermia in susceptible patients.
tail_desc: Muscular dystrophy is a group of inherited disorders of the skeletal musculature characterized by progressive muscle fiber degeneration and weakness, often caused by mutations in genes encoding muscle structural proteins such as dystrophin. It typically presents in childhood or early adulthood with proximal weakness, gait difficulty, falls, and later respiratory or cardiac involvement. Management is supportive and multidisciplinary, including physiotherapy, orthopedic care, respiratory support, and selected disease-specific therapies.

head: Flavoxate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flavoxate is a synthetic antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, chemically a flavone derivative, used to relieve symptoms of overactive or irritable bladder such as urgency, frequency, dysuria, and suprapubic pain. Its effect is attributed mainly to smooth-muscle relaxation with weak muscarinic receptor antagonism; the precise primary molecular target is not well defined. Notable adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, and it is contraindicated in urinary retention.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Tenofovir disoproxil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tenofovir disoproxil is an oral prodrug of tenofovir, an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NtRTI). After conversion to tenofovir diphosphate, it competitively inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase, causing DNA chain termination. It is used to treat HIV-1 infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable adverse effect is renal toxicity, including proximal tubulopathy/Fanconi syndrome.
tail_desc: Long QT syndrome is a cardiac electrical disorder affecting ventricular repolarization, characterized by prolongation of the QT interval on electrocardiography, usually due to inherited ion-channel defects or acquired drug/electrolyte effects. It predisposes children or adults to syncope, seizures, and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia including torsades de pointes, with risk of sudden death; management emphasizes trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, correction of reversible causes, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Thiopental (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiopental is an ultra-short-acting barbiturate intravenous anesthetic. It enhances inhibitory neurotransmission primarily by positively modulating GABA-A receptors, increasing chloride channel opening and causing rapid CNS depression. It is used for induction of general anesthesia and has established use in seizure control and reduction of intracranial pressure. A notable risk is profound respiratory and cardiovascular depression; it is contraindicated in acute porphyria.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Dexfenfluramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dexfenfluramine is the dextro isomer of fenfluramine, an anorectic amphetamine derivative formerly used as an anti-obesity agent. It acts primarily by increasing serotonergic signaling, mainly by promoting serotonin release and inhibiting its reuptake via the serotonin transporter (SERT). It was withdrawn after being linked to valvular heart disease and pulmonary hypertension.
tail_desc: Glaucoma is a group of chronic optic neuropathies affecting the eye, characterized by progressive retinal ganglion cell and optic nerve damage, often associated with elevated intraocular pressure from impaired aqueous humor outflow. It may be asymptomatic early, then cause peripheral visual field loss and eventual blindness, especially in older adults. Management aims to lower intraocular pressure with medications, laser therapy, or surgery.

head: Rilpivirine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rilpivirine is a diarylpyrimidine non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) that binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, blocking viral DNA synthesis. It is used, in combination with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection.
