A notable safety concern is dose-related QT interval prolongation, and it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inducers that can markedly reduce rilpivirine exposure.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Nitazoxanide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitazoxanide is a synthetic thiazolide antiparasitic and broad-spectrum anti-infective prodrug, rapidly converted to tizoxanide. It disrupts the pyruvate:ferredoxin oxidoreductase (PFOR) enzyme–dependent electron transfer pathway essential for anaerobic energy metabolism. It is approved for treatment of diarrhea caused by Giardia lamblia and Cryptosporidium parvum. Common adverse effects include abdominal pain, nausea, and headache.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Glutamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glutamic acid is a nonessential amino acid and acidic amino acid used mainly as a nutritional or electrolyte replacement component rather than a receptor-targeted drug. It serves as a metabolic substrate and precursor in amino acid and neurotransmitter pathways, without a well-defined primary pharmacologic target in approved therapy. Clinical use is limited to nutritional supplementation; gastrointestinal irritation may occur, and caution is warranted in severe renal or hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: End-stage renal failure is the terminal stage of chronic kidney disease in the renal system, marked by irreversible loss of kidney function and severely reduced glomerular filtration. It usually results from progressive disorders such as diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. Patients develop uremic symptoms, fluid and electrolyte disturbances, and anemia; management centers on dialysis, kidney transplantation, and treatment of complications.

head: Emtricitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Emtricitabine is a synthetic nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI), a fluorinated cytidine analogue used as an antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase by competing with deoxycytidine triphosphate and causing viral DNA chain termination. It is approved, in combination regimens, for HIV-1 infection and is also used in HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis combinations. A notable warning is severe acute exacerbation of hepatitis B after discontinuation in coinfected patients.
tail_desc: Osteomalacia is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system characterized by defective mineralization of mature bone, most often due to vitamin D deficiency, phosphate depletion, or impaired vitamin D metabolism. It causes diffuse bone pain, muscle weakness, fragility fractures, and difficulty walking, especially in adults with malnutrition, malabsorption, kidney disease, or limited sun exposure. Management targets the cause, typically vitamin D, calcium, or phosphate replacement.

head: Daptomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Daptomycin is a cyclic lipopeptide antibiotic that binds bacterial cell membranes in a calcium-dependent manner, causing rapid membrane depolarization and inhibition of protein, DNA, and RNA synthesis, primarily against Gram-positive organisms. It is used for complicated skin and soft tissue infections, Staphylococcus aureus bacteremia, and right-sided infective endocarditis. A notable adverse effect is myopathy with creatine phosphokinase elevation.
tail_desc: Eosinophilic pneumonia is an inflammatory lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by eosinophil-rich infiltration of the pulmonary interstitium and alveoli, occurring in acute or chronic forms and sometimes triggered by drugs, smoking, infections, or systemic eosinophilic disorders. Patients typically develop cough, dyspnea, fever, and pulmonary infiltrates; management centers on identifying causes and treatment with systemic corticosteroids, often with rapid improvement.

head: Arginine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Arginine (L-arginine) is a basic, semi-essential amino acid used as a nutritional/pharmaceutical supplement. It serves as the endogenous substrate for nitric oxide synthase, increasing nitric oxide production, and also participates in the urea cycle. Established uses include treatment of urea cycle disorders and supplementation in arginine deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset; caution is advised in severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Pazopanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pazopanib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets VEGFR-1, -2, and -3, PDGFR-α/β, and c-KIT, thereby inhibiting tumor angiogenesis and growth. It is approved for advanced renal cell carcinoma and advanced soft tissue sarcoma after prior chemotherapy. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Butacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic in the benzoate family. Like other local anesthetics, it acts by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used topically, particularly in ophthalmic anesthesia. As with related ester anesthetics, hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known ester local anesthetic allergy.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Labetalol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Labetalol is a mixed adrenergic antagonist of the amino alcohol class that blocks β1-, β2-, and α1-adrenergic receptors, lowering heart rate and peripheral vascular resistance. It is used for hypertension, including severe hypertension and hypertension in pregnancy. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma, overt heart failure, and greater-than-first-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Hepatic encephalopathy is a neuropsychiatric complication of acute or chronic liver failure affecting the central nervous system. It results from impaired hepatic detoxification and portosystemic shunting, leading to accumulation of neurotoxins, especially ammonia. Patients, often with cirrhosis, develop confusion, altered consciousness, asterixis, and sometimes coma; management targets precipitating factors and lowers gut-derived ammonia, commonly with lactulose and rifaximin.

head: Risedronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: Risedronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate (pyridinyl bisphosphonate) that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget’s disease of bone, and prevention or treatment of glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophageal irritation or ulceration, and it is contraindicated in hypocalcemia and significant esophageal motility disorders.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Zolmitriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zolmitriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, an indole/tryptamine derivative that acts as a selective 5-HT1B/5-HT1D serotonin receptor agonist, producing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for the acute treatment of migraine attacks, with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease because triptans can cause chest tightness and coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Ischemic bowel disease is a gastrointestinal vascular disorder caused by reduced blood flow to the intestine, leading to mucosal injury and, in severe cases, transmural necrosis. It most often affects older adults and typically presents with sudden abdominal pain, tenderness, and bloody diarrhea or gastrointestinal bleeding. Management focuses on hemodynamic stabilization, treating the cause, bowel rest, and urgent surgery for infarction, perforation, or peritonitis.

head: Naratriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naratriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It treats acute migraine attacks, with or without aura, by causing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. Notable adverse effects include paresthesia, dizziness, and chest or throat tightness; it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease and certain cerebrovascular syndromes.
tail_desc: Prinzmetal angina is a coronary vasospastic disorder of the cardiovascular system caused by transient spasm of an epicardial coronary artery, producing reversible myocardial ischemia without fixed obstructive disease in many cases. It typically causes recurrent rest angina, often nocturnal or early morning, with transient ST-segment elevation during attacks. Management centers on calcium channel blockers and nitrates, while avoiding triggers such as smoking.

head: Brimonidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Brimonidine is an imidazoline-derived selective alpha2-adrenergic receptor agonist. It lowers intraocular pressure mainly by reducing aqueous humor production and increasing uveoscleral outflow, and is approved for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension; a topical gel formulation is also used for persistent facial erythema of rosacea. A notable adverse effect is ocular hyperemia or allergic conjunctivitis.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Polythiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Polythiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the sodium-chloride cotransporter (NCC/SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used to treat hypertension and edema, including edema associated with heart failure. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; thiazides are also contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadiazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections and, with pyrimethamine, for toxoplasmosis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe rash such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome; it is generally contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy and near term pregnancy or in neonates due to kernicterus risk.
tail_desc: Urinary tract obstruction is a genitourinary disorder in which partial or complete blockage of urine flow occurs anywhere from the renal pelvis to the urethra, causing increased upstream pressure, hydronephrosis, infection risk, and potential renal impairment. Symptoms may include flank or abdominal pain, lower urinary tract symptoms, anuria, hematuria, or recurrent urinary infection. Management focuses on urgent decompression when indicated and treatment of the underlying cause.

head: Dihydrotachysterol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dihydrotachysterol is a synthetic vitamin D analog (secosteroid) used as a calcium-regulating agent. After hepatic activation, it acts via the vitamin D receptor to increase intestinal calcium absorption and help restore serum calcium. It has been used for hypocalcemia, particularly in hypoparathyroidism. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia, so it is contraindicated in patients with pre-existing hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Hyperphosphatemia is an electrolyte and mineral metabolism disorder affecting systemic homeostasis, most often due to impaired renal phosphate excretion in chronic kidney disease. It may be asymptomatic or contribute to hypocalcemia, pruritus, soft-tissue and vascular calcification, and secondary hyperparathyroidism, especially in patients with advanced kidney failure. Management focuses on treating the cause, restricting dietary phosphate, and using phosphate binders or dialysis when needed.

head: Bisacodyl (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bisacodyl is a stimulant laxative of the diphenylmethane derivative class. It acts locally in the colon as a prodrug converted to an active metabolite that stimulates enteric nerves and promotes colonic fluid and electrolyte secretion; no single defined molecular target is established. It is used for constipation and bowel evacuation before procedures. A notable adverse effect is abdominal cramping.
tail_desc: Congestive heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment reduces the heart’s ability to pump or fill effectively, causing pulmonary and/or systemic fluid congestion. It commonly affects older adults and patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy, producing dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance. Management focuses on treating the cause, diuresis, guideline-directed heart-failure medications, sodium/fluid control, and selected device or advanced therapies.

head: Zolpidem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zolpidem is a nonbenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic of the imidazopyridine class that acts as a positive allosteric modulator at the GABA-A receptor, with relative selectivity for benzodiazepine-1 (omega-1) sites containing the alpha1 subunit. It is approved for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include next-day impairment and complex sleep behaviors; it is contraindicated in patients with prior such episodes.
tail_desc: Retrograde amnesia is a memory disorder of the central nervous system characterized by impaired recall of information and events learned before the onset of brain dysfunction. It commonly follows traumatic brain injury, stroke, encephalitis, seizures, or neurodegenerative disease. Patients, often after acute neurologic injury, lose autobiographical or factual memories to varying degrees. Management focuses on treating the underlying cause, cognitive rehabilitation, and supportive care.

head: Albendazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Albendazole is a benzimidazole anthelmintic that binds parasite β-tubulin, inhibiting microtubule polymerization and impairing glucose uptake and energy metabolism. It is used to treat a range of helminth infections, including neurocysticercosis and hydatid disease, as well as intestinal nematode infections. Notable adverse effects include hepatotoxicity; it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy, especially during the first trimester.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Nitrofural (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofural (nitrofurazone) is a topical nitrofuran antibacterial. After enzymatic reduction of its nitro group in susceptible microbes, reactive intermediates damage bacterial DNA and other macromolecules, giving broad activity against many Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms. It has been used for topical prevention or treatment of skin infections in burns, grafts, wounds, and ulcers. A notable adverse effect is allergic contact dermatitis.
tail_desc: Interstitial lung disease is a heterogeneous group of parenchymal lung disorders affecting the pulmonary interstitium and alveoli, characterized by inflammation and/or progressive fibrosis that impair gas exchange. Patients, often adults, commonly present with exertional dyspnea, dry cough, and restrictive physiology with diffuse infiltrates. Management targets the underlying cause, using antigen avoidance or immunosuppression in selected cases, antifibrotic therapy for fibrotic forms, and supportive oxygen or transplantation when advanced.

head: Cabergoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cabergoline is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist that selectively stimulates dopamine D2 receptors, suppressing pituitary prolactin secretion. It is used primarily for hyperprolactinemic disorders, including prolactinomas, and is also used in some settings for Parkinson disease. A notable safety concern is cardiac valvular fibrosis with long-term or high-dose use; it is contraindicated in patients with uncontrolled hypertension or fibrotic disorders.
tail_desc: IgG4-related retroperitoneal fibrosis is a fibroinflammatory manifestation of IgG4-related disease affecting the retroperitoneum, characterized by dense lymphoplasmacytic infiltrates rich in IgG4-positive plasma cells and storiform fibrosis. It typically affects middle-aged to older adults and may cause back or abdominal pain, ureteral encasement, hydronephrosis, and renal dysfunction. Management generally involves glucocorticoids, with immunosuppressive or B-cell–depleting therapy for refractory or relapsing disease.

head: Epirubicin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Epirubicin is an anthracycline antineoplastic antibiotic, structurally related to doxorubicin. It intercalates into DNA, inhibits topoisomerase II, and generates free radicals, impairing DNA replication and transcription. It is used in breast cancer and other solid tumors, including as adjuvant therapy in early breast cancer. A notable toxicity is cumulative dose-related cardiomyopathy/heart failure.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Nilotinib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nilotinib is an oral second-generation BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase inhibitor, structurally an aminopyrimidine derivative related to imatinib. It selectively inhibits BCR-ABL and also KIT and PDGFR kinases, blocking proliferation of Philadelphia chromosome-positive leukemic cells. It is approved for Philadelphia chromosome-positive chronic myeloid leukemia. A notable safety risk is QT-interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, or congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Hyperbilirubinemia is a metabolic disorder of the hepatobiliary system characterized by elevated bilirubin in blood, caused by increased bilirubin production, impaired hepatic uptake or conjugation, or reduced biliary excretion. It commonly presents with jaundice and scleral icterus, and in neonates may cause kernicterus if severe. Management targets the cause and bilirubin reduction, including phototherapy, exchange transfusion, or treating liver or hemolytic disease.

head: Mesoridazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mesoridazine is a piperidine phenothiazine antipsychotic, historically used for schizophrenia. Like other typical antipsychotics, it acts primarily through central dopamine D2 receptor antagonism. Its clinical use became severely restricted because of dose-related QT interval prolongation and risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, including torsades de pointes, making it contraindicated in patients with known cardiac conduction abnormalities or significant QT prolongation.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Tretamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: central nervous system origin vertigo (a disease)
head_desc: Tretamine is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Central nervous system origin vertigo is a neurologic form of vertigo arising from lesions or dysfunction within the brainstem, cerebellum, or central vestibular pathways rather than the inner ear. Causes include stroke, multiple sclerosis, migraine, tumors, or other central nervous system disorders. Patients often have persistent dizziness with imbalance, nystagmus, and additional focal neurologic signs; management targets the underlying central cause and supportive rehabilitation.

head: Aurothioglucose (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aurothioglucose is a gold(I) thiolate compound formerly used as a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug for rheumatoid arthritis. Its precise mechanism is incompletely defined, but gold salts modulate immune and inflammatory cell function rather than acting on a single established molecular target. Important adverse effects include dermatitis, stomatitis, proteinuria, and bone marrow suppression; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Pulmonary fibrosis is a chronic interstitial lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by progressive scarring of lung parenchyma, which stiffens the lungs and impairs gas exchange. Patients, often older adults, typically develop exertional dyspnea, persistent dry cough, and reduced exercise tolerance. Management generally focuses on treating the cause when identified, slowing progression, supportive oxygen and pulmonary rehabilitation, and lung transplantation in selected cases.

head: Leucine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Leucine is an essential branched-chain amino acid used as a nutritional ingredient and in amino acid supplementation. It activates nutrient-sensing pathways involved in protein synthesis, notably mTORC1, but is not typically classified as a conventional targeted drug. Clinically, it is used in medical nutrition to prevent or treat amino acid deficiency; excessive intake may worsen branched-chain ketoacid metabolism disorders such as maple syrup urine disease.
tail_desc: Chronic renal failure syndrome is a chronic kidney disease of the renal system characterized by progressive, irreversible loss of nephron function, leading to reduced glomerular filtration and accumulation of metabolic waste, fluid, and electrolyte disturbances. It commonly affects adults, especially those with diabetes or hypertension, and presents with fatigue, edema, hypertension, anemia, and uremic symptoms. Management focuses on treating the cause, slowing progression, correcting complications, and renal replacement therapy when advanced.

head: Methocarbamol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methocarbamol is a centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant, chemically a carbamate derivative of guaifenesin. Its precise molecular target is not established; it is thought to produce muscle relaxation via generalized CNS depression rather than direct action on skeletal muscle. It is used to relieve acute painful musculoskeletal spasm and as adjunctive treatment for tetanus. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Piroxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Piroxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for symptomatic relief of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and other musculoskeletal pain and inflammation. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Cerebral infarction is an ischemic stroke of the central nervous system in which interrupted cerebral blood flow, usually from arterial thrombosis or embolism, causes focal brain tissue necrosis. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or visual loss, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible and secondary stroke prevention.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Heart conduction disease is a cardiac rhythm disorder of the heart’s electrical conduction system, involving the sinoatrial node, atrioventricular node, or His-Purkinje pathways. It results from congenital defects, age-related fibrosis, ischemia, inflammation, or drug effects, causing bradycardia, pauses, syncope, dizziness, or sudden death. It occurs in inherited and acquired forms across ages; management targets reversible causes and often requires pacemaker implantation.

head: Nylidrin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nylidrin is a beta-adrenergic agonist of the phenylaminoethanol class, with predominant beta-2 receptor activity producing relaxation of vascular and uterine smooth muscle. It has been used as a peripheral vasodilator for certain peripheral vascular disorders and historically as a tocolytic. Notable adverse effects include tachycardia and palpitations, and it is generally contraindicated in significant cardiac disease.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Telmisartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telmisartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl benzimidazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and to reduce cardiovascular risk in selected high-risk patients. Notable risks include hyperkalemia and renal impairment, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: obsolete hyperuricemia (disease) is a disease or medical condition.

head: Silver sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Silver sulfadiazine is a topical sulfonamide antimicrobial combining sulfadiazine with silver ions. It acts mainly by releasing silver, which disrupts bacterial cell membranes and proteins, while sulfadiazine inhibits folate synthesis via dihydropteroate synthase. It is established for prevention and treatment of wound sepsis in second- and third-degree burns. A notable contraindication is use in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Irbesartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irbesartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a biphenyl tetrazole derivative, that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is used to treat hypertension and diabetic nephropathy in patients with type 2 diabetes. A notable contraindication is pregnancy because it can cause fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Anuria is a severe urinary system disorder defined by absent or near-absent urine output, usually reflecting acute kidney failure, urinary tract obstruction, or profound renal hypoperfusion. It presents with markedly reduced urination, fluid overload, electrolyte disturbances, and uremic features, often in critically ill patients or those with obstructive uropathy. Management focuses on urgent identification and reversal of the cause, hemodynamic stabilization, relief of obstruction, and renal replacement therapy when indicated.

head: Thiothixene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiothixene is a first-generation (typical) antipsychotic of the thioxanthene class. Its antipsychotic effects are primarily attributed to antagonism of central dopamine D2 receptors, with additional activity at other neurotransmitter receptors. It is used for the treatment of schizophrenia. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including drug-induced parkinsonism and akathisia.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Zinc oxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zinc oxide is an inorganic zinc compound used topically as a skin protectant and mild astringent. Its effects are primarily physical rather than receptor-mediated, forming a protective barrier on skin and reflecting or scattering ultraviolet radiation in sunscreen formulations, without a defined primary molecular target. Established uses include diaper dermatitis, minor skin irritation, and sun protection; adverse effects are usually limited to local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Anxiety is a mental health condition involving excessive fear, worry, or apprehension, primarily affecting the central nervous system and stress-response pathways. It is multifactorial, with contributions from genetic vulnerability, neurobiological dysregulation, and psychosocial stressors. Common features include persistent nervousness, restlessness, impaired concentration, sleep disturbance, and autonomic symptoms; it can affect people of any age. Management typically includes psychotherapy, stress reduction, and when indicated, pharmacotherapy such as antidepressants.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Fondaparinux (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fondaparinux is a synthetic pentasaccharide anticoagulant that selectively binds antithrombin and indirectly inhibits factor Xa, reducing thrombin generation. It is approved for prophylaxis and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including use in acute coronary syndromes in some settings. A notable risk is major bleeding, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment and in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Magnesium chloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium chloride is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an electrolyte replacement and mineral supplement. It dissociates to provide magnesium, an essential cofactor for many enzymes and a modulator of neuromuscular transmission, membrane stability, and cardiac conduction, without a single specific receptor target. It is used to prevent or treat magnesium deficiency; adverse effects can include diarrhea, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment because of hypermagnesemia risk.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Iocetamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iocetamic acid is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Noscapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Noscapine is a phthalideisoquinoline alkaloid derived from opium poppy and used as a centrally acting antitussive. Unlike opioid analgesics, it has little narcotic activity; its cough-suppressant effect is attributed to action on the medullary cough center, and it has also been shown to bind tubulin and alter microtubule dynamics. Adverse effects can include drowsiness, and concomitant use with warfarin is a notable precaution due to bleeding risk.
tail_desc: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder affecting primarily the respiratory and gastrointestinal systems, caused by pathogenic variants in CFTR that impair chloride and water transport, producing thick secretions. It typically presents in childhood with recurrent lung infections, chronic cough, pancreatic insufficiency, malabsorption, and poor growth. Management includes airway clearance, antimicrobial therapy, nutritional support, pancreatic enzymes, and CFTR modulator therapy when eligible.

head: Etodolac (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Etodolac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), a pyranocarboxylic acid derivative, that inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, particularly COX-mediated prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation, including in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and acute pain. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Aminocaproic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminocaproic acid is a synthetic lysine analogue antifibrinolytic that inhibits plasminogen activation and plasmin binding to fibrin, thereby reducing fibrinolysis. It is used to treat or prevent bleeding due to hyperfibrinolysis, including perioperative hemorrhage in selected settings. Important risks include thrombosis, and it is generally contraindicated in active intravascular clotting or disseminated intravascular coagulation unless carefully managed.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Terfenadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Terfenadine is a second-generation piperidine antihistamine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It was used for symptomatic treatment of seasonal allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable safety concern is dose-related QT interval prolongation and torsades de pointes, especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors or hepatic impairment, leading to its withdrawal in many countries.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Edetic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Edetic acid (EDTA) is an aminopolycarboxylic acid chelating agent that binds polyvalent and divalent metal ions, especially calcium and lead, forming stable soluble complexes. Calcium disodium edetate is used for treatment of lead poisoning, while edetate disodium has been used to lower severe hypercalcemia. Notable risks include nephrotoxicity, and edetate disodium is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Armodafinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Armodafinil is the R-enantiomer of modafinil, a wake-promoting central nervous system agent. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it is known to inhibit the dopamine transporter (DAT), increasing extracellular dopamine. It is approved to improve wakefulness in narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift work disorder. Notable adverse effects include headache and rare serious rash, including Stevens–Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Pentosan polysulfate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentosan polysulfate is a semi-synthetic sulfated polysaccharide, structurally related to heparin, used orally for interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome. Its mechanism is not fully defined; it is thought to adhere to and help restore the bladder urothelial glycosaminoglycan layer, with weak anticoagulant activity. A notable adverse effect is pigmentary maculopathy with long-term use.
tail_desc: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is an immune-mediated hematologic and thrombotic complication of heparin exposure, caused by antibodies against platelet factor 4–heparin complexes that activate platelets. It typically occurs 5–10 days after heparin in hospitalized or postoperative patients, causing a platelet count fall and paradoxical venous or arterial thrombosis. Management requires immediate heparin discontinuation and initiation of a non-heparin anticoagulant.

head: Propylthiouracil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to JAGN1 deficiency (a disease)
head_desc: Propylthiouracil is a thiourea antithyroid drug (thionamide) that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking iodination and coupling steps in thyroid hormone synthesis, and also inhibits peripheral conversion of thyroxine (T4) to triiodothyronine (T3). It is used to treat hyperthyroidism, including Graves disease and thyroid storm. A notable serious adverse effect is severe hepatotoxicity; agranulocytosis can also occur.
tail_desc: Autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to JAGN1 deficiency is a rare inherited immunodeficiency of granulopoiesis causing profound neutropenia from infancy. Biallelic JAGN1 variants impair neutrophil maturation and survival, leading to recurrent severe bacterial infections, mucosal and skin infections, and sometimes congenital anomalies. Management centers on infection prevention and treatment, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, and in severe or refractory cases hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Vandetanib (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vandetanib is an oral small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the anilinoquinazoline class that targets RET, VEGFR-2, and EGFR signaling. It is approved for symptomatic or progressive medullary thyroid cancer in patients with unresectable locally advanced or metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is QT-interval prolongation, and it carries a boxed warning for torsades de pointes and sudden death.
tail_desc: Brain ischemia is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by reduced or interrupted cerebral blood flow, leading to inadequate oxygen and glucose delivery and potentially irreversible neuronal injury. It commonly presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits, altered consciousness, or cognitive impairment, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible, stabilization, and secondary prevention of further ischemic events.

head: Pantothenic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pantothenic acid (vitamin B5) is a water-soluble B-complex vitamin and precursor of coenzyme A and acyl carrier protein, supporting numerous acyl-transfer and energy-metabolism pathways rather than acting through a single drug target. It is used to prevent or treat pantothenic acid deficiency, which is rare. Adverse effects are uncommon, but high doses may cause gastrointestinal upset, including diarrhea.
tail_desc: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder of copper metabolism affecting mainly the liver and nervous system, caused by ATP7B mutations that impair biliary copper excretion and lead to toxic copper accumulation. It typically presents in children, adolescents, or young adults with hepatitis, cirrhosis, tremor, dystonia, psychiatric symptoms, and Kayser-Fleischer rings. Management centers on lifelong copper chelation or zinc therapy, dietary copper reduction, and liver transplantation for advanced hepatic failure.

head: Salsalate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salsalate is a nonacetylated salicylate NSAID and dimer of salicylic acid that is hydrolyzed to salicylate; it reduces prostaglandin synthesis primarily through cyclooxygenase inhibition, with relatively weak direct platelet inhibition compared with aspirin. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and other painful inflammatory conditions. Notable adverse effects include salicylism and gastrointestinal irritation or ulceration.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Vardenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vardenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzenesulfonamide derivative, that selectively blocks PDE5 to increase cyclic GMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation in the corpus cavernosum. It is approved for erectile dysfunction. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nitrates, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Aortic stenosis is a valvular heart disease of the cardiovascular system in which narrowing of the aortic valve obstructs left ventricular outflow, usually from age-related calcific degeneration, congenital bicuspid valve, or rheumatic scarring. It commonly affects older adults and causes exertional dyspnea, angina, syncope, and eventually heart failure; management ranges from monitoring to aortic valve replacement or transcatheter implantation when severe or symptomatic.

head: Magnesium chloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium chloride is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an electrolyte replacement and mineral supplement. It dissociates to provide magnesium, an essential cofactor for many enzymes and a modulator of neuromuscular transmission, membrane stability, and cardiac conduction, without a single specific receptor target. It is used to prevent or treat magnesium deficiency; adverse effects can include diarrhea, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment because of hypermagnesemia risk.
tail_desc: Disorder of phenylalanine metabolism is an inborn error of amino acid metabolism affecting systemic and especially neurologic development, caused by impaired conversion of phenylalanine, most commonly from phenylalanine hydroxylase or tetrahydrobiopterin defects. It typically presents in infancy or through newborn screening with hyperphenylalaninemia and risk of developmental delay if untreated; management centers on lifelong phenylalanine restriction and selected cofactor-based therapies.

head: Mechlorethamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mechlorethamine is a nitrogen mustard alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms highly reactive intermediates, causing DNA cross-linking and inhibition of DNA replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used in regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and as topical therapy for mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma). A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is also a potent vesicant if extravasated.
tail_desc: Amyloidosis is a protein misfolding disorder in which insoluble amyloid fibrils deposit extracellularly in tissues, causing progressive organ dysfunction; it may be systemic or localized and commonly affects the kidneys, heart, liver, nerves, and gastrointestinal tract. Manifestations vary by organ involved, often in adults, and management targets the precursor protein source, supportive care, and treatment of complications.

head: Ardeparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ardeparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant derived from porcine mucosal heparin. It potentiates antithrombin III, producing greater inhibition of factor Xa than thrombin (factor IIa). It has been used for prophylaxis of postoperative deep vein thrombosis, particularly after knee surgery. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it can also cause heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Retinal disease refers to a broad group of ophthalmic disorders affecting the retina, the light-sensitive neural tissue of the eye. Causes include vascular, degenerative, inflammatory, inherited, or traumatic damage, leading to photoreceptor or retinal pigment epithelium dysfunction. Common features include blurred vision, scotomas, metamorphopsia, night blindness, or vision loss. Management depends on cause and may involve risk-factor control, laser therapy, intravitreal drugs, or surgery.

head: Risedronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Risedronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate (pyridinyl bisphosphonate) that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget’s disease of bone, and prevention or treatment of glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophageal irritation or ulceration, and it is contraindicated in hypocalcemia and significant esophageal motility disorders.
tail_desc: Hyperparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the parathyroid glands causing excess parathyroid hormone, which raises blood calcium and disrupts bone and kidney homeostasis. It may be primary, secondary to chronic kidney disease or vitamin D deficiency, or tertiary after prolonged stimulation. Common features include hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, bone pain, fractures, weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; management targets the cause, with parathyroidectomy for selected cases.

head: Iodine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Iodine is an essential halogen and topical anti-infective/thyroid-active agent. As elemental iodine or iodophors, it rapidly iodinates and oxidizes microbial proteins and nucleotides, producing broad-spectrum antiseptic activity; as iodide, it serves as substrate for thyroid hormone synthesis in the thyroid gland. Established uses include skin antisepsis and prevention or treatment of iodine deficiency. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions and iodine-induced thyroid dysfunction.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, often from fibrosis, ischemia, drugs, or congenital conduction disease. It may cause bradycardia, fatigue, dizziness, syncope, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on degree and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for advanced or symptomatic block.

head: Clofedanol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clofedanol is a centrally acting non-opioid antitussive (cough suppressant), structurally classified as a diphenylmethane derivative. Its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well established, but it suppresses the cough reflex via central action. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of nonproductive cough. Notable adverse effects include drowsiness and dizziness.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Bacampicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bacampicillin is an oral ester prodrug of ampicillin, a beta-lactam aminopenicillin antibiotic. After hydrolysis to ampicillin, it binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, skin/soft tissue, and some gastrointestinal infections. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Temazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Temazepam is a benzodiazepine hypnotic used for the short-term treatment of insomnia. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride influx and neuronal inhibition. Common adverse effects include sedation, dizziness, and impaired psychomotor performance; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Irinotecan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irinotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. Its active metabolite SN-38 stabilizes the topoisomerase I-DNA complex, blocking DNA religation and causing lethal DNA damage during replication. It is used for metastatic colorectal cancer and in some regimens for other solid tumors. Notable toxicities include severe delayed diarrhea and myelosuppression, especially neutropenia.
tail_desc: Diarrheal disease is a gastrointestinal disorder characterized by the passage of loose or watery stools, usually from intestinal infection, toxins, malabsorption, or inflammatory processes that impair fluid absorption and increase secretion. It commonly causes frequent stools, abdominal cramps, nausea, and dehydration, especially in young children and older adults. Management centers on oral or intravenous rehydration, electrolyte replacement, nutritional support, and treating the underlying cause when identified.

head: Azelastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Azelastine is a second-generation antihistamine, chemically a phthalazinone derivative, used mainly as an intranasal or ophthalmic agent. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist/inverse agonist, reducing allergic inflammation. Established indications include seasonal allergic rhinitis, nonallergic vasomotor rhinitis, and allergic conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is bitter taste with intranasal use; somnolence can also occur.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Proparacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Proparacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic used topically in ophthalmology. It blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in sensory nerves, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It is indicated for short-term ocular surface anesthesia during diagnostic procedures, tonometry, and minor ophthalmic interventions. Notable adverse effects include transient stinging or burning, and repeated use can cause corneal epithelial toxicity.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Tesamorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: pituitary deficiency due to empty sella turcica syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Tesamorelin is a synthetic growth hormone–releasing hormone analog that binds pituitary GHRH receptors to stimulate endogenous growth hormone secretion and increase IGF-1. It is approved to reduce excess abdominal visceral adipose tissue in adults with HIV-associated lipodystrophy. Notable risks include glucose intolerance or hyperglycemia, and it is contraindicated in active malignancy and disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
tail_desc: Pituitary deficiency due to empty sella turcica syndrome is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary system in which herniation of cerebrospinal fluid into the sella flattens or compresses the pituitary, causing partial or multiple hormone deficiencies. Patients may have fatigue, amenorrhea, infertility, sexual dysfunction, growth or thyroid/adrenal deficiency symptoms, sometimes with headache or visual complaints. Management focuses on endocrine evaluation, hormone replacement, and treating associated causes or complications.

head: Thiopental (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thiopental is an ultra-short-acting barbiturate intravenous anesthetic. It enhances inhibitory neurotransmission primarily by positively modulating GABA-A receptors, increasing chloride channel opening and causing rapid CNS depression. It is used for induction of general anesthesia and has established use in seizure control and reduction of intracranial pressure. A notable risk is profound respiratory and cardiovascular depression; it is contraindicated in acute porphyria.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Darifenacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Darifenacin is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a selective muscarinic M3 receptor antagonist. By blocking M3 receptors on bladder detrusor smooth muscle, it reduces involuntary contractions and increases bladder capacity. It is approved for treatment of overactive bladder with urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency. A notable adverse effect is constipation; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Adenosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Adenosine is an endogenous purine nucleoside antiarrhythmic that activates cardiac A1 adenosine receptors, increasing potassium conductance and suppressing cAMP-dependent calcium currents to transiently slow sinoatrial and atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used acutely to terminate paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia and in pharmacologic cardiac stress testing. Notable adverse effects include brief flushing, chest discomfort, and bronchospasm; it is contraindicated in second- or third-degree AV block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Sick sinus syndrome is a cardiac conduction disorder of the sinoatrial node causing abnormal impulse formation, usually from age-related fibrotic degeneration or other atrial disease. It typically affects older adults and presents with sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, tachy-brady episodes, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or palpitations. Management focuses on treating reversible causes and, for symptomatic bradyarrhythmias, permanent pacemaker implantation.

head: Nevirapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nevirapine is a dipyridodiazepinone non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) that binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, blocking viral DNA synthesis. It is used, in combination antiretroviral therapy, for treatment of HIV-1 infection and has also been used to reduce mother-to-child transmission. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, including potentially fatal hepatitis, and serious rash such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Lyell syndrome, or toxic epidermal necrolysis, is a severe mucocutaneous drug hypersensitivity disorder affecting the skin and mucous membranes, characterized by widespread keratinocyte apoptosis with full-thickness epidermal necrosis and detachment. Patients, often after recent medication exposure, develop fever, painful erythema, blistering, mucosal erosions, and positive Nikolsky sign. Management requires immediate withdrawal of the culprit drug and intensive supportive, often burn-unit, care.

head: Diphenoxylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diphenoxylate is a synthetic opioid antidiarrheal, structurally related to meperidine, that acts mainly as a peripheral mu-opioid receptor agonist in the intestine to reduce gastrointestinal motility. It is used, typically with atropine, as adjunctive therapy for diarrhea. Notable risks include central nervous system and respiratory depression in overdose, and it is contraindicated in diarrhea caused by invasive enteric infection.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Proline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Proline is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Meticillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meticillin (methicillin) is a penicillinase-resistant beta-lactam antibiotic of the penicillin class. It inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It was used for infections caused by penicillinase-producing staphylococci, especially methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including serious allergic reactions.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Metipranolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metipranolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class used primarily as an ophthalmic agent. By antagonizing β1- and β2-adrenergic receptors in the ciliary epithelium, it reduces aqueous humor production and lowers intraocular pressure. It is indicated for ocular hypertension and open-angle glaucoma; notable risks include bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in asthma and severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Mifepristone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mifepristone is a synthetic steroidal antiprogestin that primarily antagonizes the progesterone receptor and also has glucocorticoid receptor antagonist activity. It is approved for medical termination of intrauterine pregnancy, management of early pregnancy loss in combination regimens, and control of hyperglycemia in endogenous Cushing syndrome. A notable contraindication is suspected ectopic pregnancy; heavy uterine bleeding can occur.
tail_desc: Sporadic porphyria cutanea tarda is the most common acquired cutaneous porphyria, affecting heme biosynthesis in the liver and skin through reduced hepatic uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity. It typically presents in adults with photosensitivity, skin fragility, blistering, hyperpigmentation, and hypertrichosis on sun-exposed areas. Management focuses on removing triggers, strict photoprotection, periodic phlebotomy, and sometimes low-dose hydroxychloroquine.

head: Maprotiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Maprotiline is a tetracyclic antidepressant that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake by blocking the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively weak effects on serotonin uptake.
