It has been used for depressive disorders, including major depression with anxiety features. A notable risk is dose-related seizures, and it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or during the acute recovery phase after myocardial infarction.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Butobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butobarbital is a barbiturate sedative-hypnotic, used primarily for the short-term treatment of insomnia and occasionally for preoperative sedation. Like other barbiturates, it enhances GABAergic inhibition by positively modulating the GABA-A receptor, increasing chloride ion influx and depressing central nervous system activity. A notable adverse effect is respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in patients with porphyria.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Molindone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to G6PC3 deficiency (a disease)
head_desc: Molindone is a typical antipsychotic of the dihydroindolone class used for the treatment of schizophrenia. Its antipsychotic effect is primarily attributed to central dopamine D2 receptor antagonism. Like other first-generation antipsychotics, it can cause extrapyramidal symptoms; tardive dyskinesia is a notable serious adverse effect with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to G6PC3 deficiency is a hereditary hematologic immunodeficiency caused by biallelic G6PC3 mutations, leading to impaired neutrophil production and survival. It presents from infancy with persistent severe neutropenia, recurrent bacterial infections, and may include congenital anomalies such as cardiac or urogenital defects and prominent superficial veins. Management centers on granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, prompt antibiotic treatment, and multidisciplinary supportive care.

head: Lurasidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lurasidone is an atypical antipsychotic of the benzisothiazole class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at 5-HT7 and 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar depression. A notable adverse effect is akathisia; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers.
tail_desc: Transient ischemic attack is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by transient focal cerebral, retinal, or spinal cord ischemia without acute infarction, usually from embolic or thrombotic vascular occlusion. It produces sudden, brief focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or vision loss, often in older adults with vascular risk factors, and requires urgent stroke evaluation and secondary prevention.

head: Chromium picolinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chromium picolinate is a nutritional supplement consisting of trivalent chromium chelated to picolinic acid, used to provide chromium in deficiency states. Its mechanism is not fully established; chromium is thought to enhance insulin signaling and glucose metabolism, but no specific primary molecular target is well defined. It is not an FDA-approved drug, and reported adverse effects include renal toxicity.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis obliterans is a chronic occlusive peripheral arterial disease, usually affecting lower-extremity arteries, caused by progressive atherosclerotic plaque formation with luminal narrowing and impaired blood flow. It typically occurs in older adults, especially smokers and patients with diabetes, presenting with intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool limbs, rest pain, or ischemic ulcers. Management focuses on risk-factor control, antiplatelet therapy, exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Trimethoprim (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trimethoprim is a synthetic diaminopyrimidine antibacterial that selectively inhibits bacterial dihydrofolate reductase, blocking tetrahydrofolate synthesis and thereby impairing nucleic acid production. It is established for treatment and prophylaxis of susceptible urinary tract infections and, in combination with sulfamethoxazole, for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and other bacterial infections. Notable adverse effects include hyperkalemia and megaloblastic anemia, especially in folate deficiency.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Sulfamethazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamethazine is a long-acting sulfonamide antibacterial (sulfonamide antifolate) that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It has been used mainly in veterinary medicine for susceptible bacterial infections and historically in some human infections. A notable risk is severe sulfonamide hypersensitivity, including Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Glutamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Glutamic acid is a nonessential amino acid and acidic amino acid used mainly as a nutritional or electrolyte replacement component rather than a receptor-targeted drug. It serves as a metabolic substrate and precursor in amino acid and neurotransmitter pathways, without a well-defined primary pharmacologic target in approved therapy. Clinical use is limited to nutritional supplementation; gastrointestinal irritation may occur, and caution is warranted in severe renal or hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Osteitis fibrosa is a metabolic bone disorder of the skeleton, usually caused by hyperparathyroidism with excess parathyroid hormone driving increased osteoclastic bone resorption and replacement by fibrous tissue. It can cause bone pain, fragility fractures, deformity, and “brown tumors,” classically in patients with severe primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism. Management targets the underlying hyperparathyroidism and correction of calcium-phosphate abnormalities.

head: Aurothioglucose (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aurothioglucose is a gold(I) thiolate compound formerly used as a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug for rheumatoid arthritis. Its precise mechanism is incompletely defined, but gold salts modulate immune and inflammatory cell function rather than acting on a single established molecular target. Important adverse effects include dermatitis, stomatitis, proteinuria, and bone marrow suppression; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Saquinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Saquinavir is a peptidomimetic HIV protease inhibitor antiretroviral that selectively inhibits the HIV-1 and HIV-2 protease enzymes, preventing cleavage of viral polyprotein precursors and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, typically with ritonavir boosting, in combination therapy for HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is PR interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in patients with significant cardiac conduction disorders.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular block is a cardiac conduction disorder of the heart’s electrical system in which impulse transmission from the atria to the ventricles is delayed or interrupted, due to fibrosis, ischemia, medications, or structural heart disease. It may cause bradycardia, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or be asymptomatic, especially in older adults. Management depends on severity and cause, including observation, treating reversible factors, and pacemaker implantation for high-grade block.

head: Proguanil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Proguanil is a biguanide antimalarial, used mainly for malaria prophylaxis and treatment in combination with atovaquone. It is a prodrug metabolized to cycloguanil, which inhibits the Plasmodium dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), impairing folate synthesis and parasite replication. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and mouth ulcers; it should be used cautiously in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Lactulose (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lactulose is a synthetic, nonabsorbable disaccharide osmotic laxative. In the colon it is metabolized by bacteria to organic acids that increase osmotic water retention and acidify colonic contents, trapping ammonia as ammonium; it has no specific human molecular target. It is used for constipation and prevention or treatment of portal-systemic/hepatic encephalopathy. Common adverse effects include flatulence, abdominal cramping, and diarrhea.
tail_desc: Cystinuria is a hereditary renal tubular transport disorder affecting the urinary system, caused by defective reabsorption of cystine and dibasic amino acids in the proximal tubule. Excess urinary cystine, which is poorly soluble, leads to recurrent cystine kidney stones, often beginning in childhood or young adulthood, with hematuria, flank pain, and obstruction. Management focuses on high fluid intake, urine alkalinization, dietary sodium reduction, and sometimes thiol drugs.

head: Sodium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium acetate is an alkalinizing electrolyte salt and acetate donor used in parenteral nutrition and intravenous fluid therapy. After metabolism, acetate is converted primarily in muscle and other tissues to bicarbonate, helping correct metabolic acidosis; it has no single receptor target. Established uses include correction of hyponatremia and acidosis in compounded IV solutions. Excess administration can cause metabolic alkalosis.
tail_desc: Periodic paralysis is a group of rare skeletal muscle channelopathies causing episodic flaccid weakness due to abnormal muscle membrane excitability, often linked to ion-channel defects affecting potassium handling. Attacks typically begin in childhood or adolescence, may be triggered by rest after exercise or carbohydrate-rich meals, and can occur with hypo-, hyper-, or normokalemia. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, potassium correction when appropriate, and subtype-specific agents such as carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.

head: Vardenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vardenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzenesulfonamide derivative, that selectively blocks PDE5 to increase cyclic GMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation in the corpus cavernosum. It is approved for erectile dysfunction. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nitrates, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Aortic valve stenosis is a valvular heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by narrowing and stiffening of the aortic valve, usually from age-related calcific degeneration or congenital bicuspid valve disease. It can cause exertional dyspnea, angina, syncope, and a systolic ejection murmur, especially in older adults. Management ranges from monitoring mild disease to valve replacement, including surgical or transcatheter approaches, for severe symptomatic cases.

head: Colesevelam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Colesevelam is a bile acid sequestrant, a nonabsorbed polymeric anion-exchange resin used to lower LDL cholesterol and improve glycemic control in type 2 diabetes. It binds bile acids in the intestinal lumen, interrupting enterohepatic reabsorption and increasing hepatic conversion of cholesterol to bile acids. Established indications include primary hyperlipidemia and adjunctive therapy for type 2 diabetes. Constipation is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Ileus is a functional, nonmechanical obstruction of the gastrointestinal tract, usually affecting the intestines through impaired bowel motility rather than a physical blockage. It commonly occurs after abdominal surgery, severe illness, infection, electrolyte disturbances, or medication use. Typical features include abdominal distension, pain, nausea, vomiting, and absent or reduced bowel sounds. Management targets the cause and supportive care, including bowel rest, fluid and electrolyte correction, and minimizing precipitating drugs.

head: Maprotiline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Maprotiline is a tetracyclic antidepressant that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake by blocking the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively weak effects on serotonin uptake. It has been used for depressive disorders, including major depression with anxiety features. A notable risk is dose-related seizures, and it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or during the acute recovery phase after myocardial infarction.
tail_desc: Angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic disease in which the peripheral iris obstructs the anterior chamber angle, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid or intermittent rise in intraocular pressure. It typically affects anatomically predisposed older adults, especially those with shallow anterior chambers. Features include severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos, nausea, and a red eye; management is urgent pressure lowering followed by laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Tiludronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tiludronic acid is a non-nitrogen bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, likely via disruption of osteoclast energy metabolism. It is used for Paget disease of bone and, in veterinary medicine, for navicular syndrome in horses. A notable adverse effect is upper gastrointestinal irritation; esophageal abnormalities are a contraindication to oral use.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Bimatoprost (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bimatoprost is a synthetic prostaglandin F2α analog (prostamide) used primarily as an ophthalmic antiglaucoma agent. It lowers intraocular pressure by increasing aqueous humor outflow, mainly through the uveoscleral pathway, via prostamide/prostaglandin receptor-mediated effects. It is approved for open-angle glaucoma, ocular hypertension, and eyelash hypotrichosis. A notable adverse effect is conjunctival hyperemia, and it may also cause increased iris pigmentation.
tail_desc: Uveitis is an inflammatory disorder of the uveal tract of the eye, often involving adjacent ocular structures. It may result from autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or be idiopathic. Typical features include eye pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision, and floaters, affecting both adults and children. Management depends on cause and severity, commonly using corticosteroids, cycloplegics, and treatment of any underlying infectious or systemic condition.

head: Vitamin A (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vitamin A comprises fat-soluble retinoids, including retinol and retinyl esters. It is converted to active retinoic acid, which binds nuclear retinoic acid receptors (RARs) and retinoid X receptors (RXRs) to regulate gene transcription involved in vision, epithelial differentiation, growth, and immune function. It is used to prevent and treat vitamin A deficiency; excessive doses can cause hepatotoxicity and teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Cyclosporine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclosporine is a calcineurin-inhibiting immunosuppressant, a cyclic undecapeptide derived from fungi. It binds cyclophilin, and the complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase, reducing NFAT activation and T-cell cytokine transcription, especially interleukin-2. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection and to treat conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and severe dry eye. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Eye infectious disease is a broad category of ocular disorders affecting the conjunctiva, cornea, eyelids, or intraocular tissues, caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites that invade eye structures and trigger inflammation. Typical features include redness, pain, discharge, photophobia, blurred vision, and sometimes vision loss, occurring in all age groups. Management generally involves prompt pathogen-directed antimicrobial therapy, supportive care, and urgent ophthalmologic evaluation for severe or sight-threatening cases.

head: Mivacurium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mivacurium is a short-acting, nondepolarizing benzylisoquinolinium neuromuscular blocking agent that competitively antagonizes nicotinic acetylcholine receptors at the skeletal muscle motor end plate, producing paralysis. It is used as an adjunct to general anesthesia to facilitate endotracheal intubation and provide skeletal muscle relaxation during surgery or mechanical ventilation. Histamine release with hypotension or bronchospasm is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Chronic renal failure syndrome is a chronic kidney disease of the renal system characterized by progressive, irreversible loss of nephron function, leading to reduced glomerular filtration and accumulation of metabolic waste, fluid, and electrolyte disturbances. It commonly affects adults, especially those with diabetes or hypertension, and presents with fatigue, edema, hypertension, anemia, and uremic symptoms. Management focuses on treating the cause, slowing progression, correcting complications, and renal replacement therapy when advanced.

head: Nalidixic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nalidixic acid is a first-generation quinolone antibacterial, a naphthyridone derivative, that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and thereby blocks DNA replication. It has been used primarily for treatment of uncomplicated urinary tract infections caused by susceptible Gram-negative organisms. Notable risks include central nervous system effects and it is generally contraindicated in infants younger than 3 months.
tail_desc: Peripheral nervous system disease comprises disorders affecting peripheral nerves, nerve roots, neuromuscular junctions, or peripheral ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord. Causes include inherited abnormalities, metabolic or immune-mediated injury, infection, trauma, and toxins. Typical features are distal weakness, sensory loss, pain, paresthesias, and reduced reflexes in children or adults. Management targets the cause, symptom control, rehabilitation, and prevention of complications.

head: Ethosuximide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethosuximide is a succinimide anticonvulsant used primarily for absence (petit mal) seizures. Its antiepileptic effect is attributed mainly to inhibition of low-threshold T-type calcium currents in thalamic neurons, reducing abnormal thalamocortical rhythmic activity. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal upset, and it can also cause serious but rare blood dyscrasias or hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Cyclobenzaprine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclobenzaprine is a centrally acting skeletal muscle relaxant, structurally related to tricyclic antidepressants. Its exact mechanism is not fully defined, but it acts within the brainstem to reduce tonic somatic motor activity rather than directly at the neuromuscular junction. It is used for short-term relief of acute painful musculoskeletal spasm; notable adverse effects include marked drowsiness and anticholinergic effects, and it is contraindicated with MAO inhibitors.
tail_desc: obsolete heart block is a disease or medical condition.

head: Digoxin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Digoxin is a cardiac glycoside that inhibits the Na+/K+-ATPase pump in cardiomyocytes, increasing intracellular calcium and enhancing cardiac contractility while augmenting vagal tone to slow atrioventricular nodal conduction. It is used for symptomatic heart failure and ventricular rate control in atrial fibrillation or flutter. A notable adverse effect is potentially life-threatening arrhythmias, especially with toxicity.
tail_desc: Familial sick sinus syndrome is an inherited cardiac conduction disorder affecting the sinoatrial node, the heart’s natural pacemaker. It results from genetic defects that impair impulse generation or conduction, causing sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses, dizziness, syncope, fatigue, or atrial arrhythmias, often in affected families across generations. Management is directed by symptoms and rhythm severity, commonly including monitoring and permanent pacemaker implantation when clinically indicated.

head: Cisapride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cisapride is a substituted piperidinyl benzamide gastroprokinetic agent that enhances gastrointestinal motility primarily by acting as a serotonin 5-HT4 receptor agonist, promoting acetylcholine release in the enteric nervous system. It was used for gastroesophageal reflux disease and other motility disorders, but its use became highly restricted because it can prolong the QT interval and cause serious ventricular arrhythmias, especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors.
tail_desc: Familial long QT syndrome is an inherited cardiac channelopathy affecting the heart’s electrical conduction system, most often caused by pathogenic variants in ion-channel genes that delay ventricular repolarization and prolong the QT interval. It typically presents in children or young adults with syncope, palpitations, seizures, or sudden cardiac arrest due to torsades de pointes, and is managed with trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: L-Lysine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: L-Lysine is an essential basic amino acid used as a nutritional supplement rather than a receptor-targeted drug. Its primary role is to replace dietary lysine deficiency and support normal protein synthesis; no specific pharmacologic molecular target is established for routine supplementation. Established use is prevention or treatment of lysine deficiency. It is generally well tolerated, but gastrointestinal upset such as abdominal pain or diarrhea can occur.
tail_desc: Digitalis poisoning is a toxic cardiovascular condition caused by excessive exposure to cardiac glycosides such as digoxin, producing inhibition of Na+/K+-ATPase and resulting electrolyte-mediated conduction abnormalities. It typically affects patients receiving digoxin, especially older adults or those with renal impairment, and presents with nausea, vomiting, confusion, visual disturbances, bradyarrhythmias, or ventricular arrhythmias. Management centers on stopping the drug, correcting electrolytes, monitoring rhythm, and using digoxin-specific antibody fragments in severe cases.

head: Oxymetazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetazoline is an imidazoline-derived sympathomimetic decongestant and vasoconstrictor that acts primarily as an alpha-adrenergic receptor agonist, producing constriction of arterioles in nasal and conjunctival mucosa. It is used topically for temporary relief of nasal congestion and ocular redness, and as a topical treatment for persistent facial erythema of rosacea. A notable risk is rebound congestion with prolonged intranasal use.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis disorder is a vascular disease of the circulatory system characterized by thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls, often related to aging, hypertension, diabetes, or lipid deposition in specific subtypes. It may be asymptomatic or cause ischemic complications such as angina, stroke, or peripheral vascular disease, especially in older adults. Management targets risk-factor control and treatment of cardiovascular complications.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Ibandronate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ibandronate is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds bone hydroxyapatite and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, reducing bone resorption. It is approved for prevention and treatment of postmenopausal osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis with oral formulations; it is contraindicated in patients with esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying or inability to remain upright.
tail_desc: Esophageal ulcer is a mucosal ulcerative lesion of the esophagus, a gastrointestinal disorder most often caused by gastroesophageal reflux, medication-induced injury, infection, or caustic damage. It commonly presents with odynophagia, dysphagia, retrosternal pain, and sometimes bleeding, particularly in patients with severe reflux, immunosuppression, or pill exposure. Management targets the cause, typically including acid suppression, withdrawal of offending agents, and treatment of infectious etiologies when present.

head: Artemether (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Artemether is a semisynthetic artemisinin antimalarial, chemically a methyl ether derivative of dihydroartemisinin. Its endoperoxide bridge is activated by iron/heme in Plasmodium parasites, generating reactive radicals that damage parasite proteins and membranes; no single primary molecular target is firmly established. It is used, typically in combination (for example with lumefantrine), to treat uncomplicated falciparum malaria. Notable adverse effects include QT-interval prolongation.
tail_desc: Long QT syndrome is a cardiac electrical disorder affecting ventricular repolarization, characterized by prolongation of the QT interval on electrocardiography, usually due to inherited ion-channel defects or acquired drug/electrolyte effects. It predisposes children or adults to syncope, seizures, and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia including torsades de pointes, with risk of sudden death; management emphasizes trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, correction of reversible causes, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Clavulanic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clavulanic acid is a beta-lactam beta-lactamase inhibitor, structurally a clavem, used with beta-lactam antibiotics such as amoxicillin to overcome bacterial resistance. It irreversibly inhibits many serine beta-lactamases, protecting the partner antibiotic rather than exerting strong antibacterial activity alone. Established uses include susceptible respiratory, skin, urinary, and other mixed infections; cholestatic hepatitis is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Oxaprozin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxaprozin is a propionic acid–derived nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity, including aspirin-sensitive asthma.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Aortic valve disease is a cardiac valvular disorder affecting the aortic valve, most commonly causing stenosis or regurgitation from age-related calcific degeneration, congenital bicuspid valve, or rheumatic damage. Patients, often older adults, may develop exertional dyspnea, angina, syncope, or heart failure. Management includes echocardiographic surveillance, medical treatment of complications, and valve repair or replacement when disease becomes severe or symptomatic.

head: Ergometrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ergometrine (ergonovine) is an ergot alkaloid uterotonic that stimulates uterine smooth muscle chiefly via serotonin 5-HT2 and alpha-adrenergic receptors, producing sustained contractions. It is used to prevent or treat postpartum and postabortion uterine atony and hemorrhage. A notable risk is severe hypertension or vasospasm, so it is contraindicated in patients with hypertension or preeclampsia.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis obliterans is a chronic occlusive peripheral arterial disease, usually affecting lower-extremity arteries, caused by progressive atherosclerotic plaque formation with luminal narrowing and impaired blood flow. It typically occurs in older adults, especially smokers and patients with diabetes, presenting with intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool limbs, rest pain, or ischemic ulcers. Management focuses on risk-factor control, antiplatelet therapy, exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Sulfamethazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfamethazine is a long-acting sulfonamide antibacterial (sulfonamide antifolate) that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It has been used mainly in veterinary medicine for susceptible bacterial infections and historically in some human infections. A notable risk is severe sulfonamide hypersensitivity, including Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Folic acid deficiency anemia is a megaloblastic anemia of the hematologic system caused by inadequate folate, impairing DNA synthesis and producing ineffective erythropoiesis with enlarged red cell precursors. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, glossitis, and macrocytosis, often in malnutrition, alcoholism, pregnancy, malabsorption, or increased demand. Management involves folate replacement and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Inositol nicotinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Inositol nicotinate is a nicotinic acid ester (hexanicotinate of inositol) used as a peripheral vasodilator. It acts as a slow-release source of niacin, although its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well defined beyond promoting vasodilation. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of peripheral vascular disorders such as intermittent claudication and Raynaud-type symptoms. Flushing is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related gastrointestinal disorder characterized by mucosal ulceration in the stomach or proximal duodenum, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It typically presents with epigastric pain, dyspepsia, nausea, or bleeding, especially in affected adults. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, and avoiding ulcerogenic medications.

head: Streptomycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Streptomycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic produced by Streptomyces griseus that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting initiation and causing mRNA misreading to inhibit protein synthesis. It is used mainly for tuberculosis in combination regimens and for plague and tularemia. A notable adverse effect is ototoxicity, and it can also cause nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Nimodipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nimodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that selectively inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels, producing cerebral arterial vasodilation. It is established for improving neurologic outcome by reducing ischemic deficits after aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage. A notable adverse effect is hypotension; it is contraindicated for intravenous administration because parenteral use has caused severe cardiovascular collapse.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Mezlocillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Mezlocillin is a broad-spectrum acylureidopenicillin (beta-lactam) antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible infections including urinary tract, respiratory, intra-abdominal, and biliary tract infections, with activity against some Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Sulindac (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulindac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the indene acetic acid derivative class, used as a prodrug whose active sulfide metabolite reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is established for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and acute gouty arthritis. Notable risks include serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated after coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
tail_desc: Congestive heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment reduces the heart’s ability to pump or fill effectively, causing pulmonary and/or systemic fluid congestion. It commonly affects older adults and patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy, producing dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance. Management focuses on treating the cause, diuresis, guideline-directed heart-failure medications, sodium/fluid control, and selected device or advanced therapies.

head: Quazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Quazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine hypnotic of the triazolobenzodiazepine family that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing inhibitory GABA signaling. It is indicated for the treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling asleep and maintaining sleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day sedation; it is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to benzodiazepines.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Ozanimod (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ozanimod is an oral sphingosine 1-phosphate (S1P) receptor modulator that selectively targets S1P1 and S1P5, reducing lymphocyte egress from lymph nodes and thereby limiting inflammatory cell trafficking. It is approved for relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis and moderately to severely active ulcerative colitis. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia, particularly after treatment initiation.
tail_desc: Cerebral infarction is an ischemic stroke of the central nervous system in which interrupted cerebral blood flow, usually from arterial thrombosis or embolism, causes focal brain tissue necrosis. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or visual loss, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible and secondary stroke prevention.

head: Lamotrigine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lamotrigine is a phenyltriazine anticonvulsant and mood-stabilizing drug that primarily inhibits voltage-gated sodium channels, reducing presynaptic glutamate release. It is approved for epilepsy, including focal and generalized seizures, and for maintenance treatment of bipolar I disorder. A notable adverse effect is serious rash, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, with risk increased by rapid dose escalation or coadministration with valproate.
tail_desc: Idiopathic aplastic anemia is an acquired bone marrow failure disorder of the hematopoietic system in which immune-mediated destruction or suppression of hematopoietic stem cells leads to hypocellular marrow and pancytopenia without an identifiable cause. Patients of any age present with fatigue, infections, and bleeding or bruising. Management centers on supportive transfusions and infection prevention, with immunosuppressive therapy or allogeneic stem cell transplantation in appropriate candidates.

head: Sildenafil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (a disease)
head_desc: Sildenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a pyrazolopyrimidinone derivative, that blocks PDE5 and increases cGMP-mediated smooth muscle relaxation, especially in the corpus cavernosum and pulmonary vasculature. It is approved for erectile dysfunction and pulmonary arterial hypertension. A notable contraindication is concomitant nitrate therapy, which can cause potentially severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin is a benign inherited hemoglobinopathy of the hematologic system caused by genetic variants that sustain gamma-globin expression and continued HbF production after birth. Affected individuals, often identified incidentally, are usually asymptomatic with normal or mild microcytic blood findings; the condition can lessen severity of sickle cell disease or beta-thalassemia. Management is typically reassurance, diagnosis confirmation, and genetic counseling.

head: Primaquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Primaquine is an 8-aminoquinoline antimalarial that is active against hepatic stages of Plasmodium, though its precise mechanism is not fully defined and likely involves oxidative metabolites causing parasite mitochondrial dysfunction. It is used for radical cure and relapse prevention of P. vivax and P. ovale malaria, and for terminal prophylaxis. A major risk is hemolytic anemia in patients with G6PD deficiency, making G6PD testing essential before use.
tail_desc: Galactosemia is an inherited metabolic disorder affecting carbohydrate metabolism, usually caused by deficiency of enzymes needed to process galactose, classically galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase. It typically presents in newborns after milk feeding with poor feeding, vomiting, jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, cataracts, and risk of sepsis. Management is prompt lifelong dietary restriction of galactose/lactose and monitoring for complications.

head: Ibutilide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ibutilide is a class III antiarrhythmic methanesulfonanilide used intravenously for rapid conversion of recent-onset atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter to sinus rhythm. It prolongs cardiac action potential duration and refractoriness mainly by enhancing a slow inward sodium current and also blocking delayed rectifier potassium currents. A notable risk is QT prolongation with torsades de pointes.
tail_desc: Long QT syndrome is a cardiac electrical disorder affecting ventricular repolarization, characterized by prolongation of the QT interval on electrocardiography, usually due to inherited ion-channel defects or acquired drug/electrolyte effects. It predisposes children or adults to syncope, seizures, and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia including torsades de pointes, with risk of sudden death; management emphasizes trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, correction of reversible causes, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Azathioprine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Azathioprine is a thiopurine antimetabolite immunosuppressant and prodrug of 6-mercaptopurine that inhibits de novo purine synthesis and incorporation into nucleic acids, suppressing proliferating T and B lymphocytes. It is used to prevent kidney transplant rejection and to treat rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression, particularly in patients with low TPMT or NUDT15 activity.
tail_desc: Infectious disease is a broad category of disorders caused by pathogenic microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, or prions, affecting virtually any organ system. It results from microbial invasion and host inflammatory response, with manifestations ranging from fever and malaise to organ-specific symptoms. It can occur in any population, especially the very young, elderly, or immunocompromised, and is managed with pathogen-directed therapy, supportive care, and prevention measures such as hygiene, vaccination, and isolation.

head: Kanamycin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: central nervous system origin vertigo (a disease)
head_desc: Kanamycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing mistranslation. It is used for treatment of susceptible serious bacterial infections and has also been used as a second-line agent for multidrug-resistant tuberculosis. A notable adverse effect is ototoxicity, with nephrotoxicity also an important risk.
tail_desc: Central nervous system origin vertigo is a neurologic form of vertigo arising from lesions or dysfunction within the brainstem, cerebellum, or central vestibular pathways rather than the inner ear. Causes include stroke, multiple sclerosis, migraine, tumors, or other central nervous system disorders. Patients often have persistent dizziness with imbalance, nystagmus, and additional focal neurologic signs; management targets the underlying central cause and supportive rehabilitation.

head: Butacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic in the benzoate family. Like other local anesthetics, it acts by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used topically, particularly in ophthalmic anesthesia. As with related ester anesthetics, hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known ester local anesthetic allergy.
tail_desc: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the colonic mucosa, typically beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. It reflects immune-mediated intestinal inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals. Common features include bloody diarrhea, urgency, abdominal pain, and relapsing course, often in adolescents and adults. Management focuses on anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapy, with colectomy for refractory or complicated disease.

head: Moxifloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Moxifloxacin is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including community-acquired pneumonia, acute bacterial sinusitis, acute exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and some skin or intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation; fluoroquinolones can also cause tendon rupture.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Biotin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Biotin (vitamin B7, vitamin H) is a water-soluble B-complex vitamin used as a nutritional supplement and replacement therapy for biotin deficiency, including deficiency associated with inherited biotinidase deficiency or prolonged parenteral nutrition. It functions as an essential cofactor for several carboxylase enzymes involved in fatty acid synthesis, gluconeogenesis, and amino acid metabolism. It is generally well tolerated, but high-dose biotin can interfere with certain laboratory immunoassays, causing misleading test results.
tail_desc: Constipation disorder is a gastrointestinal motility and defecation disorder characterized by infrequent bowel movements, hard or difficult-to-pass stools, and a sensation of incomplete evacuation. It commonly results from slow colonic transit, pelvic floor dysfunction, medications, diet, or systemic illness, and affects all ages, especially older adults. Management typically emphasizes hydration, fiber, exercise, treating causes, and use of laxatives or pelvic floor therapy.

head: Mitoxantrone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mitoxantrone is an anthracenedione antineoplastic agent that intercalates into DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, disrupting DNA replication and repair. It is used to treat certain leukemias, metastatic breast cancer, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, and worsening relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis. A notable risk is cumulative dose-related cardiotoxicity, including reduced left ventricular ejection fraction and congestive heart failure.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Flutamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flutamide is a nonsteroidal antiandrogen anilide derivative that competitively antagonizes the androgen receptor, blocking testosterone and dihydrotestosterone signaling. It is used primarily in prostate cancer, often with a gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist for metastatic disease. A notable adverse effect is potentially severe hepatotoxicity, so it is contraindicated in patients with significant hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Ketoprofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ketoprofen is a propionic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for pain and inflammatory conditions such as osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and dysmenorrhea. A notable risk is gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Dapsone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dapsone is a synthetic sulfone antimicrobial with anti-inflammatory activity. It inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis in susceptible organisms, and is established for treatment of leprosy and for dermatitis herpetiformis; it is also used for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis in selected patients. A notable adverse effect is dose-related hemolytic anemia, especially in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked inherited red blood cell enzymopathy causing reduced protection against oxidative stress, leading to episodic hemolytic anemia. It commonly affects males, especially in malaria-endemic populations, and may present with neonatal jaundice, pallor, dark urine, fatigue, and jaundice after infections, fava beans, or oxidant drugs. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and supportive care during hemolysis.

head: Phendimetrazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phendimetrazine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, chemically related to phenethylamines and used short term as an adjunct in exogenous obesity management. It acts mainly as a prodrug of phenmetrazine, increasing central norepinephrine release and suppressing appetite. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or shortly after MAOI therapy.
tail_desc: Heart valve disease is a cardiovascular disorder affecting one or more cardiac valves, causing stenosis, regurgitation, or both from congenital abnormalities, degenerative calcification, rheumatic damage, infection, or connective tissue disease. It may present with murmurs, dyspnea, fatigue, chest pain, syncope, or heart failure, especially in older adults or those with prior rheumatic fever. Management includes echocardiographic monitoring, medical treatment of complications, and valve repair or replacement when severe.

head: Aminoglutethimide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminoglutethimide is a nonsteroidal aromatase inhibitor and adrenal steroidogenesis inhibitor. It blocks conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone and inhibits aromatase (CYP19), reducing synthesis of glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, estrogens, and androgens. It has been used for Cushing syndrome and advanced breast cancer. Notable adverse effects include sedation and rash; adrenal insufficiency is an important risk.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Acetazolamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acetazolamide is a sulfonamide-derived carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reversibly inhibits carbonic anhydrase isoenzymes, reducing bicarbonate reabsorption and aqueous humor and cerebrospinal fluid production. It is used for glaucoma, prevention and treatment of acute mountain sickness, adjunctive therapy in some epilepsies, and edema. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity and severe hepatic disease.
tail_desc: X-linked adrenal hypoplasia congenita is a rare inherited primary adrenal insufficiency caused by pathogenic variants in NR0B1 (DAX1), leading to underdevelopment of the adrenal cortex and often associated hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. It typically affects boys, presenting in infancy or childhood with vomiting, dehydration, hypoglycemia, salt wasting, and failure to thrive; management centers on lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement and later endocrine care for pubertal and reproductive issues.

head: Docetaxel (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Docetaxel is a semisynthetic taxane antineoplastic agent that binds the β-subunit of tubulin, promoting microtubule assembly and inhibiting depolymerization, thereby blocking mitosis. It is used for breast cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, prostate cancer, gastric adenocarcinoma, and head and neck cancer. A notable toxicity is severe neutropenia; use is contraindicated in patients with baseline neutrophil counts below 1,500 cells/mm³.
tail_desc: Peripheral nervous system disease comprises disorders affecting peripheral nerves, nerve roots, neuromuscular junctions, or peripheral ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord. Causes include inherited abnormalities, metabolic or immune-mediated injury, infection, trauma, and toxins. Typical features are distal weakness, sensory loss, pain, paresthesias, and reduced reflexes in children or adults. Management targets the cause, symptom control, rehabilitation, and prevention of complications.

head: Nifedipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nifedipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels, predominantly in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is used for hypertension and angina, including vasospastic angina. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; immediate-release formulations are generally avoided in acute ischemic heart disease because rapid hypotension may worsen ischemia.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Estramustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Estramustine is an antineoplastic estradiol–nitrogen mustard conjugate used mainly for palliative treatment of metastatic or progressive prostate cancer. It binds microtubule-associated proteins and tubulin, disrupting microtubule assembly and mitosis, with additional estrogenic effects. A notable adverse effect is thromboembolism; it is generally contraindicated in patients with active thrombophlebitis or thromboembolic disorders.
tail_desc: Gilbert syndrome is a common, benign inherited liver disorder of bilirubin metabolism caused by reduced UGT1A1 activity, leading to impaired hepatic conjugation of bilirubin and mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. It often presents in adolescents or young adults with intermittent jaundice, especially during fasting, illness, stress, or exertion, while liver function is otherwise normal. Management is typically reassurance and avoidance of known triggers.

head: Flucytosine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flucytosine is a fluorinated pyrimidine antimetabolite antifungal. In susceptible fungi it is taken up by cytosine permease and converted to 5-fluorouracil, which inhibits thymidylate synthase and is incorporated into RNA, disrupting DNA and RNA synthesis. It is used mainly, usually with amphotericin B, for serious Candida and Cryptococcus infections such as cryptococcal meningitis. Bone marrow suppression is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Tacrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tacrine is a centrally acting aminoacridine cholinesterase inhibitor that reversibly inhibits acetylcholinesterase, and to a lesser extent butyrylcholinesterase, increasing synaptic acetylcholine. It was approved for symptomatic treatment of mild to moderate Alzheimer disease dementia. A notable limitation is hepatotoxicity, with clinically significant elevations of liver transaminases requiring monitoring and restricting its use.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Fluticasone furoate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fluticasone furoate is a synthetic trifluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class that acts as a potent agonist of the glucocorticoid receptor, suppressing inflammatory cytokine production and other mediator pathways. It is used intranasally for allergic rhinitis and by inhalation for maintenance treatment of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Notable adverse effects include local candidiasis with inhaled use.
tail_desc: Infectious disease is a broad category of disorders caused by pathogenic microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites, or prions, affecting virtually any organ system. It results from microbial invasion and host inflammatory response, with manifestations ranging from fever and malaise to organ-specific symptoms. It can occur in any population, especially the very young, elderly, or immunocompromised, and is managed with pathogen-directed therapy, supportive care, and prevention measures such as hygiene, vaccination, and isolation.

head: Modafinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Modafinil is a wakefulness-promoting agent of the diphenylmethyl-sulfinyl-acetamide class. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it inhibits the dopamine transporter (DAT), increasing extracellular dopamine. It is approved to improve wakefulness in narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift work disorder. Notable adverse effects include headache and insomnia; serious rash, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, is a rare contraindication-related concern.
tail_desc: MVP1 is a disease or medical condition.

head: Pregabalin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pregabalin is a gabapentinoid anticonvulsant and analgesic, a structural analogue of gamma-aminobutyric acid that binds the α2δ subunit of presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release. It is used for neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, adjunctive treatment of partial-onset seizures, and generalized anxiety disorder in some regions. Common adverse effects include dizziness and somnolence.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Pralatrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pralatrexate is a folate analogue antimetabolite antifolate chemotherapy agent that selectively enters cells via the reduced folate carrier and inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, disrupting DNA synthesis. It is approved for relapsed or refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma. A notable toxicity is severe mucositis; folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation are recommended to reduce hematologic and mucosal adverse effects.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Diclofenamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diclofenamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor of the sulfonamide class. It reduces aqueous humor formation by inhibiting carbonic anhydrase, primarily in the ciliary processes of the eye. It is used to lower intraocular pressure in glaucoma, including chronic open-angle glaucoma. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia is a group of inherited adrenal steroidogenesis disorders affecting the endocrine system, most commonly caused by 21-hydroxylase deficiency due to CYP21A2 mutations. Impaired cortisol synthesis, with or without aldosterone deficiency, causes excess androgen production, leading to virilization, salt-wasting, and precocious puberty in infants or children. Management centers on glucocorticoid replacement, mineralocorticoids when needed, and monitoring growth and electrolytes.

head: Choline magnesium trisalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Choline magnesium trisalicylate is a nonacetylated salicylate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for relief of mild to moderate pain, fever, and inflammatory disorders such as arthritis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal irritation or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in salicylate hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: End-stage renal failure is the terminal stage of chronic kidney disease in the renal system, marked by irreversible loss of kidney function and severely reduced glomerular filtration. It usually results from progressive disorders such as diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. Patients develop uremic symptoms, fluid and electrolyte disturbances, and anemia; management centers on dialysis, kidney transplantation, and treatment of complications.

head: Ergometrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ergometrine (ergonovine) is an ergot alkaloid uterotonic that stimulates uterine smooth muscle chiefly via serotonin 5-HT2 and alpha-adrenergic receptors, producing sustained contractions. It is used to prevent or treat postpartum and postabortion uterine atony and hemorrhage. A notable risk is severe hypertension or vasospasm, so it is contraindicated in patients with hypertension or preeclampsia.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Flurbiprofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flurbiprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the propionic acid derivative class that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for pain and inflammation, including rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis, and as an ophthalmic agent to inhibit intraoperative miosis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Dimenhydrinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dimenhydrinate is a first-generation H1 antihistamine and antiemetic, a salt of diphenhydramine with 8-chlorotheophylline. It acts primarily by antagonizing histamine H1 receptors, with additional antimuscarinic effects that suppress vestibular stimulation and the vomiting center. It is used for prevention and treatment of motion sickness, nausea, vomiting, and vertigo. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to dimenhydrinate or diphenhydramine.
tail_desc: Hypophosphatemia is an electrolyte/metabolic disorder of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, muscle, and neuromuscular function, caused by inadequate intestinal absorption, intracellular phosphate shifts, or excessive renal losses. Manifestations range from weakness, bone pain, and osteomalacia or rickets to severe respiratory, neurologic, or cardiac dysfunction. Management targets the cause and typically includes oral or intravenous phosphate replacement and correction of associated vitamin D deficiency.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Hypoalphalipoproteinemia is a lipid metabolism disorder of the cardiovascular/metabolic system characterized by abnormally low high-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, due to genetic defects in HDL synthesis or accelerated catabolism, or secondary causes such as obesity, diabetes, smoking, and some drugs. It may be asymptomatic but is associated with premature atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease; management targets secondary causes and overall cardiovascular risk reduction.

head: Tesamorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tesamorelin is a synthetic growth hormone–releasing hormone analog that binds pituitary GHRH receptors to stimulate endogenous growth hormone secretion and increase IGF-1.
