It is approved to reduce excess abdominal visceral adipose tissue in adults with HIV-associated lipodystrophy. Notable risks include glucose intolerance or hyperglycemia, and it is contraindicated in active malignancy and disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
tail_desc: Hyperglycemia is a metabolic abnormality of the endocrine system characterized by elevated blood glucose, usually due to insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, most commonly in diabetes mellitus. It may cause polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision, and weight loss, and occurs in people with diabetes or acute illness. Management focuses on correcting the cause, hydration, glucose monitoring, and insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy.

head: Oxymetazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxymetazoline is an imidazoline-derived sympathomimetic decongestant and vasoconstrictor that acts primarily as an alpha-adrenergic receptor agonist, producing constriction of arterioles in nasal and conjunctival mucosa. It is used topically for temporary relief of nasal congestion and ocular redness, and as a topical treatment for persistent facial erythema of rosacea. A notable risk is rebound congestion with prolonged intranasal use.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Tiagabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tiagabine is a nipecotic acid derivative anticonvulsant that selectively inhibits the GABA transporter type 1 (GAT-1, SLC6A1), reducing neuronal and glial reuptake of GABA and increasing synaptic GABA levels. It is approved as adjunctive therapy for partial seizures in epilepsy. A notable adverse effect is dizziness or somnolence, and it can precipitate seizures in non-epileptic patients.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Sulfadoxine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sulfadoxine is a long-acting sulfonamide antimicrobial and antimalarial antifolate that inhibits parasitic and bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used in fixed combination with pyrimethamine for treatment and intermittent preventive therapy of malaria due to susceptible Plasmodium falciparum. A notable risk is severe hypersensitivity, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome; sulfonamide allergy is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Rizatriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Rizatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, chemically an indole derivative, that acts as a selective agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors to promote cranial vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease due to risk of coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Metronidazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole antimicrobial and antiprotozoal prodrug that is reduced in anaerobic bacteria and certain protozoa to reactive radicals that damage DNA. It is used for anaerobic bacterial infections, bacterial vaginosis, Clostridioides difficile infection, and protozoal diseases such as trichomoniasis, giardiasis, and amebiasis. A notable adverse effect is a disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Choline magnesium trisalicylate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Choline magnesium trisalicylate is a nonacetylated salicylate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for relief of mild to moderate pain, fever, and inflammatory disorders such as arthritis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal irritation or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in salicylate hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Mesoridazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to CXCR2 deficiency (a disease)
head_desc: Mesoridazine is a piperidine phenothiazine antipsychotic, historically used for schizophrenia. Like other typical antipsychotics, it acts primarily through central dopamine D2 receptor antagonism. Its clinical use became severely restricted because of dose-related QT interval prolongation and risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, including torsades de pointes, making it contraindicated in patients with known cardiac conduction abnormalities or significant QT prolongation.
tail_desc: Autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to CXCR2 deficiency is a rare inherited primary immunodeficiency of the hematologic/innate immune system caused by biallelic loss of CXCR2, a chemokine receptor needed for normal neutrophil trafficking and function. Affected infants or children have persistent severe neutropenia with recurrent serious bacterial infections. Management is supportive and infection-focused, including prompt antibiotics and specialist hematology/immunology care, with granulocyte colony-stimulating factor considered.

head: Amifostine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amifostine is a cytoprotective thiophosphate prodrug dephosphorylated by alkaline phosphatase to the active free thiol WR-1065, which scavenges reactive oxygen species and donates hydrogen ions to protect normal tissues from cisplatin- and radiation-induced DNA damage. It is used to reduce cumulative nephrotoxicity from cisplatin and xerostomia during head and neck radiotherapy. Notable adverse effects include severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Sudden cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which abrupt loss of effective cardiac output, usually from a malignant ventricular arrhythmia such as ventricular fibrillation or pulseless ventricular tachycardia, causes sudden collapse, unresponsiveness, and absent pulse. It can occur in adults with structural heart disease or acute ischemia, and requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and urgent post-resuscitation care.

head: Cyclosporine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cyclosporine is a calcineurin-inhibiting immunosuppressant, a cyclic undecapeptide derived from fungi. It binds cyclophilin, and the complex inhibits calcineurin phosphatase, reducing NFAT activation and T-cell cytokine transcription, especially interleukin-2. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection and to treat conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and severe dry eye. A notable adverse effect is nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Ixabepilone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ixabepilone is a semisynthetic epothilone B analog, a microtubule-stabilizing antineoplastic agent that binds β-tubulin and promotes tubulin polymerization, disrupting mitosis and inducing apoptosis. It is approved for locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer, including disease resistant to anthracyclines, taxanes, or capecitabine, often with capecitabine. A notable toxicity is peripheral neuropathy; it is contraindicated with capecitabine in severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Lurasidone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lurasidone is an atypical antipsychotic of the benzisothiazole class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at 5-HT7 and 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar depression. A notable adverse effect is akathisia; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers.
tail_desc: Hyperprolactinemia is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis characterized by persistently elevated serum prolactin, most often from prolactin-secreting pituitary adenomas, dopamine-antagonist drugs, hypothyroidism, or stalk dysfunction. It commonly causes galactorrhea, menstrual irregularity, infertility, and hypogonadism in women, and decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and infertility in men. Management targets the cause, often with dopamine agonists and treatment of underlying lesions or endocrine abnormalities.

head: Enoxaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin (factor IIa). It is used to prevent and treat venous thromboembolism and in acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Antithrombin III deficiency is an inherited or acquired thrombophilia of the coagulation system caused by reduced antithrombin quantity or function, leading to inadequate inhibition of thrombin and factor Xa. It predisposes mainly adolescents and adults to recurrent venous thromboembolism, including deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism. Management focuses on anticoagulation, avoidance of provoking risk factors, and perioperative or pregnancy-related thromboprophylaxis.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Cartilage disease is a broad musculoskeletal category involving damage, degeneration, inflammation, or developmental abnormalities of cartilage in joints, the spine, airways, or growth plates. Manifestations vary by cause and site but commonly include pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced mobility, or deformity, affecting both children and adults. Management is directed at the underlying disorder and may include rehabilitation, analgesia, anti-inflammatory treatment, or surgery.

head: Losartan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Losartan is an angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB), a nonpeptide biphenyl tetrazole antihypertensive that selectively antagonizes the angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptor, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone effects. It is used for hypertension, diabetic nephropathy in type 2 diabetes, and to reduce stroke risk in certain patients with left ventricular hypertrophy. A notable contraindication is pregnancy due to fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Anuria is a severe urinary system disorder defined by absent or near-absent urine output, usually reflecting acute kidney failure, urinary tract obstruction, or profound renal hypoperfusion. It presents with markedly reduced urination, fluid overload, electrolyte disturbances, and uremic features, often in critically ill patients or those with obstructive uropathy. Management focuses on urgent identification and reversal of the cause, hemodynamic stabilization, relief of obstruction, and renal replacement therapy when indicated.

head: Allantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Allantoin is a diureide of glyoxylic acid used topically as a skin protectant and keratolytic in many over-the-counter dermatologic products. Its mechanism is not defined by a specific molecular target; it is thought to promote desquamation and support wound healing by softening keratin and enhancing moisture retention. Established uses include minor wounds, burns, chapped or irritated skin, and oral mucosal irritation. Adverse effects are uncommon but can include local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder affecting sebum-rich areas, linked to abnormal inflammatory responses to Malassezia yeast and skin barrier dysfunction. It causes greasy scales, erythema, and dandruff on the scalp, face, and trunk, commonly in infants and adults. Management typically uses topical antifungals, low-potency corticosteroids, and gentle skin care.

head: Clobetasol propionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clobetasol propionate is a super-high-potency topical corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune mediators and reduce vasodilation and pruritus. It is used short term for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses such as plaque psoriasis and eczema. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy, and prolonged use may cause systemic hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression.
tail_desc: Hyperglycemia is a metabolic abnormality of the endocrine system characterized by elevated blood glucose, usually due to insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, most commonly in diabetes mellitus. It may cause polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision, and weight loss, and occurs in people with diabetes or acute illness. Management focuses on correcting the cause, hydration, glucose monitoring, and insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy.

head: Ketoconazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ketoconazole is an imidazole antifungal that inhibits fungal lanosterol 14α-demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and disrupting cell membrane formation. It is used topically or as shampoo for seborrheic dermatitis, dandruff, and cutaneous fungal infections, while systemic use is now limited. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, making oral ketoconazole contraindicated in acute or chronic liver disease.
tail_desc: Addison disease is primary adrenal insufficiency, an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex causing deficient cortisol and often aldosterone production, most commonly from autoimmune adrenal destruction. Patients may develop chronic fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, and hyponatremia, with risk of acute adrenal crisis. Management is lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, often mineralocorticoid replacement, and stress-dose therapy during illness or surgery.

head: Maraviroc (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic hepatitis B virus infection (a disease)
head_desc: Maraviroc is a small-molecule CCR5 antagonist antiretroviral in the entry inhibitor class. It binds the host chemokine receptor CCR5, blocking attachment and entry of CCR5-tropic HIV-1 into CD4+ cells. It is approved, in combination with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of CCR5-tropic HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, often preceded by rash or systemic allergic features.
tail_desc: Chronic hepatitis B virus infection is a chronic viral liver disease caused by persistent hepatitis B virus replication in hepatocytes, leading to ongoing hepatic inflammation and variable fibrosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with fatigue, abnormal liver enzymes, cirrhosis, or hepatocellular carcinoma risk, often affecting perinatally infected individuals or exposed adults. Management centers on monitoring and antiviral suppression to reduce liver damage and transmission.

head: Phendimetrazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phendimetrazine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, chemically related to phenethylamines and used short term as an adjunct in exogenous obesity management. It acts mainly as a prodrug of phenmetrazine, increasing central norepinephrine release and suppressing appetite. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or shortly after MAOI therapy.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Benzyl alcohol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzyl alcohol is an aromatic alcohol used primarily as an antimicrobial preservative and, in topical formulations, as a pediculicide. Its pediculicidal effect is physical rather than receptor-mediated, obstructing lice respiratory spiracles and causing asphyxiation; no specific human molecular target is established. Clinically, it is used to preserve multidose injectable products and to treat head lice. A notable contraindication is use in neonates because of potentially fatal “gasping syndrome.”
tail_desc: Severe early-onset COPD is a chronic obstructive airway disease of the respiratory system characterized by persistent airflow limitation from emphysema and/or chronic bronchitis, arising unusually early in adult life. It commonly reflects heavy smoking, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, or other susceptibility factors, and presents with progressive dyspnea, cough, wheeze, and frequent exacerbations. Management centers on smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccination, oxygen when indicated, and selected advanced interventions.

head: Telbivudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telbivudine is a synthetic thymidine nucleoside analog antiviral active against hepatitis B virus. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HBV DNA polymerase by competing with thymidine substrates and causing DNA chain termination. It is used for chronic hepatitis B infection. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, and peripheral neuropathy risk increases with pegylated interferon alfa coadministration.
tail_desc: Fatty liver disease is a hepatic disorder characterized by excess triglyceride accumulation in hepatocytes, most commonly associated with obesity, insulin resistance, diabetes, dyslipidemia, or alcohol use. It is often asymptomatic but may cause fatigue, hepatomegaly, or abnormal liver enzymes, and can progress to steatohepatitis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, and liver failure. Management focuses on treating the cause, especially weight loss, metabolic risk-factor control, alcohol cessation, and monitoring for progression.

head: Pilocarpine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pilocarpine is a direct-acting cholinergic agonist, an alkaloid muscarinic receptor agonist that primarily stimulates M3 receptors on exocrine glands and smooth muscle. It is used to treat xerostomia, including after head and neck radiotherapy and in Sjögren syndrome, and ophthalmically for glaucoma or miosis. A notable adverse effect is sweating; it is generally contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Uveitis is an inflammatory disorder of the uveal tract of the eye, often involving adjacent ocular structures. It may result from autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or be idiopathic. Typical features include eye pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision, and floaters, affecting both adults and children. Management depends on cause and severity, commonly using corticosteroids, cycloplegics, and treatment of any underlying infectious or systemic condition.

head: Vilazodone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Vilazodone is a serotonergic antidepressant classified as a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor and 5-HT1A receptor partial agonist. Its primary target is the serotonin transporter (SERT), with additional partial agonism at 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for treatment of major depressive disorder in adults. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, particularly when combined with MAO inhibitors, which are contraindicated.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Heparin, bovine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin, bovine, is an animal-derived unfractionated heparin, a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant obtained from bovine tissues. It potentiates antithrombin III, accelerating inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism and other settings requiring anticoagulation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Placental abruption is an obstetric emergency of the reproductive system in which a normally implanted placenta prematurely separates from the uterine wall, causing maternal bleeding and impaired fetal oxygenation. It typically presents in pregnancy with vaginal bleeding, abdominal or back pain, uterine tenderness, and fetal distress. Management centers on maternal stabilization, fetal assessment, and prompt delivery when severe or ongoing.

head: Cefotetan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefotetan is a parenteral second-generation cephalosporin (cephamycin) beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, skin/soft tissue, urinary tract, and perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia/bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Darifenacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Darifenacin is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a selective muscarinic M3 receptor antagonist. By blocking M3 receptors on bladder detrusor smooth muscle, it reduces involuntary contractions and increases bladder capacity. It is approved for treatment of overactive bladder with urge urinary incontinence, urgency, and frequency. A notable adverse effect is constipation; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Hereditary angioedema is a rare inherited disorder of the complement/bradykinin pathway causing recurrent, nonpruritic swelling of the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and upper airway. It most often results from SERPING1 mutations causing C1 inhibitor deficiency or dysfunction. Symptoms usually begin in childhood or adolescence; management focuses on on-demand treatment of attacks, especially laryngeal edema, and prophylaxis to prevent recurrences.

head: Ipratropium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ipratropium is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic bronchodilator, structurally related to atropine. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle, reducing vagally mediated bronchoconstriction and secretions. It is used as an inhaled treatment for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and also for bronchospasm in asthma. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth.
tail_desc: Benign prostatic hyperplasia is a nonmalignant enlargement of the prostate in the male genitourinary system, driven by age-related stromal and epithelial hyperplasia in the periurethral transition zone. It commonly affects older men and causes lower urinary tract symptoms such as hesitancy, weak stream, frequency, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. Management ranges from observation and lifestyle measures to alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, and surgery for obstruction or complications.

head: Lopinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic hepatitis C virus infection (a disease)
head_desc: Lopinavir is an HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral, commonly coformulated with ritonavir to boost exposure via CYP3A inhibition. It inhibits the viral HIV-1 protease, preventing cleavage of gag-pol polyproteins and producing immature, noninfectious virions. It is approved, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it also has important CYP3A-mediated drug interaction contraindications.
tail_desc: Chronic hepatitis C virus infection is a chronic viral liver disease caused by persistent infection with hepatitis C virus, producing ongoing hepatic inflammation that can progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. Many patients are asymptomatic or have fatigue and abnormal liver enzymes; it occurs in infected adults and children. Management centers on antiviral therapy with direct-acting agents, plus fibrosis assessment and liver-related monitoring.

head: Doxazosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Doxazosin is a quinazoline-derived selective alpha1-adrenergic receptor antagonist that relaxes vascular and prostatic smooth muscle by blocking postsynaptic alpha1 receptors. It is used to treat hypertension and lower urinary tract symptoms associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia. A notable adverse effect is first-dose orthostatic hypotension, which may cause dizziness or syncope.
tail_desc: Pyloric stenosis is a gastrointestinal obstructive disorder, usually infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, caused by hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the pyloric muscle that narrows the gastric outlet. It typically presents in young infants with progressively forceful non-bilious vomiting, dehydration, weight loss, and sometimes a palpable “olive” in the epigastrium. Management is correction of fluid and electrolyte abnormalities followed by pyloromyotomy.

head: Ifosfamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ifosfamide is an oxazaphosphorine alkylating agent, structurally related to cyclophosphamide. It is a prodrug activated by hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes to metabolites that form DNA interstrand and intrastrand cross-links, impairing replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used for sarcomas, testicular cancer, and some lymphomas; notable toxicities include hemorrhagic cystitis and encephalopathy.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Mirtazapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mirtazapine is a tetracyclic antidepressant of the piperazino-azepine class used primarily for major depressive disorder. It acts mainly by antagonizing central presynaptic alpha-2 adrenergic autoreceptors and heteroreceptors, increasing norepinephrine and serotonin release, and also blocks 5-HT2, 5-HT3, and histamine H1 receptors. Notable adverse effects include sedation and weight gain; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Drug-induced akathisia is a medication-induced movement disorder of the central nervous system, most often caused by dopamine-blocking antipsychotics and also some antiemetics or antidepressants. It is characterized by subjective inner restlessness with repetitive movements such as pacing, shifting, or inability to sit still, especially in exposed psychiatric patients. Management typically involves reducing or stopping the offending drug and using symptomatic treatment.

head: Butacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic in the benzoate family. Like other local anesthetics, it acts by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used topically, particularly in ophthalmic anesthesia. As with related ester anesthetics, hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known ester local anesthetic allergy.
tail_desc: Familial visceral myopathy is a rare inherited smooth-muscle disorder of the gastrointestinal and often urinary tract, causing impaired visceral motility. It reflects primary degeneration or dysfunction of the muscularis propria rather than mechanical obstruction. Patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction, abdominal distension, pain, severe constipation or vomiting, and sometimes bladder involvement; management is mainly supportive, with nutritional support, decompression, and selective surgery.

head: Estramustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Estramustine is an antineoplastic estradiol–nitrogen mustard conjugate used mainly for palliative treatment of metastatic or progressive prostate cancer. It binds microtubule-associated proteins and tubulin, disrupting microtubule assembly and mitosis, with additional estrogenic effects. A notable adverse effect is thromboembolism; it is generally contraindicated in patients with active thrombophlebitis or thromboembolic disorders.
tail_desc: Premature ejaculation is a male sexual dysfunction of the genitourinary and neurobehavioral domains, characterized by ejaculation that occurs sooner than desired, often within about 1 minute of penetration in lifelong cases, with poor perceived control and associated distress. Its cause is multifactorial, involving psychologic, neurobiologic, and relational factors. Management typically includes education, behavioral and psychosexual therapy, and selected pharmacologic treatment.

head: Clonazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine anticonvulsant and anxiolytic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is used for seizure disorders, including absence and myoclonic seizures, and for panic disorder. A notable adverse effect is sedation; it is contraindicated in significant hepatic impairment and acute narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Oxandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxandrolone is an orally active synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid, a 17α-alkylated testosterone derivative. It acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein anabolism and weight gain. Established uses include promoting weight gain after extensive surgery, chronic infection, severe trauma, or unexplained weight loss, and treating bone pain in osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Ozanimod (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ozanimod is an oral sphingosine 1-phosphate (S1P) receptor modulator that selectively targets S1P1 and S1P5, reducing lymphocyte egress from lymph nodes and thereby limiting inflammatory cell trafficking. It is approved for relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis and moderately to severely active ulcerative colitis. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia, particularly after treatment initiation.
tail_desc: Congenital central hypoventilation syndrome is a rare genetic disorder of autonomic control of breathing, primarily affecting the respiratory and nervous systems, usually caused by PHOX2B mutations. It presents in neonates or infants with inadequate ventilatory response to hypercapnia and hypoxemia, especially during sleep, causing hypoventilation and apnea. Management centers on lifelong ventilatory support, monitoring, and care for associated autonomic dysfunction.

head: Nandrolone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nandrolone is a synthetic anabolic-androgenic steroid and testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, promoting protein synthesis and erythropoiesis. Its ester forms, such as nandrolone decanoate, have been used for anemia associated with chronic kidney disease and some wasting states. Notable adverse effects include virilization and other androgenic effects; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in prostate or male breast carcinoma.
tail_desc: Coronary artery disease is a cardiovascular disorder in which atherosclerotic plaque narrows or blocks the coronary arteries, reducing blood flow to the heart muscle. It commonly affects middle-aged and older adults, especially those with smoking, diabetes, hypertension, or hyperlipidemia, and may cause angina, dyspnea, or myocardial infarction. Management includes risk-factor reduction, antiplatelet and lipid-lowering therapy, and revascularization when indicated.

head: Oxaliplatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxaliplatin is a third-generation platinum-based antineoplastic (diaminocyclohexane platinum) that forms DNA intra- and interstrand crosslinks, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and triggering apoptosis. It is used mainly in colorectal cancer, including adjuvant treatment and metastatic disease, commonly in FOLFOX regimens. A notable adverse effect is cumulative peripheral sensory neuropathy, often precipitated or worsened by cold exposure.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Silver sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Silver sulfadiazine is a topical sulfonamide antimicrobial combining sulfadiazine with silver ions. It acts mainly by releasing silver, which disrupts bacterial cell membranes and proteins, while sulfadiazine inhibits folate synthesis via dihydropteroate synthase. It is established for prevention and treatment of wound sepsis in second- and third-degree burns. A notable contraindication is use in patients with sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Galactosemia is an inherited metabolic disorder affecting carbohydrate metabolism, usually caused by deficiency of enzymes needed to process galactose, classically galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase. It typically presents in newborns after milk feeding with poor feeding, vomiting, jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, cataracts, and risk of sepsis. Management is prompt lifelong dietary restriction of galactose/lactose and monitoring for complications.

head: Meclofenamic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meclofenamic acid is a fenamate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the anthranilic acid class. It nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It has been used to treat mild to moderate pain, primary dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in aspirin-sensitive NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Atorvastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atorvastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is indicated for hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, and prevention of cardiovascular events. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and during pregnancy.
tail_desc: Muscular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting skeletal muscle and the neuromuscular system, caused by diverse mechanisms including inherited structural protein defects, inflammation, metabolic abnormalities, or toxic injury. Typical features include progressive weakness, fatigue, cramps, myalgia, and functional impairment across children or adults, depending on etiology. Management is cause-directed and supportive, including rehabilitation, exercise guidance, medications for specific subtypes, and monitoring for respiratory or cardiac complications.

head: Pitavastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pitavastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) used to treat primary hyperlipidemia and mixed dyslipidemia by competitively inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and increasing LDL clearance. It lowers LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and may raise HDL cholesterol. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Congenital hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid axis present at birth, most commonly caused by thyroid dysgenesis or defects in thyroid hormone synthesis, leading to deficient thyroxine. It affects newborns and may cause prolonged jaundice, lethargy, feeding difficulty, constipation, macroglossia, and impaired growth and neurodevelopment if untreated. Management is prompt newborn screening confirmation and early levothyroxine replacement with ongoing monitoring.

head: Cholestyramine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant, an anion-exchange resin that binds bile acids in the intestinal lumen, preventing their reabsorption via the enterohepatic circulation and thereby lowering LDL cholesterol. It is used for hypercholesterolemia and pruritus due to partial biliary obstruction. Notable adverse effects include constipation; it is contraindicated in complete biliary obstruction.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Ipratropium (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ipratropium is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic bronchodilator, structurally related to atropine. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle, reducing vagally mediated bronchoconstriction and secretions. It is used as an inhaled treatment for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and also for bronchospasm in asthma. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Methadone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methadone is a synthetic diphenylheptane opioid analgesic that acts primarily as a full agonist at the mu-opioid receptor; it also has NMDA receptor antagonism and inhibits norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for severe chronic pain and for maintenance or detoxification in opioid use disorder. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation, which can predispose to torsades de pointes.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Sertraline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sertraline is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant of the naphthylamine/tetralin class that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, panic disorder, post-traumatic stress disorder, social anxiety disorder, and premenstrual dysphoric disorder. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with monoamine oxidase inhibitors, which are contraindicated.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Latanoprost (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Latanoprost is a prostaglandin F2α analog used as an ophthalmic antiglaucoma agent. It is a prodrug that activates the prostanoid FP receptor in the eye, increasing uveoscleral outflow of aqueous humor and thereby lowering intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is gradual, often permanent, increased brown pigmentation of the iris.
tail_desc: Uveitis is an inflammatory disorder of the uveal tract of the eye, often involving adjacent ocular structures. It may result from autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or be idiopathic. Typical features include eye pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision, and floaters, affecting both adults and children. Management depends on cause and severity, commonly using corticosteroids, cycloplegics, and treatment of any underlying infectious or systemic condition.

head: Aminosalicylic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminosalicylic acid, usually referring to para-aminosalicylic acid (PAS), is an aminosalicylate antibacterial and second-line antitubercular agent. It acts as a structural analog of para-aminobenzoic acid, inhibiting folate synthesis in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, primarily through dihydropteroate synthase. It is used in multidrug-resistant tuberculosis regimens. Notable adverse effects include significant gastrointestinal intolerance and hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Acquired lactic acidosis is a metabolic acid-base disorder affecting the whole body, marked by excess lactate accumulation and decreased blood pH due to tissue hypoxia or impaired lactate clearance, often in sepsis, shock, severe hypoxemia, liver failure, or drug/toxin exposure. Patients typically present acutely with tachypnea, nausea, weakness, confusion, and hemodynamic instability; management focuses on rapid treatment of the underlying cause and supportive critical care.

head: Coenzyme M (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Coenzyme M is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Hereditary hyperbilirubinemia comprises inherited disorders of bilirubin metabolism affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by defects in hepatic bilirubin conjugation, uptake, or excretion. It typically presents from infancy to adulthood with persistent or intermittent jaundice, usually without major liver dysfunction, though severity varies by subtype. Management is mainly supportive and subtype-directed, including diagnosis, monitoring, avoidance of triggers, and rarely specific therapy.

head: Methsuximide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methsuximide is a succinimide anticonvulsant used mainly for absence (petit mal) seizures, particularly when patients do not respond adequately to ethosuximide. Its antiepileptic effect is attributed to reduction of thalamic T-type calcium currents, likely mediated largely by its active metabolite N-desmethylmethsuximide. Notable adverse effects include drowsiness, and it should be used cautiously in patients with significant hepatic or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Telavancin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telavancin is a semisynthetic lipoglycopeptide antibiotic, a vancomycin derivative, that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding the D-Ala-D-Ala termini of peptidoglycan precursors and also disrupts bacterial membrane potential and permeability. It is approved for complicated skin and skin structure infections and hospital-acquired/ventilator-associated bacterial pneumonia due to susceptible Gram-positive pathogens. Notable risks include nephrotoxicity and fetal harm; avoid use in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Torsades de pointes is a polymorphic ventricular tachycardia of the cardiac conduction system associated with prolonged QT interval. It usually results from delayed ventricular repolarization, congenital or acquired, including drug effects, electrolyte disturbances, or bradycardia. Patients may have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or sudden cardiac arrest. Management focuses on removing triggers, correcting electrolytes, intravenous magnesium, and urgent rhythm stabilization if unstable.

head: Minoxidil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Minoxidil is a direct-acting arteriolar vasodilator of the pyrimidine derivative class, used orally for severe, refractory hypertension and topically for androgenetic alopecia. It is converted to minoxidil sulfate, which opens ATP-sensitive potassium channels in vascular smooth muscle, reducing peripheral resistance; the precise hair-growth mechanism is incompletely defined. Notable adverse effects include hypertrichosis and fluid retention.
tail_desc: Heart cancer usually refers to primary cardiac tumors, which are very rare and may be benign or malignant, or more commonly to metastatic spread to the heart from other cancers, affecting the cardiovascular system. Malignant cardiac tumors such as sarcomas can cause obstruction, arrhythmias, heart failure, embolic events, or pericardial effusion. Management depends on tumor type and extent, using surgical resection when feasible, plus oncology-directed systemic therapy or palliation.

head: Chlorambucil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chlorambucil is a nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the bifunctional alkylating antineoplastic class. It forms covalent DNA cross-links, primarily at the N7 position of guanine, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and triggering cell death. It is used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia and certain lymphomas. A notable adverse effect is dose-related myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Leukopenia is a hematologic abnormality of the blood and immune system defined by a reduced white blood cell count, commonly due to decreased bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or marrow disorders. It may be asymptomatic or present with recurrent infections and fever, affecting patients of any age. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, monitoring counts, and preventing or treating infection.

head: Oxybutynin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) tertiary amine used to treat overactive bladder symptoms, including urinary urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence, and some cases of neurogenic detrusor overactivity. It acts mainly by antagonizing muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, particularly M3 receptors in bladder smooth muscle, reducing detrusor contractions. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Familial visceral myopathy is a rare inherited smooth-muscle disorder of the gastrointestinal and often urinary tract, causing impaired visceral motility. It reflects primary degeneration or dysfunction of the muscularis propria rather than mechanical obstruction. Patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop chronic intestinal pseudo-obstruction, abdominal distension, pain, severe constipation or vomiting, and sometimes bladder involvement; management is mainly supportive, with nutritional support, decompression, and selective surgery.

head: Eptifibatide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Eptifibatide is an intravenous cyclic heptapeptide glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor that reversibly blocks the platelet integrin αIIbβ3 receptor, preventing fibrinogen-mediated platelet aggregation. It is used in acute coronary syndromes and during percutaneous coronary intervention to reduce ischemic complications. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it is contraindicated in active internal bleeding or severe thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Melphalan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to G6PC3 deficiency (a disease)
head_desc: Melphalan is a bifunctional nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the phenylalanine derivative class that covalently crosslinks DNA, mainly at guanine bases, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and causing cell death. It is used chiefly in multiple myeloma and ovarian carcinoma conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity or marked bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Autosomal recessive severe congenital neutropenia due to G6PC3 deficiency is a hereditary hematologic immunodeficiency caused by biallelic G6PC3 mutations, leading to impaired neutrophil production and survival. It presents from infancy with persistent severe neutropenia, recurrent bacterial infections, and may include congenital anomalies such as cardiac or urogenital defects and prominent superficial veins. Management centers on granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, prompt antibiotic treatment, and multidisciplinary supportive care.

head: Antipyrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Antipyrine (phenazone) is a pyrazolone derivative and non-opioid analgesic/antipyretic. Its effects are attributed to inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis via cyclooxygenase pathways, although it is not used for a highly selective molecular target. It has been used for pain and fever and, in otic combinations, for symptomatic relief of ear pain. Hypersensitivity reactions, including rash, can occur.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Deserpidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Deserpidine is an antihypertensive indole alkaloid of the Rauwolfia (reserpine-like) family. It irreversibly inhibits vesicular monoamine transporters (primarily VMAT2), depleting norepinephrine, dopamine, and serotonin from sympathetic nerve terminals and the CNS. It has been used for hypertension, historically often in combination products. A notable adverse effect is depression; it is contraindicated in patients with a history of depressive disorders.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Dicloxacillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dicloxacillin is an isoxazolyl, penicillinase-resistant beta-lactam penicillin antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing cell lysis. It is used for infections caused by susceptible penicillinase-producing staphylococci, including skin and soft tissue infections. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Infectious mononucleosis is an acute viral infection of the lymphoreticular system, most commonly caused by Epstein–Barr virus. It typically affects adolescents and young adults and presents with fever, pharyngitis, cervical lymphadenopathy, fatigue, and sometimes hepatosplenomegaly. Management is mainly supportive, including rest, hydration, and analgesics, with avoidance of contact sports when splenomegaly is present.

head: Clobetasol propionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clobetasol propionate is a super-high-potency topical corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune mediators and reduce vasodilation and pruritus. It is used short term for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses such as plaque psoriasis and eczema. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy, and prolonged use may cause systemic hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression.
tail_desc: Adrenocortical insufficiency is an endocrine disorder of the adrenal cortex characterized by deficient glucocorticoid production, often with mineralocorticoid deficiency in primary disease, due to autoimmune destruction, infection, hemorrhage, or impaired pituitary ACTH stimulation. Patients may develop fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hyperpigmentation in primary forms. Management centers on lifelong glucocorticoid replacement, mineralocorticoid replacement when needed, and stress-dose therapy during illness.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Disease of the orbital region is a broad category of disorders affecting the eye socket and adjacent ocular adnexal tissues. Causes include congenital, inflammatory, infectious, vascular, traumatic, and neoplastic processes involving orbital soft tissues, bone, nerves, or extraocular muscles. Typical features are proptosis, pain, diplopia, swelling, and visual impairment in children or adults. Management depends on cause and may require urgent ophthalmologic evaluation, imaging, medical therapy, surgery, or oncologic treatment.

head: Sodium fluoride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium fluoride is an inorganic fluoride salt used as an anticaries agent and fluoride supplement. It acts topically and systemically by delivering fluoride ions that promote fluorapatite formation in tooth enamel and inhibit bacterial acid production. Established uses include prevention of dental caries in toothpaste, rinses, and supplements. Notable adverse effects include dental fluorosis with excessive exposure, especially during tooth development.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Clonazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clonazepam is a benzodiazepine anticonvulsant and anxiolytic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is used for seizure disorders, including absence and myoclonic seizures, and for panic disorder. A notable adverse effect is sedation; it is contraindicated in significant hepatic impairment and acute narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Carbenicillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Carbenicillin is a semisynthetic carboxypenicillin, a beta-lactam antibiotic active particularly against gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits the transpeptidation step of bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan synthesis, leading to cell lysis. It has been used for susceptible urinary tract and systemic infections. Notable adverse effects include bleeding due to impaired platelet function, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Diazoxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diazoxide is a benzothiadiazine derivative and potassium channel opener that activates ATP-sensitive K+ channels, notably SUR1/Kir6.2 in pancreatic beta cells and vascular smooth muscle, suppressing insulin release and causing vasodilation. It is used for hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia and, historically, severe hypertension. Notable adverse effects include fluid retention and hyperglycemia; it is generally avoided in functional hypoglycemia.
tail_desc: Congestive heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment reduces the heart’s ability to pump or fill effectively, causing pulmonary and/or systemic fluid congestion. It commonly affects older adults and patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy, producing dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance. Management focuses on treating the cause, diuresis, guideline-directed heart-failure medications, sodium/fluid control, and selected device or advanced therapies.

head: Metronidazole (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole antimicrobial and antiprotozoal prodrug that is reduced in anaerobic bacteria and certain protozoa to reactive radicals that damage DNA. It is used for anaerobic bacterial infections, bacterial vaginosis, Clostridioides difficile infection, and protozoal diseases such as trichomoniasis, giardiasis, and amebiasis. A notable adverse effect is a disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol.
tail_desc: Dermatitis is a common inflammatory disorder of the skin, often caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, irritant or allergic exposures, or immune dysregulation. It typically presents with pruritus, erythema, scaling, and sometimes vesicles or lichenification, affecting both children and adults depending on subtype. Management generally focuses on trigger avoidance, skin hydration with emollients, and anti-inflammatory topical therapies such as corticosteroids.

head: Telavancin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telavancin is a semisynthetic lipoglycopeptide antibiotic, a vancomycin derivative, that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding the D-Ala-D-Ala termini of peptidoglycan precursors and also disrupts bacterial membrane potential and permeability. It is approved for complicated skin and skin structure infections and hospital-acquired/ventilator-associated bacterial pneumonia due to susceptible Gram-positive pathogens. Notable risks include nephrotoxicity and fetal harm; avoid use in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Dolasetron (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dolasetron is a serotonin 5-HT3 receptor antagonist antiemetic, administered as a prodrug rapidly converted to hydrodolasetron. By blocking 5-HT3 receptors on vagal afferents and centrally in the chemoreceptor trigger zone, it prevents nausea and vomiting, particularly chemotherapy-induced and postoperative emesis. A notable safety concern is dose-related QT interval prolongation, which can precipitate serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Atrioventricular dissociation is a cardiac conduction/rhythm disorder in which atrial and ventricular activation occur independently because the sinus node no longer controls ventricular depolarization, often due to slowing of the sinus rate, accelerated junctional or ventricular rhythms, or complete AV block. Patients may be asymptomatic or have palpitations, dizziness, syncope, or bradycardia-related symptoms; management targets the underlying cause, with urgent treatment or pacing when hemodynamic compromise or high-grade block is present.

head: Stavudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Stavudine is a thymidine analogue nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it competitively inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and causes viral DNA chain termination. It has been used, in combination therapy, for treatment of HIV infection. A notable serious adverse effect is dose-related peripheral neuropathy; pancreatitis and lactic acidosis can also occur.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Zinc chloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zinc chloride is an inorganic zinc salt and topical caustic/escharotic agent. Its pharmacologic effects are nonselective: it denatures and precipitates proteins, causing chemical tissue destruction, rather than acting at a specific molecular target. It has established use in topical preparations, including historical dermatologic/escharotic applications and zinc replacement in some compounding contexts. A notable adverse effect is severe local irritation and corrosive burns; it is contraindicated in ocular exposure.
tail_desc: Methemoglobinemia is a hematologic oxygen-transport disorder in which hemoglobin iron is oxidized to ferric (Fe3+) form, reducing oxygen delivery to tissues. It may be congenital or acquired, often from oxidant drugs or chemicals. Patients develop cyanosis, chocolate-brown blood, dyspnea, headache, fatigue, and severe cases can cause neurologic or cardiovascular compromise. Management involves removing the trigger, supportive oxygen, and methylene blue when indicated.

head: Triptorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triptorelin is a synthetic decapeptide gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that binds pituitary GnRH receptors; continuous administration suppresses LH and FSH release, lowering sex steroid production. It is used for prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial hormone “flare” that can transiently worsen symptoms.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Torasemide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Torasemide (torsemide) is a pyridine-sulfonylurea loop diuretic that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter NKCC2 (SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, increasing renal sodium, chloride, and water excretion. It is used for edema associated with heart failure, renal disease, or hepatic cirrhosis, and for hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Felodipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Felodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type voltage-gated calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation and reduced blood pressure. It is established for the treatment of hypertension and, in some settings, chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; it is generally contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Calcium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcium acetate is a calcium salt used as a phosphate binder.
