In the gastrointestinal tract it dissociates to release calcium, which binds dietary phosphate to form insoluble complexes and lowers phosphate absorption; it has no specific receptor target. It is established for control of hyperphosphatemia in patients with end-stage renal disease, and a notable risk is hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Amiloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amiloride is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pyrazinecarbonylguanidine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal nephron, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for hypertension and edema, often with other diuretics, and in Liddle syndrome. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in renal impairment or with potassium supplementation.
tail_desc: Osteitis fibrosa is a metabolic bone disorder of the skeleton, usually caused by hyperparathyroidism with excess parathyroid hormone driving increased osteoclastic bone resorption and replacement by fibrous tissue. It can cause bone pain, fragility fractures, deformity, and “brown tumors,” classically in patients with severe primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism. Management targets the underlying hyperparathyroidism and correction of calcium-phosphate abnormalities.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Racepinephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Racepinephrine is a racemic form of epinephrine, a sympathomimetic catecholamine acting as a nonselective agonist at alpha- and beta-adrenergic receptors. By causing mucosal vasoconstriction and bronchodilation, it is used as an inhaled agent for temporary relief of mild intermittent asthma symptoms and to reduce airway edema, including in croup. Notable adverse effects include tachycardia and other cardiac arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Peripheral vascular disease is a circulatory disorder of the peripheral blood vessels, most often involving atherosclerotic narrowing or occlusion of arteries supplying the legs. It commonly affects older adults and people who smoke or have diabetes, causing intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool extremities, poor wound healing, and sometimes critical limb ischemia. Management emphasizes risk-factor reduction, exercise, antiplatelet/statin therapy, and revascularization when severe.

head: Amiloride (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Amiloride is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pyrazinecarbonylguanidine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal nephron, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for hypertension and edema, often with other diuretics, and in Liddle syndrome. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in renal impairment or with potassium supplementation.
tail_desc: Diabetic nephropathy is a chronic microvascular complication of diabetes affecting the kidneys, caused by prolonged hyperglycemia leading to glomerular basement membrane thickening, mesangial expansion, and progressive glomerulosclerosis. It typically occurs in patients with longstanding type 1 or type 2 diabetes and presents with persistent albuminuria, hypertension, and declining renal function. Management focuses on tight glycemic and blood pressure control, renin-angiotensin system blockade, and slowing progression to chronic kidney disease.

head: Methacholine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methacholine is a synthetic choline ester and direct-acting muscarinic cholinergic agonist that primarily stimulates muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing bronchial smooth-muscle constriction. It is used diagnostically in methacholine challenge testing to assess airway hyperresponsiveness in suspected asthma. A notable adverse effect is bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in patients with severe airflow limitation or uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Urinary tract obstruction is a genitourinary disorder in which partial or complete blockage of urine flow occurs anywhere from the renal pelvis to the urethra, causing increased upstream pressure, hydronephrosis, infection risk, and potential renal impairment. Symptoms may include flank or abdominal pain, lower urinary tract symptoms, anuria, hematuria, or recurrent urinary infection. Management focuses on urgent decompression when indicated and treatment of the underlying cause.

head: Sodium acetate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Sodium acetate is an alkalinizing electrolyte salt and acetate donor used in parenteral nutrition and intravenous fluid therapy. After metabolism, acetate is converted primarily in muscle and other tissues to bicarbonate, helping correct metabolic acidosis; it has no single receptor target. Established uses include correction of hyponatremia and acidosis in compounded IV solutions. Excess administration can cause metabolic alkalosis.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Ethotoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethotoin is a hydantoin anticonvulsant used to treat seizure disorders, particularly tonic-clonic and complex partial seizures. Like other hydantoins, it is thought to stabilize neuronal membranes by limiting voltage-gated sodium channel–mediated repetitive firing, though its mechanism is not fully defined. A notable adverse effect is blood dyscrasia, and it is contraindicated in patients with hepatic dysfunction or hematologic abnormalities.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Aurothioglucose (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aurothioglucose is a gold(I) thiolate compound formerly used as a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug for rheumatoid arthritis. Its precise mechanism is incompletely defined, but gold salts modulate immune and inflammatory cell function rather than acting on a single established molecular target. Important adverse effects include dermatitis, stomatitis, proteinuria, and bone marrow suppression; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Necrotizing enterocolitis is a life-threatening inflammatory necrosis of the intestines, primarily affecting the gastrointestinal system of premature or low-birth-weight neonates. It is associated with intestinal immaturity, impaired mucosal barrier function, ischemia, and bacterial invasion. Typical features include feeding intolerance, abdominal distension, bloody stools, and systemic instability. Management involves bowel rest, gastric decompression, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive intensive care, and surgery for perforation or necrotic bowel.

head: Tolterodine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tolterodine is an antimuscarinic urinary antispasmodic, a tertiary amine used for overactive bladder with urinary urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence. It acts as a competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, functionally reducing detrusor muscle contractions in the bladder. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth; it is contraindicated in urinary retention and gastric retention.
tail_desc: Long QT syndrome is a cardiac electrical disorder affecting ventricular repolarization, characterized by prolongation of the QT interval on electrocardiography, usually due to inherited ion-channel defects or acquired drug/electrolyte effects. It predisposes children or adults to syncope, seizures, and polymorphic ventricular tachycardia including torsades de pointes, with risk of sudden death; management emphasizes trigger avoidance, beta-blockers, correction of reversible causes, and sometimes implantable defibrillators.

head: Mirtazapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mirtazapine is a tetracyclic antidepressant of the piperazino-azepine class used primarily for major depressive disorder. It acts mainly by antagonizing central presynaptic alpha-2 adrenergic autoreceptors and heteroreceptors, increasing norepinephrine and serotonin release, and also blocks 5-HT2, 5-HT3, and histamine H1 receptors. Notable adverse effects include sedation and weight gain; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Multiple system atrophy is a rare, adult-onset progressive neurodegenerative synucleinopathy affecting the autonomic nervous system, basal ganglia, and cerebellum. It is characterized pathologically by alpha-synuclein–positive glial cytoplasmic inclusions. Patients develop autonomic failure with parkinsonism and/or cerebellar ataxia, often with pyramidal features. Management is supportive, focusing on symptom control, autonomic dysfunction, mobility, and multidisciplinary care.

head: Diazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Diazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine that positively modulates GABA-A receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is used for anxiety disorders, acute alcohol withdrawal, muscle spasm, seizure control including status epilepticus, and procedural sedation. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation with respiratory depression, especially when combined with opioids or other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the upper airway, characterized by recurrent pharyngeal collapse during sleep causing intermittent airflow obstruction, hypoxemia, and sleep fragmentation. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity, and presents with loud snoring, witnessed apneas, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Management typically includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and selected upper-airway surgery.

head: Moxifloxacin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Moxifloxacin is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including community-acquired pneumonia, acute bacterial sinusitis, acute exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and some skin or intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation; fluoroquinolones can also cause tendon rupture.
tail_desc: Peripheral neuropathy is a disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused by damage to peripheral nerves, commonly from diabetes, toxins, nutritional deficiencies, infections, or immune-mediated disease. It typically presents with distal numbness, tingling, burning pain, weakness, and reduced reflexes, often in a length-dependent “stocking-glove” pattern, especially in adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the cause, symptom control, and rehabilitation or foot care when needed.

head: Heparin, bovine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin, bovine, is an animal-derived unfractionated heparin, a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant obtained from bovine tissues. It potentiates antithrombin III, accelerating inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism and other settings requiring anticoagulation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis obliterans is a chronic occlusive peripheral arterial disease, usually affecting lower-extremity arteries, caused by progressive atherosclerotic plaque formation with luminal narrowing and impaired blood flow. It typically occurs in older adults, especially smokers and patients with diabetes, presenting with intermittent claudication, diminished pulses, cool limbs, rest pain, or ischemic ulcers. Management focuses on risk-factor control, antiplatelet therapy, exercise, and revascularization when severe.

head: Triazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic that positively modulates GABA\(_A\) receptors by binding the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include anterograde amnesia and next-day psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inhibitors.
tail_desc: Psychotic disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting brain function and behavior, defined by impaired reality testing due to diverse causes, including primary psychotic illnesses, mood disorders, substance use, or medical conditions. Core features include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking or behavior, and reduced insight, usually emerging in adolescents or adults. Management focuses on diagnostic evaluation, treatment of underlying causes, antipsychotic medication, psychotherapy, and safety/supportive care.

head: Irinotecan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Irinotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. Its active metabolite SN-38 stabilizes the topoisomerase I-DNA complex, blocking DNA religation and causing lethal DNA damage during replication. It is used for metastatic colorectal cancer and in some regimens for other solid tumors. Notable toxicities include severe delayed diarrhea and myelosuppression, especially neutropenia.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Phentermine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Phentermine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, structurally related to amphetamine, used as a short-term adjunct to diet and exercise for exogenous obesity. It promotes weight loss primarily by increasing central norepinephrine release, with lesser effects on dopamine and serotonin, reducing appetite via hypothalamic pathways. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and heart rate, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or within 14 days of MAOI use.
tail_desc: Mental disorder is a broad category of conditions affecting the brain and mental functioning, characterized by clinically significant disturbances in cognition, emotion regulation, or behavior that impair daily functioning. Causes are multifactorial, involving genetic, neurobiological, developmental, psychological, and environmental factors. It can occur across the lifespan, with symptoms varying by syndrome. Management typically includes psychiatric assessment, psychotherapy, social support, and, when indicated, pharmacologic treatment.

head: Carmustine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Carmustine is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms DNA interstrand cross-links and carbamoylates proteins, disrupting DNA replication and RNA/protein synthesis without a single specific molecular target. It is used for brain tumors and for multiple myeloma, lymphoma, and other hematologic malignancies, including in conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is delayed, cumulative bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Ibandronate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ibandronate is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds bone hydroxyapatite and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclasts, reducing bone resorption. It is approved for prevention and treatment of postmenopausal osteoporosis. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis with oral formulations; it is contraindicated in patients with esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying or inability to remain upright.
tail_desc: Eosinophilic esophagitis is a chronic immune-mediated inflammatory disease of the esophagus characterized by eosinophil-predominant infiltration, often triggered by food allergens. It commonly affects children and young adults and causes dysphagia, food impaction, feeding difficulty, and reflux-like symptoms. Management typically includes dietary elimination, proton pump inhibitors, swallowed topical corticosteroids, and endoscopic dilation for fibrostenotic narrowing.

head: Aminoglutethimide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Aminoglutethimide is a nonsteroidal aromatase inhibitor and adrenal steroidogenesis inhibitor. It blocks conversion of cholesterol to pregnenolone and inhibits aromatase (CYP19), reducing synthesis of glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, estrogens, and androgens. It has been used for Cushing syndrome and advanced breast cancer. Notable adverse effects include sedation and rash; adrenal insufficiency is an important risk.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: Chronic renal failure syndrome is a chronic kidney disease of the renal system characterized by progressive, irreversible loss of nephron function, leading to reduced glomerular filtration and accumulation of metabolic waste, fluid, and electrolyte disturbances. It commonly affects adults, especially those with diabetes or hypertension, and presents with fatigue, edema, hypertension, anemia, and uremic symptoms. Management focuses on treating the cause, slowing progression, correcting complications, and renal replacement therapy when advanced.

head: Modafinil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Modafinil is a wakefulness-promoting agent of the diphenylmethyl-sulfinyl-acetamide class. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it inhibits the dopamine transporter (DAT), increasing extracellular dopamine. It is approved to improve wakefulness in narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift work disorder. Notable adverse effects include headache and insomnia; serious rash, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, is a rare contraindication-related concern.
tail_desc: Congenital mitral valve insufficiency is a rare congenital heart valve disorder of the cardiovascular system in which developmental malformation of the mitral valve apparatus causes mitral regurgitation. It typically presents in infants or children with a heart murmur, poor feeding, failure to thrive, dyspnea, or signs of heart failure. Management depends on severity, using medical treatment for symptoms and surgical valve repair when significant regurgitation is present.

head: Methsuximide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methsuximide is a succinimide anticonvulsant used mainly for absence (petit mal) seizures, particularly when patients do not respond adequately to ethosuximide. Its antiepileptic effect is attributed to reduction of thalamic T-type calcium currents, likely mediated largely by its active metabolite N-desmethylmethsuximide. Notable adverse effects include drowsiness, and it should be used cautiously in patients with significant hepatic or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a chronic multisystem autoimmune connective tissue disease in which loss of immune tolerance leads to autoantibody production and immune-complex–mediated inflammation affecting skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, serosa, and the nervous system. It commonly affects women of childbearing age and causes fatigue, rash, photosensitivity, arthritis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities; management uses sun protection, hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressive or biologic therapies tailored to organ involvement.

head: Noscapine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Noscapine is a phthalideisoquinoline alkaloid derived from opium poppy and used as a centrally acting antitussive. Unlike opioid analgesics, it has little narcotic activity; its cough-suppressant effect is attributed to action on the medullary cough center, and it has also been shown to bind tubulin and alter microtubule dynamics. Adverse effects can include drowsiness, and concomitant use with warfarin is a notable precaution due to bleeding risk.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Mercaptopurine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mercaptopurine is a thiopurine antimetabolite and purine analog used mainly in acute lymphoblastic leukemia and sometimes in inflammatory bowel disease. It is converted intracellularly to active thioguanine nucleotides that inhibit de novo purine synthesis and are incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing cell proliferation. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, with markedly increased toxicity in patients with TPMT or NUDT15 deficiency.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Guanfacine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Guanfacine is a centrally acting antihypertensive and selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow from the brain. It is approved for hypertension and, in extended-release form, for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Common notable adverse effects include sedation, somnolence, hypotension, and bradycardia; abrupt discontinuation may cause rebound hypertension.
tail_desc: End-stage renal failure is the terminal stage of chronic kidney disease in the renal system, marked by irreversible loss of kidney function and severely reduced glomerular filtration. It usually results from progressive disorders such as diabetes, hypertension, or glomerular disease. Patients develop uremic symptoms, fluid and electrolyte disturbances, and anemia; management centers on dialysis, kidney transplantation, and treatment of complications.

head: Naphazoline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Naphazoline is an imidazoline-derived sympathomimetic decongestant that acts primarily as an alpha-adrenergic receptor agonist, causing vasoconstriction of conjunctival and nasal blood vessels. It is used topically to relieve nasal congestion and ocular redness. A notable adverse effect is rebound congestion or hyperemia with prolonged use; it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Lamivudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lamivudine is a synthetic nucleoside analogue reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI), a cytidine analogue, that is phosphorylated intracellularly and inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus DNA polymerase, causing chain termination. It is used to treat HIV infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable risk is severe lactic acidosis with hepatomegaly and steatosis; abrupt discontinuation in hepatitis B can trigger severe exacerbations.
tail_desc: Dermatomyositis is an idiopathic inflammatory myopathy affecting skeletal muscle and skin, characterized by immune-mediated muscle fiber injury and distinctive cutaneous inflammation. It causes symmetric proximal muscle weakness with elevated muscle enzymes and rashes such as heliotrope discoloration and Gottron papules, occurring in both adults and children. Management typically involves systemic corticosteroids, steroid-sparing immunosuppressants, physical therapy, and evaluation for associated interstitial lung disease or malignancy.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Hepatic encephalopathy is a neuropsychiatric complication of acute or chronic liver failure affecting the central nervous system. It results from impaired hepatic detoxification and portosystemic shunting, leading to accumulation of neurotoxins, especially ammonia. Patients, often with cirrhosis, develop confusion, altered consciousness, asterixis, and sometimes coma; management targets precipitating factors and lowers gut-derived ammonia, commonly with lactulose and rifaximin.

head: Alendronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alendronic acid (alendronate) is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive drug. It binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated resorption, primarily by blocking farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway. It is used for osteoporosis and Paget’s disease of bone. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis; it is contraindicated in esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying.
tail_desc: Duodenitis is inflammation of the duodenal mucosa in the upper gastrointestinal tract. It commonly results from Helicobacter pylori infection, excess gastric acid exposure, or irritants such as NSAIDs. Patients may have epigastric pain, nausea, dyspepsia, or gastrointestinal bleeding, though some are asymptomatic. Management targets the cause, typically with acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori, and avoidance of offending agents.

head: Salbutamol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salbutamol (albuterol) is a short-acting β2-adrenergic receptor agonist bronchodilator, chemically a phenethylamine derivative. By selectively stimulating β2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, it increases cyclic AMP and produces rapid bronchodilation. It is used for relief and prevention of bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, including exercise-induced bronchospasm. A notable adverse effect is tremor.
tail_desc: Metabolic acidosis is an acid-base disorder affecting systemic metabolism and respiratory compensation, defined by decreased serum bicarbonate and low blood pH due to excess acid production, impaired renal acid excretion, or bicarbonate loss. Patients may develop deep, rapid breathing, nausea, weakness, confusion, and arrhythmias; it occurs across all ages. Management focuses on urgent treatment of the underlying cause, fluid and electrolyte correction, and bicarbonate in selected severe cases.

head: Telbivudine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Telbivudine is a synthetic thymidine nucleoside analog antiviral active against hepatitis B virus. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HBV DNA polymerase by competing with thymidine substrates and causing DNA chain termination. It is used for chronic hepatitis B infection. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, and peripheral neuropathy risk increases with pegylated interferon alfa coadministration.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Eltrombopag (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Eltrombopag is an oral, small-molecule thrombopoietin receptor agonist that activates the c-Mpl receptor to stimulate megakaryocyte proliferation and platelet production. It is used for thrombocytopenia in chronic immune thrombocytopenia, severe aplastic anemia, and thrombocytopenia associated with chronic hepatitis C treatment. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, requiring liver function monitoring.
tail_desc: Anemia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by reduced red blood cell mass or hemoglobin, causing impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. It results from blood loss, decreased red cell production, or increased destruction. Common features include fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and tachycardia, affecting all ages. Management targets the cause and may include iron, vitamin replacement, erythropoietic therapy, or transfusion.

head: Alanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alanine is a nonessential, proteinogenic alpha-amino acid used in amino acid formulations for parenteral or enteral nutrition. It has no specific drug-like molecular target; rather, it serves as a metabolic substrate in protein synthesis and glucose-alanine nitrogen transport. Its established clinical use is nutritional supplementation within amino acid mixtures. Excessive administration may contribute to amino acid imbalance or metabolic disturbances, particularly in severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory granulomatous disease that most often affects the lungs and intrathoracic lymph nodes. It is characterized by noncaseating granuloma formation of unclear cause, likely involving immune dysregulation in genetically susceptible people. Common features include cough, dyspnea, fatigue, skin or eye involvement, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, typically in young to middle-aged adults; management ranges from observation to corticosteroids and other immunosuppressive therapy for significant organ disease.

head: Exenatide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Exenatide is a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, a synthetic incretin mimetic based on exendin-4. It activates the GLP-1 receptor to enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, slow gastric emptying, and reduce appetite. It is approved for type 2 diabetes mellitus, including extended-release formulations. Notable adverse effects include nausea and vomiting; it is contraindicated in patients with a history of severe hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Gastroparesis is a chronic gastrointestinal motility disorder in which delayed gastric emptying occurs without mechanical obstruction, usually due to impaired gastric neuromuscular function, often related to diabetes, postsurgical vagal injury, or idiopathic causes. Typical features include early satiety, postprandial fullness, nausea, vomiting, bloating, and weight loss. Management emphasizes dietary modification, treating underlying causes, antiemetics, and prokinetic therapy, with nutritional support or procedures for refractory cases.

head: Tramadol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tramadol is a centrally acting synthetic opioid analgesic of the aminocyclohexanol class. It provides pain relief through weak agonism at the mu-opioid receptor and inhibition of norepinephrine and serotonin reuptake. It is used for moderate to moderately severe pain. A notable risk is seizures, and it is contraindicated in significant respiratory depression or acute intoxication with alcohol or other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Paracetamol poisoning is an acute toxic liver injury caused by excessive acetaminophen intake, in which toxic metabolite formation depletes glutathione and leads to hepatocellular necrosis; severe cases may also affect the kidneys and cause metabolic derangement. Patients, often after intentional overdose or dosing errors, may initially have nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain, progressing to hepatic failure. Management centers on urgent assessment, serum paracetamol measurement, activated charcoal when appropriate, and prompt N-acetylcysteine.

head: Bupropion (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Bupropion is an aminoketone antidepressant and smoking-cessation agent that inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine, with minimal direct serotonergic activity; nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonism may also contribute. It is approved for major depressive disorder, seasonal affective disorder, and smoking cessation. A notable risk is seizures, so it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or eating disorders.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Heparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Heparin is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, thereby inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa and reducing fibrin clot formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndromes, and during procedures such as hemodialysis. A notable adverse effect is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Hydroxyamphetamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxyamphetamine is a sympathomimetic amine and indirect-acting adrenergic agonist, structurally related to amphetamine, that promotes release of norepinephrine from postganglionic sympathetic nerve terminals. Ophthalmically, it is used diagnostically to aid localization of Horner syndrome and can produce mydriasis. A notable adverse effect is precipitation of angle-closure glaucoma, so it is contraindicated in patients with narrow anterior chamber angles.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Dalteparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dalteparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin. It is used for prevention and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including perioperative and certain medical patients. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Hypertensive retinopathy is a retinal microvascular disorder of the eye caused by chronic or severe systemic hypertension, leading to arteriolar narrowing, vascular leakage, and ischemic injury. It may be asymptomatic or cause blurred vision; fundus findings include arteriovenous nicking, hemorrhages, cotton-wool spots, hard exudates, and sometimes papilledema in malignant hypertension. Management centers on prompt, sustained blood pressure control and treatment of associated cardiovascular risk factors.

head: Zafirlukast (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zafirlukast is an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist that selectively blocks cysteinyl leukotriene CysLT1 receptors, inhibiting the actions of LTC4, LTD4, and LTE4 in the airways. It is approved for prophylaxis and chronic treatment of asthma, including exercise-induced bronchoconstriction prevention. A notable safety concern is hepatotoxicity, and it is not indicated for relief of acute asthma attacks.
tail_desc: Cirrhosis of the liver is a chronic end-stage liver disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by diffuse fibrosis and regenerative nodules that distort hepatic architecture, most often from chronic alcohol use, viral hepatitis, or nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. Patients may be asymptomatic initially, then develop jaundice, ascites, portal hypertension, variceal bleeding, and encephalopathy. Management targets the cause, complications, nutrition, and possible liver transplantation.

head: Rosuvastatin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rosuvastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) used to lower LDL cholesterol and triglycerides and to reduce cardiovascular risk. It competitively inhibits hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, decreasing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors to enhance LDL clearance. Established indications include hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, and prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular events. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Skeletal muscle disease comprises disorders of the neuromuscular system that primarily affect voluntary muscle fibers, including inherited and acquired myopathies. Pathology may involve structural protein defects, inflammation, metabolic abnormalities, or toxic injury, leading to muscle fiber dysfunction or degeneration. Typical features include proximal weakness, fatigue, myalgia, exercise intolerance, and elevated creatine kinase, with management directed at the cause, rehabilitation, and complication prevention.

head: Oxazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Oxazepam is a short- to intermediate-acting benzodiazepine anxiolytic that positively modulates the GABA\(_A\) receptor at the benzodiazepine site, enhancing inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders and symptomatic relief of acute alcohol withdrawal. Notable adverse effects include sedation and psychomotor impairment; benzodiazepines are contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma and should be used cautiously with other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Porphyrin metabolism disease refers to disorders of heme biosynthesis or porphyrin handling, affecting the hematologic and hepatic systems and often termed porphyrias. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and age. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, supportive care, and subtype-specific measures such as hemin for acute hepatic attacks.

head: Ropivacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ropivacaine is a long-acting amide-type local anesthetic, the pure S-enantiomer of a pipecoloxylidide derivative. It blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, inhibiting impulse conduction to produce local or regional anesthesia and analgesia. It is established for surgical anesthesia and acute pain control, including epidural and peripheral nerve block use. Notable adverse effects include dose-related CNS and cardiovascular toxicity.
tail_desc: Liver disease is a broad category of hepatic disorders affecting the hepatobiliary system, caused by diverse processes such as viral infection, alcohol-related injury, metabolic dysfunction, autoimmune disease, toxins, or biliary obstruction, leading to inflammation, steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, or liver failure. Common features include jaundice, fatigue, abdominal distension, pruritus, abnormal liver tests, and complications of portal hypertension. Management targets the cause, supports liver function, prevents complications, and may require transplantation.

head: Triazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic that positively modulates GABA\(_A\) receptors by binding the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include anterograde amnesia and next-day psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inhibitors.
tail_desc: Haddad syndrome is a rare congenital neurocristopathy affecting autonomic control of breathing and the enteric nervous system, defined by congenital central hypoventilation syndrome with Hirschsprung disease, often due to PHOX2B mutations. It typically presents in neonates with hypoventilation, especially during sleep, intestinal obstruction from aganglionosis, and autonomic dysfunction. Management centers on lifelong ventilatory support, surgical treatment of Hirschsprung disease, and multidisciplinary monitoring.

head: Flunisolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flunisolide is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid (glucocorticoid) used mainly as an intranasal or inhaled anti-inflammatory agent. It acts by binding intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and reduce airway or nasal mucosal inflammation. Established indications include allergic rhinitis and asthma prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include local candidiasis with inhaled use and nasal irritation or epistaxis intranasally.
tail_desc: A fungal infectious disease is an infection caused by pathogenic fungi that can involve the skin, nails, mucosa, lungs, bloodstream, or other organs. It results from fungal invasion and tissue inflammation, ranging from superficial disease to invasive mycosis. Typical features include rash, scaling, itching, oral or vaginal thrush, cough, fever, or sepsis, with higher risk in immunocompromised patients. Management generally relies on antifungal therapy and correction of underlying risk factors.

head: Alendronic acid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alendronic acid (alendronate) is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive drug. It binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated resorption, primarily by blocking farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway. It is used for osteoporosis and Paget’s disease of bone. A notable adverse effect is esophagitis; it is contraindicated in esophageal abnormalities delaying emptying.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Mannitol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mannitol is a sugar alcohol (polyol) used as an osmotic diuretic. It is freely filtered at the glomerulus and not substantially reabsorbed, increasing tubular fluid osmolality to promote water excretion; it also raises plasma osmolality, drawing water from tissues, without a specific receptor target. It is used to reduce intracranial or intraocular pressure and to promote diuresis; pulmonary edema can occur, and it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Congenital central hypoventilation syndrome is a rare genetic disorder of autonomic control of breathing, primarily affecting the respiratory and nervous systems, usually caused by PHOX2B mutations. It presents in neonates or infants with inadequate ventilatory response to hypercapnia and hypoxemia, especially during sleep, causing hypoventilation and apnea. Management centers on lifelong ventilatory support, monitoring, and care for associated autonomic dysfunction.

head: Isoniazid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoniazid is a first-line antitubercular hydrazide (isonicotinic acid hydrazide) prodrug activated by the mycobacterial catalase-peroxidase KatG; it inhibits mycolic acid synthesis primarily by targeting InhA, impairing mycobacterial cell-wall formation. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of tuberculosis, including latent TB infection. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity; peripheral neuropathy can also occur.
tail_desc: Porphyria comprises inherited or acquired disorders of heme biosynthesis affecting the liver or bone marrow. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or photosensitive skin disease. Manifestations include severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, psychiatric symptoms, blistering, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, treating acute attacks, and supportive specialist care.

head: Dofetilide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dofetilide is a class III antiarrhythmic agent, a methanesulfonanilide derivative, that selectively blocks the rapid component of the delayed rectifier potassium current (IKr) through hERG/Kv11.1 channels, prolonging cardiac repolarization and refractoriness. It is used for conversion and maintenance of sinus rhythm in atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter. A notable risk is QT prolongation with torsades de pointes, making it contraindicated in congenital or acquired long QT syndromes.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Phenylalanine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 (a disease)
head_desc: Phenylalanine is an essential aromatic amino acid used in medical nutrition and as a component of amino acid supplementation; it has no single drug-like molecular target, serving instead as a substrate for protein synthesis and for conversion to tyrosine. Clinically it is included in parenteral/enteral nutrition formulations. It is contraindicated in phenylketonuria, where accumulation can cause serious neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Nephrolithiasis susceptibility caused by SLC26A1 is a hereditary renal stone disorder affecting the urinary system. It is linked to biallelic pathogenic variants in SLC26A1, which encodes a sulfate/oxalate transporter, and is associated with disturbed oxalate handling and calcium oxalate stone formation. Reported patients, often presenting in childhood or early adulthood, develop recurrent nephrolithiasis; management is supportive, with standard stone-prevention measures and nephrology/urology follow-up.

head: Triptorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triptorelin is a synthetic decapeptide gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that binds pituitary GnRH receptors; continuous administration suppresses LH and FSH release, lowering sex steroid production. It is used for prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial hormone “flare” that can transiently worsen symptoms.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Alosetron (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alosetron is a selective serotonin 5-HT3 receptor antagonist used primarily for severe diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D) in women who have not responded adequately to conventional therapy. By blocking 5-HT3 receptors in the gastrointestinal tract, it reduces visceral pain, colonic transit, and intestinal secretion. Notable serious adverse effects include severe constipation and ischemic colitis.
tail_desc: Crohn disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the gastrointestinal tract, most often the terminal ileum and colon. It involves dysregulated, transmural immune-mediated inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals, causing abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, fatigue, and complications such as strictures, fistulas, and perianal disease, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood. Management focuses on inducing and maintaining remission with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, biologic, nutritional, and sometimes surgical therapy.

head: Nortriptyline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nortriptyline is a tricyclic antidepressant of the secondary amine dibenzocycloheptene class that primarily inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and, to a lesser extent, serotonin via the norepinephrine and serotonin transporters. It is used for major depressive disorder and is also established for certain neuropathic pain conditions. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic effects such as dry mouth, constipation, and sedation.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Estazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Estazolam is a triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA-A receptor, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling or staying asleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day sedation, and it is contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Docusate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Docusate is an anionic surfactant stool softener, commonly used as the sodium or calcium salt. It acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract by lowering stool surface tension and promoting water and lipid penetration into feces; no specific molecular target is established. It is used for prevention and treatment of constipation, especially when straining should be avoided. Adverse effects include abdominal cramping and diarrhea.
tail_desc: Sickle cell anemia is an inherited hemoglobinopathy of the blood caused by homozygous HBB mutation producing hemoglobin S, which polymerizes on deoxygenation and leads to red-cell sickling, chronic hemolytic anemia, and vaso-occlusion. It commonly affects people of African ancestry and causes pain crises, jaundice, infection risk, acute chest syndrome, and organ damage. Management includes vaccination, penicillin in childhood, hydroxyurea, transfusions, supportive care, and sometimes stem cell transplantation.

head: Ramipril (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ramipril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug converted to ramiprilat, which inhibits ACE and reduces formation of angiotensin II while lowering aldosterone secretion. It is used for hypertension, heart failure after myocardial infarction, and cardiovascular risk reduction in high-risk patients. A notable adverse effect is dry cough; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in patients with prior ACE inhibitor–associated angioedema.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Atazanavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atazanavir is an azapeptide HIV-1 protease inhibitor antiretroviral that selectively inhibits the viral protease, preventing cleavage of Gag-Pol polyproteins and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, typically with other antiretroviral agents, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is indirect hyperbilirubinemia with jaundice; PR-interval prolongation can also occur.
tail_desc: Hemophilia is an inherited bleeding disorder of the hematologic/coagulation system, usually caused by deficiency of clotting factor VIII (hemophilia A) or IX (hemophilia B), most often in an X-linked pattern. It causes prolonged bleeding, easy bruising, hemarthroses, and deep muscle or postoperative hemorrhage, typically affecting males. Management centers on replacement of the missing factor, prevention of bleeding, and treatment of complications.

head: Indapamide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Indapamide is a thiazide-like sulfonamide diuretic used mainly to treat hypertension and edema. It acts primarily in the distal convoluted tubule by inhibiting the sodium/chloride cotransporter (NCC), increasing urinary sodium and water excretion, with additional antihypertensive vascular effects. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria and should be used cautiously in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Pemetrexed (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pemetrexed is a folate antimetabolite chemotherapy agent that inhibits several folate-dependent enzymes, chiefly thymidylate synthase, and also dihydrofolate reductase and GARFT, thereby blocking purine and pyrimidine synthesis. It is approved for malignant pleural mesothelioma and for non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer. A notable toxicity is myelosuppression, and folic acid/vitamin B12 supplementation is routinely used to reduce it.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Levobunolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Levobunolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class used primarily as an ophthalmic agent. By blocking beta-1 and beta-2 receptors in the ciliary body, it reduces aqueous humor production and lowers intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. Notable adverse effects include bronchospasm, so it is contraindicated in patients with asthma or severe COPD.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Enoxaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin (factor IIa). It is used to prevent and treat venous thromboembolism and in acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Retinitis is inflammation of the retina, a disorder of the eye’s neurosensory visual system. It commonly results from infection, especially viral, fungal, or parasitic causes, but may also occur with autoimmune inflammatory disease. Patients develop blurred vision, floaters, photophobia, and visual field loss; management targets the cause, typically with urgent ophthalmologic evaluation and anti-infective or anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Emtricitabine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Emtricitabine is a synthetic nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI), a fluorinated cytidine analogue used as an antiretroviral. After intracellular phosphorylation, it inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase by competing with deoxycytidine triphosphate and causing viral DNA chain termination. It is approved, in combination regimens, for HIV-1 infection and is also used in HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis combinations. A notable warning is severe acute exacerbation of hepatitis B after discontinuation in coinfected patients.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Cilostazol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cilostazol is a quinolinone-derived antiplatelet and vasodilator that selectively inhibits phosphodiesterase III, increasing intracellular cAMP in platelets and vascular smooth muscle. It is approved to improve symptoms of intermittent claudication in peripheral arterial disease. Notable adverse effects include headache and palpitations, and it is contraindicated in patients with heart failure.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Rizatriptan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rizatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, chemically an indole derivative, that acts as a selective agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors to promote cranial vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease due to risk of coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Cerebral infarction is an ischemic stroke of the central nervous system in which interrupted cerebral blood flow, usually from arterial thrombosis or embolism, causes focal brain tissue necrosis. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech disturbance, or visual loss, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible and secondary stroke prevention.

head: Liraglutide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Liraglutide is a long-acting glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, a fatty-acylated incretin mimetic peptide. It activates the GLP-1 receptor to enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, delay gastric emptying, and reduce appetite. It is approved for type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic weight management. Notable risks include gastrointestinal adverse effects and a boxed warning regarding thyroid C-cell tumors.
tail_desc: Multiple endocrine neoplasia is a group of inherited cancer predisposition syndromes affecting the endocrine system, most often caused by germline mutations in MEN1 or RET. It is characterized by tumors or hyperplasia in multiple endocrine glands, causing hormone excess and related symptoms, often beginning in childhood or adulthood depending on subtype. Management centers on genetic testing, biochemical surveillance, imaging, and surgical treatment of affected glands or tumors.

head: Ketamine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ketamine is a phencyclidine-derivative dissociative anesthetic that primarily acts as a noncompetitive antagonist of the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor. It is used for induction and maintenance of anesthesia and analgesia; the S-enantiomer esketamine is approved for treatment-resistant depression. A notable adverse effect is emergence reactions with hallucinations and dissociation.
tail_desc: Respiratory failure is a life-threatening syndrome of the respiratory system in which gas exchange is inadequate, causing hypoxemia with or without hypercapnia. It results from lung, airway, neuromuscular, chest wall, or central ventilatory dysfunction. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachypnea, cyanosis, confusion, or somnolence, especially in critically ill adults or children. Management targets the underlying cause and supports oxygenation and ventilation, including oxygen therapy and noninvasive or mechanical ventilation.

head: Pentamidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Pentamidine is an aromatic diamidine antiprotozoal agent. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it binds parasite DNA and interferes with nucleic acid and protein synthesis. It is used for treatment of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia and early-stage African trypanosomiasis, and by inhalation for PCP prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity, hypoglycemia, and pancreatitis.
tail_desc: Macrocytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by enlarged red blood cells and reduced oxygen-carrying capacity, usually from impaired DNA synthesis (for example vitamin B12 or folate deficiency), alcohol use, liver disease, hypothyroidism, medications, or bone marrow disorders. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, dyspnea, and sometimes neurologic findings in B12 deficiency. Management targets the underlying cause and corrects deficient nutrients.

head: Atenolol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker, belonging to the beta-blocker class. It primarily antagonizes beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate, contractility, and blood pressure. It is used for hypertension, angina pectoris, and management after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in sinus bradycardia, heart block, and cardiogenic shock.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Cefoperazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefoperazone is a third-generation cephalosporin β-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, intra-abdominal, skin/soft-tissue, and biliary tract infections. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia with bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Blood coagulation diseases are disorders of the hemostatic system in which abnormalities of clotting factors, platelets, or regulatory pathways impair normal coagulation or promote pathologic thrombosis. They may present with easy bruising, mucosal or deep-tissue bleeding, or recurrent venous or arterial clots in children or adults. Management targets the cause, using factor replacement, anticoagulation, and supportive monitoring as appropriate.

head: Hydroxychloroquine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Hydroxychloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in lysosomes, raises endosomal pH, and inhibits toll-like receptor signaling. It is approved for malaria and widely used for systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis. A notable adverse effect is retinal toxicity with prolonged use, requiring ophthalmologic monitoring.
tail_desc: Parapsoriasis is a chronic papulosquamous skin disorder of uncertain cause, generally considered within the spectrum of cutaneous T-cell lymphoproliferative disease. It presents mainly in adults as persistent, scaly patches or thin plaques, classically in small-plaque and large-plaque forms on the trunk and proximal limbs. Management is usually dermatologic surveillance and symptom control with topical therapies or phototherapy, with monitoring for possible progression in large-plaque disease.

head: Parnaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Parnaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and to a lesser extent thrombin (factor IIa). It is used for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolic disorders, including postoperative deep vein thrombosis. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major hemorrhage or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Intracranial hemorrhage is acute bleeding within the skull affecting the central nervous system, including intracerebral, subarachnoid, subdural, and epidural spaces. It usually results from ruptured vessels due to hypertension, trauma, aneurysm, vascular malformation, or coagulopathy. Patients, often older adults or trauma patients, present with sudden headache, focal neurologic deficits, vomiting, reduced consciousness, or seizures. Management is emergent, focusing on stabilization, blood pressure and coagulation control, and neurosurgical intervention when indicated.

head: Tetracaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetracaine is an ester-type local anesthetic of the para-aminobenzoic acid class that reversibly blocks voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing impulse conduction. It is used for topical anesthesia of the eye and mucous membranes and in spinal anesthesia.
