A notable risk is systemic toxicity, including CNS and cardiovascular effects; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to ester local anesthetics.
tail_desc: Respiratory failure is a life-threatening syndrome of the respiratory system in which gas exchange is inadequate, causing hypoxemia with or without hypercapnia. It results from lung, airway, neuromuscular, chest wall, or central ventilatory dysfunction. Patients typically present with dyspnea, tachypnea, cyanosis, confusion, or somnolence, especially in critically ill adults or children. Management targets the underlying cause and supports oxygenation and ventilation, including oxygen therapy and noninvasive or mechanical ventilation.

head: Melphalan (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Melphalan is a bifunctional nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the phenylalanine derivative class that covalently crosslinks DNA, mainly at guanine bases, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and causing cell death. It is used chiefly in multiple myeloma and ovarian carcinoma conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity or marked bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Neostigmine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Neostigmine is a reversible carbamate acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (indirect cholinergic agonist) that inhibits acetylcholinesterase, increasing acetylcholine at neuromuscular junctions and parasympathetic synapses. It is used to treat myasthenia gravis and postoperative urinary retention or ileus, and to reverse nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and other muscarinic cholinergic effects; it is contraindicated in mechanical gastrointestinal or urinary obstruction.
tail_desc: Urinary tract obstruction is a genitourinary disorder in which partial or complete blockage of urine flow occurs anywhere from the renal pelvis to the urethra, causing increased upstream pressure, hydronephrosis, infection risk, and potential renal impairment. Symptoms may include flank or abdominal pain, lower urinary tract symptoms, anuria, hematuria, or recurrent urinary infection. Management focuses on urgent decompression when indicated and treatment of the underlying cause.

head: Apraclonidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Apraclonidine is a topical ophthalmic imidazoline and relatively selective alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonist that lowers intraocular pressure mainly by reducing aqueous humor production and partly increasing uveoscleral outflow. It is used short-term to prevent or control pressure spikes after anterior segment laser procedures and as adjunctive therapy in glaucoma. A notable adverse effect is allergic blepharoconjunctivitis.
tail_desc: Thrombocytopenia is a hematologic disorder of the blood characterized by a reduced platelet count due to decreased production, increased peripheral destruction or consumption, or splenic sequestration. It may cause petechiae, purpura, mucosal bleeding, and increased hemorrhagic risk, especially in severe cases. It occurs across all ages and is managed by treating the underlying cause, avoiding bleeding risks, and using platelet transfusion or disease-specific therapy when indicated.

head: Mangafodipir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mangafodipir is a manganese-containing chelate contrast agent, specifically manganese dipyridoxyl diphosphate, formerly used in magnetic resonance imaging. It acts by delivering paramagnetic Mn²⁺ that shortens T1 relaxation time, enhancing liver and pancreatic tissue contrast rather than binding a single receptor target. It was approved for MRI of hepatic lesions. Notable adverse effects include flushing and nausea; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Pyoureter is a suppurative disorder of the urinary tract in which pus accumulates within the ureter, usually secondary to ascending bacterial infection and often associated with urinary obstruction. It may present with fever, flank pain, dysuria, and signs of sepsis, typically in patients with obstructive uropathy or complicated urinary infection. Management generally requires urgent antibiotics and relief of obstruction or drainage when indicated.

head: Saquinavir (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Saquinavir is a peptidomimetic HIV protease inhibitor antiretroviral that selectively inhibits the HIV-1 and HIV-2 protease enzymes, preventing cleavage of viral polyprotein precursors and formation of mature infectious virions. It is used, typically with ritonavir boosting, in combination therapy for HIV-1 infection. A notable risk is PR interval prolongation, making it contraindicated in patients with significant cardiac conduction disorders.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Trichlormethiazide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Trichlormethiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disorders. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Choledocholithiasis is a biliary tract disorder in which gallstones are present in the common bile duct, causing partial or complete obstruction of bile flow in the hepatobiliary system. It commonly presents in adults, often with right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, cholestatic liver test abnormalities, and sometimes fever or pancreatitis. Management typically involves stone removal, usually by ERCP, followed by treatment of the underlying gallstone disease when indicated.

head: Zafirlukast (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zafirlukast is an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist that selectively blocks cysteinyl leukotriene CysLT1 receptors, inhibiting the actions of LTC4, LTD4, and LTE4 in the airways. It is approved for prophylaxis and chronic treatment of asthma, including exercise-induced bronchoconstriction prevention. A notable safety concern is hepatotoxicity, and it is not indicated for relief of acute asthma attacks.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Dimenhydrinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dimenhydrinate is a first-generation H1 antihistamine and antiemetic, a salt of diphenhydramine with 8-chlorotheophylline. It acts primarily by antagonizing histamine H1 receptors, with additional antimuscarinic effects that suppress vestibular stimulation and the vomiting center. It is used for prevention and treatment of motion sickness, nausea, vomiting, and vertigo. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to dimenhydrinate or diphenhydramine.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Rilpivirine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rilpivirine is a diarylpyrimidine non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) that binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase, blocking viral DNA synthesis. It is used, in combination with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable safety concern is dose-related QT interval prolongation, and it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inducers that can markedly reduce rilpivirine exposure.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Magnesium sulfate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium sulfate is an inorganic magnesium salt and electrolyte replacement that acts by increasing extracellular magnesium, modulating calcium influx and acetylcholine release, and functionally antagonizing NMDA receptors. It is used for hypomagnesemia, prevention and treatment of seizures in severe preeclampsia/eclampsia, torsades de pointes, and as a tocolytic in selected settings. A notable adverse effect is respiratory depression, particularly with hypermagnesemia or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Magnesium hydroxide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Magnesium hydroxide is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an antacid and osmotic laxative. It neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach and, when taken orally in larger doses, retains water in the intestinal lumen by osmotic action; it has no specific receptor target. Established uses include relief of dyspepsia/heartburn and short-term treatment of constipation. A notable risk is hypermagnesemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Chloral hydrate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Chloral hydrate is a sedative-hypnotic of the aldehyde/trichloroethanol class, historically used for short-term sedation and hypnosis, including pediatric procedural sedation. It is rapidly converted to trichloroethanol, which depresses the central nervous system, likely via potentiation of GABA\(_A\) receptor signaling. Notable adverse effects include respiratory depression; it is contraindicated in significant hepatic or renal impairment and severe cardiac disease.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Ketoprofen (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ketoprofen is a propionic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for pain and inflammatory conditions such as osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and dysmenorrhea. A notable risk is gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Cardiovascular disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the heart and blood vessels, commonly driven by atherosclerosis, hypertension, thrombosis, or structural and electrical abnormalities. It often presents in adults with chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, edema, syncope, myocardial infarction, or stroke. Management typically emphasizes risk-factor reduction, lifestyle change, medications, and selected interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Clevidipine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clevidipine is an intravenous dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that selectively inhibits L-type calcium channels in arterial smooth muscle, producing rapid arteriolar vasodilation and blood pressure reduction. It is used for acute hypertension when oral therapy is not feasible, including perioperative blood pressure control. A notable adverse effect is reflex tachycardia; it is contraindicated in patients with allergies to soy or egg products.
tail_desc: Lipoid nephrosis is the classic older term for minimal change disease, a glomerular kidney disorder and common cause of nephrotic syndrome, especially in children. It is characterized by podocyte foot-process effacement with little or no abnormality on light microscopy, leading to heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia. Management is mainly corticosteroid therapy with supportive control of edema and other nephrotic complications.

head: Etravirine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Etravirine is a second-generation non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI), a diarylpyrimidine antiretroviral that binds allosterically to HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and inhibits viral DNA synthesis. It is approved, in combination with other antiretrovirals, for treatment of HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is rash, including rare severe hypersensitivity reactions such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Peripheral neuropathy is a disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused by damage to peripheral nerves, commonly from diabetes, toxins, nutritional deficiencies, infections, or immune-mediated disease. It typically presents with distal numbness, tingling, burning pain, weakness, and reduced reflexes, often in a length-dependent “stocking-glove” pattern, especially in adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the cause, symptom control, and rehabilitation or foot care when needed.

head: Thioridazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thioridazine is a phenothiazine antipsychotic of the piperidine subclass that acts mainly by antagonizing dopamine D2 receptors, with additional anticholinergic and alpha-adrenergic blocking effects. It has been used for schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders. A notable risk is dose-related QT prolongation with potentially fatal ventricular arrhythmias, making use contraindicated in patients with significant cardiac arrhythmia.
tail_desc: Multiple system atrophy is a rare, adult-onset progressive neurodegenerative synucleinopathy affecting the autonomic nervous system, basal ganglia, and cerebellum. It is characterized pathologically by alpha-synuclein–positive glial cytoplasmic inclusions. Patients develop autonomic failure with parkinsonism and/or cerebellar ataxia, often with pyramidal features. Management is supportive, focusing on symptom control, autonomic dysfunction, mobility, and multidisciplinary care.

head: Clomifene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Clomifene is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (triphenylethylene derivative) that acts mainly as an estrogen receptor antagonist in the hypothalamus, blocking estrogen feedback and increasing GnRH, LH, and FSH release to induce ovulation. It is established for treatment of anovulatory infertility, particularly in polycystic ovary syndrome. A notable adverse effect is ovarian hyperstimulation, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecologic disorder of the female reproductive system in which endometrial-like tissue grows outside the uterus, causing estrogen-driven inflammation, scarring, and adhesions. It mainly affects reproductive-age women and commonly presents with dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility. Management is directed toward pain control, hormonal suppression, and, when needed, surgical removal of lesions.

head: Prazepam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic, structurally a 1,4-benzodiazepine derivative, used mainly for the short-term treatment of anxiety disorders. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing GABA-mediated inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated in myasthenia gravis and severe respiratory insufficiency.
tail_desc: Lung disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the respiratory system, especially the lungs and airways. It may result from infection, inflammation, smoking-related injury, environmental exposures, genetic disorders, or vascular and interstitial pathology. Common features include cough, dyspnea, wheeze, chest discomfort, and impaired gas exchange across all ages. Management depends on cause and may include risk-factor reduction, medications, oxygen, pulmonary rehabilitation, or surgery.

head: Labetalol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Labetalol is a mixed adrenergic antagonist of the amino alcohol class that blocks β1-, β2-, and α1-adrenergic receptors, lowering heart rate and peripheral vascular resistance. It is used for hypertension, including severe hypertension and hypertension in pregnancy. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma, overt heart failure, and greater-than-first-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Hyperparathyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the parathyroid glands causing excess parathyroid hormone, which raises blood calcium and disrupts bone and kidney homeostasis. It may be primary, secondary to chronic kidney disease or vitamin D deficiency, or tertiary after prolonged stimulation. Common features include hypercalcemia, nephrolithiasis, bone pain, fractures, weakness, and neuropsychiatric symptoms; management targets the cause, with parathyroidectomy for selected cases.

head: Synephrine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Synephrine is a naturally occurring protoalkaloid phenylethanolamine, structurally related to ephedrine, found notably in bitter orange (Citrus aurantium). It acts primarily as a sympathomimetic agonist at adrenergic receptors, with reported activity especially at beta-3 and some alpha receptors. It is not an approved prescription drug for established clinical indications in major jurisdictions; use is mainly in supplements. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, so it is contraindicated in significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Fexofenadine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Fexofenadine is a second-generation piperidine antihistamine and the active carboxylated metabolite of terfenadine. It selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated symptoms. It is established for seasonal allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is headache, and it is generally minimally sedating compared with first-generation antihistamines.
tail_desc: Angle-closure glaucoma is an ophthalmic disease in which the peripheral iris obstructs the anterior chamber angle, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid or intermittent rise in intraocular pressure. It typically affects anatomically predisposed older adults, especially those with shallow anterior chambers. Features include severe eye pain, headache, blurred vision, halos, nausea, and a red eye; management is urgent pressure lowering followed by laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Rifabutin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Rifabutin is a rifamycin-class ansamycin antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase by binding the β-subunit encoded by rpoB, thereby suppressing RNA synthesis. It is used primarily for treatment and prophylaxis of Mycobacterium avium complex infection and as an alternative rifamycin in tuberculosis regimens. A notable adverse effect is uveitis, especially at higher doses or with interacting drugs.
tail_desc: Agranulocytosis is a severe hematologic disorder of the immune system marked by a profound reduction in circulating neutrophils, usually from drug-induced marrow suppression, immune destruction, or less often infection or marrow disease. Patients develop fever, malaise, sore throat, and rapidly progressive bacterial or fungal infections, especially older adults taking high-risk medications. Management centers on urgent drug withdrawal, broad-spectrum antibiotics, supportive care, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Famotidine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Famotidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist of the guanidine/thiazole class that competitively blocks H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells, reducing basal and stimulated gastric acid secretion. It is used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypersecretory states such as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Notable adverse effects include headache and dizziness, and dose reduction is recommended in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Allantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Allantoin is a diureide of glyoxylic acid used topically as a skin protectant and keratolytic in many over-the-counter dermatologic products. Its mechanism is not defined by a specific molecular target; it is thought to promote desquamation and support wound healing by softening keratin and enhancing moisture retention. Established uses include minor wounds, burns, chapped or irritated skin, and oral mucosal irritation. Adverse effects are uncommon but can include local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Glycogen storage disease comprises inherited metabolic disorders, usually affecting liver and/or muscle, caused by enzyme defects in glycogen synthesis or breakdown that lead to abnormal glycogen accumulation or impaired glucose release. Typical features include fasting hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, growth delay, exercise intolerance, and muscle weakness, often presenting in infancy or childhood. Management is mainly dietary and supportive, with subtype-specific monitoring and complication prevention.

head: Tetrabenazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tetrabenazine is a benzoquinolizine derivative and vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) inhibitor that depletes presynaptic dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine stores. It is approved to treat chorea associated with Huntington disease and is also used for hyperkinetic movement disorders such as tardive dyskinesia. Notable risks include depression and suicidality, and it is contraindicated in actively suicidal patients or those with untreated depression.
tail_desc: Tardive dyskinesia is a hyperkinetic movement disorder of the nervous system, usually caused by chronic exposure to dopamine receptor–blocking drugs, especially antipsychotics and some antiemetics, likely involving dopamine receptor supersensitivity in basal ganglia circuits. It causes persistent involuntary, repetitive movements, especially of the face, mouth, tongue, and sometimes trunk or limbs, mainly in patients receiving long-term neuroleptic therapy; management focuses on reducing offending drugs when feasible and using VMAT2 inhibitors.

head: Tesamorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Tesamorelin is a synthetic growth hormone–releasing hormone analog that binds pituitary GHRH receptors to stimulate endogenous growth hormone secretion and increase IGF-1. It is approved to reduce excess abdominal visceral adipose tissue in adults with HIV-associated lipodystrophy. Notable risks include glucose intolerance or hyperglycemia, and it is contraindicated in active malignancy and disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis.
tail_desc: embryoma is a disease or medical condition.

head: Mesoridazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Mesoridazine is a piperidine phenothiazine antipsychotic, historically used for schizophrenia. Like other typical antipsychotics, it acts primarily through central dopamine D2 receptor antagonism. Its clinical use became severely restricted because of dose-related QT interval prolongation and risk of serious ventricular arrhythmias, including torsades de pointes, making it contraindicated in patients with known cardiac conduction abnormalities or significant QT prolongation.
tail_desc: Constitutional neutropenia is a benign, usually lifelong form of chronic neutropenia affecting the hematologic/immune system, characterized by persistently low absolute neutrophil counts without an acquired cause or major marrow pathology. It is often seen in otherwise healthy individuals, including some ethnic groups, and typically causes no or few infections. Management is mainly reassurance, exclusion of secondary causes, and clinical monitoring rather than routine treatment.

head: Streptozocin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Streptozocin is a nitrosourea alkylating antineoplastic agent, structurally related to glucosamine, that causes DNA alkylation and damage after uptake into pancreatic islet cells via GLUT2. It is approved for metastatic or unresectable pancreatic islet-cell carcinoma, including neuroendocrine tumors. A notable toxicity is nephrotoxicity; severe renal impairment is therefore an important contraindication or reason for caution.
tail_desc: Diabetic ketoacidosis is an acute, life-threatening metabolic complication of diabetes, mainly affecting the endocrine and systemic metabolic systems. It results from absolute or relative insulin deficiency causing hyperglycemia, ketosis, and high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. Typical features include dehydration, polyuria, abdominal pain, vomiting, Kussmaul respirations, and altered mental status, often in type 1 diabetes; management requires urgent intravenous fluids, insulin, electrolyte replacement, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Butacaine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Butacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic in the benzoate family. Like other local anesthetics, it acts by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses. It has been used topically, particularly in ophthalmic anesthesia. As with related ester anesthetics, hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known ester local anesthetic allergy.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Dimenhydrinate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dimenhydrinate is a first-generation H1 antihistamine and antiemetic, a salt of diphenhydramine with 8-chlorotheophylline. It acts primarily by antagonizing histamine H1 receptors, with additional antimuscarinic effects that suppress vestibular stimulation and the vomiting center. It is used for prevention and treatment of motion sickness, nausea, vomiting, and vertigo. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to dimenhydrinate or diphenhydramine.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Alprostadil (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Alprostadil is a prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) analog and vasodilatory smooth-muscle relaxant that activates prostaglandin E receptors, increasing cAMP. It is used to maintain ductus arteriosus patency in certain congenital heart defects and to treat erectile dysfunction. Notable adverse effects include hypotension; it is contraindicated in neonates with respiratory distress syndrome without cyanotic heart disease.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Disulfiram (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Disulfiram is a thiuram disulfide aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor used in alcohol aversion therapy for alcohol use disorder. It irreversibly inhibits hepatic aldehyde dehydrogenase, causing acetaldehyde accumulation after ethanol ingestion and an unpleasant reaction. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated with concurrent alcohol exposure because it can provoke severe disulfiram-ethanol reactions.
tail_desc: Hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland characterized by deficient thyroid hormone production, most commonly from autoimmune thyroiditis, thyroid ablation, or iodine deficiency. It causes a generalized slowing of metabolism with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, bradycardia, and depression, and is more common in women and older adults. Management typically involves diagnosing the cause and replacing hormone with levothyroxine.

head: Triamterene (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triamterene is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pteridine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal renal tubule and collecting duct, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for edema and hypertension, often with a thiazide diuretic. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria, severe renal impairment, or preexisting hyperkalemia.
tail_desc: Liver failure is a severe hepatic disorder in which extensive loss of liver function, acute or chronic, impairs metabolism, detoxification, and synthesis, usually due to massive hepatocellular injury from cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, alcohol, drugs, or ischemia. It causes jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy, ascites, and multiorgan complications, and is managed with urgent supportive care, treatment of the cause, and often liver transplantation.

head: Calcium glycerophosphate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium salt of glycerophosphoric acid used as a mineral supplement and urinary/food acid neutralizing agent. It dissociates to provide calcium and phosphate, without a specific primary molecular target. It is used in some formulations to reduce dietary acid effects and as a calcium source. Adverse effects may include hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Parnaparin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Parnaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and to a lesser extent thrombin (factor IIa). It is used for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolic disorders, including postoperative deep vein thrombosis. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major hemorrhage or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the colonic mucosa, typically beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. It reflects immune-mediated intestinal inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals. Common features include bloody diarrhea, urgency, abdominal pain, and relapsing course, often in adolescents and adults. Management focuses on anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapy, with colectomy for refractory or complicated disease.

head: Nitrofural (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofural (nitrofurazone) is a topical nitrofuran antibacterial. After enzymatic reduction of its nitro group in susceptible microbes, reactive intermediates damage bacterial DNA and other macromolecules, giving broad activity against many Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms. It has been used for topical prevention or treatment of skin infections in burns, grafts, wounds, and ulcers. A notable adverse effect is allergic contact dermatitis.
tail_desc: Cystinuria is a hereditary renal tubular transport disorder affecting the urinary system, caused by defective reabsorption of cystine and dibasic amino acids in the proximal tubule. Excess urinary cystine, which is poorly soluble, leads to recurrent cystine kidney stones, often beginning in childhood or young adulthood, with hematuria, flank pain, and obstruction. Management focuses on high fluid intake, urine alkalinization, dietary sodium reduction, and sometimes thiol drugs.

head: Nitrofurantoin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Nitrofurantoin is a nitrofuran antibacterial used primarily for treatment and prophylaxis of uncomplicated lower urinary tract infections. In bacteria, nitroreductases reduce it to reactive intermediates that damage DNA, ribosomal proteins, and other macromolecules, so it has no single primary molecular target. A notable risk is pulmonary toxicity; it is contraindicated at term in pregnancy and in significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Postinflammatory pulmonary fibrosis is a chronic interstitial lung disorder of the respiratory system in which prior pulmonary inflammation, such as severe infection or other inflammatory lung injury, heals with fibrotic scarring of the lung parenchyma. Patients may develop persistent dyspnea, dry cough, reduced exercise tolerance, and restrictive impairment on testing. Management focuses on treating the underlying cause, supportive care, and monitoring respiratory function.

head: Milrinone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Milrinone is a bipyridine phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor and inodilator that increases intracellular cAMP in cardiac myocytes and vascular smooth muscle, enhancing myocardial contractility and promoting vasodilation. It is used for short-term intravenous treatment of acute decompensated heart failure or low-output states. A notable adverse effect is ventricular arrhythmia; significant hypotension can also occur.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Dofetilide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dofetilide is a class III antiarrhythmic agent, a methanesulfonanilide derivative, that selectively blocks the rapid component of the delayed rectifier potassium current (IKr) through hERG/Kv11.1 channels, prolonging cardiac repolarization and refractoriness. It is used for conversion and maintenance of sinus rhythm in atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter. A notable risk is QT prolongation with torsades de pointes, making it contraindicated in congenital or acquired long QT syndromes.
tail_desc: Potassium deficiency disease, or hypokalemia, is an electrolyte disorder affecting neuromuscular, cardiovascular, and renal function, caused by low serum potassium from gastrointestinal losses, renal wasting, inadequate intake, or intracellular shifts. Typical features include weakness, muscle cramps, constipation, fatigue, and cardiac arrhythmias, especially in patients using diuretics or with vomiting/diarrhea. Management focuses on identifying the cause, potassium replacement, and ECG and electrolyte monitoring.

head: Acrivastine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Acrivastine is a second-generation antihistamine of the alkylamine/propylamine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used for symptomatic relief of seasonal allergic rhinitis and urticaria, including sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itching. Compared with older antihistamines it is less sedating, but drowsiness can still occur; caution is advised in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Dronabinol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dronabinol is synthetic delta-9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), a cannabinoid receptor agonist that primarily activates CB1 and CB2 receptors in the central and peripheral nervous systems. It is approved to treat chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting refractory to conventional antiemetics and anorexia-associated weight loss in patients with AIDS. A notable adverse effect is dose-related neuropsychiatric toxicity, including dizziness, euphoria, or confusion.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Lindane (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Lindane is an organochlorine insecticide and scabicide/pediculicide (the gamma isomer of hexachlorocyclohexane) used topically for scabies and head lice when safer treatments are unsuitable or have failed. It acts on parasite neuronal ion channels, particularly GABA-gated chloride channels, causing neuronal hyperexcitation. A notable risk is neurotoxicity, including seizures, so it is contraindicated in premature infants and patients with seizure disorders.
tail_desc: Parapsoriasis is a chronic papulosquamous skin disorder of uncertain cause, generally considered within the spectrum of cutaneous T-cell lymphoproliferative disease. It presents mainly in adults as persistent, scaly patches or thin plaques, classically in small-plaque and large-plaque forms on the trunk and proximal limbs. Management is usually dermatologic surveillance and symptom control with topical therapies or phototherapy, with monitoring for possible progression in large-plaque disease.

head: Isoniazid (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Isoniazid is a first-line antitubercular hydrazide (isonicotinic acid hydrazide) prodrug activated by the mycobacterial catalase-peroxidase KatG; it inhibits mycolic acid synthesis primarily by targeting InhA, impairing mycobacterial cell-wall formation. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of tuberculosis, including latent TB infection. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity; peripheral neuropathy can also occur.
tail_desc: Porphyrin metabolism disease refers to disorders of heme biosynthesis or porphyrin handling, affecting the hematologic and hepatic systems and often termed porphyrias. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and age. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, supportive care, and subtype-specific measures such as hemin for acute hepatic attacks.

head: Meticillin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
tail: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Meticillin (methicillin) is a penicillinase-resistant beta-lactam antibiotic of the penicillin class. It inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It was used for infections caused by penicillinase-producing staphylococci, especially methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including serious allergic reactions.
tail_desc: chronic Epstein-Barr virus infection syndrome is a disease or medical condition.

head: Meloxicam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Meloxicam is an oxicam-class nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that preferentially inhibits cyclooxygenase, mainly COX-2 over COX-1, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Coronary thrombosis is a cardiovascular disorder in which a thrombus acutely forms within a coronary artery, usually after rupture or erosion of an atherosclerotic plaque, reducing or blocking myocardial blood flow. It commonly presents as acute coronary syndrome with severe chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and possible myocardial infarction, especially in adults with atherosclerotic risk factors. Management is urgent reperfusion plus antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention.

head: Thalidomide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Thalidomide is an immunomodulatory glutamic acid derivative (IMiD) that binds cereblon, the substrate receptor of the CRL4 E3 ubiquitin ligase, altering degradation of transcription factors and suppressing TNF-α. It is used for multiple myeloma and erythema nodosum leprosum. A notable toxicity is profound teratogenicity, making pregnancy a strict contraindication; peripheral neuropathy can also occur.
tail_desc: Neutropenia is a hematologic disorder of the immune system defined by an abnormally low neutrophil count, most often due to reduced bone marrow production, increased peripheral destruction, or drug, infection, autoimmune, or congenital causes. It increases susceptibility to recurrent or severe bacterial and fungal infections, especially in chemotherapy patients. Management targets the cause, infection prevention and treatment, and sometimes granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.

head: Flunisolide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Flunisolide is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid (glucocorticoid) used mainly as an intranasal or inhaled anti-inflammatory agent. It acts by binding intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and reduce airway or nasal mucosal inflammation. Established indications include allergic rhinitis and asthma prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include local candidiasis with inhaled use and nasal irritation or epistaxis intranasally.
tail_desc: Dermatophytosis is a superficial fungal infection of keratinized tissues of the integumentary system, caused by dermatophytes such as Trichophyton, Microsporum, and Epidermophyton that invade the stratum corneum, hair, or nails. It typically presents as pruritic, scaly annular plaques, alopecia, or onychodystrophy in children or adults, depending on site. Management usually involves topical antifungals for limited disease and systemic therapy for scalp, nail, or extensive infection.

head: Triazolam (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine sedative-hypnotic that positively modulates GABA\(_A\) receptors by binding the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and central nervous system inhibition. It is used for the short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly sleep-onset difficulty. Notable adverse effects include anterograde amnesia and next-day psychomotor impairment; it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inhibitors.
tail_desc: Substance abuse/dependence is a substance-related addictive disorder affecting the brain and behavior, marked by compulsive use of alcohol or drugs despite harm. It involves maladaptive reward, motivation, and control pathways, often with tolerance, craving, withdrawal, impaired functioning, and medical or psychiatric comorbidity, occurring across age groups. Management centers on cessation or harm reduction, psychosocial treatment, and, when appropriate, medication-assisted therapy and withdrawal management.

head: Salbutamol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Salbutamol (albuterol) is a short-acting β2-adrenergic receptor agonist bronchodilator, chemically a phenethylamine derivative. By selectively stimulating β2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, it increases cyclic AMP and produces rapid bronchodilation. It is used for relief and prevention of bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, including exercise-induced bronchospasm. A notable adverse effect is tremor.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Benzyl alcohol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Benzyl alcohol is an aromatic alcohol used primarily as an antimicrobial preservative and, in topical formulations, as a pediculicide. Its pediculicidal effect is physical rather than receptor-mediated, obstructing lice respiratory spiracles and causing asphyxiation; no specific human molecular target is established. Clinically, it is used to preserve multidose injectable products and to treat head lice. A notable contraindication is use in neonates because of potentially fatal “gasping syndrome.”
tail_desc: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is a chronic inflammatory lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by persistent, usually progressive airflow limitation caused mainly by smoking-related small-airway disease and emphysema. Typical features include chronic cough, sputum production, dyspnea, wheeze, and exacerbations, usually in middle-aged or older adults with smoking or pollutant exposure. Management centers on smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, vaccinations, pulmonary rehabilitation, and oxygen in selected patients.

head: Dihydrotachysterol (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Dihydrotachysterol is a synthetic vitamin D analog (secosteroid) used as a calcium-regulating agent. After hepatic activation, it acts via the vitamin D receptor to increase intestinal calcium absorption and help restore serum calcium. It has been used for hypocalcemia, particularly in hypoparathyroidism. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia, so it is contraindicated in patients with pre-existing hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Sarcoidosis is a systemic inflammatory granulomatous disease that most often affects the lungs and intrathoracic lymph nodes. It is characterized by noncaseating granuloma formation of unclear cause, likely involving immune dysregulation in genetically susceptible people. Common features include cough, dyspnea, fatigue, skin or eye involvement, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, typically in young to middle-aged adults; management ranges from observation to corticosteroids and other immunosuppressive therapy for significant organ disease.

head: Cefuroxime (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Cefuroxime is a second-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including respiratory tract, skin and soft tissue, urinary tract infections, and gonorrhea; perioperative prophylaxis is also established for injectable forms. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, with contraindication in patients with serious cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Kidney disease comprises disorders of the renal system that impair kidney structure or function, commonly from diabetes, hypertension, glomerular injury, infection, obstruction, or inherited causes. Manifestations include proteinuria, hematuria, edema, hypertension, electrolyte abnormalities, and reduced glomerular filtration, sometimes progressing to chronic kidney disease or failure. Management targets the cause, controls blood pressure and metabolic complications, and may require dialysis or transplantation.

head: Testosterone propionate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Testosterone propionate is a short-acting ester of testosterone, an androgen/anabolic steroid. After hydrolysis to testosterone, it binds and activates the androgen receptor, regulating transcription of androgen-responsive genes. It has been used as testosterone replacement for male hypogonadism and delayed puberty in selected males. Notable adverse effects include virilization, and it is contraindicated in prostate or male breast cancer.
tail_desc: Obstructive sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the upper airway, characterized by recurrent pharyngeal collapse during sleep causing intermittent airflow obstruction, hypoxemia, and sleep fragmentation. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity, and presents with loud snoring, witnessed apneas, and excessive daytime sleepiness. Management typically includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and selected upper-airway surgery.

head: Triptorelin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Triptorelin is a synthetic decapeptide gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist that binds pituitary GnRH receptors; continuous administration suppresses LH and FSH release, lowering sex steroid production. It is used for prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial hormone “flare” that can transiently worsen symptoms.
tail_desc: Hyperglycemia is a metabolic abnormality of the endocrine system characterized by elevated blood glucose, usually due to insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, most commonly in diabetes mellitus. It may cause polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, blurred vision, and weight loss, and occurs in people with diabetes or acute illness. Management focuses on correcting the cause, hydration, glucose monitoring, and insulin or other glucose-lowering therapy.

head: Zaleplon (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Zaleplon is a nonbenzodiazepine hypnotic of the pyrazolopyrimidine class that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of the GABA\(_A\) receptor benzodiazepine site, with relative selectivity for α1-containing receptors. It is approved for short-term treatment of insomnia, particularly difficulty falling asleep. A notable adverse effect is next-day drowsiness or dizziness, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug.
tail_desc: Sleep apnea syndrome is a sleep-related breathing disorder of the respiratory and upper airway control systems, characterized by recurrent pauses in breathing during sleep due to upper airway collapse (obstructive) and/or impaired central respiratory drive. It causes loud snoring, witnessed apneas, fragmented sleep, and daytime sleepiness, commonly affecting overweight adults. Management includes weight loss, continuous positive airway pressure, oral appliances, and treatment of contributing conditions.

head: Metolazone (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metolazone is a quinazoline sulfonamide-like thiazide-related diuretic that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat hypertension and edema, including edema associated with congestive heart failure or renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a chronic multisystem autoimmune connective tissue disease in which loss of immune tolerance leads to autoantibody production and immune-complex–mediated inflammation affecting skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, serosa, and the nervous system. It commonly affects women of childbearing age and causes fatigue, rash, photosensitivity, arthritis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities; management uses sun protection, hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressive or biologic therapies tailored to organ involvement.

head: Orciprenaline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Orciprenaline (metaproterenol) is a sympathomimetic phenylethanolamine and selective beta-2 adrenergic receptor agonist that relaxes bronchial smooth muscle by stimulating adenylate cyclase and increasing cyclic AMP. It has been used as a bronchodilator for reversible bronchospasm in asthma and chronic obstructive airway disease. A notable adverse effect is tachycardia, and it is used cautiously in patients with significant cardiac arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Ethosuximide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Ethosuximide is a succinimide anticonvulsant used primarily for absence (petit mal) seizures. Its antiepileptic effect is attributed mainly to inhibition of low-threshold T-type calcium currents in thalamic neurons, reducing abnormal thalamocortical rhythmic activity. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal upset, and it can also cause serious but rare blood dyscrasias or hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Anagrelide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Anagrelide is an oral imidazoquinazoline antiplatelet agent that lowers platelet counts primarily by inhibiting megakaryocyte maturation, with phosphodiesterase III inhibition also documented. It is used for thrombocythemia, especially essential thrombocythemia, to reduce elevated platelets and thrombotic risk. A notable adverse effect is cardiovascular toxicity, including tachycardia and palpitations; caution is advised in patients with cardiac disease.
tail_desc: Gallbladder disease is a biliary system disorder, most commonly caused by gallstones that obstruct the cystic duct and trigger inflammation (cholecystitis). It typically presents with right upper abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, and tenderness, often after fatty meals, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management depends on cause and severity, ranging from pain control and antibiotics to cholecystectomy.

head: Griseofulvin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Griseofulvin is an oral antifungal antibiotic derived from Penicillium, used mainly for dermatophyte infections of the skin, hair, and nails (tinea). It binds fungal tubulin and disrupts microtubule function, inhibiting mitosis, and concentrates in keratin precursor cells to make new tissue resistant to invasion. Notable adverse effects include headache and hepatotoxicity; it is contraindicated in porphyria and severe liver failure.
tail_desc: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a chronic multisystem autoimmune connective tissue disease in which loss of immune tolerance leads to autoantibody production and immune-complex–mediated inflammation affecting skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, serosa, and the nervous system. It commonly affects women of childbearing age and causes fatigue, rash, photosensitivity, arthritis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities; management uses sun protection, hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressive or biologic therapies tailored to organ involvement.

head: Proline (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Proline is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy is a paraneoplastic endocrine-metabolic disorder causing systemic calcium imbalance, most often in patients with solid tumors. It usually results from tumor secretion of parathyroid hormone-related protein, which increases bone resorption and renal calcium retention. Patients develop often severe hypercalcemia with dehydration, weakness, confusion, constipation, and polyuria; management focuses on urgent calcium lowering and treatment of the underlying cancer.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is a chronic inflammatory lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by persistent, usually progressive airflow limitation caused mainly by smoking-related small-airway disease and emphysema. Typical features include chronic cough, sputum production, dyspnea, wheeze, and exacerbations, usually in middle-aged or older adults with smoking or pollutant exposure. Management centers on smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, vaccinations, pulmonary rehabilitation, and oxygen in selected patients.

head: Methylphenobarbital (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Methylphenobarbital is a barbiturate anticonvulsant, structurally related to phenobarbital and used as a prodrug that is metabolized to phenobarbital. Its antiseizure effects are attributed to barbiturate-mediated enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity, prolonging chloride channel opening and depressing central nervous system excitability. It has been used for seizure disorders; notable adverse effects include sedation and other CNS depression.
tail_desc: Pulmonary valve insufficiency is a valvular heart disease of the cardiovascular system in which the pulmonary valve fails to close properly, allowing diastolic backflow from the pulmonary artery into the right ventricle. It is often secondary to pulmonary hypertension or congenital/postoperative valve abnormalities, may be asymptomatic or cause exertional dyspnea and right-sided heart failure, and is managed by treating the cause, monitoring right ventricular function, and selected valve repair or replacement.

head: Topiramate (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Topiramate is a sulfamate-substituted monosaccharide anticonvulsant that modulates voltage-gated sodium channels, enhances GABA-A–mediated inhibition, antagonizes AMPA/kainate glutamate receptors, and weakly inhibits carbonic anhydrase. It is used for epilepsy and migraine prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include cognitive slowing and metabolic acidosis; it is contraindicated with concomitant alcohol use in the extended-release formulation.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Metoclopramide (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Metoclopramide is a substituted benzamide antiemetic and prokinetic agent that primarily antagonizes dopamine D2 receptors, with additional 5-HT4 agonist and weak 5-HT3 antagonist activity, enhancing upper gastrointestinal motility and reducing nausea. It is used for gastroparesis and prevention or treatment of nausea and vomiting. A notable risk is tardive dyskinesia, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Inherited porphyria refers to a group of rare genetic metabolic disorders of heme biosynthesis, primarily affecting the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme deficiencies cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, leading to acute neurovisceral attacks and/or cutaneous photosensitivity. Presentation varies by subtype and often begins in adolescence or adulthood. Management includes trigger avoidance, subtype-specific supportive care, and in some acute hepatic forms hemin therapy.

head: Prazosin (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Prazosin is a quinazoline-derived selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist that blocks postsynaptic alpha-1 receptors, reducing peripheral vascular resistance and blood pressure. It is an established antihypertensive and is also used for symptomatic benign prostatic hyperplasia by relaxing smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck. A notable adverse effect is first-dose orthostatic hypotension, which may cause syncope.
tail_desc: Balo concentric sclerosis is a rare inflammatory demyelinating disorder of the central nervous system, usually regarded as a variant of multiple sclerosis. It is characterized pathologically by concentric rings of demyelination in cerebral white matter. Patients, often young adults, may present with acute focal neurologic deficits, headache, seizures, or cognitive changes; management generally follows other severe demyelinating attacks, especially high-dose corticosteroids, with escalation in refractory cases.

head: Procarbazine (a drug)
relation: contraindication: drug should not be used in patients with this condition due to safety risks.
head_desc: Procarbazine is an oral methylhydrazine alkylating-like antineoplastic agent that is metabolized to reactive intermediates causing DNA strand damage and inhibition of DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis; no single primary molecular target is established. It is used mainly in combination regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and some primary brain tumors. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression; it also has monoamine oxidase–inhibiting activity, creating important drug and tyramine-food interactions.
tail_desc: Peripheral nervous system disease comprises disorders affecting peripheral nerves, nerve roots, neuromuscular junctions, or peripheral ganglia outside the brain and spinal cord. Causes include inherited abnormalities, metabolic or immune-mediated injury, infection, trauma, and toxins. Typical features are distal weakness, sensory loss, pain, paresthesias, and reduced reflexes in children or adults. Management targets the cause, symptom control, rehabilitation, and prevention of complications.

head: Belotecan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Belotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. It stabilizes the topoisomerase I–DNA cleavable complex, preventing DNA religation and causing DNA strand breaks during replication. It has been used clinically, including for small cell lung cancer in some markets. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, particularly neutropenia.
tail_desc: Ovarian small cell carcinoma is a rare, highly aggressive ovarian malignancy, usually classified as a sex cord-stromal/germ-cell–unrelated epithelial-type tumor, most often affecting young women in the hypercalcemic type. It typically presents with a rapidly enlarging adnexal mass, abdominal pain, and frequent paraneoplastic hypercalcemia. Management generally involves aggressive surgical debulking with platinum-based chemotherapy, but prognosis is usually poor.

head: Chlorpropamide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chlorpropamide is a first-generation sulfonylurea antidiabetic that lowers blood glucose by binding the SUR1 subunit of ATP-sensitive potassium channels on pancreatic beta cells, promoting insulin release. It has been used for type 2 diabetes mellitus. A notable adverse effect is prolonged hypoglycemia, particularly in older adults or those with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder of glucose regulation in the endocrine system, characterized by insulin resistance and progressive pancreatic beta-cell dysfunction causing hyperglycemia. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity or sedentary lifestyle, and may present with polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and long-term vascular complications. Management focuses on weight loss, diet, exercise, glucose-lowering medications, and cardiovascular risk reduction.

head: Erythromycin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis by blocking translocation. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin, and soft-tissue infections, and as an alternative in some penicillin-allergic patients. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, which can increase the risk of serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Diphtheria is an acute bacterial infection of the upper respiratory tract, primarily caused by toxin-producing Corynebacterium diphtheriae. Its exotoxin induces local tissue necrosis and a characteristic gray pharyngeal pseudomembrane, with fever, sore throat, cervical lymphadenopathy, and potential airway obstruction; myocarditis and neuropathy may occur. It mainly affects unvaccinated children and adults, and management centers on prompt diphtheria antitoxin, antibiotics, isolation, and supportive airway care.

head: Talaporfin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Talaporfin is a chlorin-based photosensitizer used in photodynamic therapy. After activation by red light, it transfers energy to molecular oxygen, generating reactive oxygen species that damage cellular membranes and vasculature, causing local tumor cell death. It is established for photodynamic treatment of certain cancers, notably early lung cancer in some jurisdictions. A notable adverse effect is photosensitivity requiring avoidance of strong light exposure.
tail_desc: Lung cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the respiratory system arising from epithelial cells in the lung, most commonly non-small cell or small cell carcinoma. It is strongly linked to tobacco smoke, with additional risk from radon and occupational exposures. Typical features include persistent cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, dyspnea, and weight loss, usually in older adults. Management depends on stage and type, using surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy.

head: Moclobemide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Moclobemide is a benzamide antidepressant classified as a reversible inhibitor of monoamine oxidase A (RIMA), selectively blocking MAO-A to increase synaptic serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine. It is used for major depressive disorder and, in some countries, social anxiety disorder. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome when combined with serotonergic drugs; caution is also required in bipolar disorder.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Trovafloxacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Trovafloxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It was approved for treatment of certain serious bacterial infections, including community-acquired pneumonia and complicated intra-abdominal or gynecologic infections. Its use became severely restricted because of rare but potentially fatal hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Urinary tract infection is a common bacterial infection of the urinary system, affecting the bladder, urethra, and sometimes kidneys. It usually results from ascending uropathogens, especially Escherichia coli. Typical features include dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain, and sometimes fever or flank pain. It occurs across ages, especially in women, and is managed with appropriate antibiotics, hydration, and evaluation for complicating factors.

head: Butacaine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Butacaine is an ester-type local anesthetic in the benzoate family. Like other local anesthetics, it acts by reversibly blocking voltage-gated sodium channels in neuronal membranes, preventing initiation and conduction of nerve impulses.
