It has been used topically, particularly in ophthalmic anesthesia. As with related ester anesthetics, hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with known ester local anesthetic allergy.
tail_desc: Overactive bladder is a lower urinary tract disorder of the bladder characterized by urinary urgency, usually with frequency and nocturia, with or without urge urinary incontinence, in the absence of urinary tract infection or other obvious pathology. It is commonly linked to detrusor overactivity and is more frequent in older adults. Management typically includes bladder training, pelvic floor therapy, and antimuscarinic or beta-3 agonist medications.

head: Hydroxyzine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Hydroxyzine is a first-generation piperazine antihistamine that primarily acts as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist with central sedative effects. It is used for symptomatic relief of anxiety and tension, pruritus due to allergic conditions, and as a sedative or antiemetic adjunct. A notable adverse effect is marked drowsiness; it is also associated with QT prolongation and should be avoided in patients with known prolonged QT interval.
tail_desc: Familial dermatographia is a likely inherited form of dermatographic urticaria, a physical inducible urticaria affecting the skin. It is thought to reflect exaggerated mast-cell degranulation with histamine release after minor mechanical stroking or pressure. Patients, often with symptoms beginning in childhood or adolescence and a family history, develop transient linear wheals, erythema, and pruritus. Management is mainly trigger avoidance and non-sedating H1 antihistamines.

head: Metronidazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole antimicrobial and antiprotozoal prodrug that is reduced in anaerobic bacteria and certain protozoa to reactive radicals that damage DNA. It is used for anaerobic bacterial infections, bacterial vaginosis, Clostridioides difficile infection, and protozoal diseases such as trichomoniasis, giardiasis, and amebiasis. A notable adverse effect is a disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol.
tail_desc: Giardiasis is a protozoal infection of the small intestine caused by Giardia duodenalis (also called G. lamblia), typically acquired by ingesting contaminated water or food. It commonly causes acute or chronic foul-smelling diarrhea, steatorrhea, bloating, abdominal cramps, weight loss, and malabsorption, especially in children and travelers. Management centers on rehydration and antiparasitic therapy, with attention to sanitation and transmission prevention.

head: Quizartinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with t(8;21)(q22;q22) translocation (a disease)
head_desc: Quizartinib is an oral, selective type II receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor of FLT3, including FLT3-ITD mutant forms, used in acute myeloid leukemia with FLT3 internal tandem duplication. By blocking FLT3 signaling, it suppresses leukemic cell proliferation and survival. A notable toxicity is QT interval prolongation, requiring ECG and electrolyte monitoring and caution with other QT-prolonging drugs.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with t(8;21)(q22;q22) is a distinct subtype of AML affecting the bone marrow and blood, defined by the RUNX1-RUNX1T1 fusion from this translocation, which impairs myeloid differentiation. It often occurs in younger patients and may show maturation with blasts, anemia, infections, bleeding, and sometimes chloromas. Management typically follows intensive AML chemotherapy, with risk-adapted use of stem cell transplantation.

head: Rabeprazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Rabeprazole is a substituted benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, healing and maintenance of erosive esophagitis, duodenal ulcer, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori eradication. Notable adverse effects include headache and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to rabeprazole or other proton pump inhibitors.
tail_desc: Gastrin secretion abnormality is a disorder of gastrointestinal endocrine regulation in which gastrin production is abnormally increased or decreased, disrupting gastric acid secretion. Causes include gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumors, antral G-cell hyperplasia, or secondary changes from chronic atrophic gastritis. Patients may develop peptic ulcer disease, abdominal pain, diarrhea, or impaired digestion; management targets the underlying cause and control of acid-related complications.

head: Spironolactone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Spironolactone is a steroidal potassium-sparing diuretic and aldosterone antagonist that competitively blocks the mineralocorticoid receptor, reducing sodium and water retention while conserving potassium; it also has antiandrogen activity. Established uses include heart failure, hypertension, primary hyperaldosteronism, edema from cirrhosis or nephrotic syndrome, and prevention of hypokalemia. A notable adverse effect is hyperkalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria or significant renal impairment.
tail_desc: Bartter disease is a rare inherited renal tubular salt-wasting disorder affecting the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, usually caused by mutations in ion transport genes. It produces hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, elevated renin and aldosterone with normal or low blood pressure, polyuria, dehydration, and growth impairment, often beginning prenatally or in childhood. Management includes salt and potassium replacement, NSAIDs, and careful fluid-electrolyte monitoring.

head: Methdilazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methdilazine is a phenothiazine-derived first-generation H1 antihistamine with anticholinergic and sedative properties. It acts primarily as an antagonist at histamine H1 receptors. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions such as urticaria and pruritus. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma because of antimuscarinic effects.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Chenodeoxycholic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chenodeoxycholic acid is a primary bile acid used as a bile acid replacement and litholytic agent. It reduces hepatic cholesterol secretion and cholesterol saturation of bile, promoting dissolution of radiolucent cholesterol gallstones, and also suppresses bile acid synthesis via FXR-mediated feedback. Established uses include selected cholesterol gallstones and cerebrotendinous xanthomatosis. Notable adverse effects include diarrhea and hepatotoxicity; it is contraindicated in complete biliary obstruction.
tail_desc: Cholelithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, characterized by stone formation in the gallbladder, usually from cholesterol supersaturation or, less often, pigment precipitation. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic disease typically causes episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and is more common in adults, especially women. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic cases.

head: Indomethacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Indomethacin is an indoleacetic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for pain and inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute gout, and to promote closure of patent ductus arteriosus in neonates. Notable risks include serious gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding.
tail_desc: Tenosynovitis is a musculoskeletal inflammatory disorder of the tendon synovial sheath, usually caused by repetitive overuse, infection, or systemic inflammatory disease. It typically presents with localized pain, swelling, tenderness, and impaired tendon movement, often affecting the hands, wrists, or feet in adults. Management generally includes rest, splinting, anti-inflammatory therapy, treating the underlying cause, and sometimes corticosteroid injection or surgery.

head: Moxifloxacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Moxifloxacin is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including community-acquired pneumonia, acute bacterial sinusitis, acute exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and some skin or intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation; fluoroquinolones can also cause tendon rupture.
tail_desc: Bacterial pneumonia is an acute lower respiratory tract infection of the lungs in which bacteria infect the alveoli, causing inflammation and consolidation. It commonly presents with fever, cough, purulent sputum, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and sometimes hypoxemia, especially in young children, older adults, and people with chronic illness or impaired immunity. Management typically involves prompt antibiotic therapy, supportive care, and treatment of complications.

head: Pravastatin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pravastatin is a hydrophilic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used for hypercholesterolemia and prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular events. Notable adverse effects include myopathy/rhabdomyolysis, and it is contraindicated in active liver disease and during pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hypertriglyceridemia is a metabolic lipid disorder affecting the cardiovascular and pancreatic systems, defined by elevated plasma triglycerides from primary genetic defects or secondary causes such as diabetes, obesity, alcohol use, or medications. It is often asymptomatic but can cause eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, and acute pancreatitis, especially at very high levels. Management targets underlying causes, lifestyle change, and triglyceride-lowering therapy.

head: Arformoterol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Arformoterol is a long-acting beta2-adrenergic agonist (LABA), the (R,R)-enantiomer of formoterol, that relaxes bronchial smooth muscle by stimulating beta2 receptors and increasing intracellular cyclic AMP. It is approved as a nebulized maintenance bronchodilator for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, including chronic bronchitis and emphysema. A notable risk is paradoxical bronchospasm; LABAs are not indicated for acute bronchospasm relief.
tail_desc: Bronchitis is inflammation of the bronchi in the lower respiratory tract, most often acute and caused by viral infection, or chronic as part of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, usually related to smoking or irritant exposure. It commonly causes cough, sputum production, wheeze, chest discomfort, and mild dyspnea in children or adults. Management is mainly supportive for acute disease, while chronic bronchitis requires smoking cessation and COPD-directed therapy.

head: Voxelotor (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin (a disease)
head_desc: Voxelotor is an oral hemoglobin S polymerization inhibitor, a small-molecule sickle cell disease therapy that reversibly binds the N-terminal valine of the hemoglobin alpha chain, increasing hemoglobin oxygen affinity and reducing red blood cell sickling and hemolysis. It has been approved for sickle cell disease. Notable adverse effects include headache and diarrhea; use may interfere with some hemoglobin subtype measurements.
tail_desc: Hereditary persistence of fetal hemoglobin is a benign inherited hemoglobinopathy of the hematologic system caused by genetic variants that sustain gamma-globin expression and continued HbF production after birth. Affected individuals, often identified incidentally, are usually asymptomatic with normal or mild microcytic blood findings; the condition can lessen severity of sickle cell disease or beta-thalassemia. Management is typically reassurance, diagnosis confirmation, and genetic counseling.

head: Fluticasone propionate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluticasone propionate is a synthetic trifluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts as an agonist at intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory cytokines and mediator release. It is used for asthma maintenance, allergic rhinitis, and inflammatory dermatoses, depending on formulation. A notable adverse effect is oropharyngeal candidiasis with inhaled use.
tail_desc: Primary cutaneous T-cell lymphoma is a non-Hodgkin lymphoma of skin-homing mature T lymphocytes, arising primarily in the skin without initial extracutaneous disease. It typically affects adults and presents with persistent patches, plaques, tumors, or erythroderma, often with pruritus. Management depends on stage and subtype, commonly using skin-directed therapies for limited disease and systemic or biologic treatment for advanced disease.

head: Chloramphenicol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chloramphenicol is a broad-spectrum amphenicol antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocking peptidyl transferase activity. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including meningitis, rickettsial infections, and topical treatment of bacterial conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is dose-independent aplastic anemia; use is also limited by gray baby syndrome in neonates.
tail_desc: External ear disease comprises disorders of the outer ear within the auditory system, including the auricle and external auditory canal. It commonly reflects infection, inflammation, trauma, dermatologic conditions, or obstructive cerumen. Typical features include ear pain, itching, discharge, hearing reduction, swelling, or visible lesions, affecting both children and adults. Management targets the cause with canal cleaning, topical therapy, pain control, and avoidance of irritants.

head: Cefazolin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefazolin is a first-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It is widely used for susceptible gram-positive and some gram-negative infections and for perioperative surgical prophylaxis. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, including anaphylaxis, particularly in patients with cephalosporin or serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Cholangitis is an inflammatory, usually bacterial infection of the biliary tract within the hepatobiliary system, most often caused by bile duct obstruction such as gallstones or strictures. It typically presents in adults with fever, right upper quadrant pain, and jaundice, sometimes with sepsis. Management centers on urgent antibiotics, supportive care, and relief of biliary obstruction, often by endoscopic drainage.

head: Amlodipine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Amlodipine is a long-acting dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation and reducing blood pressure. It is established for hypertension, chronic stable angina, and vasospastic angina. A notable adverse effect is dose-related peripheral edema; it should be used cautiously in severe hypotension or cardiogenic shock.
tail_desc: Prinzmetal angina is a coronary vasospastic disorder of the cardiovascular system caused by transient spasm of an epicardial coronary artery, producing reversible myocardial ischemia without fixed obstructive disease in many cases. It typically causes recurrent rest angina, often nocturnal or early morning, with transient ST-segment elevation during attacks. Management centers on calcium channel blockers and nitrates, while avoiding triggers such as smoking.

head: Methyclothiazide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methyclothiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that acts in the distal convoluted tubule by inhibiting the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3), increasing sodium and water excretion. It has been used for hypertension and edema, including edema associated with heart failure or hepatic and renal disorders. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Congestive heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment reduces the heart’s ability to pump or fill effectively, causing pulmonary and/or systemic fluid congestion. It commonly affects older adults and patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy, producing dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance. Management focuses on treating the cause, diuresis, guideline-directed heart-failure medications, sodium/fluid control, and selected device or advanced therapies.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Granulomatous slack skin disease is a rare cutaneous T-cell lymphoma variant affecting the skin, considered within mycosis fungoides. It is characterized by granulomatous dermal infiltrates with atypical T cells, elastophagocytosis, and marked loss of elastic fibers, causing slowly progressive lax, pendulous skin folds, often in flexural areas, usually in adults. Management is individualized as for cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, using skin-directed or systemic therapy with long-term follow-up.

head: Trihexyphenidyl (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Trihexyphenidyl is a centrally acting antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) piperidine derivative used mainly for Parkinson disease and for drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms, especially acute dystonia and parkinsonism. It acts by competitively blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the CNS, helping restore dopaminergic-cholinergic balance in the basal ganglia. Notable adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, confusion, and worsening angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Early-onset parkinsonism-intellectual disability syndrome is a rare neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative movement disorder affecting the central nervous system. It is characterized by childhood or adolescent onset of parkinsonism associated with intellectual disability, often with developmental delay and additional neurologic abnormalities. The condition is considered genetically determined, and management is supportive and symptomatic, including multidisciplinary developmental care and treatment of parkinsonian features.

head: Ammonia (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ammonia is a small endogenous/basic nitrogenous compound rather than a conventional therapeutic drug. Medicinally, ammonia solution is used mainly as an inhalant respiratory stimulant in managing vasovagal syncope or near-fainting, acting by irritating nasal and respiratory mucosa to trigger reflex inhalation; it has no specific molecular drug target. It can cause mucosal irritation and should not be applied directly to eyes or damaged skin.
tail_desc: Pityriasis simplex is a mild, noninflammatory scaling disorder of the scalp and hair-bearing skin, commonly regarded as simple dandruff. It is characterized by fine, dry or slightly greasy white flakes with little or no erythema, often in adolescents and adults. Management is usually conservative, focusing on regular cleansing and antidandruff shampoos such as keratolytic or antifungal preparations when needed.

head: Glasdegib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with NPM1 somatic mutations (a disease)
head_desc: Glasdegib is an oral small-molecule hedgehog pathway inhibitor of the Smoothened (SMO) receptor. It is approved, in combination with low-dose cytarabine, for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia in adults aged 75 years or older or with comorbidities precluding intensive induction chemotherapy. A notable risk is embryo-fetal toxicity, making pregnancy a contraindication.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with NPM1 somatic mutations is a distinct myeloid neoplasm of the bone marrow and blood, driven by acquired NPM1 mutations that impair normal myeloid differentiation. It commonly presents in adults with cytopenias, fatigue, infections, bleeding, and increased marrow or blood blasts, often with normal karyotype. Management follows AML risk-adapted therapy, including induction chemotherapy and molecular monitoring for measurable residual disease.

head: Loteprednol etabonate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Loteprednol etabonate is a topical ophthalmic corticosteroid, a chloromethyl ester glucocorticoid, that binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to suppress phospholipase A2–dependent inflammatory mediator production and cytokine expression. It is used for steroid-responsive ocular inflammation, including postoperative inflammation and pain and allergic conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, so caution is warranted in patients with glaucoma.
tail_desc: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the facial skin, primarily affecting the pilosebaceous and vascular systems. Its cause is multifactorial and incompletely understood, involving dysregulated innate immunity, neurovascular reactivity, and environmental triggers. It commonly affects adults with central facial flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasia, papules or pustules, and sometimes ocular involvement; management centers on trigger avoidance and topical, oral, laser, or ocular therapies according to subtype and severity.

head: Fenofibrate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fenofibrate is a fibric acid derivative lipid-lowering agent that activates peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPAR-α), increasing lipolysis and triglyceride-rich lipoprotein clearance. It is used to treat severe hypertriglyceridemia and mixed dyslipidemia or primary hypercholesterolemia in selected patients. Notable adverse effects include elevated liver enzymes and myopathy, and it is contraindicated in severe hepatic or renal impairment and gallbladder disease.
tail_desc: obsolete familial combined hyperlipidemia is a disease or medical condition.

head: Propylthiouracil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Propylthiouracil is a thiourea antithyroid drug (thionamide) that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking iodination and coupling steps in thyroid hormone synthesis, and also inhibits peripheral conversion of thyroxine (T4) to triiodothyronine (T3). It is used to treat hyperthyroidism, including Graves disease and thyroid storm. A notable serious adverse effect is severe hepatotoxicity; agranulocytosis can also occur.
tail_desc: Thyroid crisis, or thyroid storm, is a life-threatening endocrine emergency affecting the cardiovascular and central nervous systems, caused by acute decompensation of severe thyrotoxicosis, usually in patients with uncontrolled hyperthyroidism and often triggered by infection, surgery, trauma, or iodine exposure. It presents with high fever, marked tachycardia or arrhythmia, agitation or delirium, gastrointestinal symptoms, and heart failure, requiring urgent intensive care, antithyroid therapy, beta-blockade, corticosteroids, and treatment of precipitating causes.

head: Silicon dioxide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Silicon dioxide is an inorganic silicon oxide used in pharmaceutical products mainly as an excipient, including glidant, anti-caking agent, and tablet/capsule formulation aid, rather than as an active therapeutic drug. It has no defined pharmacologic mechanism or primary molecular target in routine medicinal use and no established therapeutic indication as an active agent. Inhalational exposure to crystalline silica is associated with pulmonary toxicity, including silicosis.
tail_desc: Gastroesophageal reflux disease is a chronic upper gastrointestinal disorder in which reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus, usually from lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction, causes troublesome symptoms or mucosal injury. Typical features are heartburn and acid regurgitation, sometimes chest pain, cough, or dysphagia, affecting adults and children. Management emphasizes weight loss, trigger avoidance, acid suppression with proton pump inhibitors, and selected antireflux procedures.

head: Cefoxitin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefoxitin is a second-generation cephamycin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, urinary tract, skin and soft-tissue infections, and for perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Bacterial arthritis, or septic arthritis, is an acute infectious arthritis of the musculoskeletal system caused by bacterial invasion of a joint, leading to purulent synovial inflammation and rapid cartilage damage. It typically presents with sudden monoarticular pain, swelling, warmth, restricted movement, and fever, especially in children, older adults, or immunocompromised patients. Management generally requires urgent joint drainage and targeted intravenous antibiotics.

head: Fluorometholone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluorometholone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid used mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts as a glucocorticoid receptor agonist, altering gene transcription to suppress phospholipase A2–dependent mediator production and reduce ocular inflammation. It is indicated for steroid-responsive inflammatory conditions of the palpebral and bulbar conjunctiva, cornea, and anterior segment. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, so it is contraindicated in most active ocular viral, mycobacterial, or fungal infections.
tail_desc: Uveitis is an inflammatory disorder of the uveal tract of the eye, often involving adjacent ocular structures. It may result from autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or be idiopathic. Typical features include eye pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision, and floaters, affecting both adults and children. Management depends on cause and severity, commonly using corticosteroids, cycloplegics, and treatment of any underlying infectious or systemic condition.

head: Didanosine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Didanosine is a synthetic purine nucleoside analogue antiretroviral of the nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NRTI) class. Intracellularly converted to dideoxyadenosine triphosphate, it inhibits HIV reverse transcriptase and terminates viral DNA chain elongation. It has been used in combination therapy for HIV-1 infection. A notable serious adverse effect is pancreatitis; peripheral neuropathy can also occur.
tail_desc: HIV infectious disease is a chronic viral infection of the immune system caused by human immunodeficiency virus, which infects and depletes CD4+ T lymphocytes, leading to progressive immunodeficiency. Patients may be asymptomatic initially or develop acute febrile illness, lymphadenopathy, weight loss, recurrent infections, and opportunistic diseases. It affects all age groups and is managed with lifelong combination antiretroviral therapy and prevention of transmission and opportunistic infection.

head: Dihydrotachysterol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dihydrotachysterol is a synthetic vitamin D analog (secosteroid) used as a calcium-regulating agent. After hepatic activation, it acts via the vitamin D receptor to increase intestinal calcium absorption and help restore serum calcium. It has been used for hypocalcemia, particularly in hypoparathyroidism. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia, so it is contraindicated in patients with pre-existing hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Hypophosphatemia is an electrolyte/metabolic disorder of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, muscle, and neuromuscular function, caused by inadequate intestinal absorption, intracellular phosphate shifts, or excessive renal losses. Manifestations range from weakness, bone pain, and osteomalacia or rickets to severe respiratory, neurologic, or cardiac dysfunction. Management targets the cause and typically includes oral or intravenous phosphate replacement and correction of associated vitamin D deficiency.

head: Pilocarpine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pilocarpine is a direct-acting cholinergic agonist, an alkaloid muscarinic receptor agonist that primarily stimulates M3 receptors on exocrine glands and smooth muscle. It is used to treat xerostomia, including after head and neck radiotherapy and in Sjögren syndrome, and ophthalmically for glaucoma or miosis. A notable adverse effect is sweating; it is generally contraindicated in uncontrolled asthma.
tail_desc: Ocular hypertension is an ophthalmic condition characterized by persistently elevated intraocular pressure without detectable glaucomatous optic nerve damage or visual field loss. It results from impaired aqueous humor outflow, increasing risk of primary open-angle glaucoma. Patients are often asymptomatic and identified on routine eye examination, especially adults with risk factors. Management centers on pressure-lowering eye drops and regular monitoring of optic nerves, visual fields, and intraocular pressure.

head: Opipramol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Opipramol is a dibenzazepine derivative with tricyclic antidepressant–like structure, used mainly as an anxiolytic and antidepressant. Its pharmacology differs from classical tricyclics, acting primarily as a sigma receptor agonist, especially at sigma-1 and sigma-2 sites, with relatively weak monoamine reuptake inhibition. Established indications include generalized anxiety and somatoform disorders; common adverse effects include sedation and anticholinergic symptoms.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Heparin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Heparin is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, thereby inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa and reducing fibrin clot formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndromes, and during procedures such as hemodialysis. A notable adverse effect is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Purpura fulminans is a life-threatening thrombotic disorder of the skin and systemic microvasculature, usually associated with severe sepsis or congenital/acquired protein C pathway deficiency causing disseminated intravascular coagulation. It presents with rapidly progressive purpuric skin lesions that become hemorrhagic necrosis, often with shock and multiorgan failure, especially in neonates and critically ill children or adults. Management is urgent treatment of the underlying cause, intensive supportive care, and replacement/correction of coagulation abnormalities.

head: Cyclizine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cyclizine is a first-generation antihistamine of the piperazine class with antiemetic activity, acting primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist and also having anticholinergic effects. It is used for prevention and treatment of nausea, vomiting, and motion sickness, including postoperative nausea. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to the drug.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Quinapril (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Quinapril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug converted to quinaprilat, which inhibits ACE and reduces formation of angiotensin II while decreasing aldosterone secretion. It is used for hypertension and heart failure. A notable adverse effect is cough; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in patients with a history of ACE inhibitor–associated angioedema.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Deutetrabenazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Deutetrabenazine is a deuterated vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) inhibitor, structurally related to tetrabenazine, that reduces presynaptic packaging and release of dopamine and other monoamines. It is approved for chorea associated with Huntington disease and for tardive dyskinesia. Important adverse effects include somnolence and depression/suicidality risk; it is contraindicated in patients with untreated or inadequately treated depression in Huntington disease.
tail_desc: Tardive dyskinesia is a hyperkinetic movement disorder of the nervous system, usually caused by chronic exposure to dopamine receptor–blocking drugs, especially antipsychotics and some antiemetics, likely involving dopamine receptor supersensitivity in basal ganglia circuits. It causes persistent involuntary, repetitive movements, especially of the face, mouth, tongue, and sometimes trunk or limbs, mainly in patients receiving long-term neuroleptic therapy; management focuses on reducing offending drugs when feasible and using VMAT2 inhibitors.

head: Emedastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Emedastine is a second-generation antihistamine, commonly used as the difumarate salt in ophthalmic formulations. It acts primarily as a selective histamine H1-receptor antagonist, inhibiting histamine-mediated allergic responses. It is indicated for allergic conjunctivitis, especially relief of ocular itching. A notable adverse effect is transient ocular burning or stinging after instillation.
tail_desc: Vernal conjunctivitis, usually termed vernal keratoconjunctivitis, is a chronic, recurrent allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface affecting the conjunctiva and often cornea. It is driven by hypersensitivity mechanisms, typically in children or adolescents, especially boys in warm climates, and causes intense itching, photophobia, tearing, ropy mucus, and giant papillae. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and short supervised corticosteroid or calcineurin-inhibitor therapy when severe.

head: Lamotrigine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Lamotrigine is a phenyltriazine anticonvulsant and mood-stabilizing drug that primarily inhibits voltage-gated sodium channels, reducing presynaptic glutamate release. It is approved for epilepsy, including focal and generalized seizures, and for maintenance treatment of bipolar I disorder. A notable adverse effect is serious rash, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, with risk increased by rapid dose escalation or coadministration with valproate.
tail_desc: Partial epilepsy, now more often termed focal epilepsy, is a neurologic seizure disorder arising from abnormal electrical discharges in a localized region of one cerebral hemisphere. It can occur at any age and causes focal aware or impaired-awareness seizures, sometimes progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic seizures. Management typically involves antiseizure medications, with surgery or neurostimulation considered for drug-resistant focal epilepsy.

head: Fidaxomicin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fidaxomicin is a macrocyclic antibacterial agent that inhibits the sigma-dependent initiation of bacterial RNA synthesis by targeting RNA polymerase, with activity mainly against Clostridioides difficile. It is approved for treatment of C. difficile infection, including to reduce recurrence risk. A notable adverse effect is nausea; it is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to fidaxomicin.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Bromocriptine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bromocriptine is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist, primarily targeting dopamine D2 receptors to inhibit pituitary prolactin secretion and suppress growth hormone release. It is used for hyperprolactinemia, prolactin-secreting adenomas, acromegaly, and Parkinson disease; a quick-release form is also approved for type 2 diabetes. Notable adverse effects include nausea and orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension.
tail_desc: Hyperpituitarism is an endocrine disorder of the pituitary gland characterized by excessive secretion of one or more pituitary hormones, usually from a functioning pituitary adenoma or, less often, hyperplasia. Clinical features depend on the hormone excess, such as acromegaly/gigantism, hyperprolactinemia, or Cushing disease, and may include headaches or visual defects. Management targets the cause and hormone excess with surgery, medications, and sometimes radiotherapy.

head: Fluocinolone acetonide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluocinolone acetonide is a synthetic fluorinated glucocorticoid corticosteroid. It binds the intracellular glucocorticoid receptor (NR3C1), modulating gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune mediators. It is used topically or intravitreally for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses and certain ocular inflammatory conditions, including diabetic macular edema. Notable adverse effects include skin atrophy with topical use and increased intraocular pressure or cataract with ocular administration.
tail_desc: Discoid lupus erythematosus is a chronic cutaneous autoimmune form of lupus affecting the skin, characterized by inflammatory, photosensitive, coin-shaped plaques that often occur on sun-exposed areas, especially the face and scalp, and may heal with scarring, atrophy, dyspigmentation, and permanent alopecia. It is seen most often in adults, particularly women; management centers on photoprotection, topical or intralesional corticosteroids, and antimalarial therapy for more extensive disease.

head: Gemtuzumab ozogamicin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with t(8;21)(q22;q22) translocation (a disease)
head_desc: Gemtuzumab ozogamicin is an anti-CD33 antibody-drug conjugate composed of a humanized monoclonal antibody linked to the cytotoxic calicheamicin derivative N-acetyl gamma calicheamicin. It binds CD33 on leukemic blasts, is internalized, and releases calicheamicin to induce DNA double-strand breaks and cell death. It is approved for CD33-positive acute myeloid leukemia. A notable serious toxicity is hepatotoxicity, including veno-occlusive disease/sinusoidal obstruction syndrome.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with t(8;21)(q22;q22) is a distinct subtype of AML affecting the bone marrow and blood, defined by the RUNX1-RUNX1T1 fusion from this translocation, which impairs myeloid differentiation. It often occurs in younger patients and may show maturation with blasts, anemia, infections, bleeding, and sometimes chloromas. Management typically follows intensive AML chemotherapy, with risk-adapted use of stem cell transplantation.

head: Cetirizine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cetirizine is a second-generation piperazine antihistamine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is widely used for allergic rhinitis and chronic urticaria. Compared with first-generation antihistamines it is less sedating, but somnolence can still occur; caution is advised in severe renal impairment because the drug is substantially renally eliminated.
tail_desc: The common cold is an acute, self-limited viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, primarily affecting the nose and throat. It is caused by numerous respiratory viruses, especially rhinoviruses, producing mucosal inflammation. Typical features include nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, sore throat, cough, and malaise in children and adults. Management is supportive, with rest, fluids, and symptom relief rather than antibiotics.

head: Haloperidol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Haloperidol is a high-potency first-generation (typical) antipsychotic of the butyrophenone class that primarily acts by antagonizing central dopamine D2 receptors. It is used to treat schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, acute agitation, and tics in Tourette syndrome. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including dystonia, parkinsonism, and akathisia.
tail_desc: Psychotic disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting brain function and behavior, defined by impaired reality testing due to diverse causes, including primary psychotic illnesses, mood disorders, substance use, or medical conditions. Core features include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking or behavior, and reduced insight, usually emerging in adolescents or adults. Management focuses on diagnostic evaluation, treatment of underlying causes, antipsychotic medication, psychotherapy, and safety/supportive care.

head: Diltiazem (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Diltiazem is a non-dihydropyridine benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle, reducing AV nodal conduction, heart rate, and vasomotor tone. It is used for hypertension, angina, and rate control in supraventricular tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation or flutter. Notable adverse effects include bradycardia and hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe heart block without a pacemaker.
tail_desc: Atrial fibrillation is a common supraventricular cardiac arrhythmia of the cardiovascular system caused by disorganized atrial electrical activity, leading to ineffective atrial contraction and an irregularly irregular ventricular response. It often affects older adults and patients with hypertension, valvular, ischemic, or structural heart disease, causing palpitations, dyspnea, fatigue, or stroke. Management typically focuses on rate or rhythm control and anticoagulation to reduce thromboembolic risk.

head: Cefoxitin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefoxitin is a second-generation cephamycin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, urinary tract, skin and soft-tissue infections, and for perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Acute gonococcal endometritis is an acute sexually transmitted infection of the female upper genital tract in which Neisseria gonorrhoeae infects the endometrial lining of the uterus, causing suppurative inflammation. It typically affects sexually active women and may present with pelvic or lower abdominal pain, abnormal uterine bleeding, purulent cervical discharge, fever, and uterine tenderness. Management is prompt antibiotic treatment for gonorrhea and evaluation for concurrent pelvic inflammatory disease.

head: Bupropion (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bupropion is an aminoketone antidepressant and smoking-cessation agent that inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and dopamine, with minimal direct serotonergic activity; nicotinic acetylcholine receptor antagonism may also contribute. It is approved for major depressive disorder, seasonal affective disorder, and smoking cessation. A notable risk is seizures, so it is contraindicated in patients with seizure disorders or eating disorders.
tail_desc: Endogenous depression is an older psychiatric term for a severe mood disorder within major depressive illness, affecting the central nervous system and characterized by depressive episodes thought to arise primarily from internal biological vulnerability rather than obvious external stressors. Typical features include pervasive low mood, anhedonia, psychomotor change, sleep and appetite disturbance, guilt, and suicidality, often in adults; management follows major depression treatment with antidepressants, psychotherapy, and, when severe, electroconvulsive therapy.

head: Sulfisoxazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sulfisoxazole is a short-acting sulfonamide antibacterial. It inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby suppressing growth of susceptible organisms. It has been used for treatment of susceptible urinary tract infections, otitis media, and some other bacterial infections. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe sulfonamide reactions such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Nocardiosis is an opportunistic bacterial infection caused by aerobic, filamentous Nocardia species, classically affecting the lungs with potential dissemination to the brain and skin. It occurs most often in immunocompromised patients, though cutaneous disease can follow traumatic inoculation in immunocompetent people. Typical features include pneumonia, nodules or abscesses, and management relies on prolonged targeted antibiotic therapy, often trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, with drainage when needed.

head: Opicapone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Opicapone is a peripherally acting, third-generation nitrocatechol catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) inhibitor that blocks peripheral COMT, prolonging levodopa exposure and central dopaminergic benefit. It is approved as adjunctive therapy to levodopa/dopa-decarboxylase inhibitor regimens in Parkinson’s disease patients with end-of-dose motor fluctuations. A notable adverse effect is dyskinesia, and it is contraindicated in pheochromocytoma or other catecholamine-secreting tumors.
tail_desc: Hemiparkinsonism-hemiatrophy syndrome is a rare neurologic movement disorder characterized by unilateral body or limb hemiatrophy with ipsilateral hemiparkinsonism. Its cause is incompletely understood, often linked to early-life cerebral injury or developmental asymmetry affecting basal ganglia circuits. Symptoms usually begin in childhood or early adulthood with asymmetric rigidity, bradykinesia, dystonia, and postural abnormalities. Management is symptomatic, including dopaminergic therapy and supportive rehabilitation.

head: Benzoyl peroxide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Benzoyl peroxide is a topical organic peroxide used mainly as an anti-acne agent. It decomposes in the skin to release free-radical oxygen species, producing bactericidal activity against Cutibacterium acnes and mild keratolytic/comedolytic effects. It is established for acne vulgaris treatment, alone or with other agents. Common adverse effects include local skin irritation, dryness, erythema, and peeling.
tail_desc: Contact dermatitis is an inflammatory skin disorder caused by direct exposure to irritants or allergens, producing epidermal barrier disruption and, in allergic cases, a delayed hypersensitivity reaction. It presents with pruritic, erythematous, sometimes vesicular or scaling lesions confined to contact sites, affecting all ages. Management centers on identifying and avoiding triggers, skin protection, emollients, and topical corticosteroids for flares.

head: Ruxolitinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ruxolitinib is an oral small-molecule Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitor, targeting primarily JAK1 and JAK2 to reduce dysregulated JAK-STAT signaling and inflammatory cytokine activity. It is approved for myelofibrosis, polycythemia vera, and steroid-refractory acute or chronic graft-versus-host disease. A notable adverse effect is dose-related anemia and thrombocytopenia, reflecting its myelosuppressive potential.
tail_desc: acquired polycythemia vera is a disease or medical condition.

head: Imipenem (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Imipenem is a carbapenem beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing broad-spectrum bactericidal activity. It is used, typically with cilastatin, for serious susceptible bacterial infections including intra-abdominal, lower respiratory, urinary tract, skin/soft tissue, and gynecologic infections. A notable adverse effect is increased seizure risk, especially in patients with CNS disorders or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Endocarditis is an inflammatory, usually infectious disease of the endocardium, most often involving heart valves in the cardiovascular system. It commonly results from bacteremia or, less often, fungi seeding damaged or prosthetic valves. Typical features include fever, new or changing murmur, embolic or immunologic phenomena, especially in patients with valvular disease, prosthetic valves, or injection drug use; management centers on prolonged targeted antimicrobial therapy and sometimes surgery.

head: Homatropine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Homatropine is a belladonna alkaloid derivative and antimuscarinic (parasympatholytic) agent that competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, producing mydriasis and cycloplegia and reducing secretions. It is used ophthalmically for short-term pupil dilation and relief of pain/inflammation in uveitis or iridocyclitis; as the methylbromide salt it has been used in gastrointestinal antispasmodic combinations. Adverse effects include increased intraocular pressure, so it is contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Constipation disorder is a gastrointestinal motility and defecation disorder characterized by infrequent bowel movements, hard or difficult-to-pass stools, and a sensation of incomplete evacuation. It commonly results from slow colonic transit, pelvic floor dysfunction, medications, diet, or systemic illness, and affects all ages, especially older adults. Management typically emphasizes hydration, fiber, exercise, treating causes, and use of laxatives or pelvic floor therapy.

head: Clofedanol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Clofedanol is a centrally acting non-opioid antitussive (cough suppressant), structurally classified as a diphenylmethane derivative. Its precise molecular target and mechanism are not well established, but it suppresses the cough reflex via central action. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of nonproductive cough. Notable adverse effects include drowsiness and dizziness.
tail_desc: Nasopharyngitis is an acute upper respiratory tract inflammation of the nasal passages and pharynx, usually caused by viral infection. It commonly presents with rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, sore throat, sneezing, and sometimes cough or low-grade fever, especially in children and adults with common colds. Management is typically supportive, including hydration, rest, and symptom relief; antibiotics are generally not indicated unless bacterial infection is suspected.

head: Dextroamphetamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dextroamphetamine is a central nervous system stimulant of the amphetamine class. It increases synaptic norepinephrine and dopamine chiefly by promoting presynaptic release via monoamine transporters, including DAT and NET. It is established for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and narcolepsy. Notable adverse effects include insomnia, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Central nervous system disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the brain and spinal cord, arising from diverse mechanisms including congenital, vascular, infectious, inflammatory, degenerative, neoplastic, traumatic, or toxic injury. Clinical features vary but may include headache, seizures, weakness, sensory loss, cognitive or behavioral change, gait disturbance, or altered consciousness, in children or adults. Management depends on cause and typically combines etiologic treatment, symptom control, and rehabilitation.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: B-cell neoplasm is a broad category of lymphoid malignancies arising from clonal proliferation of B lymphocytes within the hematologic and lymphatic systems. It includes leukemias, lymphomas, and plasma cell neoplasms, often presenting with lymphadenopathy, bone marrow involvement, cytopenias, or constitutional symptoms, mainly in adults. Management depends on subtype and stage, and may include observation, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted agents, radiation, or stem cell transplantation.

head: Fluocinonide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluocinonide is a high-potency synthetic fluorinated topical corticosteroid. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory cytokines and reduce vasodilation, edema, and pruritus. It is used for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses, including eczema, psoriasis, and allergic or inflammatory skin rashes. Notable adverse effects include skin atrophy; prolonged use can also cause hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression.
tail_desc: Chronic cutaneous lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune connective tissue disease primarily affecting the skin, characterized by chronic interface dermatitis with photosensitive, scarring plaques, often on sun-exposed areas such as the face and scalp. It commonly affects adults, especially women. Management focuses on photoprotection, topical or intralesional corticosteroids, antimalarials such as hydroxychloroquine, and smoking cessation.

head: Clarithromycin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Clarithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible respiratory tract and skin infections and, in combination regimens, Helicobacter pylori infection. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation with potentially serious arrhythmias, so it is contraindicated with certain interacting drugs such as cisapride or pimozide.
tail_desc: Staphylococcal infection is a bacterial infection caused by Staphylococcus species, especially S. aureus, affecting skin and soft tissue, blood, lungs, bone, or implanted devices. It typically results from colonization followed by invasion and inflammation, causing boils, cellulitis, abscesses, pneumonia, bacteremia, or sepsis, particularly in hospitalized, immunocompromised, or device-bearing patients. Management involves drainage when needed and targeted antistaphylococcal antibiotics guided by culture and resistance patterns.

head: Fimasartan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fimasartan is a nonpeptide angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB) of the biphenyl-tetrazole class that selectively antagonizes the AT1 receptor, thereby reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone-mediated effects. It is approved for the treatment of hypertension. A notable adverse effect is dizziness related to hypotension, and it is contraindicated during pregnancy because of fetal toxicity risk.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Liothyronine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Liothyronine is a synthetic form of triiodothyronine (T3), a thyroid hormone replacement that acts by binding nuclear thyroid hormone receptors (TRα and TRβ) to regulate gene transcription and metabolic activity. It is used to treat hypothyroidism and may also be used in myxedema coma or TSH suppression in selected thyroid disorders. Excess dosing can cause thyrotoxicosis, including tachyarrhythmias or angina.
tail_desc: Differentiated thyroid carcinoma is a malignant epithelial neoplasm of the thyroid gland, comprising papillary and follicular cancers derived from follicular cells. It typically presents as a thyroid nodule, cervical lymphadenopathy, or incidental imaging finding, often in adults and especially women. Management usually involves risk-stratified surgery, selected radioactive iodine, thyroid-stimulating hormone suppression, and long-term surveillance.

head: Fluorometholone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluorometholone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid used mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts as a glucocorticoid receptor agonist, altering gene transcription to suppress phospholipase A2–dependent mediator production and reduce ocular inflammation. It is indicated for steroid-responsive inflammatory conditions of the palpebral and bulbar conjunctiva, cornea, and anterior segment. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, so it is contraindicated in most active ocular viral, mycobacterial, or fungal infections.
tail_desc: Corneal ulcer is an ophthalmic disorder of the anterior eye in which an epithelial defect with underlying stromal inflammation and tissue loss affects the cornea, most often from microbial infection, trauma, contact lens wear, or severe ocular surface disease. It typically causes eye pain, redness, photophobia, tearing, and reduced vision, and requires urgent ophthalmologic evaluation with cause-directed therapy, especially topical antimicrobials, plus management of predisposing factors.

head: Magnesium oxide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Magnesium oxide is an inorganic magnesium salt and mineral supplement that acts as an antacid and osmotic laxative; it neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid and, after conversion to magnesium salts in the gut, retains water in the intestinal lumen, without a specific receptor target. It is used for magnesium replacement, relief of acid indigestion, and short-term constipation; adverse effects include diarrhea, and it is contraindicated in significant renal impairment because of hypermagnesemia risk.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system caused primarily by estrogen deficiency after menopause, which accelerates bone resorption and reduces bone mass and microarchitectural strength. It mainly affects older postmenopausal women and is often silent until fragility fractures occur, especially in the vertebrae, hip, or wrist. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Interferon alfa-2b (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Interferon alfa-2b is a recombinant type I interferon cytokine that binds the interferon-α/β receptor (IFNAR1/IFNAR2), activating JAK-STAT signaling and inducing antiviral, antiproliferative, and immunomodulatory gene expression. It has established use in chronic hepatitis B and C and certain cancers, including hairy cell leukemia and melanoma. Notable adverse effects include flu-like symptoms and depression; severe psychiatric illness is an important contraindication.
tail_desc: Kaposi’s sarcoma is an angioproliferative vascular neoplasm caused by human herpesvirus 8, affecting skin, mucous membranes, lymph nodes, and sometimes visceral organs. It typically presents as violaceous patches, plaques, or nodules, especially in people with HIV/AIDS, other immunosuppression, or classic endemic forms. Management centers on restoring immune function, reducing immunosuppression when feasible, and local or systemic anticancer therapy for symptomatic or widespread disease.

head: Esmolol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Esmolol is an ultra-short-acting cardioselective beta-blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class that primarily antagonizes beta-1 adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, contractility, and AV nodal conduction. It is used intravenously for rapid control of supraventricular tachycardia and perioperative or emergency hypertension/tachycardia. A notable risk is hypotension; it is contraindicated in severe bradycardia or advanced heart block.
tail_desc: His bundle tachycardia is a rare supraventricular arrhythmia of the cardiac conduction system arising from the His bundle, usually due to enhanced automaticity or triggered activity in this tissue. It is reported mainly in infants and children and may cause persistent narrow-complex tachycardia, palpitations, heart failure, or tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. Management typically centers on antiarrhythmic therapy, with catheter ablation considered in refractory cases.

head: Cefotetan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefotetan is a parenteral second-generation cephalosporin (cephamycin) beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, skin/soft tissue, urinary tract, and perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia/bleeding risk, and disulfiram-like reactions can occur with alcohol.
tail_desc: Streptococcal infection is a bacterial infectious disease caused by Streptococcus species, commonly affecting the throat, skin, soft tissues, or invasive systemic sites. Presentations vary from pharyngitis and impetigo to cellulitis, scarlet fever, or sepsis, occurring in both children and adults. Management depends on site and severity, typically involving appropriate antibiotics, supportive care, and drainage or urgent treatment for invasive disease.

head: Trimethadione (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Trimethadione is an oxazolidinedione anticonvulsant used primarily for refractory absence (petit mal) seizures. Its active metabolite, dimethadione, reduces thalamic low-threshold calcium currents by blocking T-type calcium channels, suppressing the cortical rhythm underlying absence seizures. A notable adverse effect is severe teratogenicity, so it is generally contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Absence epilepsy is a generalized epilepsy of the central nervous system characterized by brief, sudden lapses of awareness caused by abnormal thalamocortical electrical activity, often without convulsions. It typically begins in childhood and presents with frequent staring spells, behavioral arrest, and sometimes eyelid fluttering. Management usually involves antiseizure medication, commonly ethosuximide, valproate, or lamotrigine.

head: Daunorubicin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Daunorubicin is an anthracycline antineoplastic antibiotic used mainly to treat acute leukemias, especially acute myeloid leukemia and acute lymphoblastic leukemia. It intercalates into DNA, inhibits topoisomerase II, and generates free radicals, thereby impairing DNA replication and transcription. A notable toxicity is cumulative dose-related cardiomyopathy/heart failure, making use contraindicated or limited in patients with significant cardiac dysfunction.
tail_desc: Unclassified acute myeloid leukemia is a rare acute leukemia of the hematopoietic system in which myeloid blasts proliferate in bone marrow and blood but do not meet criteria for established AML subtypes. Patients, usually adults, commonly present with marrow failure causing anemia, infections, and bleeding. Management generally follows acute myeloid leukemia protocols, with risk-adapted chemotherapy and consideration of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Hydroxyurea (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Hydroxyurea is an antimetabolite hydroxylated urea derivative that inhibits ribonucleotide reductase, reducing deoxyribonucleotide synthesis and thereby suppressing DNA replication; it also increases fetal hemoglobin in sickle cell disease. It is used for myeloproliferative disorders and some leukemias, and to reduce painful crises in sickle cell disease. A notable adverse effect is dose-related myelosuppression, so it is contraindicated in severe bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome is a hereditary cancer predisposition affecting the female reproductive system, characterized by familial clustering of ovarian cancer without the broader tumor spectrum seen in other syndromes. It likely reflects inherited susceptibility, often involving germline defects in DNA repair genes such as BRCA1 or BRCA2. Management centers on genetic counseling, surveillance, and consideration of risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy.

head: Amikacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Amikacin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that irreversibly binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing bactericidal activity. It is used for serious infections due to susceptible Gram-negative bacteria, including complicated urinary tract, intra-abdominal, respiratory, and bloodstream infections, and has activity against some mycobacteria. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Bacterial arthritis, or septic arthritis, is an acute infectious arthritis of the musculoskeletal system caused by bacterial invasion of a joint, leading to purulent synovial inflammation and rapid cartilage damage. It typically presents with sudden monoarticular pain, swelling, warmth, restricted movement, and fever, especially in children, older adults, or immunocompromised patients. Management generally requires urgent joint drainage and targeted intravenous antibiotics.

head: Cyproheptadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cyproheptadine is a first-generation antihistamine of the piperidine class with additional antiserotonergic and anticholinergic activity. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist and also blocks serotonin 5-HT2 receptors. It is used for allergic conditions such as allergic rhinitis and urticaria, and commonly causes marked sedation; it is contraindicated in newborns and during breastfeeding.
tail_desc: Angioedema is a vascular permeability disorder of the skin and mucosa causing sudden, localized swelling, often involving the face, lips, tongue, extremities, or airway. It results from mast cell mediator release or bradykinin excess, and may be allergic, medication-induced, or hereditary. Patients present with episodic nonpitting edema, sometimes with urticaria or life-threatening airway compromise; management targets airway protection and treating the underlying mechanism.

head: Fluoxymesterone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluoxymesterone is a synthetic 17-alpha-alkylated androgenic anabolic steroid and testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, altering transcription of androgen-responsive genes. It has been used for androgen replacement in male hypogonadism and delayed puberty, and palliatively in certain breast cancers. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice; it is contraindicated in prostate carcinoma and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Eunuchism is an older term for male hypogonadism involving deficient testicular function in the endocrine and reproductive systems, often due to congenital or acquired absence, damage, or failure of the testes before puberty. It typically causes delayed or absent puberty, sparse body hair, underdeveloped genitalia, infertility, and reduced secondary sexual characteristics. Management focuses on identifying the cause and testosterone replacement when appropriate.

head: Salmeterol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Salmeterol is a long-acting beta2-adrenergic receptor agonist (LABA) bronchodilator of the phenethylamine class. It selectively stimulates beta2 receptors in airway smooth muscle, increasing cyclic AMP and producing prolonged bronchodilation. It is used for maintenance treatment of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, but not for acute bronchospasm. A notable adverse effect is tachycardia; LABA monotherapy is contraindicated in asthma because it increases the risk of asthma-related death.
tail_desc: Allergic asthma is a common phenotype of asthma, a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways in the respiratory system, driven by allergen-triggered, IgE-associated immune responses with airway hyperresponsiveness and variable airflow obstruction. It often affects children and atopic individuals, causing episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often after allergen exposure. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and standard asthma therapy, especially inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.

head: Cyclophosphamide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cyclophosphamide is an oxazaphosphorine alkylating agent (nitrogen mustard prodrug) that is hepatically activated to metabolites forming DNA cross-links, thereby impairing DNA replication and inducing cell death without a single specific molecular target. It is used for multiple malignancies, including lymphomas, leukemias, and breast cancer, and as an immunosuppressant in severe autoimmune disease. A notable adverse effect is hemorrhagic cystitis.
tail_desc: Localized pagetoid reticulosis is a rare, indolent variant of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma affecting the skin, characterized by marked epidermotropism of neoplastic T lymphocytes. It typically presents in adults as a solitary, slowly enlarging, hyperkeratotic or psoriasiform plaque, often on distal extremities. Management is usually skin-directed, with local excision or radiotherapy, and prognosis is generally favorable.

head: Aminobenzoic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Aminobenzoic acid, usually referring to para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), is a benzoic acid derivative and B-complex–related compound rather than a standard targeted drug. It has no well-defined therapeutic molecular target; historically, potassium para-aminobenzoate has been used clinically for Peyronie’s disease and certain fibrotic dermatoses. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal intolerance, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to PABA-related compounds.
tail_desc: Localized scleroderma (morphea) is an autoimmune fibrosing disorder primarily affecting the skin and sometimes underlying subcutaneous tissue, fascia, muscle, or bone, without the internal organ involvement typical of systemic sclerosis. It causes localized indurated, sclerotic plaques or linear bands, often in children or middle-aged adults. Management aims to limit inflammation and fibrosis, using topical therapies, phototherapy, or systemic immunosuppression for deeper or progressive disease.

head: Mannitol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mannitol is a sugar alcohol (polyol) used as an osmotic diuretic. It is freely filtered at the glomerulus and not substantially reabsorbed, increasing tubular fluid osmolality to promote water excretion; it also raises plasma osmolality, drawing water from tissues, without a specific receptor target. It is used to reduce intracranial or intraocular pressure and to promote diuresis; pulmonary edema can occur, and it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Pseudotumor cerebri, or idiopathic intracranial hypertension, is a neurologic disorder characterized by elevated intracranial pressure without an intracranial mass or hydrocephalus, likely related to impaired cerebrospinal fluid absorption. It typically affects obese women of childbearing age and causes headache, transient visual obscurations, pulsatile tinnitus, and papilledema. Management focuses on weight loss, acetazolamide, and vision-preserving procedures when severe.

head: Nicotinyl alcohol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Nicotinyl alcohol is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Meniere disease is a chronic inner ear disorder of the vestibular and auditory systems, classically associated with endolymphatic hydrops.
