It causes recurrent episodes of spontaneous vertigo, fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness, usually in adults and often affecting one ear initially. Management is symptomatic and preventive, including salt restriction, diuretics, vestibular suppressants during attacks, and selected intratympanic or surgical therapies in refractory cases.

head: Ursodeoxycholic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ursodeoxycholic acid is a hydrophilic secondary bile acid used as a choleretic agent. It reduces biliary cholesterol saturation and promotes bile flow, helping dissolve cholesterol gallstones and protect cholangiocytes; no single primary molecular target is established clinically. It is used for primary biliary cholangitis and selected cholesterol gallstones. Diarrhea is a notable adverse effect; complete biliary obstruction is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Cholecystolithiasis is gallstone disease of the biliary system, specifically stones within the gallbladder, usually formed by precipitation of cholesterol or pigment in bile. Many cases are asymptomatic; symptomatic patients develop episodic right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, sometimes with nausea, and complications can include cholecystitis or biliary obstruction. Management ranges from observation for asymptomatic stones to laparoscopic cholecystectomy for symptomatic disease.

head: Nelarabine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Nelarabine is a purine nucleoside antimetabolite and prodrug of arabinosylguanine (ara-G). It is converted intracellularly to ara-GTP, which is incorporated into DNA and inhibits DNA synthesis, causing cytotoxicity particularly in T lymphoblasts. It is approved for relapsed or refractory T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia and T-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma. A notable toxicity is severe neurotoxicity, including peripheral and central neurologic effects.
tail_desc: Acute lymphoblastic/lymphocytic leukemia is an acute hematologic malignancy of the blood and bone marrow caused by clonal proliferation of immature lymphoid precursor cells, impairing normal hematopoiesis. It is most common in children but also occurs in adults, presenting with fatigue, pallor, fever, infections, bleeding, bone pain, and lymphadenopathy. Management typically involves multiagent chemotherapy, central nervous system prophylaxis, targeted therapy in selected cases, and sometimes stem cell transplantation.

head: Olopatadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Olopatadine is a second-generation antihistamine and antiallergic agent, chemically a dibenzoxepin derivative, that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors and also inhibits release of inflammatory mediators from mast cells. It is used mainly for allergic conjunctivitis and allergic rhinitis. A notable adverse effect is headache; hypersensitivity to olopatadine is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Acetylsalicylic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin) is a salicylate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug that irreversibly acetylates cyclooxygenase-1 and -2, inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis and thromboxane A2–mediated platelet aggregation. It is used for pain, fever, inflammatory conditions, and secondary prevention of myocardial infarction and ischemic stroke. A notable risk is gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding; it is generally contraindicated in active bleeding disorders.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Tromethamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tromethamine (tris, THAM) is an alkalinizing agent and amino alcohol buffer used to treat metabolic acidosis, particularly when sodium load or carbon dioxide generation from bicarbonate is undesirable. It acts by proton acceptance and buffering rather than a specific receptor target, increasing blood pH. Important risks include respiratory depression and hypoglycemia; it is contraindicated in uremia and anuria.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Delavirdine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Delavirdine is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor (NNRTI) of the bisheteroarylpiperazine class used as part of combination antiretroviral therapy for HIV-1 infection. It inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase by binding a noncompetitive allosteric site, thereby blocking viral DNA synthesis. A notable adverse effect is rash, which can be severe; it is also prone to clinically important CYP3A-mediated drug interactions.
tail_desc: HIV infectious disease is a chronic viral infection of the immune system caused by human immunodeficiency virus, which infects and depletes CD4+ T lymphocytes, leading to progressive immunodeficiency. Patients may be asymptomatic initially or develop acute febrile illness, lymphadenopathy, weight loss, recurrent infections, and opportunistic diseases. It affects all age groups and is managed with lifelong combination antiretroviral therapy and prevention of transmission and opportunistic infection.

head: Saxagliptin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Saxagliptin is an oral dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor used to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus. By inhibiting DPP-4, it prolongs incretin activity, increasing glucose-dependent insulin secretion and reducing glucagon release. A notable safety concern is risk of heart failure hospitalization, and it is not indicated for type 1 diabetes or diabetic ketoacidosis.
tail_desc: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder of glucose regulation in the endocrine system, characterized by insulin resistance and progressive pancreatic beta-cell dysfunction causing hyperglycemia. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity or sedentary lifestyle, and may present with polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and long-term vascular complications. Management focuses on weight loss, diet, exercise, glucose-lowering medications, and cardiovascular risk reduction.

head: Mezlocillin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mezlocillin is a broad-spectrum acylureidopenicillin (beta-lactam) antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible infections including urinary tract, respiratory, intra-abdominal, and biliary tract infections, with activity against some Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Gonorrhea is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection of the genitourinary tract, rectum, pharynx, or conjunctiva caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae. It often causes urethritis with dysuria and purulent discharge, cervicitis, pelvic pain, or may be asymptomatic, especially in women; sexually active adolescents and adults are commonly affected. Management centers on recommended antibiotic therapy, testing for coinfections, partner treatment, and prevention of transmission.

head: Testosterone cypionate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Testosterone cypionate is a long-acting injectable testosterone ester, an androgen replacement therapy. It is hydrolyzed to testosterone, which binds and activates the androgen receptor to regulate gene transcription in androgen-responsive tissues. It is used for male hypogonadism and delayed puberty, and in certain women with metastatic breast cancer. Notable risks include erythrocytosis and contraindication in men with prostate or breast cancer.
tail_desc: Male hypogonadism is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and testes, characterized by deficient testosterone production and/or impaired spermatogenesis from primary testicular failure or secondary hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction. It causes delayed puberty, reduced libido, erectile dysfunction, infertility, decreased muscle and bone mass, and fatigue in adolescent or adult males. Management targets the cause and may include testosterone replacement or fertility-directed gonadotropin therapy.

head: Acrivastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acrivastine is a second-generation antihistamine of the alkylamine/propylamine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used for symptomatic relief of seasonal allergic rhinitis and urticaria, including sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itching. Compared with older antihistamines it is less sedating, but drowsiness can still occur; caution is advised in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Ceftazidime (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ceftazidime is a third-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall synthesis and cause cell lysis. It is established for susceptible Gram-negative infections, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, such as pneumonia, urinary tract, skin, intra-abdominal, and bloodstream infections, as well as meningitis. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions in patients with beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Bacterial arthritis, or septic arthritis, is an acute infectious arthritis of the musculoskeletal system caused by bacterial invasion of a joint, leading to purulent synovial inflammation and rapid cartilage damage. It typically presents with sudden monoarticular pain, swelling, warmth, restricted movement, and fever, especially in children, older adults, or immunocompromised patients. Management generally requires urgent joint drainage and targeted intravenous antibiotics.

head: Fluorouracil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluorouracil (5-FU) is a fluorinated pyrimidine antimetabolite and uracil analogue used as an antineoplastic agent. It is converted intracellularly to active metabolites that inhibit thymidylate synthase and can also be incorporated into RNA and DNA, disrupting nucleic acid synthesis. It is established for colorectal, breast, gastric, pancreatic, and head and neck cancers. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression, especially in patients with dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Colorectal cancer is a malignant epithelial neoplasm of the colon or rectum within the gastrointestinal system, usually arising from adenomatous polyps through accumulation of genetic and epigenetic alterations. It commonly affects older adults and may cause rectal bleeding, altered bowel habits, abdominal pain, iron-deficiency anemia, and weight loss. Management typically involves screening, surgical resection, stage-directed chemotherapy, radiation for rectal cancer, and targeted or immunotherapy in selected cases.

head: Ferrous gluconate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ferrous gluconate is an oral iron salt, a ferrous (Fe2+) gluconate preparation used as a hematinic. It replenishes body iron stores and supplies elemental iron for hemoglobin and other iron-containing proteins; it does not act via a single specific receptor target. It is used to prevent and treat iron deficiency anemia. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation, especially constipation, nausea, and dark stools.
tail_desc: Hypochromic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by red blood cells with reduced hemoglobin content, typically due to impaired hemoglobin synthesis, most often from iron deficiency, but also seen in thalassemias and some chronic diseases. Patients commonly present with pallor, fatigue, weakness, and dyspnea. Management targets the underlying cause, such as iron replacement, dietary correction, or treatment of associated disease.

head: Quizartinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with t(6;9)(p23;q34) (a disease)
head_desc: Quizartinib is an oral, selective type II receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor of FLT3, including FLT3-ITD mutant forms, used in acute myeloid leukemia with FLT3 internal tandem duplication. By blocking FLT3 signaling, it suppresses leukemic cell proliferation and survival. A notable toxicity is QT interval prolongation, requiring ECG and electrolyte monitoring and caution with other QT-prolonging drugs.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with t(6;9)(p23;q34) is a rare, high-risk AML of the hematopoietic system defined by the DEK::NUP214 fusion from t(6;9). It often affects children and younger adults, commonly presents with marrow failure symptoms, basophilia, and frequent FLT3-ITD co-mutation, and carries an adverse prognosis. Management follows intensive AML induction/consolidation, FLT3-directed therapy when indicated, and often allogeneic stem-cell transplantation in first remission.

head: Modafinil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Modafinil is a wakefulness-promoting agent of the diphenylmethyl-sulfinyl-acetamide class. Its mechanism is not fully defined, but it inhibits the dopamine transporter (DAT), increasing extracellular dopamine. It is approved to improve wakefulness in narcolepsy, obstructive sleep apnea, and shift work disorder. Notable adverse effects include headache and insomnia; serious rash, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, is a rare contraindication-related concern.
tail_desc: Central nervous system disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the brain and spinal cord, arising from diverse mechanisms including congenital, vascular, infectious, inflammatory, degenerative, neoplastic, traumatic, or toxic injury. Clinical features vary but may include headache, seizures, weakness, sensory loss, cognitive or behavioral change, gait disturbance, or altered consciousness, in children or adults. Management depends on cause and typically combines etiologic treatment, symptom control, and rehabilitation.

head: Famotidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Famotidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist of the guanidine/thiazole class that competitively blocks H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells, reducing basal and stimulated gastric acid secretion. It is used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypersecretory states such as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Notable adverse effects include headache and dizziness, and dose reduction is recommended in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is a gastroenterologic disorder caused by gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumors (gastrinomas), usually in the pancreas or duodenum, leading to marked gastric acid hypersecretion. It typically presents in adults with severe, recurrent peptic ulcers, gastroesophageal reflux, abdominal pain, and diarrhea, and may occur sporadically or with MEN1. Management focuses on potent acid suppression and localization and treatment of the tumor, including surgery when feasible.

head: Antazoline (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Antazoline is a first-generation ethanolamine antihistamine with anticholinergic properties. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conjunctivitis and rhinitis, often in topical ophthalmic or nasal formulations. A notable adverse effect is sedation; caution is warranted in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention due to anticholinergic effects.
tail_desc: Vernal conjunctivitis, usually termed vernal keratoconjunctivitis, is a chronic, recurrent allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface affecting the conjunctiva and often cornea. It is driven by hypersensitivity mechanisms, typically in children or adolescents, especially boys in warm climates, and causes intense itching, photophobia, tearing, ropy mucus, and giant papillae. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and short supervised corticosteroid or calcineurin-inhibitor therapy when severe.

head: Cimetidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cimetidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It is used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and other acid-related disorders. A notable adverse effect is inhibition of cytochrome P450 enzymes, causing clinically important drug interactions; gynecomastia can also occur.
tail_desc: Esophagitis is inflammation of the esophageal mucosa, a gastrointestinal disorder most often caused by gastroesophageal reflux, medications, infections, or eosinophilic inflammation. Typical features include odynophagia, dysphagia, heartburn, and chest pain, occurring in adults or children depending on cause. Management targets the etiology, such as acid suppression, stopping offending drugs, dietary therapy, or treatment of infection.

head: Vardenafil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Vardenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzenesulfonamide derivative, that selectively blocks PDE5 to increase cyclic GMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation in the corpus cavernosum. It is approved for erectile dysfunction. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nitrates, which can cause severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Psychologic dyspareunia is painful sexual intercourse primarily attributed to psychological factors rather than identifiable genital pathology, affecting the female sexual and genitourinary/pelvic pain domain. It commonly involves anxiety, fear, past trauma, relationship distress, or conditioned pelvic floor tension, presenting as recurrent intercourse-related pain and avoidance in sexually active women. Management typically emphasizes psychosexual counseling, treatment of comorbid mood symptoms, pelvic floor therapy, and exclusion of organic causes.

head: Mechlorethamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mechlorethamine is a nitrogen mustard alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms highly reactive intermediates, causing DNA cross-linking and inhibition of DNA replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used in regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and as topical therapy for mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma). A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is also a potent vesicant if extravasated.
tail_desc: Classic Hodgkin lymphoma is a malignant B-cell lymphoma of the lymphatic system, defined by rare Reed-Sternberg cells in an inflammatory background. It typically presents with painless cervical or mediastinal lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, and a bimodal age distribution, especially in young adults and later life. Management usually combines stage-adapted chemotherapy, often with involved-site radiotherapy, with high cure rates.

head: Codeine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Codeine is a naturally occurring opiate alkaloid and weak opioid analgesic that acts primarily as a mu-opioid receptor agonist, with analgesic effects partly mediated by hepatic conversion to morphine. It is used for mild to moderate pain and as an antitussive. Notable risks include respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in some children after tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy.
tail_desc: The common cold is an acute, self-limited viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, primarily affecting the nose and throat. It is caused by numerous respiratory viruses, especially rhinoviruses, producing mucosal inflammation. Typical features include nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, sore throat, cough, and malaise in children and adults. Management is supportive, with rest, fluids, and symptom relief rather than antibiotics.

head: Ciprofibrate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ciprofibrate is a fibric acid derivative (fibrate) lipid-lowering agent that activates peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha (PPAR-α), increasing lipoprotein lipase activity and reducing hepatic triglyceride-rich lipoprotein production. It is used to treat hypertriglyceridemia and mixed dyslipidemia. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, with increased risk when combined with statins; severe hepatic or renal impairment is a contraindication.
tail_desc: obsolete familial combined hyperlipidemia is a disease or medical condition.

head: Emedastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Emedastine is a second-generation antihistamine, commonly used as the difumarate salt in ophthalmic formulations. It acts primarily as a selective histamine H1-receptor antagonist, inhibiting histamine-mediated allergic responses. It is indicated for allergic conjunctivitis, especially relief of ocular itching. A notable adverse effect is transient ocular burning or stinging after instillation.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Amphetamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Amphetamine is a sympathomimetic central nervous system stimulant of the phenethylamine/amphetamine class that increases synaptic norepinephrine and dopamine primarily by promoting their presynaptic release via monoamine transporters. It is approved for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and narcolepsy. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Obesity is a chronic metabolic and nutritional disorder affecting whole-body energy homeostasis, characterized by excessive accumulation of adipose tissue due to sustained positive energy balance interacting with genetic, environmental, and behavioral factors. It typically presents in children or adults with elevated body mass index or central adiposity and related complications. Management centers on lifestyle intervention, treatment of comorbidities, anti-obesity pharmacotherapy, and bariatric surgery when indicated.

head: Melphalan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Melphalan is a bifunctional nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the phenylalanine derivative class that covalently crosslinks DNA, mainly at guanine bases, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and causing cell death. It is used chiefly in multiple myeloma and ovarian carcinoma conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity or marked bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Ovarian clear cell adenocarcinoma is a malignant epithelial ovarian cancer, often associated with endometriosis and characterized histologically by clear glycogen-rich cells and tubulocystic, papillary, or solid growth patterns. It typically affects adult women, often presenting with a pelvic mass, abdominal pain, or bloating. Management usually involves surgical staging and cytoreductive resection, with platinum-based chemotherapy commonly used, though chemoresistance can occur.

head: Talinolol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Talinolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist (beta1-selective blocker) of the aryloxypropanolamine class. It lowers heart rate and myocardial contractility primarily by blocking cardiac beta1-adrenoceptors. It has been used clinically for hypertension and angina pectoris. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia, and it is contraindicated in marked sinus bradycardia or advanced atrioventricular block.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Calcium glycerophosphate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium salt of glycerophosphoric acid used as a mineral supplement and urinary/food acid neutralizing agent. It dissociates to provide calcium and phosphate, without a specific primary molecular target. It is used in some formulations to reduce dietary acid effects and as a calcium source. Adverse effects may include hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which the heart abruptly stops pumping effectively, causing sudden loss of circulation. It usually results from ventricular fibrillation, pulseless ventricular tachycardia, asystole, or pulseless electrical activity, often in adults with underlying heart disease. Patients collapse, are unresponsive, pulseless, and apneic or gasping; management requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and treatment of reversible causes.

head: Diphenylpyraline (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Diphenylpyraline is a first-generation ethanolamine antihistamine with anticholinergic properties. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist (more precisely, an inverse agonist), reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions such as allergic rhinitis and urticaria. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and caution is warranted in narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: The common cold is an acute, self-limited viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, primarily affecting the nose and throat. It is caused by numerous respiratory viruses, especially rhinoviruses, producing mucosal inflammation. Typical features include nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, sore throat, cough, and malaise in children and adults. Management is supportive, with rest, fluids, and symptom relief rather than antibiotics.

head: Solriamfetol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Solriamfetol is a wake-promoting agent, structurally a phenylalanine-derived aminoketone, that acts primarily as a dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor via DAT and NET. It is approved to improve wakefulness in adults with excessive daytime sleepiness associated with narcolepsy or obstructive sleep apnea. A notable adverse effect is increased blood pressure and heart rate, so caution is needed in patients with cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Narcolepsy-cataplexy syndrome is a central nervous system sleep-wake disorder, usually corresponding to narcolepsy type 1, caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin) signaling, often on an autoimmune basis in genetically susceptible people. It presents with excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy, often with hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis, and disturbed nocturnal sleep, typically beginning in childhood or adolescence; management uses wake-promoting agents and anticataplectic therapy.

head: Tolmetin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tolmetin is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), a heteroaryl acetic acid derivative, that reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for relief of signs and symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and juvenile idiopathic arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with aspirin-sensitive asthma.
tail_desc: Spondyloarthropathy refers to a group of chronic inflammatory rheumatic diseases primarily affecting the axial skeleton, sacroiliac joints, and entheses. It is associated with immune-mediated inflammation, often linked to HLA-B27, and includes ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic, reactive, and enteropathic arthritis. Typical features are inflammatory back pain, stiffness, peripheral arthritis, and enthesitis, often in younger adults; management centers on exercise, NSAIDs, and biologic therapy when needed.

head: Cefaclor (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefaclor is an oral second-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibacterial. It binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan synthesis, causing cell lysis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including otitis media, lower respiratory tract infections, skin and soft-tissue infections, and urinary tract infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, so it is contraindicated in patients with known cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Staphylococcus aureus infection is a bacterial infection that can affect the skin and soft tissues, bloodstream, lungs, bones, joints, or heart valves. It results from invasion by S. aureus, including toxin production in some syndromes. Clinical features range from boils and abscesses to pneumonia, sepsis, and endocarditis, affecting both healthy people and hospitalized or immunocompromised patients; management relies on drainage when needed and targeted antibiotic therapy.

head: Lisuride (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Lisuride is an ergot-derived dopamine receptor agonist, with activity mainly at D2 receptors and additional serotonergic effects. It has been used clinically for hyperprolactinemia and Parkinson’s disease, and in some settings for migraine prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include nausea, orthostatic hypotension, and hallucinations; it is generally contraindicated in patients with severe psychotic disorders.
tail_desc: Hyperpituitarism is an endocrine disorder of the pituitary gland characterized by excessive secretion of one or more pituitary hormones, usually from a functioning pituitary adenoma or, less often, hyperplasia. Clinical features depend on the hormone excess, such as acromegaly/gigantism, hyperprolactinemia, or Cushing disease, and may include headaches or visual defects. Management targets the cause and hormone excess with surgery, medications, and sometimes radiotherapy.

head: Cefoxitin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefoxitin is a second-generation cephamycin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, urinary tract, skin and soft-tissue infections, and for perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Acute gonococcal cervicitis is an acute sexually transmitted bacterial infection of the female lower genital tract, specifically the cervix, caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae. It commonly affects sexually active women and may cause mucopurulent endocervical discharge, cervical friability, dysuria, intermenstrual bleeding, or no symptoms. Management is with recommended antibiotic therapy, testing for coexisting sexually transmitted infections, and partner evaluation and treatment.

head: Levomilnacipran (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Levomilnacipran is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant, the more active enantiomer of milnacipran, that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively greater norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. It is approved for major depressive disorder in adults. Notable adverse effects include increased heart rate and blood pressure, and it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Ubrogepant (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: migraine with or without aura, susceptibility to (a disease)
head_desc: Ubrogepant is an orally administered small-molecule calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) receptor antagonist of the gepant class. It blocks CGRP receptor signaling, a pathway implicated in migraine pathophysiology. It is approved for the acute treatment of migraine with or without aura in adults. Common adverse effects include nausea and somnolence; use is contraindicated with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors.
tail_desc: Migraine with or without aura, susceptibility to, denotes an inherited predisposition to a common primary neurovascular headache disorder affecting the nervous system. It is genetically heterogeneous, reflecting increased risk rather than a single causative lesion. Affected individuals have recurrent migraine attacks, with or without transient neurologic aura, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood. Management follows standard migraine care, including trigger avoidance and acute and preventive therapies.

head: Besifloxacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Besifloxacin is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic, formulated primarily as an ophthalmic suspension. It inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is approved for the treatment of bacterial conjunctivitis caused by susceptible organisms. A notable adverse effect is local eye irritation, such as conjunctival redness or blurred vision after instillation.
tail_desc: Acute contagious conjunctivitis is an acute infectious inflammation of the conjunctiva of the eye, usually caused by transmissible viral or bacterial pathogens. It typically presents with rapid-onset conjunctival redness, tearing or mucopurulent discharge, irritation, and eyelid sticking, and commonly affects children and close-contact groups. Management is usually supportive with hygiene measures to limit spread, with targeted antimicrobial therapy only when bacterial infection is suspected or confirmed.

head: Bromocriptine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bromocriptine is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist, primarily targeting dopamine D2 receptors to inhibit pituitary prolactin secretion and suppress growth hormone release. It is used for hyperprolactinemia, prolactin-secreting adenomas, acromegaly, and Parkinson disease; a quick-release form is also approved for type 2 diabetes. Notable adverse effects include nausea and orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension.
tail_desc: Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system, primarily affecting the basal ganglia. It is characterized by degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and accumulation of alpha-synuclein–containing Lewy bodies. Typical features, usually beginning in older adults, include bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and postural instability; management focuses on symptomatic dopaminergic therapy, rehabilitation, and selected device-aided or surgical treatments.

head: Allopurinol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Allopurinol is a purine analog xanthine oxidase inhibitor that lowers uric acid by blocking conversion of hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid. It is used for chronic gout/hyperuricemia, prevention of uric acid nephropathy during cancer chemotherapy, and recurrent calcium oxalate stones with hyperuricosuria. A notable risk is severe allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Flumethasone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Flumethasone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts by binding the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It has been used mainly in topical dermatologic and otic/ophthalmic preparations for inflammatory conditions. Like other corticosteroids, prolonged local use can cause skin atrophy, and it is generally contraindicated in untreated infections at the application site.
tail_desc: Seborrheic keratosis is a common benign epidermal skin tumor caused by clonal proliferation of immature keratinocytes. It typically affects middle-aged and older adults, presenting as well-circumscribed, waxy, verrucous, “stuck-on” papules or plaques, often tan to dark brown, on the trunk, face, or extremities. Management is usually reassurance; symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome lesions may be removed with cryotherapy, curettage, or shave excision.

head: Cabergoline (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cabergoline is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist that selectively stimulates dopamine D2 receptors, suppressing pituitary prolactin secretion. It is used primarily for hyperprolactinemic disorders, including prolactinomas, and is also used in some settings for Parkinson disease. A notable safety concern is cardiac valvular fibrosis with long-term or high-dose use; it is contraindicated in patients with uncontrolled hypertension or fibrotic disorders.
tail_desc: Hyperprolactinemia is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis characterized by persistently elevated serum prolactin, most often from prolactin-secreting pituitary adenomas, dopamine-antagonist drugs, hypothyroidism, or stalk dysfunction. It commonly causes galactorrhea, menstrual irregularity, infertility, and hypogonadism in women, and decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and infertility in men. Management targets the cause, often with dopamine agonists and treatment of underlying lesions or endocrine abnormalities.

head: Fluticasone propionate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluticasone propionate is a synthetic trifluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts as an agonist at intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory cytokines and mediator release. It is used for asthma maintenance, allergic rhinitis, and inflammatory dermatoses, depending on formulation. A notable adverse effect is oropharyngeal candidiasis with inhaled use.
tail_desc: Occupational dermatitis is an inflammatory skin disorder, usually affecting the hands, caused by workplace exposure to irritants or allergens that disrupt the epidermal barrier or trigger delayed hypersensitivity. It presents in exposed workers with pruritus, erythema, scaling, fissuring, and sometimes vesicles or chronic lichenification. Management centers on identifying and reducing occupational exposure, skin protection, emollients, and topical anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Etodolac (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Etodolac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), a pyranocarboxylic acid derivative, that inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, particularly COX-mediated prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation, including in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and acute pain. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic systemic autoimmune inflammatory disease primarily affecting synovial joints. Immune-mediated synovitis causes pain, swelling, morning stiffness, and progressive cartilage and bone destruction, often in a symmetric polyarticular pattern, with extra-articular manifestations possible. It occurs most often in adults, especially women. Management aims to suppress inflammation and prevent joint damage using DMARDs, biologics, and supportive care.

head: Halcinonide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Halcinonide is a high-potency topical corticosteroid (glucocorticoid) of the pregnane class. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and reduce erythema, pruritus, and dermatosis. It is used for corticosteroid-responsive inflammatory skin disorders, including eczema and psoriasis. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy, especially with prolonged use or application under occlusion.
tail_desc: Exanthem is an acute generalized eruptive skin condition, usually reflecting a systemic infection or drug hypersensitivity affecting the integumentary system. It presents as a widespread rash, often with fever and other constitutional symptoms, especially in children with viral illnesses. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, supportive care, and stopping any offending medication.

head: Cefradine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefradine is a first-generation cephalosporin β-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections including respiratory tract, skin and soft tissue, and urinary tract infections. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with known cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Pneumonia is an acute infection of the lung parenchyma, usually affecting the lower respiratory system, caused by bacteria, viruses, or less often fungi, leading to alveolar inflammation and impaired gas exchange. Typical features include fever, cough, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and sputum production, especially in young children, older adults, and immunocompromised patients. Management includes supportive care and pathogen-directed antimicrobial therapy when indicated.

head: Flurbiprofen (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Flurbiprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the propionic acid derivative class that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for pain and inflammation, including rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis, and as an ophthalmic agent to inhibit intraoperative miosis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Osteoarthritis susceptibility is a genetic or multifactorial predisposition to developing osteoarthritis, a degenerative musculoskeletal disorder affecting synovial joints. It reflects increased likelihood of cartilage breakdown with subchondral bone and synovial changes, especially in older adults or those with mechanical risk factors. Clinical manifestations, if disease develops, include joint pain, stiffness, and functional limitation; management focuses on risk reduction, symptom control, exercise, weight management, and joint-specific supportive care.

head: Terbutaline (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Terbutaline is a selective short-acting beta2-adrenergic agonist (sympathomimetic phenethylamine) that stimulates beta2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle, increasing cAMP and producing bronchodilation. It is established for relief and prevention of bronchospasm in asthma and other reversible obstructive airway diseases. A notable adverse effect is tachycardia, and it should be used cautiously in patients with significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Intrinsic asthma is a nonallergic subtype of asthma affecting the lower respiratory tract, characterized by chronic airway inflammation, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and reversible airflow obstruction without an identifiable external allergen trigger. It often begins in adulthood and presents with episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, commonly provoked by infections, irritants, exercise, or stress. Management follows standard asthma control with trigger avoidance, inhaled corticosteroids, and bronchodilators.

head: Alfacalcidol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Alfacalcidol is a vitamin D analog (1α-hydroxycholecalciferol) that is rapidly converted in the liver to calcitriol, which activates the vitamin D receptor to increase intestinal calcium and phosphate absorption and regulate bone mineral metabolism. It is used for hypocalcemia and metabolic bone disorders such as renal osteodystrophy and hypoparathyroidism. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in existing hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Osteitis fibrosa is a metabolic bone disorder of the skeleton, usually caused by hyperparathyroidism with excess parathyroid hormone driving increased osteoclastic bone resorption and replacement by fibrous tissue. It can cause bone pain, fragility fractures, deformity, and “brown tumors,” classically in patients with severe primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism. Management targets the underlying hyperparathyroidism and correction of calcium-phosphate abnormalities.

head: Cefapirin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefapirin is a first-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic. It acts by binding penicillin-binding proteins and inhibiting bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections and is also established intramammarily in veterinary medicine for bovine mastitis. Like other cephalosporins, it can cause hypersensitivity reactions and is contraindicated in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Urinary tract infection is a common bacterial infection of the urinary system, affecting the bladder, urethra, and sometimes kidneys. It usually results from ascending uropathogens, especially Escherichia coli. Typical features include dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain, and sometimes fever or flank pain. It occurs across ages, especially in women, and is managed with appropriate antibiotics, hydration, and evaluation for complicating factors.

head: Procyclidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Procyclidine is a synthetic antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent of the piperidine class that competitively blocks central and peripheral muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, helping restore dopaminergic–cholinergic balance in the basal ganglia. It is used for Parkinsonism and extrapyramidal symptoms, including drug-induced dystonia and parkinsonian reactions. A notable adverse effect is confusion, particularly in older adults; it is contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Early-onset parkinsonism-intellectual disability syndrome is a rare neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative movement disorder affecting the central nervous system. It is characterized by childhood or adolescent onset of parkinsonism associated with intellectual disability, often with developmental delay and additional neurologic abnormalities. The condition is considered genetically determined, and management is supportive and symptomatic, including multidisciplinary developmental care and treatment of parkinsonian features.

head: Ceftizoxime (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ceftizoxime is a third-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibacterial, that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, skin and soft-tissue, bone and joint, and intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Acute gonococcal endometritis is an acute sexually transmitted infection of the female upper genital tract in which Neisseria gonorrhoeae infects the endometrial lining of the uterus, causing suppurative inflammation. It typically affects sexually active women and may present with pelvic or lower abdominal pain, abnormal uterine bleeding, purulent cervical discharge, fever, and uterine tenderness. Management is prompt antibiotic treatment for gonorrhea and evaluation for concurrent pelvic inflammatory disease.

head: Testosterone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: X-linked intellectual disability with isolated growth hormone deficiency (a disease)
head_desc: Testosterone is an endogenous androgenic-anabolic steroid hormone used as testosterone replacement therapy for male hypogonadism and delayed puberty, and in some masculinizing hormone regimens. It acts as an agonist at the androgen receptor, with some effects mediated after conversion to dihydrotestosterone or estradiol. Notable risks include erythrocytosis and it is contraindicated in men with prostate or breast cancer.
tail_desc: X-linked intellectual disability with isolated growth hormone deficiency is a rare inherited neuroendocrine disorder affecting brain development and pituitary growth hormone secretion. It is characterized by intellectual disability associated with isolated growth hormone deficiency, causing proportionate short stature or poor growth in affected males. Management is supportive and multidisciplinary, with endocrine assessment and growth hormone replacement when deficiency is confirmed.

head: Droxidopa (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Droxidopa is a synthetic amino acid and norepinephrine prodrug that is converted by aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase to norepinephrine, thereby increasing adrenergic signaling. It is approved for symptomatic neurogenic orthostatic hypotension in disorders such as Parkinson disease, multiple system atrophy, and pure autonomic failure. A notable risk is supine hypertension; caution is warranted in patients with ischemic heart disease or arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system, primarily affecting the basal ganglia. It is characterized by degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and accumulation of alpha-synuclein–containing Lewy bodies. Typical features, usually beginning in older adults, include bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and postural instability; management focuses on symptomatic dopaminergic therapy, rehabilitation, and selected device-aided or surgical treatments.

head: Norethisterone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Norethisterone is a synthetic progestin (19-nortestosterone derivative) that acts primarily as an agonist at progesterone receptors, producing progestational effects that suppress ovulation and alter cervical mucus and the endometrium. It is used in hormonal contraception and for menstrual disorders such as abnormal uterine bleeding, endometriosis, and dysmenorrhea. A notable contraindication is active or previous venous thromboembolism.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal atrophic vaginitis is a genitourinary syndrome of menopause affecting the vaginal mucosa, caused by estrogen deficiency after menopause that leads to epithelial thinning, reduced lubrication, and increased tissue fragility. It commonly presents in postmenopausal women with vaginal dryness, burning, itching, dyspareunia, and sometimes urinary symptoms. Management typically includes vaginal moisturizers/lubricants and, when appropriate, local estrogen therapy.

head: Imipenem (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Imipenem is a carbapenem beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing broad-spectrum bactericidal activity. It is used, typically with cilastatin, for serious susceptible bacterial infections including intra-abdominal, lower respiratory, urinary tract, skin/soft tissue, and gynecologic infections. A notable adverse effect is increased seizure risk, especially in patients with CNS disorders or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Bacterial arthritis, or septic arthritis, is an acute infectious arthritis of the musculoskeletal system caused by bacterial invasion of a joint, leading to purulent synovial inflammation and rapid cartilage damage. It typically presents with sudden monoarticular pain, swelling, warmth, restricted movement, and fever, especially in children, older adults, or immunocompromised patients. Management generally requires urgent joint drainage and targeted intravenous antibiotics.

head: Xipamide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Xipamide is a sulfonamide-derived, thiazide-like diuretic that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used for hypertension and edema, including edema associated with heart failure. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria and severe electrolyte depletion.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Vedolizumab (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Vedolizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody that selectively targets the α4β7 integrin, blocking its interaction with MAdCAM-1 and thereby reducing gut-directed lymphocyte trafficking. It is approved for moderately to severely active ulcerative colitis and Crohn’s disease. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of infections, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious active infections.
tail_desc: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the colonic mucosa, typically beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. It reflects immune-mediated intestinal inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals. Common features include bloody diarrhea, urgency, abdominal pain, and relapsing course, often in adolescents and adults. Management focuses on anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapy, with colectomy for refractory or complicated disease.

head: Medrysone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Medrysone is a synthetic corticosteroid used primarily as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts by binding intracellular glucocorticoid receptors and altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediator production and leukocyte activity. It has been used for steroid-responsive inflammatory conditions of the eye, especially anterior segment inflammation. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure with risk of glaucoma, and it is contraindicated in untreated ocular viral or fungal infections.
tail_desc: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the facial skin, primarily affecting the pilosebaceous and vascular systems. Its cause is multifactorial and incompletely understood, involving dysregulated innate immunity, neurovascular reactivity, and environmental triggers. It commonly affects adults with central facial flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasia, papules or pustules, and sometimes ocular involvement; management centers on trigger avoidance and topical, oral, laser, or ocular therapies according to subtype and severity.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1 (a disease)
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 defect is a rare monogenic autoinflammatory/autoimmune arthropathy affecting the musculoskeletal system. It is caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in LACC1, typically presenting in childhood with early-onset chronic polyarthritis, joint swelling, pain, stiffness, and sometimes elevated inflammatory markers. Management is supportive and rheumatologic, using anti-inflammatory and disease-modifying therapies, with rehabilitation and monitoring for joint damage.

head: Amikacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: bacterial meningitis caused by gram-negative bacteria (a disease)
head_desc: Amikacin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that irreversibly binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing bactericidal activity. It is used for serious infections due to susceptible Gram-negative bacteria, including complicated urinary tract, intra-abdominal, respiratory, and bloodstream infections, and has activity against some mycobacteria. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Gram-negative bacterial meningitis is an acute bacterial infection of the central nervous system causing inflammation of the meninges, typically from organisms such as Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, or Pseudomonas. It often presents with fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered mental status, and may occur in neonates, older adults, or hospitalized patients. Management requires urgent cerebrospinal fluid evaluation and prompt intravenous antibiotics with supportive care.

head: Prazosin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Prazosin is a quinazoline-derived selective alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist that blocks postsynaptic alpha-1 receptors, reducing peripheral vascular resistance and blood pressure. It is an established antihypertensive and is also used for symptomatic benign prostatic hyperplasia by relaxing smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck. A notable adverse effect is first-dose orthostatic hypotension, which may cause syncope.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Rimexolone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Rimexolone is a synthetic corticosteroid glucocorticoid used mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts by binding intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediator production and leukocyte activity. It has been used for postoperative ocular inflammation and anterior uveitis. Like other ophthalmic steroids, it can raise intraocular pressure and should be used cautiously in glaucoma.
tail_desc: Panuveitis is a form of uveitis involving inflammation of all major layers of the uveal tract and often adjacent ocular structures, affecting the eye. It may result from autoimmune or systemic inflammatory disease, infection, or be idiopathic. Patients present with eye pain, redness, photophobia, floaters, and blurred vision. Management targets the cause and suppresses inflammation, typically with corticosteroids and other immunomodulatory or anti-infective therapy.

head: Mepyramine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mepyramine (pyrilamine) is a first-generation antihistamine of the ethylenediamine class that acts as an H1 receptor antagonist/inverse agonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions such as rhinitis, urticaria, and pruritus, and in some topical antipruritic preparations. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it can also produce anticholinergic effects.
tail_desc: Nasopharyngitis is an acute upper respiratory tract inflammation of the nasal passages and pharynx, usually caused by viral infection. It commonly presents with rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, sore throat, sneezing, and sometimes cough or low-grade fever, especially in children and adults with common colds. Management is typically supportive, including hydration, rest, and symptom relief; antibiotics are generally not indicated unless bacterial infection is suspected.

head: Melatonin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Melatonin is an endogenous indoleamine hormone used as a chronobiotic/hypnotic agent. It acts primarily as an agonist at melatonin MT1 and MT2 receptors (MTNR1A/MTNR1B), helping regulate circadian rhythm and sleep onset. It is established for insomnia and circadian sleep-wake disorders, and prolonged-release melatonin is approved in some regions for insomnia in older adults. A notable adverse effect is daytime drowsiness.
tail_desc: Insomnia is a sleep-wake disorder of the nervous system characterized by persistent difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early awakening despite adequate opportunity for sleep. It is multifactorial, often linked to hyperarousal and commonly associated with psychiatric, medical, or substance-related factors. Typical features include nonrestorative sleep and daytime fatigue or impaired concentration, especially in adults. Management emphasizes sleep hygiene, cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia, and selected pharmacologic treatment when appropriate.

head: Norepinephrine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Norepinephrine is an endogenous catecholamine sympathomimetic vasopressor that primarily activates alpha-1 and alpha-2 adrenergic receptors, with lesser beta-1 activity, producing peripheral vasoconstriction and increased blood pressure. It is used intravenously for acute severe hypotension and septic shock. A notable adverse effect is tissue ischemia or necrosis from intense vasoconstriction, especially with extravasation.
tail_desc: Allergic asthma is a common phenotype of asthma, a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways in the respiratory system, driven by allergen-triggered, IgE-associated immune responses with airway hyperresponsiveness and variable airflow obstruction. It often affects children and atopic individuals, causing episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often after allergen exposure. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and standard asthma therapy, especially inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.

head: Porfimer sodium (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Porfimer sodium is a hematoporphyrin derivative photosensitizer used in photodynamic therapy. After intravenous administration, it accumulates preferentially in dysplastic or malignant tissue and, when activated by red laser light, generates reactive oxygen species that cause local cytotoxicity and vascular injury; it has no single defined molecular target. It is used for esophageal cancer, high-grade dysplasia in Barrett esophagus, and endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer. A notable adverse effect is prolonged photosensitivity.
tail_desc: Non-small cell lung carcinoma is a malignant epithelial cancer of the lung and respiratory system, comprising mainly adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. It arises through accumulation of genetic alterations, strongly linked to tobacco exposure, causing cough, dyspnea, chest pain, weight loss, and metastases, typically in older adults. Management depends on stage and biomarkers, using surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy.

head: Testosterone enanthate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Testosterone enanthate is a long-acting ester prodrug of testosterone, an androgen/anabolic steroid. After intramuscular administration it is hydrolyzed to testosterone, which activates the androgen receptor to produce androgenic and anabolic effects. It is used as testosterone replacement therapy for male hypogonadism and delayed puberty in selected males. Notable risks include erythrocytosis and it is contraindicated in men with prostate or breast carcinoma.
tail_desc: Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) is a chromosomal disorder affecting multiple organ systems, caused by an extra copy of chromosome 13, usually from meiotic nondisjunction. It presents in fetuses and newborns with severe developmental delay, growth restriction, craniofacial anomalies, holoprosencephaly, polydactyly, congenital heart defects, and other major malformations. Management is primarily supportive and multidisciplinary, with individualized decisions about surgical and palliative care.

head: Diethylcarbamazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Diethylcarbamazine is a synthetic piperazine-derived anthelmintic used mainly for lymphatic filariasis, tropical pulmonary eosinophilia, and loiasis. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it alters filarial parasite surface membranes and host immune handling, promoting microfilarial clearance; no single primary molecular target is established. A notable adverse effect is the Mazzotti-type inflammatory reaction from rapid parasite killing, especially in heavily infected patients.
tail_desc: Pulmonary eosinophilia is a respiratory disorder characterized by eosinophilic infiltration of lung parenchyma and/or elevated eosinophils in blood or bronchoalveolar lavage, usually reflecting allergic, drug-induced, parasitic, autoimmune, or idiopathic inflammation. Patients often present with cough, wheeze, fever, dyspnea, and pulmonary infiltrates. Management focuses on identifying and removing the cause, with corticosteroids commonly used for inflammatory forms.

head: Dinoprostone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: pregnancy disorder with abortive outcome (a disease)
head_desc: Dinoprostone is a synthetic prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) analog that activates prostaglandin EP receptors in cervical and uterine tissue, promoting cervical ripening and stimulating uterine smooth muscle contractions. It is used for cervical ripening and induction of labor and for evacuation in certain obstetric settings. A notable adverse effect is uterine hyperstimulation, and it is contraindicated when vaginal delivery is inappropriate.
tail_desc: Pregnancy disorder with abortive outcome is an obstetric condition of the female reproductive system in which a pregnancy ends in spontaneous or induced abortion before fetal viability. It may result from fetal chromosomal abnormalities, maternal disease, uterine or cervical pathology, infection, or trauma. Typical features include vaginal bleeding, cramping, and pregnancy loss in pregnant patients; management depends on cause and clinical status, emphasizing stabilization, evacuation or expectant care, and treatment of complications.

head: Gilteritinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with CEBPA somatic mutations (a disease)
head_desc: Gilteritinib is an oral small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the FLT3 receptor, with activity against both FLT3 internal tandem duplication and tyrosine kinase domain mutations, and also inhibits AXL. It is approved for adults with relapsed or refractory acute myeloid leukemia harboring a FLT3 mutation. A notable serious adverse effect is differentiation syndrome; QT interval prolongation can also occur.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with CEBPA somatic mutations is a genetically defined subtype of AML, a hematologic malignancy of the bone marrow and blood, driven by acquired mutations in the CEBPA transcription factor that impair myeloid differentiation. Patients present with typical AML features such as cytopenias, infections, bleeding, and fatigue. Management generally follows AML protocols with mutation-informed risk stratification and consideration of allogeneic transplantation in selected cases.

head: Mebhydrolin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mebhydrolin is a first-generation H1 antihistamine of the ethylenediamine class. It acts primarily as an inverse agonist/antagonist at histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is used for allergic conditions such as allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and pruritic dermatoses. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness, and it should be used cautiously in patients with angle-closure glaucoma or prostatic hypertrophy.
tail_desc: Vasomotor rhinitis is a form of nonallergic rhinitis affecting the nasal mucosa, thought to result from dysregulated autonomic or neurovascular control rather than infection or IgE-mediated allergy. It commonly causes chronic nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, postnasal drip, and sneezing, often triggered by irritants, odors, temperature changes, or alcohol, especially in adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and symptom control, often with intranasal therapies.

head: Meropenem (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Meropenem is a broad-spectrum carbapenem beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins, inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis and causing bactericidal activity. It is established for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including complicated intra-abdominal and skin infections, meningitis, and hospital-acquired pneumonia. A notable adverse effect is seizure risk, particularly in patients with CNS disorders or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Viral meningitis is an infectious inflammation of the meninges in the central nervous system, usually caused by enteroviruses, though other viruses can be responsible. It commonly presents with headache, fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, and nausea, often in children and young adults. Diagnosis is supported by cerebrospinal fluid findings, and management is mainly supportive, with specific antivirals used in selected cases.

head: Quizartinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloblastic leukemia with maturation (a disease)
head_desc: Quizartinib is an oral, selective type II receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor of FLT3, including FLT3-ITD mutant forms, used in acute myeloid leukemia with FLT3 internal tandem duplication. By blocking FLT3 signaling, it suppresses leukemic cell proliferation and survival. A notable toxicity is QT interval prolongation, requiring ECG and electrolyte monitoring and caution with other QT-prolonging drugs.
tail_desc: Acute myeloblastic leukemia with maturation is a subtype of acute myeloid leukemia affecting the bone marrow and blood, characterized by clonal proliferation of myeloblasts with some maturation beyond the blast stage. It typically presents in adults with anemia, infections, bleeding, and marrow failure. Management generally involves AML-directed induction chemotherapy, risk stratification, and consideration of stem cell transplantation in eligible patients.

head: Aminobenzoic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Aminobenzoic acid, usually referring to para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), is a benzoic acid derivative and B-complex–related compound rather than a standard targeted drug. It has no well-defined therapeutic molecular target; historically, potassium para-aminobenzoate has been used clinically for Peyronie’s disease and certain fibrotic dermatoses. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal intolerance, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to PABA-related compounds.
tail_desc: Peyronie disease is an acquired fibrotic disorder of the penile tunica albuginea in the male genitourinary system, caused by localized plaque formation and scarring, likely related to repetitive microtrauma in susceptible men. It typically affects middle-aged to older men and causes penile curvature, pain, palpable plaques, and sometimes erectile dysfunction. Management ranges from observation and medical or intralesional therapy to surgical correction in stable deformity.

head: Proglumetacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Proglumetacin is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), a prodrug combining indometacin with the gastric-protective moiety proglumide. It acts mainly via conversion to indometacin, which inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It has been used for inflammatory and painful rheumatic disorders such as osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding, and it is contraindicated in active peptic ulcer disease.
tail_desc: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic systemic autoimmune inflammatory disease primarily affecting synovial joints. Immune-mediated synovitis causes pain, swelling, morning stiffness, and progressive cartilage and bone destruction, often in a symmetric polyarticular pattern, with extra-articular manifestations possible.
