It occurs most often in adults, especially women. Management aims to suppress inflammation and prevent joint damage using DMARDs, biologics, and supportive care.

head: Diflunisal (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Diflunisal is a salicylate-derived nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects. It is used for mild to moderate pain and for osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in patients with aspirin/NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic systemic autoimmune inflammatory disease primarily affecting synovial joints. Immune-mediated synovitis causes pain, swelling, morning stiffness, and progressive cartilage and bone destruction, often in a symmetric polyarticular pattern, with extra-articular manifestations possible. It occurs most often in adults, especially women. Management aims to suppress inflammation and prevent joint damage using DMARDs, biologics, and supportive care.

head: Promethazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Promethazine is a first-generation antihistamine of the phenothiazine class that primarily acts as an H1 receptor antagonist, with additional anticholinergic and antiemetic effects. It is used for allergic conditions, prevention and treatment of nausea and vomiting, motion sickness, and sedation. A notable adverse effect is marked drowsiness; it is contraindicated in children younger than 2 years because of potentially fatal respiratory depression.
tail_desc: Vernal conjunctivitis, usually termed vernal keratoconjunctivitis, is a chronic, recurrent allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface affecting the conjunctiva and often cornea. It is driven by hypersensitivity mechanisms, typically in children or adolescents, especially boys in warm climates, and causes intense itching, photophobia, tearing, ropy mucus, and giant papillae. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and short supervised corticosteroid or calcineurin-inhibitor therapy when severe.

head: Ciclesonide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ciclesonide is an inhaled and intranasal corticosteroid prodrug that is converted in the airways to the active metabolite des-ciclesonide, a glucocorticoid receptor agonist that suppresses inflammatory gene expression. It is approved for maintenance treatment of asthma and for allergic rhinitis. Notable adverse effects include oropharyngeal candidiasis with inhaled use; it is not indicated for acute bronchospasm.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Rimexolone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Rimexolone is a synthetic corticosteroid glucocorticoid used mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts by binding intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediator production and leukocyte activity. It has been used for postoperative ocular inflammation and anterior uveitis. Like other ophthalmic steroids, it can raise intraocular pressure and should be used cautiously in glaucoma.
tail_desc: Ciliary body disease refers to disorders affecting the ciliary body of the eye’s uveal tract, within the visual system. It may result from inflammation, neoplastic involvement, cysts, trauma, or degenerative change, disrupting aqueous humor production and accommodation. Clinical features can include ocular pain, redness, blurred vision, altered intraocular pressure, and visual disturbance. Management depends on cause and typically involves ophthalmologic evaluation, targeted medical therapy, and sometimes surgery.

head: Phenyltoloxamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Phenyltoloxamine is a first-generation ethanolamine antihistamine that acts primarily as a histamine H1-receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms and producing sedative effects. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions such as rhinitis and urticaria, and in some combination products as an antitussive adjunct. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness with impairment of alertness.
tail_desc: Atopic conjunctivitis is a chronic allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, especially the conjunctiva, usually associated with atopic dermatitis and other atopic disease. It is driven by IgE-mediated and T-cell–mediated hypersensitivity. Typical features include intense itching, redness, tearing, mucous discharge, photophobia, and eyelid eczema, often in adolescents or adults with severe atopy. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and specialist-directed anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Clevidipine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Clevidipine is an intravenous dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that selectively inhibits L-type calcium channels in arterial smooth muscle, producing rapid arteriolar vasodilation and blood pressure reduction. It is used for acute hypertension when oral therapy is not feasible, including perioperative blood pressure control. A notable adverse effect is reflex tachycardia; it is contraindicated in patients with allergies to soy or egg products.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Fluoxymesterone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluoxymesterone is a synthetic 17-alpha-alkylated androgenic anabolic steroid and testosterone derivative that acts as an agonist of the androgen receptor, altering transcription of androgen-responsive genes. It has been used for androgen replacement in male hypogonadism and delayed puberty, and palliatively in certain breast cancers. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, including cholestatic jaundice; it is contraindicated in prostate carcinoma and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Androgen insensitivity syndrome is an X-linked disorder of sex development affecting sexual differentiation, caused by pathogenic variants in the androgen receptor that impair tissue response to androgens in individuals with a 46,XY karyotype. It ranges from complete female external genitalia with undescended testes to partial undervirilization or infertility. Management is individualized, including endocrine, surgical, psychosocial, and genetic counseling support.

head: Magnesium citrate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Magnesium citrate is an inorganic magnesium salt of citric acid used as an osmotic laxative and magnesium replacement. In the intestine it retains water osmotically, increasing intraluminal fluid and promoting bowel evacuation; magnesium also replenishes body magnesium stores. Established uses include occasional constipation and bowel cleansing before procedures. Notable adverse effects include diarrhea and electrolyte disturbances, and it is generally avoided in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a rare clonal histiocytic neoplasm in which pathologic dendritic cells accumulate in tissues, often driven by MAPK-pathway mutations such as BRAF V600E. It can affect bone, skin, lungs, and the pituitary, especially in children, causing lytic bone lesions, rash, pain, or diabetes insipidus. Management ranges from local therapy to systemic chemotherapy or targeted treatment in multisystem disease.

head: Melphalan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Melphalan is a bifunctional nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the phenylalanine derivative class that covalently crosslinks DNA, mainly at guanine bases, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and causing cell death. It is used chiefly in multiple myeloma and ovarian carcinoma conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity or marked bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Ovarian cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the ovary, usually epithelial in origin, affecting the female reproductive system. It often presents in middle-aged or older women with abdominal bloating, pelvic pain, early satiety, ascites, or a pelvic mass, and may be diagnosed late due to nonspecific symptoms. Management typically involves surgical staging and cytoreductive surgery with platinum-based chemotherapy, guided by histology and stage.

head: Gemigliptin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Gemigliptin is an oral dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor of the gliptin class used to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus. By selectively inhibiting DPP-4, it increases endogenous incretin activity, enhancing glucose-dependent insulin secretion and reducing glucagon release. A notable adverse effect is hypoglycemia when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas.
tail_desc: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder of glucose regulation in the endocrine system, characterized by insulin resistance and progressive pancreatic beta-cell dysfunction causing hyperglycemia. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity or sedentary lifestyle, and may present with polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and long-term vascular complications. Management focuses on weight loss, diet, exercise, glucose-lowering medications, and cardiovascular risk reduction.

head: Metyrosine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Metyrosine is a tyrosine hydroxylase inhibitor and catecholamine synthesis blocker. By inhibiting tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate-limiting enzyme in catecholamine biosynthesis, it lowers production of dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine. It is used primarily to manage catecholamine excess in patients with pheochromocytoma, including preoperative preparation. A notable adverse effect is sedation; it is also associated with extrapyramidal symptoms and should be used cautiously in psychiatric illness.
tail_desc: Sympathetic paraganglioma is a neuroendocrine tumor arising from extra-adrenal paraganglionic tissue of the sympathetic nervous system, usually in the abdomen or thorax. It often secretes catecholamines, causing episodic or sustained hypertension, headaches, palpitations, and sweating; some cases are hereditary. Management typically involves biochemical testing, imaging for localization, preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade, and surgical resection when feasible.

head: Naproxen (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Naproxen is a propionic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for pain, inflammation, and fever, including osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute gout, dysmenorrhea, and minor acute pain. A notable risk is gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Insulin glulisine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Insulin glulisine is a rapid-acting recombinant human insulin analog used to improve glycemic control in adults and children with diabetes mellitus, including type 1 and type 2 diabetes. It acts as an agonist at the insulin receptor, promoting peripheral glucose uptake and inhibiting hepatic glucose output. A notable adverse effect is hypoglycemia; it is contraindicated during episodes of hypoglycemia.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Guanethidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Guanethidine is an adrenergic neuron–blocking antihypertensive of the guanidine class. It is taken up into peripheral sympathetic nerve terminals via the norepinephrine transporter (NET) and depletes or prevents release of norepinephrine, reducing sympathetic tone. It was used for severe hypertension. A notable adverse effect is marked orthostatic hypotension; it is generally ineffective in pheochromocytoma.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Frovatriptan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: migraine with or without aura, susceptibility to (a disease)
head_desc: Frovatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, a selective serotonin 5-HT1B/5-HT1D receptor agonist. It acts by constricting dilated cranial blood vessels and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is approved for the acute treatment of migraine attacks with or without aura in adults, not for prophylaxis. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease because of coronary vasospasm risk.
tail_desc: Migraine with or without aura, susceptibility to, denotes an inherited predisposition to a common primary neurovascular headache disorder affecting the nervous system. It is genetically heterogeneous, reflecting increased risk rather than a single causative lesion. Affected individuals have recurrent migraine attacks, with or without transient neurologic aura, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood. Management follows standard migraine care, including trigger avoidance and acute and preventive therapies.

head: Ranitidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ranitidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It was used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and other acid-related disorders. A notable adverse effect is headache, and use became widely withdrawn or suspended in many markets because of NDMA contamination concerns.
tail_desc: Peptic esophagitis is an inflammatory disorder of the esophagus, usually caused by reflux of acidic gastric contents in gastroesophageal reflux disease. It commonly presents in adults with heartburn, retrosternal pain, acid regurgitation, and sometimes dysphagia or odynophagia. Management focuses on acid suppression, especially proton pump inhibitors, along with lifestyle measures and treatment of the underlying reflux.

head: Voxilaprevir (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: chronic hepatitis C virus infection (a disease)
head_desc: Voxilaprevir is a direct-acting antiviral of the hepatitis C virus (HCV), classified as an NS3/4A protease inhibitor. It blocks the viral NS3/4A serine protease required for polyprotein processing and replication. It is approved, in fixed-dose combination with sofosbuvir and velpatasvir, for treatment of chronic HCV infection, including certain prior DAA failures. A notable contraindication is coadministration with strong OATP1B inhibitors such as cyclosporine, which markedly increases voxilaprevir exposure.
tail_desc: Chronic hepatitis C virus infection is a chronic viral liver disease caused by persistent infection with hepatitis C virus, producing ongoing hepatic inflammation that can progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. Many patients are asymptomatic or have fatigue and abnormal liver enzymes; it occurs in infected adults and children. Management centers on antiviral therapy with direct-acting agents, plus fibrosis assessment and liver-related monitoring.

head: Zoledronic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Zoledronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the osteoclast mevalonate pathway, suppressing bone resorption. It is used for osteoporosis, Paget disease of bone, hypercalcemia of malignancy, and prevention of skeletal-related events in patients with bone metastases or multiple myeloma. A notable risk is osteonecrosis of the jaw; severe renal impairment is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Osteoporosis is a metabolic skeletal disease of the musculoskeletal system characterized by low bone mass and deterioration of bone microarchitecture, causing increased bone fragility and fracture risk. It commonly affects older adults, especially postmenopausal women, and may be asymptomatic until vertebral, hip, or wrist fractures occur. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, adequate calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Carbidopa (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carbidopa is a peripheral aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (DOPA decarboxylase) inhibitor used with levodopa in Parkinson’s disease and parkinsonism to reduce peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine, thereby increasing central levodopa availability and lessening nausea and vomiting. It has no meaningful antiparkinsonian effect alone. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nonselective monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Postencephalitic Parkinson disease is a secondary parkinsonian syndrome of the central nervous system that develops after encephalitis, classically encephalitis lethargica. It is thought to reflect postinfectious injury to basal ganglia and related dopaminergic pathways. Patients, often years after the encephalitic illness, develop bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, postural instability, and neuropsychiatric or oculogyric features; management is symptomatic, using antiparkinsonian therapy and supportive rehabilitation.

head: Levocabastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Levocabastine is a selective second-generation antihistamine of the piperidine class that acts as an inverse agonist/antagonist at peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used topically, mainly as ophthalmic drops or nasal spray, for seasonal allergic conjunctivitis and allergic rhinitis. Because systemic absorption is low, adverse effects are usually local, such as transient eye irritation or nasal burning.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Belotecan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: malignant non-dysgerminomatous germ cell tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Belotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. It stabilizes the topoisomerase I–DNA cleavable complex, preventing DNA religation and causing DNA strand breaks during replication. It has been used clinically, including for small cell lung cancer in some markets. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, particularly neutropenia.
tail_desc: Malignant non-dysgerminomatous germ cell tumor of the ovary is a rare, aggressive ovarian germ cell malignancy arising from primitive germ cells and excluding dysgerminoma; subtypes include yolk sac tumor, embryonal carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, and mixed forms. It typically affects children and young women, presenting with a rapidly enlarging pelvic mass, abdominal pain, and sometimes elevated AFP or hCG. Management usually combines fertility-sparing surgical staging/cytoreduction with platinum-based chemotherapy.

head: Cefepime (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefepime is a fourth-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used to treat susceptible bacterial infections including pneumonia, urinary tract infections, skin and soft tissue infections, intra-abdominal infections, and febrile neutropenia. A notable adverse effect is neurotoxicity, including encephalopathy or seizures, especially in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Pseudomonas aeruginosa infectious disease is a bacterial infection that can involve the respiratory tract, urinary tract, skin, bloodstream, or wounds. It is caused by the opportunistic gram-negative bacillus P. aeruginosa, especially in hospitalized, immunocompromised, burn, ventilated, or cystic fibrosis patients. Manifestations vary by site and may be severe; management relies on source control and targeted antipseudomonal antibiotics guided by culture and susceptibility testing.

head: Meclofenamic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Meclofenamic acid is a fenamate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the anthranilic acid class. It nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It has been used to treat mild to moderate pain, primary dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia. A notable risk is serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in aspirin-sensitive NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Atorvastatin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Atorvastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that lowers cholesterol by competitively inhibiting hepatic 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is indicated for hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, and prevention of cardiovascular events. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and during pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hypertriglyceridemia is a metabolic lipid disorder affecting the cardiovascular and pancreatic systems, defined by elevated plasma triglycerides from primary genetic defects or secondary causes such as diabetes, obesity, alcohol use, or medications. It is often asymptomatic but can cause eruptive xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, and acute pancreatitis, especially at very high levels. Management targets underlying causes, lifestyle change, and triglyceride-lowering therapy.

head: Kanamycin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Kanamycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing mistranslation. It is used for treatment of susceptible serious bacterial infections and has also been used as a second-line agent for multidrug-resistant tuberculosis. A notable adverse effect is ototoxicity, with nephrotoxicity also an important risk.
tail_desc: IgG4-related sclerosing cholangitis is a fibroinflammatory biliary tract disease within the spectrum of IgG4-related disease, caused by IgG4-positive plasma cell infiltration and fibrosis of the bile ducts. It typically affects older men, often with autoimmune pancreatitis, and presents with obstructive jaundice, cholestatic liver tests, and biliary strictures. Management usually involves corticosteroids, with immunosuppressive steroid-sparing therapy or biliary intervention in selected cases.

head: Idelalisib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Idelalisib is an oral small-molecule phosphoinositide 3-kinase delta (PI3Kδ) inhibitor that selectively blocks PI3Kδ signaling in B cells. It is approved for certain relapsed hematologic malignancies, including chronic lymphocytic leukemia, follicular lymphoma, and small lymphocytic lymphoma. A notable serious adverse effect is severe hepatotoxicity, and it can also cause serious diarrhea/colitis and pneumonitis.
tail_desc: Acute lymphoblastic/lymphocytic leukemia is an acute hematologic malignancy of the blood and bone marrow caused by clonal proliferation of immature lymphoid precursor cells, impairing normal hematopoiesis. It is most common in children but also occurs in adults, presenting with fatigue, pallor, fever, infections, bleeding, bone pain, and lymphadenopathy. Management typically involves multiagent chemotherapy, central nervous system prophylaxis, targeted therapy in selected cases, and sometimes stem cell transplantation.

head: Methdilazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methdilazine is a phenothiazine-derived first-generation H1 antihistamine with anticholinergic and sedative properties. It acts primarily as an antagonist at histamine H1 receptors. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions such as urticaria and pruritus. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma because of antimuscarinic effects.
tail_desc: The common cold is an acute, self-limited viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, primarily affecting the nose and throat. It is caused by numerous respiratory viruses, especially rhinoviruses, producing mucosal inflammation. Typical features include nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, sore throat, cough, and malaise in children and adults. Management is supportive, with rest, fluids, and symptom relief rather than antibiotics.

head: Ceftriaxone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections including pneumonia, meningitis, gonorrhea, sepsis, urinary tract and intra-abdominal infections. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions in beta-lactam–allergic patients and biliary sludging/pseudolithiasis.
tail_desc: Bacterial pneumonia is an acute lower respiratory tract infection of the lungs in which bacteria infect the alveoli, causing inflammation and consolidation. It commonly presents with fever, cough, purulent sputum, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and sometimes hypoxemia, especially in young children, older adults, and people with chronic illness or impaired immunity. Management typically involves prompt antibiotic therapy, supportive care, and treatment of complications.

head: Propylthiouracil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Propylthiouracil is a thiourea antithyroid drug (thionamide) that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking iodination and coupling steps in thyroid hormone synthesis, and also inhibits peripheral conversion of thyroxine (T4) to triiodothyronine (T3). It is used to treat hyperthyroidism, including Graves disease and thyroid storm. A notable serious adverse effect is severe hepatotoxicity; agranulocytosis can also occur.
tail_desc: Hyperthyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid gland in which excess thyroid hormone production causes a hypermetabolic state, commonly from Graves disease, toxic multinodular goiter, or a toxic adenoma. Typical features include weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, often in adults, especially women. Management usually aims to control symptoms and reduce hormone excess with beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Venlafaxine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Venlafaxine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant of the phenethylamine class that blocks the serotonin and norepinephrine transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is approved for major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety disorder, and panic disorder. A notable adverse effect is dose-related hypertension, and it is contraindicated with concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Social phobia, or social anxiety disorder, is a psychiatric anxiety disorder affecting mental and behavioral function, characterized by marked fear of social or performance situations due to excessive concern about scrutiny, embarrassment, or negative evaluation. It commonly begins in adolescence and causes avoidance, distress, and impaired functioning; management typically includes cognitive-behavioral therapy and antidepressants such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors.

head: Amlodipine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Amlodipine is a long-acting dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation and reducing blood pressure. It is established for hypertension, chronic stable angina, and vasospastic angina. A notable adverse effect is dose-related peripheral edema; it should be used cautiously in severe hypotension or cardiogenic shock.
tail_desc: Stroke disorder is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by interrupted cerebral blood flow from ischemic arterial occlusion or intracranial hemorrhage, leading to acute brain injury. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as unilateral weakness, speech or visual disturbance, and occurs mainly in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management is urgent, emphasizing rapid diagnosis, reperfusion for eligible ischemic stroke, hemorrhage control, complication prevention, and rehabilitation.

head: Teneligliptin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Teneligliptin is an oral dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor of the gliptin class that enhances endogenous incretin activity by inhibiting DPP-4, thereby increasing GLP-1 and GIP levels and improving glucose-dependent insulin secretion while reducing glucagon. It is used for type 2 diabetes mellitus. A notable adverse effect is hypoglycemia, particularly when combined with insulin or sulfonylureas.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Famotidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Famotidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist of the guanidine/thiazole class that competitively blocks H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells, reducing basal and stimulated gastric acid secretion. It is used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypersecretory states such as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Notable adverse effects include headache and dizziness, and dose reduction is recommended in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Gastric ulcer is a peptic ulcer disease of the gastrointestinal system characterized by a mucosal break in the stomach wall, usually caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use that impairs mucosal defenses against acid and pepsin. Typical features include epigastric pain, nausea, dyspepsia, and possible bleeding, especially in older adults or NSAID users; management includes acid suppression, H. pylori eradication, and removal of precipitating factors.

head: Desloratadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Desloratadine is a second-generation tricyclic antihistamine and the active metabolite of loratadine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing allergic symptoms. It is used for seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, although it is generally less sedating than first-generation antihistamines; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to desloratadine or loratadine.
tail_desc: Idiopathic urticaria is a skin disorder of recurrent wheals and sometimes angioedema arising without an identifiable trigger. It is thought to reflect mast-cell activation with histamine release, although the precise cause is unknown. Patients, often adults, develop transient intensely pruritic hives. Management focuses on trigger avoidance when possible and symptom control, primarily with non-sedating H1 antihistamines.

head: Tasosartan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tasosartan is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Flumethasone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Flumethasone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts by binding the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It has been used mainly in topical dermatologic and otic/ophthalmic preparations for inflammatory conditions. Like other corticosteroids, prolonged local use can cause skin atrophy, and it is generally contraindicated in untreated infections at the application site.
tail_desc: lichen disease is a disease or medical condition.

head: Somatotropin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: isolated congenital growth hormone deficiency (a disease)
head_desc: Somatotropin is recombinant human growth hormone, a peptide hormone replacement that binds the growth hormone receptor (GHR) to stimulate IGF-1 production and promote linear growth and anabolic effects. It is used for pediatric growth hormone deficiency and certain growth-failure syndromes, and for adult growth hormone deficiency. Notable risks include intracranial hypertension and contraindication in active malignancy.
tail_desc: Isolated congenital growth hormone deficiency is a rare endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary growth axis, usually caused by congenital defects in growth hormone production or secretion, often from pathogenic variants in genes such as GH1 or GHRHR. It typically presents in infancy or childhood with proportionate short stature, poor growth velocity, delayed bone age, and sometimes neonatal hypoglycemia or micropenis. Management is centered on recombinant human growth hormone replacement and longitudinal endocrine monitoring.

head: Ceftriaxone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections including pneumonia, meningitis, gonorrhea, sepsis, urinary tract and intra-abdominal infections. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions in beta-lactam–allergic patients and biliary sludging/pseudolithiasis.
tail_desc: Staphylococcal pneumonia is a bacterial pneumonia of the lower respiratory tract caused by Staphylococcus aureus, producing acute suppurative and often necrotizing lung infection. It commonly causes fever, cough, dyspnea, purulent sputum, and severe systemic illness, and is seen particularly after influenza, in hospitalized patients, and in infants or older adults. Management centers on prompt anti-staphylococcal antibiotics and supportive respiratory care.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Burkitt lymphoma is a highly aggressive mature B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma of the lymphatic/hematologic system, driven by MYC translocation and extremely rapid cellular proliferation; Epstein-Barr virus is associated in some forms. It occurs in children and adults, presenting with rapidly enlarging jaw, abdominal, or nodal masses and frequent extranodal disease. Management typically requires urgent intensive combination chemotherapy with central nervous system prophylaxis and supportive care.

head: Tenecteplase (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tenecteplase is a genetically engineered fibrinolytic thrombolytic, a modified tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) variant that binds fibrin and converts plasminogen to plasmin, promoting clot dissolution. It is used for thrombolysis in acute ischemic stroke and has also been used for acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Major adverse effects include serious bleeding, especially intracranial hemorrhage; active internal bleeding is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Carbamazepine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carbamazepine is a dibenzazepine anticonvulsant and mood-stabilizing agent that primarily inhibits voltage-gated sodium channels, reducing high-frequency neuronal firing. It is used for focal and generalized tonic-clonic seizures, trigeminal neuralgia, and bipolar disorder, and is also approved for pain associated with true trigeminal neuralgia. A notable risk is serious cutaneous reactions, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, especially in HLA-B*1502 carriers.
tail_desc: Visual epilepsy is a form of focal reflex epilepsy involving the central nervous system, in which seizures are triggered by visual stimuli or arise from the occipital cortex. It typically causes brief visual phenomena such as flashes, colored shapes, or transient visual loss, sometimes progressing to impaired awareness or generalized seizures, often in children or adolescents. Management centers on trigger avoidance and standard antiseizure medication.

head: Triprolidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Triprolidine is a first-generation alkylamine antihistamine that acts as an inverse agonist/antagonist at histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated symptoms. It is used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and common-cold symptoms, often in combination products. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention due to anticholinergic effects.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Montelukast (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Montelukast is an oral leukotriene receptor antagonist that selectively blocks the cysteinyl leukotriene receptor CysLT1, inhibiting leukotriene D4-mediated bronchoconstriction and airway inflammation. It is approved for prophylaxis and chronic treatment of asthma, prevention of exercise-induced bronchoconstriction, and relief of seasonal or perennial allergic rhinitis. A notable risk is serious neuropsychiatric adverse effects, prompting a boxed warning.
tail_desc: Allergic asthma is a common phenotype of asthma, a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways in the respiratory system, driven by allergen-triggered, IgE-associated immune responses with airway hyperresponsiveness and variable airflow obstruction. It often affects children and atopic individuals, causing episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often after allergen exposure. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and standard asthma therapy, especially inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators.

head: Cefoxitin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefoxitin is a second-generation cephamycin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, urinary tract, skin and soft-tissue infections, and for perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Escherichia coli infection is a bacterial infection, usually affecting the gastrointestinal or urinary tract, caused by pathogenic E. coli strains acquired from contaminated food, water, or the patient’s own gut flora. It commonly presents with diarrhea, abdominal cramps, dysuria, or fever; severe strains may cause hemorrhagic colitis or hemolytic uremic syndrome, especially in children. Management is mainly supportive, with antibiotics used selectively according to syndrome and susceptibility.

head: Bepotastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bepotastine is a second-generation antihistamine, chemically a piperidine derivative, that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is approved in some markets for allergic rhinitis and urticaria, and as an ophthalmic solution for allergic conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, though it is generally less sedating than first-generation antihistamines.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Perazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Perazine is a typical antipsychotic of the phenothiazine class, used mainly for schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders. Like other phenothiazines, it acts primarily through antagonism of dopamine D2 receptors in the central nervous system, reducing psychotic symptoms. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, and it is generally contraindicated in severe CNS depression or coma.
tail_desc: Anxiety is a mental health condition involving excessive fear, worry, or apprehension, primarily affecting the central nervous system and stress-response pathways. It is multifactorial, with contributions from genetic vulnerability, neurobiological dysregulation, and psychosocial stressors. Common features include persistent nervousness, restlessness, impaired concentration, sleep disturbance, and autonomic symptoms; it can affect people of any age. Management typically includes psychotherapy, stress reduction, and when indicated, pharmacotherapy such as antidepressants.

head: Loratadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Loratadine is a second-generation tricyclic antihistamine of the piperidine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is established for allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. Compared with first-generation antihistamines it is less sedating, though somnolence can still occur; caution is warranted in severe hepatic impairment.
tail_desc: Vernal conjunctivitis, usually termed vernal keratoconjunctivitis, is a chronic, recurrent allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface affecting the conjunctiva and often cornea. It is driven by hypersensitivity mechanisms, typically in children or adolescents, especially boys in warm climates, and causes intense itching, photophobia, tearing, ropy mucus, and giant papillae. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and short supervised corticosteroid or calcineurin-inhibitor therapy when severe.

head: Posaconazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Posaconazole is a broad-spectrum triazole antifungal that inhibits fungal lanosterol 14α-demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and disrupting cell membrane formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of invasive Aspergillus and Candida infections, including oropharyngeal candidiasis. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated with certain CYP3A4-metabolized drugs due to serious interaction potential.
tail_desc: Coccidioidomycosis is a fungal infection of the respiratory system caused by inhalation of Coccidioides spores from soil in endemic regions. It commonly presents with fever, cough, chest pain, fatigue, and sometimes erythema nodosum; most cases are self-limited, but severe or disseminated disease occurs more often in immunocompromised people. Management ranges from observation for mild illness to antifungal therapy, typically with azoles, for significant disease.

head: Tenofovir disoproxil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tenofovir disoproxil is an oral prodrug of tenofovir, an acyclic nucleotide analogue reverse-transcriptase inhibitor (NtRTI). After conversion to tenofovir diphosphate, it competitively inhibits HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and hepatitis B virus polymerase, causing DNA chain termination. It is used to treat HIV-1 infection and chronic hepatitis B. A notable adverse effect is renal toxicity, including proximal tubulopathy/Fanconi syndrome.
tail_desc: HIV infectious disease is a chronic viral infection of the immune system caused by human immunodeficiency virus, which infects and depletes CD4+ T lymphocytes, leading to progressive immunodeficiency. Patients may be asymptomatic initially or develop acute febrile illness, lymphadenopathy, weight loss, recurrent infections, and opportunistic diseases. It affects all age groups and is managed with lifelong combination antiretroviral therapy and prevention of transmission and opportunistic infection.

head: Lefamulin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Lefamulin is a pleuromutilin antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the peptidyl transferase center of the 50S ribosomal subunit. It is approved for treatment of community-acquired bacterial pneumonia in adults, including oral and intravenous formulations. A notable safety concern is QT interval prolongation, and it is contraindicated with strong CYP3A inducers.
tail_desc: Streptococcal pneumonia is a bacterial lower respiratory tract infection of the lungs, most commonly caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, producing acute inflammation and alveolar consolidation. It typically presents with fever, cough, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and purulent sputum, especially in young children, older adults, and immunocompromised patients. Management centers on prompt appropriate antibiotic therapy and supportive respiratory care.

head: Cilazapril (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cilazapril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor of the dipeptide-derived, prodrug class, converted in vivo to the active metabolite cilazaprilat. It lowers blood pressure by inhibiting ACE, reducing formation of angiotensin II and aldosterone. It is used for hypertension and heart failure. A notable adverse effect is persistent dry cough; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in patients with prior ACE inhibitor–associated angioedema.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Alteplase (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Alteplase is a recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (thrombolytic/fibrinolytic protein) that binds fibrin in thrombi and converts plasminogen to plasmin, promoting clot dissolution. It is used for acute ischemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, acute massive pulmonary embolism, and restoration of function in occluded central venous catheters. Major adverse effects include serious bleeding, especially intracranial hemorrhage; active internal bleeding is a contraindication.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Cholic Acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cholic acid is a primary bile acid used as a bile acid replacement therapy. It acts by restoring the endogenous bile acid pool and activating the farnesoid X receptor (FXR), which suppresses bile acid synthesis. It is indicated for bile acid synthesis disorders due to single-enzyme defects and as adjunctive treatment of peroxisomal disorders including Zellweger spectrum disorders. Diarrhea is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Choledocholithiasis is a biliary tract disorder in which gallstones are present in the common bile duct, causing partial or complete obstruction of bile flow in the hepatobiliary system. It commonly presents in adults, often with right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, cholestatic liver test abnormalities, and sometimes fever or pancreatitis. Management typically involves stone removal, usually by ERCP, followed by treatment of the underlying gallstone disease when indicated.

head: Carboplatin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carboplatin is a second-generation platinum coordination compound (alkylating-like antineoplastic) that forms covalent DNA intra- and interstrand crosslinks, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and triggering apoptosis. It is established for ovarian cancer and is widely used in lung and other solid tumors. A notable toxicity is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia; severe hypersensitivity can also occur.
tail_desc: Ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinoma is an epithelial ovarian cancer of the female reproductive system, histologically resembling endometrial adenocarcinoma and often associated with endometriosis. It typically affects peri- or postmenopausal women and may present with pelvic pain, abdominal distension, or an adnexal mass. Management usually involves surgical staging and cytoreductive resection, with platinum-based chemotherapy according to stage and residual disease.

head: Calcium gluconate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcium gluconate is a calcium salt used as a mineral replacement and cardioprotective agent. It dissociates to provide ionized calcium, supporting neuromuscular and cardiac membrane function without a specific receptor target. Established uses include treatment of acute hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia with ECG changes, hypermagnesemia, and calcium channel blocker overdose. Notable adverse effects include tissue necrosis with extravasation.
tail_desc: Nephrocalcinosis is a renal disorder characterized by diffuse calcium salt deposition within the kidney parenchyma, especially the medulla, usually due to hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, or tubular disorders such as renal tubular acidosis. It may be asymptomatic or present with nephrolithiasis, polyuria, hematuria, or declining renal function, in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, correcting metabolic abnormalities, hydration, and monitoring kidney function.

head: Insulin human (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Human insulin is a recombinant or purified peptide hormone antidiabetic in the insulin class. It acts as an agonist at the insulin receptor (INSR), promoting peripheral glucose uptake and inhibiting hepatic glucose production. It is established for treatment of diabetes mellitus, including type 1 and type 2 diabetes. A notable adverse effect is hypoglycemia; it is contraindicated during episodes of hypoglycemia.
tail_desc: Type 1 diabetes mellitus is an autoimmune endocrine disorder of the pancreas in which immune-mediated destruction of insulin-producing beta cells causes absolute insulin deficiency and chronic hyperglycemia. It often begins in childhood or adolescence, though it can occur at any age, and commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and sometimes diabetic ketoacidosis. Management requires lifelong insulin replacement with glucose monitoring, nutrition guidance, and complication prevention.

head: Ceftriaxone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections including pneumonia, meningitis, gonorrhea, sepsis, urinary tract and intra-abdominal infections. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions in beta-lactam–allergic patients and biliary sludging/pseudolithiasis.
tail_desc: Peptostreptococcus infectious disease is an anaerobic bacterial infection caused by Peptostreptococcus species, usually arising from endogenous flora of the oral, gastrointestinal, or female genital tract. It commonly produces suppurative, polymicrobial abscesses in soft tissue, head and neck, lung, abdomen, pelvis, or bloodstream, especially after mucosal disruption. Management typically involves abscess drainage or debridement plus antibiotics active against anaerobes.

head: Pseudoephedrine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pseudoephedrine is a sympathomimetic amine decongestant of the phenethylamine/amphetamine-related class that acts mainly as an indirect adrenergic agonist, promoting norepinephrine release and stimulating alpha-adrenergic receptors to cause vasoconstriction in nasal mucosa. It is used for temporary relief of nasal and sinus congestion from colds, allergies, or sinusitis. A notable adverse effect is hypertension, and it is contraindicated with MAO inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Vasomotor rhinitis is a form of nonallergic rhinitis affecting the nasal mucosa, thought to result from dysregulated autonomic or neurovascular control rather than infection or IgE-mediated allergy. It commonly causes chronic nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, postnasal drip, and sneezing, often triggered by irritants, odors, temperature changes, or alcohol, especially in adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and symptom control, often with intranasal therapies.

head: Salicylic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Salicylic acid is a keratolytic and topical salicylate, chemically a beta-hydroxy acid. It acts mainly by solubilizing intercellular cement in the stratum corneum, promoting desquamation; unlike acetylsalicylic acid, it is not used primarily for systemic COX inhibition. It is established for acne, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, warts, and calluses/corns. Notable risks include local irritation and salicylate toxicity with excessive topical use.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Etofenamate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Etofenamate is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the anthranilic acid (fenamate) class, used mainly as a topical anti-inflammatory and analgesic for musculoskeletal pain and inflammatory rheumatic conditions. Like other NSAIDs, it inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. A notable adverse effect is local skin irritation; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to NSAIDs.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Magnesium citrate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Magnesium citrate is an inorganic magnesium salt of citric acid used as an osmotic laxative and magnesium replacement. In the intestine it retains water osmotically, increasing intraluminal fluid and promoting bowel evacuation; magnesium also replenishes body magnesium stores. Established uses include occasional constipation and bowel cleansing before procedures. Notable adverse effects include diarrhea and electrolyte disturbances, and it is generally avoided in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Teniposide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Teniposide is a semisynthetic podophyllotoxin derivative and epipodophyllotoxin antineoplastic agent. It inhibits DNA topoisomerase II, causing DNA strand breaks and cell-cycle arrest. Teniposide has established use, typically in combination chemotherapy, for refractory childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression, particularly leukopenia, and it should be used cautiously in patients with significant bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Leukemia, lymphocytic, susceptibility to denotes an inherited predisposition to developing lymphocytic leukemia, a hematologic malignancy of the blood, bone marrow, and lymphoid system. It reflects germline genetic factors that increase risk rather than the leukemia itself. Affected individuals are often identified through family history; management centers on genetic evaluation, counseling, and clinical surveillance rather than disease-specific treatment unless leukemia develops.

head: Levothyroxine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Levothyroxine is a synthetic tetraiodinated thyroid hormone (T4) replacement that is converted peripherally to triiodothyronine (T3), which binds nuclear thyroid hormone receptors (TRα/TRβ) to regulate gene transcription and metabolism. It is used primarily for hypothyroidism and TSH suppression in certain thyroid disorders, including differentiated thyroid cancer. A notable adverse effect is iatrogenic hyperthyroidism, and it is contraindicated in untreated thyrotoxicosis.
tail_desc: Congenital hypothyroidism is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid axis present at birth, most commonly caused by thyroid dysgenesis or defects in thyroid hormone synthesis, leading to deficient thyroxine. It affects newborns and may cause prolonged jaundice, lethargy, feeding difficulty, constipation, macroglossia, and impaired growth and neurodevelopment if untreated. Management is prompt newborn screening confirmation and early levothyroxine replacement with ongoing monitoring.

head: Bilastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bilastine is a second-generation, non-sedating antihistamine of the piperidine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is established for symptomatic treatment of allergic rhinoconjunctivitis and urticaria. Compared with older antihistamines it causes relatively little sedation, but somnolence can occur, and use is generally avoided in patients with hypersensitivity to bilastine.
tail_desc: Vasomotor rhinitis is a form of nonallergic rhinitis affecting the nasal mucosa, thought to result from dysregulated autonomic or neurovascular control rather than infection or IgE-mediated allergy. It commonly causes chronic nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, postnasal drip, and sneezing, often triggered by irritants, odors, temperature changes, or alcohol, especially in adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and symptom control, often with intranasal therapies.

head: Ibuprofen (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the propionic acid derivative class that reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is widely used to treat pain, fever, and inflammatory conditions such as osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and dysmenorrhea. Notable risks include gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is generally contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Midodrine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Midodrine is an orally administered prodrug and selective alpha-1 adrenergic agonist whose active metabolite, desglymidodrine, causes peripheral vasoconstriction via alpha-1 receptors. It is approved for symptomatic orthostatic hypotension. A notable adverse effect is supine hypertension, and it is generally contraindicated in patients with severe organic heart disease, acute renal disease, urinary retention, pheochromocytoma, or thyrotoxicosis.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Cefaclor (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefaclor is an oral second-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibacterial. It binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan synthesis, causing cell lysis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including otitis media, lower respiratory tract infections, skin and soft-tissue infections, and urinary tract infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, so it is contraindicated in patients with known cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Streptococcal pneumonia is a bacterial lower respiratory tract infection of the lungs, most commonly caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, producing acute inflammation and alveolar consolidation. It typically presents with fever, cough, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and purulent sputum, especially in young children, older adults, and immunocompromised patients. Management centers on prompt appropriate antibiotic therapy and supportive respiratory care.

head: Bromocriptine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bromocriptine is an ergot-derived dopamine agonist, primarily targeting dopamine D2 receptors to inhibit pituitary prolactin secretion and suppress growth hormone release. It is used for hyperprolactinemia, prolactin-secreting adenomas, acromegaly, and Parkinson disease; a quick-release form is also approved for type 2 diabetes. Notable adverse effects include nausea and orthostatic hypotension; it is contraindicated in uncontrolled hypertension.
tail_desc: Juvenile-onset Parkinson disease 19A is a hereditary neurodegenerative movement disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by early-onset parkinsonism beginning in childhood or adolescence. It is caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in DNAJC6, which impair synaptic vesicle trafficking and dopaminergic neuronal function. Typical features include bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, dystonia, and gait impairment; management is mainly symptomatic, including dopaminergic therapy and supportive multidisciplinary care.

head: Ipratropium (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ipratropium is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic bronchodilator, structurally related to atropine. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle, reducing vagally mediated bronchoconstriction and secretions. It is used as an inhaled treatment for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and also for bronchospasm in asthma. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Sulfabenzamide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sulfabenzamide is a sulfonamide antibacterial. Like other sulfonamides, it inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby suppressing susceptible bacterial growth. It has been used mainly as a topical anti-infective, including in some combination rectal or vaginal preparations. Notable risks include sulfonamide hypersensitivity reactions, so it is contraindicated in patients with known sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Bacterial vaginosis is a common vaginal dysbiosis of the female reproductive tract, caused by loss of protective Lactobacillus species and overgrowth of mixed anaerobic bacteria. It often affects reproductive-age women and may cause thin gray-white discharge and a fishy odor, though many cases are asymptomatic. Management typically involves antibiotic therapy such as metronidazole or clindamycin.

head: Travoprost (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Travoprost is a prostaglandin F2α analog ophthalmic antiglaucoma agent that acts as an agonist at the prostanoid FP receptor, increasing uveoscleral outflow of aqueous humor and thereby lowering intraocular pressure. It is used for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is conjunctival hyperemia; it can also cause gradual increased brown pigmentation of the iris.
tail_desc: Open-angle glaucoma is a chronic optic neuropathy of the eye, usually associated with impaired aqueous humor outflow through an anatomically open anterior chamber angle, often causing elevated intraocular pressure. It typically affects older adults and is characterized by progressive peripheral visual field loss and optic nerve cupping, often without early symptoms. Management focuses on lowering intraocular pressure with topical medications, laser trabeculoplasty, or surgery.

head: Methdilazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methdilazine is a phenothiazine-derived first-generation H1 antihistamine with anticholinergic and sedative properties. It acts primarily as an antagonist at histamine H1 receptors. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions such as urticaria and pruritus. A notable adverse effect is drowsiness; it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma because of antimuscarinic effects.
tail_desc: Non-histaminic angioedema is a bradykinin-mediated or otherwise non–mast cell form of angioedema affecting the skin and mucosal/submucosal tissues. It causes recurrent, nonpruritic swelling of the face, extremities, gastrointestinal tract, or airway, often without urticaria and with poor response to antihistamines, corticosteroids, or epinephrine. Management focuses on airway protection, identifying the cause, and using targeted therapies such as C1-inhibitor replacement or bradykinin-pathway agents when indicated.

head: Porfimer sodium (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Porfimer sodium is a hematoporphyrin derivative photosensitizer used in photodynamic therapy. After intravenous administration, it accumulates preferentially in dysplastic or malignant tissue and, when activated by red laser light, generates reactive oxygen species that cause local cytotoxicity and vascular injury; it has no single defined molecular target. It is used for esophageal cancer, high-grade dysplasia in Barrett esophagus, and endobronchial non-small cell lung cancer. A notable adverse effect is prolonged photosensitivity.
tail_desc: Lung cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the respiratory system arising from epithelial cells in the lung, most commonly non-small cell or small cell carcinoma. It is strongly linked to tobacco smoke, with additional risk from radon and occupational exposures. Typical features include persistent cough, hemoptysis, chest pain, dyspnea, and weight loss, usually in older adults. Management depends on stage and type, using surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy.

head: Tadalafil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tadalafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor, a benzo[d][1,3]dioxole-derived small molecule, that selectively inhibits PDE5 to increase cGMP and enhance nitric oxide–mediated smooth muscle relaxation. It is approved for erectile dysfunction, pulmonary arterial hypertension, and lower urinary tract symptoms associated with benign prostatic hyperplasia. A notable contraindication is concomitant nitrate use because of potentially severe hypotension.
tail_desc: Premature ejaculation is a male sexual dysfunction of the genitourinary and neurobehavioral domains, characterized by ejaculation that occurs sooner than desired, often within about 1 minute of penetration in lifelong cases, with poor perceived control and associated distress. Its cause is multifactorial, involving psychologic, neurobiologic, and relational factors. Management typically includes education, behavioral and psychosexual therapy, and selected pharmacologic treatment.

head: Cephalexin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cephalexin is a first-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, leading to cell lysis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including skin and soft tissue, respiratory tract, urinary tract, and bone infections. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
