tail_desc: Bacterial arthritis, or septic arthritis, is an acute infectious arthritis of the musculoskeletal system caused by bacterial invasion of a joint, leading to purulent synovial inflammation and rapid cartilage damage. It typically presents with sudden monoarticular pain, swelling, warmth, restricted movement, and fever, especially in children, older adults, or immunocompromised patients. Management generally requires urgent joint drainage and targeted intravenous antibiotics.

head: Carbidopa (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary late onset Parkinson disease (a disease)
head_desc: Carbidopa is a peripheral aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (DOPA decarboxylase) inhibitor used with levodopa in Parkinson’s disease and parkinsonism to reduce peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine, thereby increasing central levodopa availability and lessening nausea and vomiting. It has no meaningful antiparkinsonian effect alone. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nonselective monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Hereditary late-onset Parkinson disease is a genetic form of Parkinson disease, a neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system. It is associated with inherited variants that promote degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and abnormal alpha-synuclein-related pathology in some forms. It typically presents in later adulthood with bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and postural instability, and is managed with standard symptomatic Parkinson therapies plus genetic counseling.

head: Tioguanine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloblastic leukemia without maturation (a disease)
head_desc: Tioguanine (thioguanine, 6-thioguanine) is a thiopurine antimetabolite, a guanine analog that is converted intracellularly to thioguanine nucleotides, which inhibit de novo purine synthesis and become incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing replication and cell proliferation. It is used mainly in acute myeloid leukemia. A notable risk is severe myelosuppression, and hepatotoxicity including hepatic veno-occlusive disease can occur.
tail_desc: Acute myeloblastic leukemia without maturation is a subtype of acute myeloid leukemia affecting the bone marrow and blood, characterized by predominance of myeloblasts with minimal maturation due to clonal malignant transformation of myeloid precursors. It typically presents in adults with anemia, infections, bleeding, and marrow failure. Management usually involves intensive induction chemotherapy, risk-adapted consolidation, and consideration of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Pyridostigmine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pyridostigmine is a quaternary ammonium carbamate and reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that increases acetylcholine at cholinergic synapses by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase. It is established for symptomatic treatment of myasthenia gravis and is also used to reverse nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade. Notable adverse effects are muscarinic cholinergic symptoms such as diarrhea, abdominal cramping, and bradycardia; it is contraindicated in mechanical gastrointestinal or urinary obstruction.
tail_desc: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune neuromuscular junction disorder of the peripheral nervous system caused most often by antibodies against acetylcholine receptors, or less commonly MuSK, impairing neuromuscular transmission. It produces fluctuating, fatigable skeletal muscle weakness, commonly affecting ocular, bulbar, limb, and respiratory muscles, in adults or children. Management typically includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, immunosuppression, thymectomy in selected patients, and rescue therapy for crises.

head: Sitagliptin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sitagliptin is an oral dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor used for glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus. By selectively inhibiting DPP-4, it increases endogenous incretin hormones such as GLP-1 and GIP, enhancing glucose-dependent insulin secretion and reducing glucagon release. A notable adverse effect is acute pancreatitis; it is not indicated for type 1 diabetes or diabetic ketoacidosis.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Homatropine methylbromide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Homatropine methylbromide is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent, structurally related to atropine, that competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in peripheral tissues to reduce gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an antispasmodic for gastrointestinal disorders such as irritable bowel syndrome. Notable adverse effects include dry mouth and urinary retention; it is contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Irritable bowel syndrome is a functional gastrointestinal disorder of the gut–brain axis affecting the large intestine, without structural or inflammatory lesions. Its pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving altered motility, visceral hypersensitivity, and psychosocial factors. Typical features are recurrent abdominal pain with diarrhea, constipation, or both, often with bloating, most commonly in adults. Management emphasizes symptom-directed diet, lifestyle measures, and medications.

head: Insulin lispro (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Insulin lispro is a rapid-acting recombinant human insulin analog used to improve glycemic control in patients with diabetes mellitus. It binds and activates the insulin receptor, promoting peripheral glucose uptake and inhibiting hepatic glucose output. It is indicated for mealtime and corrective insulin therapy in type 1 and type 2 diabetes. A notable adverse effect is hypoglycemia; it is contraindicated during episodes of hypoglycemia.
tail_desc: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder of glucose regulation in the endocrine system, characterized by insulin resistance and progressive pancreatic beta-cell dysfunction causing hyperglycemia. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity or sedentary lifestyle, and may present with polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and long-term vascular complications. Management focuses on weight loss, diet, exercise, glucose-lowering medications, and cardiovascular risk reduction.

head: Dapsone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dapsone is a synthetic sulfone antimicrobial with anti-inflammatory activity. It inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis in susceptible organisms, and is established for treatment of leprosy and for dermatitis herpetiformis; it is also used for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia prophylaxis in selected patients. A notable adverse effect is dose-related hemolytic anemia, especially in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Dermatitis herpetiformis is a chronic autoimmune blistering skin disorder linked to gluten-sensitive enteropathy, caused by IgA deposition in the dermal papillae. It presents with intensely pruritic grouped papules and vesicles, typically on extensor surfaces, most often in adults. Management centers on a strict gluten-free diet, with dapsone commonly used for rapid symptom control.

head: Pitolisant (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pitolisant is a histamine H3-receptor antagonist/inverse agonist, chemically a piperidine derivative, that enhances histaminergic neurotransmission in the brain to promote wakefulness. It is approved for excessive daytime sleepiness in adults with narcolepsy, with or without cataplexy; in some regions it is also approved for cataplexy. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation, so caution is warranted in patients with cardiac risk factors.
tail_desc: Narcolepsy-cataplexy syndrome is a central nervous system sleep-wake disorder, usually corresponding to narcolepsy type 1, caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin) signaling, often on an autoimmune basis in genetically susceptible people. It presents with excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy, often with hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis, and disturbed nocturnal sleep, typically beginning in childhood or adolescence; management uses wake-promoting agents and anticataplectic therapy.

head: Moxisylyte (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Moxisylyte is an alpha-adrenergic antagonist, specifically a selective alpha1-adrenoceptor blocker. It has been used as a peripheral vasodilator, notably in erectile dysfunction and some peripheral circulatory disorders. By blocking postsynaptic alpha1 receptors in vascular smooth muscle, it reduces sympathetic vasoconstriction and improves blood flow. A notable adverse effect is orthostatic hypotension, with dizziness or syncope in susceptible patients.
tail_desc: Premature ejaculation is a male sexual dysfunction of the genitourinary and neurobehavioral domains, characterized by ejaculation that occurs sooner than desired, often within about 1 minute of penetration in lifelong cases, with poor perceived control and associated distress. Its cause is multifactorial, involving psychologic, neurobiologic, and relational factors. Management typically includes education, behavioral and psychosexual therapy, and selected pharmacologic treatment.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Yolk sac tumor is a malignant germ cell tumor, usually arising in the gonads, especially the testes or ovaries, and less often at extragonadal midline sites. It most commonly affects infants, children, and young adults and often produces alpha-fetoprotein. Patients present with a rapidly enlarging mass or site-specific symptoms; treatment typically combines surgical resection with platinum-based chemotherapy.

head: Pentoxyverine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pentoxyverine (carbetapentane) is a centrally acting non-opioid antitussive, chemically a piperidine derivative. Its precise mechanism is not well defined, but it suppresses the cough reflex via central action in the medulla and is not linked to a single established molecular target. It is used for symptomatic relief of nonproductive cough; notable adverse effects include drowsiness and anticholinergic effects, and it is contraindicated in glaucoma.
tail_desc: Vasomotor rhinitis is a form of nonallergic rhinitis affecting the nasal mucosa, thought to result from dysregulated autonomic or neurovascular control rather than infection or IgE-mediated allergy. It commonly causes chronic nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, postnasal drip, and sneezing, often triggered by irritants, odors, temperature changes, or alcohol, especially in adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and symptom control, often with intranasal therapies.

head: Cefdinir (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefdinir is an oral third-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections including community-acquired respiratory tract infections, acute otitis media, and uncomplicated skin infections. Notable adverse effects include diarrhea, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious cephalosporin hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Staphylococcus aureus infection is a bacterial infection that can affect the skin and soft tissues, bloodstream, lungs, bones, joints, or heart valves. It results from invasion by S. aureus, including toxin production in some syndromes. Clinical features range from boils and abscesses to pneumonia, sepsis, and endocarditis, affecting both healthy people and hospitalized or immunocompromised patients; management relies on drainage when needed and targeted antibiotic therapy.

head: Indomethacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Indomethacin is an indoleacetic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) that nonselectively inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for pain and inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, acute gout, and to promote closure of patent ductus arteriosus in neonates. Notable risks include serious gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding.
tail_desc: Exostosis is a benign bony outgrowth from the surface of a bone, affecting the skeletal system, often arising from cortical bone with a cartilage cap in osteochondroma or from chronic local irritation in sites such as the external auditory canal. It may be asymptomatic or cause a palpable mass, pain, deformity, or compression symptoms, typically in children or young adults for osteochondroma; management is usually observation, with surgical excision for symptoms, complications, or diagnostic concern.

head: Scopolamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Scopolamine is a tropane alkaloid antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent that competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, particularly in the central nervous system and vestibular pathways. It is established for prevention of motion sickness and postoperative nausea and vomiting, commonly as a transdermal patch. Notable adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, and confusion; it is contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Potassium bicarbonate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Potassium bicarbonate is an oral potassium salt and systemic alkalinizing agent. It dissociates to provide potassium and bicarbonate; bicarbonate buffers hydrogen ions and raises urinary and systemic pH, while potassium replenishes body potassium stores. It is used for prevention or treatment of hypokalemia and, in some formulations, urinary alkalinization. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Dyspepsia is a symptom complex of the upper gastrointestinal tract characterized by chronic or recurrent epigastric pain or burning, postprandial fullness, and early satiety. It may be functional, with no structural explanation, or secondary to disorders such as peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux, medications, or Helicobacter pylori infection. It occurs in adults and is managed by evaluating alarm features, addressing causes, testing/treating H. pylori, acid suppression, and lifestyle measures.

head: Mirtazapine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mirtazapine is a tetracyclic antidepressant of the piperazino-azepine class used primarily for major depressive disorder. It acts mainly by antagonizing central presynaptic alpha-2 adrenergic autoreceptors and heteroreceptors, increasing norepinephrine and serotonin release, and also blocks 5-HT2, 5-HT3, and histamine H1 receptors. Notable adverse effects include sedation and weight gain; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Endogenous depression is an older psychiatric term for a severe mood disorder within major depressive illness, affecting the central nervous system and characterized by depressive episodes thought to arise primarily from internal biological vulnerability rather than obvious external stressors. Typical features include pervasive low mood, anhedonia, psychomotor change, sleep and appetite disturbance, guilt, and suicidality, often in adults; management follows major depression treatment with antidepressants, psychotherapy, and, when severe, electroconvulsive therapy.

head: Ketoconazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ketoconazole is an imidazole antifungal that inhibits fungal lanosterol 14α-demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and disrupting cell membrane formation. It is used topically or as shampoo for seborrheic dermatitis, dandruff, and cutaneous fungal infections, while systemic use is now limited. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, making oral ketoconazole contraindicated in acute or chronic liver disease.
tail_desc: Tinea pedis is a superficial dermatophyte fungal infection of the skin of the feet, usually caused by Trichophyton species infecting keratinized tissue. It commonly affects adolescents and adults, especially in warm, moist, occlusive footwear settings, and causes interdigital scaling, erythema, pruritus, fissuring, or moccasin-type plantar hyperkeratosis. Management typically involves foot hygiene and topical antifungals, with oral therapy for extensive or refractory disease.

head: Mirtazapine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mirtazapine is a tetracyclic antidepressant of the piperazino-azepine class used primarily for major depressive disorder. It acts mainly by antagonizing central presynaptic alpha-2 adrenergic autoreceptors and heteroreceptors, increasing norepinephrine and serotonin release, and also blocks 5-HT2, 5-HT3, and histamine H1 receptors. Notable adverse effects include sedation and weight gain; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Major depressive disorder is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by persistent depressed mood or loss of interest with cognitive, sleep, appetite, energy, and psychomotor changes causing functional impairment. Its pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving genetic vulnerability, psychosocial stress, and altered neurotransmitter and neuroendocrine regulation. It occurs across the lifespan and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, and safety monitoring.

head: Paroxetine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Paroxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant of the phenylpiperidine class that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder and several anxiety-related disorders, including panic disorder, social anxiety disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, OCD, and PTSD. A notable adverse effect is sexual dysfunction; use is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Moxifloxacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Moxifloxacin is a fourth-generation fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including community-acquired pneumonia, acute bacterial sinusitis, acute exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and some skin or intra-abdominal infections. A notable risk is QT interval prolongation; fluoroquinolones can also cause tendon rupture.
tail_desc: Plague is an acute zoonotic bacterial infection caused by Yersinia pestis that primarily affects the lymphatic and systemic circulatory systems and may involve the lungs. It classically presents as bubonic, septicemic, or pneumonic disease, with fever, toxicity, painful swollen lymph nodes, sepsis, or rapidly progressive pneumonia, after rodent-flea exposure or inhalation. Management requires prompt effective antibiotics, isolation for pneumonic cases, and supportive care.

head: Salicylic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Salicylic acid is a keratolytic and topical salicylate, chemically a beta-hydroxy acid. It acts mainly by solubilizing intercellular cement in the stratum corneum, promoting desquamation; unlike acetylsalicylic acid, it is not used primarily for systemic COX inhibition. It is established for acne, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, warts, and calluses/corns. Notable risks include local irritation and salicylate toxicity with excessive topical use.
tail_desc: Plantar wart is a benign cutaneous infection of the plantar skin caused by human papillomavirus, usually HPV types 1, 2, 4, or 63, affecting the integumentary system. It presents as a rough, hyperkeratotic sole lesion with pain on pressure, often in children and young adults. Management typically involves observation, keratolytics such as salicylic acid, cryotherapy, and measures to reduce spread and recurrence.

head: Mechlorethamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mechlorethamine is a nitrogen mustard alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms highly reactive intermediates, causing DNA cross-linking and inhibition of DNA replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used in regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and as topical therapy for mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma). A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is also a potent vesicant if extravasated.
tail_desc: Mycosis fungoides and its variants are the most common primary cutaneous T-cell lymphomas, involving the skin. They arise from malignant skin-homing mature T lymphocytes, typically CD4-positive. Patients, usually older adults, develop chronic patches, plaques, tumors, or erythroderma that may itch and can progress over years. Management is stage-directed, emphasizing skin-directed therapies early and systemic treatment for advanced or refractory disease.

head: Testosterone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Testosterone is an endogenous androgenic-anabolic steroid hormone used as testosterone replacement therapy for male hypogonadism and delayed puberty, and in some masculinizing hormone regimens. It acts as an agonist at the androgen receptor, with some effects mediated after conversion to dihydrotestosterone or estradiol. Notable risks include erythrocytosis and it is contraindicated in men with prostate or breast cancer.
tail_desc: Isolated growth hormone deficiency is an endocrine pituitary disorder in which deficient secretion of growth hormone occurs without other pituitary hormone deficits, due to congenital genetic defects, pituitary developmental abnormalities, or acquired hypothalamic-pituitary damage. It typically presents in childhood with proportionate short stature, reduced growth velocity, delayed bone age, and sometimes neonatal hypoglycemia. Management is usually recombinant human growth hormone replacement with endocrine monitoring.

head: Benzoyl peroxide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Benzoyl peroxide is a topical organic peroxide used mainly as an anti-acne agent. It decomposes in the skin to release free-radical oxygen species, producing bactericidal activity against Cutibacterium acnes and mild keratolytic/comedolytic effects. It is established for acne vulgaris treatment, alone or with other agents. Common adverse effects include local skin irritation, dryness, erythema, and peeling.
tail_desc: Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder affecting sebum-rich areas, linked to abnormal inflammatory responses to Malassezia yeast and skin barrier dysfunction. It causes greasy scales, erythema, and dandruff on the scalp, face, and trunk, commonly in infants and adults. Management typically uses topical antifungals, low-potency corticosteroids, and gentle skin care.

head: Desloratadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Desloratadine is a second-generation tricyclic antihistamine and the active metabolite of loratadine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing allergic symptoms. It is used for seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, although it is generally less sedating than first-generation antihistamines; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to desloratadine or loratadine.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Systemic lupus erythematosus is a chronic multisystem autoimmune connective tissue disease in which loss of immune tolerance leads to autoantibody production and immune-complex–mediated inflammation affecting skin, joints, kidneys, blood cells, serosa, and the nervous system. It commonly affects women of childbearing age and causes fatigue, rash, photosensitivity, arthritis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities; management uses sun protection, hydroxychloroquine, corticosteroids, and other immunosuppressive or biologic therapies tailored to organ involvement.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Folliculotropic mycosis fungoides is a variant of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma in which malignant skin-homing T lymphocytes preferentially infiltrate hair follicles. It typically affects adults and presents with infiltrated plaques, acneiform or follicular papules, alopecia, and often severe pruritus, frequently on the head and neck. Management depends on stage and usually involves skin-directed therapy, with systemic treatment for more extensive or refractory disease.

head: Ferrous fumarate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ferrous fumarate is an oral iron salt, a ferrous (Fe2+) fumarate compound used as a hematinic. It supplies elemental iron for hemoglobin synthesis and repletion of body iron stores rather than acting through a specific receptor target. It is established for prevention and treatment of iron deficiency anemia. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation, especially constipation, and it is contraindicated in iron overload states such as hemochromatosis.
tail_desc: Iron deficiency anemia is a microcytic, hypochromic anemia of the hematologic system caused by insufficient iron for hemoglobin synthesis, most often due to chronic blood loss, inadequate intake, or malabsorption. Typical features include fatigue, pallor, weakness, dyspnea, and sometimes pica, occurring commonly in menstruating women, pregnant patients, children, and older adults. Management targets the cause and restores iron, usually with oral or intravenous supplementation.

head: Rizatriptan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Rizatriptan is a triptan antimigraine agent, chemically an indole derivative, that acts as a selective agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors to promote cranial vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine with or without aura. A notable contraindication is ischemic heart disease due to risk of coronary vasospasm.
tail_desc: Migraine disorder is a common primary neurologic headache disorder involving the central nervous system, with pathophysiology linked to genetic susceptibility, trigeminovascular activation, and abnormal sensory processing. It causes recurrent attacks of moderate to severe, often unilateral throbbing headache with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia, sometimes aura, affecting women more often than men. Management includes trigger avoidance, acute abortive therapy, and preventive medications for frequent or disabling attacks.

head: Carboplatin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: malignant dysgerminomatous germ cell tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Carboplatin is a second-generation platinum coordination compound (alkylating-like antineoplastic) that forms covalent DNA intra- and interstrand crosslinks, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and triggering apoptosis. It is established for ovarian cancer and is widely used in lung and other solid tumors. A notable toxicity is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia; severe hypersensitivity can also occur.
tail_desc: Malignant dysgerminomatous germ cell tumor of the ovary is a malignant ovarian germ cell neoplasm, essentially the ovarian counterpart of seminoma, arising from primitive germ cells. It typically affects adolescents and young women and may present with abdominal pain, pelvic mass, or distension. Management is usually fertility-sparing surgical staging with unilateral oophorectomy when feasible, with platinum-based chemotherapy for advanced or recurrent disease.

head: Etidronic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Etidronic acid (etidronate) is a first-generation, non-nitrogen bisphosphonate that binds hydroxyapatite in bone and is taken up by osteoclasts, where it inhibits bone resorption by disrupting ATP-dependent processes. It has been used for Paget disease of bone and heterotopic ossification. A notable adverse effect is osteomalacia with prolonged or excessive dosing; it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment and significant esophageal abnormalities.
tail_desc: Bone Paget disease is a chronic metabolic bone disorder characterized by focal increases in osteoclast-mediated bone resorption followed by disorganized, excessive bone formation, affecting the skeletal system. It usually occurs in older adults and may cause bone pain, deformity, enlargement, fractures, hearing loss, or arthritis. Management is directed at symptom control and suppression of abnormal bone turnover, typically with bisphosphonates.

head: Pamidronic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pamidronic acid is a nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate antiresorptive agent that binds hydroxyapatite in bone and inhibits osteoclast-mediated resorption, primarily via inhibition of farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway. It is used for hypercalcemia of malignancy, Paget disease of bone, and osteolytic bone metastases or multiple myeloma. Notable adverse effects include osteonecrosis of the jaw and renal toxicity.
tail_desc: Humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy is a paraneoplastic endocrine-metabolic disorder causing systemic calcium imbalance, most often in patients with solid tumors. It usually results from tumor secretion of parathyroid hormone-related protein, which increases bone resorption and renal calcium retention. Patients develop often severe hypercalcemia with dehydration, weakness, confusion, constipation, and polyuria; management focuses on urgent calcium lowering and treatment of the underlying cancer.

head: Trabectedin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary breast ovarian cancer syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Trabectedin is a marine-derived tetrahydroisoquinoline alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds the minor groove of DNA, alkylating guanine residues and disrupting transcription-coupled nucleotide excision repair, leading to DNA damage and apoptosis. It is approved for unresectable or metastatic soft tissue sarcoma and, with pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, recurrent platinum-sensitive ovarian cancer. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression, especially neutropenia.
tail_desc: Hereditary breast ovarian cancer syndrome is an inherited cancer predisposition disorder affecting the breast and female reproductive system, most often caused by germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 pathogenic variants that impair homologous recombination DNA repair. It is characterized by markedly increased risks of early-onset breast and ovarian cancer, also male breast, pancreatic, and prostate cancers. Management centers on genetic counseling/testing, intensive surveillance, and consideration of risk-reducing surgery.

head: Estramustine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Estramustine is an antineoplastic estradiol–nitrogen mustard conjugate used mainly for palliative treatment of metastatic or progressive prostate cancer. It binds microtubule-associated proteins and tubulin, disrupting microtubule assembly and mitosis, with additional estrogenic effects. A notable adverse effect is thromboembolism; it is generally contraindicated in patients with active thrombophlebitis or thromboembolic disorders.
tail_desc: Prostate cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the prostate gland in the male genitourinary system, usually an adenocarcinoma arising from prostatic epithelial cells. It mainly affects older men and may be asymptomatic or cause urinary obstruction, hematuria, bone pain, or weight loss when advanced. Management ranges from active surveillance for low-risk disease to surgery, radiotherapy, androgen-deprivation therapy, and systemic treatment for metastatic cancer.

head: Benzatropine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary late onset Parkinson disease (a disease)
head_desc: Benztropine is a synthetic antimuscarinic antiparkinson agent, chemically a tropane derivative with additional antihistaminic activity. It primarily blocks central muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, helping restore dopaminergic–cholinergic balance in the basal ganglia. It is used for Parkinsonism and drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms. Notable adverse effects include anticholinergic toxicity such as dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, and urinary retention.
tail_desc: Hereditary late-onset Parkinson disease is a genetic form of Parkinson disease, a neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system. It is associated with inherited variants that promote degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and abnormal alpha-synuclein-related pathology in some forms. It typically presents in later adulthood with bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and postural instability, and is managed with standard symptomatic Parkinson therapies plus genetic counseling.

head: Enoxacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Enoxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibacterial. It acts by inhibiting bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It has been used for susceptible urinary tract and other bacterial infections. A notable adverse effect is tendinopathy, including risk of tendon rupture; it is generally contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to quinolones.
tail_desc: Gonorrhea is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection of the genitourinary tract, rectum, pharynx, or conjunctiva caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae. It often causes urethritis with dysuria and purulent discharge, cervicitis, pelvic pain, or may be asymptomatic, especially in women; sexually active adolescents and adults are commonly affected. Management centers on recommended antibiotic therapy, testing for coinfections, partner treatment, and prevention of transmission.

head: Enasidenib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with t(6;9)(p23;q34) (a disease)
head_desc: Enasidenib is an oral small-molecule isocitrate dehydrogenase-2 (IDH2) inhibitor that selectively targets mutant IDH2 enzymes, lowering the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate and promoting myeloid differentiation. It is approved for adults with relapsed or refractory acute myeloid leukemia harboring an IDH2 mutation. A notable adverse effect is differentiation syndrome, which can be severe or fatal if not promptly recognized and treated.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with t(6;9)(p23;q34) is a rare, high-risk AML of the hematopoietic system defined by the DEK::NUP214 fusion from t(6;9). It often affects children and younger adults, commonly presents with marrow failure symptoms, basophilia, and frequent FLT3-ITD co-mutation, and carries an adverse prognosis. Management follows intensive AML induction/consolidation, FLT3-directed therapy when indicated, and often allogeneic stem-cell transplantation in first remission.

head: Tretamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: malignant Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Tretamine is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Malignant Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor of the ovary is a rare ovarian sex cord-stromal malignancy, usually arising in adolescents or young women. It contains Sertoli and Leydig cell elements and may secrete androgens, causing abdominal or pelvic mass, pain, menstrual disturbance, and virilization. Management is primarily surgical, with staging and fertility-sparing approaches when appropriate; chemotherapy is considered for advanced, poorly differentiated, or recurrent disease.

head: Moexipril (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Moexipril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug hydrolyzed to moexiprilat, which inhibits ACE and reduces conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, lowering vasoconstriction and aldosterone secretion. It is used for treatment of hypertension. Notable adverse effects include cough and angioedema, and it is contraindicated during pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Mantle cell lymphoma is an aggressive mature B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma of the lymphatic/hematologic system, usually driven by t(11;14) causing cyclin D1 overexpression. It typically affects older adults, often men, and commonly presents with generalized lymphadenopathy, bone marrow and gastrointestinal involvement, splenomegaly, and B symptoms. Management usually involves immunochemotherapy, targeted agents, and selected patients may undergo stem cell transplantation.

head: Rabeprazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Rabeprazole is a substituted benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, healing and maintenance of erosive esophagitis, duodenal ulcer, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori eradication. Notable adverse effects include headache and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to rabeprazole or other proton pump inhibitors.
tail_desc: Hyperinsulinism is an endocrine/metabolic disorder of pancreatic beta-cell insulin dysregulation causing inappropriate insulin secretion relative to blood glucose, leading to recurrent hypoglycemia. It often presents in neonates and infants, but can occur later, with jitteriness, lethargy, seizures, poor feeding, or loss of consciousness. Management focuses on rapid maintenance of euglycemia, medical suppression of insulin release, dietary support, and sometimes pancreatic surgery.

head: Aminobenzoic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Aminobenzoic acid, usually referring to para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), is a benzoic acid derivative and B-complex–related compound rather than a standard targeted drug. It has no well-defined therapeutic molecular target; historically, potassium para-aminobenzoate has been used clinically for Peyronie’s disease and certain fibrotic dermatoses. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal intolerance, and it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to PABA-related compounds.
tail_desc: Balanitis xerotica obliterans is a chronic inflammatory sclerosing dermatosis of the male genital skin, generally regarded as genital lichen sclerosus affecting the glans and foreskin. It causes porcelain-white atrophic plaques, scarring phimosis, painful fissuring, meatal or urethral narrowing, and urinary symptoms, mainly in uncircumcised males. Management centers on potent topical corticosteroids, with circumcision or surgery for refractory disease or stenosis.

head: Biperiden (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Biperiden is a centrally acting anticholinergic antiparkinsonian agent, chemically a piperidine derivative. It acts mainly as a muscarinic acetylcholine receptor antagonist, reducing cholinergic activity in the striatum. It is used to treat Parkinsonism and drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms. Notable adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, and confusion; it is contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Early-onset parkinsonism-intellectual disability syndrome is a rare neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative movement disorder affecting the central nervous system. It is characterized by childhood or adolescent onset of parkinsonism associated with intellectual disability, often with developmental delay and additional neurologic abnormalities. The condition is considered genetically determined, and management is supportive and symptomatic, including multidisciplinary developmental care and treatment of parkinsonian features.

head: Chloramphenicol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chloramphenicol is a broad-spectrum amphenicol antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocking peptidyl transferase activity. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including meningitis, rickettsial infections, and topical treatment of bacterial conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is dose-independent aplastic anemia; use is also limited by gray baby syndrome in neonates.
tail_desc: Boutonneuse fever is a tick-borne rickettsial infection affecting the systemic vasculature and skin, caused by Rickettsia conorii, classically in Mediterranean regions. It typically presents in children or adults with acute fever, headache, myalgia, a maculopapular rash, and a dark inoculation eschar (“tache noire”) at the tick bite site. Management is prompt antibiotic therapy, usually doxycycline, with supportive care.

head: Bevacizumab (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary breast ovarian cancer syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Bevacizumab is a recombinant humanized monoclonal antibody and anti-angiogenic agent that binds vascular endothelial growth factor A (VEGF-A), preventing activation of VEGF receptors and thereby inhibiting tumor neovascularization. It is approved for several solid tumors, including metastatic colorectal cancer, non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer, glioblastoma, renal cell carcinoma, cervical cancer, ovarian cancer, and hepatocellular carcinoma; notable risks include gastrointestinal perforation and serious hemorrhage.
tail_desc: Hereditary breast ovarian cancer syndrome is an inherited cancer predisposition disorder affecting the breast and female reproductive system, most often caused by germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 pathogenic variants that impair homologous recombination DNA repair. It is characterized by markedly increased risks of early-onset breast and ovarian cancer, also male breast, pancreatic, and prostate cancers. Management centers on genetic counseling/testing, intensive surveillance, and consideration of risk-reducing surgery.

head: Mebicar (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mebicar is an anxiolytic of the tetramethylbicyclooctanedione family, used mainly in some Eastern European/CIS countries for anxiety, irritability, and related neurotic symptoms. Its precise mechanism of action and primary molecular targets are not well established in the international literature, though it is described as modulating central neurotransmitter systems. Reported adverse effects are usually mild, including dyspepsia or allergic reactions; use is generally avoided in known hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Attention deficit-hyperactivity disorder is a common neurodevelopmental disorder affecting brain networks that regulate attention, impulse control, and activity level. Its cause is multifactorial, with strong genetic contribution and altered neurotransmission implicated. It typically begins in childhood and features developmentally inappropriate inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity causing functional impairment. Management includes behavioral and educational interventions, parent training, and stimulant or nonstimulant medications.

head: Tazarotene (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tazarotene is a topical third-generation retinoid (acetylenic retinoid) prodrug that is converted to tazarotenic acid, a selective agonist of retinoic acid receptors RAR-β and RAR-γ, modulating keratinocyte differentiation and proliferation. It is approved for acne vulgaris, plaque psoriasis, and photodamaged skin/facial fine wrinkling. Common adverse effects include local irritation, erythema, and peeling; it is contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenic risk.
tail_desc: Acne is a common chronic inflammatory disorder of the pilosebaceous unit of the skin, driven by follicular hyperkeratinization, excess sebum production, Cutibacterium acnes proliferation, and inflammation. It typically affects adolescents and young adults, causing comedones, papules, pustules, nodules, and possible scarring on the face, chest, and back. Management includes topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, antibiotics, hormonal therapy, and oral isotretinoin for severe disease.

head: Phentermine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Phentermine is a sympathomimetic amine anorectic, structurally related to amphetamine, used as a short-term adjunct to diet and exercise for exogenous obesity. It promotes weight loss primarily by increasing central norepinephrine release, with lesser effects on dopamine and serotonin, reducing appetite via hypothalamic pathways. Notable risks include increased blood pressure and heart rate, and it is contraindicated in cardiovascular disease and during or within 14 days of MAOI use.
tail_desc: Monogenic obesity is a rare genetic disorder of energy-balance regulation affecting the neuroendocrine/hypothalamic pathways that control appetite and body weight. It usually results from single-gene defects, often in the leptin-melanocortin pathway, causing severe early-onset obesity with marked hyperphagia, sometimes with endocrine or developmental abnormalities. Management centers on specialist genetic evaluation, intensive weight-management support, and, in selected molecularly defined cases, targeted therapy.

head: Epicriptine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Epicriptine is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Cerebellar degeneration is a neurologic disorder involving progressive loss of cerebellar neurons, especially Purkinje cells, leading to impaired coordination and balance. Causes include hereditary ataxias, alcohol toxicity, paraneoplastic or autoimmune processes, and other neurodegenerative conditions. Typical features are gait and limb ataxia, dysarthria, nystagmus, and tremor, often in adults. Management targets the underlying cause when identifiable and uses rehabilitation and supportive care.

head: Acrivastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acrivastine is a second-generation antihistamine of the alkylamine/propylamine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used for symptomatic relief of seasonal allergic rhinitis and urticaria, including sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itching. Compared with older antihistamines it is less sedating, but drowsiness can still occur; caution is advised in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Atopic conjunctivitis is a chronic allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, especially the conjunctiva, usually associated with atopic dermatitis and other atopic disease. It is driven by IgE-mediated and T-cell–mediated hypersensitivity. Typical features include intense itching, redness, tearing, mucous discharge, photophobia, and eyelid eczema, often in adolescents or adults with severe atopy. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and specialist-directed anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Flunisolide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Flunisolide is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid (glucocorticoid) used mainly as an intranasal or inhaled anti-inflammatory agent. It acts by binding intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and reduce airway or nasal mucosal inflammation. Established indications include allergic rhinitis and asthma prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include local candidiasis with inhaled use and nasal irritation or epistaxis intranasally.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Desonide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Desonide is a low-potency topical corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class that activates intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to suppress inflammatory and immune gene expression. It is established for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses, including atopic dermatitis and other eczematous or allergic inflammatory skin conditions. A notable adverse effect is local skin atrophy with prolonged use, especially under occlusion.
tail_desc: Crohn disease is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the gastrointestinal tract, most often the terminal ileum and colon. It involves dysregulated, transmural immune-mediated inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals, causing abdominal pain, diarrhea, weight loss, fatigue, and complications such as strictures, fistulas, and perianal disease, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood. Management focuses on inducing and maintaining remission with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, biologic, nutritional, and sometimes surgical therapy.

head: Ethinylestradiol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ethinylestradiol is a synthetic steroidal estrogen, commonly used in combined oral contraceptives and in some hormone replacement regimens. It acts as an agonist at estrogen receptors (ERα and ERβ), altering transcription of estrogen-responsive genes and suppressing gonadotropin secretion to inhibit ovulation. Notable risks include venous thromboembolism, and it is contraindicated in patients with active thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: obsolete atrophic vulva is a disease or medical condition.

head: Cisplatin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cisplatin is a platinum-based alkylating-like antineoplastic agent that forms intra- and interstrand DNA crosslinks, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and triggering apoptosis; it does not act through a single protein target. It is used for multiple solid tumors, including testicular, ovarian, bladder, and lung cancers. A notable toxicity is nephrotoxicity, and it can also cause ototoxicity and peripheral neuropathy.
tail_desc: Ovarian mucinous adenocarcinoma is a malignant epithelial ovarian tumor of the female reproductive system characterized by invasive mucin-producing glandular epithelium. It often presents as a large unilateral adnexal mass with abdominal distension, pelvic pain, or gastrointestinal symptoms, most often in adult women. Management is primarily surgical staging and cytoreduction, with adjuvant chemotherapy considered according to stage and pathologic risk.

head: Sorafenib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sorafenib is an oral multikinase inhibitor of the biaryl urea class that targets RAF kinases and several receptor tyrosine kinases, including VEGFR, PDGFR, FLT3, RET, and KIT, thereby suppressing tumor-cell proliferation and angiogenesis. It is approved for unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma, advanced renal cell carcinoma, and radioactive iodine-refractory differentiated thyroid carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is hand-foot skin reaction.
tail_desc: Hepatocellular carcinoma is the most common primary malignant tumor of the liver, arising from hepatocytes, usually in the setting of chronic liver disease or cirrhosis caused by hepatitis B or C, alcohol-related liver injury, or metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease. Patients, often adults with chronic liver disease, may present with weight loss, abdominal pain, jaundice, or decompensation. Management depends on stage and liver function, using resection, ablation, transplantation, embolization, and systemic therapy.

head: Dexbrompheniramine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dexbrompheniramine is a first-generation alkylamine antihistamine, the dextrorotatory isomer of brompheniramine. It acts primarily as a competitive antagonist/inverse agonist at histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated symptoms. It is used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and other allergic conditions, as well as common-cold symptoms. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it is generally contraindicated in newborns and during monoamine oxidase inhibitor therapy.
tail_desc: Atopic conjunctivitis is a chronic allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, especially the conjunctiva, usually associated with atopic dermatitis and other atopic disease. It is driven by IgE-mediated and T-cell–mediated hypersensitivity. Typical features include intense itching, redness, tearing, mucous discharge, photophobia, and eyelid eczema, often in adolescents or adults with severe atopy. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and specialist-directed anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Citalopram (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Citalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, a bicyclic phthalane derivative, that inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder and is also used for panic disorder in some jurisdictions. A notable safety concern is dose-dependent QT interval prolongation, making it contraindicated with pimozide and requiring caution in patients with congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Endogenous depression is an older psychiatric term for a severe mood disorder within major depressive illness, affecting the central nervous system and characterized by depressive episodes thought to arise primarily from internal biological vulnerability rather than obvious external stressors. Typical features include pervasive low mood, anhedonia, psychomotor change, sleep and appetite disturbance, guilt, and suicidality, often in adults; management follows major depression treatment with antidepressants, psychotherapy, and, when severe, electroconvulsive therapy.

head: Trihexyphenidyl (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Trihexyphenidyl is a centrally acting antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) piperidine derivative used mainly for Parkinson disease and for drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms, especially acute dystonia and parkinsonism. It acts by competitively blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the CNS, helping restore dopaminergic-cholinergic balance in the basal ganglia. Notable adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, confusion, and worsening angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: X-linked parkinsonism-spasticity syndrome is a rare inherited neurodegenerative movement disorder affecting the central nervous system. It is caused by pathogenic variants in ATP6AP2 on the X chromosome and typically affects males. Core features include juvenile-onset parkinsonism, spasticity, dystonia, and variable cognitive impairment. Management is supportive and symptomatic, including antiparkinsonian therapy, rehabilitation, and multidisciplinary care.

head: Dexchlorpheniramine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dexchlorpheniramine is a first-generation alkylamine antihistamine, the dextrorotatory isomer of chlorpheniramine. It acts primarily as an antagonist or inverse agonist at histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is used for allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and other allergy-related conditions. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it should be used cautiously in patients with angle-closure glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Atopic conjunctivitis is a chronic allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, especially the conjunctiva, usually associated with atopic dermatitis and other atopic disease. It is driven by IgE-mediated and T-cell–mediated hypersensitivity. Typical features include intense itching, redness, tearing, mucous discharge, photophobia, and eyelid eczema, often in adolescents or adults with severe atopy. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and specialist-directed anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Calcium lactate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcium lactate is an oral calcium salt used as a mineral supplement and antacid. It dissociates to provide elemental calcium, supporting neuromuscular function, bone mineralization, and correction of calcium deficiency, without a specific receptor target. Established uses include prevention or treatment of hypocalcemia and calcium supplementation. Notable risks include hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Tranylcypromine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tranylcypromine is a nonselective, irreversible monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI) of the cyclopropylamine class that inhibits both MAO-A and MAO-B, increasing synaptic norepinephrine, serotonin, and dopamine. It is used for major depressive disorder, particularly treatment-resistant or atypical depression. A notable risk is hypertensive crisis with tyramine-containing foods or interacting sympathomimetic/serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Cimetidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cimetidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It is used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and other acid-related disorders. A notable adverse effect is inhibition of cytochrome P450 enzymes, causing clinically important drug interactions; gynecomastia can also occur.
tail_desc: Mastocytosis is a rare clonal mast cell disorder affecting the skin and/or systemic organs, caused in many cases by activating KIT mutations that lead to abnormal mast cell accumulation and mediator release. It may present in children as mainly cutaneous disease, while adults more often have systemic involvement with flushing, pruritus, urticaria pigmentosa, anaphylaxis, gastrointestinal symptoms, and bone disease; management focuses on trigger avoidance, anti-mediator therapy, and treatment of advanced systemic disease.

head: Folic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Folic acid is a water-soluble B-vitamin (vitamin B9) and folate supplement that, after reduction to tetrahydrofolate, serves as a cofactor for one-carbon transfer reactions required for purine/thymidylate synthesis and erythropoiesis. It is used to treat and prevent folate deficiency, including megaloblastic anemia, and for neural tube defect prophylaxis in pregnancy. It can mask vitamin B12 deficiency and allow neurologic injury to progress.
tail_desc: Iron deficiency anemia is a microcytic, hypochromic anemia of the hematologic system caused by insufficient iron for hemoglobin synthesis, most often due to chronic blood loss, inadequate intake, or malabsorption. Typical features include fatigue, pallor, weakness, dyspnea, and sometimes pica, occurring commonly in menstruating women, pregnant patients, children, and older adults. Management targets the cause and restores iron, usually with oral or intravenous supplementation.

head: Enfuvirtide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Enfuvirtide is a synthetic 36-amino-acid HIV fusion inhibitor peptide. It binds the gp41 subunit of the HIV-1 envelope glycoprotein, blocking conformational changes required for fusion of viral and host cell membranes and preventing viral entry. It is used, with other antiretrovirals, for treatment-experienced patients with HIV-1 infection. A notable adverse effect is frequent injection-site reactions.
tail_desc: AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome) is the advanced stage of HIV infection, a systemic infectious disease that primarily damages the immune system through progressive depletion of CD4+ T lymphocytes. It causes severe immunosuppression, leading to opportunistic infections, certain cancers, weight loss, and constitutional symptoms, most often in untreated people with HIV. Management centers on combination antiretroviral therapy, prevention and treatment of opportunistic diseases, and ongoing supportive care.

head: Daclatasvir (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Daclatasvir is a direct-acting antiviral of the NS5A inhibitor class used against hepatitis C virus (HCV). It binds HCV nonstructural protein 5A, disrupting viral RNA replication and virion assembly. It is indicated, in combination with other antivirals, for treatment of chronic hepatitis C infection. A notable risk is severe bradycardia when coadministered with amiodarone, especially with sofosbuvir-containing regimens.
tail_desc: Hepatitis C virus infection is a viral infectious disease of the liver caused by hepatitis C virus, leading to hepatic inflammation that may become chronic and progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, or hepatocellular carcinoma. Many patients are initially asymptomatic; others have fatigue, jaundice, or elevated liver enzymes. It affects exposed adults and is managed primarily with direct-acting antiviral therapy and liver disease monitoring.

head: Povidone K30 (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Povidone K30 is a grade of povidone (polyvinylpyrrolidone), a synthetic water-soluble polymer used primarily as a pharmaceutical excipient rather than an active drug. It has no specific molecular target or therapeutic mechanism of action. Its established role is in tablet and capsule formulations as a binder, solubilizer, and film-forming agent. A notable concern is hypersensitivity, with povidone allergy reported rarely.
tail_desc: Dry eye syndrome is a common ocular surface disorder of the lacrimal functional unit in which reduced tear production or excessive tear evaporation causes tear-film instability and hyperosmolarity, leading to surface inflammation and epithelial damage. Typical symptoms are dryness, burning, foreign-body sensation, fluctuating vision, and reflex tearing, especially in older adults. Management focuses on lubricating tears, environmental measures, eyelid/meibomian care, and treating underlying inflammation or contributing causes.

head: Dextroamphetamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dextroamphetamine is a central nervous system stimulant of the amphetamine class. It increases synaptic norepinephrine and dopamine chiefly by promoting presynaptic release via monoamine transporters, including DAT and NET. It is established for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and narcolepsy. Notable adverse effects include insomnia, and it is contraindicated in patients receiving monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Balo concentric sclerosis is a rare inflammatory demyelinating disorder of the central nervous system, usually regarded as a variant of multiple sclerosis. It is characterized pathologically by concentric rings of demyelination in cerebral white matter. Patients, often young adults, may present with acute focal neurologic deficits, headache, seizures, or cognitive changes; management generally follows other severe demyelinating attacks, especially high-dose corticosteroids, with escalation in refractory cases.

head: Promazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Promazine is a first-generation phenothiazine antipsychotic with sedative and antiemetic properties. It acts primarily by antagonizing central dopamine D2 receptors, with additional anticholinergic, antihistaminic, and alpha-adrenergic blocking effects. It has been used for schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, and for agitation. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation, and it can also cause extrapyramidal symptoms and orthostatic hypotension.
tail_desc: Acquired angioedema is a rare bradykinin-mediated disorder of the vascular/immune system causing recurrent, nonpruritic swelling of the skin and submucosa, often involving the face, extremities, gastrointestinal tract, or airway. It usually occurs in adults due to acquired C1 inhibitor deficiency or autoantibodies, sometimes linked to lymphoproliferative or autoimmune disease. Management focuses on treating acute attacks, airway protection, and addressing the underlying disorder.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune connective tissue disease affecting mainly the skin and, in systemic forms, multiple organs. It involves loss of immune tolerance with autoantibody formation and immune-complex–mediated inflammation. Typical features include photosensitive rash, oral ulcers, arthritis, serositis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities, most often in women of childbearing age. Management focuses on sun protection, disease control with corticosteroids, antimalarials, and other immunosuppressive agents, plus organ-specific monitoring.

head: Flumethasone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Flumethasone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts by binding the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It has been used mainly in topical dermatologic and otic/ophthalmic preparations for inflammatory conditions. Like other corticosteroids, prolonged local use can cause skin atrophy, and it is generally contraindicated in untreated infections at the application site.
tail_desc: Occupational dermatitis is an inflammatory skin disorder, usually affecting the hands, caused by workplace exposure to irritants or allergens that disrupt the epidermal barrier or trigger delayed hypersensitivity. It presents in exposed workers with pruritus, erythema, scaling, fissuring, and sometimes vesicles or chronic lichenification. Management centers on identifying and reducing occupational exposure, skin protection, emollients, and topical anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Idelalisib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Idelalisib is an oral small-molecule phosphoinositide 3-kinase delta (PI3Kδ) inhibitor that selectively blocks PI3Kδ signaling in B cells. It is approved for certain relapsed hematologic malignancies, including chronic lymphocytic leukemia, follicular lymphoma, and small lymphocytic lymphoma. A notable serious adverse effect is severe hepatotoxicity, and it can also cause serious diarrhea/colitis and pneumonitis.
tail_desc: Neoplasm of mature B-cells is a lymphoid hematologic malignancy of differentiated B lymphocytes, involving blood, bone marrow, lymph nodes, or extranodal lymphoid tissues. It arises from clonal proliferation and accumulation of mature B cells, often associated with acquired genetic abnormalities. Presentation varies by subtype but commonly includes lymphadenopathy, cytopenias, marrow infiltration, and systemic “B” symptoms, with management directed by subtype and stage using observation, chemoimmunotherapy, targeted agents, and sometimes radiation.

head: Pasireotide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pasireotide is a somatostatin analog cyclohexapeptide that binds multiple somatostatin receptor subtypes, especially SSTR5 (and also SSTR1, 2, and 3), suppressing secretion of ACTH and growth hormone. It is approved for Cushing’s disease and acromegaly, and a long-acting form is used for acromegaly. A notable adverse effect is hyperglycemia, sometimes severe.
tail_desc: Cushing syndrome is an endocrine disorder caused by chronic excess glucocorticoid exposure, either exogenous from corticosteroid therapy or endogenous from ACTH-secreting pituitary tumors, adrenal tumors, or ectopic ACTH production. It affects adults and children and commonly causes central obesity, moon facies, proximal muscle weakness, hypertension, glucose intolerance, skin thinning, and purple striae. Management targets the cause, including tapering steroids, surgical resection, radiation, or cortisol-lowering drugs.

head: Loteprednol etabonate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Loteprednol etabonate is a topical ophthalmic corticosteroid, a chloromethyl ester glucocorticoid, that binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to suppress phospholipase A2–dependent inflammatory mediator production and cytokine expression. It is used for steroid-responsive ocular inflammation, including postoperative inflammation and pain and allergic conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, so caution is warranted in patients with glaucoma.
tail_desc: Infectious anterior uveitis is an intraocular inflammatory disorder of the anterior uveal tract, usually caused by direct ocular infection, commonly by herpes simplex virus, varicella-zoster virus, or less often bacteria, fungi, or parasites. It typically presents with ocular pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision, and anterior chamber cells/flare. Management targets the pathogen with appropriate antimicrobial therapy, often with adjunctive cycloplegia and carefully supervised anti-inflammatory treatment.

head: Cyclophosphamide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: primary central nervous system lymphoma (a disease)
head_desc: Cyclophosphamide is an oxazaphosphorine alkylating agent (nitrogen mustard prodrug) that is hepatically activated to metabolites forming DNA cross-links, thereby impairing DNA replication and inducing cell death without a single specific molecular target. It is used for multiple malignancies, including lymphomas, leukemias, and breast cancer, and as an immunosuppressant in severe autoimmune disease. A notable adverse effect is hemorrhagic cystitis.
tail_desc: Primary central nervous system lymphoma is an aggressive extranodal non-Hodgkin lymphoma, usually diffuse large B-cell type, confined to the brain, leptomeninges, spinal cord, or eyes. It typically presents with focal neurologic deficits, neuropsychiatric change, seizures, or visual symptoms, especially in older adults and immunocompromised patients. Management generally centers on high-dose methotrexate–based chemotherapy, with radiotherapy used selectively.

head: Leflunomide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
