head_desc: Leflunomide is an isoxazole immunomodulatory disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) that is converted to the active metabolite teriflunomide, which inhibits dihydroorotate dehydrogenase and suppresses de novo pyrimidine synthesis in proliferating lymphocytes. It is approved for rheumatoid arthritis, including psoriatic arthritis in some regions. Notable risks include hepatotoxicity and marked teratogenicity, making pregnancy a contraindication.
tail_desc: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic systemic autoimmune inflammatory disease primarily affecting synovial joints. Immune-mediated synovitis causes pain, swelling, morning stiffness, and progressive cartilage and bone destruction, often in a symmetric polyarticular pattern, with extra-articular manifestations possible. It occurs most often in adults, especially women. Management aims to suppress inflammation and prevent joint damage using DMARDs, biologics, and supportive care.

head: Triamterene (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Triamterene is a potassium-sparing diuretic of the pteridine class that inhibits epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the distal renal tubule and collecting duct, reducing sodium reabsorption and limiting potassium excretion. It is used for edema and hypertension, often with a thiazide diuretic. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, and it is contraindicated in anuria, severe renal impairment, or preexisting hyperkalemia.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Bendroflumethiazide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bendroflumethiazide is a thiazide diuretic (benzothiadiazine derivative) that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing renal sodium and water excretion. It is used for hypertension and edema, including edema associated with heart failure. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Brimonidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Brimonidine is an imidazoline-derived selective alpha2-adrenergic receptor agonist. It lowers intraocular pressure mainly by reducing aqueous humor production and increasing uveoscleral outflow, and is approved for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension; a topical gel formulation is also used for persistent facial erythema of rosacea. A notable adverse effect is ocular hyperemia or allergic conjunctivitis.
tail_desc: Open-angle glaucoma is a chronic optic neuropathy of the eye, usually associated with impaired aqueous humor outflow through an anatomically open anterior chamber angle, often causing elevated intraocular pressure. It typically affects older adults and is characterized by progressive peripheral visual field loss and optic nerve cupping, often without early symptoms. Management focuses on lowering intraocular pressure with topical medications, laser trabeculoplasty, or surgery.

head: Pantoprazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pantoprazole is a substituted benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing basal and stimulated acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, erosive esophagitis, and other acid-related disorders, including pathological hypersecretory states such as Zollinger–Ellison syndrome. A notable adverse effect is hypomagnesemia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Gastrin secretion abnormality is a disorder of gastrointestinal endocrine regulation in which gastrin production is abnormally increased or decreased, disrupting gastric acid secretion. Causes include gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumors, antral G-cell hyperplasia, or secondary changes from chronic atrophic gastritis. Patients may develop peptic ulcer disease, abdominal pain, diarrhea, or impaired digestion; management targets the underlying cause and control of acid-related complications.

head: Azatadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Azatadine is a first-generation tricyclic antihistamine of the benzo[c]cycloheptathiophene family that acts primarily as a histamine H1-receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis and urticaria. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation due to central H1 blockade, and it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Nasopharyngitis is an acute upper respiratory tract inflammation of the nasal passages and pharynx, usually caused by viral infection. It commonly presents with rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, sore throat, sneezing, and sometimes cough or low-grade fever, especially in children and adults with common colds. Management is typically supportive, including hydration, rest, and symptom relief; antibiotics are generally not indicated unless bacterial infection is suspected.

head: Adalimumab (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Adalimumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody that targets tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α), neutralizing this proinflammatory cytokine and reducing TNF-mediated inflammation. It is approved for several immune-mediated disorders, including rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, plaque psoriasis, Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, and hidradenitis suppurativa. A notable risk is serious infection, including reactivation of latent tuberculosis.
tail_desc: Spondyloarthropathy refers to a group of chronic inflammatory rheumatic diseases primarily affecting the axial skeleton, sacroiliac joints, and entheses. It is associated with immune-mediated inflammation, often linked to HLA-B27, and includes ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic, reactive, and enteropathic arthritis. Typical features are inflammatory back pain, stiffness, peripheral arthritis, and enthesitis, often in younger adults; management centers on exercise, NSAIDs, and biologic therapy when needed.

head: Triprolidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Triprolidine is a first-generation alkylamine antihistamine that acts as an inverse agonist/antagonist at histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated symptoms. It is used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and common-cold symptoms, often in combination products. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention due to anticholinergic effects.
tail_desc: Atopic conjunctivitis is a chronic allergic inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, especially the conjunctiva, usually associated with atopic dermatitis and other atopic disease. It is driven by IgE-mediated and T-cell–mediated hypersensitivity. Typical features include intense itching, redness, tearing, mucous discharge, photophobia, and eyelid eczema, often in adolescents or adults with severe atopy. Management centers on allergen avoidance, lubricants, topical antihistamines or mast-cell stabilizers, and specialist-directed anti-inflammatory therapy.

head: Procaine benzylpenicillin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Procaine benzylpenicillin is a depot intramuscular formulation of benzylpenicillin (penicillin G), a beta-lactam penicillin antibiotic combined with procaine. It inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including syphilis in some settings. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins, with risk of anaphylaxis.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Acetohexamide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acetohexamide is a first-generation sulfonylurea oral antidiabetic agent that lowers blood glucose by binding the sulfonylurea receptor 1 (SUR1) on pancreatic beta-cell ATP-sensitive potassium channels, promoting insulin release. It has been used for type 2 diabetes mellitus when endogenous insulin production is present. A notable adverse effect is hypoglycemia, and it is not appropriate for type 1 diabetes or diabetic ketoacidosis.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Dihydrocodeine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dihydrocodeine is a semisynthetic opioid analgesic of the phenanthrene/codeine family that acts primarily as a mu-opioid receptor agonist in the central nervous system to reduce pain perception and suppress cough. It is used for moderate pain and, in some formulations, as an antitussive. A notable adverse effect is respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in significant respiratory compromise.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Pirlindole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pirlindole is a reversible monoamine oxidase inhibitor antidepressant of the pyrazinoindole class, acting primarily through selective inhibition of MAO-A to increase central monoamine levels. It has been used for depressive disorders. As with other MAO inhibitors, a notable risk is hypertensive reactions from interactions with tyramine-rich foods or sympathomimetic drugs.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Levomilnacipran (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Levomilnacipran is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant, the more active enantiomer of milnacipran, that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively greater norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. It is approved for major depressive disorder in adults. Notable adverse effects include increased heart rate and blood pressure, and it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Major depressive disorder is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by persistent depressed mood or loss of interest with cognitive, sleep, appetite, energy, and psychomotor changes causing functional impairment. Its pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving genetic vulnerability, psychosocial stress, and altered neurotransmitter and neuroendocrine regulation. It occurs across the lifespan and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, and safety monitoring.

head: Potassium bicarbonate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Potassium bicarbonate is an oral potassium salt and systemic alkalinizing agent. It dissociates to provide potassium and bicarbonate; bicarbonate buffers hydrogen ions and raises urinary and systemic pH, while potassium replenishes body potassium stores. It is used for prevention or treatment of hypokalemia and, in some formulations, urinary alkalinization. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related gastrointestinal disorder characterized by mucosal ulceration in the stomach or proximal duodenum, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It typically presents with epigastric pain, dyspepsia, nausea, or bleeding, especially in affected adults. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, and avoiding ulcerogenic medications.

head: Cefalotin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefalotin (cephalothin) is a first-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibiotic. It binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including skin and soft-tissue, respiratory, urinary tract, and bone or joint infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Carbidopa (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carbidopa is a peripheral aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (DOPA decarboxylase) inhibitor used with levodopa in Parkinson’s disease and parkinsonism to reduce peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine, thereby increasing central levodopa availability and lessening nausea and vomiting. It has no meaningful antiparkinsonian effect alone. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nonselective monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Hemiparkinsonism-hemiatrophy syndrome is a rare neurologic movement disorder characterized by unilateral body or limb hemiatrophy with ipsilateral hemiparkinsonism. Its cause is incompletely understood, often linked to early-life cerebral injury or developmental asymmetry affecting basal ganglia circuits. Symptoms usually begin in childhood or early adulthood with asymmetric rigidity, bradykinesia, dystonia, and postural abnormalities. Management is symptomatic, including dopaminergic therapy and supportive rehabilitation.

head: Perindopril (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Perindopril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug converted to the active metabolite perindoprilat. It lowers angiotensin II formation and aldosterone secretion by inhibiting ACE (kininase II), thereby reducing vasoconstriction and increasing bradykinin. It is used for hypertension, heart failure, and stable coronary artery disease; notable risks include dry cough and potentially life-threatening angioedema, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Lasmiditan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Lasmiditan is a selective serotonin 5-HT1F receptor agonist (a “ditan”) used for the acute treatment of migraine, with or without aura, in adults. It acts by activating 5-HT1F receptors involved in trigeminal pain pathways and is not indicated for migraine prevention. A notable adverse effect is significant dizziness and central nervous system depression, so patients are advised not to drive for at least 8 hours after dosing.
tail_desc: Migraine disorder is a common primary neurologic headache disorder involving the central nervous system, with pathophysiology linked to genetic susceptibility, trigeminovascular activation, and abnormal sensory processing. It causes recurrent attacks of moderate to severe, often unilateral throbbing headache with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia, sometimes aura, affecting women more often than men. Management includes trigger avoidance, acute abortive therapy, and preventive medications for frequent or disabling attacks.

head: Trichlormethiazide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Trichlormethiazide is a thiazide diuretic of the benzothiadiazine class that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It has been used for hypertension and edema associated with conditions such as heart failure or hepatic and renal disorders. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Econazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: dermatophytosis of groin and perianal area (a disease)
head_desc: Econazole is an imidazole antifungal of the azole class that inhibits fungal lanosterol 14α-demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and disrupting fungal cell membranes. It is used topically for superficial dermatophyte and Candida infections, including tinea and cutaneous candidiasis. A notable adverse effect is local skin irritation or burning at the application site.
tail_desc: Dermatophytosis of the groin and perianal area is a superficial fungal infection of keratinized skin, usually caused by dermatophytes such as Trichophyton species. It affects the inguinal folds and adjacent perianal region, producing pruritic, erythematous, scaly plaques with an active border, often in adults with sweating or occlusion. Management typically involves keeping the area dry and applying topical antifungals; extensive or refractory cases may need oral therapy.

head: Chlorambucil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chlorambucil is a nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the bifunctional alkylating antineoplastic class. It forms covalent DNA cross-links, primarily at the N7 position of guanine, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and triggering cell death. It is used for chronic lymphocytic leukemia and certain lymphomas. A notable adverse effect is dose-related myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Hodgkin's paragranuloma is an obsolete term for a lymphocyte-predominant form of Hodgkin lymphoma, a malignant lymphoid neoplasm affecting the lymphatic system. It is characterized pathologically by predominance of small lymphocytes with scattered neoplastic “popcorn” or Hodgkin/Reed-Sternberg–type cells. Patients often present with localized, painless peripheral lymphadenopathy, frequently in younger males; management follows modern Hodgkin lymphoma subtype-specific staging and treatment, commonly radiotherapy and/or chemotherapy.

head: Atenolol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Atenolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker, belonging to the beta-blocker class. It primarily antagonizes beta-1 adrenergic receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate, contractility, and blood pressure. It is used for hypertension, angina pectoris, and management after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in sinus bradycardia, heart block, and cardiogenic shock.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Testosterone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Testosterone is an endogenous androgenic-anabolic steroid hormone used as testosterone replacement therapy for male hypogonadism and delayed puberty, and in some masculinizing hormone regimens. It acts as an agonist at the androgen receptor, with some effects mediated after conversion to dihydrotestosterone or estradiol. Notable risks include erythrocytosis and it is contraindicated in men with prostate or breast cancer.
tail_desc: Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) is a chromosomal disorder affecting multiple organ systems, caused by an extra copy of chromosome 13, usually from meiotic nondisjunction. It presents in fetuses and newborns with severe developmental delay, growth restriction, craniofacial anomalies, holoprosencephaly, polydactyly, congenital heart defects, and other major malformations. Management is primarily supportive and multidisciplinary, with individualized decisions about surgical and palliative care.

head: Clindamycin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Clindamycin is a lincosamide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible anaerobic and gram-positive bacterial infections, including skin and soft-tissue, bone and joint, intra-abdominal, and some dental infections, and is also used topically for acne. A notable adverse effect is Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea/pseudomembranous colitis.
tail_desc: Staphylococcus aureus infection is a bacterial infection that can affect the skin and soft tissues, bloodstream, lungs, bones, joints, or heart valves. It results from invasion by S. aureus, including toxin production in some syndromes. Clinical features range from boils and abscesses to pneumonia, sepsis, and endocarditis, affecting both healthy people and hospitalized or immunocompromised patients; management relies on drainage when needed and targeted antibiotic therapy.

head: Temoporfin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Temoporfin is a chlorin-based photosensitizer used in photodynamic therapy. After activation by red light, it transfers energy to molecular oxygen to generate reactive oxygen species, causing localized cellular and vascular damage rather than binding a single specific molecular target. It is approved in some regions for palliative treatment of advanced head and neck squamous cell carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is prolonged photosensitivity requiring strict light avoidance.
tail_desc: Head and neck cancer is a group of malignancies arising in the upper aerodigestive tract, including the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures, most often squamous cell carcinomas. Major causes include tobacco and alcohol exposure and, for some oropharyngeal tumors, human papillomavirus infection. Typical features are a persistent neck mass, sore throat, dysphagia, hoarseness, or oral lesions, usually in adults; management commonly involves surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy, and multidisciplinary supportive care.

head: Cefapirin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefapirin is a first-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic. It acts by binding penicillin-binding proteins and inhibiting bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections and is also established intramammarily in veterinary medicine for bovine mastitis. Like other cephalosporins, it can cause hypersensitivity reactions and is contraindicated in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Streptococcal pneumonia is a bacterial lower respiratory tract infection of the lungs, most commonly caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, producing acute inflammation and alveolar consolidation. It typically presents with fever, cough, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and purulent sputum, especially in young children, older adults, and immunocompromised patients. Management centers on prompt appropriate antibiotic therapy and supportive respiratory care.

head: Ketoconazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ketoconazole is an imidazole antifungal that inhibits fungal lanosterol 14α-demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and disrupting cell membrane formation. It is used topically or as shampoo for seborrheic dermatitis, dandruff, and cutaneous fungal infections, while systemic use is now limited. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, making oral ketoconazole contraindicated in acute or chronic liver disease.
tail_desc: Tinea corporis is a superficial dermatophyte infection of the keratinized skin of the body, excluding scalp, groin, hands, and feet. It is caused by fungi such as Trichophyton, Microsporum, or Epidermophyton species and typically presents in children or adults as pruritic, annular, scaly plaques with central clearing. Management usually involves topical antifungal therapy, with oral agents for extensive, refractory, or immunocompromised cases.

head: Phenindamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Phenindamine is a first-generation antihistamine of the alkylamine class that acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist. It has been used to relieve allergic symptoms such as allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and pruritus. Like other sedating antihistamines, it can cause drowsiness and anticholinergic effects, and caution is warranted in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Vasomotor rhinitis is a form of nonallergic rhinitis affecting the nasal mucosa, thought to result from dysregulated autonomic or neurovascular control rather than infection or IgE-mediated allergy. It commonly causes chronic nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, postnasal drip, and sneezing, often triggered by irritants, odors, temperature changes, or alcohol, especially in adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and symptom control, often with intranasal therapies.

head: Etoposide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Etoposide is a semisynthetic epipodophyllotoxin antineoplastic agent that inhibits topoisomerase II, causing DNA strand breaks and cell-cycle arrest. It is used in established regimens for small cell lung cancer, testicular cancer, and several hematologic or solid malignancies. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially neutropenia; it is generally avoided in severe bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Seminoma is a malignant germ cell tumor of the testis, arising from primordial germ cells in the male reproductive system. It typically affects young to middle-aged men and presents as a painless testicular mass or enlargement. It is usually radiosensitive and highly curable; management generally involves orchiectomy, with stage-dependent surveillance, radiotherapy, or platinum-based chemotherapy.

head: Clidinium (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Clidinium is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent that competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, reducing gastrointestinal smooth-muscle spasm and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer disease and for irritable bowel syndrome or other functional gastrointestinal disorders. Notable adverse effects include dry mouth, blurred vision, and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Neurocirculatory asthenia is a functional autonomic disorder affecting the cardiovascular system, historically overlapping with anxiety-related syndromes and “Da Costa syndrome.” It lacks a defined structural cardiac lesion and is thought to reflect dysregulation of autonomic and psychophysiologic responses. Typical features include palpitations, chest discomfort, fatigue, dyspnea, dizziness, and exercise intolerance, often in younger adults; management is mainly reassurance, conditioning, and treatment of associated anxiety or stress.

head: Selegiline (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Selegiline is a selective, irreversible monoamine oxidase type B (MAO-B) inhibitor of the propargylamine class. By inhibiting MAO-B, it reduces dopamine breakdown in the central nervous system. It is established for Parkinson disease and, in transdermal form, major depressive disorder. Notable risks include insomnia and potentially serious hypertensive or serotonergic interactions with contraindicated sympathomimetic or serotonergic drugs.
tail_desc: Parkinsonian-pyramidal syndrome is a neurologic movement disorder characterized by combined parkinsonism and pyramidal tract dysfunction, affecting the central nervous system. It is classically linked to neurodegenerative disorders, including some hereditary forms such as ATP13A2-related Kufor-Rakeb syndrome. Typical features include bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability, spasticity, hyperreflexia, and gait impairment, often with juvenile or young-adult onset; management is supportive and symptomatic, including dopaminergic therapy, rehabilitation, and genetic evaluation when indicated.

head: Chlorthalidone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chlorthalidone is a thiazide-like sulfonamide diuretic that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter (NCC, SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing sodium and water excretion. It is established for treatment of hypertension and edema, including edema associated with heart failure. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Tazobactam (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tazobactam is a penicillanic acid sulfone beta-lactamase inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits many class A beta-lactamases, protecting susceptible beta-lactam antibiotics such as piperacillin from enzymatic degradation. It is used in fixed combinations, notably piperacillin/tazobactam, for treatment of susceptible bacterial infections including intra-abdominal, skin and soft-tissue, and lower respiratory tract infections. Hypersensitivity reactions can occur, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Staphylococcus aureus infection is a bacterial infection that can affect the skin and soft tissues, bloodstream, lungs, bones, joints, or heart valves. It results from invasion by S. aureus, including toxin production in some syndromes. Clinical features range from boils and abscesses to pneumonia, sepsis, and endocarditis, affecting both healthy people and hospitalized or immunocompromised patients; management relies on drainage when needed and targeted antibiotic therapy.

head: Levobunolol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Levobunolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class used primarily as an ophthalmic agent. By blocking beta-1 and beta-2 receptors in the ciliary body, it reduces aqueous humor production and lowers intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. Notable adverse effects include bronchospasm, so it is contraindicated in patients with asthma or severe COPD.
tail_desc: Ocular hypertension is an ophthalmic condition characterized by persistently elevated intraocular pressure without detectable glaucomatous optic nerve damage or visual field loss. It results from impaired aqueous humor outflow, increasing risk of primary open-angle glaucoma. Patients are often asymptomatic and identified on routine eye examination, especially adults with risk factors. Management centers on pressure-lowering eye drops and regular monitoring of optic nerves, visual fields, and intraocular pressure.

head: Bromodiphenhydramine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bromodiphenhydramine is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Iproniazid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Iproniazid is a hydrazine monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI), originally developed as an isonicotinic acid hydrazide derivative. It irreversibly inhibits monoamine oxidase, increasing synaptic norepinephrine, serotonin, and dopamine. It was historically used as an antidepressant and also in tuberculosis treatment development, but was withdrawn because of severe hepatotoxicity, a notable adverse effect and contraindication to use.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Aluminum hydroxide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Aluminum hydroxide is an inorganic aluminum salt used as an antacid and phosphate-binding agent. It neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach and binds dietary phosphate in the gastrointestinal tract, reducing phosphate absorption, without a specific receptor target. Established uses include symptomatic relief of acid indigestion and hyperphosphatemia in chronic kidney disease. A notable adverse effect is constipation; prolonged use may cause hypophosphatemia.
tail_desc: Peptic ulcer disease is an acid-related gastrointestinal disorder characterized by mucosal ulceration in the stomach or proximal duodenum, most commonly caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It typically presents with epigastric pain, dyspepsia, nausea, or bleeding, especially in affected adults. Management focuses on acid suppression, eradication of H. pylori when present, and avoiding ulcerogenic medications.

head: Cefalotin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefalotin (cephalothin) is a first-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibiotic. It binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including skin and soft-tissue, respiratory, urinary tract, and bone or joint infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Bacterial arthritis, or septic arthritis, is an acute infectious arthritis of the musculoskeletal system caused by bacterial invasion of a joint, leading to purulent synovial inflammation and rapid cartilage damage. It typically presents with sudden monoarticular pain, swelling, warmth, restricted movement, and fever, especially in children, older adults, or immunocompromised patients. Management generally requires urgent joint drainage and targeted intravenous antibiotics.

head: Dirithromycin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dirithromycin is a semisynthetic macrolide antibiotic and an ester prodrug of erythromycylamine. After conversion to the active compound, it binds the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It has been used for susceptible mild-to-moderate respiratory tract infections and uncomplicated skin and soft-tissue infections. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, and it is contraindicated in patients with macrolide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Streptococcal infection is a bacterial infectious disease caused by Streptococcus species, commonly affecting the throat, skin, soft tissues, or invasive systemic sites. Presentations vary from pharyngitis and impetigo to cellulitis, scarlet fever, or sepsis, occurring in both children and adults. Management depends on site and severity, typically involving appropriate antibiotics, supportive care, and drainage or urgent treatment for invasive disease.

head: Zinc oxide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Zinc oxide is an inorganic zinc compound used topically as a skin protectant and mild astringent. Its effects are primarily physical rather than receptor-mediated, forming a protective barrier on skin and reflecting or scattering ultraviolet radiation in sunscreen formulations, without a defined primary molecular target. Established uses include diaper dermatitis, minor skin irritation, and sun protection; adverse effects are usually limited to local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Dermatitis is a common inflammatory disorder of the skin, often caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, irritant or allergic exposures, or immune dysregulation. It typically presents with pruritus, erythema, scaling, and sometimes vesicles or lichenification, affecting both children and adults depending on subtype. Management generally focuses on trigger avoidance, skin hydration with emollients, and anti-inflammatory topical therapies such as corticosteroids.

head: Alprazolam (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Alprazolam is a short-acting triazolobenzodiazepine anxiolytic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is approved for the treatment of anxiety disorders and panic disorder. A notable adverse effect is sedation with psychomotor impairment; concomitant use with opioids can cause profound respiratory depression.
tail_desc: Anxiety is a mental health condition involving excessive fear, worry, or apprehension, primarily affecting the central nervous system and stress-response pathways. It is multifactorial, with contributions from genetic vulnerability, neurobiological dysregulation, and psychosocial stressors. Common features include persistent nervousness, restlessness, impaired concentration, sleep disturbance, and autonomic symptoms; it can affect people of any age. Management typically includes psychotherapy, stress reduction, and when indicated, pharmacotherapy such as antidepressants.

head: Captopril (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Captopril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a sulfhydryl-containing antihypertensive agent. It inhibits ACE, reducing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II and decreasing aldosterone secretion. Established indications include hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and it is also used after myocardial infarction in selected patients. A notable adverse effect is cough; it is contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Ceftriaxone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections including pneumonia, meningitis, gonorrhea, sepsis, urinary tract and intra-abdominal infections. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions in beta-lactam–allergic patients and biliary sludging/pseudolithiasis.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Clobetasol propionate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Clobetasol propionate is a super-high-potency topical corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It binds the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune mediators and reduce vasodilation and pruritus. It is used short term for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses such as plaque psoriasis and eczema. A notable adverse effect is skin atrophy, and prolonged use may cause systemic hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis suppression.
tail_desc: Contact dermatitis is an inflammatory skin disorder caused by direct exposure to irritants or allergens, producing epidermal barrier disruption and, in allergic cases, a delayed hypersensitivity reaction. It presents with pruritic, erythematous, sometimes vesicular or scaling lesions confined to contact sites, affecting all ages. Management centers on identifying and avoiding triggers, skin protection, emollients, and topical corticosteroids for flares.

head: Dihydroergotamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dihydroergotamine is a semisynthetic ergot alkaloid antimigraine agent that acts mainly as an agonist at serotonin 5-HT1B and 5-HT1D receptors, producing cranial vasoconstriction and inhibiting trigeminal neuropeptide release. It is used for acute treatment of migraine attacks and cluster headache. A notable risk is peripheral or coronary vasospasm, so it is contraindicated in ischemic heart disease.
tail_desc: Hemicrania continua is a primary headache disorder of the nervous system characterized by a continuous strictly unilateral headache, often with superimposed exacerbations and ipsilateral autonomic features such as tearing or nasal congestion. Its pathophysiology is not fully defined. It usually affects adults, more often women, and is distinguished by a prompt, complete response to indomethacin, which guides diagnosis and treatment.

head: Imiquimod (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Imiquimod is a topical imidazoquinoline immune response modifier that acts as an agonist of toll-like receptor 7, inducing local production of interferon-α and other proinflammatory cytokines. It is approved for external genital and perianal warts, actinic keratosis, and superficial basal cell carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is marked local skin inflammation, including erythema, erosion, and burning.
tail_desc: Common wart (verruca vulgaris) is a benign viral infection of the skin caused by human papillomavirus, usually affecting the epidermis of the hands, fingers, knees, or periungual areas. It presents as firm, rough, hyperkeratotic papules with a verrucous surface, especially in children and young adults. Many resolve spontaneously; management includes topical salicylic acid, cryotherapy, and other destructive or immunomodulatory treatments when bothersome or persistent.

head: Dapoxetine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dapoxetine is a short-acting selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) of the phenoxypropylamine class that inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved in several countries for the on-demand treatment of premature ejaculation in adult men. Common adverse effects include nausea and dizziness, and it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Psychologic dyspareunia is painful sexual intercourse primarily attributed to psychological factors rather than identifiable genital pathology, affecting the female sexual and genitourinary/pelvic pain domain. It commonly involves anxiety, fear, past trauma, relationship distress, or conditioned pelvic floor tension, presenting as recurrent intercourse-related pain and avoidance in sexually active women. Management typically emphasizes psychosexual counseling, treatment of comorbid mood symptoms, pelvic floor therapy, and exclusion of organic causes.

head: Cefadroxil (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefadroxil is a first-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibiotic, that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing cell lysis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections such as pharyngitis/tonsillitis, skin and soft-tissue infections, and uncomplicated urinary tract infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Streptococcal infection is a bacterial infectious disease caused by Streptococcus species, commonly affecting the throat, skin, soft tissues, or invasive systemic sites. Presentations vary from pharyngitis and impetigo to cellulitis, scarlet fever, or sepsis, occurring in both children and adults. Management depends on site and severity, typically involving appropriate antibiotics, supportive care, and drainage or urgent treatment for invasive disease.

head: Procyclidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Procyclidine is a synthetic antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent of the piperidine class that competitively blocks central and peripheral muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, helping restore dopaminergic–cholinergic balance in the basal ganglia. It is used for Parkinsonism and extrapyramidal symptoms, including drug-induced dystonia and parkinsonian reactions. A notable adverse effect is confusion, particularly in older adults; it is contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Hemiparkinsonism-hemiatrophy syndrome is a rare neurologic movement disorder characterized by unilateral body or limb hemiatrophy with ipsilateral hemiparkinsonism. Its cause is incompletely understood, often linked to early-life cerebral injury or developmental asymmetry affecting basal ganglia circuits. Symptoms usually begin in childhood or early adulthood with asymmetric rigidity, bradykinesia, dystonia, and postural abnormalities. Management is symptomatic, including dopaminergic therapy and supportive rehabilitation.

head: Crizotinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Crizotinib is an oral small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor of ALK, ROS1, and MET. It blocks signaling from these oncogenic kinases, suppressing tumor cell proliferation and survival. It is approved for ALK-positive or ROS1-positive non-small cell lung cancer. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and it can also cause QT-interval prolongation or interstitial lung disease.
tail_desc: Non-small cell lung carcinoma is a malignant epithelial cancer of the lung and respiratory system, comprising mainly adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. It arises through accumulation of genetic alterations, strongly linked to tobacco exposure, causing cough, dyspnea, chest pain, weight loss, and metastases, typically in older adults. Management depends on stage and biomarkers, using surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy.

head: Gemcitabine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Gemcitabine is a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog antimetabolite chemotherapy drug. Intracellular phosphorylation generates active metabolites that inhibit ribonucleotide reductase and are incorporated into DNA, blocking DNA synthesis and inducing apoptosis. It is used for pancreatic cancer and also non-small cell lung, bladder, breast, and ovarian cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Ovarian clear cell adenocarcinoma is a malignant epithelial ovarian cancer, often associated with endometriosis and characterized histologically by clear glycogen-rich cells and tubulocystic, papillary, or solid growth patterns. It typically affects adult women, often presenting with a pelvic mass, abdominal pain, or bloating. Management usually involves surgical staging and cytoreductive resection, with platinum-based chemotherapy commonly used, though chemoresistance can occur.

head: Testosterone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: pituitary deficiency due to Rathke's pouch cysts (a disease)
head_desc: Testosterone is an endogenous androgenic-anabolic steroid hormone used as testosterone replacement therapy for male hypogonadism and delayed puberty, and in some masculinizing hormone regimens. It acts as an agonist at the androgen receptor, with some effects mediated after conversion to dihydrotestosterone or estradiol. Notable risks include erythrocytosis and it is contraindicated in men with prostate or breast cancer.
tail_desc: Pituitary deficiency due to Rathke’s pouch cysts is an endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary system caused by a benign cystic remnant of Rathke’s pouch that compresses normal pituitary tissue or the stalk. It may occur in children or adults and presents with partial or multiple pituitary hormone deficiencies, sometimes with headache or visual symptoms. Management typically involves hormone replacement and, when symptomatic or compressive, neurosurgical drainage or resection.

head: Sulfisoxazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sulfisoxazole is a short-acting sulfonamide antibacterial. It inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby suppressing growth of susceptible organisms. It has been used for treatment of susceptible urinary tract infections, otitis media, and some other bacterial infections. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe sulfonamide reactions such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
tail_desc: Ornithosis, or psittacosis, is a zoonotic bacterial infection of the respiratory system caused by Chlamydia psittaci, usually acquired by inhaling aerosolized secretions or droppings from infected birds. It typically presents with fever, headache, myalgia, dry cough, and atypical pneumonia, especially in bird owners or poultry workers. Management is primarily antibiotic therapy, classically with tetracyclines, plus supportive care and exposure control.

head: Calcitriol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcitriol is the active form of vitamin D (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3), a vitamin D analog that binds the nuclear vitamin D receptor to regulate calcium and phosphate homeostasis and suppress parathyroid hormone. It is used for hypocalcemia and secondary hyperparathyroidism in chronic kidney disease and for hypoparathyroidism. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in hypercalcemia or vitamin D toxicity.
tail_desc: Hypophosphatemia is an electrolyte/metabolic disorder of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, muscle, and neuromuscular function, caused by inadequate intestinal absorption, intracellular phosphate shifts, or excessive renal losses. Manifestations range from weakness, bone pain, and osteomalacia or rickets to severe respiratory, neurologic, or cardiac dysfunction. Management targets the cause and typically includes oral or intravenous phosphate replacement and correction of associated vitamin D deficiency.

head: Sulfamethizole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sulfamethizole is a short-acting sulfonamide antibacterial. Like other sulfonamides, it inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby bacterial growth. It has been used mainly for susceptible urinary tract infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, including severe sulfonamide skin reactions such as Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy.
tail_desc: Shigellosis is an acute bacterial enteric infection of the gastrointestinal tract caused by Shigella species, producing inflammatory colitis through invasion of the colonic mucosa and, in some strains, Shiga toxin. It typically presents with fever, abdominal cramps, and diarrhea that may become bloody, especially in young children and outbreak settings. Management is mainly rehydration, with antibiotics used selectively based on severity and resistance patterns.

head: Megestrol acetate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Megestrol acetate is a synthetic progestin derivative of progesterone that acts primarily as an agonist of the progesterone receptor, with additional glucocorticoid activity. It is used as an appetite stimulant for anorexia, cachexia, or significant weight loss, including in AIDS, and as hormonal therapy for advanced endometrial carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of thromboembolic events.
tail_desc: Uterine corpus cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the uterine body, usually endometrial carcinoma arising from the endometrial lining of the female reproductive system. It most often affects postmenopausal women and commonly presents with abnormal uterine bleeding. Major risk factors include prolonged estrogen exposure and obesity. Management is typically centered on surgery, with radiation, chemotherapy, or hormone therapy used according to stage and histology.

head: Cefazolin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefazolin is a first-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It is widely used for susceptible gram-positive and some gram-negative infections and for perioperative surgical prophylaxis. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, including anaphylaxis, particularly in patients with cephalosporin or serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Streptococcal pneumonia is a bacterial lower respiratory tract infection of the lungs, most commonly caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, producing acute inflammation and alveolar consolidation. It typically presents with fever, cough, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and purulent sputum, especially in young children, older adults, and immunocompromised patients. Management centers on prompt appropriate antibiotic therapy and supportive respiratory care.

head: Paroxetine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Paroxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant of the phenylpiperidine class that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder and several anxiety-related disorders, including panic disorder, social anxiety disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, OCD, and PTSD. A notable adverse effect is sexual dysfunction; use is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Dicoumarol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dicoumarol is a 4-hydroxycoumarin oral anticoagulant of the vitamin K antagonist class. It inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1), reducing activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. Historically it was used for prevention and treatment of thromboembolic disorders, but has largely been replaced by warfarin. A major adverse effect is serious bleeding; it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Probenecid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Probenecid is a uricosuric benzoic acid derivative that lowers serum urate by inhibiting renal tubular reabsorption of uric acid, chiefly via URAT1 (SLC22A12) and related organic anion transporters. It is used for chronic gout/hyperuricemia and to prolong beta-lactam antibiotic levels by reducing renal secretion. Notable risks include uric acid nephrolithiasis; it is contraindicated in patients with kidney stones or acute gout attacks.
tail_desc: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy of the musculoskeletal system caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals due to hyperuricemia. It typically presents with recurrent acute attacks of severe, red, swollen joint pain—classically the first metatarsophalangeal joint—and may progress to tophi and chronic joint damage, often in adult men and people with renal disease or diuretic use. Management includes NSAIDs, colchicine or corticosteroids for flares, and urate-lowering therapy plus lifestyle measures for prevention.

head: Calcium chloride (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcium chloride is an inorganic calcium salt and electrolyte replacement that dissociates to provide ionized calcium, directly increasing extracellular calcium to support myocardial contractility, neuromuscular transmission, and stabilization of the cardiac membrane. It is used to treat acute hypocalcemia, hyperkalemia-associated cardiotoxicity, hypermagnesemia, and calcium channel blocker overdose. A notable adverse effect is severe tissue necrosis with extravasation.
tail_desc: Cardiac arrest is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency in which the heart abruptly stops pumping effectively, causing sudden loss of circulation. It usually results from ventricular fibrillation, pulseless ventricular tachycardia, asystole, or pulseless electrical activity, often in adults with underlying heart disease. Patients collapse, are unresponsive, pulseless, and apneic or gasping; management requires immediate cardiopulmonary resuscitation, defibrillation when indicated, and treatment of reversible causes.

head: Linezolid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 23S rRNA of the 50S ribosomal subunit, blocking formation of the initiation complex. It is used for susceptible gram-positive infections, including pneumonia, skin and soft-tissue infections, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus infections. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Staphylococcus aureus infection is a bacterial infection that can affect the skin and soft tissues, bloodstream, lungs, bones, joints, or heart valves. It results from invasion by S. aureus, including toxin production in some syndromes. Clinical features range from boils and abscesses to pneumonia, sepsis, and endocarditis, affecting both healthy people and hospitalized or immunocompromised patients; management relies on drainage when needed and targeted antibiotic therapy.

head: Chlorothiazide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chlorothiazide is a thiazide diuretic (benzothiadiazine sulfonamide) that inhibits the Na+/Cl− cotransporter NCC (SLC12A3) in the distal convoluted tubule, increasing sodium and water excretion. It is used for hypertension and edema, including edema due to heart failure, hepatic cirrhosis, or corticosteroid/estrogen therapy. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Congestive heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment reduces the heart’s ability to pump or fill effectively, causing pulmonary and/or systemic fluid congestion. It commonly affects older adults and patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy, producing dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance. Management focuses on treating the cause, diuresis, guideline-directed heart-failure medications, sodium/fluid control, and selected device or advanced therapies.

head: Dexchlorpheniramine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dexchlorpheniramine is a first-generation alkylamine antihistamine, the dextrorotatory isomer of chlorpheniramine. It acts primarily as an antagonist or inverse agonist at histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is used for allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and other allergy-related conditions. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it should be used cautiously in patients with angle-closure glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Familial dermatographia is a likely inherited form of dermatographic urticaria, a physical inducible urticaria affecting the skin. It is thought to reflect exaggerated mast-cell degranulation with histamine release after minor mechanical stroking or pressure. Patients, often with symptoms beginning in childhood or adolescence and a family history, develop transient linear wheals, erythema, and pruritus. Management is mainly trigger avoidance and non-sedating H1 antihistamines.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: classic Hodgkin lymphoma, lymphocyte-rich type (a disease)
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Classic Hodgkin lymphoma, lymphocyte-rich type is a rare subtype of classic Hodgkin lymphoma, a B-cell lymphoid malignancy of the lymphatic system characterized by scattered Hodgkin/Reed-Sternberg cells in a background rich in small lymphocytes. It often presents in older men with localized peripheral lymphadenopathy and fewer B symptoms, and is generally managed with stage-adapted combination chemotherapy with or without radiotherapy.

head: Gramicidin D (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Gramicidin D is a mixture of linear peptide antibiotics (gramicidins A, B, and C) from Bacillus species, used topically as an antibacterial agent. It inserts into bacterial cytoplasmic membranes and forms monovalent cation channels, disrupting membrane permeability and causing cell death, mainly against Gram-positive bacteria. Established use is limited to topical treatment or prevention of superficial skin and ocular infections; systemic use is avoided because of marked hemolytic toxicity.
tail_desc: Conjunctivitis is an inflammation of the conjunctiva, the ocular surface membrane of the eye, most commonly caused by viral or bacterial infection, allergy, or irritant exposure. It typically presents with eye redness, tearing or discharge, irritation, and sometimes itching, in both children and adults. Management depends on cause and generally includes hygiene, symptomatic relief, avoidance of triggers, and targeted antimicrobial therapy when bacterial infection is suspected.

head: Cytarabine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloblastic leukemia with maturation (a disease)
head_desc: Cytarabine is a pyrimidine nucleoside antimetabolite, an arabinose-containing cytidine analog used mainly in acute myeloid leukemia and also acute lymphoblastic leukemia and lymphomatous meningitis. Intracellularly converted to ara-CTP, it inhibits DNA polymerase and is incorporated into DNA, blocking DNA synthesis in S phase. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; high-dose therapy can also cause cerebellar toxicity.
tail_desc: Acute myeloblastic leukemia with maturation is a subtype of acute myeloid leukemia affecting the bone marrow and blood, characterized by clonal proliferation of myeloblasts with some maturation beyond the blast stage. It typically presents in adults with anemia, infections, bleeding, and marrow failure. Management generally involves AML-directed induction chemotherapy, risk stratification, and consideration of stem cell transplantation in eligible patients.

head: Aztreonam (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Aztreonam is a monobactam beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding protein 3 in susceptible Gram-negative bacteria, inhibiting cell wall synthesis and causing bacterial lysis. It is used for infections due to aerobic Gram-negative organisms, including urinary tract, lower respiratory, intra-abdominal, gynecologic, skin, and septicemic infections. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with known aztreonam allergy.
tail_desc: Endometritis is an inflammatory, usually infectious disorder of the endometrial lining of the uterus in the female reproductive system, commonly arising postpartum, after miscarriage, or following gynecologic procedures from ascending polymicrobial infection. Typical features include fever, lower abdominal or uterine tenderness, abnormal vaginal bleeding or discharge, and pelvic pain. Management generally involves prompt broad-spectrum antibiotics and treatment of the underlying source.

head: Metixene (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Metixene is an antimuscarinic antiparkinsonian agent of the piperidine class. It acts primarily as a centrally active muscarinic acetylcholine receptor antagonist, helping restore the dopamine–acetylcholine balance in the basal ganglia. It has been used for symptomatic treatment of Parkinsonism, including drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms. Notable adverse effects are typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth, blurred vision, and urinary retention; it is contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Parkinson disease is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system, primarily affecting the basal ganglia. It is characterized by degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and accumulation of alpha-synuclein–containing Lewy bodies. Typical features, usually beginning in older adults, include bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and postural instability; management focuses on symptomatic dopaminergic therapy, rehabilitation, and selected device-aided or surgical treatments.

head: Torasemide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Torasemide (torsemide) is a pyridine-sulfonylurea loop diuretic that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter NKCC2 (SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, increasing renal sodium, chloride, and water excretion. It is used for edema associated with heart failure, renal disease, or hepatic cirrhosis, and for hypertension. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in anuria.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Roxatidine acetate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Roxatidine acetate is an H2-receptor antagonist (histamine H2 blocker), a prodrug converted to roxatidine that selectively inhibits gastric parietal cell H2 receptors and reduces basal and stimulated gastric acid secretion. It has been used for gastric and duodenal ulcer disease and reflux esophagitis. Notable adverse effects include constipation, diarrhea, and occasional liver function abnormalities.
tail_desc: Peptic esophagitis is an inflammatory disorder of the esophagus, usually caused by reflux of acidic gastric contents in gastroesophageal reflux disease. It commonly presents in adults with heartburn, retrosternal pain, acid regurgitation, and sometimes dysphagia or odynophagia. Management focuses on acid suppression, especially proton pump inhibitors, along with lifestyle measures and treatment of the underlying reflux.

head: Mechlorethamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mechlorethamine is a nitrogen mustard alkylating antineoplastic agent that forms highly reactive intermediates, causing DNA cross-linking and inhibition of DNA replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used in regimens for Hodgkin lymphoma and as topical therapy for mycosis fungoides (cutaneous T-cell lymphoma). A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is also a potent vesicant if extravasated.
tail_desc: Polycythemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by increased red blood cell mass, causing elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit and increased blood viscosity. It may be primary, as in polycythemia vera from clonal myeloproliferation, or secondary to excess erythropoietin from hypoxia or tumors. Patients, often adults, may have headache, dizziness, pruritus, thrombosis, or ruddy complexion; management targets the cause and reduces thrombotic risk, often with phlebotomy.

head: Fluconazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluconazole is a triazole antifungal that inhibits fungal lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and impairing cell membrane formation. It is used to treat and prevent susceptible Candida infections, including oropharyngeal, esophageal, and vulvovaginal candidiasis, as well as cryptococcal meningitis. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, and it can prolong the QT interval.
tail_desc: Oral candidiasis is a fungal infection of the oral mucosa, usually caused by overgrowth of Candida albicans when local or systemic immunity is impaired. It commonly presents with removable white plaques, erythema, soreness, or angular cheilitis, especially in infants, denture wearers, and immunocompromised patients. Management typically involves correcting predisposing factors and using topical or, if needed, systemic antifungal therapy.

head: Fluvastatin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluvastatin is a synthetic statin (HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor) that competitively inhibits hepatic 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used to treat hypercholesterolemia and mixed dyslipidemia and to lower cardiovascular risk. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; it is contraindicated in active liver disease and pregnancy.
tail_desc: Arteriosclerosis disorder is a vascular disease of the circulatory system characterized by thickening, hardening, and loss of elasticity of arterial walls, often related to aging, hypertension, diabetes, or lipid deposition in specific subtypes. It may be asymptomatic or cause ischemic complications such as angina, stroke, or peripheral vascular disease, especially in older adults. Management targets risk-factor control and treatment of cardiovascular complications.

head: Simvastatin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Simvastatin is a lipid-lowering statin, a semisynthetic derivative of a fermentation product, that acts as a prodrug inhibiting hepatic HMG-CoA reductase (3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase), reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors. It is used for hypercholesterolemia and cardiovascular risk reduction. A notable adverse effect is myopathy, including rare rhabdomyolysis; active liver disease is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Hyperlipoproteinemia is a metabolic disorder of lipid transport affecting the cardiovascular system, characterized by abnormally elevated plasma lipoproteins from inherited defects or secondary causes such as diabetes, hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, or diet. It may be asymptomatic or present with xanthomas, lipemia retinalis, pancreatitis, and accelerated atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Management targets the cause and lowers lipid levels through diet, lifestyle change, and lipid-lowering medications.

head: Loteprednol etabonate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
