head_desc: Loteprednol etabonate is a topical ophthalmic corticosteroid, a chloromethyl ester glucocorticoid, that binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to suppress phospholipase A2–dependent inflammatory mediator production and cytokine expression. It is used for steroid-responsive ocular inflammation, including postoperative inflammation and pain and allergic conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, so caution is warranted in patients with glaucoma.
tail_desc: Idiopathic uveitis is intraocular inflammation of the uveal tract with no identifiable infectious, systemic, or other specific cause after appropriate evaluation. It may involve the anterior, intermediate, posterior, or pan-uveal eye and can affect both children and adults, causing eye pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision, and floaters. Management typically centers on ophthalmic assessment, exclusion of underlying etiologies, and anti-inflammatory therapy, often corticosteroids with steroid-sparing immunomodulation in recurrent or severe disease.

head: Cytarabine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: precursor T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (a disease)
head_desc: Cytarabine is a pyrimidine nucleoside antimetabolite, an arabinose-containing cytidine analog used mainly in acute myeloid leukemia and also acute lymphoblastic leukemia and lymphomatous meningitis. Intracellularly converted to ara-CTP, it inhibits DNA polymerase and is incorporated into DNA, blocking DNA synthesis in S phase. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; high-dose therapy can also cause cerebellar toxicity.
tail_desc: Precursor T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia is an aggressive hematologic malignancy of immature T-lymphoid precursors arising in the bone marrow and often involving blood and lymphoid tissues. It commonly affects children and adolescents and may present with marrow failure, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, or a mediastinal mass. Management typically uses intensive multiagent chemotherapy, central nervous system prophylaxis, and selected high-risk cases may undergo allogeneic stem cell transplantation.

head: Calcium carbonate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcium carbonate is an inorganic calcium salt used as a calcium supplement, phosphate binder, and antacid. It acts by neutralizing gastric hydrochloric acid and by binding dietary phosphate in the gastrointestinal tract; it also supplies elemental calcium. Established uses include dyspepsia/heartburn, prevention or treatment of hypocalcemia, and hyperphosphatemia in chronic kidney disease. Notable adverse effects include constipation and hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Cefotaxime (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefotaxime is a third-generation cephalosporin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing cell lysis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including pneumonia, urinary tract, skin/soft-tissue, intra-abdominal, gynecologic infections, septicemia, and meningitis. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, with contraindication in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Staphylococcal pneumonia is a bacterial pneumonia of the lower respiratory tract caused by Staphylococcus aureus, producing acute suppurative and often necrotizing lung infection. It commonly causes fever, cough, dyspnea, purulent sputum, and severe systemic illness, and is seen particularly after influenza, in hospitalized patients, and in infants or older adults. Management centers on prompt anti-staphylococcal antibiotics and supportive respiratory care.

head: Decitabine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Decitabine is a hypomethylating antineoplastic agent, a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog. After intracellular activation, it incorporates into DNA and inhibits DNA methyltransferases, causing DNA hypomethylation and altered gene expression. It is approved for myelodysplastic syndromes and used in acute myeloid leukemia. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression, including neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Myeloid leukemia is a hematologic malignancy of the bone marrow and blood arising from clonal proliferation of myeloid precursor cells, causing accumulation of immature or abnormal myeloid cells and suppression of normal hematopoiesis. It may present with anemia, infections, bleeding, fatigue, and splenomegaly, affects both children and adults depending on subtype, and is managed with subtype-directed chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and sometimes hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Cefapirin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefapirin is a first-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic. It acts by binding penicillin-binding proteins and inhibiting bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections and is also established intramammarily in veterinary medicine for bovine mastitis. Like other cephalosporins, it can cause hypersensitivity reactions and is contraindicated in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Pheniramine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pheniramine is a first-generation alkylamine antihistamine that acts primarily as an H1 receptor antagonist (inverse agonist), reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is used for allergic rhinitis, urticaria, pruritus, and related hypersensitivity conditions, including some cold preparations. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it should be used cautiously in angle-closure glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Epinephrine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Epinephrine (adrenaline) is an endogenous catecholamine and nonselective adrenergic agonist that activates alpha-1, alpha-2, beta-1, and beta-2 receptors, producing vasoconstriction, increased cardiac output, and bronchodilation. It is established for anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest, severe bronchospasm, and as a vasoconstrictor adjunct to local anesthetics. Notable adverse effects include tachyarrhythmias and marked hypertension.
tail_desc: Open-angle glaucoma is a chronic optic neuropathy of the eye, usually associated with impaired aqueous humor outflow through an anatomically open anterior chamber angle, often causing elevated intraocular pressure. It typically affects older adults and is characterized by progressive peripheral visual field loss and optic nerve cupping, often without early symptoms. Management focuses on lowering intraocular pressure with topical medications, laser trabeculoplasty, or surgery.

head: Amantadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Amantadine is an adamantane derivative antiviral and antiparkinsonian agent. It blocks the influenza A M2 proton channel and, in the CNS, increases dopaminergic signaling and has NMDA receptor antagonist activity. It is used for Parkinson disease and drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms; antiviral use is limited by resistance. Notable adverse effects include confusion, hallucinations, and livedo reticularis.
tail_desc: Postencephalitic Parkinson disease is a secondary parkinsonian syndrome of the central nervous system that develops after encephalitis, classically encephalitis lethargica. It is thought to reflect postinfectious injury to basal ganglia and related dopaminergic pathways. Patients, often years after the encephalitic illness, develop bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, postural instability, and neuropsychiatric or oculogyric features; management is symptomatic, using antiparkinsonian therapy and supportive rehabilitation.

head: Ceftibuten (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ceftibuten is an oral third-generation cephalosporin antibiotic (a beta-lactam) that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis. It is used for susceptible infections including otitis media, pharyngitis/tonsillitis, acute bacterial exacerbations of chronic bronchitis, and uncomplicated urinary tract infections. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea; it is contraindicated in patients with known serious hypersensitivity to ceftibuten or other cephalosporins.
tail_desc: Tonsillitis is an inflammatory disorder of the palatine tonsils in the upper respiratory tract, usually caused by viral infection and less often by bacteria such as group A Streptococcus. It commonly affects children and adolescents, causing sore throat, fever, painful swallowing, enlarged erythematous tonsils, and sometimes cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is mainly supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Cyanocobalamin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cyanocobalamin is a synthetic vitamin B12 (cobalamin) used as a hematinic replacement therapy. It is converted in vivo to active coenzyme forms of vitamin B12 that support methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, restoring DNA synthesis and normal hematopoiesis in deficiency. It is indicated for prevention and treatment of vitamin B12 deficiency, including pernicious anemia. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to cobalt or cobalamins.
tail_desc: Vitamin B12 deficiency is a nutritional or malabsorptive disorder affecting hematologic and neurologic systems, caused by inadequate cobalamin intake, impaired absorption (for example pernicious anemia), or gastrointestinal disease. It commonly causes megaloblastic anemia, glossitis, paresthesias, gait disturbance, and cognitive changes, especially in older adults, vegans, or patients with malabsorption. Management involves identifying the cause and replacing vitamin B12, orally or parenterally.

head: Nitrazepam (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Nitrazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine hypnotic. It acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors by binding the benzodiazepine site, enhancing GABA-mediated chloride channel opening and neuronal inhibition. It has been used for short-term treatment of severe insomnia and as an anticonvulsant in some seizure disorders. Notable adverse effects include next-day sedation; it is contraindicated in myasthenia gravis and severe respiratory insufficiency.
tail_desc: Early myoclonic encephalopathy is a severe neonatal epileptic encephalopathy of the central nervous system, usually linked to metabolic or genetic disorders causing diffuse cerebral dysfunction. It presents in newborns or very young infants with erratic fragmentary myoclonus, frequent seizures, profound encephalopathy, and a burst-suppression EEG pattern. Management is supportive and seizure-directed, while urgently evaluating and treating any identifiable underlying metabolic cause.

head: Salicylic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Salicylic acid is a keratolytic and topical salicylate, chemically a beta-hydroxy acid. It acts mainly by solubilizing intercellular cement in the stratum corneum, promoting desquamation; unlike acetylsalicylic acid, it is not used primarily for systemic COX inhibition. It is established for acne, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, warts, and calluses/corns. Notable risks include local irritation and salicylate toxicity with excessive topical use.
tail_desc: Tinea corporis is a superficial dermatophyte infection of the keratinized skin of the body, excluding scalp, groin, hands, and feet. It is caused by fungi such as Trichophyton, Microsporum, or Epidermophyton species and typically presents in children or adults as pruritic, annular, scaly plaques with central clearing. Management usually involves topical antifungal therapy, with oral agents for extensive, refractory, or immunocompromised cases.

head: Olopatadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Olopatadine is a second-generation antihistamine and antiallergic agent, chemically a dibenzoxepin derivative, that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors and also inhibits release of inflammatory mediators from mast cells. It is used mainly for allergic conjunctivitis and allergic rhinitis. A notable adverse effect is headache; hypersensitivity to olopatadine is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Venlafaxine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Venlafaxine is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant of the phenethylamine class that blocks the serotonin and norepinephrine transporters, increasing synaptic monoamine levels. It is approved for major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety disorder, and panic disorder. A notable adverse effect is dose-related hypertension, and it is contraindicated with concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Unipolar depression is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by one or more major depressive episodes without a history of mania or hypomania. Its pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving genetic vulnerability, stress-related neurobiological changes, and neurotransmitter dysregulation. Typical features include persistent low mood, anhedonia, sleep and appetite disturbance, fatigue, and impaired concentration; management commonly combines psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, and safety monitoring.

head: Vilanterol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Vilanterol is a long-acting inhaled β2-adrenergic receptor agonist (LABA) bronchodilator. By selectively stimulating β2 receptors in airway smooth muscle, it increases intracellular cAMP and promotes prolonged bronchodilation. It is approved in fixed-dose combinations for maintenance treatment of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and, with an inhaled corticosteroid, for asthma. Notable adverse effects include tachycardia and tremor; LABA monotherapy in asthma is contraindicated.
tail_desc: Severe early-onset COPD is a chronic obstructive airway disease of the respiratory system characterized by persistent airflow limitation from emphysema and/or chronic bronchitis, arising unusually early in adult life. It commonly reflects heavy smoking, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, or other susceptibility factors, and presents with progressive dyspnea, cough, wheeze, and frequent exacerbations. Management centers on smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccination, oxygen when indicated, and selected advanced interventions.

head: Phentolamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Phentolamine is a nonselective, competitive alpha-adrenergic antagonist of the imidazoline class that blocks alpha1 and alpha2 receptors, producing peripheral vasodilation. It is used to prevent or control catecholamine-induced hypertensive episodes in pheochromocytoma and for treatment of dermal necrosis or ischemia after norepinephrine extravasation. Notable adverse effects include orthostatic hypotension and reflex tachycardia.
tail_desc: Brain ischemia is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system caused by reduced or interrupted cerebral blood flow, leading to inadequate oxygen and glucose delivery and potentially irreversible neuronal injury. It commonly presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits, altered consciousness, or cognitive impairment, especially in older adults with vascular risk factors. Management focuses on urgent reperfusion when eligible, stabilization, and secondary prevention of further ischemic events.

head: Hydroxyurea (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: malignant dysgerminomatous germ cell tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Hydroxyurea is an antimetabolite hydroxylated urea derivative that inhibits ribonucleotide reductase, reducing deoxyribonucleotide synthesis and thereby suppressing DNA replication; it also increases fetal hemoglobin in sickle cell disease. It is used for myeloproliferative disorders and some leukemias, and to reduce painful crises in sickle cell disease. A notable adverse effect is dose-related myelosuppression, so it is contraindicated in severe bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Malignant dysgerminomatous germ cell tumor of the ovary is a malignant ovarian germ cell neoplasm, essentially the ovarian counterpart of seminoma, arising from primitive germ cells. It typically affects adolescents and young women and may present with abdominal pain, pelvic mass, or distension. Management is usually fertility-sparing surgical staging with unilateral oophorectomy when feasible, with platinum-based chemotherapy for advanced or recurrent disease.

head: Pseudoephedrine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pseudoephedrine is a sympathomimetic amine decongestant of the phenethylamine/amphetamine-related class that acts mainly as an indirect adrenergic agonist, promoting norepinephrine release and stimulating alpha-adrenergic receptors to cause vasoconstriction in nasal mucosa. It is used for temporary relief of nasal and sinus congestion from colds, allergies, or sinusitis. A notable adverse effect is hypertension, and it is contraindicated with MAO inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Fluphenazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Fluphenazine is a first-generation phenothiazine antipsychotic that primarily antagonizes dopamine D2 receptors in the central nervous system. It is used for the treatment of schizophrenia and other chronic psychotic disorders, including as a long-acting decanoate formulation. A notable adverse effect is extrapyramidal symptoms, including drug-induced parkinsonism and tardive dyskinesia.
tail_desc: Psychotic disorder is a psychiatric condition affecting brain function and behavior, defined by impaired reality testing due to diverse causes, including primary psychotic illnesses, mood disorders, substance use, or medical conditions. Core features include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking or behavior, and reduced insight, usually emerging in adolescents or adults. Management focuses on diagnostic evaluation, treatment of underlying causes, antipsychotic medication, psychotherapy, and safety/supportive care.

head: Citalopram (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Citalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, a bicyclic phthalane derivative, that inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder and is also used for panic disorder in some jurisdictions. A notable safety concern is dose-dependent QT interval prolongation, making it contraindicated with pimozide and requiring caution in patients with congenital long QT syndrome.
tail_desc: Unipolar depression is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by one or more major depressive episodes without a history of mania or hypomania. Its pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving genetic vulnerability, stress-related neurobiological changes, and neurotransmitter dysregulation. Typical features include persistent low mood, anhedonia, sleep and appetite disturbance, fatigue, and impaired concentration; management commonly combines psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, and safety monitoring.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis is a heterogeneous group of chronic autoimmune/autoinflammatory arthritides affecting the joints in children under 16 years. It causes persistent synovial inflammation of unknown exact cause, leading to joint pain, swelling, stiffness, and reduced mobility, sometimes with systemic or eye involvement such as uveitis. Management aims to control inflammation and preserve function using NSAIDs, disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs, biologics, and physical therapy.

head: Olaparib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Olaparib is an oral poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitor, primarily targeting PARP1 and PARP2 to block DNA repair and promote synthetic lethality in homologous recombination–deficient tumors, including those with BRCA1/2 mutations. It is approved for selected ovarian, breast, pancreatic, and prostate cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, particularly anemia.
tail_desc: Ovarian cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the ovary, usually epithelial in origin, affecting the female reproductive system. It often presents in middle-aged or older women with abdominal bloating, pelvic pain, early satiety, ascites, or a pelvic mass, and may be diagnosed late due to nonspecific symptoms. Management typically involves surgical staging and cytoreductive surgery with platinum-based chemotherapy, guided by histology and stage.

head: Esomeprazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Esomeprazole is the S-isomer proton pump inhibitor of omeprazole, a substituted benzimidazole that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, erosive esophagitis, peptic ulcer disease including NSAID-associated ulcers, and Helicobacter pylori eradication regimens. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea.
tail_desc: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is a gastroenterologic disorder caused by gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumors (gastrinomas), usually in the pancreas or duodenum, leading to marked gastric acid hypersecretion. It typically presents in adults with severe, recurrent peptic ulcers, gastroesophageal reflux, abdominal pain, and diarrhea, and may occur sporadically or with MEN1. Management focuses on potent acid suppression and localization and treatment of the tumor, including surgery when feasible.

head: Tobramycin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tobramycin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, disrupting protein synthesis and causing mistranslation and bactericidal activity, particularly against aerobic Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas aeruginosa. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections and as inhaled therapy in cystic fibrosis airway infection. Notable adverse effects include nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Niacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Niacin (nicotinic acid, vitamin B3) is a water-soluble vitamin used pharmacologically as an antihyperlipidemic agent. At therapeutic doses it lowers triglycerides and LDL cholesterol and raises HDL, partly via activation of the hydroxycarboxylic acid receptor 2 (HCAR2/GPR109A) in adipose tissue, reducing lipolysis and hepatic VLDL synthesis. It is used for dyslipidemia, and notable adverse effects include flushing and hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Vitamin deficiency disorder is a nutritional deficiency disease that can affect multiple organ systems, arising from inadequate intake, impaired absorption, increased requirements, or abnormal metabolism of one or more vitamins. Manifestations vary by the deficient vitamin but commonly include fatigue, anemia, neuropathy, mucocutaneous changes, and impaired growth or bone health, especially in malnourished, elderly, alcohol-dependent, or malabsorptive patients. Management focuses on identifying the cause, replacing the deficient vitamin, correcting diet, and treating complications.

head: Latanoprost (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Latanoprost is a prostaglandin F2α analog used as an ophthalmic antiglaucoma agent. It is a prodrug that activates the prostanoid FP receptor in the eye, increasing uveoscleral outflow of aqueous humor and thereby lowering intraocular pressure. It is indicated for open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension. A notable adverse effect is gradual, often permanent, increased brown pigmentation of the iris.
tail_desc: Ocular hypertension is an ophthalmic condition characterized by persistently elevated intraocular pressure without detectable glaucomatous optic nerve damage or visual field loss. It results from impaired aqueous humor outflow, increasing risk of primary open-angle glaucoma. Patients are often asymptomatic and identified on routine eye examination, especially adults with risk factors. Management centers on pressure-lowering eye drops and regular monitoring of optic nerves, visual fields, and intraocular pressure.

head: Carbidopa (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carbidopa is a peripheral aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase (DOPA decarboxylase) inhibitor used with levodopa in Parkinson’s disease and parkinsonism to reduce peripheral conversion of levodopa to dopamine, thereby increasing central levodopa availability and lessening nausea and vomiting. It has no meaningful antiparkinsonian effect alone. A notable contraindication is concomitant use with nonselective monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Parkinsonian disorder refers to a group of neurodegenerative movement disorders affecting the central nervous system, characterized by parkinsonism due to dopaminergic pathway dysfunction, often involving degeneration of nigrostriatal neurons. Core features include bradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor, and postural instability, typically in older adults. Management is mainly symptomatic, using dopaminergic medications, rehabilitation, and treatment of underlying causes when identifiable.

head: Cyanocobalamin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cyanocobalamin is a synthetic vitamin B12 (cobalamin) used as a hematinic replacement therapy. It is converted in vivo to active coenzyme forms of vitamin B12 that support methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, restoring DNA synthesis and normal hematopoiesis in deficiency. It is indicated for prevention and treatment of vitamin B12 deficiency, including pernicious anemia. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to cobalt or cobalamins.
tail_desc: Folic acid deficiency anemia is a megaloblastic anemia of the hematologic system caused by inadequate folate, impairing DNA synthesis and producing ineffective erythropoiesis with enlarged red cell precursors. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, glossitis, and macrocytosis, often in malnutrition, alcoholism, pregnancy, malabsorption, or increased demand. Management involves folate replacement and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Astemizole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Astemizole is a second-generation antihistamine of the benzimidazole/piperidine class that acts as a selective peripheral H1 histamine receptor antagonist. It was used for allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation leading to serious ventricular arrhythmias, especially with CYP3A4 inhibitors, which led to its withdrawal in many countries.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Acemetacin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acemetacin is an indole acetic acid derivative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) and a prodrug of indomethacin. It exerts analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects primarily through cyclooxygenase inhibition, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is used for painful inflammatory rheumatic disorders, including rheumatoid arthritis, osteoarthritis, and ankylosing spondylitis. A notable adverse effect is gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding; it is contraindicated in active peptic ulcer disease.
tail_desc: Spondyloarthropathy susceptibility refers to genetic predisposition to the spondyloarthropathies, a group of inflammatory rheumatic disorders primarily affecting the axial skeleton, sacroiliac joints, entheses, and sometimes peripheral joints. It is strongly associated with immune-mediated mechanisms, especially HLA-B27 and related loci. Typical manifestations include inflammatory back pain, stiffness, enthesitis, and uveitis, often beginning in young adults; management follows the specific spondyloarthropathy phenotype with anti-inflammatory therapy and physical rehabilitation.

head: Entacapone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary late onset Parkinson disease (a disease)
head_desc: Entacapone is a nitrocatechol catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) inhibitor that acts peripherally to block levodopa metabolism, prolonging levodopa exposure and central dopaminergic benefit. It is used as adjunct therapy to levodopa/carbidopa for Parkinson’s disease, especially in patients with end-of-dose “wearing-off” fluctuations. A notable adverse effect is dyskinesia, and it may also cause diarrhea or orange-brown urine discoloration.
tail_desc: Hereditary late-onset Parkinson disease is a genetic form of Parkinson disease, a neurodegenerative disorder of the central nervous system. It is associated with inherited variants that promote degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra and abnormal alpha-synuclein-related pathology in some forms. It typically presents in later adulthood with bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and postural instability, and is managed with standard symptomatic Parkinson therapies plus genetic counseling.

head: Lorazepam (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic and sedative-hypnotic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA\(_A\) receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders, short-term relief of anxiety symptoms, status epilepticus, preanesthetic sedation, and insomnia in selected settings. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation and respiratory depression, especially with other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Anxiety is a mental health condition involving excessive fear, worry, or apprehension, primarily affecting the central nervous system and stress-response pathways. It is multifactorial, with contributions from genetic vulnerability, neurobiological dysregulation, and psychosocial stressors. Common features include persistent nervousness, restlessness, impaired concentration, sleep disturbance, and autonomic symptoms; it can affect people of any age. Management typically includes psychotherapy, stress reduction, and when indicated, pharmacotherapy such as antidepressants.

head: Beractant (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Beractant is a natural bovine lung–derived pulmonary surfactant replacement containing phospholipids and surfactant-associated proteins (mainly SP-B and SP-C). It lowers alveolar surface tension at the air–liquid interface rather than acting on a specific receptor, improving lung compliance and gas exchange. It is used for prevention and treatment of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome in premature infants. A notable adverse effect is transient bradycardia with oxygen desaturation during intratracheal administration.
tail_desc: Acute respiratory distress syndrome is a severe inflammatory lung disorder of the respiratory system, usually triggered by sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, or major trauma, causing diffuse alveolar-capillary injury, noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, and profound hypoxemia. Patients, often critically ill adults, develop rapid-onset dyspnea, tachypnea, and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Management is supportive and directed at the cause, especially lung-protective mechanical ventilation and intensive care.

head: Nintedanib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Nintedanib is an oral small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the indolinone class that targets VEGFR, FGFR, and PDGFR signaling, thereby inhibiting angiogenic and fibroproliferative pathways. It is approved for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, other chronic fibrosing interstitial lung diseases with a progressive phenotype, systemic sclerosis-associated interstitial lung disease, and, with docetaxel, previously treated non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma. A notable adverse effect is diarrhea.
tail_desc: Acute interstitial pneumonia is a rare, idiopathic, fulminant diffuse parenchymal lung disease affecting the respiratory system, considered an acute interstitial pneumonia pattern of idiopathic interstitial pneumonia. Pathologically it shows diffuse alveolar damage, causing rapid onset dyspnea, cough, fever, severe hypoxemia, and often progression to acute respiratory failure in previously healthy adults. Management is mainly intensive supportive care, often with mechanical ventilation, with corticosteroids commonly tried despite limited evidence.

head: Enasidenib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with NPM1 somatic mutations (a disease)
head_desc: Enasidenib is an oral small-molecule isocitrate dehydrogenase-2 (IDH2) inhibitor that selectively targets mutant IDH2 enzymes, lowering the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate and promoting myeloid differentiation. It is approved for adults with relapsed or refractory acute myeloid leukemia harboring an IDH2 mutation. A notable adverse effect is differentiation syndrome, which can be severe or fatal if not promptly recognized and treated.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with NPM1 somatic mutations is a distinct myeloid neoplasm of the bone marrow and blood, driven by acquired NPM1 mutations that impair normal myeloid differentiation. It commonly presents in adults with cytopenias, fatigue, infections, bleeding, and increased marrow or blood blasts, often with normal karyotype. Management follows AML risk-adapted therapy, including induction chemotherapy and molecular monitoring for measurable residual disease.

head: Salicylic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Salicylic acid is a keratolytic and topical salicylate, chemically a beta-hydroxy acid. It acts mainly by solubilizing intercellular cement in the stratum corneum, promoting desquamation; unlike acetylsalicylic acid, it is not used primarily for systemic COX inhibition. It is established for acne, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, warts, and calluses/corns. Notable risks include local irritation and salicylate toxicity with excessive topical use.
tail_desc: Tinea pedis is a superficial dermatophyte fungal infection of the skin of the feet, usually caused by Trichophyton species infecting keratinized tissue. It commonly affects adolescents and adults, especially in warm, moist, occlusive footwear settings, and causes interdigital scaling, erythema, pruritus, fissuring, or moccasin-type plantar hyperkeratosis. Management typically involves foot hygiene and topical antifungals, with oral therapy for extensive or refractory disease.

head: Acarbose (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acarbose is an oral alpha-glucosidase inhibitor, a pseudotetrasaccharide antidiabetic agent that competitively inhibits intestinal brush-border alpha-glucosidases and pancreatic alpha-amylase, delaying carbohydrate digestion and reducing postprandial glucose excursions. It is used to improve glycemic control in type 2 diabetes mellitus. Common adverse effects are flatulence and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in inflammatory bowel disease or intestinal obstruction.
tail_desc: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder of glucose regulation in the endocrine system, characterized by insulin resistance and progressive pancreatic beta-cell dysfunction causing hyperglycemia. It commonly affects adults, especially those with obesity or sedentary lifestyle, and may present with polyuria, polydipsia, fatigue, and long-term vascular complications. Management focuses on weight loss, diet, exercise, glucose-lowering medications, and cardiovascular risk reduction.

head: Desonide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Desonide is a low-potency topical corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class that activates intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to suppress inflammatory and immune gene expression. It is established for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses, including atopic dermatitis and other eczematous or allergic inflammatory skin conditions. A notable adverse effect is local skin atrophy with prolonged use, especially under occlusion.
tail_desc: Vasomotor rhinitis is a form of nonallergic rhinitis affecting the nasal mucosa, thought to result from dysregulated autonomic or neurovascular control rather than infection or IgE-mediated allergy. It commonly causes chronic nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, postnasal drip, and sneezing, often triggered by irritants, odors, temperature changes, or alcohol, especially in adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance and symptom control, often with intranasal therapies.

head: Vorapaxar (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke (a disease)
head_desc: Vorapaxar is an oral antiplatelet agent of the protease-activated receptor-1 (PAR-1) antagonist class. It selectively inhibits thrombin-mediated platelet activation by blocking PAR-1 on platelets. It is approved to reduce thrombotic cardiovascular events in patients with prior myocardial infarction or peripheral arterial disease. A notable risk is serious bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with prior stroke, transient ischemic attack, or intracranial hemorrhage.
tail_desc: obsolete susceptibility to ischemic stroke is a disease or medical condition.

head: Acefylline (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acefylline is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Heart disease is a broad category of cardiovascular disorders affecting the heart, including coronary artery disease, heart failure, arrhythmias, and valvular disease. Common mechanisms include atherosclerosis, structural abnormalities, and impaired electrical conduction. Typical features include chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, or edema, especially in older adults or those with risk factors. Management generally targets risk reduction, medications, lifestyle change, and sometimes interventional or surgical treatment.

head: Cefonicid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefonicid is a second-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin and soft-tissue, urinary tract, and gynecologic infections. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Pneumonia is an acute infection of the lung parenchyma, usually affecting the lower respiratory system, caused by bacteria, viruses, or less often fungi, leading to alveolar inflammation and impaired gas exchange. Typical features include fever, cough, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and sputum production, especially in young children, older adults, and immunocompromised patients. Management includes supportive care and pathogen-directed antimicrobial therapy when indicated.

head: Esketamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Esketamine is the S-enantiomer of ketamine, a dissociative anesthetic and noncompetitive NMDA receptor antagonist that modulates glutamatergic signaling. It is approved intranasally, with an oral antidepressant, for treatment-resistant major depressive disorder and for depressive symptoms in adults with major depressive disorder and acute suicidal ideation or behavior. Notable adverse effects include dissociation and transient increases in blood pressure.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Metronidazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Metronidazole is a nitroimidazole antimicrobial and antiprotozoal prodrug that is reduced in anaerobic bacteria and certain protozoa to reactive radicals that damage DNA. It is used for anaerobic bacterial infections, bacterial vaginosis, Clostridioides difficile infection, and protozoal diseases such as trichomoniasis, giardiasis, and amebiasis. A notable adverse effect is a disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol.
tail_desc: Subacute bacterial endocarditis is a slowly progressive infective endocarditis of the cardiovascular system, usually involving heart valves damaged by prior valvular or congenital heart disease. It is typically caused by low-virulence bacteria, classically viridans streptococci, producing infected vegetations. Patients often have prolonged low-grade fever, malaise, weight loss, anemia, new or changing murmur, and embolic or immunologic phenomena; management centers on prolonged intravenous bactericidal antibiotics and valve surgery when complications occur.

head: Levodopa (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Levodopa (L-DOPA) is a dopamine precursor and antiparkinsonian agent that crosses the blood-brain barrier and is decarboxylated by aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase to dopamine, replenishing striatal dopamine. It is an established treatment for Parkinson’s disease and parkinsonism, usually combined with a peripheral decarboxylase inhibitor such as carbidopa. A notable adverse effect is dyskinesia.
tail_desc: Juvenile-onset Parkinson disease 19A is a hereditary neurodegenerative movement disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by early-onset parkinsonism beginning in childhood or adolescence. It is caused by biallelic pathogenic variants in DNAJC6, which impair synaptic vesicle trafficking and dopaminergic neuronal function. Typical features include bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, dystonia, and gait impairment; management is mainly symptomatic, including dopaminergic therapy and supportive multidisciplinary care.

head: Procaine benzylpenicillin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Procaine benzylpenicillin is a depot intramuscular formulation of benzylpenicillin (penicillin G), a beta-lactam penicillin antibiotic combined with procaine. It inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins and blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including syphilis in some settings. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins, with risk of anaphylaxis.
tail_desc: Subacute bacterial endocarditis is a slowly progressive infective endocarditis of the cardiovascular system, usually involving heart valves damaged by prior valvular or congenital heart disease. It is typically caused by low-virulence bacteria, classically viridans streptococci, producing infected vegetations. Patients often have prolonged low-grade fever, malaise, weight loss, anemia, new or changing murmur, and embolic or immunologic phenomena; management centers on prolonged intravenous bactericidal antibiotics and valve surgery when complications occur.

head: Olaratumab (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Olaratumab is a recombinant human IgG1 monoclonal antibody that targets platelet-derived growth factor receptor alpha (PDGFR-α), blocking ligand binding and downstream signaling involved in tumor stromal growth. It was approved, in combination with doxorubicin, for certain advanced soft tissue sarcomas not amenable to curative treatment, but was later withdrawn after lack of confirmed benefit. Notable adverse effects include infusion-related reactions.
tail_desc: Soft tissue sarcoma is a heterogeneous group of malignant mesenchymal tumors arising in soft tissues such as muscle, fat, fibrous tissue, vessels, or nerves. It typically presents in adults as a painless enlarging deep soft-tissue mass, though symptoms depend on size and location. Management usually centers on wide surgical excision, often with radiotherapy, and selected cases require systemic chemotherapy or targeted therapy.

head: Belotecan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Belotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. It stabilizes the topoisomerase I–DNA cleavable complex, preventing DNA religation and causing DNA strand breaks during replication. It has been used clinically, including for small cell lung cancer in some markets. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, particularly neutropenia.
tail_desc: Ovarian cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the ovary, usually epithelial in origin, affecting the female reproductive system. It often presents in middle-aged or older women with abdominal bloating, pelvic pain, early satiety, ascites, or a pelvic mass, and may be diagnosed late due to nonspecific symptoms. Management typically involves surgical staging and cytoreductive surgery with platinum-based chemotherapy, guided by histology and stage.

head: Thiabendazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Thiabendazole is a benzimidazole anthelmintic and antifungal agent. It acts by binding parasite β-tubulin, inhibiting microtubule polymerization and glucose uptake, leading to impaired energy metabolism. It has been used for treatment of strongyloidiasis and cutaneous larva migrans. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset and dizziness, and it is generally avoided in patients with hypersensitivity to benzimidazoles.
tail_desc: Enterobiasis is an intestinal helminth infection caused by the pinworm Enterobius vermicularis, affecting the gastrointestinal and perianal region, especially in children. It commonly presents with nocturnal perianal itching, disturbed sleep, and sometimes abdominal discomfort; many cases are asymptomatic. Management typically involves antihelminthic therapy, treatment of close contacts, and strict hygiene measures to reduce reinfection.

head: Alteplase (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Alteplase is a recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (thrombolytic/fibrinolytic protein) that binds fibrin in thrombi and converts plasminogen to plasmin, promoting clot dissolution. It is used for acute ischemic stroke, acute myocardial infarction, acute massive pulmonary embolism, and restoration of function in occluded central venous catheters. Major adverse effects include serious bleeding, especially intracranial hemorrhage; active internal bleeding is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Pulmonary embolism is an acute thromboembolic disease of the pulmonary vasculature, usually caused by embolization of a deep venous thrombosis to the pulmonary arteries. It commonly presents with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, hypoxemia, and sometimes hemoptysis or syncope, especially in patients with venous thromboembolism risk factors. Management centers on prompt anticoagulation, with thrombolysis or embolectomy for hemodynamically unstable cases.

head: Cefamandole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefamandole is a second-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing bactericidal activity. It has been used parenterally for susceptible respiratory, urinary tract, skin/soft-tissue, bone and joint, and gynecologic infections, and perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypoprothrombinemia with bleeding risk due to its N-methylthiotetrazole side chain.
tail_desc: Streptococcal pneumonia is a bacterial lower respiratory tract infection of the lungs, most commonly caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, producing acute inflammation and alveolar consolidation. It typically presents with fever, cough, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and purulent sputum, especially in young children, older adults, and immunocompromised patients. Management centers on prompt appropriate antibiotic therapy and supportive respiratory care.

head: Racementhol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Racementhol is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Dermatitis is a common inflammatory disorder of the skin, often caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, irritant or allergic exposures, or immune dysregulation. It typically presents with pruritus, erythema, scaling, and sometimes vesicles or lichenification, affecting both children and adults depending on subtype. Management generally focuses on trigger avoidance, skin hydration with emollients, and anti-inflammatory topical therapies such as corticosteroids.

head: Gilteritinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with t(9;11)(p22;q23) (a disease)
head_desc: Gilteritinib is an oral small-molecule tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the FLT3 receptor, with activity against both FLT3 internal tandem duplication and tyrosine kinase domain mutations, and also inhibits AXL. It is approved for adults with relapsed or refractory acute myeloid leukemia harboring a FLT3 mutation. A notable serious adverse effect is differentiation syndrome; QT interval prolongation can also occur.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with t(9;11)(p22;q23) is a subtype of AML affecting the hematopoietic system, defined by a KMT2A (MLL)-MLLT3 fusion from the t(9;11) translocation. It commonly shows monoblastic/monocytic differentiation, occurs in children and adults, and presents with typical AML features such as cytopenias and organ infiltration. Management follows AML-directed multiagent chemotherapy, with risk-adapted consideration of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Loracarbef (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Loracarbef is an oral carbacephem beta-lactam antibiotic, structurally related to second-generation cephalosporins. It binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis. It has been used for susceptible infections including otitis media, sinusitis, pharyngitis/tonsillitis, lower respiratory tract infections, and uncomplicated skin or urinary tract infections. A notable risk is hypersensitivity, particularly in patients with cephalosporin or penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: The common cold is an acute, self-limited viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, primarily affecting the nose and throat. It is caused by numerous respiratory viruses, especially rhinoviruses, producing mucosal inflammation. Typical features include nasal congestion, rhinorrhea, sneezing, sore throat, cough, and malaise in children and adults. Management is supportive, with rest, fluids, and symptom relief rather than antibiotics.

head: Carbamazepine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carbamazepine is a dibenzazepine anticonvulsant and mood-stabilizing agent that primarily inhibits voltage-gated sodium channels, reducing high-frequency neuronal firing. It is used for focal and generalized tonic-clonic seizures, trigeminal neuralgia, and bipolar disorder, and is also approved for pain associated with true trigeminal neuralgia. A notable risk is serious cutaneous reactions, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, especially in HLA-B*1502 carriers.
tail_desc: Cutis verticis gyrata is a rare disorder of the scalp in which excessive skin and subcutaneous tissue form thick folds and furrows resembling cerebral gyri. It may be primary or secondary to endocrine, inflammatory, or neoplastic conditions. It typically affects postpubertal males and presents with cosmetic deformity and hygiene difficulties; management focuses on treating underlying causes and, when needed, surgical correction.

head: Nizatidine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Nizatidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It is used for duodenal ulcer, benign gastric ulcer, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and erosive esophagitis. Notable adverse effects include headache and diarrhea, and dose adjustment is recommended in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Esophagitis is inflammation of the esophageal mucosa, a gastrointestinal disorder most often caused by gastroesophageal reflux, medications, infections, or eosinophilic inflammation. Typical features include odynophagia, dysphagia, heartburn, and chest pain, occurring in adults or children depending on cause. Management targets the etiology, such as acid suppression, stopping offending drugs, dietary therapy, or treatment of infection.

head: Balsalazide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Balsalazide is an azo-bonded 5-aminosalicylate prodrug that is cleaved by colonic bacteria to release mesalamine in the colon. Mesalamine acts locally as an anti-inflammatory agent, inhibiting cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase pathways and reducing prostaglandin and leukotriene production. It is approved for mild to moderate ulcerative colitis, and notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity and worsening colitis symptoms.
tail_desc: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the colonic mucosa, typically beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. It reflects immune-mediated intestinal inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals. Common features include bloody diarrhea, urgency, abdominal pain, and relapsing course, often in adolescents and adults. Management focuses on anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapy, with colectomy for refractory or complicated disease.

head: Golimumab (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Golimumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody that targets and neutralizes tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α), reducing inflammatory signaling. It is approved for several immune-mediated inflammatory disorders, including rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, ulcerative colitis, and polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis. A notable risk is serious infection, including reactivation of latent tuberculosis.
tail_desc: Psoriatic arthritis is a chronic inflammatory musculoskeletal disease associated with psoriasis, affecting joints, entheses, and sometimes the axial skeleton. It is immune mediated, with synovial and entheseal inflammation leading to pain, stiffness, swelling, dactylitis, and potential joint damage. It typically occurs in adults with psoriasis. Management aims to control inflammation and prevent structural damage using NSAIDs, conventional DMARDs, and biologic or targeted therapies.

head: Calcium glucoheptonate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcium glucoheptonate is a soluble organic calcium salt used as a mineral supplement to prevent or treat calcium deficiency states. It acts by supplying elemental calcium, increasing extracellular calcium availability rather than acting at a specific molecular target. Established uses include hypocalcemia and supplementation during periods of increased calcium requirement. Adverse effects may include constipation or, with excessive intake, hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Dihydrocodeine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dihydrocodeine is a semisynthetic opioid analgesic of the phenanthrene/codeine family that acts primarily as a mu-opioid receptor agonist in the central nervous system to reduce pain perception and suppress cough. It is used for moderate pain and, in some formulations, as an antitussive. A notable adverse effect is respiratory depression, and it is contraindicated in significant respiratory compromise.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Diphenylpyraline (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Diphenylpyraline is a first-generation ethanolamine antihistamine with anticholinergic properties. It acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist (more precisely, an inverse agonist), reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic conditions such as allergic rhinitis and urticaria. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and caution is warranted in narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Angioedema is a vascular permeability disorder of the skin and mucosa causing sudden, localized swelling, often involving the face, lips, tongue, extremities, or airway. It results from mast cell mediator release or bradykinin excess, and may be allergic, medication-induced, or hereditary. Patients present with episodic nonpitting edema, sometimes with urticaria or life-threatening airway compromise; management targets airway protection and treating the underlying mechanism.

head: Chlordiazepoxide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chlordiazepoxide is a long-acting benzodiazepine anxiolytic and sedative-hypnotic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA\(_A\) receptors, enhancing inhibitory GABA signaling in the central nervous system. It is used for anxiety disorders, preoperative apprehension, and management of acute alcohol withdrawal. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation, and it is contraindicated in acute narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Azithromycin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Azithromycin is a macrolide (azalide) antibiotic that binds the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin and soft tissue, and certain sexually transmitted infections such as chlamydia. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, so caution is warranted in patients with arrhythmia risk.
tail_desc: Chlamydial pneumonia is an infectious lower respiratory tract disease of the lungs caused by Chlamydia species, most commonly Chlamydia pneumoniae and, in infants, Chlamydia trachomatis. It typically produces atypical pneumonia with dry cough, fever, and malaise; infants may have an afebrile pneumonia with tachypnea. Management is directed by clinical severity and usually involves appropriate antibiotics such as macrolides or tetracyclines.

head: Cefuroxime (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefuroxime is a second-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including respiratory tract, skin and soft tissue, urinary tract infections, and gonorrhea; perioperative prophylaxis is also established for injectable forms. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, with contraindication in patients with serious cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Bacterial meningitis is an acute, life-threatening infection of the meninges affecting the central nervous system, usually caused by bacteria invading the cerebrospinal fluid and provoking intense inflammation. It commonly presents with fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental status, and sometimes seizures, especially in infants, children, older adults, and immunocompromised patients. Management requires urgent empiric intravenous antibiotics, often with adjunctive dexamethasone, followed by pathogen-directed therapy and supportive care.

head: Mitoxantrone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with CEBPA somatic mutations (a disease)
head_desc: Mitoxantrone is an anthracenedione antineoplastic agent that intercalates into DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, disrupting DNA replication and repair. It is used to treat certain leukemias, metastatic breast cancer, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, and worsening relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis. A notable risk is cumulative dose-related cardiotoxicity, including reduced left ventricular ejection fraction and congestive heart failure.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with CEBPA somatic mutations is a genetically defined subtype of AML, a hematologic malignancy of the bone marrow and blood, driven by acquired mutations in the CEBPA transcription factor that impair myeloid differentiation. Patients present with typical AML features such as cytopenias, infections, bleeding, and fatigue. Management generally follows AML protocols with mutation-informed risk stratification and consideration of allogeneic transplantation in selected cases.

head: Aminolevulinic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Aminolevulinic acid is a naturally occurring heme precursor and photosensitizing prodrug. After topical or oral administration, it is converted intracellularly to protoporphyrin IX, which accumulates preferentially in dysplastic or neoplastic cells and, on illumination with specific light, generates reactive oxygen species causing local cytotoxicity. It is used for photodynamic diagnosis or treatment of actinic keratoses and certain bladder cancers. Photosensitivity is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Skin disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the integumentary system, including inflammatory, infectious, autoimmune, genetic, neoplastic, and environmental conditions of the skin. Common manifestations include rash, pruritus, pain, pigment change, scaling, ulceration, or lesions in patients of any age. Management depends on cause and severity and may involve skin care, trigger avoidance, topical or systemic therapy, and specialist evaluation.

head: Docetaxel (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Docetaxel is a semisynthetic taxane antineoplastic agent that binds the β-subunit of tubulin, promoting microtubule assembly and inhibiting depolymerization, thereby blocking mitosis. It is used for breast cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, prostate cancer, gastric adenocarcinoma, and head and neck cancer. A notable toxicity is severe neutropenia; use is contraindicated in patients with baseline neutrophil counts below 1,500 cells/mm³.
tail_desc: Gastric neoplasm is a tumor of the stomach within the gastrointestinal system, encompassing benign and malignant lesions, most commonly gastric adenocarcinoma arising from dysplastic transformation of the gastric mucosa. Patients, often older adults, may present with weight loss, epigastric pain, early satiety, nausea, anemia, or gastrointestinal bleeding. Management depends on histology and stage, typically involving endoscopic or surgical resection, with systemic therapy for advanced disease.

head: Mogamulizumab (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: mature T-cell and NK-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (a disease)
head_desc: Mogamulizumab is a humanized, defucosylated IgG1 kappa monoclonal antibody targeting CCR4, enhancing antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity against CCR4-expressing malignant T cells. It is approved for relapsed or refractory mycosis fungoides and Sézary syndrome after prior systemic therapy. A notable adverse effect is severe rash, and serious infusion-related reactions can also occur.
tail_desc: Mature T-cell and NK-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma is an aggressive, heterogeneous group of lymphoid malignancies arising from post-thymic T lymphocytes or natural killer cells, affecting lymph nodes, blood, marrow, skin, and extranodal tissues. It typically occurs in adults and presents with lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, extranodal disease, and often advanced stage. Management generally relies on subtype-specific staging and systemic chemotherapy, with selected use of radiotherapy, targeted agents, or stem cell transplantation.

head: Sulindac (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sulindac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) of the indene acetic acid derivative class, used as a prodrug whose active sulfide metabolite reversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase COX-1 and COX-2, reducing prostaglandin synthesis. It is established for osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, and acute gouty arthritis. Notable risks include serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding, and it is contraindicated after coronary artery bypass graft surgery.
tail_desc: Periarthritis is a musculoskeletal disorder involving inflammation and degenerative change in tissues around a joint, such as the capsule, tendons, bursae, and ligaments, rather than the articular surfaces themselves. It typically causes localized pain, tenderness, and restricted movement, often in the shoulder. Management is usually conservative, with rest, physiotherapy, analgesics or NSAIDs, and sometimes corticosteroid injection.

head: Amantadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Amantadine is an adamantane derivative antiviral and antiparkinsonian agent. It blocks the influenza A M2 proton channel and, in the CNS, increases dopaminergic signaling and has NMDA receptor antagonist activity. It is used for Parkinson disease and drug-induced extrapyramidal symptoms; antiviral use is limited by resistance. Notable adverse effects include confusion, hallucinations, and livedo reticularis.
tail_desc: Parkinsonian-pyramidal syndrome is a neurologic movement disorder characterized by combined parkinsonism and pyramidal tract dysfunction, affecting the central nervous system. It is classically linked to neurodegenerative disorders, including some hereditary forms such as ATP13A2-related Kufor-Rakeb syndrome. Typical features include bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability, spasticity, hyperreflexia, and gait impairment, often with juvenile or young-adult onset; management is supportive and symptomatic, including dopaminergic therapy, rehabilitation, and genetic evaluation when indicated.

head: Tulobuterol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tulobuterol is a selective β2-adrenergic receptor agonist bronchodilator of the phenethylamine class. By stimulating β2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, it increases cyclic AMP and promotes relaxation of the airways. It is used for asthma and other reversible obstructive airway diseases, including chronic bronchitis. A notable adverse effect is tachycardia; it should be used cautiously in patients with significant cardiovascular disease.
tail_desc: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disease of the lower airways characterized by variable, usually reversible airflow obstruction and bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It commonly causes episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and dyspnea, often triggered by allergens, exercise, infections, or irritants, in children and adults. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, inhaled bronchodilators for relief, and inhaled corticosteroids or other controller therapies to reduce inflammation and exacerbations.

head: Levothyroxine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Levothyroxine is a synthetic tetraiodinated thyroid hormone (T4) replacement that is converted peripherally to triiodothyronine (T3), which binds nuclear thyroid hormone receptors (TRα/TRβ) to regulate gene transcription and metabolism. It is used primarily for hypothyroidism and TSH suppression in certain thyroid disorders, including differentiated thyroid cancer. A notable adverse effect is iatrogenic hyperthyroidism, and it is contraindicated in untreated thyrotoxicosis.
tail_desc: Myxedema is a severe manifestation of hypothyroidism affecting the endocrine system, caused by profound deficiency of thyroid hormone, with dermal glycosaminoglycan accumulation producing nonpitting edema. It typically occurs in adults with longstanding untreated hypothyroidism and features fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, dry skin, and facial puffiness; management centers on thyroid hormone replacement and treatment of precipitating factors, with myxedema coma requiring urgent intensive care.

head: Ipratropium (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ipratropium is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic bronchodilator, structurally related to atropine. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle, reducing vagally mediated bronchoconstriction and secretions. It is used as an inhaled treatment for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and also for bronchospasm in asthma. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth.
tail_desc: Bronchial disease is a broad respiratory category involving the bronchi, encompassing inflammatory, obstructive, or infectious disorders rather than a single specific diagnosis. Pathology may include bronchial wall inflammation, mucus hypersecretion, bronchospasm, or structural damage, causing cough, wheeze, sputum production, and dyspnea in children or adults. Management depends on cause and typically includes trigger control, bronchodilators, anti-inflammatory therapy, and treatment of infection when present.

head: Ticarcillin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ticarcillin is a semisynthetic carboxypenicillin, a beta-lactam antipseudomonal antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing cell lysis. It has been used for susceptible gram-negative infections, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, often in combination with clavulanate. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Urinary tract infection is a common bacterial infection of the urinary system, affecting the bladder, urethra, and sometimes kidneys. It usually results from ascending uropathogens, especially Escherichia coli. Typical features include dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, suprapubic pain, and sometimes fever or flank pain. It occurs across ages, especially in women, and is managed with appropriate antibiotics, hydration, and evaluation for complicating factors.

head: Cysteine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid used pharmaceutically as a nutritional additive, especially in parenteral nutrition solutions. It primarily serves as a metabolic precursor for protein synthesis and glutathione production rather than acting through a discrete drug target or receptor. Clinically, it is indicated to meet amino acid requirements, particularly in neonates and infants. Adverse effects may include hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder affecting primarily the respiratory and gastrointestinal systems, caused by pathogenic variants in CFTR that impair chloride and water transport, producing thick secretions. It typically presents in childhood with recurrent lung infections, chronic cough, pancreatic insufficiency, malabsorption, and poor growth. Management includes airway clearance, antimicrobial therapy, nutritional support, pancreatic enzymes, and CFTR modulator therapy when eligible.

head: Vonicog Alfa (a drug)
