relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Vonicog alfa is a recombinant von Willebrand factor (vWF) replacement product. It restores deficient or dysfunctional vWF, promoting platelet adhesion at sites of vascular injury and stabilizing endogenous factor VIII. It is indicated for on-demand treatment and control of bleeding episodes, perioperative bleeding management, and routine prophylaxis in adults with von Willebrand disease. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions and thromboembolic events.
tail_desc: Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder, affecting the hemostatic system. It results from quantitative or qualitative deficiency of von Willebrand factor, causing impaired platelet adhesion and sometimes reduced factor VIII levels. Patients, often presenting from childhood, have mucocutaneous bleeding such as epistaxis, easy bruising, menorrhagia, and prolonged bleeding after procedures; management includes desmopressin, von Willebrand factor replacement, and antifibrinolytics.

head: Dexbrompheniramine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Dexbrompheniramine is a first-generation alkylamine antihistamine, the dextrorotatory isomer of brompheniramine. It acts primarily as a competitive antagonist/inverse agonist at histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated symptoms. It is used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis, urticaria, and other allergic conditions, as well as common-cold symptoms. A notable adverse effect is sedation, and it is generally contraindicated in newborns and during monoamine oxidase inhibitor therapy.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Aurothioglucose (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Aurothioglucose is a gold(I) thiolate compound formerly used as a disease-modifying antirheumatic drug for rheumatoid arthritis. Its precise mechanism is incompletely defined, but gold salts modulate immune and inflammatory cell function rather than acting on a single established molecular target. Important adverse effects include dermatitis, stomatitis, proteinuria, and bone marrow suppression; use is contraindicated in patients with prior severe gold toxicity.
tail_desc: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic systemic autoimmune inflammatory disease primarily affecting synovial joints. Immune-mediated synovitis causes pain, swelling, morning stiffness, and progressive cartilage and bone destruction, often in a symmetric polyarticular pattern, with extra-articular manifestations possible. It occurs most often in adults, especially women. Management aims to suppress inflammation and prevent joint damage using DMARDs, biologics, and supportive care.

head: Calcifediol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcifediol (25-hydroxyvitamin D3, calcidiol) is a vitamin D metabolite and prohormone used to raise vitamin D status. It is converted in the kidney to calcitriol, which activates the vitamin D receptor (VDR) to increase intestinal calcium and phosphate absorption and suppress parathyroid hormone. It is used for vitamin D deficiency/insufficiency and secondary hyperparathyroidism in chronic kidney disease; notable risks include hypercalcemia and hyperphosphatemia.
tail_desc: Hypophosphatemia is an electrolyte/metabolic disorder of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, muscle, and neuromuscular function, caused by inadequate intestinal absorption, intracellular phosphate shifts, or excessive renal losses. Manifestations range from weakness, bone pain, and osteomalacia or rickets to severe respiratory, neurologic, or cardiac dysfunction. Management targets the cause and typically includes oral or intravenous phosphate replacement and correction of associated vitamin D deficiency.

head: Caffeine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Caffeine is a methylxanthine central nervous system stimulant that primarily acts as a nonselective antagonist of adenosine A1 and A2A receptors. It is established for treatment of apnea of prematurity (as caffeine citrate) and is also used as an analgesic adjuvant. Notable adverse effects include insomnia, tachycardia, and nervousness; it should be used cautiously in patients with significant arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Salicylic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Salicylic acid is a keratolytic and topical salicylate, chemically a beta-hydroxy acid. It acts mainly by solubilizing intercellular cement in the stratum corneum, promoting desquamation; unlike acetylsalicylic acid, it is not used primarily for systemic COX inhibition. It is established for acne, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, warts, and calluses/corns. Notable risks include local irritation and salicylate toxicity with excessive topical use.
tail_desc: Seborrheic keratosis is a common benign epidermal skin tumor caused by clonal proliferation of immature keratinocytes. It typically affects middle-aged and older adults, presenting as well-circumscribed, waxy, verrucous, “stuck-on” papules or plaques, often tan to dark brown, on the trunk, face, or extremities. Management is usually reassurance; symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome lesions may be removed with cryotherapy, curettage, or shave excision.

head: Methylprednisolone hemisuccinate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methylprednisolone hemisuccinate is a water-soluble ester prodrug of methylprednisolone, a synthetic glucocorticoid corticosteroid. After conversion to methylprednisolone, it binds the glucocorticoid receptor (NR3C1) to regulate gene transcription and suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It is used parenterally for severe allergic, inflammatory, autoimmune, and adrenal insufficiency states. Notable adverse effects include hyperglycemia and increased infection risk; systemic fungal infection is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Nephrotic syndrome is a glomerular disorder of the kidneys characterized by increased glomerular permeability causing heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and often hyperlipidemia. It can occur in children and adults, commonly from primary glomerular diseases or secondary systemic conditions such as diabetes. Management targets the underlying cause and includes edema control, reduction of proteinuria, and prevention of complications.

head: Olanzapine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Olanzapine is an atypical antipsychotic of the thienobenzodiazepine class that acts mainly as an antagonist at dopamine D2 and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors, with additional activity at histamine, muscarinic, and adrenergic receptors. It is used for schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, including acute mania; notable adverse effects include marked weight gain and metabolic syndrome.
tail_desc: Manic bipolar affective disorder is a mood disorder within the bipolar spectrum affecting the central nervous system, characterized by manic episodes with abnormally elevated or irritable mood, increased energy, reduced need for sleep, pressured speech, impulsivity, and possible psychosis, often alternating with depression. It typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood, and management centers on mood stabilizers, antipsychotics, and ongoing psychiatric care.

head: Etoposide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Etoposide is a semisynthetic epipodophyllotoxin antineoplastic agent that inhibits topoisomerase II, causing DNA strand breaks and cell-cycle arrest. It is used in established regimens for small cell lung cancer, testicular cancer, and several hematologic or solid malignancies. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially neutropenia; it is generally avoided in severe bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Spermatocytic seminoma is a rare testicular germ cell tumor arising in the male reproductive system, now generally termed spermatocytic tumor. It likely originates from postpubertal germ cells and typically affects older men, presenting as a slow-growing, usually painless unilateral testicular mass. Metastasis is uncommon, and management is primarily radical orchiectomy, with further therapy rarely needed unless sarcomatous transformation occurs.

head: Lercanidipine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Lercanidipine is a lipophilic dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing peripheral arterial vasodilation and lowering blood pressure. It is established for the treatment of essential hypertension. A notable adverse effect is peripheral edema; it is contraindicated in severe hypotension and in patients with significant aortic stenosis or unstable angina.
tail_desc: Hypertension is a chronic cardiovascular disorder defined by persistently elevated systemic arterial blood pressure, usually arising from multifactorial interactions among genetic susceptibility, increased vascular resistance, neurohormonal dysregulation, and environmental factors; most cases are primary, while some are secondary to renal or endocrine disease. It is common in adults, often asymptomatic, but may cause headache or end-organ damage, and is managed with lifestyle modification and antihypertensive medication.

head: Cefapirin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefapirin is a first-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic. It acts by binding penicillin-binding proteins and inhibiting bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, producing bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections and is also established intramammarily in veterinary medicine for bovine mastitis. Like other cephalosporins, it can cause hypersensitivity reactions and is contraindicated in patients with cephalosporin allergy.
tail_desc: Staphylococcus aureus infection is a bacterial infection that can affect the skin and soft tissues, bloodstream, lungs, bones, joints, or heart valves. It results from invasion by S. aureus, including toxin production in some syndromes. Clinical features range from boils and abscesses to pneumonia, sepsis, and endocarditis, affecting both healthy people and hospitalized or immunocompromised patients; management relies on drainage when needed and targeted antibiotic therapy.

head: Cabozantinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cabozantinib is an oral small-molecule multikinase inhibitor of the tyrosine kinase inhibitor class that targets MET, VEGFR2, AXL, and related kinases, suppressing tumor growth and angiogenesis. It is approved for renal cell carcinoma, hepatocellular carcinoma previously treated with sorafenib, and differentiated thyroid cancer after prior VEGFR-targeted therapy. A notable serious adverse effect is hemorrhage.
tail_desc: Medullary thyroid gland carcinoma is a neuroendocrine malignancy of the thyroid arising from calcitonin-producing parafollicular C cells, occurring sporadically or in hereditary MEN2 syndromes due to RET mutations. It commonly presents as a thyroid nodule, cervical lymphadenopathy, or diarrhea/flushing from hormone secretion. Management is primarily total thyroidectomy with nodal evaluation, with genetic testing and targeted therapy for advanced disease.

head: Pimavanserin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pimavanserin is an atypical antipsychotic of the piperidine/urea class that acts primarily as a selective serotonin 5-HT2A receptor inverse agonist and antagonist, with lesser activity at 5-HT2C receptors and no meaningful dopaminergic blockade. It is approved for hallucinations and delusions associated with Parkinson’s disease psychosis. A notable risk is QT-interval prolongation, and it carries a boxed warning for increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis.
tail_desc: Parkinsonian-pyramidal syndrome is a neurologic movement disorder characterized by combined parkinsonism and pyramidal tract dysfunction, affecting the central nervous system. It is classically linked to neurodegenerative disorders, including some hereditary forms such as ATP13A2-related Kufor-Rakeb syndrome. Typical features include bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability, spasticity, hyperreflexia, and gait impairment, often with juvenile or young-adult onset; management is supportive and symptomatic, including dopaminergic therapy, rehabilitation, and genetic evaluation when indicated.

head: Cilastatin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cilastatin is a renal dehydropeptidase I inhibitor, a carbapenem adjunct rather than an antibacterial itself. It blocks brush-border DHP-I in the kidney, preventing renal metabolism of imipenem and reducing nephrotoxic metabolites, thereby increasing urinary recovery of active imipenem. It is approved in fixed combination with imipenem/cilastatin for susceptible bacterial infections; adverse effects can include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Subacute bacterial endocarditis is a slowly progressive infective endocarditis of the cardiovascular system, usually involving heart valves damaged by prior valvular or congenital heart disease. It is typically caused by low-virulence bacteria, classically viridans streptococci, producing infected vegetations. Patients often have prolonged low-grade fever, malaise, weight loss, anemia, new or changing murmur, and embolic or immunologic phenomena; management centers on prolonged intravenous bactericidal antibiotics and valve surgery when complications occur.

head: Ipratropium (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ipratropium is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic bronchodilator, structurally related to atropine. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors in airway smooth muscle, reducing vagally mediated bronchoconstriction and secretions. It is used as an inhaled treatment for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and also for bronchospasm in asthma. A notable adverse effect is dry mouth.
tail_desc: Bronchitis is inflammation of the bronchi in the lower respiratory tract, most often acute and caused by viral infection, or chronic as part of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, usually related to smoking or irritant exposure. It commonly causes cough, sputum production, wheeze, chest discomfort, and mild dyspnea in children or adults. Management is mainly supportive for acute disease, while chronic bronchitis requires smoking cessation and COPD-directed therapy.

head: Reboxetine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Reboxetine is a selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (NRI) antidepressant of the morpholine/phenoxybenzyl class that acts primarily by inhibiting the norepinephrine transporter (NET), increasing synaptic norepinephrine. It has been approved in some countries for major depressive disorder. Notable adverse effects include insomnia, dry mouth, constipation, and tachycardia; it is generally contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Anxiety disorder is a mental disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry with dysregulation of neural circuits involved in threat processing, arousal, and emotion regulation. Common features include restlessness, autonomic symptoms, sleep disturbance, and impaired functioning, affecting children and adults. Management typically combines psychotherapy, especially cognitive behavioral therapy, with antidepressants or other anxiolytic treatment when indicated.

head: Enalaprilat (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Enalaprilat is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, the active diacid metabolite of enalapril, that blocks ACE (kininase II) to reduce conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II and decrease aldosterone secretion. It is used intravenously for treatment of hypertension, including when oral therapy is not feasible. A notable adverse effect is hypotension; it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Congestive heart failure is a cardiovascular syndrome in which structural or functional cardiac impairment reduces the heart’s ability to pump or fill effectively, causing pulmonary and/or systemic fluid congestion. It commonly affects older adults and patients with coronary artery disease, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy, producing dyspnea, fatigue, edema, and exercise intolerance. Management focuses on treating the cause, diuresis, guideline-directed heart-failure medications, sodium/fluid control, and selected device or advanced therapies.

head: Cefoxitin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cefoxitin is a second-generation cephamycin beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis, producing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including intra-abdominal, gynecologic, urinary tract, skin and soft-tissue infections, and for perioperative prophylaxis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Staphylococcal infection is a bacterial infection caused by Staphylococcus species, especially S. aureus, affecting skin and soft tissue, blood, lungs, bone, or implanted devices. It typically results from colonization followed by invasion and inflammation, causing boils, cellulitis, abscesses, pneumonia, bacteremia, or sepsis, particularly in hospitalized, immunocompromised, or device-bearing patients. Management involves drainage when needed and targeted antistaphylococcal antibiotics guided by culture and resistance patterns.

head: Aldesleukin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with t(8;16)(p11;p13) translocation (a disease)
head_desc: Aldesleukin is a recombinant human interleukin-2 (IL-2) cytokine immunotherapy that binds IL-2 receptors (CD25/CD122/CD132) on lymphocytes, promoting T-cell and natural killer cell proliferation and activation. It is approved for metastatic renal cell carcinoma and metastatic melanoma. A notable serious adverse effect is capillary leak syndrome, which can cause hypotension and organ dysfunction.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with t(8;16)(p11;p13) is a rare AML subtype of the hematopoietic system defined by a KAT6A::CREBBP fusion from the translocation. It often presents with monocytic/myelomonocytic morphology, prominent erythrophagocytosis, disseminated intravascular coagulation, and extramedullary involvement, occurring in both infants and adults, including therapy-related cases. Management follows intensive AML-directed chemotherapy and supportive care; allogeneic transplantation may be considered in eligible patients.

head: Erythromycin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis by blocking translocation. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin, and soft-tissue infections, and as an alternative in some penicillin-allergic patients. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, which can increase the risk of serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Infectious otitis media is an infection of the middle ear within the ear, nose, and throat system, most often caused by viral or bacterial pathogens ascending through the Eustachian tube, frequently after an upper respiratory infection. It is especially common in young children and typically presents with ear pain, fever, irritability, and reduced hearing. Management usually involves analgesia, observation in selected cases, and antibiotics when bacterial infection is suspected or symptoms are severe.

head: Sulfadiazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sulfadiazine is a sulfonamide antibacterial that competitively inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections and, with pyrimethamine, for toxoplasmosis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, including severe rash such as Stevens-Johnson syndrome; it is generally contraindicated in patients with sulfonamide allergy and near term pregnancy or in neonates due to kernicterus risk.
tail_desc: Hirudiniasis is an infestation by leeches (annelid parasites) affecting exposed skin or mucosal surfaces, especially of the nasopharynx, larynx, conjunctiva, or genitourinary tract after contact with contaminated freshwater. It causes local attachment with blood loss, leading to bleeding, anemia, pain, foreign-body sensation, cough, hemoptysis, or airway symptoms. Management centers on careful leech removal, control of bleeding, and supportive treatment such as anemia correction.

head: Bosentan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bosentan is an oral dual endothelin receptor antagonist, chemically a sulfonamide derivative, that blocks endothelin ETA and ETB receptors to reduce endothelin-1–mediated vasoconstriction and vascular proliferation. It is approved for pulmonary arterial hypertension to improve exercise capacity and delay clinical worsening. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity with elevated liver aminotransferases, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Localized scleroderma (morphea) is an autoimmune fibrosing disorder primarily affecting the skin and sometimes underlying subcutaneous tissue, fascia, muscle, or bone, without the internal organ involvement typical of systemic sclerosis. It causes localized indurated, sclerotic plaques or linear bands, often in children or middle-aged adults. Management aims to limit inflammation and fibrosis, using topical therapies, phototherapy, or systemic immunosuppression for deeper or progressive disease.

head: Omeprazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Omeprazole is a benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric parietal cell H+/K+-ATPase, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, peptic ulcer disease, erosive esophagitis, and Helicobacter pylori eradication regimens, and for hypersecretory states such as Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea.
tail_desc: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is a gastroenterologic disorder caused by gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumors (gastrinomas), usually in the pancreas or duodenum, leading to marked gastric acid hypersecretion. It typically presents in adults with severe, recurrent peptic ulcers, gastroesophageal reflux, abdominal pain, and diarrhea, and may occur sporadically or with MEN1. Management focuses on potent acid suppression and localization and treatment of the tumor, including surgery when feasible.

head: Salmon calcitonin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Salmon calcitonin is a synthetic or recombinant form of the salmon peptide hormone calcitonin, an antiresorptive agent that binds calcitonin receptors on osteoclasts to inhibit bone resorption and lower serum calcium. It has been used for postmenopausal osteoporosis, Paget disease of bone, and hypercalcemia. A notable adverse effect is increased malignancy risk with long-term use; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system caused primarily by estrogen deficiency after menopause, which accelerates bone resorption and reduces bone mass and microarchitectural strength. It mainly affects older postmenopausal women and is often silent until fragility fractures occur, especially in the vertebrae, hip, or wrist. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Trabectedin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Trabectedin is a marine-derived tetrahydroisoquinoline alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds the minor groove of DNA, alkylating guanine residues and disrupting transcription-coupled nucleotide excision repair, leading to DNA damage and apoptosis. It is approved for unresectable or metastatic soft tissue sarcoma and, with pegylated liposomal doxorubicin, recurrent platinum-sensitive ovarian cancer. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression, especially neutropenia.
tail_desc: Hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome is a hereditary cancer predisposition affecting the female reproductive system, characterized by familial clustering of ovarian cancer without the broader tumor spectrum seen in other syndromes. It likely reflects inherited susceptibility, often involving germline defects in DNA repair genes such as BRCA1 or BRCA2. Management centers on genetic counseling, surveillance, and consideration of risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy.

head: Acetylcysteine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acetylcysteine (N-acetylcysteine) is a cysteine derivative and mucolytic/antidote that replenishes glutathione and directly detoxifies the toxic acetaminophen metabolite N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI) via sulfhydryl donation; in airways, it disrupts disulfide bonds in mucus glycoproteins. It is used for acetaminophen overdose and as a mucolytic in respiratory secretions. Notable adverse effects include nausea/vomiting and anaphylactoid reactions with intravenous administration.
tail_desc: Bronchiectasis is a chronic structural lung disease of the respiratory system characterized by irreversible dilation and distortion of bronchi due to recurrent infection, inflammation, or impaired mucus clearance. It typically causes chronic productive cough, purulent sputum, recurrent chest infections, wheeze, dyspnea, and sometimes hemoptysis, in both children and adults. Management focuses on treating underlying causes, airway-clearance therapy, prompt antibiotics for exacerbations, and prevention of further infection.

head: Gemcitabine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Gemcitabine is a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog antimetabolite chemotherapy drug. Intracellular phosphorylation generates active metabolites that inhibit ribonucleotide reductase and are incorporated into DNA, blocking DNA synthesis and inducing apoptosis. It is used for pancreatic cancer and also non-small cell lung, bladder, breast, and ovarian cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Ovarian mucinous adenocarcinoma is a malignant epithelial ovarian tumor of the female reproductive system characterized by invasive mucin-producing glandular epithelium. It often presents as a large unilateral adnexal mass with abdominal distension, pelvic pain, or gastrointestinal symptoms, most often in adult women. Management is primarily surgical staging and cytoreduction, with adjuvant chemotherapy considered according to stage and pathologic risk.

head: Calcium glycerophosphate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium salt of glycerophosphoric acid used as a mineral supplement and urinary/food acid neutralizing agent. It dissociates to provide calcium and phosphate, without a specific primary molecular target. It is used in some formulations to reduce dietary acid effects and as a calcium source. Adverse effects may include hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in patients with hypercalcemia or severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Quizartinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloid leukemia with CEBPA somatic mutations (a disease)
head_desc: Quizartinib is an oral, selective type II receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor of FLT3, including FLT3-ITD mutant forms, used in acute myeloid leukemia with FLT3 internal tandem duplication. By blocking FLT3 signaling, it suppresses leukemic cell proliferation and survival. A notable toxicity is QT interval prolongation, requiring ECG and electrolyte monitoring and caution with other QT-prolonging drugs.
tail_desc: Acute myeloid leukemia with CEBPA somatic mutations is a genetically defined subtype of AML, a hematologic malignancy of the bone marrow and blood, driven by acquired mutations in the CEBPA transcription factor that impair myeloid differentiation. Patients present with typical AML features such as cytopenias, infections, bleeding, and fatigue. Management generally follows AML protocols with mutation-informed risk stratification and consideration of allogeneic transplantation in selected cases.

head: Warfarin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Warfarin is an oral coumarin-derived vitamin K antagonist anticoagulant that inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase complex 1 (VKORC1), reducing activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X and proteins C and S. It is used for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolism and prevention of thromboembolic complications in atrial fibrillation and mechanical heart valves. Major bleeding is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Intracranial embolism is a cerebrovascular disorder of the central nervous system in which an embolus, usually thrombotic material from the heart or proximal arteries, lodges in an intracranial artery and abruptly reduces cerebral blood flow, causing ischemic stroke. It typically presents with sudden focal neurologic deficits such as hemiparesis, aphasia, or visual loss, especially in patients with atrial fibrillation or other embolic sources. Management focuses on urgent stroke evaluation, reperfusion when eligible, and secondary prevention.

head: Anagliptin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Anagliptin is an oral dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor used as an antidiabetic agent. It lowers blood glucose by inhibiting DPP-4, thereby prolonging endogenous incretin activity, including GLP-1 and GIP, to enhance glucose-dependent insulin secretion and suppress glucagon release. It is approved for type 2 diabetes mellitus. A notable adverse effect is hypoglycemia, especially when combined with sulfonylureas or insulin.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Cysteine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cysteine is a sulfur-containing amino acid used pharmaceutically as a nutritional additive, especially in parenteral nutrition solutions. It primarily serves as a metabolic precursor for protein synthesis and glutathione production rather than acting through a discrete drug target or receptor. Clinically, it is indicated to meet amino acid requirements, particularly in neonates and infants. Adverse effects may include hypersensitivity reactions.
tail_desc: Idiopathic bronchiectasis is a chronic respiratory disorder of the lower airways characterized by permanent bronchial dilatation and recurrent neutrophilic airway inflammation when no identifiable cause is found despite evaluation. It typically presents in adults with chronic productive cough, recurrent chest infections, dyspnea, and sometimes hemoptysis. Management focuses on airway-clearance therapy, prompt treatment of exacerbations, vaccination, and long-term antibiotics in selected patients.

head: Choline magnesium trisalicylate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Choline magnesium trisalicylate is a nonacetylated salicylate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis to provide analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic effects. It is used for relief of mild to moderate pain, fever, and inflammatory disorders such as arthritis. Notable risks include gastrointestinal irritation or bleeding, and it is contraindicated in salicylate hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Osteoarthritis is a common degenerative joint disease of the musculoskeletal system characterized by progressive loss of articular cartilage, subchondral bone remodeling, osteophyte formation, and mild synovial inflammation. It typically affects older adults, causing activity-related joint pain, stiffness after inactivity, reduced range of motion, and functional limitation, often in knees, hips, hands, and spine. Management focuses on exercise, weight loss, analgesia, and sometimes joint replacement.

head: Iproclozide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Iproclozide is a hydrazine monoamine oxidase inhibitor (MAOI), specifically an irreversible, nonselective inhibitor of MAO-A and MAO-B that increases synaptic monoamine concentrations. It was used as an antidepressant for depressive disorders. A notable safety issue was severe hepatotoxicity, including cases of fatal liver injury, leading to its withdrawal and making liver disease a major contraindication.
tail_desc: Neurotic disorder is an older psychiatric term for nonpsychotic mental disorders affecting emotional and behavioral regulation, typically within the central nervous system’s functional domain. It generally refers to conditions such as anxiety, phobic, obsessive, or somatoform syndromes without loss of reality testing. Symptoms include distress, tension, avoidance, and impaired coping, and management usually involves psychotherapy, psychoeducation, and sometimes anxiolytic or antidepressant medication.

head: Belotecan (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: borderline epithelial tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Belotecan is a semisynthetic camptothecin derivative and antineoplastic topoisomerase I inhibitor. It stabilizes the topoisomerase I–DNA cleavable complex, preventing DNA religation and causing DNA strand breaks during replication. It has been used clinically, including for small cell lung cancer in some markets. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, particularly neutropenia.
tail_desc: Borderline epithelial tumor of the ovary is an epithelial ovarian neoplasm of low malignant potential, arising from the ovarian surface epithelial-stromal system and characterized histologically by epithelial proliferation and atypia without destructive stromal invasion. It often affects younger or perimenopausal women and may present with abdominal distension, pelvic pain, or an adnexal mass. Management is primarily surgical, with fertility-sparing approaches considered when appropriate.

head: Olaparib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Olaparib is an oral poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitor, primarily targeting PARP1 and PARP2 to block DNA repair and promote synthetic lethality in homologous recombination–deficient tumors, including those with BRCA1/2 mutations. It is approved for selected ovarian, breast, pancreatic, and prostate cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, particularly anemia.
tail_desc: Immature ovarian teratoma is a rare malignant ovarian germ cell tumor of the female reproductive system, composed of embryonic-type immature tissues, especially neuroectodermal elements. It typically affects children, adolescents, and young women, presenting with a rapidly enlarging pelvic or abdominal mass, pain, or distension. Management is primarily surgical staging and resection, with platinum-based chemotherapy used according to grade and stage.

head: Cariprazine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Cariprazine is an atypical antipsychotic of the piperazine derivative class that acts mainly as a partial agonist at dopamine D3/D2 receptors, with preference for D3, and also at serotonin 5-HT1A receptors. It is approved for schizophrenia and bipolar I disorder, including manic, mixed, and depressive episodes. Notable adverse effects include akathisia and extrapyramidal symptoms.
tail_desc: Schizophrenia is a chronic severe psychiatric disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by disturbances in thought, perception, emotion, and behavior. Its cause is multifactorial, involving genetic susceptibility and neurodevelopmental and neurotransmitter dysregulation. Typical features include delusions, hallucinations, disorganized thinking, negative symptoms, and cognitive impairment, usually beginning in late adolescence or early adulthood. Management combines antipsychotic medication, psychosocial interventions, and long-term follow-up.

head: Azatadine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Azatadine is a first-generation tricyclic antihistamine of the benzo[c]cycloheptathiophene family that acts primarily as a histamine H1-receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It has been used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis and urticaria. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation due to central H1 blockade, and it should be used cautiously in patients with narrow-angle glaucoma or urinary retention.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Levomilnacipran (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Levomilnacipran is a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI) antidepressant, the more active enantiomer of milnacipran, that blocks the serotonin transporter (SERT) and norepinephrine transporter (NET), with relatively greater norepinephrine reuptake inhibition. It is approved for major depressive disorder in adults. Notable adverse effects include increased heart rate and blood pressure, and it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Endogenous depression is an older psychiatric term for a severe mood disorder within major depressive illness, affecting the central nervous system and characterized by depressive episodes thought to arise primarily from internal biological vulnerability rather than obvious external stressors. Typical features include pervasive low mood, anhedonia, psychomotor change, sleep and appetite disturbance, guilt, and suicidality, often in adults; management follows major depression treatment with antidepressants, psychotherapy, and, when severe, electroconvulsive therapy.

head: Sodium oxybate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Sodium oxybate is the sodium salt of gamma-hydroxybutyrate (GHB), a central nervous system depressant that acts primarily as a GABA_B receptor agonist and also binds GHB-specific sites. It is approved for narcolepsy, particularly cataplexy and excessive daytime sleepiness. A notable risk is profound respiratory and CNS depression, making concomitant alcohol or sedative use contraindicated.
tail_desc: Narcolepsy-cataplexy syndrome is a central nervous system sleep-wake disorder, usually corresponding to narcolepsy type 1, caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin) signaling, often on an autoimmune basis in genetically susceptible people. It presents with excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy, often with hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis, and disturbed nocturnal sleep, typically beginning in childhood or adolescence; management uses wake-promoting agents and anticataplectic therapy.

head: Caffeine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Caffeine is a methylxanthine central nervous system stimulant that primarily acts as a nonselective antagonist of adenosine A1 and A2A receptors. It is established for treatment of apnea of prematurity (as caffeine citrate) and is also used as an analgesic adjuvant. Notable adverse effects include insomnia, tachycardia, and nervousness; it should be used cautiously in patients with significant arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Nasopharyngitis is an acute upper respiratory tract inflammation of the nasal passages and pharynx, usually caused by viral infection. It commonly presents with rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, sore throat, sneezing, and sometimes cough or low-grade fever, especially in children and adults with common colds. Management is typically supportive, including hydration, rest, and symptom relief; antibiotics are generally not indicated unless bacterial infection is suspected.

head: Primidone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: epilepsy with generalized tonic-clonic seizures (a disease)
head_desc: Primidone is a barbiturate-related anticonvulsant and essential tremor therapy, metabolized to phenobarbital and phenylethylmalonamide. Its antiseizure activity is attributed to parent drug and metabolites, with enhancement of GABAergic inhibitory transmission and reduced neuronal excitability. It is used for partial and generalized tonic-clonic seizures and essential tremor. Notable adverse effects include sedation, dizziness, and ataxia; it is contraindicated in porphyria.
tail_desc: Epilepsy with generalized tonic-clonic seizures is a generalized epilepsy of the central nervous system characterized by recurrent seizures with bilateral convulsive stiffening and jerking, usually due to widespread abnormal cortical neuronal discharges rather than a focal lesion. It often begins in childhood or adolescence, with postictal confusion common, and is typically managed with antiseizure medications, trigger avoidance, and ongoing neurologic follow-up.

head: Enalapril (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Enalapril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug converted to enalaprilat that inhibits ACE, reducing formation of angiotensin II and aldosterone. It is established for treating hypertension, heart failure, and asymptomatic left ventricular dysfunction. A notable adverse effect is persistent dry cough; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and in patients with a history of ACE inhibitor–associated angioedema.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Omarigliptin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Omarigliptin is a long-acting dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitor used as an oral antihyperglycemic agent. By selectively inhibiting DPP-4, it prolongs endogenous incretin activity, increasing glucose-dependent insulin secretion and reducing glucagon levels. It is approved in some countries for type 2 diabetes mellitus. A notable adverse effect is risk of hypoglycemia when combined with sulfonylureas or insulin.
tail_desc: Diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic/endocrine disease affecting glucose regulation, caused by insufficient insulin secretion, impaired insulin action, or both, leading to persistent hyperglycemia. It commonly presents with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, fatigue, and long-term vascular, renal, neurologic, and ocular complications, in children or adults depending on type. Management centers on glucose monitoring, diet and exercise, and medications including insulin or other antihyperglycemics.

head: Ebastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ebastine is a second-generation piperidine antihistamine that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors, reducing histamine-mediated symptoms with minimal sedation. It is established for allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is QT interval prolongation, so it should be used cautiously or avoided in patients with known cardiac repolarization abnormalities or interacting QT-prolonging drugs.
tail_desc: Idiopathic urticaria is a skin disorder of recurrent wheals and sometimes angioedema arising without an identifiable trigger. It is thought to reflect mast-cell activation with histamine release, although the precise cause is unknown. Patients, often adults, develop transient intensely pruritic hives. Management focuses on trigger avoidance when possible and symptom control, primarily with non-sedating H1 antihistamines.

head: Naftifine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Naftifine is a topical allylamine antifungal. It inhibits fungal squalene epoxidase, blocking ergosterol synthesis and causing toxic squalene accumulation in susceptible fungi. It is used for dermatophyte skin infections such as tinea pedis, tinea cruris, and tinea corporis. Common adverse effects include local application-site burning, stinging, dryness, or irritation; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Tinea infection is a superficial fungal infection of keratinized tissues in the integumentary system, caused by dermatophytes such as Trichophyton, Microsporum, or Epidermophyton. It typically presents with pruritic, scaly, annular plaques or localized involvement of the scalp, feet, groin, nails, or body, affecting both children and adults. Management usually involves topical antifungals, with oral therapy for scalp, nail, extensive, or refractory disease.

head: Methoxsalen (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: primary cutaneous T-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (a disease)
head_desc: Methoxsalen is a psoralen (furocoumarin) photosensitizing agent used in PUVA therapy. After intercalating into DNA, it is activated by UVA light to form covalent adducts and cross-links, inhibiting DNA synthesis and cellular proliferation. It is used for psoriasis, vitiligo, and cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. A notable risk is severe phototoxicity, so use is contraindicated in conditions associated with photosensitivity.
tail_desc: Primary cutaneous T-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma is a group of extranodal non-Hodgkin lymphomas arising from mature T lymphocytes in the skin, without initial extracutaneous disease. It typically affects adults and presents with persistent patches, plaques, tumors, or erythroderma, sometimes with pruritus. Management is stage-directed, often using skin-directed therapies for limited disease and systemic or biologic treatments for advanced or refractory cases.

head: Phenobarbital (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Phenobarbital is a long-acting barbiturate anticonvulsant and sedative-hypnotic that enhances inhibitory GABAergic neurotransmission primarily by positively modulating GABA\(_A\) receptors, increasing chloride channel opening duration; at higher concentrations it can directly activate the receptor. It is used for epilepsy and other seizure disorders, including status epilepticus, and for sedation. Notable adverse effects include marked CNS and respiratory depression.
tail_desc: obsolete vascular headache is a disease or medical condition.

head: Medrysone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Medrysone is a synthetic corticosteroid used primarily as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts by binding intracellular glucocorticoid receptors and altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediator production and leukocyte activity. It has been used for steroid-responsive inflammatory conditions of the eye, especially anterior segment inflammation. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure with risk of glaucoma, and it is contraindicated in untreated ocular viral or fungal infections.
tail_desc: Panuveitis is a form of uveitis involving inflammation of all major layers of the uveal tract and often adjacent ocular structures, affecting the eye. It may result from autoimmune or systemic inflammatory disease, infection, or be idiopathic. Patients present with eye pain, redness, photophobia, floaters, and blurred vision. Management targets the cause and suppresses inflammation, typically with corticosteroids and other immunomodulatory or anti-infective therapy.

head: Etodolac (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Etodolac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), a pyranocarboxylic acid derivative, that inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, particularly COX-mediated prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation, including in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and acute pain. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Meropenem (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Meropenem is a broad-spectrum carbapenem beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins, inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis and causing bactericidal activity. It is established for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including complicated intra-abdominal and skin infections, meningitis, and hospital-acquired pneumonia. A notable adverse effect is seizure risk, particularly in patients with CNS disorders or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Meningococcal infection is an acute invasive bacterial disease of the central nervous system and bloodstream caused by Neisseria meningitidis. It commonly presents as meningitis or septicemia with fever, headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, petechial or purpuric rash, and rapid shock in severe cases, especially in infants, adolescents, and young adults. Management requires urgent intravenous antibiotics, supportive care, and prophylaxis for close contacts.

head: Pirlindole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pirlindole is a reversible monoamine oxidase inhibitor antidepressant of the pyrazinoindole class, acting primarily through selective inhibition of MAO-A to increase central monoamine levels. It has been used for depressive disorders. As with other MAO inhibitors, a notable risk is hypertensive reactions from interactions with tyramine-rich foods or sympathomimetic drugs.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Adalimumab (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Adalimumab is a fully human monoclonal antibody that targets tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α), neutralizing this proinflammatory cytokine and reducing TNF-mediated inflammation. It is approved for several immune-mediated disorders, including rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, plaque psoriasis, Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, and hidradenitis suppurativa. A notable risk is serious infection, including reactivation of latent tuberculosis.
tail_desc: Inflammatory bowel disease is a chronic relapsing inflammatory disorder of the gastrointestinal tract, mainly comprising Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis. It is thought to result from dysregulated immune responses to intestinal microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals. Typical features include abdominal pain, diarrhea, rectal bleeding, weight loss, and fatigue, often beginning in adolescence or early adulthood; management aims to induce and maintain remission with anti-inflammatory, immunomodulatory, biologic, nutritional, and sometimes surgical therapy.

head: Carbinoxamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carbinoxamine is a first-generation ethanolamine-class antihistamine that acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis, conjunctival irritation, urticaria, and other hypersensitivity manifestations. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation with anticholinergic effects; it is contraindicated in children younger than 2 years because of risk of serious toxicity.
tail_desc: Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated inflammatory disorder of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, triggered by exposure to allergens such as pollen, dust mites, or animal dander. It commonly causes sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, and itching, often in atopic children and adults. Management typically includes allergen avoidance, intranasal corticosteroids, antihistamines, and sometimes allergen immunotherapy.

head: Guaifenesin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Guaifenesin is an expectorant of the guaiacol/glyceryl ether class used to help loosen and thin bronchial mucus in symptomatic relief of chest congestion and productive cough, typically in upper respiratory tract infections. Its precise molecular target is not well defined; it is believed to increase respiratory tract fluid and reduce mucus viscosity. Common adverse effects include nausea or gastrointestinal upset.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Estradiol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Estradiol is the principal endogenous estrogen, a steroid hormone used as an estrogen replacement therapy. It binds and activates nuclear estrogen receptors ERα and ERβ, regulating transcription in estrogen-responsive tissues. Clinically it is used for menopausal symptoms, vulvovaginal atrophy, hypoestrogenism, and prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis. A notable risk is venous thromboembolism; it is contraindicated in estrogen-dependent malignancy and active thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal atrophic vaginitis is a genitourinary syndrome of menopause affecting the vaginal mucosa, caused by estrogen deficiency after menopause that leads to epithelial thinning, reduced lubrication, and increased tissue fragility. It commonly presents in postmenopausal women with vaginal dryness, burning, itching, dyspareunia, and sometimes urinary symptoms. Management typically includes vaginal moisturizers/lubricants and, when appropriate, local estrogen therapy.

head: Nicardipine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Nicardipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that inhibits L-type calcium channels in vascular smooth muscle, producing arterial vasodilation with relatively little direct cardiac depression. It is used to treat hypertension, including acute severe hypertension with the intravenous formulation, and chronic stable angina. A notable adverse effect is reflex tachycardia; it is contraindicated in advanced aortic stenosis.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Magnesium trisilicate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Magnesium trisilicate is an inorganic magnesium silicate antacid. It acts locally in the stomach by chemically neutralizing gastric hydrochloric acid, raising intragastric pH; it has no specific molecular receptor target. It is used for symptomatic relief of hyperacidity, heartburn, and dyspepsia. Notable adverse effects include diarrhea, and it should be used cautiously in significant renal impairment because magnesium can accumulate.
tail_desc: Dyspepsia is a symptom complex of the upper gastrointestinal tract characterized by chronic or recurrent epigastric pain or burning, postprandial fullness, and early satiety. It may be functional, with no structural explanation, or secondary to disorders such as peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux, medications, or Helicobacter pylori infection. It occurs in adults and is managed by evaluating alarm features, addressing causes, testing/treating H. pylori, acid suppression, and lifestyle measures.

head: Tafamidis (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Tafamidis is an oral transthyretin stabilizer, a benzoxazole derivative, that selectively binds the thyroxine-binding sites of transthyretin (TTR) to prevent tetramer dissociation and subsequent amyloid formation. It is approved for transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy and, in some regions, early-stage transthyretin amyloid polyneuropathy. Common adverse effects include urinary tract infection and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to tafamidis or its excipients.
tail_desc: AH amyloidosis is a very rare form of systemic amyloidosis in which amyloid deposits are derived from immunoglobulin heavy chains produced by a monoclonal plasma cell or B-cell disorder. It most often affects the kidneys, heart, liver, and peripheral nerves, causing progressive organ dysfunction in adults. Management generally targets the underlying clonal disorder and provides supportive care for involved organs.

head: Altretamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Altretamine is an oral antineoplastic alkylating-like agent of the hexamethylmelamine family. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it is thought to undergo metabolic activation to reactive intermediates that damage DNA. It has been used, in combination regimens, for palliative treatment of persistent or recurrent ovarian cancer after failure of first-line therapy. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting peripheral neuropathy.
tail_desc: Hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome is a hereditary cancer predisposition affecting the female reproductive system, characterized by familial clustering of ovarian cancer without the broader tumor spectrum seen in other syndromes. It likely reflects inherited susceptibility, often involving germline defects in DNA repair genes such as BRCA1 or BRCA2. Management centers on genetic counseling, surveillance, and consideration of risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy.

head: Pentoxyverine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pentoxyverine (carbetapentane) is a centrally acting non-opioid antitussive, chemically a piperidine derivative. Its precise mechanism is not well defined, but it suppresses the cough reflex via central action in the medulla and is not linked to a single established molecular target. It is used for symptomatic relief of nonproductive cough; notable adverse effects include drowsiness and anticholinergic effects, and it is contraindicated in glaucoma.
tail_desc: Rhinitis is an inflammatory condition of the nasal mucosa in the upper respiratory tract, most commonly caused by allergic IgE-mediated reactions, viral infection, or irritant exposure. It typically presents with rhinorrhea, nasal congestion, sneezing, itching, and sometimes postnasal drip, affecting both children and adults. Management depends on cause and generally includes trigger avoidance, saline irrigation, antihistamines, intranasal corticosteroids, and treatment of underlying infection or irritation.

head: Trifluridine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Trifluridine is a fluorinated pyrimidine nucleoside analog antimetabolite. In oncology, it is incorporated into DNA after intracellular phosphorylation, disrupting DNA synthesis; in ophthalmic use, it inhibits viral DNA synthesis in herpes simplex virus. It is established for metastatic colorectal and gastric cancers in combination with tipiracil, and as ophthalmic treatment for HSV keratitis. Myelosuppression, especially neutropenia, is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Malignant colon neoplasm is a cancer of the large intestine, usually a colorectal adenocarcinoma arising from dysplastic glandular epithelium through accumulated genetic alterations, often from adenomatous polyps. It typically affects older adults and may cause occult or overt gastrointestinal bleeding, altered bowel habits, abdominal pain, iron-deficiency anemia, weight loss, or obstruction. Management centers on staging and surgical resection, with systemic chemotherapy for selected advanced or high-risk cases.

head: Mebicar (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Mebicar is an anxiolytic of the tetramethylbicyclooctanedione family, used mainly in some Eastern European/CIS countries for anxiety, irritability, and related neurotic symptoms. Its precise mechanism of action and primary molecular targets are not well established in the international literature, though it is described as modulating central neurotransmitter systems. Reported adverse effects are usually mild, including dyspepsia or allergic reactions; use is generally avoided in known hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Anxiety is a mental health condition involving excessive fear, worry, or apprehension, primarily affecting the central nervous system and stress-response pathways. It is multifactorial, with contributions from genetic vulnerability, neurobiological dysregulation, and psychosocial stressors. Common features include persistent nervousness, restlessness, impaired concentration, sleep disturbance, and autonomic symptoms; it can affect people of any age. Management typically includes psychotherapy, stress reduction, and when indicated, pharmacotherapy such as antidepressants.

head: Flumethasone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Flumethasone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid of the glucocorticoid class. It acts by binding the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory and immune responses. It has been used mainly in topical dermatologic and otic/ophthalmic preparations for inflammatory conditions. Like other corticosteroids, prolonged local use can cause skin atrophy, and it is generally contraindicated in untreated infections at the application site.
tail_desc: Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder affecting sebum-rich areas, linked to abnormal inflammatory responses to Malassezia yeast and skin barrier dysfunction. It causes greasy scales, erythema, and dandruff on the scalp, face, and trunk, commonly in infants and adults. Management typically uses topical antifungals, low-potency corticosteroids, and gentle skin care.

head: Naltrexone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Naltrexone is a synthetic opioid antagonist, structurally related to oxymorphone, that competitively blocks opioid receptors, primarily the mu-opioid receptor, with activity at kappa and delta receptors. It is used for maintenance treatment of opioid dependence after detoxification and for alcohol use disorder. A notable contraindication is current opioid use or acute opioid withdrawal, as it can precipitate severe withdrawal.
tail_desc: Obesity is a chronic metabolic and nutritional disorder affecting whole-body energy homeostasis, characterized by excessive accumulation of adipose tissue due to sustained positive energy balance interacting with genetic, environmental, and behavioral factors. It typically presents in children or adults with elevated body mass index or central adiposity and related complications. Management centers on lifestyle intervention, treatment of comorbidities, anti-obesity pharmacotherapy, and bariatric surgery when indicated.

head: Caroxazone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Caroxazone is an oxazolidinone-class antidepressant that acts as a monoamine oxidase inhibitor, increasing central monoamine neurotransmitters by inhibiting MAO activity. It has been used clinically for depressive disorders. A notable safety concern is the potential for hypertensive reactions or serotonin toxicity when combined with tyramine-rich foods or serotonergic medications.
tail_desc: Dysthymic disorder, now termed persistent depressive disorder, is a chronic mood disorder affecting the central nervous system through dysregulation of affective and neurobiological pathways. It is characterized by persistently depressed mood for at least two years in adults, often with low energy, poor self-esteem, sleep or appetite disturbance, and hopelessness. It commonly begins in adolescence or adulthood and is typically managed with psychotherapy, antidepressant medication, or both.

head: Rabeprazole (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Rabeprazole is a substituted benzimidazole proton pump inhibitor that irreversibly inhibits the gastric H+/K+-ATPase in parietal cells, suppressing gastric acid secretion. It is used for gastroesophageal reflux disease, healing and maintenance of erosive esophagitis, duodenal ulcer, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori eradication. Notable adverse effects include headache and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to rabeprazole or other proton pump inhibitors.
tail_desc: Mastocytosis is a rare clonal mast cell disorder affecting the skin and/or systemic organs, caused in many cases by activating KIT mutations that lead to abnormal mast cell accumulation and mediator release. It may present in children as mainly cutaneous disease, while adults more often have systemic involvement with flushing, pruritus, urticaria pigmentosa, anaphylaxis, gastrointestinal symptoms, and bone disease; management focuses on trigger avoidance, anti-mediator therapy, and treatment of advanced systemic disease.

head: Retapamulin (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Retapamulin is a topical pleuromutilin antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by selectively binding the 50S ribosomal subunit at peptidyl transferase center elements. It is approved for topical treatment of impetigo caused by susceptible Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes. A notable adverse effect is local application-site irritation, and it is not intended for oral, intranasal, or ophthalmic use.
tail_desc: Bullous impetigo is a superficial bacterial infection of the skin, usually caused by toxin-producing Staphylococcus aureus that induces intraepidermal blister formation through epidermal splitting. It mainly affects infants and young children and presents with fragile bullae that rupture easily, leaving shallow erosions and a collarette of scale. Management typically involves hygiene measures and anti-staphylococcal topical or systemic antibiotics depending on extent.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Mycosis fungoides and its variants are the most common primary cutaneous T-cell lymphomas, involving the skin. They arise from malignant skin-homing mature T lymphocytes, typically CD4-positive. Patients, usually older adults, develop chronic patches, plaques, tumors, or erythroderma that may itch and can progress over years. Management is stage-directed, emphasizing skin-directed therapies early and systemic treatment for advanced or refractory disease.

head: Ceftriaxone (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ceftriaxone is a third-generation cephalosporin beta-lactam antibiotic that inhibits bacterial cell-wall synthesis by binding penicillin-binding proteins, blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking. It is used for susceptible infections including pneumonia, meningitis, gonorrhea, sepsis, urinary tract and intra-abdominal infections. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions in beta-lactam–allergic patients and biliary sludging/pseudolithiasis.
tail_desc: Streptococcal infection is a bacterial infectious disease caused by Streptococcus species, commonly affecting the throat, skin, soft tissues, or invasive systemic sites. Presentations vary from pharyngitis and impetigo to cellulitis, scarlet fever, or sepsis, occurring in both children and adults. Management depends on site and severity, typically involving appropriate antibiotics, supportive care, and drainage or urgent treatment for invasive disease.

head: Amphetamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Amphetamine is a sympathomimetic central nervous system stimulant of the phenethylamine/amphetamine class that increases synaptic norepinephrine and dopamine primarily by promoting their presynaptic release via monoamine transporters. It is approved for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and narcolepsy. Notable risks include hypertension and tachycardia, and it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Central nervous system disease is a broad category of disorders affecting the brain and spinal cord, arising from diverse mechanisms including congenital, vascular, infectious, inflammatory, degenerative, neoplastic, traumatic, or toxic injury. Clinical features vary but may include headache, seizures, weakness, sensory loss, cognitive or behavioral change, gait disturbance, or altered consciousness, in children or adults. Management depends on cause and typically combines etiologic treatment, symptom control, and rehabilitation.

head: Diazepam (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Diazepam is a long-acting benzodiazepine that positively modulates GABA-A receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission in the central nervous system. It is used for anxiety disorders, acute alcohol withdrawal, muscle spasm, seizure control including status epilepticus, and procedural sedation. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation with respiratory depression, especially when combined with opioids or other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome is a neuropsychiatric disorder of the central nervous system caused by thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, classically associated with chronic alcohol use or malnutrition. It combines acute Wernicke encephalopathy—confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia—with Korsakoff amnestic syndrome, marked by severe memory impairment and confabulation. Management centers on urgent parenteral thiamine replacement, nutritional support, and treatment of the underlying cause.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Chronic cutaneous lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune connective tissue disease primarily affecting the skin, characterized by chronic interface dermatitis with photosensitive, scarring plaques, often on sun-exposed areas such as the face and scalp. It commonly affects adults, especially women. Management focuses on photoprotection, topical or intralesional corticosteroids, antimalarials such as hydroxychloroquine, and smoking cessation.

head: Carteolol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carteolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity. By blocking beta-1 and beta-2 receptors, it lowers intraocular pressure and can reduce heart rate and blood pressure. It is used topically for open-angle glaucoma or ocular hypertension and orally for hypertension. Notable risks include bronchospasm; it is contraindicated in bronchial asthma and some bradyarrhythmias.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Ergocalciferol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ergocalciferol (vitamin D2) is a secosteroid vitamin D analog. After hepatic and renal activation to calcitriol, it binds the vitamin D receptor, increasing intestinal calcium and phosphate absorption and supporting bone mineralization. It is used to prevent and treat vitamin D deficiency, including rickets or osteomalacia. A notable adverse effect is hypercalcemia; it is contraindicated in hypervitaminosis D or hypercalcemia.
tail_desc: Postmenopausal osteoporosis is a metabolic bone disease of the skeletal system caused primarily by estrogen deficiency after menopause, which accelerates bone resorption and reduces bone mass and microarchitectural strength.
