It mainly affects older postmenopausal women and is often silent until fragility fractures occur, especially in the vertebrae, hip, or wrist. Management focuses on fracture prevention with exercise, calcium/vitamin D, fall reduction, and antiresorptive or anabolic therapy.

head: Pimecrolimus (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Pimecrolimus is a topical calcineurin inhibitor of the ascomycin macrolactam class. It binds FKBP-12, inhibiting calcineurin phosphatase and thereby reducing T-cell activation and inflammatory cytokine production. It is approved for mild to moderate atopic dermatitis (eczema), particularly in non-immunocompromised patients aged 2 years and older. A notable adverse effect is local burning or irritation at the application site.
tail_desc: Atopic eczema is a chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin disorder, usually beginning in infancy or childhood, driven by epidermal barrier dysfunction and immune dysregulation associated with atopy. It causes intense itching, dry skin, and eczematous lesions with age-dependent distribution. Management centers on trigger avoidance, regular emollients, topical corticosteroids or calcineurin inhibitors, and escalation to systemic or biologic therapy in severe disease.

head: Hydroxyurea (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Hydroxyurea is an antimetabolite hydroxylated urea derivative that inhibits ribonucleotide reductase, reducing deoxyribonucleotide synthesis and thereby suppressing DNA replication; it also increases fetal hemoglobin in sickle cell disease. It is used for myeloproliferative disorders and some leukemias, and to reduce painful crises in sickle cell disease. A notable adverse effect is dose-related myelosuppression, so it is contraindicated in severe bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinoma is an epithelial ovarian cancer of the female reproductive system, histologically resembling endometrial adenocarcinoma and often associated with endometriosis. It typically affects peri- or postmenopausal women and may present with pelvic pain, abdominal distension, or an adnexal mass. Management usually involves surgical staging and cytoreductive resection, with platinum-based chemotherapy according to stage and residual disease.

head: Acrivastine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acrivastine is a second-generation antihistamine of the alkylamine/propylamine class that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used for symptomatic relief of seasonal allergic rhinitis and urticaria, including sneezing, rhinorrhea, and itching. Compared with older antihistamines it is less sedating, but drowsiness can still occur; caution is advised in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Pharyngitis is an inflammatory disorder of the pharynx in the upper respiratory tract, most often caused by viral infection and less commonly by Group A Streptococcus or other irritants. It typically presents in children and adults with sore throat, pain on swallowing, fever, and pharyngeal erythema, sometimes with cervical lymphadenopathy. Management is usually supportive, with antibiotics reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected bacterial cases.

head: Temozolomide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Temozolomide is an oral alkylating antineoplastic, a triazene derivative, that spontaneously converts to MTIC and methylates DNA, primarily at O6- and N7-guanine residues, causing cytotoxic damage. It is approved for glioblastoma multiforme and refractory anaplastic astrocytoma. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially thrombocytopenia and neutropenia.
tail_desc: Anaplastic astrocytoma is a high-grade, WHO grade III diffuse astrocytic tumor of the central nervous system, arising from infiltrating neoplastic astrocytes in the brain. It typically affects adults and presents with seizures, headaches, focal neurologic deficits, or cognitive change. Management usually involves maximal safe surgical resection followed by radiotherapy, often with chemotherapy such as temozolomide.

head: Carbinoxamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Carbinoxamine is a first-generation ethanolamine-class antihistamine that acts primarily as an H1 histamine receptor antagonist, reducing histamine-mediated allergic symptoms. It is used for symptomatic relief of allergic rhinitis, conjunctival irritation, urticaria, and other hypersensitivity manifestations. A notable adverse effect is marked sedation with anticholinergic effects; it is contraindicated in children younger than 2 years because of risk of serious toxicity.
tail_desc: Papillary conjunctivitis is an inflammatory disorder of the ocular surface, specifically the palpebral conjunctiva, characterized by papillary hypertrophy from chronic mechanical irritation, allergy, or contact lens/prosthesis-related immune stimulation. Patients commonly have itching, redness, mucus discharge, foreign-body sensation, and visible tarsal papillae. Management focuses on removing triggers, improving lens hygiene or fit, and using topical anti-inflammatory/antiallergic therapy as appropriate.

head: Ibrutinib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ibrutinib is an oral small-molecule Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor, a covalent kinase inhibitor used in B-cell malignancies. By irreversibly binding BTK, it blocks B-cell receptor signaling and impairs malignant B-cell survival and proliferation. It is approved for chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma, mantle cell lymphoma, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, and chronic graft-versus-host disease. A notable adverse effect is increased bleeding risk.
tail_desc: Lymphosarcoma is an older term historically used for malignant lymphoma, a cancer of lymphoid tissue affecting the lymphatic and hematopoietic systems. It arises from neoplastic proliferation of lymphocytes in lymph nodes or extranodal sites. Typical features include painless lymphadenopathy, constitutional “B” symptoms, and organ-specific effects, occurring mainly in adults. Management generally follows lymphoma subtype and stage, using chemotherapy, immunotherapy, radiotherapy, and sometimes stem cell transplantation.

head: Acetylsalicylic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin) is a salicylate nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug that irreversibly acetylates cyclooxygenase-1 and -2, inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis and thromboxane A2–mediated platelet aggregation. It is used for pain, fever, inflammatory conditions, and secondary prevention of myocardial infarction and ischemic stroke. A notable risk is gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding; it is generally contraindicated in active bleeding disorders.
tail_desc: Arthropathy is a general term for joint disease affecting the musculoskeletal system, encompassing inflammatory, degenerative, metabolic, infectious, traumatic, and neuropathic disorders. It involves damage or dysfunction of cartilage, synovium, bone, or periarticular tissues, causing joint pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced range of motion, and sometimes deformity. It can occur at any age depending on cause, and management targets the underlying etiology with symptom control, rehabilitation, and sometimes surgery.

head: Zinc oxide (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Zinc oxide is an inorganic zinc compound used topically as a skin protectant and mild astringent. Its effects are primarily physical rather than receptor-mediated, forming a protective barrier on skin and reflecting or scattering ultraviolet radiation in sunscreen formulations, without a defined primary molecular target. Established uses include diaper dermatitis, minor skin irritation, and sun protection; adverse effects are usually limited to local irritation or hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Tinea corporis is a superficial dermatophyte infection of the keratinized skin of the body, excluding scalp, groin, hands, and feet. It is caused by fungi such as Trichophyton, Microsporum, or Epidermophyton species and typically presents in children or adults as pruritic, annular, scaly plaques with central clearing. Management usually involves topical antifungal therapy, with oral agents for extensive, refractory, or immunocompromised cases.

head: Decitabine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: acute myeloblastic leukemia without maturation (a disease)
head_desc: Decitabine is a hypomethylating antineoplastic agent, a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog. After intracellular activation, it incorporates into DNA and inhibits DNA methyltransferases, causing DNA hypomethylation and altered gene expression. It is approved for myelodysplastic syndromes and used in acute myeloid leukemia. A notable adverse effect is severe myelosuppression, including neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Acute myeloblastic leukemia without maturation is a subtype of acute myeloid leukemia affecting the bone marrow and blood, characterized by predominance of myeloblasts with minimal maturation due to clonal malignant transformation of myeloid precursors. It typically presents in adults with anemia, infections, bleeding, and marrow failure. Management usually involves intensive induction chemotherapy, risk-adapted consolidation, and consideration of hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Plasmodium vivax malaria is a parasitic infectious disease of the blood and reticuloendothelial system caused by Plasmodium vivax, transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes. It invades hepatocytes and reticulocytes, with dormant liver hypnozoites causing relapses. Typical features include recurrent fever, chills, headache, malaise, anemia, and splenomegaly, especially in people in endemic regions or travelers. Management uses antimalarials for blood-stage infection plus radical cure of liver hypnozoites when appropriate.

head: Salicylic acid (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Salicylic acid is a keratolytic and topical salicylate, chemically a beta-hydroxy acid. It acts mainly by solubilizing intercellular cement in the stratum corneum, promoting desquamation; unlike acetylsalicylic acid, it is not used primarily for systemic COX inhibition. It is established for acne, psoriasis, seborrheic dermatitis, warts, and calluses/corns. Notable risks include local irritation and salicylate toxicity with excessive topical use.
tail_desc: Osteoarthritis susceptibility is a genetic or multifactorial predisposition to developing osteoarthritis, a degenerative musculoskeletal disorder affecting synovial joints. It reflects increased likelihood of cartilage breakdown with subchondral bone and synovial changes, especially in older adults or those with mechanical risk factors. Clinical manifestations, if disease develops, include joint pain, stiffness, and functional limitation; management focuses on risk reduction, symptom control, exercise, weight management, and joint-specific supportive care.

head: Ferrous sulfate anhydrous (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ferrous sulfate anhydrous is an oral iron salt and hematinic used to prevent and treat iron deficiency and iron-deficiency anemia. It supplies elemental ferrous iron (Fe2+), which is absorbed mainly in the duodenum and incorporated into hemoglobin and other iron-containing proteins; it does not act via a specific receptor target. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation, especially nausea or constipation.
tail_desc: Hypochromic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by red blood cells with reduced hemoglobin content, typically due to impaired hemoglobin synthesis, most often from iron deficiency, but also seen in thalassemias and some chronic diseases. Patients commonly present with pallor, fatigue, weakness, and dyspnea. Management targets the underlying cause, such as iron replacement, dietary correction, or treatment of associated disease.

head: Bisoprolol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Bisoprolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class that primarily antagonizes beta-1 receptors in the heart, reducing heart rate, contractility, and renin release. It is used for hypertension, angina, and chronic heart failure. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia, and it is contraindicated in severe bradycardia or advanced atrioventricular block.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Ferric maltol (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
head_desc: Ferric maltol is an oral iron replacement product consisting of ferric iron complexed with maltol, used to treat iron deficiency with or without anemia in adults. It delivers iron for absorption in the gastrointestinal tract, replenishing body iron stores rather than acting at a specific receptor target. Common adverse effects include abdominal pain, flatulence, constipation, and diarrhea; it is contraindicated in iron overload disorders.
tail_desc: Microcytic anemia is a hematologic disorder characterized by abnormally small red blood cells, usually due to impaired hemoglobin synthesis from iron deficiency, thalassemia, anemia of chronic disease, or sideroblastic processes. Patients may present with fatigue, pallor, weakness, and sometimes pica, and it occurs in both children and adults. Management focuses on identifying and treating the underlying cause, with iron replacement or other cause-specific therapy as appropriate.

head: Rucaparib (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: malignant germ cell tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Rucaparib is an oral small-molecule poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibitor, primarily targeting PARP1, PARP2, and PARP3 to impair DNA repair and promote tumor cell death, especially in homologous recombination-deficient cancers. It is approved for certain BRCA-mutated ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, including anemia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Malignant germ cell tumor of the ovary is a rare ovarian gonadal neoplasm arising from primitive germ cells, typically affecting children, adolescents, and young women. Histologic types include dysgerminoma, yolk sac tumor, and immature teratoma. Patients often present with a rapidly enlarging pelvic mass, abdominal pain, or elevated tumor markers. Management usually combines fertility-sparing surgical staging with platinum-based chemotherapy.

head: Altretamine (a drug)
relation: indication: drug is clinically indicated for the treatment of disease.
tail: borderline epithelial tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Altretamine is an oral antineoplastic alkylating-like agent of the hexamethylmelamine family. Its precise mechanism is not fully defined, but it is thought to undergo metabolic activation to reactive intermediates that damage DNA. It has been used, in combination regimens, for palliative treatment of persistent or recurrent ovarian cancer after failure of first-line therapy. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting peripheral neuropathy.
tail_desc: Borderline epithelial tumor of the ovary is an epithelial ovarian neoplasm of low malignant potential, arising from the ovarian surface epithelial-stromal system and characterized histologically by epithelial proliferation and atypia without destructive stromal invasion. It often affects younger or perimenopausal women and may present with abdominal distension, pelvic pain, or an adnexal mass. Management is primarily surgical, with fertility-sparing approaches considered when appropriate.

head: Anastrozole (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Anastrozole is a nonsteroidal triazole aromatase inhibitor that selectively blocks CYP19A1, reducing peripheral conversion of androgens to estrogens. It is used primarily for hormone receptor–positive breast cancer in postmenopausal women, including adjuvant and advanced disease settings. A notable adverse effect is accelerated bone loss with increased fracture risk; it is not effective in premenopausal women without ovarian suppression.
tail_desc: Familial ovarian cancer is a hereditary cancer predisposition affecting the female reproductive system, most often linked to inherited pathogenic variants in BRCA1, BRCA2, or other homologous-recombination DNA repair genes. It typically presents as epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer, often at younger ages and with family histories of ovarian or breast cancer. Management centers on genetic counseling/testing, risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy, surveillance, and standard oncologic treatment when cancer develops.

head: Ethinylestradiol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ethinylestradiol is a synthetic steroidal estrogen, commonly used in combined oral contraceptives and in some hormone replacement regimens. It acts as an agonist at estrogen receptors (ERα and ERβ), altering transcription of estrogen-responsive genes and suppressing gonadotropin secretion to inhibit ovulation. Notable risks include venous thromboembolism, and it is contraindicated in patients with active thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: Polycystic ovary syndrome is a common endocrine-metabolic disorder of the female reproductive system, affecting reproductive-age women. It is characterized by ovarian dysfunction with hyperandrogenism and often insulin resistance, causing irregular or absent ovulation, hirsutism, acne, infertility, and polycystic ovarian morphology. Management typically targets symptoms and metabolic risk with lifestyle measures, hormonal therapy, and treatment for insulin resistance or infertility.

head: Sodium citrate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Sodium citrate is an alkalinizing and calcium-chelating citrate salt used as a urinary/systemic alkalinizer and as an anticoagulant in blood collection and extracorporeal circuits. It is metabolized to bicarbonate, raising pH, and it prevents coagulation by binding ionized calcium, thereby inhibiting calcium-dependent clotting steps. Established uses include treatment of metabolic acidosis and anticoagulation; notable risks include metabolic alkalosis and hypernatremia.
tail_desc: Galactosemia is an inherited metabolic disorder affecting carbohydrate metabolism, usually caused by deficiency of enzymes needed to process galactose, classically galactose-1-phosphate uridylyltransferase. It typically presents in newborns after milk feeding with poor feeding, vomiting, jaundice, hepatomegaly, failure to thrive, cataracts, and risk of sepsis. Management is prompt lifelong dietary restriction of galactose/lactose and monitoring for complications.

head: Levofloxacin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Levofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, the active L-isomer of ofloxacin, that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including respiratory tract, urinary tract, skin and soft-tissue infections, and some forms of plague or anthrax exposure. A notable risk is tendinitis and tendon rupture.
tail_desc: Dysentery is an acute infectious inflammatory disorder of the intestines, especially the colon, most often caused by Shigella bacteria or Entamoeba histolytica. It is characterized by frequent small-volume diarrhea containing blood and mucus, abdominal cramps, tenesmus, and fever, affecting both children and adults, particularly in poor-sanitation settings. Management centers on rehydration, electrolyte replacement, and targeted antimicrobial therapy when indicated.

head: Acitretin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Acitretin is an oral second-generation retinoid, a synthetic aromatic vitamin A derivative. It acts mainly by binding nuclear retinoic acid receptors (RARs), altering gene transcription to normalize keratinocyte differentiation and reduce epidermal hyperproliferation. It is established for severe psoriasis, especially pustular or erythrodermic forms. A major risk is marked teratogenicity, making pregnancy a strict contraindication.
tail_desc: Primary cutaneous T-cell lymphoma is a non-Hodgkin lymphoma of skin-homing mature T lymphocytes, arising primarily in the skin without initial extracutaneous disease. It typically affects adults and presents with persistent patches, plaques, tumors, or erythroderma, often with pruritus. Management depends on stage and subtype, commonly using skin-directed therapies for limited disease and systemic or biologic treatment for advanced disease.

head: Levonorgestrel (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestin (19-nortestosterone derivative) that acts as an agonist at progesterone receptors, suppressing the luteinizing hormone surge, inhibiting or delaying ovulation, and altering cervical mucus and the endometrium. It is used for contraception, including emergency contraception and intrauterine systems, and for some heavy menstrual bleeding indications. Irregular uterine bleeding is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecologic disorder of the female reproductive system in which endometrial-like tissue grows outside the uterus, causing estrogen-driven inflammation, scarring, and adhesions. It mainly affects reproductive-age women and commonly presents with dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility. Management is directed toward pain control, hormonal suppression, and, when needed, surgical removal of lesions.

head: Docetaxel (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Docetaxel is a semisynthetic taxane antineoplastic agent that binds the β-subunit of tubulin, promoting microtubule assembly and inhibiting depolymerization, thereby blocking mitosis. It is used for breast cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, prostate cancer, gastric adenocarcinoma, and head and neck cancer. A notable toxicity is severe neutropenia; use is contraindicated in patients with baseline neutrophil counts below 1,500 cells/mm³.
tail_desc: Hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome is a hereditary cancer predisposition affecting the female reproductive system, characterized by familial clustering of ovarian cancer without the broader tumor spectrum seen in other syndromes. It likely reflects inherited susceptibility, often involving germline defects in DNA repair genes such as BRCA1 or BRCA2. Management centers on genetic counseling, surveillance, and consideration of risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy.

head: Levocetirizine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Levocetirizine is a second-generation antihistamine, the active R-enantiomer of cetirizine, that selectively antagonizes peripheral histamine H1 receptors. It is used for symptomatic treatment of allergic rhinitis and chronic idiopathic urticaria. A notable adverse effect is somnolence, though less than with older antihistamines; it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment, especially in dialysis-dependent patients.
tail_desc: Angioedema is a vascular permeability disorder of the skin and mucosa causing sudden, localized swelling, often involving the face, lips, tongue, extremities, or airway. It results from mast cell mediator release or bradykinin excess, and may be allergic, medication-induced, or hereditary. Patients present with episodic nonpitting edema, sometimes with urticaria or life-threatening airway compromise; management targets airway protection and treating the underlying mechanism.

head: Gemcitabine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Gemcitabine is a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog antimetabolite chemotherapy drug. Intracellular phosphorylation generates active metabolites that inhibit ribonucleotide reductase and are incorporated into DNA, blocking DNA synthesis and inducing apoptosis. It is used for pancreatic cancer and also non-small cell lung, bladder, breast, and ovarian cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Yolk sac tumor is a malignant germ cell tumor, usually arising in the gonads, especially the testes or ovaries, and less often at extragonadal midline sites. It most commonly affects infants, children, and young adults and often produces alpha-fetoprotein. Patients present with a rapidly enlarging mass or site-specific symptoms; treatment typically combines surgical resection with platinum-based chemotherapy.

head: Paclitaxel (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Paclitaxel is a taxane antineoplastic agent that binds the β-subunit of tubulin in microtubules, promoting their stabilization and preventing depolymerization, thereby arresting mitosis. It is used for ovarian, breast, and non-small cell lung cancers, and AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma. A notable toxicity is peripheral neuropathy; severe hypersensitivity reactions can also occur, especially with solvent-based formulations.
tail_desc: Testicular cancer is a malignant neoplasm of the male gonad, usually arising from germ cells in the testis. It most often affects adolescents and young adult men and typically presents as a painless testicular mass or swelling, sometimes with discomfort. Management generally involves radical inguinal orchiectomy, with stage- and histology-based surveillance, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or additional surgery.

head: Melphalan (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the central nervous system (a disease)
head_desc: Melphalan is a bifunctional nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the phenylalanine derivative class that covalently crosslinks DNA, mainly at guanine bases, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and causing cell death. It is used chiefly in multiple myeloma and ovarian carcinoma conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity or marked bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the central nervous system is an aggressive primary CNS non-Hodgkin lymphoma arising from malignant large B cells within the brain, spinal cord, leptomeninges, or eyes. It often affects older or immunocompromised adults and presents with focal neurologic deficits, neuropsychiatric change, seizures, or visual symptoms. Management typically centers on high-dose methotrexate-based chemotherapy, with selected radiotherapy or rituximab-containing regimens.

head: Meclocycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Meclocycline is a tetracycline-class antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by reversibly binding the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment. It has been used mainly as a topical anti-infective for superficial bacterial skin infections. A notable adverse effect is photosensitivity; like other tetracyclines, it is generally contraindicated during pregnancy and in young children because of tooth discoloration and effects on bone growth.
tail_desc: Sclerosing cholangitis is a chronic cholestatic disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by inflammation, fibrosis, and multifocal stricturing of intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic bile ducts, leading to impaired bile flow and progressive liver injury. Patients may have fatigue, pruritus, jaundice, or recurrent cholangitis; many with primary disease have inflammatory bowel disease. Management focuses on symptom control, treating complications, endoscopic relief of dominant strictures, and liver transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Triamcinolone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Triamcinolone is a synthetic fluorinated glucocorticoid corticosteroid that binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to regulate gene transcription, producing anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. It is used in topical, intra-articular, intralesional, inhaled, and nasal formulations for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses, allergic rhinitis, asthma, and inflammatory joint or soft-tissue disorders. Notable adverse effects include adrenal suppression, and it is contraindicated in untreated systemic fungal infection.
tail_desc: Hepatitis is inflammation of the liver, a hepatobiliary disorder most commonly caused by viral infection, alcohol use, medications, toxins, or autoimmune injury, leading to hepatocellular damage. It may be acute or chronic, with fatigue, nausea, right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, and elevated liver enzymes; some patients are asymptomatic. Management targets the cause, provides supportive care, and monitors for complications such as liver failure or cirrhosis.

head: Fondaparinux (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Fondaparinux is a synthetic pentasaccharide anticoagulant that selectively binds antithrombin and indirectly inhibits factor Xa, reducing thrombin generation. It is approved for prophylaxis and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including use in acute coronary syndromes in some settings. A notable risk is major bleeding, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment and in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Ganciclovir (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ganciclovir is a guanine nucleoside analogue antiviral active against herpesviruses. After phosphorylation, including by CMV UL97 kinase, its triphosphate inhibits viral DNA polymerase and terminates or slows viral DNA elongation. It is used for treatment and prevention of cytomegalovirus infection, including CMV retinitis in immunocompromised patients. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially neutropenia.
tail_desc: His bundle tachycardia is a rare supraventricular arrhythmia of the cardiac conduction system arising from the His bundle, usually due to enhanced automaticity or triggered activity in this tissue. It is reported mainly in infants and children and may cause persistent narrow-complex tachycardia, palpitations, heart failure, or tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. Management typically centers on antiarrhythmic therapy, with catheter ablation considered in refractory cases.

head: Gemcitabine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Gemcitabine is a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog antimetabolite chemotherapy drug. Intracellular phosphorylation generates active metabolites that inhibit ribonucleotide reductase and are incorporated into DNA, blocking DNA synthesis and inducing apoptosis. It is used for pancreatic cancer and also non-small cell lung, bladder, breast, and ovarian cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Testicular teratoma is a germ cell tumor of the testis, usually composed of tissues derived from multiple embryonic germ layers. It most often affects children and young men, presenting as a painless testicular mass or enlargement. In postpubertal males it is considered malignant regardless of differentiation. Management typically involves radical inguinal orchiectomy, with staging and additional therapy guided by metastatic risk and associated germ cell tumor components.

head: Melphalan (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: primary central nervous system lymphoma (a disease)
head_desc: Melphalan is a bifunctional nitrogen mustard alkylating agent of the phenylalanine derivative class that covalently crosslinks DNA, mainly at guanine bases, disrupting DNA replication and transcription and causing cell death. It is used chiefly in multiple myeloma and ovarian carcinoma conditioning regimens. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; it is contraindicated in patients with prior hypersensitivity or marked bone marrow suppression.
tail_desc: Primary central nervous system lymphoma is an aggressive extranodal non-Hodgkin lymphoma, usually diffuse large B-cell type, confined to the brain, leptomeninges, spinal cord, or eyes. It typically presents with focal neurologic deficits, neuropsychiatric change, seizures, or visual symptoms, especially in older adults and immunocompromised patients. Management generally centers on high-dose methotrexate–based chemotherapy, with radiotherapy used selectively.

head: Imipramine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Imipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant of the dibenzazepine class that primarily inhibits neuronal reuptake of norepinephrine and serotonin via the NET and SERT transporters. It is used for major depressive disorder and nocturnal enuresis in children. A notable adverse effect is anticholinergic toxicity, and it is contraindicated with concomitant monoamine oxidase inhibitor use.
tail_desc: Narcolepsy-cataplexy syndrome is a central nervous system sleep-wake disorder, usually corresponding to narcolepsy type 1, caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin) signaling, often on an autoimmune basis in genetically susceptible people. It presents with excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy, often with hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis, and disturbed nocturnal sleep, typically beginning in childhood or adolescence; management uses wake-promoting agents and anticataplectic therapy.

head: Metoprolol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Metoprolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class that primarily antagonizes β1-adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, contractility, and renin release. It is used for hypertension, angina, heart failure, and after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in severe bradycardia, cardiogenic shock, and second- or third-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Thyrotoxicosis is an endocrine disorder of the thyroid system caused by excess circulating thyroid hormones, most commonly from hyperthyroidism such as Graves disease, toxic nodular goiter, or thyroiditis. It typically presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, and tachycardia, especially in adults. Management targets the cause and symptom control, using beta-blockers and measures such as antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery.

head: Demeclocycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Demeclocycline is a tetracycline-class antibiotic that binds the 30S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections and, notably, to treat chronic hyponatremia due to syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) by reducing renal responsiveness to ADH. A notable adverse effect is photosensitivity; it is also contraindicated in pregnancy and young children because of tooth discoloration and effects on bone growth.
tail_desc: Suppurative cholangitis is an acute bacterial infection of the biliary tract, usually arising from biliary obstruction that causes infected bile under pressure within the bile ducts. It affects the hepatobiliary system and typically presents with fever, right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, and possible sepsis, often in adults with gallstones or strictures. Management requires urgent antibiotics and biliary decompression, usually by endoscopic drainage.

head: Fusidic acid (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Fusidic acid is a steroidal antibiotic of the fusidane class that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding elongation factor G (EF-G). It is used mainly against susceptible staphylococcal infections, including skin and soft-tissue infections, and is available in topical and systemic formulations in some regions. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated with statins because of serious rhabdomyolysis risk.
tail_desc: Lymphogranuloma venereum is a sexually transmitted infection of the genital and lymphatic systems caused by invasive Chlamydia trachomatis serovars L1–L3. It typically begins with a small transient genital ulcer, followed by painful inguinal or femoral lymphadenopathy, proctocolitis, or systemic symptoms, especially in sexually active adults. Management is with appropriate antibiotic therapy, partner evaluation, and screening for coexisting sexually transmitted infections.

head: Ampicillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ampicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, gastrointestinal, and meningitis pathogens such as Listeria monocytogenes and Enterococcus species. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Campylobacter fetus infectious disease is a bacterial infection, usually systemic rather than enteric, caused by Campylobacter fetus with a predilection for bloodstream and vascular tissues. It most often affects older adults, pregnant people, and immunocompromised patients, causing bacteremia, fever, cellulitis, thrombophlebitis, endovascular infection, or meningitis. Management typically requires targeted antibiotic therapy and evaluation for metastatic or vascular foci.

head: Ardeparin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ardeparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant derived from porcine mucosal heparin. It potentiates antithrombin III, producing greater inhibition of factor Xa than thrombin (factor IIa). It has been used for prophylaxis of postoperative deep vein thrombosis, particularly after knee surgery. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it can also cause heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged interruption of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation; management centers on urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, symptom control, and secondary prevention.

head: Cimetidine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Cimetidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It is used for peptic ulcer disease, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and other acid-related disorders. A notable adverse effect is inhibition of cytochrome P450 enzymes, causing clinically important drug interactions; gynecomastia can also occur.
tail_desc: Angioedema is a vascular permeability disorder of the skin and mucosa causing sudden, localized swelling, often involving the face, lips, tongue, extremities, or airway. It results from mast cell mediator release or bradykinin excess, and may be allergic, medication-induced, or hereditary. Patients present with episodic nonpitting edema, sometimes with urticaria or life-threatening airway compromise; management targets airway protection and treating the underlying mechanism.

head: Megestrol acetate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Megestrol acetate is a synthetic progestin derivative of progesterone that acts primarily as an agonist of the progesterone receptor, with additional glucocorticoid activity. It is used as an appetite stimulant for anorexia, cachexia, or significant weight loss, including in AIDS, and as hormonal therapy for advanced endometrial carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of thromboembolic events.
tail_desc: Ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinoma is an epithelial ovarian cancer of the female reproductive system, histologically resembling endometrial adenocarcinoma and often associated with endometriosis. It typically affects peri- or postmenopausal women and may present with pelvic pain, abdominal distension, or an adnexal mass. Management usually involves surgical staging and cytoreductive resection, with platinum-based chemotherapy according to stage and residual disease.

head: Timolol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Timolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist (beta blocker) of the aryloxypropanolamine class that blocks β1 and β2 receptors, reducing cardiac stimulation and decreasing aqueous humor production. It is used for glaucoma or ocular hypertension and for hypertension, angina, and post-myocardial infarction risk reduction. Notable risks include bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in asthma, severe COPD, bradycardia, and heart block.
tail_desc: Congenital mitral valve insufficiency is a rare congenital heart valve disorder of the cardiovascular system in which developmental malformation of the mitral valve apparatus causes mitral regurgitation. It typically presents in infants or children with a heart murmur, poor feeding, failure to thrive, dyspnea, or signs of heart failure. Management depends on severity, using medical treatment for symptoms and surgical valve repair when significant regurgitation is present.

head: Triamcinolone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Triamcinolone is a synthetic fluorinated glucocorticoid corticosteroid that binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to regulate gene transcription, producing anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. It is used in topical, intra-articular, intralesional, inhaled, and nasal formulations for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses, allergic rhinitis, asthma, and inflammatory joint or soft-tissue disorders. Notable adverse effects include adrenal suppression, and it is contraindicated in untreated systemic fungal infection.
tail_desc: Severe early-onset COPD is a chronic obstructive airway disease of the respiratory system characterized by persistent airflow limitation from emphysema and/or chronic bronchitis, arising unusually early in adult life. It commonly reflects heavy smoking, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, or other susceptibility factors, and presents with progressive dyspnea, cough, wheeze, and frequent exacerbations. Management centers on smoking cessation, inhaled bronchodilators, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccination, oxygen when indicated, and selected advanced interventions.

head: Meclocycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Meclocycline is a tetracycline-class antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by reversibly binding the 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment. It has been used mainly as a topical anti-infective for superficial bacterial skin infections. A notable adverse effect is photosensitivity; like other tetracyclines, it is generally contraindicated during pregnancy and in young children because of tooth discoloration and effects on bone growth.
tail_desc: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the facial skin, primarily affecting the pilosebaceous and vascular systems. Its cause is multifactorial and incompletely understood, involving dysregulated innate immunity, neurovascular reactivity, and environmental triggers. It commonly affects adults with central facial flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasia, papules or pustules, and sometimes ocular involvement; management centers on trigger avoidance and topical, oral, laser, or ocular therapies according to subtype and severity.

head: Paclitaxel (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Paclitaxel is a taxane antineoplastic agent that binds the β-subunit of tubulin in microtubules, promoting their stabilization and preventing depolymerization, thereby arresting mitosis. It is used for ovarian, breast, and non-small cell lung cancers, and AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma. A notable toxicity is peripheral neuropathy; severe hypersensitivity reactions can also occur, especially with solvent-based formulations.
tail_desc: Esophageal cancer is a malignant tumor of the esophagus, usually squamous cell carcinoma or adenocarcinoma, arising from dysplastic epithelial changes. It typically affects older adults and commonly presents with progressive dysphagia, weight loss, and sometimes odynophagia or bleeding. Management depends on stage and histology, and may include endoscopic therapy for early disease, esophagectomy, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and palliative stenting or supportive care.

head: Potassium bicarbonate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Potassium bicarbonate is an oral potassium salt and systemic alkalinizing agent. It dissociates to provide potassium and bicarbonate; bicarbonate buffers hydrogen ions and raises urinary and systemic pH, while potassium replenishes body potassium stores. It is used for prevention or treatment of hypokalemia and, in some formulations, urinary alkalinization. A notable risk is hyperkalemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Multiple endocrine neoplasia is a group of inherited cancer predisposition syndromes affecting the endocrine system, most often caused by germline mutations in MEN1 or RET. It is characterized by tumors or hyperplasia in multiple endocrine glands, causing hormone excess and related symptoms, often beginning in childhood or adulthood depending on subtype. Management centers on genetic testing, biochemical surveillance, imaging, and surgical treatment of affected glands or tumors.

head: Metoprolol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Metoprolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class that primarily antagonizes β1-adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, contractility, and renin release. It is used for hypertension, angina, heart failure, and after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in severe bradycardia, cardiogenic shock, and second- or third-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Congenital mitral valve insufficiency is a rare congenital heart valve disorder of the cardiovascular system in which developmental malformation of the mitral valve apparatus causes mitral regurgitation. It typically presents in infants or children with a heart murmur, poor feeding, failure to thrive, dyspnea, or signs of heart failure. Management depends on severity, using medical treatment for symptoms and surgical valve repair when significant regurgitation is present.

head: Megestrol acetate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Megestrol acetate is a synthetic progestin derivative of progesterone that acts primarily as an agonist of the progesterone receptor, with additional glucocorticoid activity. It is used as an appetite stimulant for anorexia, cachexia, or significant weight loss, including in AIDS, and as hormonal therapy for advanced endometrial carcinoma. A notable adverse effect is increased risk of thromboembolic events.
tail_desc: Ovarian carcinosarcoma is a rare, highly aggressive malignant tumor of the ovary in the female reproductive system, composed of both epithelial carcinomatous and mesenchymal sarcomatous elements. It typically affects postmenopausal women and presents with abdominal distension, pelvic pain, or a mass, often at advanced stage. Management usually involves cytoreductive surgery followed by platinum-based chemotherapy.

head: Ferrous fumarate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ferrous fumarate is an oral iron salt, a ferrous (Fe2+) fumarate compound used as a hematinic. It supplies elemental iron for hemoglobin synthesis and repletion of body iron stores rather than acting through a specific receptor target. It is established for prevention and treatment of iron deficiency anemia. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal irritation, especially constipation, and it is contraindicated in iron overload states such as hemochromatosis.
tail_desc: Amenorrhea is a reproductive-endocrine disorder of the female menstrual system defined by absence of menstruation, classified as primary or secondary. It results from pregnancy or disruption of hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian, uterine, or outflow-tract function, including ovarian insufficiency, endocrine disease, low energy availability, or structural abnormalities. Patients present with absent menses, sometimes with delayed puberty or infertility. Management targets the underlying cause, with pregnancy exclusion and hormonal, nutritional, or surgical treatment as appropriate.

head: Benzylpenicillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Benzylpenicillin (penicillin G) is a natural beta-lactam penicillin antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins (transpeptidases), inhibiting bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking and causing bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including streptococcal and pneumococcal disease, meningococcal infection, syphilis, and some clostridial infections. A notable risk is immediate hypersensitivity, including anaphylaxis; it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Ophthalmia neonatorum is acute conjunctivitis of the eye occurring in the first month of life, usually caused by perinatal exposure to infectious agents such as Neisseria gonorrhoeae or Chlamydia trachomatis, or less often by chemical irritation. It presents in newborns with conjunctival redness, eyelid swelling, and purulent or mucopurulent discharge. Management is urgent etiologic evaluation and prompt topical and/or systemic antimicrobial treatment, with prophylaxis helping prevent some cases.

head: Polyethylene glycol 300 (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Polyethylene glycol 300 is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Dry eye syndrome is a common ocular surface disorder of the lacrimal functional unit in which reduced tear production or excessive tear evaporation causes tear-film instability and hyperosmolarity, leading to surface inflammation and epithelial damage. Typical symptoms are dryness, burning, foreign-body sensation, fluctuating vision, and reflex tearing, especially in older adults. Management focuses on lubricating tears, environmental measures, eyelid/meibomian care, and treating underlying inflammation or contributing causes.

head: Ardeparin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ardeparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant derived from porcine mucosal heparin. It potentiates antithrombin III, producing greater inhibition of factor Xa than thrombin (factor IIa). It has been used for prophylaxis of postoperative deep vein thrombosis, particularly after knee surgery. A notable adverse effect is bleeding, and it can also cause heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Coronavinae infectious disease is a disease or medical condition.

head: Triamcinolone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Triamcinolone is a synthetic fluorinated glucocorticoid corticosteroid that binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to regulate gene transcription, producing anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. It is used in topical, intra-articular, intralesional, inhaled, and nasal formulations for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses, allergic rhinitis, asthma, and inflammatory joint or soft-tissue disorders. Notable adverse effects include adrenal suppression, and it is contraindicated in untreated systemic fungal infection.
tail_desc: Anti-glomerular basement membrane disease is a rare autoimmune small-vessel disorder affecting the kidneys and often the lungs, caused by autoantibodies against the glomerular basement membrane (classically the α3 chain of type IV collagen). It typically presents with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, hematuria, renal failure, and sometimes pulmonary hemorrhage, usually in adults. Management is urgent immunosuppression with corticosteroids and cyclophosphamide plus plasmapheresis.

head: Clindamycin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Clindamycin is a lincosamide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible anaerobic and gram-positive bacterial infections, including skin and soft-tissue, bone and joint, intra-abdominal, and some dental infections, and is also used topically for acne. A notable adverse effect is Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea/pseudomembranous colitis.
tail_desc: Myiasis is a parasitic infestation of the skin or body cavities by fly larvae (maggots), affecting cutaneous, wound, ocular, nasal, or gastrointestinal sites. It results from deposition of eggs or larvae in tissue, especially in tropical regions, poor hygiene, wounds, or debilitated patients. Typical features include painful or pruritic furuncle-like lesions, visible larvae, discharge, and tissue destruction. Management is directed at larval removal, wound care, and treatment of secondary infection.

head: Tetracycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Tetracycline is a broad-spectrum tetracycline-class antibiotic that reversibly binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including acne, respiratory and skin infections, and certain atypical infections. A notable adverse effect is permanent tooth discoloration and impaired bone growth, so it is generally contraindicated during pregnancy and in young children.
tail_desc: Suppurative cholangitis is an acute bacterial infection of the biliary tract, usually arising from biliary obstruction that causes infected bile under pressure within the bile ducts. It affects the hepatobiliary system and typically presents with fever, right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, and possible sepsis, often in adults with gallstones or strictures. Management requires urgent antibiotics and biliary decompression, usually by endoscopic drainage.

head: Quinacrine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Quinacrine is an acridine derivative antiprotozoal with additional antihelminthic and anti-inflammatory activity. Its antiparasitic action is not fully defined but is associated with intercalation into nucleic acids and interference with parasite cellular function. It has been used for giardiasis and, historically, malaria and lupus erythematosus. Notable adverse effects include yellow discoloration of the skin and risk of aplastic anemia.
tail_desc: Lupus erythematosus is an autoimmune connective tissue disease affecting mainly the skin and, in systemic forms, multiple organs. It involves loss of immune tolerance with autoantibody formation and immune-complex–mediated inflammation. Typical features include photosensitive rash, oral ulcers, arthritis, serositis, nephritis, and hematologic abnormalities, most often in women of childbearing age. Management focuses on sun protection, disease control with corticosteroids, antimalarials, and other immunosuppressive agents, plus organ-specific monitoring.

head: Ifosfamide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ifosfamide is an oxazaphosphorine alkylating agent, structurally related to cyclophosphamide. It is a prodrug activated by hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes to metabolites that form DNA interstrand and intrastrand cross-links, impairing replication and transcription without a single specific molecular target. It is used for sarcomas, testicular cancer, and some lymphomas; notable toxicities include hemorrhagic cystitis and encephalopathy.
tail_desc: Bone osteosarcoma is a primary malignant mesenchymal tumor of the skeletal system characterized by production of osteoid by malignant cells. It most often arises in the metaphyses of long bones in adolescents and young adults, causing progressive localized bone pain, swelling, and sometimes pathologic fracture. Management typically involves neoadjuvant multiagent chemotherapy, wide surgical resection or limb-salvage surgery, and adjuvant chemotherapy.

head: Medroxyprogesterone acetate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Medroxyprogesterone acetate is a synthetic progestin, a progesterone receptor agonist that suppresses gonadotropin secretion and induces endometrial changes. It is used for contraception (including depot injection), abnormal uterine bleeding, secondary amenorrhea, and endometrial hyperplasia prevention with estrogen therapy. Notable adverse effects include irregular bleeding and weight gain; it is contraindicated in pregnancy and active thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: Polycystic ovary syndrome is a common endocrine-metabolic disorder of the female reproductive system, affecting reproductive-age women. It is characterized by ovarian dysfunction with hyperandrogenism and often insulin resistance, causing irregular or absent ovulation, hirsutism, acne, infertility, and polycystic ovarian morphology. Management typically targets symptoms and metabolic risk with lifestyle measures, hormonal therapy, and treatment for insulin resistance or infertility.

head: Doxycycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Doxycycline is a semisynthetic tetracycline antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat a wide range of bacterial infections, including respiratory, skin, sexually transmitted, and tick-borne infections, and for malaria prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include photosensitivity, and it is generally avoided during pregnancy and in young children due to tooth discoloration.
tail_desc: Plasmodium vivax malaria is a parasitic infectious disease of the blood and reticuloendothelial system caused by Plasmodium vivax, transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes. It invades hepatocytes and reticulocytes, with dormant liver hypnozoites causing relapses. Typical features include recurrent fever, chills, headache, malaise, anemia, and splenomegaly, especially in people in endemic regions or travelers. Management uses antimalarials for blood-stage infection plus radical cure of liver hypnozoites when appropriate.

head: Iodine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Iodine is an essential halogen and topical anti-infective/thyroid-active agent. As elemental iodine or iodophors, it rapidly iodinates and oxidizes microbial proteins and nucleotides, producing broad-spectrum antiseptic activity; as iodide, it serves as substrate for thyroid hormone synthesis in the thyroid gland. Established uses include skin antisepsis and prevention or treatment of iodine deficiency. Notable risks include hypersensitivity reactions and iodine-induced thyroid dysfunction.
tail_desc: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder of the endocrine system and the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, caused by thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins that activate the thyrotropin receptor. It typically affects women more often than men and causes diffuse goiter, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and sometimes ophthalmopathy. Management usually includes beta-blockers for symptoms and antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or thyroidectomy.

head: Fondaparinux (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Fondaparinux is a synthetic pentasaccharide anticoagulant that selectively binds antithrombin and indirectly inhibits factor Xa, reducing thrombin generation. It is approved for prophylaxis and treatment of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, including use in acute coronary syndromes in some settings. A notable risk is major bleeding, and it is contraindicated in severe renal impairment and in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Acute coronary syndrome is a cardiovascular emergency encompassing unstable angina and myocardial infarction caused by abrupt reduction in coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, or radiation to the arm or jaw. Management centers on urgent risk stratification, antiplatelet/antithrombotic therapy, anti-ischemic treatment, and timely coronary reperfusion when indicated.

head: Trandolapril (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Trandolapril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a prodrug converted to trandolaprilat, which inhibits ACE and reduces formation of angiotensin II while decreasing aldosterone secretion. It is used to treat hypertension and to improve survival or reduce cardiovascular risk after myocardial infarction with left ventricular dysfunction. A notable adverse effect is angioedema; it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Diastolic heart failure, now commonly termed heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, is a cardiovascular disorder in which impaired left ventricular relaxation and increased stiffness cause abnormal diastolic filling and elevated filling pressures despite near-normal systolic function. It typically presents with exertional dyspnea, edema, and fatigue, especially in older adults with hypertension; management focuses on diuresis, blood pressure control, and treating contributing comorbidities.

head: Doxycycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Doxycycline is a semisynthetic tetracycline antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat a wide range of bacterial infections, including respiratory, skin, sexually transmitted, and tick-borne infections, and for malaria prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include photosensitivity, and it is generally avoided during pregnancy and in young children due to tooth discoloration.
tail_desc: Yaws is a chronic infectious treponemal disease of the skin, soft tissues, and bones caused by Treponema pallidum pertenue. It primarily affects children in tropical rural regions and begins with papillomatous or ulcerative skin lesions, followed by widespread lesions and periostitis; late disease can cause destructive deformities. Management is with antibiotics, typically single-dose azithromycin or injectable penicillin, plus community control measures.

head: Clindamycin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Clindamycin is a lincosamide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible anaerobic and gram-positive bacterial infections, including skin and soft-tissue, bone and joint, intra-abdominal, and some dental infections, and is also used topically for acne. A notable adverse effect is Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea/pseudomembranous colitis.
tail_desc: Malaria is a mosquito-borne parasitic infection of the blood and reticuloendothelial system caused by Plasmodium species, transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes. Parasite replication in hepatocytes and erythrocytes leads to cyclical fever, chills, headache, anemia, and splenomegaly; severe cases can cause cerebral, renal, or respiratory complications, especially in young children, pregnant women, and nonimmune travelers. Management relies on prompt antiparasitic therapy, supportive care, and prevention through vector control and chemoprophylaxis.

head: Metipranolol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Metipranolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class used primarily as an ophthalmic agent. By antagonizing β1- and β2-adrenergic receptors in the ciliary epithelium, it reduces aqueous humor production and lowers intraocular pressure. It is indicated for ocular hypertension and open-angle glaucoma; notable risks include bronchospasm, and it is contraindicated in asthma and severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
tail_desc: Closed-angle glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder in which the anterior chamber angle becomes obstructed, blocking aqueous humor outflow and causing a rapid rise in intraocular pressure that can damage the optic nerve. It often presents in older, hyperopic adults with sudden severe eye pain, blurred vision, halos, headache, nausea, and a red eye. Management is urgent pressure lowering followed by definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

head: Clavulanic acid (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Clavulanic acid is a beta-lactam beta-lactamase inhibitor, structurally a clavem, used with beta-lactam antibiotics such as amoxicillin to overcome bacterial resistance. It irreversibly inhibits many serine beta-lactamases, protecting the partner antibiotic rather than exerting strong antibacterial activity alone. Established uses include susceptible respiratory, skin, urinary, and other mixed infections; cholestatic hepatitis is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Infectious otitis media is an infection of the middle ear within the ear, nose, and throat system, most often caused by viral or bacterial pathogens ascending through the Eustachian tube, frequently after an upper respiratory infection. It is especially common in young children and typically presents with ear pain, fever, irritability, and reduced hearing. Management usually involves analgesia, observation in selected cases, and antibiotics when bacterial infection is suspected or symptoms are severe.

head: Hydrocortisone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Hydrocortisone is a glucocorticoid corticosteroid, the pharmaceutical form of cortisol. It binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to regulate gene transcription, producing anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects; it also has some mineralocorticoid activity. It is used for adrenal insufficiency replacement therapy and for inflammatory, allergic, and dermatologic disorders. Notable adverse effects include hyperglycemia, and systemic use is generally contraindicated in untreated systemic fungal infection.
tail_desc: X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy is an inherited peroxisomal disorder affecting the nervous system and adrenal cortex, caused by ABCD1 mutations that impair very-long-chain fatty acid transport and degradation. It typically affects boys and men, causing progressive cerebral demyelination or adrenomyeloneuropathy with spastic paraparesis, cognitive decline, and adrenal insufficiency. Management includes adrenal hormone replacement, supportive neurologic care, and early hematopoietic stem cell transplantation for cerebral disease.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: malignant germ cell tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: Malignant germ cell tumor of the ovary is a rare ovarian gonadal neoplasm arising from primitive germ cells, typically affecting children, adolescents, and young women. Histologic types include dysgerminoma, yolk sac tumor, and immature teratoma. Patients often present with a rapidly enlarging pelvic mass, abdominal pain, or elevated tumor markers. Management usually combines fertility-sparing surgical staging with platinum-based chemotherapy.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Familial porphyria cutanea tarda is an inherited hepatic porphyria affecting heme biosynthesis, usually caused by reduced uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity. It leads to porphyrin accumulation and chronic photosensitivity, with blistering, skin fragility, hyperpigmentation, and scarring on sun-exposed areas, often in adults. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, photoprotection, periodic phlebotomy, and sometimes low-dose hydroxychloroquine.

head: Amoxicillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Amoxicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibacterial agent. It binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to cell lysis. It is widely used for susceptible infections such as otitis media, sinusitis, streptococcal pharyngitis, lower respiratory, urinary tract, skin infections, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins.
tail_desc: Sclerosing cholangitis is a chronic cholestatic disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by inflammation, fibrosis, and multifocal stricturing of intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic bile ducts, leading to impaired bile flow and progressive liver injury. Patients may have fatigue, pruritus, jaundice, or recurrent cholangitis; many with primary disease have inflammatory bowel disease. Management focuses on symptom control, treating complications, endoscopic relief of dominant strictures, and liver transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Chloramphenicol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Chloramphenicol is a broad-spectrum amphenicol antibiotic that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocking peptidyl transferase activity. It is used for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including meningitis, rickettsial infections, and topical treatment of bacterial conjunctivitis. A notable adverse effect is dose-independent aplastic anemia; use is also limited by gray baby syndrome in neonates.
tail_desc: Conjunctivitis is an inflammation of the conjunctiva, the ocular surface membrane of the eye, most commonly caused by viral or bacterial infection, allergy, or irritant exposure. It typically presents with eye redness, tearing or discharge, irritation, and sometimes itching, in both children and adults. Management depends on cause and generally includes hygiene, symptomatic relief, avoidance of triggers, and targeted antimicrobial therapy when bacterial infection is suspected.

head: Lisdexamfetamine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Lisdexamfetamine is a central nervous system stimulant and lysine-conjugated prodrug of dextroamphetamine that, after enzymatic conversion, increases synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine mainly by promoting their presynaptic release and reversing DAT and NET transport. It is approved for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and moderate-to-severe binge-eating disorder in adults. Notable adverse effects include insomnia and decreased appetite; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Narcolepsy is a chronic neurologic sleep-wake disorder of the central nervous system, often caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin) signaling, especially in type 1 disease. It is characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness, irresistible sleep attacks, and sometimes cataplexy, sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations, usually beginning in adolescence or young adulthood. Management focuses on symptom control with wake-promoting agents, scheduled naps, and cataplexy-directed therapy.

head: Fluconazole (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Fluconazole is a triazole antifungal that inhibits fungal lanosterol 14-alpha-demethylase (CYP51), blocking ergosterol synthesis and impairing cell membrane formation. It is used to treat and prevent susceptible Candida infections, including oropharyngeal, esophageal, and vulvovaginal candidiasis, as well as cryptococcal meningitis. A notable risk is hepatotoxicity, and it can prolong the QT interval.
tail_desc: White piedra is a superficial fungal infection of the hair shaft caused by Trichosporon species, affecting the integumentary system. It is characterized by soft white to light-brown nodules adherent to scalp, facial, axillary, or pubic hair, often in otherwise healthy people in tropical or temperate climates. Management typically involves shaving or cutting affected hair and applying topical antifungal therapy.

head: Phentolamine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Phentolamine is a nonselective, competitive alpha-adrenergic antagonist of the imidazoline class that blocks alpha1 and alpha2 receptors, producing peripheral vasodilation. It is used to prevent or control catecholamine-induced hypertensive episodes in pheochromocytoma and for treatment of dermal necrosis or ischemia after norepinephrine extravasation.
