Notable adverse effects include orthostatic hypotension and reflex tachycardia.
tail_desc: Premature ejaculation is a male sexual dysfunction of the genitourinary and neurobehavioral domains, characterized by ejaculation that occurs sooner than desired, often within about 1 minute of penetration in lifelong cases, with poor perceived control and associated distress. Its cause is multifactorial, involving psychologic, neurobiologic, and relational factors. Management typically includes education, behavioral and psychosexual therapy, and selected pharmacologic treatment.

head: Triamcinolone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Triamcinolone is a synthetic fluorinated glucocorticoid corticosteroid that binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to regulate gene transcription, producing anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. It is used in topical, intra-articular, intralesional, inhaled, and nasal formulations for corticosteroid-responsive dermatoses, allergic rhinitis, asthma, and inflammatory joint or soft-tissue disorders. Notable adverse effects include adrenal suppression, and it is contraindicated in untreated systemic fungal infection.
tail_desc: Musculoskeletal system diseases are a broad category of disorders affecting bones, joints, muscles, tendons, ligaments, and related connective tissues. They arise from degenerative, inflammatory, traumatic, congenital, infectious, metabolic, or autoimmune processes. Typical features include pain, stiffness, swelling, weakness, deformity, and impaired mobility, occurring across all ages. Management depends on cause and commonly includes rehabilitation, analgesia, anti-inflammatory or disease-specific therapy, and sometimes surgery.

head: Mitomycin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Mitomycin is an antineoplastic antibiotic of the mitomycin/aziridine alkylating-agent class that is enzymatically activated to form DNA cross-links, inhibiting DNA synthesis and replication without a single specific molecular target. It is used in several cancers, including gastric and pancreatic adenocarcinomas, and intravesically for superficial bladder cancer. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; hemolytic-uremic syndrome can also occur.
tail_desc: Urinary bladder neoplasm is a benign or, more commonly, malignant tumor of the urinary bladder within the urinary tract, usually arising from the urothelial lining; smoking and certain occupational chemical exposures are major risk factors. It often presents in older adults with painless hematuria and irritative voiding symptoms. Management depends on stage and grade, typically involving transurethral resection, intravesical therapy, and for invasive disease, radical cystectomy and/or systemic therapy.

head: Nitroglycerin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Nitroglycerin is an organic nitrate vasodilator that is bioactivated to nitric oxide, which stimulates soluble guanylate cyclase in vascular smooth muscle, increases cGMP, and promotes relaxation, predominantly venodilation. It is used for acute relief and prophylaxis of angina pectoris, and in some settings for acute heart failure or blood pressure control. A notable adverse effect is headache; it is contraindicated with phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors because of severe hypotension risk.
tail_desc: Myocardial infarction is an acute ischemic heart disease of the cardiovascular system caused by prolonged reduction or cessation of coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombotic occlusion, leading to myocardial necrosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pain or pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, and biomarker elevation, and requires urgent reperfusion, antithrombotic therapy, and secondary prevention.

head: Etacrynic acid (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Etacrynic acid is a non-sulfonamide loop diuretic of the aryloxyacetic acid class that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter (NKCC2, SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of Henle, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat edema associated with heart failure, renal disease, or hepatic cirrhosis. A notable adverse effect is ototoxicity, especially with high doses or intravenous use.
tail_desc: Hypertensive disorder is a cardiovascular condition characterized by persistently elevated arterial blood pressure due to increased peripheral vascular resistance and/or dysregulation of renal, neurohormonal, and vascular control mechanisms. It is often asymptomatic but may cause headache or end-organ damage involving the heart, brain, kidneys, and eyes, especially in adults. Management centers on lifestyle modification, risk reduction, and antihypertensive medication.

head: Mitomycin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Mitomycin is an antineoplastic antibiotic of the mitomycin/aziridine alkylating-agent class that is enzymatically activated to form DNA cross-links, inhibiting DNA synthesis and replication without a single specific molecular target. It is used in several cancers, including gastric and pancreatic adenocarcinomas, and intravesically for superficial bladder cancer. A notable toxicity is severe myelosuppression; hemolytic-uremic syndrome can also occur.
tail_desc: Upper aerodigestive tract neoplasm is a tumor of the upper respiratory and digestive passages, including the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures, most often representing squamous cell carcinoma arising from mucosal epithelium. It commonly affects adults, especially with tobacco and alcohol exposure, and may cause dysphagia, hoarseness, odynophagia, neck mass, or weight loss; management typically centers on staging with surgery, radiotherapy, and/or chemotherapy.

head: Minocycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Minocycline is a semisynthetic tetracycline-class antibiotic that binds the 30S bacterial ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, notably acne vulgaris and certain skin, respiratory, and sexually transmitted infections. A notable adverse effect is vestibular toxicity causing dizziness/vertigo; like other tetracyclines, it is contraindicated during pregnancy and in young children because of tooth discoloration and impaired bone growth.
tail_desc: Mycoplasma pneumoniae pneumonia is an atypical community-acquired bacterial pneumonia of the respiratory tract caused by Mycoplasma pneumoniae, which adheres to respiratory epithelium and triggers tracheobronchial and interstitial inflammation. It commonly affects school-aged children, adolescents, and young adults, causing gradual fever, dry cough, malaise, headache, and patchy radiographic infiltrates. Management is mainly with macrolides, doxycycline, or fluoroquinolones plus supportive care.

head: Mercaptopurine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Mercaptopurine is a thiopurine antimetabolite and purine analog used mainly in acute lymphoblastic leukemia and sometimes in inflammatory bowel disease. It is converted intracellularly to active thioguanine nucleotides that inhibit de novo purine synthesis and are incorporated into DNA and RNA, impairing cell proliferation. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, with markedly increased toxicity in patients with TPMT or NUDT15 deficiency.
tail_desc: Ulcerative colitis is a chronic inflammatory bowel disease affecting the colonic mucosa, typically beginning in the rectum and extending proximally in a continuous pattern. It reflects immune-mediated intestinal inflammation in genetically susceptible individuals. Common features include bloody diarrhea, urgency, abdominal pain, and relapsing course, often in adolescents and adults. Management focuses on anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive therapy, with colectomy for refractory or complicated disease.

head: Tamoxifen (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Tamoxifen is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM), a triphenylethylene derivative, that primarily antagonizes estrogen receptor signaling in breast tissue. It is used for hormone receptor–positive breast cancer treatment and prevention, including adjuvant and metastatic settings. Important adverse effects include increased risk of venous thromboembolism and endometrial cancer, so it is generally avoided in patients with prior thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: Hereditary site-specific ovarian cancer syndrome is a hereditary cancer predisposition affecting the female reproductive system, characterized by familial clustering of ovarian cancer without the broader tumor spectrum seen in other syndromes. It likely reflects inherited susceptibility, often involving germline defects in DNA repair genes such as BRCA1 or BRCA2. Management centers on genetic counseling, surveillance, and consideration of risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy.

head: Cladribine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Cladribine is a synthetic purine nucleoside analog (2-chlorodeoxyadenosine) antimetabolite that is phosphorylated intracellularly and incorporated into DNA, causing strand breaks and apoptosis, particularly in lymphocytes. It is approved for hairy cell leukemia and, in oral tablet form, relapsing multiple sclerosis. A notable risk is prolonged lymphopenia with serious infections; it is contraindicated in pregnancy.
tail_desc: Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a rare clonal histiocytic neoplasm in which pathologic dendritic cells accumulate in tissues, often driven by MAPK-pathway mutations such as BRAF V600E. It can affect bone, skin, lungs, and the pituitary, especially in children, causing lytic bone lesions, rash, pain, or diabetes insipidus. Management ranges from local therapy to systemic chemotherapy or targeted treatment in multisystem disease.

head: Nizatidine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Nizatidine is a histamine H2-receptor antagonist that reduces gastric acid secretion by competitively blocking H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells. It is used for duodenal ulcer, benign gastric ulcer, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and erosive esophagitis. Notable adverse effects include headache and diarrhea, and dose adjustment is recommended in renal impairment.
tail_desc: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is a gastroenterologic disorder caused by gastrin-secreting neuroendocrine tumors (gastrinomas), usually in the pancreas or duodenum, leading to marked gastric acid hypersecretion. It typically presents in adults with severe, recurrent peptic ulcers, gastroesophageal reflux, abdominal pain, and diarrhea, and may occur sporadically or with MEN1. Management focuses on potent acid suppression and localization and treatment of the tumor, including surgery when feasible.

head: Chlorpropamide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Chlorpropamide is a first-generation sulfonylurea antidiabetic that lowers blood glucose by binding the SUR1 subunit of ATP-sensitive potassium channels on pancreatic beta cells, promoting insulin release. It has been used for type 2 diabetes mellitus. A notable adverse effect is prolonged hypoglycemia, particularly in older adults or those with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Diabetes insipidus is an endocrine disorder of water balance involving the hypothalamic-pituitary-renal axis, caused by deficient antidiuretic hormone secretion (central) or renal resistance to it (nephrogenic). It presents with polyuria, polydipsia, dilute urine, nocturia, and risk of dehydration and hypernatremia in children or adults. Management targets the cause, with desmopressin for central disease and hydration plus measures for nephrogenic forms.

head: Doxycycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Doxycycline is a semisynthetic tetracycline antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat a wide range of bacterial infections, including respiratory, skin, sexually transmitted, and tick-borne infections, and for malaria prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include photosensitivity, and it is generally avoided during pregnancy and in young children due to tooth discoloration.
tail_desc: Mycobacterial infectious disease is a bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium species, most often affecting the lungs but also skin, lymph nodes, and disseminated sites. It results from intracellular infection with granulomatous inflammation. Clinical features vary from chronic cough, fever, weight loss, and night sweats to localized soft-tissue or nodal disease, especially in immunocompromised patients. Management relies on prolonged multidrug antimycobacterial therapy, with source evaluation and public health measures when tuberculosis is involved.

head: Fluorometholone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Fluorometholone is a synthetic fluorinated corticosteroid used mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts as a glucocorticoid receptor agonist, altering gene transcription to suppress phospholipase A2–dependent mediator production and reduce ocular inflammation. It is indicated for steroid-responsive inflammatory conditions of the palpebral and bulbar conjunctiva, cornea, and anterior segment. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, so it is contraindicated in most active ocular viral, mycobacterial, or fungal infections.
tail_desc: External ear disease comprises disorders of the outer ear within the auditory system, including the auricle and external auditory canal. It commonly reflects infection, inflammation, trauma, dermatologic conditions, or obstructive cerumen. Typical features include ear pain, itching, discharge, hearing reduction, swelling, or visible lesions, affecting both children and adults. Management targets the cause with canal cleaning, topical therapy, pain control, and avoidance of irritants.

head: Sulfamethoxazole (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Sulfamethoxazole is a sulfonamide antibacterial that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby DNA precursor production; it is commonly combined with trimethoprim for synergistic activity. Established uses include urinary tract infections, acute otitis media, bronchitis exacerbations, shigellosis, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia treatment or prophylaxis. Notable risks include severe hypersensitivity reactions such as Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Granuloma inguinale (donovanosis) is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection of the genital and perineal skin and mucosa caused by Klebsiella granulomatis. It produces slowly progressive, painless, beefy-red ulcerative lesions that bleed easily, most often in sexually active adults in endemic tropical and subtropical regions. Management is prolonged antibiotic therapy, typically with azithromycin or alternatives, until complete healing.

head: Nicotinamide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Nicotinamide, the amide form of vitamin B3 (niacin), is a water-soluble vitamin and precursor of the coenzymes NAD and NADP, supporting cellular redox and energy metabolism rather than acting through a single receptor target. It is used to prevent and treat niacinamide deficiency, including pellagra. Adverse effects are usually mild, but high doses can cause hepatotoxicity.
tail_desc: Methemoglobinemia is a hematologic oxygen-transport disorder in which hemoglobin iron is oxidized to ferric (Fe3+) form, reducing oxygen delivery to tissues. It may be congenital or acquired, often from oxidant drugs or chemicals. Patients develop cyanosis, chocolate-brown blood, dyspnea, headache, fatigue, and severe cases can cause neurologic or cardiovascular compromise. Management involves removing the trigger, supportive oxygen, and methylene blue when indicated.

head: Doxycycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Doxycycline is a semisynthetic tetracycline antibiotic that binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat a wide range of bacterial infections, including respiratory, skin, sexually transmitted, and tick-borne infections, and for malaria prophylaxis. Notable adverse effects include photosensitivity, and it is generally avoided during pregnancy and in young children due to tooth discoloration.
tail_desc: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the facial skin, primarily affecting the pilosebaceous and vascular systems. Its cause is multifactorial and incompletely understood, involving dysregulated innate immunity, neurovascular reactivity, and environmental triggers. It commonly affects adults with central facial flushing, persistent erythema, telangiectasia, papules or pustules, and sometimes ocular involvement; management centers on trigger avoidance and topical, oral, laser, or ocular therapies according to subtype and severity.

head: Sucralfate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Sucralfate is a basic aluminum salt of sulfated sucrose, classified as a gastroprotective antiulcer agent. In acidic gastric contents it polymerizes and binds positively charged proteins at ulcer bases, forming a protective barrier and adsorbing pepsin and bile salts rather than acting via a specific receptor target. It is used for treatment and maintenance of duodenal ulcer and can cause constipation.
tail_desc: Peptic esophagitis is an inflammatory disorder of the esophagus, usually caused by reflux of acidic gastric contents in gastroesophageal reflux disease. It commonly presents in adults with heartburn, retrosternal pain, acid regurgitation, and sometimes dysphagia or odynophagia. Management focuses on acid suppression, especially proton pump inhibitors, along with lifestyle measures and treatment of the underlying reflux.

head: Captopril (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Captopril is an angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitor, a sulfhydryl-containing antihypertensive agent. It inhibits ACE, reducing conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II and decreasing aldosterone secretion. Established indications include hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and it is also used after myocardial infarction in selected patients. A notable adverse effect is cough; it is contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal toxicity.
tail_desc: Diastolic heart failure, now commonly termed heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, is a cardiovascular disorder in which impaired left ventricular relaxation and increased stiffness cause abnormal diastolic filling and elevated filling pressures despite near-normal systolic function. It typically presents with exertional dyspnea, edema, and fatigue, especially in older adults with hypertension; management focuses on diuresis, blood pressure control, and treating contributing comorbidities.

head: Lisdexamfetamine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Lisdexamfetamine is a central nervous system stimulant and lysine-conjugated prodrug of dextroamphetamine that, after enzymatic conversion, increases synaptic dopamine and norepinephrine mainly by promoting their presynaptic release and reversing DAT and NET transport. It is approved for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder and moderate-to-severe binge-eating disorder in adults. Notable adverse effects include insomnia and decreased appetite; it is contraindicated with monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
tail_desc: Narcolepsy-cataplexy syndrome is a central nervous system sleep-wake disorder, usually corresponding to narcolepsy type 1, caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin) signaling, often on an autoimmune basis in genetically susceptible people. It presents with excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy, often with hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis, and disturbed nocturnal sleep, typically beginning in childhood or adolescence; management uses wake-promoting agents and anticataplectic therapy.

head: Metoprolol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Metoprolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class that primarily antagonizes β1-adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, contractility, and renin release. It is used for hypertension, angina, heart failure, and after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in severe bradycardia, cardiogenic shock, and second- or third-degree heart block.
tail_desc: His bundle tachycardia is a rare supraventricular arrhythmia of the cardiac conduction system arising from the His bundle, usually due to enhanced automaticity or triggered activity in this tissue. It is reported mainly in infants and children and may cause persistent narrow-complex tachycardia, palpitations, heart failure, or tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. Management typically centers on antiarrhythmic therapy, with catheter ablation considered in refractory cases.

head: Bacitracin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Bacitracin is a cyclic polypeptide antibiotic produced by Bacillus species. It inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis by binding undecaprenyl pyrophosphate (bactoprenol), blocking dephosphorylation and peptidoglycan precursor transport, mainly against Gram-positive organisms. It is used topically for minor skin, eye, and external ear infections; systemic use is limited by notable nephrotoxicity.
tail_desc: Dacryocystitis is an infection and inflammation of the lacrimal sac within the nasolacrimal drainage system, usually caused by nasolacrimal duct obstruction leading to tear stasis and secondary bacterial overgrowth. It presents with painful swelling, redness, and tenderness at the medial canthus, tearing, and sometimes purulent discharge, affecting infants or adults. Management typically includes systemic antibiotics, warm compresses, and relief of obstruction, often by dacryocystorhinostomy.

head: Desipramine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Desipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant, specifically a secondary amine dibenzazepine, that primarily inhibits norepinephrine reuptake via the norepinephrine transporter (NET), with less effect on serotonin uptake. It is used for major depressive disorder and has also been used for neuropathic pain in some settings. A notable risk is cardiotoxicity, including arrhythmias, especially in overdose.
tail_desc: Narcolepsy is a chronic neurologic sleep-wake disorder of the central nervous system, often caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin) signaling, especially in type 1 disease. It is characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness, irresistible sleep attacks, and sometimes cataplexy, sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations, usually beginning in adolescence or young adulthood. Management focuses on symptom control with wake-promoting agents, scheduled naps, and cataplexy-directed therapy.

head: Sulfamethoxazole (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Sulfamethoxazole is a sulfonamide antibacterial that inhibits bacterial dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis and thereby DNA precursor production; it is commonly combined with trimethoprim for synergistic activity. Established uses include urinary tract infections, acute otitis media, bronchitis exacerbations, shigellosis, and Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia treatment or prophylaxis. Notable risks include severe hypersensitivity reactions such as Stevens–Johnson syndrome; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Paratyphoid fever is an enteric systemic bacterial infection of the gastrointestinal tract caused by Salmonella enterica serovars Paratyphi A, B, or C, transmitted fecal-orally via contaminated food or water. It typically presents with sustained fever, headache, abdominal pain, malaise, and sometimes diarrhea or constipation, especially in exposed travelers or residents of endemic areas. Management relies on appropriate antibiotics, rehydration, and supportive care.

head: Ciprofloxacin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ciprofloxacin is a fluoroquinolone antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication and transcription. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections including urinary tract infections, gastrointestinal infections, skin infections, and certain respiratory infections, and for post-exposure anthrax. A notable adverse effect is tendinitis and tendon rupture; it is generally avoided in patients with prior fluoroquinolone-associated tendon disorders.
tail_desc: Invasive non-typhoidal salmonellosis is a systemic bacterial infection caused by non-typhoidal Salmonella spreading beyond the gut, affecting the bloodstream and sometimes focal sites such as meninges or bones. It presents with fever, sepsis, and bacteremia, often with little diarrhea, and occurs especially in infants, older adults, and immunocompromised people. Management typically requires prompt antibiotic therapy and supportive care.

head: Heparin, bovine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Heparin, bovine, is an animal-derived unfractionated heparin, a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant obtained from bovine tissues. It potentiates antithrombin III, accelerating inhibition of thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism and other settings requiring anticoagulation. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Serine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Serine is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Meningeal tuberculosis is a severe form of extrapulmonary tuberculosis affecting the central nervous system, caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection of the meninges. It typically presents subacutely with fever, headache, vomiting, neck stiffness, altered mental status, and cranial nerve deficits, especially in children and immunocompromised patients. Management requires prompt multidrug antituberculous therapy, usually with adjunctive corticosteroids and supportive neurologic care.

head: Primaquine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Primaquine is an 8-aminoquinoline antimalarial that is active against hepatic stages of Plasmodium, though its precise mechanism is not fully defined and likely involves oxidative metabolites causing parasite mitochondrial dysfunction. It is used for radical cure and relapse prevention of P. vivax and P. ovale malaria, and for terminal prophylaxis. A major risk is hemolytic anemia in patients with G6PD deficiency, making G6PD testing essential before use.
tail_desc: Malaria is a mosquito-borne parasitic infection of the blood and reticuloendothelial system caused by Plasmodium species, transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes. Parasite replication in hepatocytes and erythrocytes leads to cyclical fever, chills, headache, anemia, and splenomegaly; severe cases can cause cerebral, renal, or respiratory complications, especially in young children, pregnant women, and nonimmune travelers. Management relies on prompt antiparasitic therapy, supportive care, and prevention through vector control and chemoprophylaxis.

head: Citric acid (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Citric acid is a weak tricarboxylic organic acid and urinary/urine-acidifying alkalinizing adjunct used mainly in citrate salt formulations. Its clinical effects are physicochemical rather than receptor-mediated: citrate complexes calcium and, after metabolism of citrate salts, can increase systemic bicarbonate and urinary citrate. Established uses include prevention of certain kidney stones and urinary alkalinization; gastrointestinal irritation is a notable adverse effect, and caution is required in severe renal impairment.
tail_desc: Renal tubular acidosis is a group of kidney tubular disorders causing normal anion gap metabolic acidosis from impaired renal acid excretion or bicarbonate reabsorption. It may be inherited or acquired. Typical features include hyperchloremia, growth failure in children, nephrolithiasis, hypokalemia or hyperkalemia, and muscle weakness. Management targets the cause and corrects acidosis with alkali therapy, with electrolyte-specific treatment as needed.

head: Parnaparin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Parnaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and to a lesser extent thrombin (factor IIa). It is used for prevention and treatment of venous thromboembolic disorders, including postoperative deep vein thrombosis. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in active major hemorrhage or a history of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Acute coronary syndrome is a cardiovascular emergency encompassing unstable angina and myocardial infarction caused by abrupt reduction in coronary blood flow, usually from atherosclerotic plaque rupture with thrombosis. It typically presents in adults with chest pressure, dyspnea, diaphoresis, or radiation to the arm or jaw. Management centers on urgent risk stratification, antiplatelet/antithrombotic therapy, anti-ischemic treatment, and timely coronary reperfusion when indicated.

head: Etodolac (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Etodolac is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), a pyranocarboxylic acid derivative, that inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, particularly COX-mediated prostaglandin synthesis. It is used to relieve pain and inflammation, including in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, and acute pain. Like other NSAIDs, it can cause serious gastrointestinal ulceration or bleeding and is contraindicated in patients with NSAID hypersensitivity.
tail_desc: Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory spondyloarthritis affecting the axial skeleton, especially the sacroiliac joints and spine. It is associated with immune-mediated inflammation and a strong HLA-B27 association, which can lead to new bone formation and spinal ankylosis. Typical features include inflammatory back pain, morning stiffness, reduced spinal mobility, and onset in young adults. Management focuses on exercise, physical therapy, NSAIDs, and biologic therapy when needed.

head: Silicon dioxide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Silicon dioxide is an inorganic silicon oxide used in pharmaceutical products mainly as an excipient, including glidant, anti-caking agent, and tablet/capsule formulation aid, rather than as an active therapeutic drug. It has no defined pharmacologic mechanism or primary molecular target in routine medicinal use and no established therapeutic indication as an active agent. Inhalational exposure to crystalline silica is associated with pulmonary toxicity, including silicosis.
tail_desc: Systemic mastocytosis is a clonal hematologic mast cell neoplasm in which abnormal mast cells accumulate in bone marrow and other extracutaneous organs, often driven by activating KIT mutations. It causes mediator-related symptoms such as flushing, pruritus, abdominal pain, and anaphylaxis, and may produce organ infiltration with cytopenias or hepatosplenomegaly, mainly in adults. Management includes trigger avoidance, anti-mediator therapy, and cytoreductive or targeted treatment in advanced disease.

head: Meropenem (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Meropenem is a broad-spectrum carbapenem beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins, inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis and causing bactericidal activity. It is established for serious susceptible bacterial infections, including complicated intra-abdominal and skin infections, meningitis, and hospital-acquired pneumonia. A notable adverse effect is seizure risk, particularly in patients with CNS disorders or renal impairment.
tail_desc: Nocardiosis is an opportunistic bacterial infection caused by aerobic, filamentous Nocardia species, classically affecting the lungs with potential dissemination to the brain and skin. It occurs most often in immunocompromised patients, though cutaneous disease can follow traumatic inoculation in immunocompetent people. Typical features include pneumonia, nodules or abscesses, and management relies on prolonged targeted antibiotic therapy, often trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, with drainage when needed.

head: Acetazolamide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Acetazolamide is a sulfonamide-derived carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reversibly inhibits carbonic anhydrase isoenzymes, reducing bicarbonate reabsorption and aqueous humor and cerebrospinal fluid production. It is used for glaucoma, prevention and treatment of acute mountain sickness, adjunctive therapy in some epilepsies, and edema. Notable adverse effects include metabolic acidosis and hypokalemia; it is contraindicated in sulfonamide hypersensitivity and severe hepatic disease.
tail_desc: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder of the central nervous system characterized by a persistent predisposition to recurrent unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal, excessive, synchronous neuronal activity in the brain. It affects all ages and presents with focal or generalized seizures, sometimes with loss of awareness or convulsions. Management centers on antiseizure medications, with surgery, neuromodulation, and trigger avoidance in selected cases.

head: Ampicillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ampicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, gastrointestinal, and meningitis pathogens such as Listeria monocytogenes and Enterococcus species. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Listeria meningitis is a bacterial infection of the central nervous system caused by Listeria monocytogenes, producing inflammation of the meninges. It occurs most often in neonates, older adults, pregnant people, and immunocompromised patients. Typical features include fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered mental status, and sometimes sepsis. Management requires prompt intravenous antibiotics, typically ampicillin-based therapy, with supportive care.

head: Docetaxel (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: malignant sex cord stromal tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Docetaxel is a semisynthetic taxane antineoplastic agent that binds the β-subunit of tubulin, promoting microtubule assembly and inhibiting depolymerization, thereby blocking mitosis. It is used for breast cancer, non-small cell lung cancer, prostate cancer, gastric adenocarcinoma, and head and neck cancer. A notable toxicity is severe neutropenia; use is contraindicated in patients with baseline neutrophil counts below 1,500 cells/mm³.
tail_desc: Malignant ovarian sex cord-stromal tumor is a rare ovarian neoplasm arising from steroid hormone-producing sex cord or stromal cells of the ovary. It often affects children or younger women and may present with an adnexal mass, abdominal pain, or hormonal manifestations such as precocious puberty, menstrual abnormalities, or virilization. Management is primarily surgical, with staging and selective adjuvant chemotherapy for advanced, recurrent, or high-risk disease.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: theca steroid-producing cell malignant tumor of ovary, not further specified (a disease)
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: Theca steroid-producing cell malignant tumor of ovary, not further specified, is a rare malignant sex cord-stromal tumor of the ovary. It arises from steroid hormone-producing ovarian stromal cells, typically theca-like cells, and may cause androgenic or other endocrine manifestations. Patients may present with an adnexal mass, abdominal pain, or virilization. Management is primarily surgical, with further oncologic treatment individualized by stage and pathology.

head: Clindamycin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: skin disease caused by infection (a disease)
head_desc: Clindamycin is a lincosamide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used to treat susceptible anaerobic and gram-positive bacterial infections, including skin and soft-tissue, bone and joint, intra-abdominal, and some dental infections, and is also used topically for acne. A notable adverse effect is Clostridioides difficile–associated diarrhea/pseudomembranous colitis.
tail_desc: Skin disease caused by infection is a broad category of integumentary disorders in which bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites invade the skin and trigger local inflammation and tissue injury. Typical features include erythema, rash, pustules, vesicles, scaling, pain, pruritus, or fever, affecting people of any age. Management depends on the pathogen and severity, using appropriate antimicrobial therapy, hygiene, wound care, and complication monitoring.

head: Capecitabine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: malignant sex cord stromal tumor of ovary (a disease)
head_desc: Capecitabine is an oral fluoropyrimidine antimetabolite and prodrug of 5-fluorouracil that is converted in tumors and tissues to metabolites inhibiting thymidylate synthase and incorporating into RNA and DNA, thereby impairing nucleic acid synthesis. It is used for colorectal and breast cancers, including metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is hand-foot syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with severe dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Malignant ovarian sex cord-stromal tumor is a rare ovarian neoplasm arising from steroid hormone-producing sex cord or stromal cells of the ovary. It often affects children or younger women and may present with an adnexal mass, abdominal pain, or hormonal manifestations such as precocious puberty, menstrual abnormalities, or virilization. Management is primarily surgical, with staging and selective adjuvant chemotherapy for advanced, recurrent, or high-risk disease.

head: Vincristine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Vincristine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds β-tubulin, inhibits microtubule polymerization, and arrests mitosis. It is used in combination chemotherapy for acute lymphoblastic leukemia, lymphomas, and several pediatric solid tumors. A notable toxicity is dose-limiting peripheral neuropathy; intrathecal administration is contraindicated because it is uniformly fatal.
tail_desc: Ewing sarcoma is a malignant small round blue cell tumor of bone and soft tissue, usually arising in children and adolescents. It is driven by characteristic chromosomal translocations, most often creating an EWSR1-FLI1 fusion. Patients typically present with localized pain, swelling, and sometimes fever. Management generally combines multiagent chemotherapy with surgery and/or radiotherapy for local control.

head: Demeclocycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Demeclocycline is a tetracycline-class antibiotic that binds the 30S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis. It has been used for susceptible bacterial infections and, notably, to treat chronic hyponatremia due to syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) by reducing renal responsiveness to ADH. A notable adverse effect is photosensitivity; it is also contraindicated in pregnancy and young children because of tooth discoloration and effects on bone growth.
tail_desc: Sclerosing cholangitis is a chronic cholestatic disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by inflammation, fibrosis, and multifocal stricturing of intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic bile ducts, leading to impaired bile flow and progressive liver injury. Patients may have fatigue, pruritus, jaundice, or recurrent cholangitis; many with primary disease have inflammatory bowel disease. Management focuses on symptom control, treating complications, endoscopic relief of dominant strictures, and liver transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Potassium Iodide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Potassium iodide is an inorganic iodide salt and thyroid-active agent. It supplies iodide to saturate the sodium-iodide symporter in the thyroid, acutely inhibiting thyroid hormone release and blocking uptake of radioactive iodine. Established uses include thyroid protection in radiation emergencies, preoperative management of hyperthyroidism, and use as an expectorant. Notable risks include iodism or hypersensitivity; it is contraindicated in iodine sensitivity and some thyroid disorders.
tail_desc: Sporotrichosis is a subacute to chronic fungal infection of the skin and subcutaneous lymphatics, caused by traumatic inoculation of Sporothrix species from soil, plants, or animal scratches. It typically affects gardeners, agricultural workers, and cat-exposed people, producing nodules, ulcers, and lymphangitic spread; pulmonary or disseminated disease can occur. Management usually involves prolonged antifungal therapy, commonly itraconazole.

head: Estradiol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Estradiol is the principal endogenous estrogen, a steroid hormone used as an estrogen replacement therapy. It binds and activates nuclear estrogen receptors ERα and ERβ, regulating transcription in estrogen-responsive tissues. Clinically it is used for menopausal symptoms, vulvovaginal atrophy, hypoestrogenism, and prevention of postmenopausal osteoporosis. A notable risk is venous thromboembolism; it is contraindicated in estrogen-dependent malignancy and active thromboembolic disease.
tail_desc: Amenorrhea is a reproductive-endocrine disorder of the female menstrual system defined by absence of menstruation, classified as primary or secondary. It results from pregnancy or disruption of hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian, uterine, or outflow-tract function, including ovarian insufficiency, endocrine disease, low energy availability, or structural abnormalities. Patients present with absent menses, sometimes with delayed puberty or infertility. Management targets the underlying cause, with pregnancy exclusion and hormonal, nutritional, or surgical treatment as appropriate.

head: Leuprolide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: hereditary breast ovarian cancer syndrome (a disease)
head_desc: Leuprolide is a synthetic gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist peptide that initially stimulates, then downregulates pituitary GnRH receptors, suppressing luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone release and thereby reducing sex steroid production. It is used for advanced prostate cancer, endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and central precocious puberty. A notable adverse effect is an initial tumor or symptom flare at treatment onset.
tail_desc: Hereditary breast ovarian cancer syndrome is an inherited cancer predisposition disorder affecting the breast and female reproductive system, most often caused by germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 pathogenic variants that impair homologous recombination DNA repair. It is characterized by markedly increased risks of early-onset breast and ovarian cancer, also male breast, pancreatic, and prostate cancers. Management centers on genetic counseling/testing, intensive surveillance, and consideration of risk-reducing surgery.

head: Capecitabine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Capecitabine is an oral fluoropyrimidine antimetabolite and prodrug of 5-fluorouracil that is converted in tumors and tissues to metabolites inhibiting thymidylate synthase and incorporating into RNA and DNA, thereby impairing nucleic acid synthesis. It is used for colorectal and breast cancers, including metastatic disease. A notable toxicity is hand-foot syndrome; it is contraindicated in patients with severe dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase deficiency.
tail_desc: Ovarian endometrioid adenocarcinoma is an epithelial ovarian cancer of the female reproductive system, histologically resembling endometrial adenocarcinoma and often associated with endometriosis. It typically affects peri- or postmenopausal women and may present with pelvic pain, abdominal distension, or an adnexal mass. Management usually involves surgical staging and cytoreductive resection, with platinum-based chemotherapy according to stage and residual disease.

head: Cilastatin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Cilastatin is a renal dehydropeptidase I inhibitor, a carbapenem adjunct rather than an antibacterial itself. It blocks brush-border DHP-I in the kidney, preventing renal metabolism of imipenem and reducing nephrotoxic metabolites, thereby increasing urinary recovery of active imipenem. It is approved in fixed combination with imipenem/cilastatin for susceptible bacterial infections; adverse effects can include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious beta-lactam allergy.
tail_desc: Campylobacter fetus infectious disease is a bacterial infection, usually systemic rather than enteric, caused by Campylobacter fetus with a predilection for bloodstream and vascular tissues. It most often affects older adults, pregnant people, and immunocompromised patients, causing bacteremia, fever, cellulitis, thrombophlebitis, endovascular infection, or meningitis. Management typically requires targeted antibiotic therapy and evaluation for metastatic or vascular foci.

head: Terbinafine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Terbinafine is an allylamine antifungal that inhibits fungal squalene epoxidase, blocking ergosterol synthesis and causing toxic squalene accumulation in dermatophytes. It is used for onychomycosis and superficial dermatophyte infections such as tinea pedis, tinea corporis, and tinea cruris. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity; oral terbinafine is contraindicated in patients with chronic or active liver disease.
tail_desc: Cutaneous candidiasis is a superficial fungal infection of the skin caused mainly by Candida species, usually Candida albicans, affecting warm, moist intertriginous areas. It presents with erythematous, macerated plaques and characteristic satellite pustules or papules, especially in infants, obese individuals, and immunocompromised patients. Management focuses on reducing moisture and friction, addressing predisposing factors, and using topical antifungals, with systemic therapy reserved for extensive or refractory disease.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: inborn disorder of porphyrin metabolism (a disease)
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Inborn disorders of porphyrin metabolism are inherited porphyrias affecting heme biosynthesis, chiefly involving the liver and/or erythropoietic system. Enzyme defects cause accumulation of porphyrin precursors or porphyrins, producing acute neurovisceral attacks, photosensitive cutaneous lesions, or both. They may present from childhood to adulthood depending on subtype. Management focuses on trigger avoidance, supportive care, and subtype-specific measures such as hemin or photoprotection.

head: Rifampicin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Rifampicin is a rifamycin-class ansamycin antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase by binding the β-subunit encoded by rpoB, thereby blocking RNA synthesis. It is a cornerstone of combination therapy for tuberculosis and is also used for leprosy and prophylaxis of Neisseria meningitidis and Haemophilus influenzae type b exposure. Notable adverse effects include hepatotoxicity and potent cytochrome P450 induction causing major drug interactions.
tail_desc: Mycobacterial infectious disease is a bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium species, most often affecting the lungs but also skin, lymph nodes, and disseminated sites. It results from intracellular infection with granulomatous inflammation. Clinical features vary from chronic cough, fever, weight loss, and night sweats to localized soft-tissue or nodal disease, especially in immunocompromised patients. Management relies on prolonged multidrug antimycobacterial therapy, with source evaluation and public health measures when tuberculosis is involved.

head: Lorazepam (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Lorazepam is a benzodiazepine anxiolytic and sedative-hypnotic that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA\(_A\) receptors, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission. It is used for anxiety disorders, short-term relief of anxiety symptoms, status epilepticus, preanesthetic sedation, and insomnia in selected settings. A notable adverse effect is dose-related sedation and respiratory depression, especially with other CNS depressants.
tail_desc: Insomnia is a sleep-wake disorder of the nervous system characterized by persistent difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early awakening despite adequate opportunity for sleep. It is multifactorial, often linked to hyperarousal and commonly associated with psychiatric, medical, or substance-related factors. Typical features include nonrestorative sleep and daytime fatigue or impaired concentration, especially in adults. Management emphasizes sleep hygiene, cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia, and selected pharmacologic treatment when appropriate.

head: Hydroxychloroquine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Hydroxychloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in lysosomes, raises endosomal pH, and inhibits toll-like receptor signaling. It is approved for malaria and widely used for systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis. A notable adverse effect is retinal toxicity with prolonged use, requiring ophthalmologic monitoring.
tail_desc: Familial porphyria cutanea tarda is an inherited hepatic porphyria affecting heme biosynthesis, usually caused by reduced uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity. It leads to porphyrin accumulation and chronic photosensitivity, with blistering, skin fragility, hyperpigmentation, and scarring on sun-exposed areas, often in adults. Management focuses on avoiding triggers, photoprotection, periodic phlebotomy, and sometimes low-dose hydroxychloroquine.

head: Fluoxetine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Fluoxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, a phenoxyphenylpropylamine derivative, that inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT) to increase synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, bulimia nervosa, panic disorder, and certain bipolar depression regimens. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with monoamine oxidase inhibitors, making concomitant use contraindicated.
tail_desc: Narcolepsy-cataplexy syndrome is a central nervous system sleep-wake disorder, usually corresponding to narcolepsy type 1, caused by loss of hypothalamic orexin (hypocretin) signaling, often on an autoimmune basis in genetically susceptible people. It presents with excessive daytime sleepiness and cataplexy, often with hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis, and disturbed nocturnal sleep, typically beginning in childhood or adolescence; management uses wake-promoting agents and anticataplectic therapy.

head: Ampicillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ampicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bactericidal activity. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory, urinary tract, gastrointestinal, and meningitis pathogens such as Listeria monocytogenes and Enterococcus species. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Pasteurellosis is a zoonotic bacterial infection, usually involving the skin and soft tissues after animal bites or scratches, most commonly caused by Pasteurella multocida from cats or dogs. It typically presents with rapid-onset pain, erythema, swelling, and sometimes cellulitis or abscess; severe cases may involve respiratory infection or sepsis in vulnerable patients. Management centers on wound care and appropriate antibiotics.

head: Rifampicin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Rifampicin is a rifamycin-class ansamycin antibiotic that inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase by binding the β-subunit encoded by rpoB, thereby blocking RNA synthesis. It is a cornerstone of combination therapy for tuberculosis and is also used for leprosy and prophylaxis of Neisseria meningitidis and Haemophilus influenzae type b exposure. Notable adverse effects include hepatotoxicity and potent cytochrome P450 induction causing major drug interactions.
tail_desc: Gastrointestinal anthrax is a severe gastrointestinal infection caused by ingestion of Bacillus anthracis spores in contaminated meat, leading to ulcerative, hemorrhagic inflammation of the oropharyngeal or intestinal mucosa and systemic toxemia. It typically affects exposed individuals in endemic or outbreak settings and presents with fever, severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, bloody diarrhea, ascites, oropharyngeal ulcers, and sepsis; management requires urgent intravenous antibiotics and supportive care, with possible surgical treatment for complications.

head: Methotrexate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: non-papillary transitional cell carcinoma of the bladder (a disease)
head_desc: Methotrexate is an antifolate antimetabolite and folic acid analog that primarily inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, reducing thymidylate and purine synthesis; anti-inflammatory effects also involve increased adenosine signaling. It is used for cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and ectopic pregnancy. A notable risk is myelosuppression, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Non-papillary transitional cell carcinoma of the bladder is a urothelial malignancy of the urinary tract characterized by a flat or solid, often invasive growth pattern rather than papillary fronds. It typically affects older adults and commonly presents with painless hematuria and irritative voiding symptoms. Management is guided by stage and grade, using transurethral resection, intravesical therapy, and for muscle-invasive disease, radical cystectomy with systemic chemotherapy.

head: Chloroquine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Chloroquine is a 4-aminoquinoline antimalarial and immunomodulatory agent that accumulates in acidic vesicles; in Plasmodium it inhibits heme detoxification within the parasite food vacuole. It is used for treatment and prophylaxis of chloroquine-sensitive malaria and for some autoimmune disorders, notably rheumatoid arthritis and lupus. A notable risk is retinal toxicity, especially with prolonged use.
tail_desc: Porphyria cutanea tarda is the most common cutaneous porphyria, affecting heme biosynthesis in the liver through deficient uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity, usually acquired and less often familial. It causes photosensitive skin fragility, blistering, erosions, hyperpigmentation, and hypertrichosis on sun-exposed areas, typically in adults. Management centers on trigger removal, strict photoprotection, periodic phlebotomy, and sometimes low-dose hydroxychloroquine.

head: Etacrynic acid (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Etacrynic acid is a non-sulfonamide loop diuretic of the aryloxyacetic acid class that inhibits the Na+/K+/2Cl− cotransporter (NKCC2, SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of Henle, increasing urinary sodium and water excretion. It is used to treat edema associated with heart failure, renal disease, or hepatic cirrhosis. A notable adverse effect is ototoxicity, especially with high doses or intravenous use.
tail_desc: Hypercalcemia is a metabolic disorder of calcium homeostasis affecting multiple organ systems, characterized by elevated serum calcium, most commonly from primary hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Patients may be asymptomatic or develop polyuria, polydipsia, constipation, nausea, neurocognitive changes, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac abnormalities. Management focuses on treating the cause and lowering calcium with hydration, medications, and urgent measures in severe cases.

head: Vitamin A (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Vitamin A comprises fat-soluble retinoids, including retinol and retinyl esters. It is converted to active retinoic acid, which binds nuclear retinoic acid receptors (RARs) and retinoid X receptors (RXRs) to regulate gene transcription involved in vision, epithelial differentiation, growth, and immune function. It is used to prevent and treat vitamin A deficiency; excessive doses can cause hepatotoxicity and teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Dermatitis is a common inflammatory disorder of the skin, often caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, irritant or allergic exposures, or immune dysregulation. It typically presents with pruritus, erythema, scaling, and sometimes vesicles or lichenification, affecting both children and adults depending on subtype. Management generally focuses on trigger avoidance, skin hydration with emollients, and anti-inflammatory topical therapies such as corticosteroids.

head: Oxacillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Oxacillin is a penicillinase-resistant, semisynthetic beta-lactam penicillin used mainly for infections caused by methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus and other susceptible gram-positive bacteria. It binds bacterial penicillin-binding proteins, inhibiting peptidoglycan cross-linking and cell wall synthesis. A notable adverse effect is hypersensitivity, and it is contraindicated in patients with a serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Suppurative cholangitis is an acute bacterial infection of the biliary tract, usually arising from biliary obstruction that causes infected bile under pressure within the bile ducts. It affects the hepatobiliary system and typically presents with fever, right upper quadrant pain, jaundice, and possible sepsis, often in adults with gallstones or strictures. Management requires urgent antibiotics and biliary decompression, usually by endoscopic drainage.

head: Fusidic acid (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Fusidic acid is a steroidal antibiotic of the fusidane class that inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by binding elongation factor G (EF-G). It is used mainly against susceptible staphylococcal infections, including skin and soft-tissue infections, and is available in topical and systemic formulations in some regions. A notable adverse effect is hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated with statins because of serious rhabdomyolysis risk.
tail_desc: Yersinia infectious disease is a bacterial infection, usually involving the gastrointestinal system, caused by Yersinia species such as Y. enterocolitica or Y. pseudotuberculosis. It is acquired mainly through contaminated food or water and can produce fever, diarrhea, abdominal pain, mesenteric adenitis, and sometimes post-infectious reactive arthritis, especially in children. Management is largely supportive, with antibiotics reserved for severe, invasive, or high-risk cases.

head: Epirubicin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Epirubicin is an anthracycline antineoplastic antibiotic, structurally related to doxorubicin. It intercalates into DNA, inhibits topoisomerase II, and generates free radicals, impairing DNA replication and transcription. It is used in breast cancer and other solid tumors, including as adjuvant therapy in early breast cancer. A notable toxicity is cumulative dose-related cardiomyopathy/heart failure.
tail_desc: Classic Hodgkin lymphoma is a malignant B-cell lymphoma of the lymphatic system, defined by rare Reed-Sternberg cells in an inflammatory background. It typically presents with painless cervical or mediastinal lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, and a bimodal age distribution, especially in young adults and later life. Management usually combines stage-adapted chemotherapy, often with involved-site radiotherapy, with high cure rates.

head: Enoxaparin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin (factor IIa). It is used to prevent and treat venous thromboembolism and in acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Dexpanthenol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Dexpanthenol is the alcohol analog of pantothenic acid (provitamin B5), used mainly as a topical dermatologic and ophthalmic epithelial protectant and moisturizer. It is converted in tissues to pantothenic acid, a precursor of coenzyme A, supporting epithelial barrier repair and hydration rather than acting through a single receptor target. Established uses include minor skin irritation, wound healing support, and dry eye formulations; contact dermatitis or local irritation can occur.
tail_desc: Dermatitis is a common inflammatory disorder of the skin, often caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, irritant or allergic exposures, or immune dysregulation. It typically presents with pruritus, erythema, scaling, and sometimes vesicles or lichenification, affecting both children and adults depending on subtype. Management generally focuses on trigger avoidance, skin hydration with emollients, and anti-inflammatory topical therapies such as corticosteroids.

head: Valproic acid (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Valproic acid is a branched-chain fatty acid anticonvulsant and mood-stabilizing agent. Its mechanisms are multifactorial, including increased central GABA availability, blockade of voltage-gated sodium channels, and modulation of T-type calcium currents. It is used for epilepsy, bipolar disorder, and migraine prophylaxis. A notable risk is severe hepatotoxicity, and it is contraindicated in pregnancy because of major teratogenicity.
tail_desc: Major affective disorder is not a standard, specific diagnostic term in contemporary medicine; it has been used inconsistently to refer broadly to severe mood disorders such as major depressive disorder or bipolar disorder. Because the exact entity is ambiguous, its pathology, clinical features, affected population, and management cannot be described reliably without further specification.

head: Mepenzolate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Mepenzolate is a quaternary ammonium antimuscarinic (anticholinergic) agent. It competitively blocks acetylcholine at muscarinic receptors, reducing gastrointestinal motility and secretions. It has been used as an adjunct in peptic ulcer management and for gastrointestinal hypermotility disorders. Notable adverse effects include typical anticholinergic reactions such as dry mouth and constipation, and it is contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma.
tail_desc: Clostridium difficile colitis is an infectious inflammatory disease of the colon caused by overgrowth of toxin-producing C. difficile, usually after antibiotic exposure disrupts normal gut flora. It typically affects hospitalized or older adults and causes watery diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis, with risks of pseudomembranous colitis and toxic megacolon. Management centers on stopping the precipitating antibiotic, initiating targeted anti-C. difficile therapy, and supportive care.

head: Metoprolol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Metoprolol is a cardioselective beta-adrenergic receptor blocker of the aryloxypropanolamine class that primarily antagonizes β1-adrenergic receptors, reducing heart rate, contractility, and renin release. It is used for hypertension, angina, heart failure, and after myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bradycardia; it is contraindicated in severe bradycardia, cardiogenic shock, and second- or third-degree heart block.
tail_desc: Familial mitral valve prolapse is an inherited cardiovascular disorder affecting the mitral valve, characterized by myxomatous degeneration and superior displacement of one or both leaflets into the left atrium during systole. It often presents in multiple family members with a mid-systolic click, mitral regurgitation, palpitations, chest discomfort, or may be asymptomatic. Management centers on echocardiographic surveillance, symptom control, and valve repair or replacement when regurgitation becomes severe.

head: Levonorgestrel (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Levonorgestrel is a synthetic progestin (19-nortestosterone derivative) that acts as an agonist at progesterone receptors, suppressing the luteinizing hormone surge, inhibiting or delaying ovulation, and altering cervical mucus and the endometrium. It is used for contraception, including emergency contraception and intrauterine systems, and for some heavy menstrual bleeding indications. Irregular uterine bleeding is a notable adverse effect.
tail_desc: Endometriosis of the uterus is a gynecologic disorder of the female reproductive system in which ectopic endometrial glands and stroma involve uterine tissue, often presenting during the reproductive years. It commonly causes dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, abnormal uterine bleeding, and sometimes infertility. Management is typically directed toward symptom control and fertility goals, using hormonal suppression or surgery when needed.

head: Cyclopentolate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Cyclopentolate is an antimuscarinic mydriatic and cycloplegic agent, chemically a synthetic tertiary amine, that competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the iris sphincter and ciliary muscle. It is used ophthalmically to induce mydriasis and cycloplegia for eye examination and refraction. A notable adverse effect is transient ocular irritation, and it should be used cautiously in patients with angle-closure glaucoma.
tail_desc: Uveitis is an inflammatory disorder of the uveal tract of the eye, often involving adjacent ocular structures. It may result from autoimmune disease, infection, trauma, or be idiopathic. Typical features include eye pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision, and floaters, affecting both adults and children. Management depends on cause and severity, commonly using corticosteroids, cycloplegics, and treatment of any underlying infectious or systemic condition.

head: Escitalopram (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Escitalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, the S-enantiomer of citalopram, that primarily inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT) to increase synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety disorder. A notable adverse effect is QT interval prolongation, and it should be used cautiously with other serotonergic or QT-prolonging drugs.
tail_desc: Bipolar disorder is a mood disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by recurrent episodes of mania or hypomania and depression. Its cause is multifactorial, involving genetic susceptibility and dysregulation of brain neurotransmitter and circadian systems. It typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood and is managed with mood stabilizers, atypical antipsychotics, psychotherapy, and relapse prevention.

head: Mezlocillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Mezlocillin is a broad-spectrum acylureidopenicillin (beta-lactam) antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to bactericidal activity. It has been used for susceptible infections including urinary tract, respiratory, intra-abdominal, and biliary tract infections, with activity against some Gram-negative organisms including Pseudomonas. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions; it is contraindicated in patients with penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: IgG4-related sclerosing cholangitis is a fibroinflammatory biliary tract disease within the spectrum of IgG4-related disease, caused by IgG4-positive plasma cell infiltration and fibrosis of the bile ducts. It typically affects older men, often with autoimmune pancreatitis, and presents with obstructive jaundice, cholestatic liver tests, and biliary strictures. Management usually involves corticosteroids, with immunosuppressive steroid-sparing therapy or biliary intervention in selected cases.

head: Amoxicillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Amoxicillin is an aminopenicillin, a beta-lactam antibacterial agent. It binds penicillin-binding proteins to inhibit bacterial cell-wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, leading to cell lysis. It is widely used for susceptible infections such as otitis media, sinusitis, streptococcal pharyngitis, lower respiratory, urinary tract, skin infections, and in combination regimens for Helicobacter pylori. A notable contraindication is hypersensitivity to penicillins.
tail_desc: Salmonellosis is an infectious gastrointestinal disease caused by Salmonella bacteria, usually acquired from contaminated food or water. It typically produces acute diarrhea, fever, abdominal cramps, nausea, and sometimes vomiting, and can affect all ages, with more severe invasive disease in infants, older adults, and immunocompromised people. Management is mainly supportive with rehydration; antibiotics are reserved for severe or high-risk cases.

head: Magnesium hydroxide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Magnesium hydroxide is an inorganic magnesium salt used as an antacid and osmotic laxative. It neutralizes gastric hydrochloric acid in the stomach and, when taken orally in larger doses, retains water in the intestinal lumen by osmotic action; it has no specific receptor target. Established uses include relief of dyspepsia/heartburn and short-term treatment of constipation. A notable risk is hypermagnesemia, especially in patients with renal impairment.
tail_desc: Mastocytosis is a rare clonal mast cell disorder affecting the skin and/or systemic organs, caused in many cases by activating KIT mutations that lead to abnormal mast cell accumulation and mediator release. It may present in children as mainly cutaneous disease, while adults more often have systemic involvement with flushing, pruritus, urticaria pigmentosa, anaphylaxis, gastrointestinal symptoms, and bone disease; management focuses on trigger avoidance, anti-mediator therapy, and treatment of advanced systemic disease.

head: Magnesium trisilicate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Magnesium trisilicate is an inorganic magnesium silicate antacid. It acts locally in the stomach by chemically neutralizing gastric hydrochloric acid, raising intragastric pH; it has no specific molecular receptor target. It is used for symptomatic relief of hyperacidity, heartburn, and dyspepsia. Notable adverse effects include diarrhea, and it should be used cautiously in significant renal impairment because magnesium can accumulate.
tail_desc: Mastocytosis is a rare clonal mast cell disorder affecting the skin and/or systemic organs, caused in many cases by activating KIT mutations that lead to abnormal mast cell accumulation and mediator release.
