It may present in children as mainly cutaneous disease, while adults more often have systemic involvement with flushing, pruritus, urticaria pigmentosa, anaphylaxis, gastrointestinal symptoms, and bone disease; management focuses on trigger avoidance, anti-mediator therapy, and treatment of advanced systemic disease.

head: Erythromycin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
tail: skin disease caused by infection (a disease)
head_desc: Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis by blocking translocation. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin, and soft-tissue infections, and as an alternative in some penicillin-allergic patients. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, which can increase the risk of serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Skin disease caused by infection is a broad category of integumentary disorders in which bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites invade the skin and trigger local inflammation and tissue injury. Typical features include erythema, rash, pustules, vesicles, scaling, pain, pruritus, or fever, affecting people of any age. Management depends on the pathogen and severity, using appropriate antimicrobial therapy, hygiene, wound care, and complication monitoring.

head: Erythromycin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis by blocking translocation. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin, and soft-tissue infections, and as an alternative in some penicillin-allergic patients. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, which can increase the risk of serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Chancroid is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection of the genital skin and mucosa caused by Haemophilus ducreyi. It typically presents in sexually active adults with one or more painful, nonindurated genital ulcers and tender suppurative inguinal lymphadenopathy (buboes). Management is with appropriate antibiotics, drainage of fluctuant nodes when needed, and evaluation and treatment of sexual partners.

head: Metyrapone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Metyrapone is an adrenal steroidogenesis inhibitor, a pyridine derivative that selectively inhibits 11beta-hydroxylase (CYP11B1), reducing cortisol synthesis and increasing 11-deoxycortisol. It is used to diagnose hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis function and to treat endogenous Cushing syndrome, particularly hypercortisolemia. Notable adverse effects include hypertension and hirsutism from accumulation of mineralocorticoid and androgen precursors.
tail_desc: Cushing syndrome is an endocrine disorder caused by chronic excess glucocorticoid exposure, either exogenous from corticosteroid therapy or endogenous from ACTH-secreting pituitary tumors, adrenal tumors, or ectopic ACTH production. It affects adults and children and commonly causes central obesity, moon facies, proximal muscle weakness, hypertension, glucose intolerance, skin thinning, and purple striae. Management targets the cause, including tapering steroids, surgical resection, radiation, or cortisol-lowering drugs.

head: Serine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Serine is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Leprosy is a chronic infectious disease caused by Mycobacterium leprae that primarily affects the skin and peripheral nerves. It produces hypoesthetic or anesthetic skin lesions, peripheral neuropathy, weakness, and sometimes deformity, with a spectrum from paucibacillary to multibacillary disease. It occurs mainly in endemic regions and is managed with prolonged multidrug antibiotic therapy, early diagnosis, and disability prevention.

head: Gemcitabine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Gemcitabine is a deoxycytidine nucleoside analog antimetabolite chemotherapy drug. Intracellular phosphorylation generates active metabolites that inhibit ribonucleotide reductase and are incorporated into DNA, blocking DNA synthesis and inducing apoptosis. It is used for pancreatic cancer and also non-small cell lung, bladder, breast, and ovarian cancers. A notable adverse effect is myelosuppression, especially neutropenia and thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Head and neck cancer is a group of malignancies arising in the upper aerodigestive tract, including the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures, most often squamous cell carcinomas. Major causes include tobacco and alcohol exposure and, for some oropharyngeal tumors, human papillomavirus infection. Typical features are a persistent neck mass, sore throat, dysphagia, hoarseness, or oral lesions, usually in adults; management commonly involves surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy, and multidisciplinary supportive care.

head: Pentostatin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Pentostatin is a purine analog antineoplastic agent that potently inhibits adenosine deaminase, disrupting purine metabolism and causing accumulation of deoxyadenosine nucleotides with resultant lymphocyte toxicity. It is an established treatment for hairy cell leukemia. A notable adverse effect is severe immunosuppression with risk of serious infections; myelosuppression can also occur.
tail_desc: Richter syndrome is the transformation of chronic lymphocytic leukemia/small lymphocytic lymphoma into an aggressive lymphoma, most often diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, affecting the hematologic/lymphoid system. It typically occurs in patients with CLL and presents with rapidly enlarging lymph nodes, B symptoms, elevated LDH, and clinical deterioration. Management usually involves urgent lymphoma-directed chemoimmunotherapy, with consideration of stem cell transplantation in selected patients.

head: Propranolol (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Propranolol is a nonselective beta-adrenergic receptor antagonist of the aryloxypropanolamine class that blocks β1 and β2 receptors, reducing heart rate, contractility, and renin release. It is used for hypertension, angina, arrhythmias, secondary prevention after myocardial infarction, migraine prophylaxis, essential tremor, and symptomatic hyperthyroidism. A notable risk is bronchospasm, so it is contraindicated in asthma.
tail_desc: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome is a chronic autonomic nervous system disorder of orthostatic intolerance, characterized by an excessive heart-rate increase on standing without orthostatic hypotension. It likely reflects heterogeneous dysautonomia with hypovolemia, hyperadrenergic states, or neuropathic mechanisms. Patients, often adolescent girls or young women, develop palpitations, lightheadedness, fatigue, exercise intolerance, and sometimes syncope. Management emphasizes fluids, salt, compression, graded exercise, and selected medications.

head: Pentobarbital (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Pentobarbital is a short-acting barbiturate sedative-hypnotic that enhances inhibitory neurotransmission by positively modulating GABA-A receptors, increasing chloride channel opening. It is used clinically for sedation and preoperative hypnosis, emergency control of certain refractory seizures, and induction of coma to reduce intracranial pressure. Major risks include profound respiratory depression; it is contraindicated in acute intermittent porphyria.
tail_desc: Intracranial hypertension is a neurologic disorder characterized by abnormally elevated pressure within the skull due to increased brain tissue, cerebrospinal fluid, blood volume, or impaired CSF absorption/outflow. It commonly causes headache, nausea, vomiting, papilledema, visual disturbance, and sometimes altered consciousness. Management targets the cause and pressure reduction, using urgent monitoring, medical therapy, CSF diversion, or decompressive procedures when severe.

head: Carbamazepine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Carbamazepine is a dibenzazepine anticonvulsant and mood-stabilizing agent that primarily inhibits voltage-gated sodium channels, reducing high-frequency neuronal firing. It is used for focal and generalized tonic-clonic seizures, trigeminal neuralgia, and bipolar disorder, and is also approved for pain associated with true trigeminal neuralgia. A notable risk is serious cutaneous reactions, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome, especially in HLA-B*1502 carriers.
tail_desc: Neurohypophyseal diabetes insipidus is a central endocrine disorder of the hypothalamic-pituitary system caused by deficient secretion of arginine vasopressin from the posterior pituitary. It produces inability to concentrate urine, leading to polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, and risk of hypernatremia, in both children and adults. Causes include idiopathic, genetic, traumatic, neoplastic, or inflammatory lesions; treatment centers on desmopressin and correction of the underlying cause.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Head and neck cancer is a group of malignancies arising in the upper aerodigestive tract, including the oral cavity, pharynx, larynx, and related structures, most often squamous cell carcinomas. Major causes include tobacco and alcohol exposure and, for some oropharyngeal tumors, human papillomavirus infection. Typical features are a persistent neck mass, sore throat, dysphagia, hoarseness, or oral lesions, usually in adults; management commonly involves surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy, and multidisciplinary supportive care.

head: Hydrocortisone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Hydrocortisone is a glucocorticoid corticosteroid, the pharmaceutical form of cortisol. It binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to regulate gene transcription, producing anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects; it also has some mineralocorticoid activity. It is used for adrenal insufficiency replacement therapy and for inflammatory, allergic, and dermatologic disorders. Notable adverse effects include hyperglycemia, and systemic use is generally contraindicated in untreated systemic fungal infection.
tail_desc: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia is a group of inherited adrenal steroidogenesis disorders affecting the endocrine system, most commonly caused by 21-hydroxylase deficiency due to CYP21A2 mutations. Impaired cortisol synthesis, with or without aldosterone deficiency, causes excess androgen production, leading to virilization, salt-wasting, and precocious puberty in infants or children. Management centers on glucocorticoid replacement, mineralocorticoids when needed, and monitoring growth and electrolytes.

head: Enoxaparin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Enoxaparin is a low-molecular-weight heparin anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, preferentially inhibiting factor Xa and, to a lesser extent, thrombin (factor IIa). It is used to prevent and treat venous thromboembolism and in acute coronary syndromes such as unstable angina and non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. A notable adverse effect is bleeding; it is contraindicated in patients with active major bleeding.
tail_desc: Coronavinae infectious disease is a disease or medical condition.

head: Oxytetracycline (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Oxytetracycline is a tetracycline-class broad-spectrum antibiotic that reversibly binds the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit, blocking aminoacyl-tRNA attachment and inhibiting protein synthesis. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including certain respiratory, skin, and ocular infections, and has established veterinary use. A notable adverse effect is permanent tooth discoloration and impaired bone growth, so it is contraindicated during pregnancy and in young children.
tail_desc: Enterocolitis is inflammation of the small intestine and colon, affecting the gastrointestinal system. It commonly results from infectious, inflammatory, ischemic, or treatment-related injury to the intestinal mucosa, causing diarrhea, abdominal pain, fever, vomiting, and sometimes dehydration or bleeding. It occurs in both children and adults. Management depends on the cause and severity, including fluid and electrolyte support, nutritional care, and targeted treatment of the underlying trigger.

head: Vinblastine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Vinblastine is a vinca alkaloid antineoplastic agent that binds tubulin, inhibiting microtubule assembly and mitotic spindle formation, leading to metaphase arrest. It is used in cancers including Hodgkin lymphoma, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, testicular cancer, Kaposi sarcoma, and histiocytosis. A notable adverse effect is dose-limiting myelosuppression, especially leukopenia; intrathecal administration is contraindicated due to fatal neurotoxicity.
tail_desc: Urinary bladder neoplasm is a benign or, more commonly, malignant tumor of the urinary bladder within the urinary tract, usually arising from the urothelial lining; smoking and certain occupational chemical exposures are major risk factors. It often presents in older adults with painless hematuria and irritative voiding symptoms. Management depends on stage and grade, typically involving transurethral resection, intravesical therapy, and for invasive disease, radical cystectomy and/or systemic therapy.

head: Bumetanide (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Bumetanide is a sulfonamide loop diuretic that inhibits the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter NKCC2 (SLC12A1) in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, increasing renal sodium, chloride, and water excretion. It is used to treat edema associated with heart failure, hepatic cirrhosis, and renal disease. A notable adverse effect is hypokalemia.
tail_desc: Phosphorus metabolism disease comprises metabolic disorders of phosphate homeostasis affecting bone, kidney, and endocrine regulation. They result from abnormal intestinal absorption, renal tubular handling, or hormonal control of phosphate and vitamin D, causing hypo- or hyperphosphatemia. Clinical features may include rickets or osteomalacia, bone pain, fractures, muscle weakness, or ectopic calcification. Management targets the cause and corrects phosphate and vitamin D balance.

head: Erythromycin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that binds the 50S bacterial ribosomal subunit, inhibiting protein synthesis by blocking translocation. It is used for susceptible bacterial infections, including respiratory tract, skin, and soft-tissue infections, and as an alternative in some penicillin-allergic patients. A notable adverse effect is QT-interval prolongation, which can increase the risk of serious arrhythmias.
tail_desc: Necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis is an acute necrotizing periodontal infection of the gingiva caused by polymicrobial bacterial overgrowth in the setting of impaired host defenses. It classically causes severe gingival pain, bleeding, fetid breath, and punched-out necrosis of interdental papillae, often in smokers, malnourished, stressed, or immunocompromised patients. Management centers on gentle debridement, improved oral hygiene, antiseptic rinses, analgesia, and antibiotics when systemic involvement is present.

head: Difluprednate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Difluprednate is a difluorinated prednisolone-derived corticosteroid used mainly as an ophthalmic anti-inflammatory agent. It acts as a glucocorticoid receptor agonist, altering gene transcription to suppress inflammatory mediators and leukocyte activity. It is approved for postoperative ocular inflammation and pain and for endogenous anterior uveitis. A notable adverse effect is increased intraocular pressure, and it is contraindicated in most active ocular viral, mycobacterial, or fungal infections.
tail_desc: Iridocyclitis is an anterior uveitis affecting the iris and ciliary body of the eye. It results from intraocular inflammation, which may be idiopathic or associated with autoimmune disease, infection, or trauma. Typical features include eye pain, redness, photophobia, blurred vision, and sometimes small irregular pupils. Management generally targets the cause and inflammation, commonly with corticosteroid and cycloplegic eye drops.

head: Zinc gluconate (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Zinc gluconate is an oral zinc salt supplement and trace element preparation. It supplies zinc ions, an essential cofactor for numerous metalloenzymes and transcription factors, thereby correcting or preventing zinc deficiency rather than acting through a single specific molecular target. It is used for prevention and treatment of zinc deficiency. Notable adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset, especially nausea; hypersensitivity is a contraindication.
tail_desc: Attention deficit-hyperactivity disorder is a common neurodevelopmental disorder affecting brain networks that regulate attention, impulse control, and activity level. Its cause is multifactorial, with strong genetic contribution and altered neurotransmission implicated. It typically begins in childhood and features developmentally inappropriate inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity causing functional impairment. Management includes behavioral and educational interventions, parent training, and stimulant or nonstimulant medications.

head: Heparin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Heparin is a sulfated glycosaminoglycan anticoagulant that potentiates antithrombin III, thereby inhibiting thrombin (factor IIa) and factor Xa and reducing fibrin clot formation. It is used for prophylaxis and treatment of venous thromboembolism, pulmonary embolism, and acute coronary syndromes, and during procedures such as hemodialysis. A notable adverse effect is heparin-induced thrombocytopenia.
tail_desc: Hemoglobinopathy is an inherited red blood cell disorder of the hematologic system caused by mutations affecting hemoglobin structure or globin-chain production, such as in sickle cell disease and thalassemias. It commonly presents from infancy or childhood with chronic hemolytic anemia, jaundice, fatigue, pain crises or organ complications, depending on subtype. Management focuses on diagnosis by hemoglobin testing, supportive care, transfusion strategies, complication prevention, and sometimes curative stem cell transplantation.

head: Polyethylene glycol 3500 (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Polyethylene glycol 3500 is a pharmaceutical drug used in clinical practice.
tail_desc: Dry eye syndrome is a common ocular surface disorder of the lacrimal functional unit in which reduced tear production or excessive tear evaporation causes tear-film instability and hyperosmolarity, leading to surface inflammation and epithelial damage. Typical symptoms are dryness, burning, foreign-body sensation, fluctuating vision, and reflex tearing, especially in older adults. Management focuses on lubricating tears, environmental measures, eyelid/meibomian care, and treating underlying inflammation or contributing causes.

head: Fluoxetine (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Fluoxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) antidepressant, a phenoxyphenylpropylamine derivative, that inhibits the serotonin transporter (SERT) to increase synaptic serotonin. It is approved for major depressive disorder, obsessive-compulsive disorder, bulimia nervosa, panic disorder, and certain bipolar depression regimens. A notable risk is serotonin syndrome, especially when combined with monoamine oxidase inhibitors, making concomitant use contraindicated.
tail_desc: Myofascial pain syndrome is a regional musculoskeletal pain disorder involving skeletal muscle and surrounding fascia, characterized by hyperirritable trigger points within taut muscle bands. It causes localized or referred pain, tenderness, stiffness, and limited range of motion, often in adults after overuse, strain, or stress. Management typically emphasizes physical therapy, stretching, ergonomic correction, analgesics, and sometimes trigger-point injections or dry needling.

head: Ticarcillin (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Ticarcillin is a semisynthetic carboxypenicillin, a beta-lactam antipseudomonal antibiotic that binds penicillin-binding proteins and inhibits bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan cross-linking, causing cell lysis. It has been used for susceptible gram-negative infections, including Pseudomonas aeruginosa, often in combination with clavulanate. Notable adverse effects include hypersensitivity reactions, and it is contraindicated in patients with serious penicillin allergy.
tail_desc: Sclerosing cholangitis is a chronic cholestatic disease of the hepatobiliary system characterized by inflammation, fibrosis, and multifocal stricturing of intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic bile ducts, leading to impaired bile flow and progressive liver injury. Patients may have fatigue, pruritus, jaundice, or recurrent cholangitis; many with primary disease have inflammatory bowel disease. Management focuses on symptom control, treating complications, endoscopic relief of dominant strictures, and liver transplantation in advanced cases.

head: Hydrocortisone (a drug)
relation: off-label use: drug is sometimes used clinically for an indication outside of its approved label.
head_desc: Hydrocortisone is a glucocorticoid corticosteroid, the pharmaceutical form of cortisol. It binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors to regulate gene transcription, producing anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects; it also has some mineralocorticoid activity. It is used for adrenal insufficiency replacement therapy and for inflammatory, allergic, and dermatologic disorders. Notable adverse effects include hyperglycemia, and systemic use is generally contraindicated in untreated systemic fungal infection.
tail_desc: Acute respiratory distress syndrome is a severe inflammatory lung disorder of the respiratory system, usually triggered by sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, or major trauma, causing diffuse alveolar-capillary injury, noncardiogenic pulmonary edema, and profound hypoxemia. Patients, often critically ill adults, develop rapid-onset dyspnea, tachypnea, and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Management is supportive and directed at the cause, especially lung-protective mechanical ventilation and intensive care.
